### [1] [A Viscosity Semigroup Framework for Stable Image Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20620)
*Arina Oberoi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper develops a viscosity-solution framework for multiscale image representations using degenerate elliptic-parabolic PDEs, and applies it to a hybrid reconstruction operator combining learned inverse maps with nonlinear diffusion, achieving stable CT-based mesothelioma classification (AUC 0.875) compared to unstable baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a rigorous theoretical foundation for multiscale image representations using viscosity-solution theory, and to leverage this framework to design a stable reconstruction operator for medical image analysis.

Method: Uses viscosity-solution theory (comparison principles, well-posedness, contraction in supremum norm) for degenerate elliptic-parabolic PDEs; proposes a hybrid reconstruction operator combining a learned inverse map with nonlinear diffusion evolution.

Result: The proposed framework achieves AUC of 0.875 with negligible variation across epochs on CT-based mesothelioma classification, while the baseline model shows unstable AUC from 0.49 to 0.80.

Conclusion: The viscosity-solution framework provides theoretical stability guarantees (non-expansiveness), which translates into empirically stable and superior classification performance, validating the practical benefits of the proposed theory.

Abstract: Starting from the axiomatic formulation of scale-space theory, we develop a viscosity-solution framework for multiscale image representations arising from degenerate elliptic-parabolic partial differential equations. Rather than introducing a new semigroup theory, we work within the standard viscosity-solution setting, using comparison principles to obtain well-posedness, uniqueness, and contraction in the supremum norm. This perspective is used to motivate a hybrid reconstruction operator in which a learned inverse map is followed by a nonlinear diffusion evolution. At the continuous level, the diffusion operator satisfies non-expansiveness, which provides stability for the reconstruction process; this framework is then evaluated on a CT-based mesothelioma classification task, where it attains an AUC of 0.875 with negligible variation across epochs, while the baseline model acquires AUC values from 0.49 to 0.80 without a clear convergence pattern. These observations are consistent with the stabilizing role suggested by the discussed viscosity theory.

</details>


### [2] [A Projection-Based Surrogate Gradient Interpretation for Neural Codec Wrappers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20671)
*Esteban Pesnel,Julien Le Tanou,Michael Ropert,Aline Roumy,Thomas Maugey*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper shows that the SCALED surrogate gradient for training neural wrappers (pre/post-processing networks) for video codecs can be interpreted as a first-order local approximation of the codec. It demonstrates effectiveness for full neural wrapping across multiple codecs, quality factors, and tasks, achieving BD-Rate reductions up to -23.59% on x264 and -20.07% on VVenC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training neural wrappers for conventional video codecs is challenging due to the non-differentiability of codecs caused by discrete encoding decisions. Surrogate gradients have emerged as a solution, but the SCALED method lacked interpretability.

Method: The authors reinterpret the SCALED surrogate gradient as a first-order local approximation of the video codec, and apply it to train full neural wrappers (pre-and post-processing networks) across various codecs, quality factors, and tasks including multiple downscaling ratios.

Result: BD-Rate (PSNR) reductions of up to -23.59% on x264 and -20.07% on VVenC relative to standard resampling baselines, demonstrating generalization across different codecs and settings.

Conclusion: The SCALED surrogate gradient is interpretable as a local first-order approximation and is effective for full neural wrapping tasks beyond simple downscaling, generalizing well across diverse conditions.

Abstract: Neural wrappers are learned pre-and postprocessing networks designed to enhance the performance of conventional video codecs. Although these approaches can significantly improve compression efficiency, training them remains challenging due to the non-differentiability of video codecs, which arises from the multiple discrete decisions involved in the encoding process. Surrogate gradients have recently emerged as an effective solution for enabling end-to-end learning with conventional codecs. They offer two main advantages: they avoid training an additional network to mimic the codec, and they can improve compression performance. In particular, the recently proposed SCALED method, which leverages the true compression error, has shown strong results for training neural pre-processors such as downscalers. However, this SCALED gradient was originally introduced as a reparameterization trick, which limits its interpretability. In this paper, we show that this surrogate gradient can be interpreted as a first-order local approximation of the video codec, providing insight into its effectiveness. We further demonstrate that it is effective not only for learning downscaling operations, but also for the more challenging task of full neural wrapping with pre-and post-processing networks. Finally, we show that the approach generalizes well across different video codecs, quality factors, and tasks, including multiple downscaling ratios, yielding BD-Rate (PSNR) reductions of up to -23.59% on x264 and -20.07% on VVenC relative to standard resampling baselines.

</details>


### [3] [Jury Duty: Calibration and Orientation Failures in MLLM-as-a-Judge Under Cultural Ambiguity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20676)
*Daniel Lee,Harsh Sharma,Eunkyu Park,Pranav Narayanan Venkit,Jeonghwan Kim,Kah Mun Chia,Andreas Vlachos,Shafiq Joty*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces VOIR DIRE, a benchmark of 626 culturally paired image-text items across U.S. and Chinese contexts, showing that MLLM-as-a-Judge validation fails when human pools are culturally heterogeneous due to two bias types: positivity-floor calibration failure and orientation failure toward one cultural norm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MLLM-as-a-Judge validation relies on agreement with human annotations, but this metric is undefined when human evaluators come from culturally heterogeneous backgrounds with conflicting norms.

Method: Created VOIR DIRE benchmark with 626 culturally paired items (food/fashion/architecture) across U.S. and Chinese contexts, with culturally separate annotation pools, and tested six MLLMs for bias decomposition.

Result: Bias decomposes into positivity-floor calibration failure (compressed scale use) and orientation failure (defaulting to one cultural norm). Persona prompting helps calibration but not orientation; reference-pool in-context demonstrations deepen orientation bias.

Conclusion: Recommend reporting alignment against each reference pool separately and treating cross-pool divergence as a property of the judge, not a flaw to be eliminated.

Abstract: MLLM-as-a-Judge is conventionally validated by agreement with human annotations, but this metric is undefined when the human pool is culturally heterogeneous. We introduce VOIR DIRE, a multimodal benchmark of 626 culturally paired image--prompt artifacts spanning U.S. and mainland Chinese contexts across food, fashion, and architecture, with annotator pools that are within-pool reliable (a = 0.86/0.74) but cross-pool divergent on evaluation (Q1 r = -0.12). Across six MLLMs, the bias decomposes into two failures: a positivity-floor calibration failure (compressed scale use) and an orientation failure (default to one cultural norm). On this corpus, where contested items are sampled to split the two pools, the floor mechanically validates the more-permissive Chinese reading; persona prompting partially recovers calibration, but the orientation residual survives, evidence the tilt is not reducible to scale compression. Reference-pool in-context demonstrations deepen the orientation residual and inflate the high end rather than restoring use of the low end. Model origin adds a small additive tilt (~0.10 MAE) that is approximately invariant under demonstration. We recommend reporting alignment against each reference pool separately and treating cross-pool divergence as a judge property.

</details>


### [4] [Beyond ROC-AUC: Operating-Point Performance Reporting for Biometric Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20680)
*Ajan Ahmed,Masudul H. Imtiaz*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper argues that full ROC-AUC is misleading for biometric evaluation because it weights all FMRs equally, while real systems operate only at low FMRs. The authors demonstrate through experiments across four modalities that AUC can rank systems opposite to their actual low-FMR performance, and re-iterate DET curves and FNMR at fixed FMR as the primary reporting standard.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current practice often reports threshold-independent metrics like ROC-AUC and EER even though biometric systems are deployed at strict low-FMR operating points. The full ROC-AUC equally weights the entire FMR range (0 to 1), placing most weight on regions where the system is never used, potentially hiding poor low-FMR behavior and even reversing system rankings.

Method: The authors test seven pretrained matchers across four modalities (face, voice, iris, fingerprint), using bootstrap confidence intervals and paired bootstrap tests to compare performance on full ROC-AUC versus FNMR at a fixed low FMR (10^-3).

Result: A system with higher full ROC-AUC can be significantly worse at FMR=10^-3. For face recognition, FaceNet achieved higher full AUC, while ArcFace achieved higher TMR at FMR=10^-3, with both gaps significant and non-overlapping confidence intervals.

Conclusion: The DET curve and FNMR at a fixed FMR should be the primary reporting metrics for biometric evaluation, with ROC-AUC and EER retained only as supplementary context. This aligns with the existing ISO/IEC 19795-1 standard.

Abstract: A biometric verifier is often deployed with a strict false match budget, so only a narrow, low false match rate (FMR) slice of the score range is used. A reporting standard for this setting already exists. ISO/IEC 19795-1 asks for error rates at stated operating points, for the detection error tradeoff (DET) curve as the view of the trade-off between FMR and the false non-match rate (FNMR), and for an interval of uncertainty on every value. In practice, a single area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (ROC-AUC), the equal error rate (EER), or a verification accuracy is still reported as the resolution, which is a threshold-independent summary that the standard does not endorse. The full ROC-AUC averages the true match rate (TMR) with equal weight over the whole FMR range from 0 to 1, so almost all of its weight is placed where the system is never operated; low-FMR behavior can then be hidden, and the order of two systems can even be reversed. The guideline is revisited in this paper and tested against seven pretrained matchers across four modalities, face, voice, iris, and fingerprint, each reported with bootstrap confidence intervals and paired bootstrap tests. A system that looks stronger on full ROC-AUC is shown to be significantly worse at FMR = 10^-3. For face, a higher full AUC was obtained by FaceNet, whereas a higher TMR at FMR = 10^-3 was obtained by ArcFace, and both gaps were significant with non-overlapping intervals. Hence, the DET curve and the FNMR at a fixed FMR are re-iterated in this paper as the primary report, with ROC-AUC and EER retained as supplementary context.

</details>


### [5] [A UAV-Based Multi-Modal Vision System for Automated Sideslope Deformation Monitoring and Hazard Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20681)
*Jingfeng Zhang,Yi Li,Xianchong Liang,Huan Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents an automated UAV-borne LiDAR workflow for detecting expressway slope hazards, integrating single-observation hazard screening (RandLA-Net) and multi-epoch deformation monitoring (grid-wise elevation differencing), validated through field experiments showing centimeter-level deformation detection capability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Slope hazards threaten expressway infrastructure safety, but conventional manual inspection is inefficient and unsafe, especially for severely deteriorated slopes. There is an urgent need for automated, high-precision large-area slope observation and analysis.

Method: A three-part workflow: (1) shared data-acquisition and ground-surface extraction from UAV-borne LiDAR, (2) single-observation hazard-screening branch using RandLA-Net, and (3) multi-epoch deformation-monitoring branch using grid-wise elevation differencing.

Result: The workflow extracts usable ground-surface point clouds under vegetation cover, identifies potential hazard zones from single-observation data, and quantifies centimeter-level elevation changes via multi-epoch grid differencing, validated through controlled experiments, field tests, and simulations.

Conclusion: This end-to-end UAV-borne LiDAR workflow provides an implementable, automated solution for slope-hazard monitoring and intelligent early warning, demonstrating feasibility for real expressway slope environments.

Abstract: Slope hazards constitute a major safety threat to expressway infrastructure, and their evolution is typically manifested as slow surface deformation. Conventional manual inspection suffers from low efficiency and inadequate operational safety, especially on severely deteriorated slopes. Accordingly, there is an urgent need for an automated, high-precision solution capable of large-area slope observation and analysis. This study aims to develop a highly automated workflow for slope hazard detection using Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV)-borne Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR). The proposed workflow consists of a shared data-acquisition and ground-surface extraction stage, a single-observation hazard-screening branch based on RandLA-Net, and a multi-epoch deformation-monitoring branch based on grid-wise elevation differencing. To validate the effectiveness of the proposed system, we conducted multiple UAV-borne LiDAR data-acquisition flights in real expressway slope environments. The results show that the workflow can extract usable ground-surface point clouds under vegetation cover, identify potential hazard zones from single-observation point clouds, and quantify centimeter-level elevation changes using multi-epoch grid differencing. This study establishes an end-to-end UAV-borne LiDAR-based workflow for slope inspection and demonstrates its feasibility through controlled experiments, field tests, and simulation-based validation, thereby providing an implementable solution for automated slope-hazard monitoring and intelligent early warning.

</details>


### [6] [Open Annotations and Synthetic Data for Field Localisation in Indian Bank Cheques](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20682)
*Jaganadh Gopinadhan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The authors release bounding-box annotations for 112 Indian bank cheque images, produce 295 synthetic cheques via cut-paste, and provide a ResNet-50 baseline. However, a no-learning mean-box baseline achieves 0.691 mIoU, and synthetic data offers no measurable improvement over real-only training—a cautionary negative result for fixed-layout documents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The IDRBT Cheque Image Dataset lacks field annotations and has unclear redistribution terms, hindering automated cheque processing research.

Method: (1) Manual six-field bounding-box annotation of all 112 real cheques; (2) A cut-paste pipeline generating 295 synthetic cheques by compositing real patches onto content-erased templates; (3) A ResNet-50 direct-regression baseline predicting all fields in one forward pass.

Result: A mean-box baseline (predicting training-set averages) reaches 0.691 mIoU and 80% accuracy at IoU≥0.5. Cut-paste synthetic data yields no measurable improvement over real-only training when controlling for seed and compute.

Conclusion: Appearance-only augmentation does not help fixed-layout documents; layout-varying synthesis is needed instead. The released annotations and synthetic images are openly available on Hugging Face Hub.

Abstract: Automated cheque processing requires localising key fields (date, legal amount, IFSC code, account number, signature, and payee name)
  before any recognition step. The IDRBT Cheque Image Dataset is, to our knowledge, the only public collection of Indian bank cheques, but it
  ships without field annotations and with no stated licence, so its redistribution terms are unclear. We address both limitations. First,
  we release six-field bounding-box annotations for all 112 cheques in the dataset, distributed annotations-only and keyed to the original
  filenames so that the IDRBT redistribution terms are respected. Second, we release 295 fully redistributable synthetic cheque images
  produced by a cut-paste pipeline that composites annotated field regions from real cheques onto content-erased, bank-specific canvas
  templates; because patches are pasted at their source coordinates, annotations carry forward unchanged. Third, we provide a ResNet-50
  direct-regression baseline that predicts all six fields in a single forward pass, and use it for a controlled test of the synthetic data.
  The test is sobering: because cheque layouts are rigid, a no-learning baseline that simply predicts each field's mean training box already
  reaches 0.691 mean IoU and 80% accuracy at IoU >= 0.5, and once seed variance and training compute are accounted for, the cut-paste
  synthetic data yields no measurable improvement over real data alone (an equal-compute real-only model matches or beats the
  synthetic-augmented model on every aggregate metric). We report this negative result in full, since it cautions against assuming
  appearance-only augmentation helps fixed-layout documents and points instead to layout-varying synthesis. The annotations and synthetic
  images are released as reusable resources on the Hugging Face Hub under permissive licences.

</details>


### [7] [Shear-Free Viewport Magnification for 360-Degree via Spherical Mobius Boosts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20684)
*Boyang Li,Hezhao Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces spherical Möbius boosts, conformal maps that enable fixed-budget viewport magnification in 360-degree imaging without introducing anisotropic stretching or shear. Evaluated on SUN360 dataset, the method achieves a median +3.26 dB PSNR gain over offset cubemap, but trades global quality for viewport fidelity, making it suitable for prediction-conditioned foveated resampling rather than universal encoding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing view-adaptive 360° projections improve viewport resolution through non-conformal warps, which introduce anisotropic stretching and shear. The authors aim to develop a conformal (angle-preserving) mapping that can reallocate sampling budget toward a target viewport without geometric distortion.

Method: The authors formulate spherical Möbius boosts as exact conformal maps (quasiconformal dilatation K=1) for fixed-budget viewport magnification. These continuous spherical warps reallocate samples toward a target direction while preserving local angles.

Result: On SUN360 saliency audit (72 panoramas, 216 paired viewport targets), C1 Möbius boosting improves viewport PSNR over optimized offset cubemap in all paired cases: case-level median gain +3.26 dB, image-level median gain +3.23 dB, panorama-level bootstrap 95% CI [+3.15, +3.33] dB. However, Pareto analysis shows this trades full-sphere WS-PSNR for shear-free viewport fidelity.

Conclusion: Möbius boosting is positioned as a geometric primitive for prediction-conditioned foveated 360° resampling rather than a universal encode-once layout. Strong boosts are useful for accurately targeted viewports, while large target uncertainty calls for weaker boosts or fallback.

Abstract: Viewport-adaptive 360-degree imaging seeks to allocate a fixed sampling budget to the region a viewer is likely to observe. Existing view-biased projections increase viewport resolution through non-conformal warps, which can introduce anisotropic stretching and shear. We formulate spherical Mobius boosts as exact conformal maps for fixed-budget viewport magnification. The continuous spherical warp has quasiconformal dilatation K = 1, reallocating samples toward a target direction while preserving local angles. On a SUN360 saliency audit with 72 panoramas and 216 paired viewport targets, C1 Mobius boosting improves viewport PSNR over optimized offset cubemap on all paired cases, with case-level median gain +3.26 dB, image-level median gain +3.23 dB, and panorama-level bootstrap 95% CI [+3.15, +3.33] dB. Pareto analysis shows that this is not a free global-quality improvement: C1 trades full-sphere WS-PSNR for shear-free viewport fidelity. Prediction-error and filtering studies identify the operating envelope: strong boosts are useful for accurately targeted viewports, while large target uncertainty calls for weaker boosts or fallback. These results position Mobius boosting as a geometric primitive for prediction-conditioned foveated 360-degree resampling rather than a universal encode-once layout.

</details>


### [8] [ARGUSTRACK: A Multi-View Annotation System for Multi-Object Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20687)
*Hao Vo,Duc Nguyen,Ngan Le*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ARGUS-TRACK is a multi-camera annotation system that streamlines cross-view labeling by working directly on a bird's-eye-view plane, using temporal propagation and semi-automatic detection to reduce annotation time for Multi-Camera Multi-Target tracking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The scarcity of annotated multi-view data is a major bottleneck for MCMT tracking. Existing annotation tools focus on single-camera workflows or depend on LiDAR, making cross-view labeling tedious and impractical for camera-only setups.

Method: ARGUS-TRACK allows annotators to label on a BEV plane, automatically projecting annotations to 2D bounding boxes across views via calibrated camera parameters. It includes a Temporal Aware module (propagates annotations from previous frames) and a Multi-camera Semi-annotation module (uses 2D detectors + foot-point estimation to generate candidate BEV positions).

Result: Evaluated via a pilot study on multi-camera broiler tracking, ARGUS-TRACK substantially reduces annotation time compared to conventional single-camera labeling workflows.

Conclusion: ARGUS-TRACK effectively addresses the multi-camera annotation bottleneck, ensuring identity consistency and accelerating labeling through BEV-based interaction and semi-automated mechanisms, suitable for camera-only MCMT setups.

Abstract: Multi-Camera Multi-Target (MCMT) tracking has emerged as a critical capability for applications ranging from autonomous driving to animal behavior monitoring. While recent advances have yielded sophisticated tracking algorithms, the availability of annotated multi-view data remains a significant bottleneck. Existing annotation tools predominantly support single-camera workflows or rely on LiDAR sensors, making cross-view labeling tedious and impractical for camera-only setups. We present ARGUS-TRACK, a multi-camera annotation system that addresses these limitations by enabling annotators to work directly on a bird's-eye-view (BEV) plane. Given calibrated camera parameters, a single ground-plane annotation is automatically projected into 2D bounding boxes across all relevant views, inherently ensuring identity consistency without manual cross-view alignment. To further accelerate the labeling process, ARGUSTRACK incorporates two complementary mechanisms: a Temporal Aware module that propagates annotations from preceding frames to initialize new ones, requiring only minor positional adjustments; and a Multi-camera Semi-annotation module that leverages off-the-shelf 2D detectors combined with foot-point estimation to automatically generate candidate BEV positions for annotator verification. We evaluate ARGUSTRACK through a pilot study on multi-camera broiler tracking and demonstrate that it substantially reduces annotation time compared to conventional single-camera labeling workflows.

</details>


### [9] [NeoJaundice-AI: Smartphone-Based Neonatal Jaundice Detection Using Dual-Input Deep Learning and Synthetic Augmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20689)
*Rahul Patel,Nirjala Jarpula*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NeoJaundice-AI is a smartphone-based, offline system using dual-branch EfficientNet-B0 to detect neonatal jaundice from skin and sclera photos, achieving 91.8% accuracy and 1.4 mg/dL bilirubin MAE, with synthetic jaundice generation addressing data scarcity for darker skin tones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neonatal jaundice affects ~15 million newborns annually in India. Standard blood tests are impractical in rural clinics with limited lab facilities, motivating a cheap, fast, offline smartphone screening alternative.

Method: Dual-branch EfficientNet-B0 architecture independently processes skin and sclera images. Deep features are fused with handcrafted YCbCr color statistics for both 4-class severity classification and continuous bilirubin regression. Includes synthetic jaundice generation via YCbCr channel modifications, skin-tone normalization for Fitzpatrick Types IV-VI, and INT8 quantization/ONNX conversion for mobile deployment.

Result: 91.8% classification accuracy, 93.5% clinical sensitivity, 1.4 mg/dL bilirubin MAE. Model size reduced to 8.3 MB after quantization, inference under 3 seconds on Android devices.

Conclusion: First India-focused neonatal jaundice AI system combining multimodal image fusion, skin-tone adaptation, synthetic data augmentation, and fully offline mobile deployment in a single framework.

Abstract: Neonatal jaundice (hyperbilirubinemia) is one of the most common conditions affecting newborns worldwide, with India alone recording roughly 15 million cases per year. Early detection is critical, yet standard diagnosis requires blood tests that are often impractical in rural clinics where laboratory facilities are limited. This paper presents NeoJaundice-AI, a smartphone-based screening system that uses photographs of a baby's skin and sclera (eye white) to estimate jaundice severity and predict serum bilirubin levels in under three seconds without requiring internet connectivity.
  The proposed system is built on a dual-branch EfficientNet-B0 architecture that independently processes skin and sclera images. Deep features are fused with handcrafted YCbCr color statistics to jointly perform four-class severity classification and continuous bilirubin regression. A key contribution is a synthetic jaundice generation method that simulates bilirubin-induced yellowing through controlled YCbCr channel modifications on normal neonatal skin images. This approach addresses data scarcity, particularly for severe jaundice cases and darker Indian skin tones (Fitzpatrick Types IV to VI). In addition, a skin-tone normalization module improves prediction consistency across diverse neonatal complexions.
  Experimental results demonstrate an overall classification accuracy of 91.8 percent, a clinical sensitivity of 93.5 percent, and a bilirubin mean absolute error of 1.4 mg/dL. After INT8 quantization and ONNX conversion, the model size is reduced to 8.3 MB while maintaining inference times below three seconds on standard Android devices. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first India-focused neonatal jaundice AI system that combines multimodal image fusion, skin-tone adaptation, synthetic data augmentation, and fully offline mobile deployment within a single framework.

</details>


### [10] [Spatio-Temporal Wildfire Spread Prediction in Canada using a Video Swin-Hybrid-U-Net and Satellite Imagery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20693)
*Maulik Srivastava,Esha Saha,Hao Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents a deep learning framework using a U-Net with Video Swin Transformer encoder for predicting next-day wildfire spread in Canada, trained on public data from 2014-2023, achieving strong predictive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Wildfires in Canada are increasingly threatening, and existing models lack scalability or fail to capture temporal dynamics effectively, motivating the need for accurate forecasting tools.

Method: A U-Net architecture integrating a Video Swin Transformer encoder with a convolutional decoder, processing three-day sequences of meteorological and environmental variables from public Google Earth Engine data, trained on major Canadian wildfire events (2014-2023).

Result: Strong predictive performance, effectively leveraging spatio-temporal attention to forecast next-day fire incidence maps, capturing complex wildfire dynamics unique to Canada.

Conclusion: The model successfully captures Canada's complex wildfire dynamics and temporal variability, paving the way for advanced spatio-temporal forecasting research and operational applications using public datasets.

Abstract: Background: Wildfires in Canada present increasing threats to ecosystems, communities, and infrastructure, demanding accurate forecasting tools to aid mitigation efforts. Existing models often lack scalability or fail to capture temporal dynamics effectively. Aims: This study aims to develop a deep learning framework tailored to Canadian wildfire spread prediction that captures spatio-temporal patterns in environmental data. Methods: We propose a U-Net architecture integrating a Video Swin Transformer encoder with a convolutional decoder to model three-day sequences of meteorological and environmental variables. Data are exclusively sourced from public repositories via Google Earth Engine, ensuring transparency and scalability. The model is trained and tested on a curated dataset of major Canadian wildfire events from 2014 to 2023. Key results: Our approach achieves strong predictive performance by effectively leveraging spatio-temporal attention to forecast next-day fire incidence maps. Conclusions: The model successfully captures complex wildfire dynamics unique to Canada's landscape and temporal variability. Implications: This framework paves the way for advanced spatio-temporal wildfire forecasting research and operational applications using publicly accessible datasets.

</details>


### [11] [AEF-Econ: Toward Plug-and-Play Socioeconomic Foundation Embeddings from AlphaEarth for Urban Remote Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20697)
*Shuyang Hou,Ziqi Liu,Haoyue Jiao,Lutong Xie,Yaxian Qing,Xiaopu Zhang,Qingyang Xu,Zhangyan Xu,Xuefeng Guan,Huayi Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces AEF-Econ, a socioeconomic remote sensing foundation embedding that improves upon AlphaEarth Foundations (AEF) by integrating seven heterogeneous data streams across 36 Chinese cities over eight years. Through 31 controlled experiments, the authors identify a bottleneck where low-dimensional semantic streams are suppressed by high-dimensional streams under shared reconstruction, and propose Capacity-Adaptive Reconstruction (CAR) with per-stream decoders and stream-level losses, raising cross-region R² from 0.301 to 0.848 and cross-tier R² from 0.160 to 0.693.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AlphaEarth Foundations (AEF) embeddings are pretrained on physical land-surface signals and perform poorly on socioeconomic tasks (R²=0.301 for cross-region, 0.160 for cross-tier). The authors aim to create socioeconomic remote sensing foundation embeddings that can capture cross-city hierarchies and intra-urban spatial organization.

Method: Integrated seven heterogeneous data streams (AEF embeddings, population, nighttime lights, remote sensing indices, POIs, urban morphology, cross-lingual text) across 36 Chinese cities over eight years; constructed CHN-Econ benchmark with 16 labels; conducted 31 controlled experiments along five axes (fusion architecture, self-supervised objective, text integration, embedding dimensionality, normalization); proposed Capacity-Adaptive Reconstruction (CAR) with per-stream decoders and stream-level losses to replace shared reconstruction.

Result: Five-axis ablated backbone improved R² from 0.301 to 0.832 (cross-region) and 0.160 to 0.671 (cross-tier). CAR further raised R² to 0.848 and 0.693, restored collapsed labels from negative to stable range. Inferred 14.4 million pixels, released AEF-Econ with 128d and 64d versions.

Conclusion: AEF-Econ captures cross-city hierarchies and intra-urban spatial organization under unsupervised settings, providing a socioeconomic remote sensing foundation embedding complementary to AEF physical embeddings.

Abstract: AlphaEarth Foundations (AEF) unify global remote sensing foundation embeddings through multimodal self-supervised learning, but their pretraining focuses on physical land-surface signals, limiting plug-and-play use in socioeconomic tasks. We integrate seven heterogeneous data streams across 36 Chinese cities over eight years - AEF embeddings, population, nighttime lights, remote sensing indices, points of interest (POIs), urban morphology, and cross-lingual text - and construct CHN-Econ, a socioeconomic benchmark with 16 labels in three categories. We conduct 31 controlled experiments along five axes: fusion architecture, self-supervised objective, text integration, embedding dimensionality, and normalization. Used alone as a linear probe, AEF achieves R2 values of only 0.301 for cross-region and 0.160 for cross-tier evaluation. The five-axis ablated backbone improves these scores to 0.832 and 0.671, respectively, but reveals that low-dimensional semantic streams are consistently suppressed by high-dimensional streams under shared reconstruction. To address this bottleneck, we propose Capacity-Adaptive Reconstruction (CAR), replacing shared reconstruction with per-stream decoders and stream-level losses to mitigate inter-stream capacity competition. CAR further raises cross-region and cross-tier R2 to 0.848 and 0.693, and restores collapsed labels from negative R2 to a stable range. Using CAR, we infer 14.4 million pixels across 36 cities and eight years and release AEF-Econ, including 128d and 64d compressed versions. Self-diagnostics and case studies show that AEF-Econ captures cross-city hierarchies and intra-urban spatial organization under unsupervised settings, providing a socioeconomic remote sensing foundation embedding complementary to AEF physical embeddings.

</details>


### [12] [Beyond Templates: Revisiting Zero-Shot Remote Sensing through Meta-Prompting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20702)
*Eirini Baltzi,Dionysis Christopoulos,Sotiris Spanos,Valsamis Ntouskos,Konstantinos Karantzalos*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VLMs show highly variable zero-shot performance in Earth Observation tasks depending on textual prompt design. LLM-generated descriptions introduce noise despite semantic richness, and lightweight query embedding calibration consistently improves results across 17 VLM variants and 12 RS datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why semantically rich LLM-generated class descriptions do not yield consistent gains over simple domain-adapted CLIP descriptions in zero-shot remote sensing tasks, and to explore the sensitivity of VLM performance to textual design choices.

Method: Evaluates 17 VLM variants and 12 RS datasets under Meta-Prompting for Visual Recognition (MPVR), conducts text log-likelihood analysis in whitened CLIP feature space, and studies query embedding calibration techniques.

Result: LLM descriptions introduce noise in text embedding space despite higher semantic expressiveness; lightweight calibration of query space consistently improves zero-shot classification and retrieval.

Conclusion: There is a trade-off between semantic richness and robustness; embedding calibration is a simple yet effective tool for improving zero-shot RS performance.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) have sparked growing interest in zero-shot Earth Observation (EO) downstream tasks, with further gains enabled by remote-sensing-adapted models. We examine this setting across 17 VLM variants and 12 remote sensing (RS) datasets under Meta-Prompting for Visual Recognition (MPVR), and show that zero-shot performance remains highly sensitive to textual design choices, from the meta-prompts used to guide the LLM in generating class descriptions to the descriptions themselves. We explore why semantically rich LLM-generated class descriptions do not translate into consistent gains over simple domain-adapted CLIP-style descriptions. While LLM descriptions are more semantically expressive, they can also introduce noise in the text embedding space, reducing robustness in downstream tasks. We support this observation through a text log-likelihood analysis in the whitened CLIP feature space, comparing LLM-generated and template-based descriptions. Building on this finding, we study query embedding calibration and show that lightweight calibration of the query space consistently yields strong improvements in zero-shot classification and retrieval. Overall, our results provide practical insight into the trade-off between semantic richness and robustness, and identify embedding calibration as a simple and effective tool for improving zero-shot remote sensing performance.

</details>


### [13] [Robust Image-Driven Phenotyping of Ovarian Tumor Cells using Optimized Dynamic Features in Hyperbolic Channels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20703)
*Hong-Fei Li,Xi-Lin Gao,Yi-Juan Xiang,Shu-Song Huang,Yi-lin Wang,Chun-Dong Xue,Zhuo Yang,Yong-Jiang Li,Xu-Qu Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A stability-guided analytical framework decouples flow-induced noise from biological signatures in label-free cellular mechanophenotyping, reducing flow-associated variance from 69.9% to 9.3% while maintaining diagnostic fidelity across ML models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Flow rate variations in microfluidic devices distort feature distributions, linking them to hydrodynamic artifacts rather than intrinsic biology, necessitating a method to separate noise from authentic mechanobiological signatures.

Method: Tracked 93-dimensional feature space (morphodynamic, kinematic, optical-density trajectories) from ovarian cells; applied cross-flow screening based on structural consistency and statistical persistence to isolate robust descriptors; used variance-attribution analysis to neutralize flow-conditioned artifacts.

Result: Reduced flow-associated variance from 69.9% to 9.3% in subtyping; identified 20 features for binary classification and 25 for subtyping; found binary discrimination relies on bulk kinematics while subtyping requires intracellular optical heterogeneity.

Conclusion: Establishes a robust, flow-independent foundation for continuous dynamic phenotyping in microfluidic devices, maintaining diagnostic fidelity across multiple architectures and restricted sampling.

Abstract: Label-free, image-based cellular mechanophenotyping in microfluidic devices provides a high-throughput method for single-cell profiling. However, while complex microchannels (e.g., hyperbolic geometries) reveal transient deformation dynamics under continuous extensional stress, the resulting high-dimensional feature spaces are highly susceptible to hydrodynamic artifacts. Flow rate variations often distort discriminative boundaries, linking feature distributions to fluid conditions rather than intrinsic biology. To overcome this, we introduce a stability-guided analytical framework that decouples flow-induced noise from authentic mechanobiological signatures. We tracked the morphodynamic, kinematic, and intracellular optical-density trajectories of healthy and malignant ovarian cells to build a 93-dimensional feature space. Using a cross-flow screening strategy based on structural consistency and statistical persistence, we isolated robust descriptors, creating task-adapted subsets (20 features for binary classification; 25 for cancer subtyping). Variance-attribution analysis confirmed the neutralization of flow-conditioned artifacts; notably, flow-associated variance in the primary principal component fell from 69.9% to 9.3% in the subtyping task. We also found that macroscopic binary discrimination depends on bulk kinematic transitions, while clonal subtyping requires localized intracellular optical heterogeneity. These optimized subsets maintained diagnostic fidelity across multiple machine learning architectures and restricted sampling conditions. This framework establishes a robust, flow-independent foundation for continuous dynamic phenotyping.

</details>


### [14] [MotionPyramid: Hierarchical Motion Representation and Residual Interfaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20705)
*Gao Zhu,Zaishuo Xia,Yubei Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MotionPyramid learns a hierarchical motion representation from data (like perceptual hierarchies), enabling reinforcement learning policies to control humanoids at multiple temporal resolutions - from coarse segments to fine motor commands - with learned structure that supports composition, interpolation, and residual control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether motion, like visual perception, can be organized into a hierarchical representation (edges→parts→objects in perception; motor commands→segments→skills in motion), and to enable structured abstract action representations for humanoid control.

Method: Train a recursive stack of latent decoders from a motion tracking teacher; low levels decode to immediate motor commands, high levels unfold through lower levels into temporally extended motion. Freeze the hierarchy and use it as action interfaces for RL policies. Introduce Residual Interfaces for multi-scale residual control.

Result: Coarse interfaces improve early learning and motion regularity; fine interfaces preserve precision. Hierarchy supports traversal, interpolation, transition, and qualitative composition. Residual Interfaces allow coarse programs and fine corrections to coexist.

Conclusion: Motion can be organized into a reusable multi-level representation like perception, providing structured abstraction without sacrificing controllability.

Abstract: We ask whether the representational hierarchy seen in perception, from local primitives such as edges to higher level structures such as parts and objects, can be established for motion. In humanoid control, low level actions specify immediate motor commands, while meaningful behavior is organized over longer temporal scales, including contacts, gait fragments, balance recovery, reaching, and whole body skills. We introduce MotionPyramid, a hierarchical action representation that learns such structure from motion data. Starting from a motion tracking teacher, it trains a recursive stack of latent decoders: low level latents decode to immediate full body motor commands, while higher level latents unfold through lower levels into temporally extended motion programs. After pretraining, the hierarchy is frozen and reused by downstream reinforcement learning policies as a family of action interfaces at different control resolutions. Experiments show the learned levels form a motion hierarchy: coarser interfaces improve early learning and motion regularity by constraining exploration to structured segments, while finer interfaces preserve feedback control and final task precision. Representation probes show the hierarchy supports traversal, interpolation, transition, and qualitative composition, exposing editable control handles across temporal scales. Finally, we introduce Residual Interfaces, letting a downstream policy maintain coarse, segment level, and frame level residual commands through the frozen hierarchy. Analogous to residual or skip connections in deep networks, this allows coarse motion programs and fine residual corrections to coexist within one controller. MotionPyramid shows that motion, like perception, can be organized into a reusable multi level representation, providing structured abstraction without sacrificing controllability.

</details>


### [15] [GEOPHYS: The Geometry of Physical Plausibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20707)
*Christian Internò,Alexander Pondaven,Habon Issa,Fabio Pizzati,Francesco Pinto,Markus Olhofer,Ivan Laptev,Philip Torr,Eero P. Simoncelli,Barbara Hammer,David Klindt*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GEOPHYS uses five geometric properties of per-frame embeddings from frozen image encoders to detect physical implausibility in videos, achieving state-of-the-art results (98.3% on LikePhys, 93.3% on IntPhys2) while being far more efficient than LLM-based methods, and improving video generation verification from 50.01% to 64.50% on PhysicsIQ.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humans detect implausible physical events in milliseconds, but existing ML approaches are slow and expensive—relying on external multimodal-LLM judges or requiring ad-hoc training modifications. The goal is to find a lightweight, efficient alternative.

Method: GEOPHYS extracts five geometric properties from per-frame embeddings of frozen image encoders—no fine-tuning needed. These properties correlate with human EEG responses to object-permanence violations and serve as indicators of physical plausibility.

Result: Achieves 98.3% on LikePhys and 93.3% on IntPhys2, outperforming V-JEPA 2, GPT-4o, Gemini, and twelve video diffusion models (near chance). As a verifier for video generation, lifts MAGI-1 from 50.01% to 64.50% on PhysicsIQ with 1.5x lower wall-clock and 4.65x lower memory than V-JEPA 2.

Conclusion: Physical plausibility in videos can be assessed efficiently by leveraging emergent geometric properties of temporal features from frozen image encoders, without expensive LLMs or custom training.

Abstract: While humans can identify physically implausible events within milliseconds, machine learning approaches addressing the same problem are extremely slow and expensive. They either rely on external multimodal-LLM judges or require ad-hoc modifications to the training procedure. In this work, we argue that indicators of physical plausibility are implicitly captured by five geometric properties of the per-frame embeddings produced by frozen image encoders. In aggregate, we call them GEOPHYS. First, we show that these signals correlate with human EEG responses to two forms of object-permanence violations. Second, GEOPHYS robustly discriminates physically implausible videos from realistic ones, achieving state-of-the-art physics-violation detection: 98.3% on LikePhys and 93.3% on IntPhys2, whereas V-JEPA 2, GPT-4o, Gemini, and twelve modern video diffusion models perform near chance. Third, used as a best-of-N verifier for physical alignment during video generation, GEOPHYS lifts MAGI-1 24B from 50.01% to 64.50% on PhysicsIQ at 1.5x lower wall-clock and 4.65x lower memory than the V-JEPA 2 world-model verifier. Ultimately, GEOPHYS demonstrates that physical plausibility in videos can be assessed by leveraging the emergent geometric properties of temporal features extracted from image encoders.

</details>


### [16] [TeleStyle V2: Beyond Content-Preserving Style Transfer with Self-Distillation and Distribution-Matching-Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20709)
*Shiwen Zhang,Yifan Xu,Haibin Huang,Chi Zhang,Xuelong Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TeleStyle V2 is an upgraded content-preserving style transfer model that supports all combinations of realistic/stylized content and style references, using Self-Distillation data synthesis and Distribution Matching Distillation to achieve strong performance comparable to commercial models like Gemini 3 Pro.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TeleStyle V1 only worked with photorealistic content and artistic style references, failing when content was artistic or style was realistic. The authors aimed to create a unified model handling all four reference combinations (RnR, RnS, SnR, SnS) while preserving general image editing capabilities.

Method: 1) Self-Distillation data synthesis strategy to construct training triplets from TeleStyle V1; 2) Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) to preserve text-guided image editing capability and fix content consistency degradation from SFT; 3) Prompt enhancer to solve content/style reference order confusion; 4) Uses Qwen-Image-Edit's VLM encoder (Qwen2.5-VL-7B) for free content/style prompt generation.

Result: TeleStyle V2-QIE-2509-DMD performs at least on par with Qwen-Image-Edit-2509-DMD in general image editing; achieves comparable style transfer performance with Gemini 3 Pro Image Preview (state-of-the-art commercial model).

Conclusion: TeleStyle V2 successfully extends style transfer to all content-style reference combinations, and the DMD technique effectively preserves general editing capabilities while maintaining style transfer quality equal to leading commercial solutions.

Abstract: Given a content reference and a style reference, content-preserving style transfer requires the model to generate stylized outputs with content and style consistency. We introduced TeleStyle V1 to tackle this problem. However, TeleStyle V1 is trained with photorealistic content reference and artistic style reference, which makes it incapable to cope with artistic content reference and realistic style reference in most cases. In this paper, we designed a Self-Distillation data synthesis strategy to construct such triplets from TeleStyle V1. Trained with such self-distilled triplets, our TeleStyle V2 supports Content-Style references in the forms of Realistic-and-Realistic (RnR), Realistic-and-Stylized (RnS), Stylized-and-Realistic (SnR), Stylized-and-Stylized (SnS). In addition, we found Distribution Matching Distillation could preserve the general text-guided image editing capability of the foundation model and fix the content consistency degradation caused by SFT process. Through quantitative evaluations, our TeleStyleV2-QIE-2509-DMD performs at least on par with Qwen-Image-Edit-2509-DMD, demonstrating strong general image editing skills beyond content-preserving style transfer. We observed the content/style reference order confusion problem in TeleStyle V1 and further introduced prompt enhancer to solve it. TeleStyle V2 uses Qwen-Image-Edit's VLM encoder, Qwen2.5-VL-7B, to generate content prompt and style prompt for free. TeleStyle V2 could achieve comparable style transfer performance with state-of-the-art commercial model, gemini-3-pro-image-preview.

</details>


### [17] [Video2Code: Generating Interactive Webpages from UI Videos via Action-Aware Revisit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20711)
*Mingde Xu,Zhen Yang,Yan Wang,Yu Wang,Xijun Liu,Zijun Dou,Wenyi Hong,Xiaotao Gu,Bin Xu,Jie Tang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Video2Code recovers executable UI state transitions from videos by first locating action-critical regions via coarse video understanding, then revisiting them at higher temporal resolution before generating HTML/CSS/JavaScript code, improving functional correctness over direct video observation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video-capable vision-language models fail to capture short action boundaries in UI videos, breaking the state-action-state transitions needed to implement webpage behavior. This is identified as state-transition misalignment.

Method: Video2Code performs coarse video understanding to locate action-critical regions, then uses a temporal clipping tool to revisit these regions at higher temporal resolution before generating interactive webpage code (HTML/CSS/JavaScript).

Result: Video2Code improves functional correctness over direct video observation, especially on dense multi-step interactions, strengthening the underlying open-source model for UI video-to-code generation.

Conclusion: Action-aware temporal resolution allocation is effective for recovering executable UI state transitions from videos, outperforming uniform sparse sampling approaches.

Abstract: UI videos provide a natural input for generating interactive webpages, as they capture both webpage appearance and action-triggered state transitions. However, directly applying video-capable vision-language models to this task remains insufficient. Existing models typically rely on sparse sampling or compressed temporal representations, which may miss short action boundaries and break the state-action-state transitions needed to implement webpage behavior. We formulate UI video-to-code generation as executable state-transition recovery from interaction videos, and identify this failure mode as state-transition misalignment. We introduce Video2Code, an action-aware video-to-code approach for recovering executable UI state transitions. Rather than allocating the visual budget uniformly across the video, Video2Code first performs coarse video understanding to locate action-critical regions, then invokes a temporal clipping tool to revisit these regions at higher temporal resolution before generating HTML/CSS/JavaScript code. We instantiate Video2Code with action-aligned video-code supervision and evaluate it under both visual and functional criteria. Experiments show that Video2Code substantially strengthens the underlying open-source model for UI video-to-code generation, improving functional correctness over direct video observation, especially on dense multi-step interactions.

</details>


### [18] [CDER-SME: A Cross-Device Event-RGB Micro-Expression Dataset under Multi-Level Stress Induction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20715)
*Jingting Li,Hui Sha,Su-Jing Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CDER-SME is a new cross-device Event-RGB dataset for micro-expression recognition, collected under stress induction with 92 subjects, featuring a hardware-agnostic alignment pipeline and demonstrating that cross-modal fusion outperforms single-modality methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MER benchmarks are limited to lab-controlled settings with rigid hardware-coupled sensing, lacking ecological validity and temporal sensitivity needed for real-world applications.

Method: A cross-device Event-RGB dataset collected via multi-level stress induction (cognitive and social), with a hardware-agnostic alignment pipeline for temporal synchronization and landmark-guided spatial registration, featuring 92 subjects and 1,963 expert-annotated samples.

Result: 790 Event-RGB pairs and 210 high-fidelity aligned pairs; cross-modal fusion improves performance over single-modality counterparts, confirming complementarity of event dynamics and RGB cues.

Conclusion: CDER-SME removes the need for coaxial calibration, offering a practical benchmark for cross-device alignment and deployable Event-RGB MER in real-world affective intelligence.

Abstract: Micro-expression recognition (MER) in realistic scenarios demands high temporal sensitivity and ecological validity, yet existing benchmarks are largely constrained to laboratory-controlled settings and rigid hardware-coupled sensing. We introduce CDER-SME, a cross-device Event-RGB dataset collected under a multi-level stress induction framework (cognitive and social) to elicit spontaneous emotional leakage. To enable reproducible acquisition with independent, decoupled sensors, we provide a hardware-agnostic alignment pipeline for temporal synchronization and landmark-guided spatial registration. CDER-SME adopts a three-tier structure with 92 subjects and 1,963 expert-annotated samples (Action Units and emotions), including 790 Event-RGB pairs and 210 high-fidelity aligned pairs. We further report a reproducible multimodal baseline, where cross-modal fusion improves performance over single-modality counterparts, supporting the complementarity of event dynamics and RGB cues. By removing the need for coaxial calibration, CDER-SME offers a practical benchmark for cross-device alignment and deployable Event-RGB MER in real-world affective intelligence.

</details>


### [19] [MIRAGE: Stealthy Visual Prompt Injection for Vulnerability Detection in Web Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20717)
*Xuelong Dai,Jianyu Ma,Boyang Ma,Biwei Yan,Yijun Yang,Yue Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes MIRAGE, a visual indirect prompt injection attack framework that generates perceptually benign adversarial images within spatially constrained regions (e.g., ad slots) to hijack MLLM-based web agents' next actions, operating under realistic third-party threat model constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing adversarial evaluations against MLLM-based web agents rely on permissive threat models and visually conspicuous artifacts, failing to capture realistic attack scenarios where the attacker is an unprivileged third party controlling only a small, spatially constrained region on a trusted web platform.

Method: MIRAGE uses diffusion models to generate perceptually benign adversarial images confined to attacker-controlled boundaries, combined with a robust optimization technique that leverages curvature-aware adversarial diffusion guidance and sparse dark-pixel residual perturbations for stealth and efficacy.

Result: Comprehensive evaluations against SeeAct and OpenClaw demonstrate the potency, realism, and stealth of the proposed MIRAGE attack framework.

Conclusion: MLLM-based web agents are vulnerable to realistic, constrained visual prompt injection attacks from unprivileged third parties, highlighting the need for stronger defenses against this novel attack surface.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM)-based web agents provide practical, high-precision solutions for visual browser automation; however, they inherently expand the attack surface, introducing novel vision-based vulnerabilities. Existing adversarial evaluations targeting these agents frequently rely on permissive threat models and visually conspicuous artifacts. In this paper, we investigate a constrained vulnerability detection setting: a trusted web platform where the evaluator acts solely as an unprivileged third party, such as a merchant or advertiser, controlling only a semantically legitimate, spatially constrained region, such as an ad slot, a sponsored card, or a localized widget. Operating under these realistic constraints, we propose MIRAGE, a novel visual indirect prompt injection framework for targeted next-action hijacking. Our approach leverages diffusion models to generate perceptually benign adversarial images strictly confined to the attacker-controlled boundaries permitted by the trusted service provider. To maximize attack efficacy within such a restrictive setting, we introduce a robust optimization technique combining curvature-aware adversarial diffusion guidance with sparse, dark-pixel residual perturbations. Comprehensive evaluations against prominent MLLM web agent frameworks, specifically SeeAct and OpenClaw, empirically demonstrate the potency, realism, and stealth of our proposed MIRAGE.

</details>


### [20] [Evaluation of Medical Vision Language Models HuluMed and MedGemma, and general purpose chatbots Gemma 3, ChatGPT Plus, and Claude Pro on real previously unseen wound images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20723)
*Yunzhe Xue,Mohammed Saim Ahmed Quadri,Neal Panse,Justin W. Ady,Usman Roshan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This study evaluates 6 Vision-Language Models (VLMs) on clinical wound assessment using 20 diverse wound cases and 240 clinician-graded decisions. ChatGPT (72.50%) and Claude (62.08%) outperformed specialized medical models like HuluMed (40.00%), MedGemma 27B (17.50%). Frontier general-purpose VLMs demonstrated stronger performance than medically specialized alternatives, though substantial limitations remain in advanced wound management reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chronic wound assessment is clinically challenging, requiring interpretation of morphology, tissue composition, vascular characteristics, and infection risk. VLMs offer potential for automated multimodal wound analysis, but their relative performance for this specialized clinical task was unknown.

Method: Six VLMs (ChatGPT, Claude, HuluMed, Gemma 3, MedGemma 4B, MedGemma 27B) were evaluated on an expanded dataset of 20 clinically diverse wounds. A structured twelve-question clinical framework was used covering classification, infection risk, vascular intervention, debridement urgency, therapy selection, and management planning. Performance was compared against 240 clinician-graded decisions.

Result: ChatGPT achieved 174/240 (72.50%), Claude 149/240 (62.08%). Among open-source/specialized models: HuluMed 96/240 (40.00%), Gemma 3 81/240 (33.75%), MedGemma 4B 62/240 (25.83%), MedGemma 27B 42/240 (17.50%). General-purpose frontier VLMs significantly outperformed medically specialized alternatives.

Conclusion: Frontier general-purpose multimodal systems demonstrate substantially stronger wound-analysis performance than medically specialized alternatives, highlighting the importance of broad multimodal reasoning alongside domain-specific knowledge. However, substantial limitations remain in advanced wound-management reasoning, procedural planning, and autonomous clinical reliability.

Abstract: Chronic wound assessment remains a clinically challenging task that requires accurate interpretation of wound morphology, tissue composition, vascular characteristics, and infection risk. Recent advances in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have introduced the possibility of automated multimodal wound analysis through image understanding combined with clinical reasoning. This study evaluates the performance of several general-purpose and medically specialized open-source and proprietary VLMs for clinical wound assessment using an expanded, curated dataset of 20 clinically diverse wounds spanning vascular, surgical, ischemic, venous, lymphedema, and amputation-related etiologies. Six VLMs were evaluated using a structured twelve-question clinical framework covering wound classification, infection risk, vascular intervention recommendations, debridement urgency, wound therapy selection, and advanced management planning. Across 20 wound cases and 240 clinician-graded wound-analysis decisions, ChatGPT achieved the highest overall performance with 174/240 correct responses (72.50%), followed by Claude with 149/240 (62.08%). Among the open-source and medically specialized models, HuluMed achieved the strongest performance with 96/240 correct responses (40.00%), followed by Gemma 3 (81/240, 33.75%), MedGemma 4B (62/240, 25.83%), and MedGemma 27B (42/240, 17.50%). The findings suggest that frontier general-purpose multimodal systems currently demonstrate substantially stronger wound-analysis performance than medically specialized alternatives, highlighting the continued importance of broad multimodal reasoning capabilities alongside domain-specific medical knowledge. Although current VLMs demonstrate promising potential for clinical decision support, substantial limitations remain in advanced wound-management reasoning, procedural planning, and autonomous clinical reliability.

</details>


### [21] [D2HDMap: Non-visible Driveline Map Prior for Online Vectorized HD Map Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20725)
*Seojun Shon,Chikao Tsuchiya,Dhaval Bhanderi,David Ilstrup,Hsinmin Cheng,Christopher Ostafew*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: D2HDMap is an online mapping system that uses a lightweight driveline prior (easier to maintain than HD maps) to guide estimation of visible road structures, achieving 44.8 mAP on geographically disjoint splits and showing robustness to localization noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing HD maps are costly to maintain and become outdated, while pure sensor-based online mapping struggles with long-range reliability and occlusion. The authors aim to combine the benefits of both approaches with a lightweight prior that is easier to create and update.

Method: D2HDMap injects a lightweight, non-visible driveline prior to guide estimation of visible road structures (lane dividers, road boundaries, crosswalks). Training with this prior improves generalization even when the prior is unavailable at inference time. Noise-aware training is also used to increase robustness to localization error.

Result: On a geographically disjoint split, D2HDMap achieves 44.8 mAP, surpassing recent state-of-the-art. Models retain performance even when priors are unavailable at inference. Noise-aware training substantially increases robustness to realistic localization error.

Conclusion: D2HDMap offers an effective compromise between HD map reliance and pure online mapping, using a lightweight driveline prior that improves both performance and robustness while being easier to maintain.

Abstract: Accurate, up-to-date representations of road structures are critical for the safe operation of autonomous vehicles. Existing systems rely either on costly, maintenance-heavy high-definition (HD) maps which compromise safety when outdated, or purely sensor-based online mapping which struggles with long-range reliability and occlusion. Systems incorporating map prior information into online mapping seek to overcome drawbacks of both approaches by combining them in some way. We propose 'Driveline To HD Map' (D2HDMap), an online mapping system that injects a lightweight, non-visible driveline prior to guide the estimation of visible road structures such as lane dividers, road boundaries and crosswalks. This prior incurs less effort to create and update compared to full HD map priors used in other approaches. We also show that training with such a prior can improve generalization at inference time when no prior is available. Ablation studies conducted on the nuScenes and Argoverse 2 dataset demonstrate that models trained using a driveline prior largely retain performance even when priors are not available. On a geographically disjoint split, D2HDMap achieves 44.8 mAP, surpassing recent state-of-the-art. Additionally, noise-aware training substantially increases robustness to realistic localization error.

</details>


### [22] [How Well Can Your Video Model Remember? Measuring Memory-Budget Trade-offs in Long Video Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20726)
*Yixian Tian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces a compact empirical model (a scaling law) that predicts how answer accuracy degrades in long video understanding as a function of frame budget B and temporal distance D. By fitting ~155K predictions across ten models, they find logit-accuracy scales linearly in log-budget with a distance-dependent exponent α(D) that decays log-linearly with distance. They show dramatic differences in budget effectiveness between streaming and base models (e.g., 7.4× difference at D=1000s) and propose α(D) as a diagnostic metric for streaming video models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: No prior framework predicts how accuracy degrades as frame budget shrinks and temporal distance increases in long video understanding, despite long-form models operating under strict budgets.

Method: Fitted a weighted least-squares model on ~155,000 binary predictions across ten models and three sampling strategies, deriving a law where logit-accuracy scales linearly in log-budget with a distance-dependent exponent α(D) that decays log-linearly with distance.

Result: The law achieves cell-level weighted R² = 0.05-0.75 across models. Budget effectiveness at D=1000s differs by ~7.4× between the best streaming (α=1.26) and base models (α=0.17). A 10× budget increase at D=1000s yields +29 pp for STREAMINGVLM vs +4 pp for base model.

Conclusion: α(D) enables principled budget allocation, reveals model-ranking reversal at long distance, and is proposed as a diagnostic metric for streaming video models.

Abstract: We introduce a compact empirical model that quantifies how answer accuracy degrades as a function of frame budget B and temporal distance D in long video understanding -- analyzing performance when recalling content from D seconds in the past using a fraction B of total frames. Long-form models operate under strict budgets, yet no prior framework predicts how accuracy degrades as B shrinks and events recede. We fit a weighted least-squares model on ~155,000 binary predictions across ten models and three sampling strategies, deriving a law where logit-accuracy scales linearly in log-budget with a distance-dependent exponent that decays log-linearly with distance. This budget exponent α(D) captures the marginal value of extra frames at distance D. The law achieves cell-level weighted R^2 = 0.05-0.75 across models. Notably, budget effectiveness at D = 1000 s differs by \approx 7.4\times between the best streaming and base models. STREAMINGVLM achieves α(1000) = 1.26 (95% CI: [1.06, 1.58]), meaning a tenfold budget increase substantially improves long-distance accuracy, while the best Qwen3-VL base model reaches only α(1000) = 0.17 (CI: [0.04, 0.34]). In accuracy space, a 10\times budget increase at D = 1000 s yields +29 percentage points for STREAMINGVLM versus +4 pp for the base model. Sampling strategies show model-dependent trade-offs: random sampling yields higher base sensitivity but steeper distance decay. We demonstrate how α(D) enables principled budget allocation, including a model-ranking reversal at long distance, and propose it as a diagnostic metric for streaming video models.

</details>


### [23] [VTOS: Learning to Orchestrate Vision Tools by Co-Searching Solutions and Observers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20728)
*Jinchao Ge,Lingqiao Liu,Shuwen Zhao,Lei Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VTOS is a framework that jointly searches for both solution programs (composing vision tools) and observer programs (diagnosing failures) to adaptively orchestrate vision tools, outperforming static pipelines on dense object counting and domain-shifted segmentation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing visual-programming agents generate fixed solution pipelines that are brittle under dense objects, occlusion, small targets, and domain shift, lacking adaptive tool orchestration.

Method: VTOS co-searches executable solution programs (composing tools like Grounding DINO, SAM, NMS, slice-and-detect) with observer programs that diagnose failures and generate feedback, accumulated in a shared VisionThoughts knowledge base to guide subsequent search.

Result: Outperforms static tool pipelines and agentic visual-programming baselines on dense object counting (LVIS-Count) and zero-shot plant-disease segmentation (PlantSeg-OOD).

Conclusion: Co-searching solutions and observers is an effective strategy for adapting vision tools to challenging computer vision tasks across diverse orchestration challenges.

Abstract: Vision foundation tools such as open-vocabulary detectors, segmentation models, and post-processing operators are powerful building blocks for computer vision, but their effectiveness depends heavily on how they are orchestrated: which tools are used, in what order, with what parameters, and under what visual conditions. Existing visual-programming agents typically generate a fixed solution pipeline, making them brittle under dense objects, occlusion, small targets, and domain shift. We introduce VTOS (Vision Tools Orchestration Search), a framework for adaptive visual tool orchestration through joint solution--observer search. VTOS co-searches executable solution programs that compose vision tools such as Grounding DINO, SAM, NMS, and slice-and-detect, together with observer programs that diagnose candidate solutions, identify failure modes, and generate actionable feedback. These observations are accumulated in a shared VisionThoughts knowledge base to guide subsequent search. We evaluate VTOS through two case studies: dense object counting on LVIS-Count and zero-shot plant-disease segmentation on PlantSeg-OOD, which stress different orchestration challenges including threshold calibration, NMS, slicing, mask refinement, and domain generalization. Across both tasks, VTOS outperforms static tool pipelines and agentic visual-programming baselines, showing that co-searching solutions and observers is an effective strategy for adapting vision tools to challenging computer vision tasks.

</details>


### [24] [XmoPipe: A Pipeline for Large-Scale In-the-Wild Human Motion Dataset Construction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20731)
*Nathan Salazar,Emmanuel Dellandréa,Mathieu Lefort,Alexandre Meyer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents a scalable pipeline that automatically constructs large-scale in-the-wild human motion datasets from online videos using keyword-based retrieval, monocular 3D motion extraction, and automatic text description generation, achieving performance comparable to traditional motion capture datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional marker-based motion capture is costly and limited in scale/diversity, creating a need for a scalable method to collect diverse, large-scale human motion data from unconstrained real-world videos.

Method: A scalable pipeline that: (1) retrieves videos from the web using keywords, (2) extracts 3D body and facial motion via monocular motion capture, and (3) generates high-level textual descriptions automatically. The pipeline supports targeted collection of motions, multi-person interactions, and expressive behaviors.

Result: Models trained on this pipeline's output (motion reconstruction and motion generation) achieve performance comparable to those trained on traditional motion capture datasets, with strong cross-dataset generalization.

Conclusion: The proposed pipeline is an effective, scalable alternative to costly marker-based motion capture for constructing diverse, large-scale human motion datasets from unconstrained online videos.

Abstract: Large-scale human motion datasets are essential for training robust motion models for analysis, synthesis, and understanding. While marker-based motion capture provides precise data, it is costly and limited in scale and diversity. Recent advances in monocular motion capture and video-language understanding open the way to extract plausible motion from unconstrained online videos. We present a scalable pipeline for constructing in-the-wild human motion datasets. From a few keywords, the system retrieves videos, extracts 3D body and facial motion, and generates high-level textual descriptions. The pipeline is flexible, enabling targeted collection of various motions, multi-person interactions, or expressive behaviors. We demonstrate its quality by training motion reconstruction and motion generation models, showing performance comparable to models trained on traditional motion capture datasets and strong cross-dataset generalization.

</details>


### [25] [UniSLAD: A Unified Framework for Structural and Logical Industrial Visual Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20768)
*Changyi Li,Chao Yang,Yu Xiao,Kari Tammi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UniSLAD is a unified, training-free framework that jointly detects structural and logical anomalies in industrial visual inspection by combining CNN and Transformer backbones with dual-granularity feature representations, achieving 99.4% and 93.1% accuracy on two benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing anomaly detection methods focus mainly on structural defects, while logical anomalies are underexplored. Since both types co-occur in practice, a unified solution is needed for comprehensive industrial anomaly detection.

Method: UniSLAD uses a dual-feature extractor (CNN for local textures + Transformer for global context), dual-granularity representation (patch-level memory banks with Mahalanobis Transform, image-level distribution maps with LUM and PMP), and operates without additional training.

Result: Achieves 99.4% and 93.1% on two industrial benchmarks, with ablation studies confirming each component's contribution.

Conclusion: UniSLAD provides a practical, training-free unified solution for both structural and logical anomaly detection, advancing comprehensive industrial automation.

Abstract: Visual anomaly detection is a fundamental task in industrial automation. While existing approaches have achieved notable progress in identifying structural defects, the detection of logical anomalies remains relatively underexplored. In practice, structural and logical anomalies frequently co-occur in industrial workflows. Therefore, a solution capable of detecting both structural and logical anomalies is crucial for advancing comprehensive anomaly detection research. To address this limitation, we propose a unified framework, termed UniSLAD, which jointly addresses logical and structural anomalies without additional training, enabling a practical solution for dynamic industrial environments. First, we introduce a dual-feature extractor that synergistically integrates a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) backbone for local texture perception with a Transformer backbone for global contextual reasoning, yielding richer and more comprehensive representations. Building on this foundation, we design dual-granularity feature representation modules. At the patch level, memory banks enhanced by the Mahalanobis Transform (MT) preserve representative features and support more discriminative anomaly scoring. At the image level, distribution maps are aggregated using Lower-Upper Mean (LUM) and Power Mean Pooling (PMP), yielding a more robust global representation than conventional average pooling. Extensive experiments on the two industrial benchmarks demonstrate that UniSLAD achieves competitive performance in comprehensive anomaly detection, achieving 99.4% and 93.1%, respectively. Furthermore, ablation studies verify the individual contributions and effectiveness of each proposed component.

</details>


### [26] [Robust Zero-Shot Generalization for Open-Vocabulary Action Recognition via Task Arithmetic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20734)
*Francesca Morandi,Omayma Moussadek,Federico Venturini,Mauro Suardi,Alessandro Banzatti,Francesco Cannarile,Angelo Porrello,Simone Calderara*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a method for Open Vocabulary Action Recognition that avoids domain-specific fine-tuning by merging task vectors from models trained on diverse public datasets, achieving superior zero-shot generalization in out-of-distribution settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional OVAR requires costly domain-specific fine-tuning that raises privacy and regulatory concerns. The authors aim to bypass target-domain training entirely.

Method: The approach uses model merging and task arithmetic to extract and combine task vectors from models fine-tuned on various public OVAR datasets, creating a merged model without target-domain training.
