Result: The merged model achieves superior zero-shot generalization in out-of-distribution settings compared to the pre-trained base model.

Conclusion: Knowledge recombination from existing datasets and models via task arithmetic is an effective alternative to domain-specific fine-tuning for robust OVAR.

Abstract: Open Vocabulary Action Recognition (OVAR) enables the recognition of novel actions by leveraging vision-language representations, overcoming the limitations of traditional closed-set approaches. However, achieving robust performance in real-world scenarios typically requires domain-specific fine-tuning, which is often costly and raises privacy and regulatory concerns. In this work, we propose an alternative paradigm that bypasses target-domain training and recombines knowledge from existing datasets and models. Leveraging model merging and task arithmetic, we extract and combine task vectors from models fine-tuned on diverse public OVAR datasets. We show that, in out-of-distribution settings, the resulting merged model achieves superior zero-shot generalization to the pre-trained base model. Code is available at https://github.com/omaymaMoussadek/robust-ovar

</details>


### [27] [REKEY: Metadata-Grounded Visual-Key Regeneration for Contamination-Resilient VQA Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20736)
*Tengjie Lin,Yutao Sun,Jingwei Ni,Shuhan Ge,Hao-Xuan Ma,Yanting Miao,Wangyue Lu,Mingshuai Chen,Tiancheng Zhao,Jianwei Yin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ReKey is a live benchmark protocol that randomly regenerates visual details in VQA images to prevent memorization and keep evaluation fresh, revealing that current VLMs score 9.5-18.8% higher on leaked benchmarks than on regenerated variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static VQA benchmarks become obsolete once items leak into training corpora, causing scores to reflect memorization rather than genuine visual ability. Rebuilding benchmarks is costly, and new static releases quickly become leaked artifacts.

Method: ReKey uses human-validated edit slots to randomly regenerate the answer-bearing visual key in real images at evaluation time, producing fresh instances with new answers, construction-grounded labels, and controlled visual-search difficulty.

Result: On V*Bench, the ReKey-regenerated benchmark revealed a 9.5-18.8 percentage point score drop across eight frontier VLMs compared to original items, indicating significant memorization effects.

Conclusion: By making the visual key renewable, ReKey keeps evaluation fresh as models and training data evolve, offering a practical solution to benchmark leakage in VQA.

Abstract: Static visual question answering (VQA) benchmarks age quickly: Once the items leak into training corpora, scores can reflect memorization rather than genuine visual ability, thus obscuring real progress. Rebuilding high-quality benchmarks such as V*Bench requires substantial human annotation, yet each static release can quickly become another leaked artifact. We propose ReKey, a live benchmark protocol that randomly regenerates the answer-bearing local detail, or visual key, in real images at evaluation time. Using human-validated edit slots, ReKey samples fresh instances with new answers, construction-grounded labels, and controlled visual-search difficulty. On V*Bench, the ReKey regenerated benchmark reveals a sharp score jump across eight frontier vision-language models (VLMs): The original items score 9.5--18.8 percentage points higher than the regenerated variants. By making the visual key renewable, ReKey keeps evaluation fresh as models and training data evolve.

</details>


### [28] [BELDE: Building a Large-scale Earth-observation Land-cover Dataset for Europe](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20909)
*Ümit Mert Çağlar,Alptekin Temizel*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces BELDE, a large-scale publicly available RGB land-cover segmentation dataset for Europe (1M+ image pairs from Sentinel-2 and ESA WorldCover), plus supplementary datasets for Korea and the US, and establishes baselines showing significant geographic domain shift challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing land-cover segmentation datasets are often limited in geographic coverage, scale, or public accessibility, while RGB imagery remains widely used due to cost and deployment constraints. There is a need for a large-scale, publicly available RGB dataset to support robust and transferable Earth observation models.

Method: The authors constructed BELDE from Sentinel-2 true-color images and ESA WorldCover annotations, producing 1,088,385 image-segmentation map pairs across Europe with 7 land-cover classes at 10m resolution. They also created BELDE-K (Korea) and BELDE-CA-NV (US) for cross-region evaluation. Multiple semantic segmentation architectures were used to establish baselines.

Result: Models trained on BELDE achieve 83.0% F1 on European test sets, but performance drops to 66.4% on BELDE-CA-NV and 58.3% on BELDE-K, demonstrating significant geographic domain shift challenges.

Conclusion: BELDE provides a continental-scale RGB segmentation benchmark that supports development of robust and transferable Earth observation models, while highlighting the critical problem of out-of-distribution geographic domain shift.

Abstract: Earth observation imagery plays a critical role in environmental monitoring, urban planning, disaster assessment, and climate analysis. While multi-spectral sensors are increasingly available, true-color (RGB) imagery remains widely used due to the power, cost, and deployment constraints of many satellite and aerial platforms. However, existing land-cover segmentation datasets are often limited in geographic coverage, scale, or public accessibility.
  To bridge this gap, we introduce BELDE (Building a Large-scale Earth-observation Land-cover Dataset for Europe), a publicly available dataset tailored for RGB-based remote sensing semantic segmentation. Constructed from Sentinel-2 true-color images and ESA WorldCover data annotations, BELDE contains 1,088,385 curated image-segmentation map pairs spanning Europe with 7 land-cover classes at 10 m spatial resolution, making it one of the largest publicly available RGB land-cover segmentation datasets for Earth observation. To facilitate cross-region generalization studies, we additionally introduce BELDE-K (16,607 pairs) covering the Republic of Korea and BELDE-CA-NV (88,155 pairs) covering California and Nevada in the United States.
  We establish baseline results using multiple semantic segmentation architectures and evaluate both in-domain and cross-domain performance. Models trained on BELDE achieve an F1 score of 83.0% on the European test set, while performance decreases to 66.4% on BELDE-CA-NV and 58.3% on BELDE-K, highlighting the challenges posed by out-of-distribution geographic domain shift. By providing a continental-scale RGB segmentation and evaluation benchmark, BELDE supports the development of robust and transferable Earth observation models. The dataset and benchmark resources will be publicly released.

</details>


### [29] [An approach with Visual and Tabular Mamba to multimodal medical data using Mixed Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20738)
*Matheus B. Rocha,Gustavo B. Dettogni,Renato A. Krohling*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Mamba-based (state space model) multimodal fusion architecture called Mixed Fusion for cancer classification, using two Mamba variants (one for visual, one for tabular data). It achieves competitive performance on two medical datasets (PAD-UFES-20 and NDB-UFES), with slightly lower balanced accuracy than Transformers on one dataset but superior on the other, along with notable recall gains and enhanced interpretability via SHAP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a complementary approach for integrating multimodal medical data (images + clinical/sociodemographic data) in cancer classification that improves interpretability and sensitivity, addressing limitations of existing methods like black-box Transformer-based models.

Method: A Mixed Fusion architecture based on two Mamba (state space model) variants: one for visual processing (lesion image classification), and another for tabular processing (using class probabilities from the visual Mamba plus clinical/sociodemographic data to produce the final diagnosis). Interpretability is enhanced via SHAP.

Result: On PAD-UFES-20 (skin lesions): slightly lower balanced accuracy than Transformer-based approaches. On NDB-UFES (oral cancer): superior balanced accuracy. Substantial gains were observed in recall. Mixed Fusion enables SHAP-based interpretability.

Conclusion: Mamba-based models are a suitable alternative for multimodal medical classification, particularly in scenarios where sensitivity (recall) is an important requirement.

Abstract: This article presents a complementary approach for integrating multimodal medical data in cancer classification, based on state space models represented by the Mamba architecture. To this end, a mixed multimodal fusion architecture, called Mixed Fusion, was employed and developed to enhance the interpretability of the decision-making process. The proposed approach explores two variants of Mamba: one dedicated to visual processing, responsible for classifying the lesion image and generating probabilities associated with the target classes, and another focused on tabular processing, which uses these probabilities together with clinical and/or sociodemographic data to produce the final diagnosis. The experiments were conducted on two medical datasets: PAD-UFES-20, composed of clinical images and information associated with skin lesions, and NDB-UFES, consisting of histopathological images and sociodemographic data related to oral cancer. The results indicate slightly lower performance in balanced accuracy, compared with Transformer-based approaches, on PAD-UFES-20, and superior performance on NDB-UFES. Additionally, substantial gains were observed in the recall metric. Furthermore, the adoption of the Mixed Fusion architecture enables the application of the Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP) method, increasing the interpretability of the results. These findings indicate that Mamba-based models constitute a suitable alternative for multimodal classification in medical data, especially in scenarios in which sensitivity is a relevant requirement.

</details>


### [30] [MS-rPPG: Multi-spectral State Space Model for Remote Photoplethysmography in Driver Monitoring Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21115)
*Jiho Choi,Sang Jun Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MS-rPPG is a multi-spectral framework combining RGB and NIR face videos with a cross-spectral linear modulation (CSLM) strategy and MS-Mamba state space model to robustly estimate heart rate for driver health monitoring under challenging conditions, outperforming prior methods on the MR-NIRP Car and collected MS-Drive datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Driving environments are uncontrolled settings where videos suffer from varying illumination and frequent head movements, making accurate rPPG-based heart rate estimation difficult. Existing methods lack robustness under these challenging conditions.

Method: Proposes MS-rPPG framework combining RGB and NIR video; uses cross-spectral linear modulation (CSLM) based on frequency-domain analysis to merge complementary features; introduces MS-Mamba, a state space model for capturing long-range temporal dependencies and cross-channel interactions between multi-spectral features.

Result: Collected MS-Drive dataset from 50 participants during real driving; evaluated on MR-NIRP Car and MS-Drive datasets; MS-rPPG demonstrated better robustness and heart rate estimation accuracy than previous methods.

Conclusion: MS-rPPG shows promise for practical driver health monitoring by effectively handling illumination variation and head motion through multi-spectral fusion and advanced temporal modeling.

Abstract: Remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) is a camera-based technique for measuring physiological signals, particularly cardiac activity. From the remotely measured signals, heart rate can be estimated, which is crucial for health monitoring. In this study, we investigate a driver health monitoring system based on remote heart rate estimation. However, driving environments represent uncontrolled settings where videos are subject to varying illumination conditions and frequent head movements. We introduce MS-rPPG, a multi-spectral framework that combines RGB with near-infrared (NIR) face video to alleviate rPPG estimation under challenging driving conditions. To combine the complementary features from two spectral videos, we propose a cross-spectral linear modulation (CSLM) strategy based on frequency-domain analysis. Moreover, we introduce MS-Mamba, a novel state space model designed to effectively model long-range temporal dependencies while jointly capturing cross-channel interactions between multi-spectral features. We collected a real-world dataset called MS-Drive, which was recorded from 50 participants while driving the vehicle. The proposed method was evaluated on the MR-NIRP Car dataset and MS-Drive datasets. The experimental results indicate that MS-rPPG shows better robustness and heart rate estimation accuracy than previous methods, highlighting its promise for driver health monitoring. The codes are available at github.com/ziiho08/MS-rPPG.

</details>


### [31] [Mirage: a Clean-Label Backdoor against LiDAR 3D Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20752)
*Ziba Parsons,Ang Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Mirage is a black-box, clean-label backdoor attack against LiDAR 3D object detection that injects only 0.5% poisoning samples to achieve 73% misclassification success without compromising normal detection performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing backdoor attacks on LiDAR 3DOD require white-box access or label modification, and mainly focus on geometric attacks. The authors aim to develop a more stealthy and practical attack that works under black-box, clean-label settings.

Method: Mirage injects a small number of label-consistent poisoning samples into the training set, creating a malicious association between a trigger pattern and an attacker-chosen target class while preserving normal training semantics (clean-label and black-box).

Result: Achieves 73% misclassification success rate with only 0.5% poisoning rate, while maintaining detection performance close to benign models across multiple state-of-the-art LiDAR 3DOD models and benchmark datasets.

Conclusion: Mirage demonstrates that LiDAR 3DOD systems are vulnerable to stealthy backdoor attacks even under realistic black-box, clean-label constraints, highlighting serious security concerns for safety-critical autonomous systems.

Abstract: Deep neural network-based LiDAR 3D object detection serves as a critical perception component in safety-critical autonomous systems. However, recent studies have revealed its vulnerability to backdoor attacks. Existing attacks typically require white-box access or label modification and focus on geometric attacks such as object disappearance or bounding-box manipulation. In this paper, we present Mirage, a black-box and clean-label backdoor attack against deep neural network-based LiDAR 3DOD. Mirage injects a small number of label-consistent poisoning samples into the training set, causing the model to learn a malicious association between a trigger pattern and an attacker-chosen target class while preserving normal training semantics. As a result, the compromised model behaves normally on benign inputs yet systematically misclassifies triggered objects as the target class during deployment. We evaluate Mirage on multiple state-of-the-art LiDAR 3DOD models and benchmark datasets. Experimental results show that Mirage achieves a 73% misclassification success rate with a poisoning rate of only 0.5%, while maintaining detection performance close to that of benign models.

</details>


### [32] [MaRS: Robust Out-of-Distribution Detection via Mahalanobis Residual Scoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22649)
*Francesco Di Salvo,Sebastian Doerrich,Christian Ledig*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MaRS improves OOD detection in medical images by using Mahalanobis distance on reconstruction residuals instead of standard L2 norms, achieving state-of-the-art results across modalities and distribution shifts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Foundation models for medical imaging degrade under distribution shifts. Existing reconstruction-based OOD detection in latent space underperforms, and the authors show this stems from flawed scoring (L2 norms collapsing anisotropic residuals), not poor reconstruction quality.

Method: MaRS uses a lightweight autoencoder to learn an in-distribution manifold, then computes Mahalanobis distance on reconstruction residuals (variance-aware) rather than standard L2 residual norms to score OOD samples.

Result: Outperforms confidence-based, distance-based, and reconstruction-based baselines across three imaging modalities, multiple distribution shift types, and different model families/scales, while being fully post-hoc and lightweight.

Conclusion: The limitation of latent-space reconstruction OOD detection lies in how residuals are scored (L2 norms collapse informative structure), and MaRS fixes this with variance-aware Mahalanobis scoring.

Abstract: Foundation models provide highly descriptive representations for medical images, yet their reliability degrades under distribution shifts arising from changes in patients, devices, or acquisition conditions. Reliable out-of-distribution (OOD) detection is therefore essential for safe deployment. Recent post-hoc detectors efficiently exploit frozen embeddings (\emph{e.g.}, kNN), whereas reconstruction-based OOD detection in latent feature space has seen limited adoption due to inconsistent performance. In this work, we show that the limitation of reconstruction-based methods in latent space does not stem from poor reconstruction quality, but from how reconstruction errors are scored. Standard $L_2$ residual norms collapse the anisotropic residual structure, thereby suppressing informative deviations. To address this limitation, we introduce \texttt{MaRS} (Mahalanobis Residual Scoring), a label-free OOD detector that learns an in-distribution manifold using a lightweight autoencoder and measures deviation via a Mahalanobis distance on reconstruction residuals, yielding variance-aware OOD scores. Across three imaging modalities, multiple types of distribution shift, and different model families and scales, \texttt{MaRS} outperforms established confidence-, distance-, and reconstruction-based baselines, while remaining fully post-hoc and lightweight. The code is available at https://github.com/francescodisalvo05/mars.

</details>


### [33] [One Image is All You Need: Agentic One-Shot Image Generation via Text-Based World Models for Long-Tail Spatial Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20764)
*Keqin Zeng,Shuting Su,Shihao Lin,Ziyue Li,Rui Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: WMGen-v1 is an agentic text-based world model framework that uses LVLM and LLM to generate physically grounded, spatially consistent long-tail synthetic data for improving visual perception in autonomous driving and maritime surveillance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world spatiotemporal data exhibits severe heterogeneity with extreme long-tail distributions for safety-critical scenarios, causing dataset shift that degrades detection performance and poses safety risks. Existing generative approaches lack explicit spatial grounding and structural constraints.

Method: WMGen-v1 uses an LVLM to construct structured scene representations from a single reference image, then an LLM performs guidance-based scene expansion under physical plausibility and commonsense constraints. A diffusion model then generates diverse, physically grounded long-tail training data conditioned on these structured semantic representations.

Result: Experiments on internal industrial datasets, ROADWork, and LaRS benchmarks show WMGen-v1 outperforms baselines. Detectors trained solely on WMGen-v1 synthetic data approach real-only performance on aggregate dataset-level metrics.

Conclusion: WMGen-v1 effectively alleviates long-tail data scarcity for downstream spatial perception by generating physically consistent synthetic training data.

Abstract: Reliable spatial decision automation, such as autonomous driving and maritime surveillance, critically depends on robust visual perception. However, real-world spatiotemporal data exhibits severe heterogeneity, often manifesting as extreme long-tail distributions for safety-critical scenarios. This data scarcity induces dataset shift that degrades detection performance and pose safety risks. While synthetic data generation offers a potential solution, existing generative approaches, such as diffusion models and Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), often lack explicit spatial grounding and structural constraints, resulting in spatial and physical inconsistencies in generated scenes. To address these challenges, we introduce WMGen-v1, an agentic text-based world model framework for long-tail spatial data generation. WMGen-v1 employs a Large Vision-Language Model (LVLM) to construct a structured scene representation from a single reference image, while a Large Language Model (LLM) performs guidance-based scene expansion under physical plausibility and commonsense constraints. Subsequently, conditioned on the structured semantic representations produced by this reasoning process, a diffusion model generates diverse and physically grounded long-tail training data. Experiments on internal industrial datasets, ROADWork, and LaRS benchmarks demonstrate that WMGen-v1 outperforms baseline approaches. Notably, detectors trained solely on WMGen-v1 synthetic data approach real-only performance on aggregate dataset-level metrics, highlighting its potential to alleviate long-tail data scarcity for downstream spatial perception.

</details>


### [34] [TriMotion: Modality-Agnostic Camera Control for Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20774)
*Seunghyun Shin,Jifei Song,Wooseok Jeon,Hae-Gon Jeon,Jiankang Deng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TriMotion is a modality-agnostic framework for camera-controlled video generation that maps video, pose, and text inputs into a shared motion embedding space, enabling flexible camera motion control across heterogeneous user inputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing camera motion control methods are limited to single specific modalities (e.g., pose trajectories or reference videos), restricting their ability to support diverse user inputs. The authors aim to create a unified framework that handles video, pose, and text inputs describing the same camera trajectory.

Method: TriMotion introduces: (1) a shared motion embedding space that maps video, pose, and text inputs describing the same camera trajectory; (2) the Motion Triplet Dataset built by extending a Multi-Cam Video Dataset with geometry-grounded motion descriptions from camera extrinsics; (3) a latent motion consistency objective that encourages generated videos to follow target camera trajectories directly in latent space.

Result: TriMotion generates high-quality videos that accurately follow target camera trajectories across all three modalities (video, pose, text). It also enables flexible applications like sequential motion composition and cross-modal motion interpolation.

Conclusion: The shared motion embedding space effectively handles heterogeneous inputs for camera-controlled video generation, overcoming the limitation of single-modality conditioning in existing methods.

Abstract: Camera motion control is essential for directing viewpoint changes in generative systems. However, existing methods typically condition the generation process on a single specific modality, such as explicit pose trajectories or reference videos, limiting their ability to support heterogeneous user inputs. To address this limitation, we present TriMotion, a modality-agnostic framework for camera-controlled video generation that maps video, pose, and text inputs, describing the same camera trajectory into a shared motion embedding space. Learning such a space requires synchronized supervision across modalities. Therefore, we build the Motion Triplet Dataset by extending a Multi-Cam Video Dataset with geometry-grounded motion descriptions derived from camera extrinsics. We further introduce a latent motion consistency objective that leverages the motion embedding space to encourage the generated video to follow the target camera trajectory directly in latent space, avoiding the cost of pixel-space decoding. Extensive experiments show that TriMotion generates high-quality videos that accurately follow the target camera trajectories across all three modalities. Beyond standard generation, the shared motion embedding space also enables flexible applications such as sequential motion composition and cross-modal motion interpolation.

</details>


### [35] [GroundShot: Visually Consistent Multi-Shot Long Video Generation via Entity-Grounded Shot Scheduling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20799)
*Yixuan Lai,Tianjia Shao,Kun Zhou,Weijia Dou,Siyu Zhu,Jingdong Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GroundShot is a training-free framework that improves multi-shot video consistency by building entity-level visual memory, scheduling shot order, and retrieving suitable entity references during generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-shot videos suffer from visual inconsistencies across shots, where entities (characters, objects, locations) drift away from their first appearance as the number of shots increases.

Method: GroundShot uses an agentic framework with entity-level visual memory: (1) schedules shots by entity usefulness, (2) grounds entities from generated videos, (3) verifies reliability before memory addition, and (4) retrieves suitable references before each shot. Also introduces GroundBench for entity-level consistency evaluation.

Result: GroundShot improves multi-shot consistency over existing methods without additional training or model modification.

Conclusion: Entity-grounded visual memory and reference retrieval effectively address multi-shot consistency drift in a training-free manner.

Abstract: Generating visually consistent multi-shot videos remains an open challenge. As videos span more shots, inconsistencies can accumulate across shots, causing entities that reappear across shots -- characters, objects, and locations -- to drift away from how they first appear. We observe that viewers judge consistency by comparing each later appearance of an entity with its first clear appearance; the visual quality of this initial appearance sets the consistency ceiling for all that follows. Motivated by this, we present \textbf{GroundShot}, a training-free, model-agnostic agentic framework for entity-grounded multi-shot generation. GroundShot builds an entity-level visual memory online from accepted generated shots: it schedules shots' generation order by their expected usefulness as entity references, grounds entities from generated videos, verifies their reliability before adding them to memory, and retrieves suitable entity references from memory before each shot is generated. To evaluate this entity-centered view of consistency, we further introduce \textbf{GroundBench}, a diagnostic benchmark that measures consistency at the entity level while isolating controlled challenge dimensions. Experiments show that GroundShot improves multi-shot consistency over existing methods while requiring no additional training or model modification.

</details>


### [36] [NeoLoc-68: End-to-end 68-point neonatal facial landmark localisation in neonatal clinical environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20823)
*Abdullah Bin-Obaid,Maria M. Cobo,Rebeccah Slater,Lionel Tarassenko,Mauricio Villarroel*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes the first end-to-end 68-point facial landmark detector specifically for neonatal faces in clinical environments, using a YOLO-based model trained on combined public datasets and manually annotated neonatal data, achieving state-of-the-art performance on both public benchmarks and clinical neonatal test sets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing facial landmark detectors trained on adult faces perform poorly on neonates in clinical settings due to occlusions from medical equipment, varied head poses, and motion blur from pain-related movements. Automated pain assessment requires reliable neonatal facial landmark detection.

Method: A YOLO-based keypoint model was adapted to regress 68 facial landmarks, initialised with weights from a pretrained neonatal face detector. Training data combined 37,459 images from 11 public datasets (standardised to 68-point markup) with 1,123 manually annotated neonatal clinical frames (76,000+ landmarks).

Result: On public datasets: NME=5.37, FR=12.5%, AUC0.08=38.00%, AUC0.1=48.70%. On clinical neonatal set (before fine-tuning): DFR=5.3% (lowest among baselines). After fine-tuning: NME=6.36, FR=22.30%, DFR=1.77%, AUC0.08=29.24%, AUC0.1=40.25%.

Conclusion: This is the first end-to-end 68-point neonatal facial landmark detection model, showing strong generalisation to clinical environments. With further dataset expansion, it could support downstream neonatal health monitoring and pain-related facial analysis tasks.

Abstract: Facial landmark localisation is a prerequisite for developing automated, non-contact neonatal pain assessment methods. Clinicians use pain scales to judge the severity of pain, many of which rely on facial expression. However, facial landmark detectors trained on adult faces perform poorly in neonatal clinical environments due to frequent occlusions caused by medical equipment, varied head poses, and challenging imaging conditions, including motion blur triggered by sudden pain-related movements. We propose an end-to-end facial landmark detector capable of predicting 68 landmarks on neonatal faces in clinical environments. We combined 37,459 single-face images from 11 public datasets, standardised to 68-point markup, with 1,123 manually annotated frames from a neonatal research dataset (totalling over 76,000 landmarks). A YOLO-based keypoint model was adapted to regress the facial landmarks, initialised with weights from a pretrained neonatal face detector. On public datasets, our proposed model achieved state-of-the-art performance: Normalised Mean Error (NME) = 5.37, Failure Rate (FR) = 12.5%, Area Under the Cumulative Error Curve (AUC) at AUC0.08 = 38.00% and AUC0.1 = 48.70%. On the clinical neonatal test set, before fine-tuning, the model achieved the lowest Detection Failure Rate (DFR) = 5.3% among all baselines and showed strong generalisation. After fine-tuning, performance improved further to NME = 6.36, FR = 22.30%, DFR = 1.77%, AUC0.08 = 29.24% and AUC0.1 = 40.25%. To the best of our knowledge, this represents the first end-to-end 68-point neonatal facial landmark detection model. With further dataset expansion and refinement, it could support downstream tasks in neonatal health monitoring and pain-related facial analysis.

</details>


### [37] [From Uncertainty to Stability and Fidelity: Guiding Sparse-View 3D Gaussian Splatting with Fisher Information](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20842)
*Junbao Zhou,Qingshan Xu,Yuan Zhou,Xiaolong Shen,Beier Zhu,Kesen Zhao,Yiming Zeng,Chen Bai,Cheng Lu,Hanwang Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A new sparse-view 3DGS method uses Fisher Information to guide stereo augmentation (selecting informative views) and uncertainty-aware regularization (adaptively adjusting Gaussian removal probability), achieving SOTA performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse-view 3DGS suffers from overfitting and instability. Existing prior-based or regularization-based methods lack principled guidance and introduce randomness (random novel views, random Gaussian removal), limiting fidelity.

Method: (1) Stereo augmentation with Fisher Information: actively selects the most informative supporting views and uses depth priors to generate reliable pseudo ground truths. (2) Uncertainty-aware regularization: uses Fisher Information to measure each Gaussian's uncertainty and adaptively adjusts Dropout removal probability.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on sparse-view novel view synthesis benchmarks, with improved rendering fidelity and reduced artifacts.

Conclusion: Fisher Information provides principled guidance for both augmentation and regularization in sparse-view 3DGS, effectively mitigating overfitting and improving stability.

Abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has emerged as a promising technique for novel view synthesis. However, 3DGS requires dense input views to achieve high-quality rendering. In sparse-view scenarios, 3DGS often prones to overfitting, resulting in noticeable artifacts and degraded rendering quality. Previous methods explore to address this issue by introducing additional priors (e.g. depth priors) or integrating regularization techniques (e.g. Dropout). However, these methods are often applied without principled guidance. In particular, prior-based augmentation typically samples novel viewpoints randomly, while Dropout-based regularization randomly removes Gaussians. The compounded randomness introduces uncertainty and instability, limiting the fidelity of novel view synthesis. In this paper, we propose a novel method for sparse-view 3DGS that incorporates Fisher Information to quantitatively guide the utilization of geometric priors and regularization. Specifically, our method comprises two key components: (1) Stereo augmentation with Fisher Information. By leveraging Fisher Information, we actively select most informative supporting views and use depth priors to curate reliable pseudo ground truths, which reduces randomness in augmentation and improves stability and rendering fidelity; (2) Uncertainty-aware regularization. We reduce the instability of Dropout-based regularization by using Fisher Information to quantitatively measure the uncertainty of each 3D Gaussian, and adaptively adjust the removal probability, leading to more stable and effective regularization. With these two components, our method effectively mitigates overfitting and improves the stability of optimization in sparse-view 3DGS, resulting in superior rendering fidelity. Extensive experiments show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in sparse-view novel view synthesis benchmarks.

</details>


### [38] [Translating Inference-Time Control to Radiology Vision-Language Models: Activation Steering for Pneumonia Classification on Chest X-rays](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20852)
*Eduardo Moreno Judice de Mattos Farina,Mateus A. Esmeraldo,Felipe Akio Matsuoka,Paulo Eduardo de Aguiar Kuriki,Felipe Campos Kitamura*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This study evaluates Contrastive Activation Addition (CAA) for improving pneumonia classification in three chest radiograph VLMs without fine-tuning. Results show CAA can alter predictions and calibrate thresholds, with meaningful F1 improvements in one model (NV-Reason-CXR-3B: 0.7692→0.8727), suggesting activation steering as a lightweight adaptation method, though benefits were inconsistent across models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether inference-time engineering (specifically Contrastive Activation Addition) can improve diagnostic performance in medical vision-language models without the computational cost of fine-tuning.

Method: Three frozen VLMs were evaluated on the Kermany pneumonia test set using CAA steering vectors (answer-bias control, pneumonia text contrast, and image-conditioned contrast). A 200-image development set was used for layer/scale selection and threshold calibration. Performance was assessed via ROC-AUC, PR-AUC, F1, and multiple control analyses with bootstrap confidence intervals.

Result: Fixed-threshold F1 improvements were common but not consistently indicative of better diagnostics. NV-Reason-CXR-3B showed the strongest gains (F1: 0.7692→0.8727). CheXOne-3B showed modest improvement (0.8528→0.8666, CI crossed zero). MedGemma-4B-IT showed no consistent benefit.

Conclusion: CAA substantially alters prediction distributions and operating characteristics without fine-tuning, but meaningful gains were observed in only one of three VLMs. Activation steering may serve as a lightweight approach for adapting medical VLM behavior, though results are model-dependent.

Abstract: Inference-time engineering can alter model behavior without fine-tuning. However, its utility for improving diagnostic performance in medical vision-language models (VLMs) remains unclear. We aim to evaluate whether Contrastive Activation Addition (CAA) can improve pneumonia classification in chest radiograph VLMs without updating model weights. Three frozen chest radiograph VLMs (MedGemma-4B-IT, NV-Reason-CXR-3B, and CheXOne-3B) were evaluated on the public Kermany pneumonia test set. Classification was based on the logits of the tokens Yes and No under a binary prompt. Steering vectors included a 30-pair answer-bias control, a 30-pair pneumonia text contrast, and an image-conditioned contrast derived from 30 pneumonia and 30 normal development images. A deterministic 200-image development set was used for layer and scale selection (100 images) and threshold calibration (100 images). Performance was assessed using ROC-AUC, PR-AUC, F1 score, threshold analyses, reverse-vector controls, random-vector controls, and conditional bootstrap confidence intervals. Fixed-threshold F1 improvements were frequently observed but did not consistently indicate improved diagnostic performance. For MedGemma-4B-IT. NV-Reason-CXR-3B showed the strongest benefit: calibrated F1 improved from 0.7692 in the zero-shot setting to 0.8619 with pneumonia-text steering and to 0.8727 with image-conditioned steering. For CheXOne-3B, pneumonia-text steering increased calibrated F1 from 0.8528 to 0.8666, although the confidence interval crossed zero. On this public pneumonia benchmark, CAA substantially altered prediction score distributions and operating characteristics without fine-tuning. Meaningful performance gains were observed in one of three evaluated VLMs, suggesting that activation steering may serve as a lightweight approach for adapting medical VLM behavior.

</details>


### [39] [Stochastic Signed Distance Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20856)
*Hiroki Sakuma,Masatoshi Okutomi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes Stochastic Signed Distance Processes (SSDP) for multi-view surface reconstruction, reformulating SDF-based volume rendering as probabilistic surface rendering via first-passage-time distributions, and shows it generalizes NeuS while outperforming baselines on DTU and MobileBrick datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reformulate SDF-based volume rendering as probabilistic surface rendering by modeling the SDF along each ray as a stochastic process, reducing reliance on silhouette supervision and enabling uncertainty quantification.

Method: Introduces Stochastic Signed Distance Processes (SSDP) that model the SDF along each ray as a stochastic process, deriving first-passage-time probabilities via Bayesian filtering with practical approximations for parallel rendering.

Result: Outperforms baselines in surface reconstruction and uncertainty quantification on DTU and MobileBrick datasets, with NeuS shown as a special case.

Conclusion: The first-passage formulation is effective for surface reconstruction and uncertainty quantification, with code publicly available.

Abstract: Multi-view surface reconstruction is a core problem in computer vision. One prominent line of work represents the surface implicitly as a signed distance field (SDF), optimizing it based on the photometric loss between rendered and observed pixel colors. These approaches typically employ SDF-based volume rendering to obtain a differentiable relaxation of discontinuous visibility along rays, thereby reducing reliance on silhouette supervision. In this paper, we reformulate SDF-based volume rendering as probabilistic surface rendering, where each pixel color is modeled as a mixture distribution induced by the random first ray-surface intersection. To this end, we introduce Stochastic Signed Distance Processes (SSDP), which model the SDF along each ray as a stochastic process, inducing a first-passage-time distribution for each ray. We then derive the first-passage probability for each sampling interval based on Bayesian filtering, together with its practical approximation for parallel rendering. We further show that NeuS, an existing SDF-based volume rendering method, arises as a special case of our formulation. Experiments on the DTU and MobileBrick datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms baselines in both surface reconstruction and uncertainty quantification, supporting the effectiveness of our first-passage formulation. Our code is available at https://github.com/skmhrk1209/SSDP.

</details>


### [40] [FOCA: Future-Oriented Conditioning for Data-Efficient Vision-Language-Action Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20867)
*Duc Minh Nguyen,Nghiem Tuong Diep,Binh Gia Nguyen,Trong-Bao Ho,Doanh Le,Tan Q. Nguyen,Thien-Loc Ha,Nhiem Tran,Bao Thach,Nhat X. Tran,Tuan A. Tran,Artur Habuda,Philip Lund Møller,Tran Nguyen Le,Daniel Sonntag,Matthias Niepert,Khoa D. Doan,Vu Duong,Hung Ngo,Minh N. Vu,Duy M. H. Nguyen,An Thai Le,Ngo Anh Vien*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FOCA is a future-oriented conditioning framework that improves few-shot VLA adaptation by combining future interaction embeddings with goal alignment, achieving SOTA results with as few as 20 demonstrations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA models suffer severe performance degradation under few-shot imitation learning (limited demonstrations), revealing a key weakness in current adaptation strategies.

Method: FOCA combines explicit prediction of task-grounded future interaction embeddings with implicit alignment to future goal observations, enabling long-horizon reasoning in latent space without pixel-level prediction. It supports action-free co-training with synthetic videos from world models.

Result: 95.7% success with 20 demos on LIBERO, 7-12% improvement on RoboCasa, up to 26% absolute gains on real robots.

Conclusion: FOCA establishes a new state of the art in few-shot VLA adaptation, demonstrating that future-conditioned representations are highly effective for data-efficient robotic learning.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models enable general-purpose robotic control via large-scale multimodal pretraining, yet their effectiveness under few-shot imitation learning remains limited. We conduct a systematic stress test of state-of-the-art VLA models and show that performance degrades sharply as demonstrations are reduced, revealing a key weakness of existing adaptation strategies. To address this, we introduce FOCA, a future-oriented conditioning framework for data-efficient VLA adaptation. FOCA combines explicit prediction of task-grounded future interaction embeddings with implicit alignment to future goal observations, enabling long-horizon reasoning in latent space without pixel-level prediction. This formulation naturally supports action-free co-training with synthetic videos from video world models and can be interpreted as learning a future-conditioned value-like representation. Extensive experiments demonstrate FOCA achieves 95.7% success with 20 demonstrations on LIBERO, improves 7-12% on RoboCasa, and delivers up to 26% absolute gains on real robots, establishing a new state of the art in few-shot VLA adaptation.

</details>


### [41] [Resolving Multi-Target Association in OFDM-based ISAC via Vision-aided Multi-Modal Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22195)
*Meng Hua,Chenghong Bian,Deniz Gunduz*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a vision-assisted OFDM-ISAC framework that fuses wireless sensing data with deep joint source-channel coded visual information to resolve multi-target ambiguity and improve localization accuracy, achieving 16 cm RMSE and 10.8 ns delay RMSE on a Blender-rendered vehicular testbed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OFDM-ISAC systems suffer from ambiguity in multi-target scenarios: the delay-Doppler map cannot associate peaks with specific physical targets, and closely spaced targets within the same resolution cell are inseparable. The authors aim to overcome these fundamental limitations by incorporating visual modality.

Method: The transmitter encodes street-view images using DeepJSCC and transmits them over the same OFDM waveform used for sensing. The receiver reconstructs the image, runs a fine-tuned YOLOv5 detector, and fuses per-target features (bounding boxes, class labels) with the DDM and geometry via a learned multi-modal network. A KL divergence loss with triangular soft labels is used to stabilize training of delay/Doppler classifiers.

Result: The framework achieves 16 cm localization RMSE and 10.8 ns delay RMSE on a Blender-rendered vehicular testbed. Ablation study shows removing visual modality causes a 60x degradation in localization performance.

Conclusion: Vision assistance can effectively overcome the data-association and resolution limits of single-modality ISAC systems, highlighting the potential of multi-modal fusion for ISAC.

Abstract: Orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM)-based integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems commonly extract target parameters by peak-searching a delay-Doppler map (DDM) constructed from reflected pilots. In multi-target scenarios, this results in ambiguity: the DDM does not reveal which physical target produced which peak, and two targets within the same delay-Doppler resolution cell cannot be separated. We propose a vision-assisted OFDM-ISAC framework that resolves both limitations by fusing wireless and visual modalities. The transmitter encodes an onboard street-view image with deep joint source-channel coding (DeepJSCC) and transmits it over the same OFDM waveform used for sensing; the receiver reconstructs the image, runs a fine-tuned YOLOv5 detector and fuses the resulting per-target features (bounding-box coordinates and class labels) with the DDM and transmitter-receiver geometry through a learned multi-modal network. To stabilize training of the high dimensional delay and Doppler classifiers, we introduce a Kullback Leibler loss against triangular soft labels centered on the ground-truth bin. On a Blender-rendered vehicular testbed, the proposed framework achieves a 16 cm localization root mean square error (RMSE) and a 10.8 ns delay RMSE. An ablation study confirms that removing the visual modality causes a 60x degradation in localization. These results highlight the potential of vision to overcome the data-association and resolution limits of single-modality ISAC.

</details>


### [42] [Toward Parking Spot Occupancy Recognition: A Self-Supervised Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20886)
*Luan Marko Kujavski,Rayson Laroca,Paulo Lisboa de Almeida*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A self-supervised parking spot occupancy recognition method using SimCLR and ResNet-50 achieves 97.2-97.8% accuracy without target-parking-lot labels, validated on three public datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Urban expansion requires automatic parking monitoring, but obtaining labeled data for every parking lot is impractical. The authors aim to develop a label-efficient, scalable solution that works without target-specific labeled samples.

Method: Proposes a two-stage self-supervised training strategy: (1) SimCLR pre-training on unlabeled generic data, (2) self-supervised fine-tuning on unlabeled target-specific data, then supervised fine-tuning with only generic parking lot labels. Also introduces a deployment strategy: Strong General Model first, then Specialized Model with unlabeled images from first N days.

Result: Strong General Model achieves 97.2% average accuracy; two-stage strategy improves to 97.8%. Outperforms both supervised and self-supervised baselines under leave-one-out cross-environment evaluation on PKLot, CNRPark-EXT, and PLds.

Conclusion: Self-supervised learning enables scalable, label-efficient real-world parking occupancy monitoring without requiring labeled data from the target parking lot.

Abstract: As urban areas expand, automatic monitoring of parking lots becomes essential for efficient and sustainable cities. This work proposes a self-supervised approach for parking spot occupancy recognition that requires no labeled samples from the target parking lot. Building upon a self-supervised transfer learning fine-tuning protocol, the proposed training strategy consists of two self-supervised stages: first on unlabeled generic data and then on unlabeled target-specific data, followed by supervised fine-tuning using only generic parking lot labels. We adopt SimCLR with a ResNet-50 encoder and evaluate the method under a leave-one-out cross-environment protocol on three public datasets: PKLot, CNRPark-EXT, and PLds. We also introduce a two-stage deployment strategy in which a Strong General Model is initially deployed, followed by a Specialized Model that incorporates unlabeled images collected during the first N days of deployment in a self-supervised manner. Experimental results show that the Strong General Model alone outperforms supervised and self-supervised baselines, achieving an average accuracy of 97.2%, which further improves to 97.8% with the proposed two-stage strategy. These results demonstrate that self-supervised learning enables a scalable and labelefficient solution for real-world parking occupancy monitoring. Our trained models and source code are publicly available at https://github.com/LoanMaikon/Parking-Spot-Occupancy-Recognition.

</details>


### [43] [Fine-grained Human Motion Understanding with Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20888)
*Thomas Markhorst,Zhi-Yi Lin,Jouh Yeong Chew,Jan van Gemert,Xucong Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes an LLM-based model for fine-grained human motion understanding that uses explicit timestamped skeletal poses, diverse pose- and motion-level supervision, and a unified 2D/3D pose encoder, achieving SOTA on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable LLMs to reason about fine-grained motion aspects (order, duration, rhythm) and investigate what supervision signals are needed for motion-language reasoning, moving beyond coarse action recognition.

Method: Represents motion as a sequence of skeletal poses with explicit timestamp tokens; constructs diverse training mixture (pose captioning, pose QA, motion captioning, motion QA); uses a unified pose encoder supporting both 2D and 3D; optionally incorporates video for context.

Result: Achieves SOTA on BABEL-QA, HuMMan-QA, CompMo, NTU-RGB+D, QEVD-Coach; even 2D skeletal input outperforms previous 3D-based methods.

Conclusion: Explicit temporal encoding and diverse pose- and motion-level supervision are highly effective for fine-grained motion understanding.

Abstract: In this work, we propose \methodname, an LLM-based model for fine-grained human motion understanding that represents motion as a sequence of skeletal poses with explicit timestamps for each pose. Each pose encodes body joint positions and is temporally grounded with timestamp tokens, allowing the model to reason about motion order, duration, and rhythm. To study what supervision is needed for motion-language reasoning, we construct a diverse training mixture spanning pose captioning, pose question answering, motion captioning, and motion question answering. Our ablations show that the primary gains come from the diversity of pose- and motion-level supervision, while staged training provides a smaller additional benefit. Different from previous works that rely on ground-truth 3D motion capture, our approach supports both 2D and 3D skeletal motion representations through a unified pose encoder, and can optionally incorporate video to provide contextual information. Extensive experiments on BABEL-QA, HuMMan-QA, CompMo, NTU-RGB+D, and QEVD-Coach demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks, highlighting the effectiveness of explicit temporal encoding and diverse pose- and motion-level supervision for fine-grained human motion understanding. Notably, even when using only 2D skeletal input, our approach surpasses previous 3D-based methods.

</details>


### [44] [Go-with-the-Track: Video Compositing and Motion Control with Point Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20891)
*Koichi Namekata,Yash Kant,Zhizheng Liu,Ryan D Burgert,Yuancheng Xu,Kuan Heng Lin,Emmett Steven,Julien Philip,Li Ma,Andrea Vedaldi,Paul Debevec,Ning Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Go-with-the-Track unifies motion control and reference image compositing in video generation by jointly conditioning on multiple reference images and reference-anchored point-tracks, using spatially-aware point-track embeddings injected into a video diffusion transformer via a lightweight adapter, achieving superior motion and reference control in a single model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods treat motion control and reference image compositing separately: point-track-conditioned models restrict content insertion to the first frame, while reference-to-video models lack fine-grained spatial-temporal control over how reference content integrates across frames. The paper aims to unify both capabilities in a single model.

Method: (1) Proposes reference-anchored point-tracks that extend conventional point-tracks to establish correspondences between generated frames and reference images. (2) Introduces spatially-aware point-track embeddings using a coordinate-wise MLP with temporal pooling to capture spatial characteristics. (3) Injects embeddings into video diffusion transformer via a lightweight adapter to resolve pixel-to-patch resolution mismatch. (4) Uses hybrid training on dynamic, static, and synthetic scene video datasets.

Result: Achieves superior motion and reference control in a single model. Enables new capabilities: multi-reference conditioned video generation with point-track driven compositing, and camera control for both static and dynamic scenes.

Conclusion: Go-with-the-Track successfully unifies motion control and reference image compositing, overcoming limitations of previous separate approaches, and demonstrates that joint conditioning on multiple reference images and reference-anchored point-tracks enables more precise and flexible video generation control.

Abstract: Filmmaking demands precise motion control and reference image compositing -- capabilities that existing methods treat separately. Point-track-conditioned image-to-video models restrict content insertion to the first frame, while reference-to-video models lack fine-grained spatial-temporal control over how reference content integrates across frames.
  We present Go-with-the-Track, which unifies both capabilities by jointly conditioning on multiple reference images and reference-anchored point-tracks -- extending conventional point-tracks to explicitly establish correspondences between generated frames and reference images, thus enabling precise compositing and motion control throughout the video.
  To achieve this, we introduce spatially-aware point-track embeddings that encode the full sequence of point-track coordinates using a coordinate-wise MLP followed by temporal pooling. This representation captures the spatial characteristics of each point-track (serving as a unique identifier), while the embedding similarity correlates directly with spatial proximity, enhancing the model's ability to distinguish and associate point-tracks. We inject these point-track embeddings into a video diffusion transformer via a lightweight adapter, resolving the pixel-to-patch resolution mismatch while avoiding the substantial motion detail loss inherent in naive point-track subsampling.
  We use a hybrid training strategy to train jointly on dynamic, static, and synthetic scene video datasets to boost motion controllability. Experiments demonstrate that Go-with-the-Track achieves superior motion and reference control in a single model and enables new capabilities: multi-reference conditioned video generation with point-track driven compositing, as well as camera control for both static and dynamic scenes. Project Page: https://eyeline-labs.github.io/Go-with-the-Track/

</details>


### [45] [PROTON: Prototype-Based Test-Time Online OOD Detection for Medical VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20913)
*Abhijit Das,Nichula Wasalathilaka,Yifan Lu,Adinath Dukre,Dwarikanath Mahapatra,Shadab Khan,Imran Razzak*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PROTON is a lightweight post-hoc OOD detection module for medical VLMs that builds an online prototype bank from test-time predictions and adaptively fuses prototype distance with MCM scoring, achieving significant AUROC gains across covariate, semantic, and far-OOD shifts without model modification or training data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: No static scoring method works across all shift types for zero-shot clinical image classification — e.g., MCM on FLAIR achieves 76.4% AUROC for far-OOD but only 42.4% (random) for covariate shifts like ultra-wide-field fundus images.

Method: PROTON maintains an online prototype bank from high-confidence test predictions, computes prototype distances, and adaptively fuses this with MCM scoring via stream-level variance statistics.

Result: On FLAIR + FIVES, PROTON improves MCM by +23.9 AUROC on covariate shift, +8.8 on semantic shift, and +8.1 on far-OOD.

Conclusion: PROTON is the only zero-shot method to improve all three OOD types without hierarchical prompts or labeled data, with open-source code available.

Abstract: Medical vision-language models (VLMs) enable zero-shot clinical image classification, yet reliably detecting out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs at deployment remains an open problem. No static scoring method works across all shift types: Maximum Concept Matching (MCM) on FLAIR achieves 76.4% AUROC for far-OOD but only 42.4% for covariate shifts such as ultra-wide-field fundus images, effectively random. We trace this to a structural mismatch: covariate-shifted inputs are indistinguishable from in-distribution samples in softmax space, yet occupy distinct regions in the VLM embedding space. To exploit this untapped signal, we propose PROTON (PROtotype-based Test-time ONline OOD detection), a lightweight post-hoc module that maintains an online prototype bank from high-confidence test predictions and adaptively fuses prototype distance with MCM scoring via stream-level variance statistics, requiring no model modification, training data, or prompt engineering. On the ophthalmology benchmark FLAIR + FIVES, PROTON improves MCM by +23.9 AUROC on covariate shift, +8.8 on semantic shift, and +8.1 on far-OOD, making it the only zero-shot method to improve all three without hierarchical prompts or labeled data. Code is available at https://github.com/GenMI-Lab/PROTON, and the project page is available at https://genmi-lab.github.io/PROTON.

</details>


### [46] [GIM-ENDO: A Multimodal Endoscopic Image and Video Dataset for Gastric Intestinal Metaplasia Morphology and Pathology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20919)
*Mojgan Forootan,Mahziar Setayeshfar,Ali Darvishi,Mohammad Tashakoripour,Hamidreza Bolhasani*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GIM-ENDO is a publicly available, histopathologically validated endoscopic dataset for gastric intestinal metaplasia (GIM), featuring detailed annotations, endoscopic signs, histological subtypes, and staging systems from 24 patients using Olympus EVIS X1 with WLE, NBI, and M-NBI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is no publicly available, histopathologically validated dataset combining detailed endoscopic annotations, histological subtypes, standardized grading, and normal mucosal patterns for AI development in GIM detection and characterization.

Method: Dataset collected from 24 patients (22 GIM-positive, 2 controls) using Olympus EVIS X1 with WLE, NBI, and M-NBI; annotations cover six IEE signs, two additional findings, GIM subtypes, and OLGA/OLGIM staging.

Result: A publicly accessible dataset (Zenodo) with histopathologically validated endoscopic images/videos, detailed annotations of endoscopic signs, GIM subtypes, and staging information.

Conclusion: GIM-ENDO fills a critical gap in AI research for GIM by providing a standardized, publicly available resource for developing and benchmarking AI models for real-time endoscopic detection and characterization.

Abstract: Gastric intestinal metaplasia (GIM) is a precursor lesion to gastric dysplasia and adenocarcinoma whose early detection is crucial for intervening in the carcinogenesis cascade. Artificial intelligence (AI) holds considerable promise for real-time endoscopic detection and characterization of GIM. However, development of reliable AI models has been constrained by the absence of publicly available, histopathologically validated datasets that combine detailed endoscopic annotations, histological subtype (complete and incomplete), standardized grading systems, and normal mucosal patterns. GIM-ENDO was designed to fill this gap. The dataset comprises demographic data, endoscopic findings, histopathological results, and H. pylori status acquired using the Olympus EVIS X1 system with white-light endoscopy (WLE) and image-enhanced endoscopy (IEE), including narrow-band imaging (NBI) and magnifying NBI (M-NBI), along with images and video clips from 24 patients (22 GIM-positive, 2 normal controls). Annotations cover six primary IEE endoscopic signs -- light blue crest (LBC), marginal turbid band (MTB), white opaque substance (WOS), TV pattern (Fusion), atrophy, and map-like erythema (MLE) -- plus two additional endoscopic findings (AHP and GA) recorded where present. GIM subtypes (complete and incomplete) are annotated for all GIM-positive cases; OLGA and OLGIM staging are provided where complete histological sampling was available. The dataset is publicly accessible at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20707267. For the latest updates and further information regarding this dataset, readers are referred to the DataBioX website: https://databiox.com
  A short version of this work has been submitted to MICCAI 2026 Open Data Track.

</details>


### [47] [ELDiff: When Evidential Learning Meets Text-to-Image Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20924)
*Qingtao Pan,Kai Ye,Zhihao Dou,Bing Ji,Shuo Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ELDiff is an evidential learning-supervised T2I diffusion model that enhances object-wise consistency by using pixel evidence loss to handle unreliable segmentation maps and token conflict loss to suppress semantic conflicts, outperforming existing models across multiple backbones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing token supervised diffusion models suffer from segmentation map bias and semantic overlap conflict when handling multiple objects, as consistency constraints are underemphasized in the denoising process.

Method: ELDiff proposes (1) a pixel evidence loss with evidential regularization to restrain overconfidence in unreliable segmentation labels, and (2) a token conflict loss that optimizes a measured conflict factor to weaken semantic contradictions.

Result: Outperforms existing training-based and training-free T2I diffusion models on SD v1.4, SD v2.1, SDXL, SD v3.5, and Qwen-Image without additional inference-time manipulations.

Conclusion: ELDiff effectively strengthens object-wise consistency learning via uncertainty metrics and conflict detection, and can be seamlessly integrated into existing T2I training pipelines.

Abstract: In multi-object text-to-image (T2I) diffusion, ensuring semantic consistency between textual prompts and generated visual content is crucial for image synthesis. However, such consistency constraint is often underemphasized in the denoising process of diffusion models. Although token supervised diffusion models can mitigate this issue by learning object-wise consistency between the image content and object segmentation maps, it tends to suffer from the problems of segmentation map bias and semantic overlap conflict, especially when involving multiple objects. In this paper, we propose ELDiff, a new evidential learning-supervised T2I diffusion model, which leverages the advantages of uncertainty metric and conflict detection to enhance the fault tolerance of unreliable segmentation maps and suppress semantic conflicts, strengthening object-wise consistency learning. Specifically, a pixel evidence loss is proposed to restrain overconfidence in unreliable labels through evidential regularization, and a token conflict loss is designed to weaken the contradiction between semantics through optimizing a measured conflict factor. Extensive experiments show that our ELDiff outperforms existing training based and train-free based T2I diffusion models on SD v1.4, SD v2.1, SDXL, SD v3.5, and Qwen-Image, without requiring additional inference-time manipulations. Notably, ELDiff can be seamlessly extended to the existing training pipeline of T2I diffusion models. Code can be found at https://github.com/QingtaoPan/ELDiff.

</details>


### [48] [CogniRoute: Learning to Route Social Evidence in Omni-Modal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20970)
*Yifan Shen,Pei Tian,Xinzhuo Li,Bowen Fang,Shujun Xia,Bingxuan Li,Ana Jojic,Wenming Ye,Xu Cao,James Matthew Rehg,Ismini Lourentzou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CogniRoute is a schema-guided Mixture-of-Experts framework for social omni reasoning that uses cognitive schema factorization, route-aware reinforcement learning, and a new benchmark (OmniSocialBench) to achieve 59.38% accuracy on social video QA, outperforming both proprietary and open-source omni baselines by large margins.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unified access to multiple modalities (video, audio, text) does not guarantee that omni-modal models use the right evidence, especially in social video question answering where answers depend on gestures, vocal tone, temporal cues, or cross-modal mismatches.

Method: CogniRoute uses a training-only cognitive schema that factorizes examples by cross-modal relation, reasoning demand, and temporal scope, aligning global routing signatures with this structure during supervised fine-tuning. It also introduces route-aware reinforcement learning that jointly optimizes token generation and expert allocation using rewards for answer correctness, modality-consistent reasoning, and cognitive temporal grounding.

Result: CogniRoute achieves 59.38% average accuracy on OmniSocialBench, improving over the strongest proprietary baseline by 15.33 percentage points and the strongest open-source omni baseline by 26.77 points, with the largest gains on questions requiring audio-visual coordination, conflict resolution, and temporally grounded social inference.

Conclusion: Schema-guided expert routing and route-aware reinforcement learning significantly improve omni-modal social reasoning by ensuring the model uses the right evidence from the right modalities at the right time.

Abstract: Omni-modal models can ingest video, audio, and text, but unified access to multiple modalities does not guarantee that a model uses the right evidence. This gap is especially pronounced in social video question answering, where the answer may hinge on a gesture, vocal tone, temporal cue, or mismatch between what is said and what is visually expressed. We introduce CogniRoute, a schema-guided Mixture-of-Experts framework for social omni reasoning. CogniRoute uses a training-only cognitive schema that factorizes each example by cross-modal relation, reasoning demand, and temporal scope, and aligns global routing signatures with this structure during supervised fine-tuning. We further introduce route-aware reinforcement learning, which jointly optimizes token generation and expert allocation using rewards for answer correctness, modality-consistent reasoning, and cognitive temporal grounding. To support training and evaluation, we construct OmniSocialBench, a diagnostic social video QA resource with 118K structured training examples, grounded reasoning traces, schema labels, temporal evidence spans, and a manually verified evaluation split. CogniRoute achieves 59.38\% average accuracy on OmniSocialBench, improving over the strongest proprietary baseline by 15.33 percentage points and the strongest open-source omni baseline by 26.77 points, with the largest gains on questions requiring audio-visual coordination, conflict resolution, and temporally grounded social inference.

</details>


### [49] [UNITY: Attention Flow Networks for Adaptive Conditioning in Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20971)
*Aryan Das,Koushik Biswas,Moloud Abdar,Vinay Kumar Verma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UNITY introduces a two-stage training paradigm (Universal + Specialization) with Morphable Attention Flow Network and Morph Wrapper modules for efficient composite conditioning in diffusion-based image generation, achieving SOTA fidelity with reduced memory and latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods train separate adapters for each conditioning modality, which is inefficient and not scalable. The goal is to create a unified, efficient adapter that handles multiple conditioning types jointly.

Method: A two-stage training paradigm: (1) Universal Stage learns cross-modal representations across all conditioning modalities, (2) Specialization Stage refines modality-specific features. Core modules: Morphable Attention Flow (MAF) Network and Morph Wrapper for channel-aware, spatially adaptive feature alignment via learnable flow fields and attention-based fusion.

Result: State-of-the-art image fidelity across multiple datasets, with significantly reduced inference latency and memory consumption under both single and composite conditioning settings.

Conclusion: UNITY provides a scalable, memory-efficient solution for composite conditioning in diffusion models without modifying the underlying architecture, demonstrating universal-to-specialized learning is effective for multi-modal conditioning.

Abstract: We introduce UNITY, a Universal-to-Specialized adapter for efficient and scalable composite conditioning in diffusion based image generation. Unlike prior methods that train separate adapters for each conditioning modality, UNITY jointly learns shared semantics across multiple conditioning types and subsequently specializes without modifying the underlying architecture. The proposed two stage training paradigm consists of a Universal Stage that captures cross modal representations across all conditioning modalities using half of the total training steps, followed by a Specialization Stage that refines modality specific features using the remaining training budget. At the core of UNITY are the Morphable Attention Flow (MAF) Network and Morph Wrapper modules, which enable channel aware and spatially adaptive feature alignment through learnable flow fields and attention based fusion. This constant complexity formulation supports flexible operation under both single and composite conditioning settings while significantly reducing inference latency and memory consumption. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets demonstrate that UNITY achieves state of the art image fidelity while maintaining superior memory efficiency. Code: https://github.com/arya-domain/UNITY

</details>


### [50] [Robusto-2: Benchmarking Humans & VLMs for Autonomous Driving in Lima & New York City](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20980)
*Adrian Cespedes,Marcelo Chincha,Dunant Cusipuma,Victor Flores-Benites,David Ortega,Arturo Deza*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper studies how well humans and VLMs generalize to out-of-distribution driving scenarios across different geographies (Lima and NYC), finding that humans answer similarly regardless of origin while VLMs diverge from humans, with geography not strongly modulating responses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how well Self-Driving Cars using VLMs as cognitive backbones will generalize in new geographic settings, particularly in OOD edge-case scenarios where no autonomous driving companies currently operate.

Method: Full factorial analysis comparing human drivers from Lima, human drivers from NYC, and VLMs, using dashcam footage from both cities under a VQA paradigm spanning Factual, Ratings, Counterfactual, and Reasoning question categories.

Result: Humans and VLMs diverge in responses, modulated by question type; humans answer similarly regardless of origin; no strong geography-modulated differences were found for either group.

Conclusion: VLMs do not yet align well with human driving judgment in OOD geographic settings, and geography does not strongly affect responses, likely due to the high out-of-distribution nature of the scenarios.

Abstract: As Self-Driving Cars continue to expand internationally and use multi-modal systems such as VLMs as a cognitive backbone for their Action models; how well will these systems generalize in new settings, in particular out-of-distribution (OOD) edge-case scenarios in new geographies? In this paper, we study this open question by providing a full factorial analysis with human drivers of Lima, human drivers from New York City, and VLMs and showing them dashcam footage collected from Lima and New York City -- prompting them with a variety of questions under a Visual Question Answering (VQA) paradigm. In particular, we pick these two cities as they are highly challenging driving locations where no Self-Driving Car company currently operates in, and ask questions that span 4 categories: Factual, Ratings, Counterfactual and Reasoning. We find that Humans and VLMs diverge in their responses -- though this is modulated by the type of questions asked, and that Humans answer similarly independent of where they are from (Lima/NYC). To our surprise, we did not find a strong difference in terms of answers (Humans or VLMs) that was modulated by geography, likely due to their high out-of-distribution nature. Our dataset is available at: https://huggingface.co/datasets/Artificio/robusto-2

</details>


### [51] [CheXpercept: A Benchmark for Evaluating Expert-Level Lesion Perception in Chest X-rays](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21020)
*Geon Choi,Hangyul Yoon,Nalee Kim,Jeong Yun Jang,Hyunju Shin,Hyunki Park,Sang Hoon Seo,Edward Choi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CheXpercept introduces a multi-level perception benchmark for chest X-ray VLMs, covering coarse detection, fine contour evaluation, and semantic attribute extraction. Testing 14 VLMs reveals adequate performance only at coarse levels, with medical VLMs showing no advantage over general-domain models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLM evaluation for chest X-ray analysis is limited to disease-presence classification without visual grounding, failing to verify expert-level lesion perception needed for clinical reliability.

Method: A semi-automated generation pipeline with six medical expert reviewers created 10,400 QA items from 2,100 CXRs across seven lesions. The benchmark follows a radiologist's cognitive workflow with three perception levels: coarse detection, fine contour evaluation/revision, and semantic attribute extraction.

Result: 14 VLMs (general and medical) were benchmarked. Models performed adequately only at coarse level, with accuracy dropping sharply on deeper visual tasks. Medical VLMs showed almost no perceptual advantage over general-domain models.

Conclusion: Current VLMs lack deep visual perception for clinically reliable CXR analysis, and medical domain adaptation fails to improve perceptual capabilities, highlighting a systemic flaw in current approaches.

Abstract: The evaluation of vision-language models (VLMs) for chest X-ray (CXR) analysis has largely been limited to disease-presence classification without visual grounding. Such evaluations fail to verify the expert-level lesion perception necessary to ensure the clinical reliability of VLMs. To address these limitations, we introduce CheXpercept, a sequential, multi-level perception benchmark that mirrors a radiologist's cognitive workflow across coarse-level detection, fine-level contour evaluation and revision, and semantic-level attribute extraction. To ensure high clinical fidelity at scale, we construct the dataset using a semi-automated generation pipeline paired with a review by six medical experts. CheXpercept contains 10,400 QA items derived from 2,100 CXRs, covering seven clinically critical pulmonary and cardiac lesions. To demonstrate the current landscape of VLM perception, we benchmark 14 general and medical VLMs on CheXpercept. The models achieve adequate performance only at the coarse level, with accuracy degrading precipitously on deeper visual tasks. Notably, medical VLMs show almost no perceptual advantage over their general-domain counterparts, highlighting a systemic flaw in current domain adaptation. The code and dataset will be publicly available.

</details>


### [52] [Sparse Point-Guided Fusion of Supervised and Self-Supervised Learning Model for Seaweed Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21026)
*Tatsuya Suzuki,Kazuya Ijuin,Hideki Tomimori,Megumi Chikano,Katsushi Sakai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a two-stage seaweed segmentation technique combining supervised and self-supervised learning (SSL.Prop.) with MaskFusion (MF) to improve blue carbon quantification in Ocean Digital Twins, achieving 0.068 mIoU improvement over USIS-SAM on Yamaguchi Prefecture underwater images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Precise seaweed classification is needed for blue carbon quantification in Ocean Digital Twin initiatives, but conventional supervised learning suffers from data scarcity and domain gaps, while self-supervised learning cannot assign class labels.

Method: A two-stage technique: (1) SSL.Prop. uses supervised learning to provide initial class info and approximate locations to guide self-supervised learning for detailed segmentation; (2) MaskFusion (MF) refines results by merging instance-level masks.

Result: The full method (SSL.Prop.+MF) achieved a 0.068 mIoU improvement over USIS-SAM, with significant accuracy gains particularly for small seaweed species.

Conclusion: This integrated approach demonstrates strong potential for improving blue carbon quantification and marine ecosystem monitoring by enabling automatic class label assignment and mitigating domain gap effects.

Abstract: The ocean plays a critical role in sustainable development, particularly in climate change mitigation.
