TL;DR: HPP decouples perception from reasoning in long video understanding by using a coding-capable LLM to iteratively probe video segments via programmatic exploration, achieving significant gains across multiple long-video benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLMs couple perception and multi-step reasoning within a single forward pass, which is bottlenecked by the LLM's limited capacity to discover and execute multi-step strategies in latent representations, especially for long videos requiring fine-grained perception and complex temporal reasoning.

Method: HPP reformulates long video understanding as iterative, programmatic exploration of hierarchically segmented video. It uses: (1) information-density-aware hierarchical segmentation, (2) late-interaction semantic retrieval, and (3) structured probing functions for coarse-to-fine temporal localization. A coding-capable LLM plans and executes multi-step strategies in an interactive coding environment, invoking a VLM for localized perception on demand.

Result: Validated on LongVideoBench (fine-grained perception + long-range reasoning), with further gains demonstrated on EgoSchema, VideoMME, and MLVU benchmarks.

Conclusion: Decoupling perception from higher-order temporal reasoning via iterative programmatic probing substantially improves long video understanding compared to coupled latent approaches in VLMs.

Abstract: Understanding long videos requires fine-grained perception and multi-step, higher-order reasoning over complex, long-range spatio-temporal dynamics. Vision-language models (VLMs) encode video frames into visual tokens and attempt to perform both perception and multi-step planning latently, within a single forward pass. This coupled formulation, however, is bottlenecked by the LLM's limited capacity to discover and execute multi-step strategies in its latent representations. To address this bottleneck, we propose Hierarchical Programmatic Probing (HPP), a framework that decouples semantic perception from higher-order temporal reasoning by reformulating long video understanding as iterative, programmatic exploration of a hierarchically segmented video. Specifically, a coding-capable LLM plans and executes a multi-step strategy in an interactive coding environment, probing the video for information and invoking a VLM for localized perception on demand. To make probing tractable over long videos, we introduce three components: information-density-aware hierarchical segmentation, late-interaction semantic retrieval, and structured probing functions for coarse-to-fine temporal localization. We validate HPP on LongVideoBench, which requires both fine-grained perception and long-range relational reasoning, and show that decoupling the two via iterative programmatic probing yields substantial gains. Further results on EgoSchema, VideoMME, and MLVU demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach across diverse long-video benchmarks.

</details>


### [107] [Adversarial Domain Prompt Tuning and Generation for Single Domain Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21736)
*Zhipeng Xu,De Cheng,Xinyang Jiang,Nannan Wang,Dongsheng Li,Xinbo Gao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes PAPT, a progressive adversarial prompt tuning framework for pre-trained diffusion models, which learns abstract prompts to generate diverse domain-style training images for single domain generalization, outperforming SOTA methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single domain generalization struggles with limited training data. Using T2I foundation models for augmentation is promising, but manually designing prompts for all possible domains is impractical, especially for abstract characteristics hard to describe with words.

Method: Proposes Progressive Adversarial Prompt Tuning (PAPT) for pre-trained diffusion models, learning two sets of abstract prompts: one for domain-invariant category information and another for domain-specific styles, trained via adversarial learning to generate diverse domain-style images.

Result: Extensive experiments show PAPT achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art single-domain generalization approaches.

Conclusion: Leveraging pre-trained T2I models with learned abstract prompts (PAPT) is an effective strategy for single-domain generalization, overcoming the limitations of manual prompt design.

Abstract: Single domain generalization (SDG) aims to learn a robust model, which could perform well on many unseen domains while there is only one single domain available for training. One of the promising directions for achieving single-domain generalization is to generate out-of-domain (OOD) training data through data augmentation or image generation. Given the rapid advancements in AI-generated content (AIGC), this paper is the first to propose leveraging powerful pre-trained text-to-image (T2I) foundation models to create the training data. However, manually designing textual prompts to generate images for all possible domains is often impractical, and some domain characteristics may be too abstract to describe with words. To address these challenges, we propose a novel Progressive Adversarial Prompt Tuning (PAPT) framework for pre-trained diffusion models. Instead of relying on static textual domains, our approach learns two sets of abstract prompts as conditions for the diffusion model: one that captures domain-invariant category information and another that models domain-specific styles. This adversarial learning mechanism enables the T2I model to generate images in various domain styles while preserving key categorical features. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method, achieving superior performances to state-of-the-art single-domain generalization approaches.

</details>


### [108] [Quantile Adaptive Temperature Scaling for Confidence Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21749)
*Omprakash Chakraborty,Leo Fillioux,Ismail Ben Ayed,Jose Dolz*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: QaTS improves post-hoc neural network calibration by adapting temperature scaling based on prediction confidence quantiles, outperforming standard methods across diverse scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Temperature Scaling applies a uniform global temperature to logits, failing to address the heterogeneous structure of miscalibration across the confidence spectrum, especially in low-confidence regions where correctness-confidence discrepancies are largest.

Method: QaTS maps predictions into quantile space, learning a monotone temperature function that adapts across different confidence quantiles. It optimizes a reparameterized Expected Calibration Error (ECE) objective to produce sample-wise temperatures while preserving well-calibrated high-confidence predictions.

Result: Across diverse datasets, architectures, and tasks (including class imbalance and distributional shifts), QaTS consistently and substantially outperforms state-of-the-art post-hoc calibration methods.

Conclusion: QaTS delivers more reliable and trustworthy confidence estimates without modifying model predictions, effectively addressing the limitations of global temperature scaling.

Abstract: Deep neural networks often produce poorly calibrated confidence estimates, overstating their certainty even when predictions are incorrect. Temperature Scaling remains the most widely used posthoc calibration method due to its simplicity and effectiveness, yet its global, uniform rescaling of logits fails to correct the highly heterogeneous structure of miscalibration observed across the confidence spectrum. In particular, the largest correctness confidence discrepancies arise in different quantile regions depending on the setting, low confidence predictions, where uncertainty matters most, tend to exhibit the largest correctness confidence discrepancies, which standard TS leaves largely unaddressed. We introduce Quantile Adaptive Temperature Scaling (QaTS), a simple and efficient post hoc calibration method that adapts the temperature as a function of a predictions empirical confidence quantile. By mapping confidences into the quantile space, QaTS normalizes the calibration problem, makes the structure of miscalibration explicit and enables a monotone temperature function that adapts across quantiles while leaving well calibrated high confidence predictions largely unchanged. preserving high confidence behavior. This quantile aware formulation aligns naturally with a reparameterized Expected Calibration Error (ECE) objective and yields a sample wise temperature that is robust across a variety of challenging scenarios, such as class imbalance and distributional shifts. Across a broad range of datasets, architectures, evaluation scenarios and diverse tasks, QaTS consistently, and substantially, outperforms state of the art post hoc calibration methods, delivering more reliable and trustworthy confidence estimates without modifying model predictions.

</details>


### [109] [From Gradient Clipping to Structural Refinement: Improving DPSGD for Medical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21763)
*Shiva Parsarad,Parth Shandilya,Isabel Wagner*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper evaluates DPSGD variants (Auto-S, NSGD, PSAC) for differentially private medical image segmentation, showing prior assumptions don't hold (especially for PSAC), and proposes an adaptive DP-Morph method combining clipping with morphological refinement for improved multi-class segmentation under privacy constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical image segmentation uses sensitive data, raising privacy concerns because trained models can leak information. Differential privacy offers a solution but degrades model utility.

Method: Evaluates DPSGD variants (Auto-S, NSGD, PSAC) on binary and multi-class medical segmentation tasks, analyzes gradient alignment, combines clipping strategies with morphological refinement, and proposes an adaptive DP-Morph variant for class-specific structures.

Result: Prior assumptions (especially for PSAC) do not consistently hold in medical segmentation. Combining clipping with morphological refinement improves segmentation under privacy constraints. The proposed adaptive DP-Morph enhances multi-class performance.

Conclusion: DPSGD variants behave differently in medical segmentation vs. classification; adaptive morphological refinement helps overcome utility loss under differential privacy.

Abstract: Medical image segmentation is widely used for disease detection but relies on sensitive data, raising privacy concerns as trained models can leak information. Differential privacy, typically implemented via Differential Private Stochastic Gradient Descent (DPSGD), provides a solution, though at the cost of reduced utility. Recent DPSGD variants, including Automatic clipping (Auto-S), Normalised SGD with perturbation (NSGD), and Per-sample adaptive clipping (PSAC), have shown promise in image classification, but their behavior in medical segmentation remains underexplored. We evaluate these methods across binary and multi-class tasks and analyze gradient alignment, showing that prior assumptions, particularly for PSAC, do not consistently hold. We further demonstrate that combining clipping strategies with morphological refinement improves segmentation quality under privacy constraints. Finally, we propose an adaptive DP-Morph variant that captures class-specific structures and enhances performance in multi-class settings.

</details>


### [110] [Motion-Aware Reinforcement Learning For Object Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21764)
*Prithvi Raj Singh,Satyendra Singh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MARLNet is a PPO-based bounding-box refinement agent that uses a constant-velocity motion prior and action smoothness penalty to improve object detection. It achieves consistent gains on Pascal VOC 2012 (+0.011) and VisDrone 2019 (+0.007) by preventing overshooting, and identifies reward interference issues as well as representational ceilings when sharing a backbone with the base detector.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve bounding box refinement in object detection by incorporating motion priors and action smoothness penalties into a reinforcement learning framework, addressing issues like overshooting that plague plain PPO approaches.

Method: MARLNet uses a PPO-based agent with a 268-dimensional observation state (current proposal, kinematic prediction, previous action, EfficientNet-B0 features) and a 5D policy for coordinate adjustments and termination. It applies a constant-velocity motion prior and action smoothness penalty in the reward function.

Result: +0.011 detection success gain on Pascal VOC 2012 (IoU ≥ 0.5, λ_phys=0.10) and +0.007 on VisDrone 2019 (λ_phys=0.70). Unconstrained PPO gains +0.025 on VisDrone. Reward interference identified when combining deviation penalty with absolute IoU term.

Conclusion: Motion priors stabilize training and prevent overshooting in RL-based refinement. Replacing deviation penalty with action smoothness resolves trigger collapse. A representational ceiling exists when refinement agents share a backbone with their base detector.

Abstract: We present MARLNet (Motion-Aware Reinforcement Learning Network), a PPO-based bounding-box refinement agent that incorporates a constant-velocity motion prior into the observation state and an action smoothness penalty into the reward function. The agent operates on 268-dimensional observations encoding the current proposal, a kinematic prediction, the previous action, and a 256-dimensional EfficientNet-B0 crop feature, and learns a five-dimensional policy controlling coordinate adjustments and a binary termination trigger. Evaluated on Pascal VOC 2012 and VisDrone 2019, MARLNet trains stably across all regularization strengths tested and achieves consistent gains in detection success rate at $\text{IoU} \geq 0.5$: up to $+0.011$ on VOC ($λ_\text{phys}{=}0.10$), where the motion prior prevents the overshooting that causes plain PPO to regress on this metric, and $+0.007$ on VisDrone ($λ_\text{phys}{=}0.70$), where unconstrained PPO achieves a larger gain ($+0.025$) owing to the weaker base detector. Through reward design ablations and training dynamics analysis, we identify a reward interference in which combining a constant-velocity deviation penalty with an absolute IoU term causes trigger collapse, and show that replacing it with the action smoothness penalty resolves this failure. We further characterize a representational ceiling facing crop-feature refinement agents that share a backbone with their base detector, confirmed through a global-plus-local observation ablation. Project page: https://prithviraj97.github.io/marl-net

</details>


### [111] [RAPID: A Reproducible Multi-Agent Pipeline for Interpretable Disaster Damage Assessment from Satellite and Street-View Imagery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21819)
*Yifan Yang,Wenjing Gong,Kaili Zhang,Lei Zou,Zhengzhong Tu,Hao Li,Zongrong Li,Xinyue Ye*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RAPID is a multi-agent pipeline for zero-shot disaster damage assessment that coordinates specialized agents to analyze satellite and street-view images, producing interpretable damage assessments and actionable reports without task-specific fine-tuning, achieving 0.92 accuracy in multi-disaster classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing disaster damage assessment methods rely on supervised learning and task-specific fine-tuning, which fail to generalize under domain shifts, long-tailed data distributions, and heterogeneous geospatial sources, and cannot integrate multimodal geospatial information like satellite and street-view images.

Method: RAPID employs a multi-agent pipeline that coordinates specialized agents for cross-view understanding, image restoration, structured damage recognition, and geographical reasoning across heterogeneous data modalities, enabling zero-shot damage assessment without task-specific fine-tuning.

Result: RAPID achieves 0.92 overall accuracy for multi-disaster type classification and up to 0.627 for cross-view damage severity prediction across hurricanes, floods, wildfires, and earthquakes.

Conclusion: RAPID demonstrates strong potential as a foundational framework for autonomous disaster intelligence, providing fine-grained, interpretable assessments and location-specific disaster reports to support early-stage emergency response.

Abstract: Due to the increasing frequency and intensity of extreme climate events, there is a clear demand for intelligent, scalable, and autonomous approaches to disaster damage assessment. Existing methods, largely based on supervised learning and task-specific fine-tuning, struggle to generalize under domain shifts, long-tailed data distributions, and heterogeneous geospatial data sources, especially in disaster scenarios. They also often lack the ability to integrate and reason across multimodal geospatial information, such as satellite images and street-view images. In this paper, we introduce RAPID, a reproducible multi-agent pipeline for interpretable disaster damage assessment, including damage-level assessment, damage-type interpretation, and actionable suggestions for response, remediation, and recovery. RAPID coordinates specialized agents to perform cross-view understanding, image restoration, structured damage recognition, and geographical reasoning across heterogeneous data modalities. Without task-specific fine-tuning, RAPID supports zero-shot damage assessment by jointly using complementary information from remote sensing and ground-level perspectives. The system produces fine-grained, interpretable assessments and automatically generates location-specific, decision-relevant disaster reports to support early-stage emergency response. We evaluate RAPID across hurricanes, floods, wildfires, and earthquakes using multiple cross-view imagery inputs, including pre- and post-disaster street-view images, post-disaster remote sensing imagery, and street-view image pairs. Experiments show that RAPID achieves 0.92 overall accuracy for multi-disaster type classification and up to 0.627 for cross-view damage severity prediction, highlighting its potential as a foundational framework for autonomous disaster intelligence.

</details>


### [112] [Beyond Flat Labels: Level-Restricted Contrastive Learning for Hierarchical Fine-Grained Vision Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21838)
*Zhiyuan Tao,Srikumar Sastry,Matthew J Thompson,Elizabeth G Campolongo,Net Zhang,Ziheng Zhang,Hilmar Lapp,Yu Su,Tanya Berger-Wolf,Nathan Jacobs,Wei-Lun Chao,Jianyang Gu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper addresses hierarchical inconsistency in multimodal contrastive learning (e.g., BioCLIP) by restricting contrastive comparisons to the same taxonomic level and using group-balanced optimization, achieving 30.47% average accuracy improvement on iNat21.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal contrastive learning methods produce predictions inconsistent across hierarchical label levels (e.g., predicting a fine-grained category whose parent contradicts the predicted higher-level label), due to false negative labels when contrasting across taxonomic levels.

Method: (1) Restrict contrastive comparisons to categories within the same taxonomic level to avoid false negatives. (2) Adopt a group-balanced design to ensure each taxonomic level receives adequate optimization.

Result: Trained on TreeOfLife-10M with BioCLIP; achieves significantly improved hierarchical consistency in both Euclidean and hyperbolic spaces; on iNat21, improves average accuracy across levels by 30.47% over baseline.

Conclusion: The proposed framework enhances both hierarchical consistency and classification accuracy from coarse to fine granularity, demonstrating effectiveness for hierarchical zero-shot classification.

Abstract: Multimodal contrastive learning has enabled zero-shot visual classification by aligning images with textual categories. However, in hierarchically structured label spaces, existing methods often produce predictions that are inconsistent across taxonomic levels. For example, a model may predict a fine-grained category whose parent category contradicts its simultaneously predicted higher-level label. By analysis, the issue originates from false negative labels when contrastive comparison involves multiple taxonomic levels. To this end, we propose to restrict contrastive comparisons to categories within the same taxonomic level. In addition, we adopt a group-balanced design, ensuring each taxonomic level receives adequate optimization. As a result, the proposed framework improves both hierarchical consistency and classification accuracy from coarse to fine granularity. We train our model with TreeOfLife-10M based on BioCLIP and evaluate it across multiple hierarchical classification benchmarks, where the model demonstrates significantly improved hierarchical consistency in both Euclidean and hyperbolic spaces. Notably, on iNaturalist 2021 (iNat21), our method improves average accuracy across levels by 30.47% over the baseline, highlighting its effectiveness for hierarchical zero-shot classification.

</details>


### [113] [Zero-Shot Vision-Language Models for Classroom Engagement Recognition: A Benchmark Study of Prompt Sensitivity and Cross-Dataset Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21861)
*Aman Goyal,Kshama Nitin Shah,Kemmannu Vineet Venkatesh Rao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper benchmarks five VLMs (CLIP, BLIP-VQA, GPT-4o, LLaVA-1.5-7B, Qwen2.5VL-7B) for zero-shot classroom engagement recognition, finding near-random performance on individual students (kappa≤0.10), severe class collapse (85-100% single-level predictions), extreme prompt sensitivity (up to 32% accuracy swings), and practical deployment barriers like GPT-4o rejecting 98% of individual-face queries, though scene-level classification performs better (kappa≈0.60).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically evaluate whether modern Vision-Language Models (VLMs) can recognize student engagement in zero-shot settings, an underexplored yet promising direction for scalable learning analytics.

Method: Five VLMs (CLIP, BLIP-VQA, GPT-4o, LLaVA-1.5-7B, Qwen2.5VL-7B) were tested on two datasets (DAiSEE: 300 individual-student video clips; SCB: 1,168 scene-level images) using three prompt designs (minimal, rubric-anchored, chain-of-thought), with performance measured via Cohen's kappa and accuracy.

Result: Zero-shot VLMs perform near-randomly on individual students (kappa≤0.10 on DAiSEE), exhibit severe class collapse (85-100% single-level predictions), and are extremely prompt-sensitive (up to 32% accuracy swings). Scene-level classification on SCB is more tractable (kappa≈0.60 for CLIP and GPT-4o). GPT-4o safety filters block 98% of chain-of-thought requests on individual faces.

Conclusion: Current VLMs are unsuitable for zero-shot individual student engagement recognition but show promise for scene-level classification. The findings provide a calibrated baseline and highlight critical design considerations (prompt engineering, safety filters, class balance) for deploying VLMs in educational observation systems.

Abstract: Automated classroom engagement recognition holds substantial promise for scalable learning analytics, yet the suitability of modern Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for this task under zero-shot conditions remains largely unexplored. We present a systematic benchmark that evaluates five widely-used VLMs: CLIP, BLIP-VQA, GPT-4o, LLaVA-1.5-7B, and Qwen2.5VL-7B-Instruct across two complementary educational datasets: DAiSEE, an individual-student video dataset (300 sampled test clips), and the Student Classroom Behaviour dataset (SCB, 1,168 scene-level images). Each model is probed with three prompt variants spanning minimal, rubric-anchored, and chain-of-thought designs. Our experiments reveal three primary failure modes of zero-shot VLMs for engagement recognition: (1) near-random performance on individual students, with Cohen's kappa never exceeding 0.10 on DAiSEE; (2) severe class collapse, where models assign 85-100% of predictions to a single engagement level regardless of visual content; and (3) extreme prompt sensitivity, with accuracy swings of up to 32 percentage points on identical images depending solely on prompt phrasing. Remarkably, scene-level classification on SCB is substantially more tractable: CLIP and GPT-4o achieve kappa approximately 0.60 when prompted with behaviorally-grounded rubrics. We also document a practical barrier for deployment: GPT-4o's safety filters reject 98% of chain-of-thought requests involving individual student faces. Our findings provide a calibrated baseline and surface critical design considerations for the use of VLMs in educational observation systems.

</details>


### [114] [Prompt-Calibrated SAM 3 for Open-Vocabulary Remote Sensing Semantic Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21863)
*Yanghui Song,Nanqing Liu,Haonan Yin,Yingjie Gao,Chengfu Yang,Qi Ming*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ProC-SAM3 improves SAM-based open-vocabulary segmentation in remote sensing by using offline prompt pools, cached text embeddings, and presence-guided fusion, achieving 56.1% mIoU across eight benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SAM-based OVSS methods suffer from three issues: insufficient semantic coverage from single-class prompts, redundant online text encoding when expanding prompts, and noisy activation propagation when aggregating multiple prompts.

Method: (1) Offline prompt pool with Category Matcher and Expansion Constraints; (2) cached text embeddings for reuse; (3) Presence-Guided Residual Fusion with peak-preserving class aggregation.

Result: 56.1% average mIoU over eight benchmarks, outperforming previous best training-free method by 3.9 percentage points.

Conclusion: ProC-SAM3 effectively calibrates SAM's prompt interface for remote sensing OVSS, setting a new state-of-the-art for training-free methods.

Abstract: Open-vocabulary semantic segmentation (OVSS) in remote sensing images aims to segment categories beyond a fixed label space. Recent SAM 3-based methods provide a promising training-free foundation, yet three key issues remain: (1) a single class-name prompt lacks sufficient semantic coverage for complex remote sensing categories; (2) expanding each category into multiple prompts introduces redundant online text encoding; and (3) directly aggregating multiple prompt responses propagates noisy activations into the final prediction. To address these issues, we propose ProC-SAM3, which calibrates SAM 3's prompt interface for remote sensing OVSS from three complementary aspects. First, we construct an offline prompt pool where a Category Matcher groups MLLM-generated candidates into per-category sets, and Expansion Constraints further refine each set using category-specific prior knowledge. Second, the resulting text embeddings are cached and reused across all test images, eliminating repeated text encoding. Third, we introduce Presence-Guided Residual Fusion to gate unreliable decoder outputs by prompt presence and confidence, followed by peak-preserving class aggregation that retains fine-grained activations for small and sparse objects. Experiments on eight benchmarks show that ProC-SAM3 achieves an average mIoU of 56.1%, outperforming the previous best training-free method by 3.9 percentage points. Code will be available at https://github.com/YanghuiSong/ProC-SAM3.

</details>


### [115] [Fidelity- and Perception-Aware Local Implicit Attention for Arbitrary-Scale Image Super-Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21910)
*Yu-Syuan Xu,Hao-Lun Sun,Hao-Wei Chen,Hsien-Kai Kuo,Chun-Yi Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FPLIA is a framework combining fidelity-oriented features with diffusion models for arbitrary-scale image super-resolution, achieving realistic textures while avoiding structural hallucinations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional ASISR methods produce overly smooth results lacking details, while diffusion methods can hallucinate structures. The goal is to achieve both perceptual realism and reconstruction fidelity.

Method: Proposes FPLIA with two key modules: (1) FPAM applies self-attention and cross-attention to fidelity and perceptual features, (2) FPSM adaptively selects the most representative features for RGB prediction.

Result: Qualitative and quantitative results on standard ASISR benchmarks show superior perceptual realism while maintaining reconstruction accuracy.

Conclusion: FPLIA effectively integrates fidelity-oriented features into diffusion pipelines, addressing the fidelity-perception trade-off in arbitrary-scale super-resolution.

Abstract: Arbitrary-scale image super-resolution (ASISR) aims to reconstruct high-resolution images from low-resolution inputs over a continuous range of upscaling factors. While traditional pixel-regression approaches often produce overly smooth results that lack realistic details, recent diffusion methods can produce sharper and more realistic textures. However, these diffusion techniques frequently introduce the risk of structural hallucinations. To address these issues, we propose Fidelity- and Perception-Aware Local Implicit Attention (FPLIA), a framework that effectively integrates fidelity-oriented features into a diffusion pipeline to produce realistic and faithful reconstructions for ASISR. We introduce a Fidelity and Perception Attention Module (FPAM), which applies both self-attention and cross-attention to fidelity-oriented and perceptual features to enhance representational capacity. To further exploit their complements, we design a Fidelity and Perception Select Module (FPSM) that adaptively selects the most representative features for RGB values prediction. We conduct extensive experiments to validate the effectiveness of these components. Both qualitative and quantitative results show that FPLIA delivers superior perceptual realism while maintaining reconstruction accuracy on standard ASISR benchmarks. The source code is accessible at the following repository: https://github.com/XUSean0118/FPLIA.

</details>


### [116] [Rethinking the Adaptation of Vision Foundation Models for Efficient Cell Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21913)
*Qing Xu,Xiangjian He,Wenting Duan,Jiebo Luo,Zhen Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EffiCell-Seg is a highly efficient cell segmentation framework that leverages frozen Vision Foundation Model representations using only ~5M trainable parameters (130x fewer than fine-tuned counterparts), achieving state-of-the-art performance through a Cell Structure Prompt Encoder and Synergistic Mask Decoder without re-training the visual encoder.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the resource-intensive nature of existing cell segmentation methods that rely on fine-tuning heavy visual encoders, requiring extensive computational overhead and large-scale annotations. The goal is to unlock the potential of Vision Foundation Models for cellular imaging without the burden of full fine-tuning.

Method: The EffiCell-Seg framework introduces two key components: (1) a Cell Structure Prompt Encoder (CSP-Encoder) that synthesizes semantic-aware saliency and principal morphological features from frozen VFM representations into explicit structural prior maps, leveraging the intrinsic global saliency and local morphological priors encoded in pretrained VFMs; and (2) a Synergistic Mask Decoder (SM-Decoder) that enforces contextual consistency by jointly predicting geometric distance fields and semantic maps via mutual cross-guidance.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods across diverse cell imaging modalities while requiring only ~5M trainable parameters, which is over 130x fewer than fully fine-tuned VFM counterparts. Code is publicly available.

Conclusion: EffiCell-Seg demonstrates that effective cell segmentation can be achieved without re-training heavy visual encoders by creatively exploiting frozen VFM representations, offering a highly parameter-efficient and annotation-light alternative to existing fine-tuning paradigms.

Abstract: Cell segmentation is critical for computational pathology and biomedical discovery. While recent Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) have demonstrated remarkable universal feature representations, unlocking their full potential for cellular imaging is currently bottlenecked by resource-intensive adaptation paradigms. Existing methods typically rely on fine-tuning heavy visual encoders, leading to extensive computational overhead and a dependency on large-scale annotations. To address this, we propose the EffiCell-Seg framework for highly efficient cell segmentation without re-training the visual encoder. Our core insight is that pretrained VFMs intrinsically encode complementary structural priors: global saliency for localizing potential cells, and local morphological patterns for delineating cellular structures. To harness these priors, we devise a Cell Structure Prompt Encoder (CSP-Encoder) that synthesizes semantic-aware saliency and principal morphological features from frozen VFM representations into explicit structural prior maps. Moreover, we propose a Synergistic Mask Decoder (SM-Decoder) that enforces contextual consistency by jointly predicting geometric distance fields and semantic maps via mutual cross-guidance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that EffiCell-Seg outperforms state-of-the-art methods across diverse cell imaging modalities while requiring only ~5M trainable parameters, over 130x fewer than fully fine-tuned VFM counterparts. The code is available at https://github.com/xq141839/EffiCell-Seg.

</details>


### [117] [GTA-Net: Cooperative Game Theory for Vision-Language Alignment in Chest X-Ray Report Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21915)
*Saif ur Rehman Khan,Imad Ahmed Waqar,Sebastian Vollmer,Muhammad Nabeel Asim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GTA-Net is a game-theoretic vision-language framework for chest X-ray report generation that uses BinaryGameAligner for region-word correspondence and Disease-Aware Ternary Aligner for clinical consistency, achieving SOTA on CheXpertPlus and IU-XRay.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLMs rely on implicit attention mechanisms that fail to enforce explicit region-word correspondence and disease-level consistency, leading to clinically unreliable descriptions.

Method: Formulates report generation as cooperative game-theoretic alignment, with BinaryGameAligner (Shapley-weighted similarity payoff matrices) and Disease-Aware Ternary Aligner (joint image-report-disease concept interactions), combining Swin visual encoder + LoRA-adapted LLM under unified training.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on CheXpertPlus and IU-XRay across standard generation metrics with improved clinical consistency.

Conclusion: Explicit game-theoretic alignment is effective for medical vision-language generation, outperforming implicit attention mechanisms.

Abstract: Automated chest X-ray report generation requires precise cross-modal grounding to ensure clinically reliable descriptions. However, existing vision-language models rely on implicit attention mechanisms that fail to enforce explicit region-word correspondence and disease-level consistency. We propose Game-Theoretic Alignment Network (GTA-Net), a vision-language framework that formulates report generation as a cooperative game-theoretic alignment problem. The model introduces a BinaryGameAligner that models interactions between image regions and text tokens using similarity-based payoff matrices with Shapley-inspired importance weighting. To enforce clinical semantics, we further develop a Disease-Aware Ternary Aligner, which captures joint interactions among images, reports, and structured disease concepts. GTA-Net combines a Swin-based visual encoder with a LoRA-adapted large language model and is trained with a unified objective for generation and alignment. Experiments on CheXpertPlus and IU-XRay demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across standard generation metrics and improved clinical consistency, highlighting the effectiveness of explicit game-theoretic alignment for medical vision-language generation.

</details>


### [118] [CoSA: Correlation-Guided Change Attention with Learnable Residual Gating for Remote Sensing Change Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21932)
*Abdirashid Omar,Jonghyuk Park*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CoSA is a lightweight decoder-side refinement module for remote sensing change detection that uses bi-temporal feature correlation as a control signal for adaptive feature enhancement, achieving 1.5-2.6% F1 gains on four benchmark datasets with negligible overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robust change detection remains challenging under sparse changes, noisy labels, and appearance variations. Existing attention mechanisms rely on implicit feature weighting without explicit temporal control.

Method: CoSA computes normalized same-location cross-correlation between paired decoder features, converts low correlation into a change gate, and injects gated residuals at 1/8 and 1/16 feature scales via learnable residual scaling, within an FC-Siam framework.

Result: Achieves 1.5-2.6% improvements in changed-class F1 over strong baselines on LEVIR-CD, S2Looking, DSIFN, and CLCD datasets, with negligible parameter overhead.

Conclusion: CoSA establishes a practical and effective refinement paradigm for enhancing temporal discriminability in Siamese change detection frameworks, with multiscale placement and learnable residual gating being key for peak performance.

Abstract: Remote sensing change detection (CD) from bi-temporal imagery is critical for applications such as urban monitoring, disaster assessment, and environmental management, yet robust localization remains challenging under sparse changes, noisy labels, and appearance variations. In this paper, we propose Context Sampling Attention (CoSA), a lightweight decoder-side refinement module that explicitly leverages bi-temporal feature correlation as a control signal for adaptive change-aware feature enhancement. This differs from conventional attention mechanisms that rely on implicit feature weighting without explicit temporal control. In the implemented FC-Siam setting, CoSA computes normalized same-location cross-correlation between paired decoder features, converts low correlation into a change gate, and injects the resulting gated residual at native 1/8 and 1/16 feature scales through learnable residual scaling. This design enables effective discrimination between stable and ambiguous regions without relying on computationally expensive global attention. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets (LEVIR-CD, S2Looking, DSIFN, and CLCD) demonstrate consistent improvements over strong baselines, achieving 1.5-2.6% gains in changed-class F1 while introducing negligible parameter overhead. Ablation studies confirm that multiscale placement and learnable residual gating are both important for peak performance. These results indicate that CoSA establishes a practical and effective refinement paradigm for enhancing temporal discriminability in Siamese change detection frameworks.

</details>


### [119] [Artic-O: End-to-End Articulated Object Reconstruction via Latent Geometry Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21938)
*Xuyang Wang,Zhenyu Li,Jian Ding,Habib Slim,Peter Wonka,Hongdong Li,Mohamed Elhoseiny*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Artic-O is an end-to-end feed-forward framework for articulated object reconstruction from sparse images, using latent geometry learning and a pretrained decoder to recover complete geometry, part segmentation, and articulation parameters simultaneously, achieving high quality and efficiency (0.3 seconds per object vs 9 minutes for prior work).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods separate geometry reconstruction, part reasoning, and articulation estimation into different stages, which weakens consistency between shape, active parts, and motion, and incurs substantial inference cost.

Method: Artic-O maps sparse multi-state observations into a pretrained latent geometry space with a frozen flow-matching decoder. It fuses visual tokens, geometry latents, and point-wise decoder features in an image-grounded part-reasoning module for active-part segmentation and articulation prediction. Training uses a geometry-to-articulation curriculum and a decoupled two-pass strategy.

Result: On PartNet-Mobility, Artic-O reduces Chamfer Distance, improves F-score, achieves comparable or better articulation accuracy across most joint metrics, and reduces inference time from 9 minutes to ~0.3 seconds per object compared to LARM.

Conclusion: Artic-O demonstrates that end-to-end latent geometry learning can effectively unify geometry reconstruction and articulation reasoning, achieving both high quality and dramatic efficiency gains over staged prior methods.

Abstract: Reconstructing articulated objects from sparse images requires recovering complete geometry, movable parts, and motion parameters. Recent methods typically separate geometry reconstruction, part reasoning, and articulation estimation into different stages. This separation can weaken consistency between shape, active parts, and motion, while also incurring substantial inference cost. We introduce Artic-O, an end-to-end, feed-forward framework for articulated object reconstruction via latent geometry learning. Instead of fitting geometry in image or view space, Artic-O maps sparse multi-state observations into a pretrained latent geometry space, where a frozen flow-matching decoder provides a complete-shape prior for recovering visible and occluded structures. To connect geometry with articulation, Artic-O fuses visual tokens, geometry latents, and point-wise decoder features in an image-grounded part-reasoning module for active-part segmentation and articulation prediction. We further train the model with a geometry-to-articulation curriculum and a decoupled two-pass strategy to balance reconstruction and part-level supervision. On PartNet-Mobility, Artic-O achieves strong reconstruction quality while being substantially more efficient than LARM, a strong prior method. It reduces Chamfer Distance, improves F-score, and achieves comparable or better articulation accuracy across most joint metrics, while reducing inference time from 9 minutes to about 0.3 seconds per object.

</details>


### [120] [ScalePredictor: Instance-aware Scale Learning for Accurate Quantization of Vision Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21947)
*Changjun Li,Runqing Jiang,Lian Xu,Ye Zhang,Qingyong Hu,Yulan Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ScalePredictor is a dynamic quantization framework for Vision Transformers that leverages a hidden correlation between shallow-layer activation distributions and optimal deeper-layer scales, using efficient range extraction and Taylor-motivated polynomial projection to generate quantization scales with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PTQ methods use static quantization uniformly across all instances, but natural images have diverse activation distributions, making static approaches suboptimal. The deployment of ViTs on edge devices is challenging due to high computational demands.

Method: The authors reveal a hidden correlation between shallow-layer activation distribution ranges and optimal deeper-layer scales. They develop a scale learning mechanism with: (1) an efficient range extraction approach to capture robust range statistics at shallow stages, and (2) a Taylor-motivated polynomial scale projection module to generate all quantization scales simultaneously.

Result: Extensive experiments on ImageNet show ScalePredictor consistently outperforms prior PTQ methods, achieving a more favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off with insignificant computational overhead.

Conclusion: ScalePredictor provides an effective dynamic quantization solution for ViTs, enabling better deployment on edge devices by avoiding costly just-in-time calibration while maintaining high accuracy.

Abstract: Vision Transformers have achieved remarkable success in many fields, yet their deployment on edge devices remains challenging due to their substantial computational demands. Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) offers an attractive solution by compressing models using a small calibration set with minimal training overhead. However, most existing PTQ works adopt a static quantization paradigm that is uniformly applied to all instances. Given the substantial diversity of natural images, the activation distributions vary significantly across samples, making these methods inherently suboptimal. In this paper, we propose ScalePredictor, a dynamic quantization framework for accurate and efficient quantization scale learning of ViTs. We first reveal a hidden correlation between the distribution range of shallow-layer activations and the optimal scales of deeper layers. Based on this, we develop a scale learning mechanism that integrates an efficient range extraction approach to capture robust range statistics at the shallow stage, which are then fed into a Taylor-motivated polynomial scale projection module to generate all quantization scales simultaneously. With the efficiency of polynomial approximation, ScalePredictor introduces insignificant computational overhead while avoiding costly just-in-time calibration. Extensive experiments on ImageNet demonstrate that ScalePredictor consistently outperforms prior PTQ methods, achieving a more favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off. Code and additional results are shown in the supplementary materials.

</details>


### [121] [CapRiCorn-1K: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Video Captioning and Subject Referential Consistency Across Temporal Scales](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21949)
*Xinlong Chen,Jiafu Tang,Yue Ding,Yizhuo Jia,Bozhou Li,Bohan Zeng,Yang Shi,Shihao Li,Yiyan Ji,Qiang Liu,Weihong Lin,Yuanxing Zhang,Pengfei Wan,Liang Wang,Tieniu Tan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CapRiCorn-1K is a new benchmark for evaluating video caption quality and subject referential consistency across diverse durations, revealing that current models struggle with both accuracy and consistent subject references, especially as video length increases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks lack the ability to objectively and comprehensively evaluate video caption accuracy, completeness, and consistent subject references across diverse durations and scenarios, hindering progress in video captioning models.

Method: The authors propose CapRiCorn-1K, a comprehensive benchmark supporting both audiovisual and visual-only settings, designed to evaluate video captioning quality and subject referential consistency over long temporal horizons and diverse video domains.

Result: Current models struggle to generate accurate, comprehensive captions with consistent subject references. Both caption quality and referential consistency decline as video duration increases. The evaluation metrics strongly correlate with downstream task performance.

Conclusion: CapRiCorn-1K effectively evaluates video captioning models, revealing key limitations in current approaches, and its metrics are validated through strong correlations with downstream understanding and generation tasks.

Abstract: Accurate and comprehensive video captions with consistent subject references are critical for downstream understanding and generation tasks. However, few existing benchmarks can objectively and comprehensively evaluate these properties across diverse durations and scenarios, thereby hindering the advancement of video captioning models. To bridge this gap, we propose CapRiCorn-1K, a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate both video captioning quality and subject referential consistency across long temporal horizons and diverse video domains. To accommodate varied evaluation needs, our benchmark supports both audiovisual and visual-only settings. Extensive experiments on CapRiCorn-1K reveal that current models generally struggle to generate accurate and comprehensive captions while maintaining consistent subject references. Moreover, as video duration increases, both the overall caption quality and subject referential consistency decline. Notably, our evaluation metrics exhibit strong correlations with the performance of downstream understanding and generation tasks conditioned on the generated captions, further validating their effectiveness. The project is available at https://github.com/xlchen0205/CapRiCorn-1K .

</details>


### [122] [Denoising-Enhanced Coarse-to-Fine Infrared Small Target Detection with Attention Prior-Guided Knowledge Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21956)
*Houzhang Fang,Ruixuan Huang,Qiuhuan Chen,Xiaolin Wang,Yi Chang,Luxin Yan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ECFNet proposes a coarse-to-fine infrared small target detection framework with attention prior-guided knowledge distillation, using a region binary classification network (RBCN) with denoising-assisted training and a lightweight detector to achieve high accuracy and real-time efficiency on complex backgrounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) in high-resolution images is challenging due to small target sizes, weak features, and complex dynamic background interference. Existing methods suffer from redundant computations on non-target regions and insufficient context exploitation.

Method: (1) Coarse stage: Region binary classification network (RBCN) on grid-based multi-scale feature maps to identify target-containing region proposals; denoising-assisted training strategy with noisy GT masks to enhance discrimination. (2) Fine stage: Lightweight target detector customized to region proposals; knowledge distillation with teacher-student cross-attention prior to focus on critical target regions.

Result: Outperforms existing single-stage and two-stage methods on three real infrared datasets while maintaining high real-time processing efficiency.

Conclusion: The coarse-to-fine framework with attention-guided distillation effectively balances accuracy and efficiency for IRSTD in complex backgrounds.

Abstract: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) in high-resolution images is crucial for many practical applications, such as surveillance of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) and UAV-based ground monitoring. However, IRSTD remains challenging due to the small size and weak features of targets, as well as significant interference from complex dynamic backgrounds. Existing detection methods often suffer from redundant computations on non-target background regions and insufficient exploitation of target context information, which limits their performance in complex backgrounds. To address these issues, we propose an efficient coarse-to-fine infrared small target detection framework with attention prior-guided knowledge distillation, termed ECFNet. In the coarse stage, we design a region binary classification network (RBCN) on grid-based multi-scale feature maps to efficiently recognize target-containing context region proposals while suppressing complex backgrounds. Moreover, we introduce a novel denoising-assisted training strategy that incorporates noisy ground-truth (GT) masks into the feature maps of RBCN and trains the network to reconstruct the GT masks through a denoising task, thereby enhancing its ability to distinguish target proposals from background regions and accelerating convergence. In the fine stage, we customize a lightweight target detector to the coarse stage's region proposals for balancing accuracy and efficiency. Furthermore, we propose a knowledge distillation strategy guided by the teacher-student cross-attention prior. This mechanism directs the student to focus on critical target regions, thereby enhancing the discriminative feature representation for infrared small targets. Extensive experiments on three real infrared datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms both existing single-stage and two-stage approaches while maintaining high real-time processing efficiency.

</details>


### [123] [Look Before You Zoom: Adaptive Routing for the Resolution-Context Trade-off in Visual RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21968)
*Oanh N. Tran,Thanh Quoc Hung Le,Oscar Chew,Kuan-Hao Huang,Khoa D. Doan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ViRGo improves VLM performance on small objects by adaptively routing between global perception, patch-based retrieval, and attention-based retrieval based on object scale and token confidence, improving accuracy-efficiency trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs struggle with small query-relevant objects. Existing training-free retrieval methods (patch zooming, attention retrieval) have trade-offs: patch zooming destroys global context for large objects, while attention retrieval is unreliable for tiny details. Indiscriminately applying retrieval ignores this resolution-context trade-off.

Method: ViRGo is a lightweight framework that formulates visual retrieval as adaptive routing. It estimates object scale from the VLM's intrinsic localization heads during the initial forward pass and combines it with semantic token confidence to select among three modes: global perception, patch-based retrieval, and attention-based retrieval, with minimal extra computation.

Result: Across multiple VQA benchmarks and object-size groups, ViRGo matches patch retrieval on small details, leverages attention-based retrieval for larger objects, and reduces inference time by routing to the global baseline when zooming is unnecessary.

Conclusion: ViRGo effectively addresses the resolution-context trade-off in VLM visual retrieval by adaptively routing between strategies, achieving a better accuracy-efficiency balance than uniform retrieval approaches.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) struggle as query-relevant objects become smaller. To address this, recent training-free approaches dynamically retrieve and zoom into local image regions. However, we show that indiscriminately applying retrieval ignores a critical vulnerability: the resolution-context trade-off. Patch-based zooming recovers details for small targets, but can split large objects and destroy global spatial context; attention-based retrieval better preserves large objects, but remains less reliable on tiny details; and global perception is often fastest when retrieval is unnecessary. Motivated by these failure modes, we introduce ViRGo (Visual Retrieval or Global Perception), a lightweight framework that formulates visual retrieval as an adaptive routing problem. ViRGo estimates object scale from the VLM's intrinsic localization heads during the initial forward pass and combines it with semantic token confidence to select between global perception, patch-based retrieval, and attention-based retrieval with minimal additional computation. Experiments across multiple VQA benchmarks and object-size groups show that ViRGo improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off: it matches patch retrieval on small details, leverages attention-based retrieval for larger objects, and reduces inference time by routing to the global baseline when zooming is unnecessary.

</details>


### [124] [CoDMD: Copula-aware Distribution Matching Distillation for Fast Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21982)
*Wenhu Zhang,Kun Cheng,Changyuan Wang,Shiyao Li,Yuechen Zhang,Wenbo Li,Jiajun Zha,Jingyi Zhang,Kang Zhao,Jiaya Jia*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CoDMD is a lightweight relational regularizer that fixes distribution matching distillation for video diffusion models by enforcing copula structure across samples and frames, achieving 25× speed-up with better quality than prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) degrades under few-step budgets for video generation, causing layout instability, oversaturation, and broken motion dynamics due to its intra-sample objective lacking relational constraints across batch elements or temporal frames.

Method: CoDMD reuses score estimates from frozen teacher and online fake model to construct pairwise relation matrices across samples and frames, then matches them through a supplementary distributional objective—requiring no extra networks, datasets, or sampling trajectories.

Result: On Wan-2.1-T2V (1.3B & 14B), CoDMD distills 50-step teachers into 4-step students (~25× speed-up), achieving VBench scores 84.46 & 84.87, outperforming rCM (82.81 & 84.05) and DMD (83.38 & 83.81).

Conclusion: Explicitly regulating the copula/relational geometry across samples and frames is crucial for few-step video distillation, and CoDMD provides an effective, lightweight solution for this.

Abstract: Few-step distillation for video diffusion models has attracted significant attention, driven by the urgent demand for efficient deployment in real-world scenarios. However, Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD), a leading paradigm, tends to degrade under limited NFE budgets, manifesting in video generation as layout instability, oversaturation, and broken motion dynamics. We trace this failure to a structural limitation: standard DMD is an intra-sample distribution-matching objective with coordinate-wise gradients, and thus imposes no explicit constraint on the relational geometry across batch elements or temporal frames, leaving the underlying copula largely unregulated. Combined with the mode-seeking tendency of its reverse-KL objective, this absence of relational guidance makes DMD prone to collapsing into local optima in the few-step regime. Motivated by this insight, we propose Copula-aware DMD (CoDMD), a lightweight relational regularizer that reuses score estimates already produced by the frozen teacher and the online fake model to construct pairwise relation matrices across samples and frames. These are matched through a supplementary distributional objective that requires no additional networks, datasets, or sampling trajectories. On the Wan-2.1-T2V model series at 1.3B & 14B scales, CoDMD distills 50-step teachers into 4-step students, achieving an approximate 25$\times$ speed-up while attaining VBench scores of 84.46 & 84.87, outperforming prior trajectory-based (rCM 82.81 & 84.05) and distribution-based (DMD 83.38 & 83.81) methods.

</details>


### [125] [One-Shot Data Selection for Medical Image Classification via Graph Coverage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22002)
*Zahiriddin Rustamov,Nadia Badawi,Rafat Damseh,Nazar Zaki*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a graph-based one-shot active learning method that uses frozen foundation model embeddings and a heat diffusion kernel on k-NN graphs to select class-balanced, coverage-maximizing subsets for medical image classification, achieving superior performance across MedMNIST datasets without any model training during selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training medical image classifiers on entire datasets wastes annotation budgets; active learning reduces costs but requires iterative retraining and oracle access, while existing one-shot geometry-based methods ignore local manifold structure.

Method: Uses frozen foundation model embeddings to construct a k-NN graph over all training samples, derives a two-term coverage kernel from the heat diffusion kernel (capturing direct and two-hop neighborhood relationships), and applies greedy facility location on this kernel for class-balanced subset selection.

Result: Achieves highest balanced accuracy on 9 out of 10 dataset-ratio conditions across five MedMNIST datasets, with largest gains on class-imbalanced datasets; matches full spectral heat kernel behavior with reduced computation.

Conclusion: Graph-based one-shot selection with a two-term heat diffusion kernel is an effective, efficient alternative to iterative active learning for medical image annotation, especially under class imbalance.

Abstract: Training medical image classifiers on entire datasets is wasteful when annotation budgets are limited: not all samples contribute equally, yet acquiring expert labels is expensive. Active learning reduces annotation cost through iterative querying, but assumes repeated access to an oracle and requires multiple rounds of model training. One-shot geometry-based methods such as facility location avoid retraining but operate on pairwise distances that ignore the local structure of the data manifold. We propose a graph-based one-shot selection method that operates entirely on frozen foundation model embeddings. Given embeddings from a pretrained encoder, we construct a k-nearest neighbor graph over all training samples and derive a two-term coverage kernel from the heat diffusion kernel, capturing both direct and two-hop neighborhood relationships. Greedy facility location on this kernel selects class-balanced subsets that maximize coverage of the data manifold. The two-term kernel matches the full spectral heat kernel in selection behavior while reducing computation to sparse matrix operations with a single hyperparameter. We evaluate on five MedMNIST datasets spanning histopathology, radiology, and microscopy, comparing against both training-dynamics and geometry-based baselines. Our method achieves the highest balanced accuracy on nine of ten dataset-ratio conditions, with the largest gains on class-imbalanced datasets where global graph construction captures cross-class structure that per-class methods miss, all without any model training during selection. Code is available at https://github.com/zahiriddin-rustamov/graph-coverage-selection.

</details>


### [126] [Topological summaries of fingerprint ridge patterns carry identity information](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22029)
*Chad M. Topaz,Niny Arcila-Maya,Elizabeth Munch,Zofia Stanley,Lori Ziegelmeier*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper replaces traditional minutiae-based fingerprint verification with topological data analysis (persistent homology) to directly capture ridge pattern geometry, achieving competitive results (AUC 0.91) and showing that fusion of topological methods outperforms individual approaches, offering a transparent alternative to standard pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional fingerprint verification relies on a complex multi-stage pipeline (enhancement, skeletonization, minutiae detection, alignment) which can be brittle. The authors explore whether topological data analysis can bypass these steps by directly representing the full ridge/valley pattern.

Method: Applied persistent homology to fingerprint ridge patterns to generate multi-scale topological summaries of loops (cycles). Compared multiple verification approaches: simple topological summaries (no parameters), a trained method (AUC 0.91), an optimal-transport method, and a fusion of the two best methods.

Result: Topological summaries substantially outperform geometry-only baselines. The trained method achieves AUC 0.91, the optimal-transport method excels at strict false-accept thresholds, and fusion yields the best performance across all low false-accept thresholds tested.

Conclusion: Topological summaries capture substantial fingerprint identity information and are far more effective than raw pixel geometry. The approach offers a transparent, openly specified complement to minutiae-based systems, with a modular framework provided for future development.

Abstract: Fingerprints are the most widely deployed biometric. Verifying whether two impressions come from the same finger typically relies on minutiae, small landmarks such as skin ridge endings and bifurcations. These landmarks are extracted through a multi-stage pipeline of image enhancement, skeletonization, minutiae detection, and alignment. We investigate an alternative: using topological data analysis to represent the full pattern of skin ridges and valleys directly, bypassing minutiae detection and the downstream matching pipeline. We apply persistent homology, a topological tool that tracks how loops in the ridge pattern form and fill in across spatial scales, producing multi-scale summaries of ridge geometry. We develop and compare a range of verification methods on a standard benchmark dataset, FVC2000 DB1. Even the simplest topological summaries, with no trained parameters, substantially outperform geometry-only baselines. A trained method achieves an AUC of 0.91, while an optimal-transport method excels at the strictest false-accept thresholds, suggesting they capture different aspects of the ridge pattern. Fusing these two approaches yields the best performance at every low false-accept threshold we examine. Our results establish that these topological summaries capture substantial fingerprint identity information, far more effective for verification than raw pixel-level geometry. Because the entire pipeline is openly specified, it offers a transparent complement to minutiae-based systems, and we provide a modular framework for constructing, evaluating, and combining topological verification methods.

</details>


### [127] [IDAG-Edit: Multi-Object Video Editing via Instance-Decoupled Attention and Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22042)
*Yuan-Zhih Lin,Huu-Thang Nguyen,Huu-Phu Do,Hong-Han Shuai,Ching-Chun Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: IDAGEdit is a training-free framework for fine-grained multi-object video editing that uses Layout-guided Attention Modulation and Instance-level Masks to prevent attention leakage and identity drift, achieving strong temporal consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion-based video editing struggles with precise object-level control in multi-object scenarios due to attention leakage, identity drift, and unstable temporal dynamics.

Method: A training-free framework employing (1) Layout-guided Attention Modulation for coherent multi-object editing, and (2) Instance-level Masks to preserve individual object identity and enforce localized attention within each object region.

Result: Improves temporal stability and multi-object controllability over state-of-the-art video editing approaches, validated by both qualitative and quantitative evaluations.

Conclusion: IDAGEdit enables precise, temporally consistent multi-object video editing without additional training, outperforming existing methods.

Abstract: Diffusion-based video editing has made significant progress; however, achieving precise and temporally consistent object-level control, especially in multi-object scenarios, remains challenging due to attention leakage, identity drift, and unstable temporal dynamics. In this work, we propose IDAGEdit, a training-free framework for fine-grained multi-object video editing with strong temporal consistency. The framework adopts Layout-guided Attention Modulation to facilitate coherent multi-object editing, while Instance-level Masks are introduced to preserve individual object identity and enforce localized attention within each object region, thereby enabling fine-grained, object-level editing. Extensive qualitative and quantitative evaluations demonstrate that our method improves temporal stability and multi-object controllability over state-of-the-art video editing approaches.

</details>


### [128] [A Controlled Study of CLIP-Based Body-Scene Fusion for Emotion Recognition in Context](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22072)
*Zubair Abbas,Muhammad Umair,Muqaddas Hameed*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A two-stream model (ResNet-18 body + CLIP ViT-B/16 scene) for context-aware emotion recognition on EMOTIC achieves 34.52% mAP; no tested de-biasing or rare-class training variant improves over the clean baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Apparent emotion in natural images is often conveyed by body posture and scene context rather than facial expression alone, especially when faces are small, hidden, or neutral.

Method: A ResNet-18 encodes the target-person crop (body stream) and a CLIP ViT-B/16 encodes the full image (scene stream); features are fused to predict 26 emotion categories and continuous VAD values. Variants tested include CCIM-style intervention, CLEF-lite context-bias subtraction, ASL tuning, and class-balanced sampling.

Result: No tested variant improves over the clean two-stream model, which achieves 34.52% mAP on EMOTIC test split. Most remaining errors are in rare and subtle emotion categories.

Conclusion: CLIP provides broad scene semantics but simplified causal, counterfactual, and rare-class methods do not automatically improve performance; future work should focus on label relationships and finer subject-context interaction.

Abstract: Apparent emotion in natural images is often not visible from the face alone. The face may be small, hidden, or neutral, while posture and scene context carry much of the evidence. This work studies context-aware emotion recognition on EMOTIC with an image-only two-stream model. A ResNet-18 body stream encodes the target-person crop, and a CLIP ViT-B/16 scene stream encodes the full image. The fused feature predicts 26 categorical emotion labels and the continuous valence, arousal, and dominance values. This study examines whether small context-debiasing or rare-class training changes still help after adding a CLIP scene encoder. The clean two-stream model is compared with simplified CCIM-style intervention, CLEF-lite context-bias subtraction, ASL tuning, and class-balanced sampling under the same implementation pipeline. No tested variant improves over the clean two-stream model, which achieves 34.52% mAP on the EMOTIC test split. CLIP gives the model broad scene semantics, but the simplified causal, counterfactual, and rare-class changes do not automatically improve performance. Most remaining errors are in rare and subtle emotion categories, so the next step should focus on label relationships and finer subject-context interaction.

</details>


### [129] [Learning Cross-View Semantic Priors for Single-Reference Unseen Object Pose Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22076)
*Jiahong Chen,Jinghao Wang,Ziwen Wang,Zi Wang,Banglei Guan,Qifeng Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper improves single-reference unseen object 6D pose estimation by introducing early cross-view semantic interaction (CVSI) between query and reference VFM tokens, plus two training constraints (IVSP and RAGC losses) to preserve token structure and enforce geometric consistency, achieving SOTA results on six benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing correspondence-based pipelines treat VFM features as intra-view descriptors, failing to exchange dense visual-semantic cues (appearance, structure, context) across views before geometric decoding, making correspondence estimation difficult in challenging cases.

Method: (1) Cross-view semantic interaction (CVSI) for early semantic context exchange between query and reference VFM tokens; (2) Intra-view structure preservation (IVSP) loss to maintain original token affinity structure during CVSI; (3) Reference-anchored geometric consistency (RAGC) loss for spatial representation consistency; (4) Weighted SVD for final pose recovery from learned correspondences.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on six benchmarks (YCB-V, TUD-L, etc.) under different view-pair settings, with comparable inference speed.

Conclusion: Early cross-view semantic prior combined with structure-preserving and geometric-consistency constraints significantly improves unseen object 6D pose estimation robustness, especially in challenging matching scenarios.

Abstract: Single-reference unseen object 6D pose estimation reduces object onboarding by estimating poses of arbitrary novel objects from only one reference view. Recent correspondence-based pipelines have achieved robust performance with vision foundation model (VFM) features. However, they typically treat these features as intra-view descriptors, leaving dense visual-semantic cues, including appearance, structure, and context, insufficiently exchanged across views before geometric decoding. Consequently, the decoded point features may lack joint semantic and geometric discriminability, making correspondence estimation still difficult in challenging cases. Instead of processing features independently, we build the correspondence pipeline around an early cross-view semantic prior. Specifically, cross-view semantic interaction (CVSI) enables dense query and reference VFM tokens to exchange semantic context and form a cross-view prior. Nevertheless, direct CVSI may disturb the VFM token structure, while the resulting semantic prior still needs 3D representation consistency for rigid correspondence. To make this CVSI prior reliable for 3D correspondence learning, we introduce two complementary training-time constraints: the intra-view structure preservation (IVSP) loss preserves the original intra-view token affinity structure during interaction, while the reference-anchored geometric consistency (RAGC) loss enforces spatial representation consistency of decoded point features. The final pose is recovered from learned correspondences through weighted SVD. We further construct a challenging view-pair protocol from the BOP Challenge datasets YCB-V and TUD-L to evaluate robustness in difficult matching scenarios. Extensive experiments on six benchmarks under different view-pair settings show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance while maintaining comparable inference speed.

</details>


### [130] [Morphology-Aware Multimodal Representation Learning for Insect Phylogenetic Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22077)
*Zixuan Liu,Kaijie Yu,Chun He,Xiaoxu Cai,Xinhai Ye,Haishuai Wang,Gongyin Ye,Jiajun Bu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a morphology-aware multimodal alignment framework that combines insect images and text descriptions using vision transformers and contrastive learning to improve phylogenetic reconstruction compared to single-modality approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing image-based phylogenetic methods use single-modality visual features from deep learning but fail to incorporate explicit morphological semantics, limiting their phylogenetic informativeness.

Method: A framework combining specimen images and morphological descriptions via vision transformer fine-tuning, supervised contrastive learning, and image-text alignment in shared latent space; learned embeddings serve as continuous traits for Bayesian phylogenetic reconstruction.

Result: On the Rove-Tree-11 dataset, multimodal alignment outperforms single-modality baselines across various backbones and adaptation strategies, achieving better topological agreement with the reference phylogeny.

Conclusion: The proposed multimodal alignment framework effectively derives morphology-aware visual traits that improve computational phylogenetic reconstruction.

Abstract: Morphological traits provide important evidence for phylogenetic reconstruction and evolutionary relationship analysis. Recent image-based approaches have introduced deep learning, particularly convolutional models, to derive morphological features from specimen images, but these methods generally rely on single-modality visual representations and do not explicitly incorporate morphological semantics. This study proposes a morphology-aware multimodal alignment framework for insect phylogenetic reconstruction. The framework combines specimen images with curated morphological descriptions by adapting a vision transformer through parameter-efficient fine-tuning and supervised contrastive learning, followed by image-text alignment in a shared latent space. The learned image embeddings are then used as continuous traits for Bayesian phylogenetic reconstruction. On the public Rove-Tree-11 dataset, comparative and ablation experiments across multiple visual backbones and feature adaptation strategies demonstrate that multimodal alignment improves topological agreement with the reference phylogeny. The results indicate that the proposed framework can derive morphology-aware visual traits for computational phylogenetic reconstruction.

</details>


### [131] [BAC-JEPA: Label-Efficient Breast Arterial Calcification Segmentation via Synthetic Mammography-Guided Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22089)
*Scott Chase Waggener,Lakshman Tamil*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BAC-JEPA is a label-efficient framework for breast arterial calcification segmentation that uses procedurally generated synthetic mammograms with exact masks, combined with self-supervised Vision Transformers, achieving competitive segmentation (IoU 0.5325, Dice 0.6357) and strong image-level classification (AUROC 0.8719) without requiring human pixel-level annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Breast arterial calcification (BAC) is an emerging cardiovascular risk biomarker, but quantitative segmentation requires costly expert pixel-level labels. The authors aim to develop a label-efficient approach that avoids the expense of manual annotation.

Method: BAC-JEPA generates synthetic mammograms by inserting procedurally generated arterial calcifications (with varied structure, burden, appearance, and hard-negative distractors) into real mammographic backgrounds. It uses self-supervised Vision Transformer encoders (trained on 75,472 studies) paired with a high-resolution convolutional decoder, trained entirely on synthetic images from 10,000 backgrounds.

Result: On synthetic validation: IoU 0.5325, Dice 0.6357. On BacSeg real data: image-level classification AUROC 0.8719 (larger backbone) and 0.8547 (smaller backbone). Four-view inference runs in 110–213 ms on RTX 5090; synthetic generation averages 2.7 s per image.

Conclusion: BAC-specific synthetic supervision enables useful transfer to real mammograms without human pixel-level training masks, though expert-reviewed real-mammogram segmentation remains necessary for clinical validation and calibration.

Abstract: Breast arterial calcification (BAC) on screening mammograms is an emerging cardiovascular risk biomarker, but quantitative use requires reproducible segmentation and expert pixel-level labels are costly. We present BAC-JEPA, a label-efficient segmentation framework trained on procedurally generated arterial calcification inserted into real mammographic backgrounds with exact masks. Candidate backgrounds were selected from model-screened mammograms with low predicted BAC response; the generator samples arterial structure, disease burden, radiographic appearance, and hard-negative distractors including nonarterial calcifications and metallic objects. Synthetic masks are paired with mammography self-supervised Vision Transformer encoders and a high-resolution convolutional decoder to produce full-resolution segmentation maps.
