Experimental results on a large-scale real-world dataset, nuScenes, demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework.

</details>


### [213] [Hybrid Compression: Integrating Pruning and Quantization for Optimized Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22935)
*Minh-Loi Nguyen,Long-Bao Nguyen,Van-Hieu Huynh,Minh-Triet Tran,Trung-Nghia Le*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a two-phase model compression method that first applies pruning and quantization to reduce model size, then uses Mixture of Experts (MoE) to combine compressed models, achieving significant FLOPs/parameter reductions with minimal accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep neural networks face deployment challenges on embedding and edge devices due to limited memory and computational resources, necessitating effective compression techniques that balance model size and performance.

Method: The method has two phases: (1) applying model compression techniques (pruning and quantization) to reduce model size, followed by (2) using Mixture of Experts (MoE) to route among multiple compressed expert models to enhance performance while maintaining inference efficiency.

Result: Experiments on benchmark datasets show substantial reductions in FLOPs and parameters for CNN models with only negligible accuracy drop.

Conclusion: The proposed two-phase approach combining compression with MoE routing effectively addresses the trade-off between model size and performance, making deep neural networks more deployable on resource-constrained devices.

Abstract: Deep neural networks have witnessed remarkable advancements in recent years and have become integral to various applications. However, alongside these developments, training and deployment of neural network models on embedding and edge devices face significant challenges due to limited memory and computational resources. These problems can be addressed with deep neural network compression, which involves a trade-off between model size and performance. In this paper, we propose a novel method for model compression through two phases. First, we utilize model compression techniques, such as pruning and quantization, to significantly reduce the model size. Then, we use Mixture of Experts to route the previously compressed models to enhance performance while maintaining a balance in inference efficiency. MoEs consist of multiple expert models (i.e., compressed models) that are moderately sized and deliver stable performance. Experimental results on several benchmark datasets show that our method successfully compresses CNN models which achieves substantial reductions in FLOPs and parameters with a negligible accuracy drop.

</details>


### [214] [Evaluating self-supervised echocardiographic representations across downstream extraction strategies for left-ventricular segmentation and ejection fraction estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22943)
*Sylwia Majchrowska,Philip Teare*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper shows that how you evaluate self-supervised representations in medical imaging drastically changes conclusions about their quality. Using echocardiography (heart ultrasound), the authors demonstrate that simple heuristic extraction severely underestimates representation quality compared to using frozen lightweight decoders, which can approach supervised baseline performance. They argue that multi-strategy evaluation is essential for SSL in dense medical image analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Representation quality in self-supervised learning for medical imaging is typically judged using a single downstream evaluation setting, which can confound representation quality with the capacity of the downstream model used to recover task-relevant information.

Method: Systematic evaluation comparing a hierarchy of extraction strategies (heuristic extraction, frozen linear probes, frozen lightweight decoder probes, partial fine-tuning) across two representation families (DINOv3 and BYOS) for left-ventricular segmentation and ejection fraction estimation from echocardiography on EchoNet-Dynamic.

Result: Heuristic extraction substantially understated recoverable information. DINOv3 improved from Dice 0.684/EF MAE 13.01 to Dice 0.906/EF MAE 9.65 with a frozen decoder. BYOS improved from Dice 0.687/EF MAE 17.83 to Dice 0.902/EF MAE 8.74. Both approached supervised U-Net baseline (Dice 0.915, EF MAE 9.72).

Conclusion: Conclusions about self-supervised representation quality in dense echocardiographic analysis depend strongly on the downstream extraction strategy used for evaluation. Multi-strategy evaluation is an important methodological consideration for SSL in dense medical image analysis.

Abstract: Self-supervised learning (SSL) is increasingly used in medical imaging to reduce annotation requirements, but representation quality is often judged using a single downstream evaluation setting. For dense clinical tasks, this can confound representation quality with the capacity of the downstream model used to recover task-relevant information. We present a systematic evaluation of self-supervised representations for left-ventricular segmentation and ejection fraction (EF) estimation from apical four-chamber echocardiography on EchoNet-Dynamic. Rather than relying on a single downstream probe, we compare a hierarchy of extraction strategies with increasing expressivity: heuristic extraction without mask-supervised training, frozen linear probes, frozen lightweight decoder probes, and partial fine-tuning. We apply this framework to two complementary representation families: generic frozen self-DIstillation with NO labels (DINOv3) features and a task-adapted dense self-supervised representation, Bootstrap Your Own Segmentation (BYOS). In both families, heuristic extraction substantially understated what was recoverable from the frozen representation. For DINOv3, performance improved from Dice 0.684 and EF mean absolute error (MAE) 13.01 under heuristic extraction to Dice 0.906 and EF MAE 9.65 with a frozen lightweight decoder, approaching a supervised U-Net baseline (Dice 0.915, EF MAE 9.72). For BYOS, performance improved from Dice 0.687 and EF MAE 17.83 under heuristic extraction to Dice 0.902 and EF MAE 8.74 with a frozen lightweight decoder. These results show that conclusions about self-supervised representation quality in dense echocardiographic analysis depend strongly on the downstream extraction strategy used for evaluation. We therefore argue that multi-strategy evaluation is an important methodological consideration for SSL in dense medical image analysis.

</details>


### [215] [Evo-RAD: Navigating Rare Retinal Disease Diagnosis via Self-Evolving Agentic Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22955)
*Wangding Xia,Ye Du,Jiashi Lin,Meng Wang,Danli Shi,Shujun Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Evo-RAD is a self-evolving agentic framework for rare disease diagnosis that transforms retrieval-augmented diagnosis into a dynamic Markov Decision Process, using GRPO optimization to improve diagnostic homogeneity and outperforming existing methods by +3.56% to +21.04% on retinal disease benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale foundation models struggle with rare diseases due to underrepresentation in clinical datasets, and conventional static retrieval methods suffer from the hubness problem (retrieving visually similar but semantically incorrect common diseases).

Method: Formulates retrieval as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) where a graph-based agent can DELETE discordant evidence, INSERT pathologically consistent samples, or TERMINATE the process. Optimized via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a homogeneity-aware reward to maximize diagnostic homogeneity of the support reference set.

Result: On retinal disease benchmarks, Evo-RAD improves rare-disease diagnosis by +21.04% over retinal foundation models and surpasses retrieval-based and parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods by +3.56%.

Conclusion: The self-evolving agentic framework effectively addresses the hubness problem in rare disease diagnosis through dynamic evidence acquisition, achieving state-of-the-art results on retinal disease benchmarks.

Abstract: Large-scale pretrained foundation models have revolutionized general medical screening, but often falter on rare diseases because such conditions are underrepresented in real-world clinical datasets. While retrieval-augmented diagnosis attempts to mitigate this, conventional static methods frequently succumb to the hubness problem, retrieving visually similar but semantically incorrect common diseases. To address this, we propose Evo-RAD, a self-evolving agentic framework that transforms evidence acquisition into a dynamic decision-making task. We formulate retrieval as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) where a graphbased agent observes the reference set state and executes actions to purge discordant evidence (DELETE), acquire pathologically consistent samples (INSERT), or conclude the evolution (TERMINATE). Optimized via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a homogeneityaware reward, the agent learns to maximize the diagnostic homogeneity of the support reference set. Experiments on retinal disease benchmarks show that Evo-RAD substantially improves rare-disease diagnosis, outperforming retinal foundation models by +21.04%, while also surpassing retrieval-based and parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods by +3.56%. Code is available at https://github.com/SDH-Lab/Evo-RAD.

</details>


### [216] [Concept Alignment Contrast and Long-Short Prompt Memory for Test-Time Adaptation of SAM3 in Medical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22963)
*Yubo Zhou,Jianghao Wu,Ping Ye,Shaoting Zhang,Guotai Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CM-TTA adapts SAM3 for medical image segmentation via Test-Time Adaptation using Concept Alignment Contrast (CAC) metric and Long-Short Prompt Memory (LSPM), outperforming existing methods on prostate and skin lesion tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SAM3 generalizes well on natural images but degrades on medical images due to domain gap. Existing TTA methods use image-level uncertainty which fails for region-level semantic correctness in segmentation, and lack stability in one-pass adaptation without dense supervision.

Method: 1) Concept Alignment Contrast (CAC): uses textual-visual semantic consistency across augmented views to select best supervision. 2) Long-Short Prompt Memory (LSPM): short memory for agile local adaptation, long memory for stable global pseudo-labels. 3) Densely Supervised Prompt Update (DSPU): optimizes prompt embeddings using enhanced pseudo-labels.

Result: Extensive experiments on prostate and skin lesion segmentation show CM-TTA significantly outperforms existing TTA methods for SAM3 adaptation.

Conclusion: The proposed CM-TTA framework effectively addresses domain gap in medical image segmentation through concept-level alignment and balanced adaptation memory, achieving state-of-the-art TTA performance for SAM3.

Abstract: Concept segmentation models like Segment Anything Model 3 (SAM3) show strong generalization on natural images, yet their performance degrades in medical imaging due to the domain gap caused by different imaging principles and styles. Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) is essential for improving the testing performance by updating the model on the fly without annotations. However, existing vision-language TTA methods are mainly driven by image-level uncertainty minimization, which does not necessarily reflect region-level semantic correctness in medical segmentation. Moreover, they often lack mechanisms to maintain stability in continual one-pass adaptation, leading to limited performance when reliable dense supervision is missing for segmentation. To address these issues, we propose Concept Alignment Contrast and LongShort Prompt Memory for Test-Time Adaptation (CM-TTA) of SAM3 for medical images. First, for a test sample with multiple augmentations, we introduce a novel Concept Alignment Contrast (CAC) metric, which leverages textual-visual semantic consistency to robustly evaluate prediction quality to select the best augmented view as the supervision. Second, to balance rapid and stable adaptation, we design a Long-Short Prompt Memory (LSPM) module. The short memory dynamically fuses recent prompts based on CAC scores for agile local adaptation, while the long memory maintains a stable global prompt to generate enhanced pseudo-labels. Finally, a Densely Supervised Prompt Update (DSPU) strategy is proposed to optimize the prompt embeddings with enhanced pseudo labels as dense supervision. Extensive experiments on prostate and skin lesion segmentation demonstrate that our CM-TTA framework significantly outperforms existing methods for TTA of SAM3.

</details>


### [217] [Subject-Level Unknown-Identity Identification from Leap Motion Controller 2 Hand Landmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22986)
*Bahar Moharrer,Susanna Cifani,Marco Raoul Marini,Luigi Cinque,Maria De Marsico*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper evaluates subject recognition using Leap Motion Controller 2 hand landmarks under an open-set (unknown-identity) protocol on the ML2HP dataset, finding that Extra Trees outperforms neural methods due to better score separation between known and unknown subjects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To study subject recognition from Leap Motion Controller 2 hand landmark data under a realistic unknown-identity identification protocol, where subjects not seen during enrollment must be rejected at test time.

Method: Uses only landmark modality with fingertip-to-palm distances and palm-normalized inter-finger angular descriptors. Evaluates under Leave-One-Subject-Out (LOSO) with inner validation for threshold selection. Compares Extra Trees, centroid-matching embedding baseline, and MLP+OpenMax.

Result: Extra Trees achieves the best performance overall, outperforming neural alternatives. The main challenge is not discriminating known enrolled subjects, but achieving robust score separation between known and unknown probes.

Conclusion: Compact, interpretable landmark-based descriptors are feasible for contactless hand-based unknown-subject rejection and identification on small-cohort datasets.

Abstract: This work studies subject recognition from Leap Motion Controller 2 (LMC2) hand landmark data under a subject-level unknown-identity identification protocol on the Multi View Leap2 Hand Pose (ML2HP) dataset. Using only the landmark modality, we retain the original geometric representation and enrich it with fingertip-to-palm distances and palm-normalized inter-finger angular descriptors. Evaluation is performed under a Leave-One-Subject-Out (LOSO) protocol in which, for each outer fold, one subject is excluded from the enrolled set and treated as unknown at test time. To avoid tuning on the true outer unknown subject, the unknown-rejection threshold is selected in an inner validation step by temporarily withholding one enrolled subject from the inner gallery and using it only for threshold estimation. We compare a tree ensemble baseline with two neural alternatives: a learned embedding baseline based on centroid matching and cosine-similarity-based rejection, and an MLP+OpenMax model, which represents a more established open-set recognition approach. Under this evaluation setup, Extra Trees remains the strongest overall method, indicating that the main challenge on this benchmark is not enrolled-subject discrimination alone, but robust score separation between known and unknown probes. The results support the feasibility of compact, interpretable landmark-based descriptors for contactless hand-based unknown-subject rejection and identification on a small-cohort dataset.

</details>


### [218] [Black-Box Continual Learning for Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22999)
*Yuting Li,Weihang Fang,Haoyuan Gao,Linghe Kong,Yexin Li,Lichao Sun,Weiran Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces Black-CL, a realistic black-box continual learning benchmark for VLMs, and proposes BETA—a parameter-efficient method using only textual prototype optimization—that achieves white-box-level performance with 180–3000× fewer trainable parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional continual learning relies on white-box paradigms (access to gradients, weights, architecture), which is unrealistic for modern cloud-hosted VLMs. The authors aim to address this gap by defining a more practical black-box setting with weight/architecture inaccessibility, constrained computation, and task-agnostic inference.

Method: BETA has three components: (1) Semantic Projection Accumulation (SPA) for incremental knowledge acquisition, (2) Latent Distribution Replay (LDR) to anchor embedding space against forgetting, and (3) Test-Time Prototype Adaptation (TTPA) for dynamic instance-aware boundary refinement. It optimizes only textual prototypes without backbone backpropagation or parameter expansion.

Result: Across 10 diverse datasets and various backbones, BETA significantly outperforms existing black-box tuners. With only 0.05M trainable parameters (180–3000× reduction vs. competitors), it achieves performance on par with or exceeding white-box CL methods.

Conclusion: Black-CL and BETA provide a foundational framework for black-box continual learning in VLMs, bridging the gap between academic CL research and real-world deployment scenarios.

Abstract: The rapid deployment of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) in dynamic environments necessitates the ability to learn continuously without forgetting. However, traditional continual learning (CL) settings often rely on white-box paradigms, which is increasingly invalidated by the shift toward cloud-hosted models. In this paper, we introduce Black-CL, a more realistic benchmark for VLMs that enforces three primary real-world challenges: weight and architecture inaccessibility, constrained computation, and task-agnostic inference. The learner can query only output embeddings or logits, with no gradient flow through or structural modification of the backbone. Current CL methodologies, which rely on backbone backpropagation or complex parameter expansion, are fundamentally incompatible with these constraints. Under this setting, we propose BETA, a simple yet effective baseline built on the key insight that solely optimizing textual prototypes can navigate the complexities of CL. BETA integrates three core components: Semantic Projection Accumulation (SPA) for incremental knowledge acquisition, Latent Distribution Replay (LDR) for anchoring the embedding space against catastrophic forgetting, and Test-Time Prototype Adaptation (TTPA) for dynamic, instance-aware boundary refinement. Extensive experiments across ten diverse datasets and various backbones demonstrate that BETA significantly outperforms existing black-box tuners. Remarkably, with only 0.05 M trainable parameters, a 180--3000$\times$ reduction compared to competitive methods, BETA achieves performance on par with or even exceeding white-box CL methods. We believe Black-CL and BETA provide a foundational framework for future advancements in continual learning and accelerates the transition of continual learning from academia to real-world systems.

</details>


### [219] [MotionMAR: Multi-scale Auto-Regressive Human Motion Reconstruction from Sparse Observations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23000)
*Yuhua Luo,Junsheng Zhang,Mengyin Liu,Xincheng Lin,Ming Yan,Zhudi Chen,Chenglu Wen,Lan Xu,Siqi Shen,Cheng Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MotionMAR is a coarse-to-fine multi-level autoregressive framework that reconstructs human motion from sparse observations by first estimating global trajectories and then refining temporal details, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy on AMASS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human motion has a temporal hierarchical structure (low-frequency global to high-frequency details), but existing methods don't explicitly model this coarse-to-fine hierarchy for motion reconstruction from sparse observations.

Method: Four components: (1) TMT VQ-VAE encodes motion at multiple temporal resolutions; (2) MAN autoregressively predicts coarse-to-fine latent indices; (3) SAC integrates sparse tracking data for real-world alignment; (4) MRN smooths poses and removes quantization artifacts.

Result: State-of-the-art accuracy on the AMASS dataset for motion reconstruction from sparse observations.

Conclusion: The coarse-to-fine hierarchical approach provides a reliable and structure-aware framework for human motion reconstruction, with open-source code available.

Abstract: Human motion follows a temporal hierarchical structure, transitioning from low-frequency global trajectories to high-frequency details. Inspired by the success of multi-level autoregressive models in computer vision, we propose MotionMAR, a coarse-to-fine framework for motion reconstruction from sparse observations. It first estimates the global trajectory of human motion and then gradually refines the temporal details. This architecture consists of four integrated components. The Temporal Multi-scale Tokenization (TMT) VQ-VAE encodes the data at multiple temporal resolutions, separating semantic motion from minor jitters. The Motion Autoregressive Network (MAN) operates in this latent space, predicting motion across scales. It first establishes the global structure through coarse indices and then generates finer indices to recover specific details. Meanwhile, the Scale-Aware Control (SAC) module integrates sparse tracking data to ensure the generated output aligns with actual observations. The Motion Refinement Network (MRN) subsequently smooths consecutive poses and eliminates quantization artifacts. Experiments show that MotionMAR achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on the AMASS dataset, providing a reliable and structure-aware approach for motion reconstruction. The source code is publicly available at http://www.lidarhumanmotion.net/motionmar/.

</details>


### [220] [From Point Estimates to Distributions: GMM Pooling for MIL in Preterm Birth Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23005)
*Hussain Alasmawi,Numan Saeed,Soha Said,Mohammad Yaqub*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) pooling for preterm birth prediction from multiple transvaginal ultrasound images per patient, formulated as a multiple instance learning problem. GMM pooling models the feature distribution of images in a bag, improving PR-AUC from 0.44 to 0.56 for PTB prediction and achieving state-of-the-art on a lymph node benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing ultrasound-based PTB prediction models use only a single selected frame per patient, ignoring the multiple cervical images routinely acquired. This discards valuable intra-patient variability information.

Method: Formulates PTB prediction as a MIL problem with variable-sized bags of TVUS images per patient. Proposes GMM pooling that summarizes all images in a bag into a fixed-length representation by modeling their feature distribution as a Gaussian Mixture Model, capturing intra-patient variability beyond standard point-estimate aggregators.

Result: GMM pooling improves PTB prediction PR-AUC from 0.44 to 0.56. On lymph node metastasis benchmark: 0.91 F1, 0.89 ROC-AUC for classification, 0.18 MAE for regression, achieving state-of-the-art.

Conclusion: Modeling the distribution of features across multiple images per patient via GMM pooling improves PTB prediction and generalizes well to other medical imaging tasks like lymph node metastasis analysis.

Abstract: Preterm birth (PTB) prediction can enable targeted surveillance and timely intervention, yet most ultrasound-based models use a single selected transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) frame per patient despite routine exams acquiring multiple cervical images. We formulate PTB prediction as a multiple instance learning (MIL) problem, representing each patient as a variable-sized bag of TVUS images with a single outcome label. To move beyond standard MIL aggregators that collapse a bag into a point estimate, we propose a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) pooling, which summarizes all images in a bag into a fixed-length representation by modeling their feature distribution. This design captures intra-patient variability. We evaluate the method on a private clinical cohort and on a public lymph node metastasis benchmark. For PTB prediction, GMM pooling improves over the instance-based model PR-AUC from 0.44 to 0.56. On the lymph node benchmark, it achieves state-of-the-art performance with 0.91 F1-score and 0.89 ROC-AUC for classification and 0.18 MAE for regression. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/HussainAlasmawi/GMM_Pooling.

</details>


### [221] [ScalingAttention: Discovering Intrinsic Sparse Attention Topology for Video Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23019)
*Ruiliang Zhou,Xuecheng Wu,Kang He,Guangyun Han,Bin Liu,Qinqin Chen,Wende Xu,Qingjie Zhao,Chengru Song*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ScalingAttention is a training-free framework that accelerates Diffusion Transformers for video generation by exploiting a key insight: attention heads have stable, weight-encoded sparse topologies. It decouples topology discovery (WEST) from sparsity control (FAST) and uses a custom hardware-aligned kernel, achieving up to 1.90X speedup with superior fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) achieve high-fidelity video generation but suffer from quadratic computational cost due to 3D full attention. Existing sparse methods are unsatisfactory: dynamic pruning introduces runtime overhead and memory fragmentation, while static heuristics miss fine-grained dependencies.

Method: ScalingAttention introduces two components: (1) WEST (Weight-Encoded Sparse Topology) extracts a stable, block-sparse prior mask offline from the model weights, eliminating runtime search; (2) FAST (Fidelity-Aware Sensitivity Tuning) adaptively adjusts per-head sparsity levels based on diffusion fidelity requirements. A hardware-aligned bit-wise block-sparse kernel is co-designed for practical acceleration.

Result: On Wan2.1, ScalingAttention achieves up to 1.90X end-to-end speedup while maintaining superior fidelity, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines and establishing a new Pareto frontier.

Conclusion: ScalingAttention demonstrates that attention heads in DiTs converge to input-agnostic intrinsic sparse topologies encoded in weights, enabling efficient offline pruning. The training-free, hardware-aligned design achieves significant speedup without sacrificing quality.

Abstract: While Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) have revolutionized high-fidelity video generation, their reliance on 3D full attention creates a quadratic computational bottleneck. Existing sparse methods face a dilemma: dynamic pruning suffers from prohibitive runtime overhead and memory fragmentation, while static heuristics fail to capture fine-grained dependencies. In this work, we propose ScalingAttention, a training-free framework grounded in a key inductive bias: while individual activations are input-dependent, the high-mass attention regions for each head rapidly converge to a stable, prompt-agnostic Intrinsic Sparse Topology. This topology is weight-encoded, scale-invariant, and efficient to extract. ScalingAttention decouples topology discovery from sparsity control via: (1) WEST (Weight-Encoded Sparse Topology), which extracts a robust block-sparse prior mask offline to eliminate runtime search; (2) FAST (Fidelity-Aware Sensitivity Tuning), which adaptively tunes head-wise sparsity based on diffusion fidelity requirements. To ensure practical acceleration, we co-design a hardware-aligned bit-wise block-sparse kernel. Experiments on Wan2.1 show up to 1.90X end-to-end speedup with superior fidelity, establishing a new Pareto frontier over state-of-the-art baselines.

</details>


### [222] [Boosting Neural Video Codec via Scale-Driven Online Flow Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23023)
*Tiange Zhang,Rongqun Lin,Haocheng Tang,Xiandong Meng,Weijia Jiang,Zhimeng Huang,Siwei Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SOFR is a training-free, plug-and-play module that refines optical flow in neural video codecs by fusing coarse and fine-scale motion information dynamically, achieving significant bitrate savings without extra training or high computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: State-of-the-art neural video codecs (NVCs) struggle with generalization to unseen complex motion patterns; online fine-tuning is expensive, so a cost-effective solution is needed.

Method: A training-free, scale-driven online flow refinement (SOFR) module that integrates coarse and fine-scale motion, dynamically fuses them based on warping accuracy, and uses a rate-aware strategy with reliability checks to ensure robustness.

Result: On USTC-TD dataset, SOFR brings 2.84% (PSNR) and 4.05% (MS-SSIM) average bitrate savings to DCVC-FM with negligible coding time increase, and generalizes across multiple NVC frameworks.

Conclusion: SOFR effectively bridges the domain gap for NVCs on complex motions without training or fine-tuning, offering a lightweight and generalizable plug-and-play solution for flow refinement.

Abstract: Although state-of-the-art neural video codecs (NVCs) have achieved remarkable performance, they suffer from limited generalization when encountering complex motion patterns unseen during training. To bridge this domain gap without the expensive cost of online fine-tuning, we propose a Training-Free Scale-Driven Online Flow Refinement (SOFR) method. Serving as a plug-and-play module, SOFR integrates motion information from coarse and fine scales and dynamically fuses them according to warping accuracy, effectively rectifying motion estimation errors with negligible computational overhead. Furthermore, we design a rate-aware strategy that selects different dynamic fusion strategies according to bitrate modes, and employs a reliability check based on warping error to ensure robustness. Extensive experiments on the USTC-TD dataset verify the effectiveness and generalization of SOFR across various NVC frameworks, including DCVC-SDD, DCVC-FM, and EHVC. Notably, it brings an average of 2.84% and 4.05% bitrate savings in terms of PSNR and MS-SSIM, respectively, to DCVC-FM with negligible coding time increase. Our code is available at https://github.com/SunnyMass/SOFR.

</details>


### [223] [Learning Stable Canonical Worlds for Novel View Synthesis and Beyond](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23027)
*Xiaoyu Xu,Jian Zou,Sheyang Tang,Zhihua Wang,Jing Liao,Kede Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CanonicalGS is a feed-forward Gaussian splatting pipeline that aggregates multi-view observations into a stable, scene-centric representation using uncertainty-aware fusion, achieving up to 2.5 dB PSNR improvement in novel view synthesis and 11% gain in semantic segmentation accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current FFGS methods tend to accumulate noisy or redundant evidence as more input views are added, and remain tied to view-dependent predictions rather than converging to a stable scene representation.

Method: CanonicalGS extracts view-centric evidence from depth, semantic features, and uncertainty estimates, then aggregates them in a canonical latent world using uncertainty-aware fusion to emphasize reliable observations and suppress uncertain/redundant ones.

Result: Up to 2.5 dB improvement in PSNR for novel view synthesis and 11% improvement in semantic segmentation accuracy.

Conclusion: CanonicalGS produces representations that scale more effectively for novel view synthesis and transfer well to downstream visual perception tasks.

Abstract: Feed-forward Gaussian splatting (FFGS) facilitates real-time novel view synthesis, yet current methods often remain tied to view-dependent predictions. As more input views are added, they may accumulate noisy or redundant evidence instead of converging to a stable scene representation. In this paper, we introduce CanonicalGS, a feed-forward pipeline that maps cluttered multi-view observations into a stable, scene-centric representation. CanonicalGS first extracts view-centric evidence from depth, semantic features, and uncertainty estimates, and then aggregates this evidence in a canonical latent world using uncertainty-aware fusion. By emphasizing reliable observations while suppressing uncertain or redundant ones, CanonicalGS produces representations that scale more effectively for novel view synthesis and transfer to downstream visual perception tasks. Experiments show up to a $2.5$ dB improvement in peak signal-to-noise ratio for synthesizing novel views and an $11\%$ gain in semantic segmentation accuracy.

</details>


### [224] [Physics-Guided Spatiotemporal State Space Modeling for Lookahead Molten Pool Segmentation in Laser Wire-Feed Welding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23028)
*Sen Li,Haichao Cui,Changhao Yin,Chendong Shao,Yaqi Wang,Xinhua Tang,Fenggui Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes WeldMamba, a physics-guided spatiotemporal network that uses historical weld-pool imagery, process parameters, and electrical signals to predict future weld-pool segmentation (keyhole, wire, molten pool) at 500 ms lookahead, achieving 74.63% mIoU.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time weld-pool perception for closed-loop control in laser wire-feed welding is critical but suffers from unavoidable delays due to sensing, computation, and actuator response. The authors aim to predict future weld-pool states to compensate for these delays.

Method: The model (WeldMamba) combines a visual encoder, process- and sensor-conditioned feature normalization, patch-level temporal state space modeling, horizon-conditioned latent prediction, dense future feature prediction, and a motion-aware mask decoder. It uses auxiliary supervision including signed-distance-function, temporal consistency, feature distillation, and fine-grained keyhole losses.

Result: On a 43-sequence laser welding dataset, WeldMamba achieves 74.63% mIoU at 500 ms lookahead. Ablation studies show temporal history, patch-level state space modeling, and keyhole motion awareness are the key contributors.

Conclusion: The proposed physics-guided spatiotemporal approach effectively predicts future weld-pool segmentation, enabling lookahead perception for delay-compensated closed-loop welding control.

Abstract: Real-time weld-pool perception is critical for closed-loop control in laser wire-feed welding, where sensing, computation, and actuator response introduce unavoidable delay. This paper presents a physics-guided spatiotemporal state space network for lookahead weld-pool segmentation. The model uses historical coaxial grayscale images, welding process parameters, and aligned wire-state electrical signals to predict the future semantic layout of three physically meaningful regions: keyhole, wire, and molten pool. It combines a visual encoder, process- and sensor-conditioned feature normalization, patch-level temporal state space modeling, horizon-conditioned latent prediction, dense future feature prediction, and a motion-aware mask decoder. Auxiliary signed-distance-function supervision, temporal consistency, feature distillation, and fine-grained keyhole losses further constrain the predicted geometry and local motion. Experiments on a 43-sequence laser welding dataset show that the proposed WeldMamba reaches 74.63\% mIoU at a 500 ms lookahead. Ablation studies further show that temporal history, patch-level state space modeling, and keyhole motion awareness are the main contributors to robust future segmentation.

</details>


### [225] [DrivingVoxels: Compositional Sparse Voxel Rasterization for Dynamic Driving Scene Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23031)
*Tania Aguirre,Luis Roldão,Moussab Bennehar,Nathan Piasco,Dzmitry Tsishkou,Simone Rossi,Pietro Michiardi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DrivingVoxels is a compositional sparse voxel rendering framework for dynamic driving scenes that uses multiple independent octrees (one per dynamic object plus static background) with LiDAR-guided initialization, achieving competitive perceptual quality and better structural metrics than 3DGS methods while requiring shorter training times.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods struggle with dynamic urban scene reconstruction: sparse voxel methods are fast but designed for static scenes, while 3D Gaussian Splatting methods achieve high fidelity but are time-consuming and suffer from uncontrollable memory growth in large driving scenarios.

Method: A compositional sparse voxel rendering framework that jointly rasterizes sparse voxels from multiple independent octrees in a single rendering pass. Each rigid dynamic object has its own octree in its local coordinate frame, plus a separate static octree. Uses fully explicit, neural-free representation with LiDAR-guided structural initialization.

Result: Evaluated on PandaSet benchmark, DrivingVoxels performs on par on perceptual metrics and better on structural metrics for novel view synthesis and reconstruction, while requiring shorter training times than previous 3DGS-based methods.

Conclusion: The proposed compositional sparse voxel approach effectively addresses the trade-off between reconstruction quality and efficiency for dynamic driving scenes, leveraging LiDAR priors to accelerate optimization.

Abstract: Reconstructing dynamic urban scenes remains challenging due to the unbounded nature of driving environments and the presence of multiple dynamic objects. Currently, potentially faster sparse voxel methods are mainly designed for static scenarios. On the other hand, dynamic approaches based on 3D Gaussian Splatting, despite their high-fidelity, are often time-consuming for driving scenarios and exhibit uncontrollable memory growth in large scenes. To address these limitations, we present DrivingVoxels, a compositional sparse voxel rendering framework for dynamic driving scenes. Our method jointly rasterizes sparse voxels from multiple independent octrees within a single rendering pass. Each rigid dynamic object is represented by an octree defined in its local coordinate frame, while a separate static octree models the stationary background. DrivingVoxels adopts a fully explicit, neural-free representation together with a LiDAR-guided structural initialization that efficiently captures scene geometry. We evaluate our framework on the PandaSet benchmark, demonstrating that DrivingVoxels performs on par on perceptual metrics and better on structural metrics for NVS and reconstruction while requiring shorter training times than previous 3DGS-base methods to an efficient optimization workflow anchored by a strong LiDAR prior.

</details>


### [226] [SPAR: Semantic-Pixel Self-Alignment and Adaptive Routing for Unified Multimodal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23041)
*Hongxiang Li,Hongxu Chen,Chenyang Zhu,Xiaoshuang Huang,Jiayin Cai,Xiaolong Jiang,Yao Hu,Long Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SPAR proposes a unified multimodal framework with semantic-pixel self-alignment and adaptive routing, achieving state-of-the-art generation and reconstruction quality while preserving visual understanding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs excel at visual understanding but struggle with visual generation due to the fundamental discrepancy between semantic perception (classification-level features) and pixel-level reconstruction (fine-grained details). Existing approaches rely on external teacher models, creating dependencies.

Method: (1) An asymmetric dual-stream unified tokenizer (semantic stream + Transformer-augmented pixel stream) to reconcile perception and reconstruction. (2) Self-aligned generation paradigm using the tokenizer as an internal teacher for diffusion models. (3) Dynamic Token Routing for adaptive multi-layer MLLM feature aggregation.

Result: SPAR achieves state-of-the-art results among unified architectures, delivering exceptional generation and reconstruction quality while maintaining visual understanding capabilities.

Conclusion: The proposed SPAR framework effectively bridges the semantic-pixel gap in MLLMs without external teacher dependencies, offering a scalable unified architecture for both understanding and generation tasks.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable success in visual understanding but remain constrained in visual generation due to the fundamental feature discrepancy between semantic perception and pixel-level reconstruction. Bridging this gap requires overcoming two core challenges: endowing semantic encoders with high-fidelity reconstruction capabilities, and effectively aligning generative models with semantic spaces without relying on external teachers. To this end, we propose a novel unified multimodal framework featuring \textbf{S}emantic-\textbf{P}ixel self-alignment and \textbf{A}daptive \textbf{R}outing (\textbf{SPAR}). First, to reconcile semantic perception with pixel-level reconstruction, we introduce an asymmetric dual-stream unified tokenizer. A lightweight semantic stream anchors discriminative features, while a Transformer-augmented pixel stream recovers fine-grained visual details into a unified compact latent space. Second, to eliminate external dependencies, we propose a self-aligned generation paradigm that natively leverages this optimized tokenizer as an internal alignment teacher for the diffusion model. Furthermore, to facilitate flexible multimodal interaction within this unified space, we introduce Dynamic Token Routing, which enables each token to adaptively aggregate multi-layer MLLM features based on its distinct semantic demands. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SPAR establishes the state-of-the-art for unified architectures, achieving exceptional generation and reconstruction quality while preserving foundational visual understanding capabilities.

</details>


### [227] [UECP: Uncertainty-Enhanced Collaborative Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23046)
*Kang Yang,Tianci Bu,Peng Wang,Deying Li,Wen Jie,Yongcai Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes UECP, a collaborative perception framework that uses a physically-grounded uncertainty map (supervised by LiDAR point density) rather than a co-trained confidence map to weight agent contributions, featuring Uncertainty-Aware Pyramid Fusion (UAPF) with coarse-to-fine Uncertainty-Weighted Downsampling (UWD) and Uncertainty-Guided Residual Fusion (UGRF) for robust fusion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing confidence maps are co-trained with detection heads, producing biased evidence correlated with detection noise. There is a need for unbiased, physically-grounded evidence to quantify agent contributions, and deeper integration of such evidence into cooperative fusion.

Method: (1) Proposes an uncertainty map directly supervised by real-time LiDAR point density, decoupled from detection noise. (2) Develops UECP framework with Uncertainty-Aware Pyramid Fusion (UAPF), comprising Uncertainty-Weighted Downsampling (UWD) for feature preservation and Uncertainty-Guided Residual Fusion (UGRF) for noise suppression and robust fusion.

Result: Extensive experiments on real-world datasets show UECP outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both effectiveness and robustness.

Conclusion: Embedding a physically-grounded uncertainty map into collaborative fusion significantly improves perception quality and robustness over confidence-based methods.

Abstract: Collaborative perception serves as a pivotal solution to enhance the perception capability of individual agents in autonomous driving, where a core challenge lies in seeking reliable evidence to quantify and weight the contribution of each participating agent. Existing methods typically rely on a confidence map, which is co-trained with the detection head, but it is inherently correlated with the detection results and thus fails to provide unbiased physical evidence. Furthermore, how to deeply integrate evidence into the cooperative fusion process remains an open question. To address these issues, this paper first proposes an uncertainty map, a physically grounded and unambiguous metric for evaluating perception quality. This map is directly supervised by real-time sensor signals, i.e., LiDAR point density, ensuring decoupling from detection noise and thereby providing physical scenario-aware evidence for weighting agent contribution. Based on this map, we develop the Uncertainty-Enhanced Collaborative Perception (UECP) framework, centered on the Uncertainty-Aware Pyramid Fusion (UAPF) module. UAPF uses a coarse-to-fine strategy, with two key components: Uncertainty-Weighted Downsampling (UWD) for high-fidelity feature preservation, and Uncertainty-Guided Residual Fusion (UGRF) to reinforce ego features, suppressing noise and ensuring robust fusion. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets show UECP outperforms state-of-the-art methods in effectiveness and robustness by embedding the uncertainty map into fusion. Code will be publicly available.

</details>


### [228] [Unlimited OCR Works](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23050)
*Youyang Yin,Huanhuan Liu,YY,Qunyi Xie,Chaorun Liu,Shiqi Yang,Shaohua Wang,Zhanlong Liu,Hao Zou,Jinyue Chen,Shu Wei,Jingjing Wu,Mingxin Huang,Zhen Wu,Guibin Wang,Tengyu Du,Lei Jia*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Unlimited OCR replaces standard attention with Reference Sliding Window Attention (R-SWA) to maintain constant KV cache and computational cost during long-sequence OCR decoding, enabling transcription of dozens of pages in a single 32K-token forward pass.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based OCR decoders suffer from growing KV cache and slowing generation as output sequence lengthens, unlike humans who maintain efficiency during long copying tasks.

Method: Proposes Reference Sliding Window Attention (R-SWA) to replace all attention layers in the decoder, keeping KV cache constant throughout decoding while reducing attention computation costs.

Result: Can transcribe dozens of pages in a single forward pass under standard 32K max length, with constant KV cache and reduced computation.

Conclusion: R-SWA is a general-purpose parsing attention mechanism applicable beyond OCR to tasks like ASR and translation; codes and weights are publicly available.

Abstract: Recently, end-to-end OCR models, exemplified by DeepSeek OCR, have once again thrust OCR into the spotlight. A widely held view is that employing a large language model (LLM) as the decoder allows the model to leverage the prior distribution of language, leading to improved OCR performance. However, the downside is equally evident: as the output sequence lengthens, the accumulated KV cache drives up memory consumption and progressively slows down generation. This stands in stark contrast to humans, who exhibit no such decline in efficiency during long-horizon copying tasks. In this technical report, we propose Unlimited OCR, a model designed to emulate human parsing working memory. Taking DeepSeek OCR as the baseline, we replace all attention layers in the decoder with our proposed Reference Sliding Window Attention (R-SWA), which reduces attention computation costs while maintaining a constant KV cache throughout the entire decoding process. By combining the high compression rate of DeepSeek OCR's encoder with our constant KV cache design, Unlimited OCR can transcribe dozens of pages of documents in a single forward pass under a standard maximum length of 32K. More importantly, R-SWA is a general-purpose parsing attention mechanism - beyond OCR, it is equally applicable to tasks such as ASR, translation, etc. Codes and model weights are publicly available at http://github.com/baidu/Unlimited-OCR.

</details>


### [229] [Three-Step Hierarchical Transformer for Multi-Pedestrian Trajectory Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23058)
*Raphaël Delécluse,Hazem Wannous,Laurent Grisoni,Laurent Guimas*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A hierarchical Transformer for pedestrian trajectory prediction that separates temporal encoding, multimodal fusion, and social interaction into three explicit stages, achieving SOTA on real-world datasets with improved interpretability and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods address temporal, multimodal, and social factors separately or entangle them in costly attention blocks, limiting scalability, flexibility, and interpretability.

Method: A three-step hierarchical Transformer with: (1) temporal encoding via lightweight GRU summaries, (2) efficient cross-modal attention for multimodal fusion, and (3) social attention over time-agent tokens for scene-level interaction reasoning.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on JRDB and Urban datasets, competitive results on JTA. Ablation and qualitative analyses confirm each stage's contribution and the model's ability to anticipate complex behaviors like early turning.

Conclusion: The proposed hierarchical separation of temporal, multimodal, and social reasoning improves interpretability and efficiency while maintaining or surpassing SOTA performance on real-world pedestrian trajectory prediction.

Abstract: Pedestrian trajectory prediction requires modeling temporal dynamics, multimodal cues, and social interactions in crowded environments. Existing methods often address these factors separately or entangle them in costly attention blocks, limiting scalability, flexibility, and interpretability. We propose a three-step hierarchical Transformer that explicitly separates temporal encoding, multimodal fusion, and scene-level interaction reasoning. Lightweight GRU summaries enable efficient cross-modal attention, while social attention over time--agent tokens captures inter-pedestrian influences at manageable cost. Experiments on JTA, JRDB, and the Pedestrians and Cyclists in Road Traffic dataset show state-of-the-art performance on real-world datasets (JRDB, Urban) and competitive results on JTA. Ablation and qualitative analyses confirm the contribution of each stage and the model's ability to anticipate complex behaviors such as early turning.

</details>


### [230] [MotionHalluc: Diagnosing Kinematic Hallucinations in Fine-Grained Motion Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23061)
*Weile Guo,Shenghong He,Danying Mo,Chengdong Xu,Xuexun Liu,Chao Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces MotionHalluc, a benchmark for detecting motion hallucinations in cross-video comparison, and proposes PPV, a training-free method that injects kinematic measurements to reduce hallucinations, achieving an average 10.6% performance gain across models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing models often generate motion instructions with hallucinations when comparing paired videos, failing to reflect actual kinematic differences. No dedicated benchmark exists to systematically evaluate these hallucinations.

Method: MotionHalluc benchmark with 1540 fine-grained questions over 553 video pairs, evaluating three hallucination types (directional, attributional, temporal). PPV (Perceive-Parse-Verify) extracts and injects kinematic measurements at inference time without training.

Result: State-of-the-art large multimodal models show high susceptibility to motion hallucinations. PPV's measurement injection yields an average 10.6% performance gain across models.

Conclusion: Motion reasoning with explicit quantitative measurements is a key factor in reducing hallucinations in cross-video comparison.

Abstract: Motion instruction generation in cross-video comparison aims to produce corrective feedback that describes the differences between a query and a reference motion. However, existing models often generate instructions that exhibit motion hallucinations, failing to reflect actual kinematic differences between paired videos. To systematically investigate these hallucinations, we introduce MotionHalluc, a dedicated benchmark for evaluating motion hallucinations in paired-video comparison. MotionHalluc comprises 1540 fine-grained questions over 553 video pairs, evaluating hallucinations along three core dimensions: (1)directional hallucination, (2)attributional hallucination, and (3)temporal hallucination. Extensive evaluations of state-of-the-art large multimodal models demonstrate high susceptibility to these hallucinations. Furthermore, we provide Perceive-Parse-Verify (PPV) as a training-free measurements extraction and verification baseline that converts candidate instructions into executable measurement queries and supplies kinematic measurements at inference time. Our results show that this simple measurements injection yields an average 10.6% performance gain across models, suggesting that motion reasoning with explicit quantitative measurements is a key factor in reducing hallucinations in cross-video comparison. Our code and dataset will be made publicly available upon acceptance.

</details>


### [231] [Attention-Spectrum Regularization for Replay-Free Continual Multimodal LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23063)
*Chuangxin Zhao,Canran Xiao,Siyuan Ma,Mengyao Lyu,Yanbiao Ma,Jun Xia,Guiguang Ding,Yang Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes Attention-Spectrum Regularization (ASR), a replay-free continual learning method for multimodal LLMs that preserves cross-modal attention structures via spectral statistics of attention maps, reducing forgetting without storing old data or pseudo-examples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs need to adapt to non-stationary streams of visual domains and instructions, but continual fine-tuning causes severe forgetting of previously acquired multimodal skills. Existing methods lack control over how internal cross-modal attention patterns drift during adaptation.

Method: ASR treats cross-attention maps as 2D signals, summarizes their scale/directional properties into spectral statistics, stores only skill-wise prototype distributions (no replay data), and uses a phase-invariant spectral regularizer to constrain harmful drift while allowing adaptation to new tasks.

Result: Experiments on continual VQA and multimodal instruction-tuning benchmarks (VQA v2, VQACL, CLT-VQA, CoIN, UCIT) show ASR consistently improves final performance and reduces forgetting over strong replay-, regularization-, and adapter-based baselines.

Conclusion: Preserving skill-level attention structure is an effective and lightweight mechanism for continual MLLMs. ASR is replay-free, stores only compact spectral prototypes, and provides theoretical guarantees on forgetting control under spectral sufficiency assumptions.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are increasingly required to adapt to non-stationary streams of visual domains, question types, and user instructions, yet continual fine-tuning often causes severe forgetting of previously acquired multimodal skills. Existing continual vision-language methods mainly preserve outputs, replay data or pseudo-data, regularize embedding geometry, or allocate task-specific parameters, but they provide limited control over how internal cross-modal attention patterns supporting old skills drift during adaptation. We propose Attention-Spectrum Regularization (ASR), a replay-free continual learning framework that preserves skill-conditioned structures of cross-modal attention. ASR treats cross-attention maps as two-dimensional signals, summarizes their scale and directional properties into compact spectral statistics, and stores only skill-wise prototype distributions instead of replaying past image-question pairs, generated pseudo-examples, or old-stage teacher snapshots. In later stages, a phase-invariant spectral regularizer constrains harmful drift of these prototypes while allowing instance-level attention to adapt to new tasks. We provide theoretical analysis showing that skill-conditioned spectral drift controls forgetting under a spectral sufficiency assumption, and that Fourier power spectra are stable to spatial translations and bounded perturbations. Experiments on continual VQA and multimodal instruction-tuning benchmarks, including VQA v2, VQACL, CLT-VQA, CoIN, and UCIT, show that ASR consistently improves final performance and reduces forgetting over strong replay-, regularization-, and adapter-based baselines. Preserving skill-level attention structure is an effective and lightweight mechanism for continual MLLMs. Code is available at https://github.com/Creative-zcx/attention-spectrum-replay

</details>


### [232] [Rethinking Prototype-based Similarity Learning for Few-Shot Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23069)
*KunHo Heo,Seungjae kim,Wongyu Lee,SuYeon Kim,MyeongAh Cho*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces TSMa and SHARe to improve few-shot object detection by addressing class confusion and insufficient localization cues, achieving +10.1 nAP improvement on COCO.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing prototype-based similarity learning methods for few-shot object detection suffer from (i) class confusion due to inter-class similarity margin collapse, and (ii) insufficient visual cues for precise localization since similarity scores only capture class-level semantics without spatial information.

Method: Two complementary components: (1) Text-Anchored Semantic Mask (TSMa) uses class-level text features as semantic anchors via channel-wise interaction to suppress style-induced spurious responses and emphasize class-intrinsic signals; (2) Stage-Aligned Hierarchical Autoregressive Regression (SHARe) reformulates localization as hierarchical autoregressive process, aligning feature abstraction levels (deeper for coarse, shallower for fine details) with regression stages.

Result: State-of-the-art on COCO, outperforming previous best by +10.1 nAP.

Conclusion: The proposed components effectively mitigate class confusion and improve localization precision in few-shot object detection, achieving significant gains.

Abstract: Few-shot object detection aims to detect novel object categories from only a few labeled examples, avoiding costly large-scale annotation. Recent prototype-based similarity learning approaches enable training-free adaptation by matching query features with class prototypes. However, they suffer from two fundamental limitations: (i) class confusion arising from inter-class similarity margin collapse, and (ii) insufficient visual cues for precise localization, as similarity scores capture only class-level semantic affinity while providing limited spatial information. To address these issues, we introduce two complementary components. Text-Anchored Semantic Mask (TSMa) leverages class-level text features as semantic anchors to identify semantically aligned channels through channel-wise interaction between visual and text features. By suppressing style-induced spurious responses and emphasizing class-intrinsic signals, TSMa enlarges inter-class similarity margins and mitigates class confusion. We further propose Stage-Aligned Hierarchical Autoregressive Regression (SHARe), which reformulates localization as a hierarchical autoregressive process that progressively refines bounding boxes across multiple stages. SHARe leverages the layer-wise characteristics of ViT representations by aligning feature abstraction levels with regression stages: deeper layers guide early coarse localization, while shallower layers rich in edge and texture cues refine spatial details in later stages. Experiments on COCO demonstrate a new state of the art, outperforming the previous best by +10.1 nAP, with extensive analysis validating each component. The code is available at https://github.com/VisualScienceLab-KHU/ReSet.

</details>


### [233] [Poisson2Gaussian: Noise Gaussianization to Enhance Image Denoising](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23098)
*Xirou Zhou,Zijing Xu,Yibo Qu,Qi Zhang,Xiaowan Hu,Xinyang Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Poisson2Gaussian (P2G), a noise Gaussianization method that converts complex Poisson-mixed noise to i.i.d. Gaussian noise via probability density matching, enabling superior denoising performance across diverse datasets without requiring paired clean data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing denoisers struggle with Poisson-mixed noise due to its signal-dependency, heteroscedasticity, and statistical asymmetry, while they perform well on i.i.d. Gaussian noise which is easier to model.

Method: Poisson2Gaussian (P2G) converts complex real-world noise to i.i.d. Gaussian noise via probability density matching beyond low-order moments, combined with an unbiased denoising framework that works without paired clean data or explicit noise parameters.

Result: State-of-the-art performance across diverse datasets; improves PSNR by up to 0.75 dB in challenging scenarios with strongly non-Gaussian noise; architecture-agnostic with universal improvements for various denoisers.

Conclusion: P2G effectively bridges the gap between real-world noise and denoiser-friendly Gaussian noise, offering a universal, architecture-agnostic solution for improving denoising performance.

Abstract: The quantum nature of light determines the inherent Poisson stochasticity of photon detection, which is ubiquitous in photography, microscopy, and astronomy. However, our controlled numerical studies reveal that the signal-dependency, heteroscedasticity, and statistical asymmetry of Poisson-mixed noise make it challenging for existing denoisers to learn. In contrast, i.i.d. Gaussian noise, with its statistical independence and symmetric distribution, is easier to model for networks. To address this gap, we propose Poisson2Gaussian (P2G), a noise Gaussianization method that explicitly converts complex real-world noise to i.i.d. Gaussian noise via probability density matching beyond low-order moments. We also design an unbiased denoising framework that synergizes P2G with downstream denoisers, ensuring convergence to the underlying signal without requiring paired clean data or explicit noise parameters. Extensive experiments demonstrate that P2G consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse datasets. In challenging scenarios where noise strongly deviates from Gaussian statistics, our method improves the PSNR by up to 0.75 dB. Notably, P2G is architecture-agnostic and can provide universal improvements for various denoisers. The source code will be publicly available.

</details>


### [234] [Scene-agnostic ALS boresight self-calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23101)
*Aurélien Brun,Jan Skaloud*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents a new boresight calibration method for ALS that replaces traditional plane-based constraints with automatically extracted point-to-point correspondences from overlapping strips, enabling calibration during routine mapping missions without special flight patterns or scene requirements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional ALS boresight calibration requires dedicated flight patterns over structured scenes with planar surfaces, limiting its applicability during routine mapping missions. The authors aim to remove these constraints and enable boresight calibration as part of normal operations.

Method: Two formulations are proposed: (1) a simpler parametric adjustment using INS/GNSS trajectory, and (2) a rigorous factor-graph formulation treating GNSS and raw inertial data with boresight as an additional parameter. Both use scene-agnostic point-to-point correspondences from overlapping ALS strips instead of plane-based constraints.

Result: Evaluated across four operational ALS flights with five inertial systems. The lightweight formulation works well for tactical/navigation grade INS. The factor-graph approach is superior when inertial sensor errors are less observable. A minimum of 3-4 overlapping strips is sufficient.

Conclusion: Boresight calibration can be performed without particular scene prerequisites during routine mapping operations using 3-4 overlapping strips, provided INS/GNSS trajectory quality is sufficient. The factor-graph approach offers better robustness for lower-grade inertial systems.

Abstract: ALS boresight calibration has relied for two decades on dedicated flight patterns over structured scenes containing planar surfaces of varied aspect and slope. While reliable, this approach imposes constraints on the scene content and operations, which limits its applicability to boresight recovery within routine mapping missions. We present a practical approach that substantially relaxes these requirements by replacing plane-based constraints with scene-agnostic point-to-point correspondences extracted automatically from overlapping ALS strips. Two complementary formulations are proposed to estimate boresight with laser vector observations: (i) a simpler parametric adjustment utilizing INS/GNSS trajectory; (ii) a rigorous formulation treating GNSS and raw inertial data within an existing factor-graph, i.e. a dynamic network, where boresight is added as an additional parameter. Both formulations are evaluated across four operational ALS flights equipped with five inertial systems, covering a wide range of flight altitudes, overlap geometries, terrain types and inertial sensor classes. The analysis draws a clear boundary between the legacy plane-based conditioning that falls short outside the calibration scenario and the proposed formulations, which either recover or absorb boresight effects under conventional mapping geometry. Among them, the lightweight formulation is sufficient for boresight recovery using tactical and navigation grade inertial sensors, while the general factor-graph approach is clearly superior when the inertial sensor errors are less observable within an optimal smoother. This supports the hypothesis that, for INS/GNSS trajectory of sufficient quality, the boresight calibration can be performed without particular scene prerequisites during routine mapping operations using a minimum of 3-4 overlapping strips, with either proposed formulation...

</details>


### [235] [Compression and Retrieval: Implicit Memory Retrieval for Video World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23105)
*Zhan Peng,Jie Ma,Huiqiang Sun,Chong Gao,Zhijie Xue,Zhiyu Pan,Zhiguo Cao,Jun Liang,Jing Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CaR introduces an attention-driven implicit memory retrieval mechanism with lightweight context compression for consistent long-term video world modeling across complex camera trajectories, achieving SOTA results on benchmarks and open-domain scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for long-term memory in video world models rely on computationally expensive context scaling or rigid heuristic retrieval, which lacks generalization to varying camera trajectories and environments.

Method: CaR uses viewpoint-injected positional encoding for flexible attention-driven memory retrieval, plus a lightweight context compression network for efficient extended context processing. A new synthetic dataset SceneFly with realistic trajectories and frame annotations is also introduced.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on established benchmarks and demonstrates strong generalization to open-domain scenes.

Conclusion: The attention-driven implicit memory retrieval with compression effectively addresses long-term consistency in video world models across varied camera trajectories.

Abstract: Video world models hold promise for simulating interactive environments, yet maintaining consistent long-term memory across complex camera trajectories remains a critical challenge. Existing methods typically rely on computationally expensive context scaling or rigid heuristic retrieval mechanisms, which lacks generalization to varying camera trajectories and environments. In this paper, we propose Compression and Retrieval (CaR), an attention-driven implicit memory retrieval mechanism to overcome these limitations. By injecting viewpoint information via positional encoding, our method performs flexible memory retrieval through attention computation. To efficiently process extended contexts with minimal computational overhead, we further introduce a lightweight context compression network. Furthermore, we construct SceneFly, a large-scale synthetic dataset featuring realistic camera trajectories and frame-level annotations to train and evaluate long-horizon video world models. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art results on established benchmarks and exhibits strong generalization to open-domain scenes.

</details>


### [236] [Technical Report for the ICRA 2026 GOOSE 2D Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentation Challenge: Pretraining-Diverse Ensemble of Foundation Vision Encoders for Robust Outdoor Scene Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23113)
*Boyan Wang,Yongxi Huang,Wenjing Li,Tianrui Hui,Shaofei Huang,Nan Pu,Zhun Zhong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The authors propose a solution for the GOOSE 2D Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentation Challenge, combining three foundation vision encoders (DINOv3, SigLIP2, InternImage) with a Mask2Former decoder and strong training techniques, achieving 75.40% mIoU and second place.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of parsing unstructured outdoor scenes into 56 fine-grained categories across four camera platforms in the ICRA 2026 GOOSE competition.

Method: Pairs foundation vision encoders (DINOv3, SigLIP2, InternImage) with Mask2Former decoder, trained with long schedules, EMA, larger crop sizes, and multi-scale+flip TTA. Encoders are combined via per-class validation-IoU weighted ensemble.

Result: Achieves 75.40% composite mIoU on the official GOOSE test set, winning second place in the challenge.

Conclusion: The encoder's pretraining recipe is the dominant factor for accuracy, more important than parameter count or decoder design.

Abstract: This report presents our solution for the ICRA 2026 GOOSE 2D Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentation Challenge, which requires parsing unstructured outdoor scenes from four camera platforms into 56 fine-grained categories. Our approach pairs foundation vision encoders (including DINOv3, SigLIP2, and InternImage) with a Mask2Former decoder, and trains them with a strong recipe including long training schedules, exponential moving average, a larger crop size, and multi-scale plus flip test-time augmentation. The three encoders, chosen for their complementary pretraining objectives, are combined into a pretraining-diverse ensemble through per-class validation-IoU weighting. Evaluated on the official GOOSE test set, our submission achieves 75.40% composite mIoU and wins the second place of the challenge. Our study further shows that the encoder's pretraining recipe, rather than its parameter count or the decoder design, is the dominant factor for accuracy on this benchmark.

</details>


### [237] [LUMINA-26: Low-Light Understanding for Modeling and Interpreting Night-time Actions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23118)
*Aman Kumar Pandey,Anil Singh Parihar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces LUMINA-26, a new low-light human action recognition dataset with 6,784 clips across 26 action classes, and Illumi-Net, an illumination-adaptive Mixture-of-Experts network that achieves state-of-the-art performance on both ELLAR and LUMINA-26 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-light action recognition is challenging due to poor illumination, noise, and motion ambiguity. Existing low-light datasets lack action diversity, capture realism, and balanced class distribution, limiting robust model development.

Method: Illumi-Net uses an Illumination-Adaptive Mixture-of-Experts Network that leverages video-level illumination cues for adaptive enhancement, transformer-based spatio-temporal feature extraction, and expert-conditioned decision fusion.

Result: Surpasses previous SOTA on ELLAR (Top-1: 55.13%, Top-5: 78.87%) and establishes strong baseline on LUMINA-26 (Top-1: 75.95%, Top-5: 93.58%).

Conclusion: LUMINA-26 provides a practical benchmark for future low-light action recognition research, and Illumi-Net offers an effective approach for this challenging domain.

Abstract: Low-light human action recognition remains a challenging problem due to poor illumination, amplified noise, motion ambiguity, and diverse real-world scenes. Existing low-light datasets often lack sufficient action diversity, capture realism, or balanced class distribution, limiting the development of robust models. To address this, we introduce LUMINA-26: Low-Light Understanding for Modeling and Interpreting Night-time Actions, comprising 6,784 clips across 26 action classes, recorded from 22 subjects across 20 indoor and outdoor locations under naturally occurring low-light conditions. We also propose Illumi-Net: An Illumination-Adaptive Mixture-of-Experts Network, which leverages video-level illumination cues to guide adaptive enhancement and transformer-based spatio-temporal feature extraction, with expert-conditioned decision fusion. Our method surpasses previous state-of-the-art performance on ELLAR (Top-1: 55.13%, Top-5: 78.87%) and establishes a strong baseline on LUMINA-26 (Top-1: 75.95%, Top-5: 93.58%), offering a practical benchmark for future low-light action recognition research.

</details>


### [238] [MambaADv2: Evolving Duality-enhanced State Space Model for Unsupervised Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23126)
*Xiaobin Hu,Haoyang He,Bo Yin,Yu He,Lei Xie,Jiangning Zhang,Yu-Gang Jiang,Shuicheng Yan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MambaADv2 is a multi-class unsupervised anomaly detection framework using Mamba-inspired architectures with Duality-enhanced State Space modules, Hybrid State Space blocks, and a semantics-adaptive progressive scanning strategy to overcome CNN and Transformer limitations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CNNs struggle with long-range dependencies and Transformers suffer from quadratic complexity; Mamba-based architectures offer linear complexity with strong long-range modeling. The paper aims to evolve Mamba lineage into a tailored anomaly detection framework.

Method: The framework uses a pre-trained encoder and Mamba-inspired decoder with multi-scale DSS modules. DSS integrates parallel-cascaded HSS blocks (SSD-based Mamba lineage with Mamba3-style position-aware modeling) and frequency-enhanced convolutions. A semantics-adaptive progressive scanning strategy reduces complexity along the feature pyramid.

Result: Not explicitly stated in the abstract, but the method is designed to precisely reconstruct normal representations while magnifying anomalous deviations in multi-class unsupervised settings.

Conclusion: MambaADv2 effectively combines linear computational complexity with superior long-range dependency modeling for anomaly detection, leveraging dual computational paths (linear recurrence + parallel matrix formulation) to balance local continuity modeling and global contextual comparison.

Abstract: While recent advancements in anomaly detection have demonstrated the efficacy of CNN- and Transformer-based approaches, these architectures face inherent limitations: CNNs struggle to capture long-range dependencies, whereas Transformers suffer from quadratic computational complexity. Consequently, Mamba-based architectures have attracted considerable attention, as they successfully combine superior long-range dependency modeling with linear computational complexity. By critically rethinking the structural evolution across the Mamba lineage 1-3 series, this paper proposes MambaADv2, a framework tailored for multi-class unsupervised anomaly detection. MambaADv2 comprises a pre-trained encoder and a Mamba-inspired decoder, equipped with Duality-enhanced State Space (DSS) modules across multiple scales. The proposed DSS module effectively models both global dependencies and local representations by integrating parallel-cascaded Hybrid State Space (HSS) blocks and frequency-enhanced convolution operations. The structure of the Hybrid State Space (HSS) block is tailored by following the SSD-based Mamba lineage and incorporating Mamba3-style position-aware state-space modeling, leveraging the dual computational paths of linear recurrence and parallel matrix formulation to model local continuity and global contextual comparison, thereby better serving the core anomaly detection objective of precisely reconstructing normal representations while magnifying anomalous deviations. Additionally, we propose a semantics-adaptive progressive scanning strategy that decays scanning complexity along the feature pyramid.

</details>


### [239] [Spectral Gating via Damped Oscillations for Adaptive Implicit Neural Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23129)
*Alex Costanzino,Pierluigi Zama Ramirez,Giuseppe Lisanti,Luigi Di Stefano*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes modeling neuron activations as damped harmonic oscillators to resolve the spectral dilemma in Implicit Neural Representations, achieving adaptive spectral selectivity without hyperparameter tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing INRs face a trade-off between periodic activations (capture fine details but memorize noise) and spatially compact activations (regularize well but have low-frequency bias). Current solutions introduce computational overhead or tuning difficulties.

Method: Each neuron's activation is modeled as the steady-state response of a sinusoidally-forced damped harmonic oscillator. The amplitude naturally governs spectral selectivity during training. Oscillator parameters and network weights are jointly optimized, starting from the stopband for a coarse-to-fine learning curriculum.

Result: The method achieves state-of-the-art or competitive results against established INRs across comprehensive experiments, without requiring task-specific hyperparameter tuning.

Conclusion: The oscillator-based activation mechanism effectively resolves the spectral dilemma, enabling adaptive spectral selectivity and automatic coarse-to-fine learning without explicit regularization or hyperparameter tuning.

Abstract: Implicit Neural Representations (INRs) have been proven successful in encoding continuous signals through coordinate-based networks, yet facing a spectral dilemma: periodic activations capture fine details but act as all-pass filters that memorise noise, while spatially compact activations regularise effectively but suffer from low-frequency bias. Existing attempts to resolve this trade-off introduce computational overhead or tuning frailty. We propose to model each neuron's activation as the steady-state response of a sinusoidally-forced damped harmonic oscillator, whose amplitude naturally governs the network's spectral selectivity during training. By jointly optimising the oscillator parameters alongside the network weights, our method adapts to the target signal's spectral content without explicit regularisation.
