Method: An MPC framework combining JAX for large-scale parallelization and MuJoCo MJX for high-fidelity simulation, deployed on a Franka Research 3 via real-to-sim-to-real pipeline, with MTP (structured global sampling) and evaluation of online domain randomization.

Result: MTP outperforms CEM, MPPI, and PS in mode-switching tasks. Contact-initiation parameters provide interpretable adaptation signals, while global physics parameters yield feedback too weak for reliable real-time exploitation at typical replanning frequencies.

Conclusion: Key challenges for sampling-based MPC in contact-rich manipulation include contact sensitivity, tight compute budgets, and difficulty obtaining informative domain-randomization signals in real time.

Abstract: Sampling-based Model Predictive Control (SMPC) is a promising strategy for contact-rich robotic manipulation, combining gradient-free optimization with massively parallel GPU simulation. Yet, most prior work relies on simplified dynamics or remains confined to simulation. We present an MPC framework that leverages JAX for large-scale parallelization and efficient computation, coupled with the high-fidelity MuJoCo MJX simulator, and deploy it on a Franka Research 3 executing the Push-T manipulation task through a complete real-to-sim-to-real pipeline. The MTP variant with structured global sampling outperforms unimodal baselines such as CEM, MPPI, and PS across tasks that require mode switching, both in simulation and on hardware. Furthermore, we evaluate online domain randomization within the MPC sample budget, showing that contact-initiation parameters yield interpretable adaptation signals, whereas global physics parameters provide feedback that is too weak for reliable exploitation at typical replanning frequencies. These findings highlight key challenges for sampling-based MPC in contact-rich manipulation-contact sensitivity, tight compute budgets, and the difficulty of obtaining informative domain-randomization signals in real time.

</details>


### [294] [Coupled Routing and Configuration Optimization for Multi-Viewpoint Robotic Inspection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20739)
*Minh Nhat Vu,Khang Nguyen,Vu Trung Tran,Vien Ngo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A unified framework jointly optimizing visiting order and IK configurations for time-optimal, collision-free 9-DoF robotic inspection routes, reducing trajectory solves from quadratic to linear.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modular pipelines fix a single IK configuration per viewpoint and decouple routing from trajectory optimization, preventing global time-optimality.

Method: Parameterizes 3D self-motion manifolds per viewpoint in closed form, uses a double-integrator surrogate for travel time, encodes tours via random keys, and optimizes with CMA-ES; final trajectories are certified via direct collocation.

Result: Simulations and real-robot experiments on KUKA LBR iiwa + 2-DoF linear stage validate feasibility, smooth execution, and reduced inspection time vs. modular and naive baselines.

Conclusion: Jointly optimizing order and configurations in a single search eliminates decoupling losses, achieves global time-optimality, and scales linearly in trajectory solves.

Abstract: We present a unified framework that turns a set of 6-DoF inspection viewpoints into a time-optimal, collision-free route for a 9-DoF robotic system. Unlike modular pipelines that fix a single inverse-kinematics (IK) configuration per viewpoint, build an all-pairs travel-time map, and then route, our method jointly optimizes the visiting order and the per-viewpoint configuration in a single global search. The three-dimensional self-motion manifold of each viewpoint is parameterized in closed form so that the pose constraint holds by construction, the rest-to-rest travel time is approximated by a closed-form admissible double-integrator surrogate, and the tour is encoded by random keys. A derivative-free optimizer (CMA-ES) minimizes a cheap penalized objective over order and configuration, after which direct-collocation trajectory optimization is applied only to the edges of the selected route to certify dynamic feasibility and torque limits, and to return exact timings. This reduces the trajectory solves from quadratic to linear in the number of viewpoints and removes the decoupling that prevents modular pipelines from being globally time-optimal. Simulations and real-robot experiments on a KUKA LBR iiwa with a 2-DoF linear stage validate feasibility, smooth execution, and reduced end-to-end inspection time relative to modular and naive distance-based baselines.

</details>


### [295] [A Digital Twin Framework for Traffic-Aware UAV Pavement Monitoring without Lane Closure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20742)
*Yamil Uchani,Grace Abigail Luna Verdueta,Mauricio Figueroa,Edwin Salcedo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a Unity-based digital twin framework for traffic-aware UAV pavement monitoring, using YOLOv8n detection and three occlusion recovery strategies, achieving up to 99.26% accuracy and 97.95% coverage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world UAV pavement inspection is challenging due to traffic, pedestrians, and temporary occlusions, and current methods lack safe, controllable environments for developing traffic-aware strategies before deployment.

Method: A Unity digital twin integrates procedurally generated road defects, dynamic agents, autonomous UAV navigation, and a two-stage perception pipeline (YOLOv8n detector + classifier). Three occlusion recovery strategies (hover-and-recheck, micro-repositioning, skip-and-revisit) are evaluated across traffic densities and altitudes.

Result: The pipeline achieved 99.26% accuracy on simulator test set. Hover-and-recheck reached 97.03% coverage under medium/high traffic; skip-and-revisit achieved 97.95% coverage in low-traffic scenarios at medium altitude.

Conclusion: Digital twins can effectively support development and evaluation of traffic-aware UAV inspection strategies before real-world deployment, with flight altitude strongly influencing coverage and adaptive recovery improving performance under occlusion.

Abstract: UAV-based pavement inspection can reduce the cost and risk of road-surface monitoring, but real-world deployment remains difficult when traffic, pedestrians, and temporary occlusions affect the visibility of defects. This paper presents a Unity-based digital twin framework for traffic-aware UAV pavement monitoring without lane closure. The proposed environment integrates procedurally generated road defects, dynamic vehicles and pedestrians, autonomous UAV navigation, and an embedded road-damage perception pipeline. The perception module uses a two-stage approach: a lightweight YOLOv8n detector first localises road defects, pedestrians, and vehicles, while a second classifier distinguishes among potholes, single cracks, and crocodile cracks. On the simulator test set, the full pipeline achieved 99.26% overall accuracy across five classes. The digital twin was then used to evaluate three recovery strategies for occluded road segments: hover-and-recheck, micro-repositioning, and skip-and-revisit. Experiments were conducted across different traffic densities and flight altitudes using coverage, mission time, energy consumption, and revisit ratio as operational metrics. Results show that flight altitude has a strong influence on inspection coverage and that adaptive recovery improves performance under occlusion. In particular, hover-and-recheck achieved the most consistent coverage under medium and high traffic conditions, reaching up to 97.03% coverage, while skip-and-revisit was most effective in low-traffic scenarios, reaching 97.95\% coverage at medium altitude. These results demonstrate that digital twins can support the development and evaluation of traffic-aware UAV inspection strategies before real-world deployment.

</details>


### [296] [Toward Machine Risk Perception: Integrating Trust Calibration and Precursor-Based Risk Estimation for Humanoid](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20748)
*He Wen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a precursor-driven, trust-calibrated framework for proactive humanoid risk perception based on Logistic-Exponential (LE) modeling and trust as inverse accident probability, enabling dynamic, evidence-based safety inference for human-robot collaboration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humanoid robots in smart manufacturing introduce unique safety risks from human-like movements that conventional reactive force/distance limits cannot address.

Method: Accident evolution modeled via Logistic-Exponential (LE) formulation combining logistic escalation from diverse precursors with exponential decay for dissipation; trust defined as inverse estimated accident probability for real-time behavior adaptation.

Result: Analysis of 126 documented events and 241 precursors identified 12 dominant accident modes; most evolve through overlapping cues within one second. A "fall-onto-human" case study demonstrated early intervention capability.

Conclusion: Advances humanoid safety from static thresholds to dynamic, evidence-based inference, supporting risk-aware and trustworthy human-robot collaboration in Industry 5.0.

Abstract: Humanoid robots are emerging as co-workers in smart manufacturing, yet their dynamic, human-like movements introduce safety risks that differ fundamentally from those of fixed or wheeled robots. Conventional safety paradigms based on reactive force or distance limits fail to capture the sequential, uncertain nature of humanoid failures. This study proposes a precursor-driven, trust-calibrated framework to enable proactive humanoid risk perception. Accident evolution is modeled through sequential precursor cues using a Logistic-Exponential (LE) formulation that couples logistic escalation from diverse precursors with exponential decay for temporal dissipation. Trust is defined as the inverse of the estimated accident probability, allowing humanoids to adapt behavior in real time, reducing aggressiveness when risk intensifies, and restoring confidence as stability returns. A multi-source dataset of 126 documented events and 241 precursors revealed twelve dominant accident modes, most evolving through overlapping cues within one second. A simulated case study ("fall-onto-human") demonstrated how the LE-Trust coupling can trigger early intervention and prevent collapse. The results advance humanoid safety from static thresholds toward dynamic, evidence-based inference, establishing a foundation for risk-aware and trustworthy human-robot collaboration in Industry 5.0 environments.

</details>


### [297] [Perturbation-Based Uncertainty for Failure Detection in Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20754)
*Yousung Lee,Dongsoo Har*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a label-free, model-agnostic method for uncertainty estimation in VLA models by injecting Gaussian perturbations into hidden activations and measuring prediction disagreement, showing improved failure detection under distribution shift on LIBERO benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA models lack reliable uncertainty quantification under distribution shift; autoregressive uncertainty methods don't apply to regression/flow-based action generation, and current approaches rely on stochastic sampling or expensive failure labels.

Method: A Bayesian-inspired framework that injects Gaussian noise into transformer hidden activations during inference, then estimates epistemic uncertainty from the disagreement among multiple perturbed action predictions.

Result: On LIBERO and LIBERO-PRO benchmarks, perturbation-based uncertainty consistently outperforms sampling-based uncertainty for failure detection under distribution shift.

Conclusion: The proposed method provides a practical, label-free, and model-agnostic uncertainty signal for diverse pretrained VLA models, enhancing their reliability in deployment scenarios.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown strong performance in robotic manipulation, but reliable uncertainty quantification remains challenging, particularly under distribution shift. Unlike autoregressive policies, many modern VLA models generate continuous actions through regression or flow-based generation, where explicit predictive probabilities are unavailable. Moreover, existing approaches often rely on stochastic action sampling or supervised failure labels, limiting their applicability across diverse pretrained VLA models. In this work, we propose a label-free and model-agnostic framework for inference-time uncertainty estimation through hidden activation perturbations, motivated by Bayesian perspectives on local model variations. Specifically, we inject Gaussian perturbations into transformer hidden activations and estimate epistemic signals from disagreement across perturbed action predictions. Experiments on LIBERO and LIBERO-PRO show that perturbation-based uncertainty consistently improves failure detection under distribution shift compared to sampling-based uncertainty, providing a practical uncertainty signal for VLA models.

</details>


### [298] [UNSEEN: Uncertainty-aware Navigation via Sparse Estimation in Unknown Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20755)
*Tommaso Faraci,Marco Camurri,Daniele Fontanelli,Luigi Palopoli*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: UNSEEN is a unified uncertainty- and perception-aware navigation framework using only a front-mounted camera that couples localization, mapping, and planning to improve robustness in unknown environments, achieving up to 45% better estimation accuracy and 100% task success rate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing vision-only navigation methods suffer from performance degradation under motion blur, low texture, and illumination changes because they neglect the tight coupling between commanded motion and perception, while perception-aware methods optimize individual modules without propagating uncertainty consistently across the navigation stack.

Method: UNSEEN estimates sparse maps and robot poses with associated uncertainties at 6Hz using only a front-mounted camera, and leverages them to plan trajectories that jointly optimize task progress and estimation accuracy in a receding-horizon fashion.

Result: UNSEEN-SLAM reduces absolute translational error by 9.8%; UNSEEN-Plan improves estimation accuracy by up to 45% compared to SOTA methods; achieves 100% task success rate in simulations and real-world experiments.

Conclusion: The unified coupling of localization, mapping, and planning with explicit uncertainty propagation significantly improves robustness and task success for vision-only navigation in unknown environments.

Abstract: Visual navigation in unknown environments remains a core challenge in mobile robotics, especially for resource-constrained platforms. Most existing approaches rely on loosely coupled modular pipelines and strong assumptions on perception quality or environmental structure, often resorting to multi-modal sensor suites that increase system complexity and deployment cost. Vision-only navigation offers a lightweight alternative, but its performance degrades severely under motion blur, low texture, and illumination changes, largely because they neglect the tight coupling between commanded motion and perception. While perception-aware methods partially address this issue, they typically optimize individual modules and fail to propagate uncertainty consistently across the navigation stack. In this paper, we present UNSEEN, a unified uncertainty- and perception-aware navigation framework that explicitly couples localization, mapping, and planning using only a front-mounted camera. UNSEEN estimates sparse maps and robot poses with associated uncertainties at 6Hz, and leverages them to plan trajectories that jointly optimize task progress and estimation accuracy in receding-horizon. Simulations and extensive real-world experiments in unknown environments demonstrate the robustness of the proposed approach, with UNSEEN-SLAM reducing absolute translational error by 9.8% and UNSEEN-Plan improving estimation accuracy by up to 45% compared to state-of-the-art methods, while achieving a 100% task success rate.

</details>


### [299] [Mind the Privileged-to-Camera Gap: Actor-Centric Sidecar Supervision for Camera-First Open-Loop Waypoint Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20772)
*Feeza Khan Khanzada,Jaerock Kwon*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a supervised representation learning approach for open-loop waypoint prediction in camera-first autonomous driving. By adding simulator-derived sidecar labels (actor grounding, relevance, motion) during training only, they achieve 32.6% reduction in FDE compared to a plain baseline (1.223m vs 1.815m), with consistent gains across all actor-conditioned subsets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing camera-first autonomous-driving models predict future ego waypoints using images, ego-state, and route commands, but waypoint supervision alone does not explicitly supervise actor-level representations of nearby road users, potentially missing crucial information for safe navigation.

Method: The model uses multi-view RGB, ego state, and route commands at inference. During training, simulator-derived "sidecar" labels are used to supervise: actor grounding, privileged hindsight actor relevance relative to the logged ego trajectory, and selected-actor short-horizon motion. These sidecar labels are never used at inference. They evaluate on route-disjoint splits with matched architecture, optimizer, validation criterion, checkpoint selection, and three seeds.

Result: The road-user sidecar supervision (RU-sidecar) achieves 1.223±0.01m FDE, a 32.6% reduction over the plain baseline (1.815±0.02m) and 28.7% over the matched no-teacher non-sidecar RGB control (1.716±0.02m). It improves over the plain baseline on 1445/1494 routes and over the control on 1417/1494 routes. All actor-conditioned subsets show gains, including 29.1% reduction for ≥4 sidecar actors and 30.0% when vulnerable road users are present.

Conclusion: Sidecar supervision significantly improves waypoint prediction by explicitly supervising actor-level representations, even though sidecar labels are only available in simulation during training. The privileged-to-camera gap persists, as non-deployable simulator-state diagnostics remain stronger. Evidence is limited to open-loop simulation diagnostics.

Abstract: Camera-first autonomous-driving models predict future ego waypoints from images, ego-state features, and route commands, but waypoint supervision alone does not explicitly supervise actor-level representations of nearby road users. We study this as supervised representation learning for open-loop waypoint prediction. The deployable model uses multi-view RGB, ego state, and route command at inference. During training, simulator-derived sidecar labels supervise actor grounding, privileged hindsight actor relevance relative to the logged ego trajectory, and selected-actor short-horizon motion; these labels are never inference inputs. We evaluate route-disjoint splits with matched architecture, optimizer, validation criterion, checkpoint selection, and three seeds. A plain waypoint-only RGB baseline obtains 1.815$\pm$0.02 m final displacement error (FDE), and the matched no-teacher non-sidecar RGB control obtains 1.716$\pm$0.02 m. Road-user sidecar supervision (RU-sidecar) reduces FDE to 1.223$\pm$0.01 m, a 32.6% reduction over the plain baseline and 28.7% over the matched no-teacher non-sidecar RGB control. It improves over the plain baseline on 1445/1494 routes and over the matched no-teacher non-sidecar RGB control on 1417/1494 routes. Actor-conditioned slices show gains in all nonempty subsets, including 29.1% reduction for samples with at least four valid sidecar actors and 30.0% when a vulnerable road user is present. Optional simulator-state teacher alignment reaches 1.186$\pm$0.15 m FDE, but higher seed variability makes it secondary. Non-deployable simulator-state diagnostics remain stronger, indicating a privileged-to-camera gap. The evidence is limited to open-loop simulation diagnostics.

</details>


### [300] [World Action Models: A Survey](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20781)
*Qiuhong Shen,Shihua Zhang,Yue Liao,Qi Li,Zhenxiong Tan,Shizun Wang,Shuicheng Yan,Xinchao Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This survey clarifies the boundary between World Action Models (WAMs) and related video generation, world model, and policy methods. It organizes works by what they generate (rendered/latent futures or action reasoning) and decomposes methods by substrate, backbone, action coupling, and deployment. Key finding: WAMs trade representational richness for compute/action cost, trending toward generating less future while preserving control-essential information.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The rapid expansion of WAMs has blurred boundaries among world models, video generation models, action-grounded video world models, Vision-Language-Action policies, and WAMs themselves. A clear taxonomy and unified account are needed to organize the field.

Method: The survey clarifies boundaries between related fields, then organizes works via two complementary views: (1) what each method generates (rendered futures, latent futures, video-generation-free action reasoning), and (2) decomposition by predictive substrate, backbone, action coupling, and deployment regime.

Result: A consistent design pattern emerges: WAMs trade off representational richness against compute, memory, latency, and action-label cost. The field is trending toward generating less of the future while preserving what control requires.

Conclusion: WAMs are not simply video generators with action heads, but predictive-action methods with distinct design trade-offs. The field moves toward efficient methods that generate minimal futures sufficient for control.

Abstract: World Action Models (WAMs) are embodied predictive-action models that make a forecast of the future available to action. Recent WAMs repurpose large video generation models, and a parallel line relies on language or vision-language backbones without a video-generation core. This rapid expansion has blurred the boundary among broad world models, video generation models, action-grounded video world models, Vision-Language-Action policies, and WAMs. This survey gives the field a common account. It first clarifies these boundaries, then organizes existing works through two complementary views. The first view asks what each method is required to generate, spanning rendered futures, latent futures, and video-generation-free action reasoning. The second view decomposes each method by predictive substrate, backbone, action coupling, and deployment regime. This anatomy supports a unified discussion of interactability, causality, persistence, physical plausibility, and generalization, followed by data, evaluation, and open challenges. Across these axes, a consistent design pattern emerges: WAMs are not simply video generators with action heads, but predictive-action methods whose design choices trade representational richness against compute, memory, latency, and action-label cost. The field is moving toward methods that generate less of the future while preserving what control requires. The survey homepage is available at https://world-action-models.github.io/.

</details>


### [301] [Geometric Entropy: When Trajectory Diversity Helps and Hurts in Imitation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20871)
*Qian Luo,Ruizhe Liu,Pei Zhou,Xunzhe Zhou,Yanchao Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces Geometric Entropy (H_G), a metric quantifying trajectory-shape diversity in demonstration data, and finds an inverted-U relationship between diversity and imitation learning performance — too little hurts robustness, too much causes strategy ambiguity — with optimal diversity shifting lower as task mastery increases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how trajectory-shape diversity in demonstrations affects imitation learning performance, and to develop a task-agnostic metric that can predict dataset quality before training.

Method: Proposes Geometric Entropy (H_G), which measures intrinsic trajectory diversity after normalizing away extrinsic variation (goal pose, workspace scale) via target-frame alignment. Validates across multiple IL architectures, simulated and real-robot contact-rich tasks.

Result: Consistent inverted-U relationship between success rate and H_G; optimal entropy shifts lower with more data, easier tasks, or stronger priors; pretrained VLA models show near-monotonic decreasing trend.

Conclusion: H_G enables fast pre-training auditing of demonstration datasets and provides a guideline for calibrating demonstration diversity to the learnable regime.

Abstract: We study how trajectory-shape diversity in demonstrations affects imitation learning (IL) performance across models, tasks, and data scales. We introduce Geometric Entropy (H_G), a task-agnostic metric that quantifies the intrinsic diversity of transit trajectories after normalizing away extrinsic variation, such as goal pose and workspace scale, via target-frame alignment. Across multiple IL architectures and both simulated and real-robot contact-rich manipulation tasks, we observe a consistent inverted-U relationship between success and H_G: increasing geometric diversity improves robustness in low-diversity regimes but degrades performance once diversity induces strategy ambiguity. Moreover, the optimal entropy shifts toward lower values as task mastery increases through more data, easier tasks, or stronger priors, and for a pretrained vision-language-action model the trend becomes effectively monotonic decreasing. Practically, H_G enables fast pre-training auditing of demonstration datasets and offers a simple guideline for calibrating demonstrations toward the learnable regime.

</details>


### [302] [Vesta: A Generalist Embodied Reasoning Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20905)
*Johan Bjorck,Zhiqi Li,Yunze Man,Jing Wang,An-Chieh Cheng,Sifei Liu,Shihao Wang,Zhiding Yu,Abhishek Badki,Stan Birchfield,Valts Blukis,Yevgen Chebotar,Siyi Chen,Sicong Leng,Yu-Cheng Chou,Tianli Ding,Boyi Li,Zhengyi Luo,Hang Su,Jonathan Tremblay,Tingwu Wang,Bowen Wen,Jimmy Wu,Xianghui Xie,Hanrong Ye,Hongxu Yin,K. R. Zentner,Liangyan Gui,Yu-Xiong Wang,Yuke Zhu,Linxi "Jim" Fan,Jan Kautz*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Vesta is a unified embodied generalist foundation model that integrates localization, spatial reasoning, navigation, and long-horizon planning into a single model, outperforming specialist models by >20% and ensembles by >10% across diverse benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots operating in open-world environments need multiple capabilities (localization, reasoning, navigation, planning) but deploying separate specialist models is computationally expensive and prone to cascading errors.

Method: Vesta combines a diverse, massive curated corpus designed for spatial grounding with a simple multimodal memory harness for extended temporal reasoning, all within a single foundation model.

Result: Outperforms individual SOTA baselines by >20% and ensemble of per-category-best baselines by >10% across diverse benchmarks; improves real-world task success by >35% on tasks requiring memory and reasoning.

Conclusion: A single generalist model is a feasible, scalable, and arguably preferable alternative to combining multiple specialist models for embodied AI.

Abstract: Robots operating in open-world environments must seamlessly integrate localization, spatial reasoning, navigation, and long-horizon planning. While specialist models excel at individual tasks, deploying a multi-model stack is computationally expensive and prone to cascading errors. We present Vesta, a unified embodied generalist that consolidates these capabilities into a single foundation model. Our approach combines a diverse and massive curated corpus designed to induce spatial grounding and a simple multimodal memory harness that enables reasoning over extended time horizons. Across diverse benchmarks, Vesta on average beats individual SOTA baselines by >$20\%$ and beats an ensemble of per-category-best baselines by $>10\%$ -- thus demonstrating that a generalist model can match or exceed specialists. On real-world robotic tasks requiring memory and reasoning, Vesta improves task success by >35\%. Our work thus demonstrates that a single generalist is a feasible, scalable, and arguably preferable alternative to combining specialists.

</details>


### [303] [Learning-Based Modeling of Soft Robots via Cosserat Rod Theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20958)
*Mohammad Ali,Nithin Senthur Kumar,Eric J. Barth,Thomas Beckers*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces a port-Hamiltonian Gaussian Process Regression framework that combines Cosserat rod theory with data-driven learning to model soft robot dynamics, achieving accurate and energy-consistent simulations of planar rod-like soft robots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modeling soft robot dynamics is challenging due to their continuum structure and nonlinear dynamics. First-principles models are time-demanding with limited expressiveness, while purely data-driven models lack interpretability and physical consistency.

Method: The proposed port-Hamiltonian Gaussian Process Regression framework integrates Cosserat rod theory and Hamiltonian physics with data-driven inference to preserve the system's energy structure while accurately learning rod dynamics.

Result: Numerical simulations show accurate and energy-consistent representations of a rod-like soft robot, demonstrating robust and interpretable modeling of complex continuum mechanics.

Conclusion: The framework offers a robust and interpretable pathway for modeling complex continuum mechanics of soft robots, combining the best of physics-based and data-driven approaches.

Abstract: Modeling soft robot dynamics is challenging due to their continuum structure and typically nonlinear dynamics. Creating models based on first-order principles is typically time-demanding, and their expressiveness is limited, whereas data-driven models lack interpretability and physical consistency. This work aims to overcome these challenges by introducing a port-Hamiltonian Gaussian Process Regression framework for learning and simulating the dynamics of planar, rod-like soft robots. In detail, the proposed model integrates Cosserat rod theory and Hamiltonian physics with data-driven inference to preserve the system's energy structure while accurately learning the rod dynamics. Numerical simulations show that we can achieve accurate and energy-consistent representations of a rod-like soft robot, showing the potential for a robust and interpretable pathway for modeling complex continuum mechanics.

</details>


### [304] [Heterogeneous Policy Networks for Composite Robot Team Communication and Coordination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20962)
*Esmaeil Seraj,Rohan Paleja,Luis Pimentel,Kin Man Lee,Zheyuan Wang,Daniel Martin,Matthew Sklar,John Zhang,Zahi Kakish,Matthew Gombolay*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HetNet uses heterogeneous graph-attention networks to enable efficient multi-agent communication and coordination, achieving up to 707.65% performance improvement and 200x bandwidth reduction over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MARL methods fail to properly model agent heterogeneity (different state, action, observation spaces), causing communication to be unhelpful or harmful. Prior homogeneous graph networks cannot capture diverse roles in heterogeneous teams.

Method: Extends Heterogeneous Policy Networks (HetNet) using heterogeneous graph-attention networks to learn diverse communication protocols, supports scaling to larger heterogeneous robot teams, and enables end-to-end binarized messaging for efficient bandwidth usage.

Result: 5.84% to 707.65% performance improvement over next-best baseline across multiple domains, and 200x reduction in required communication bandwidth.

Conclusion: HetNet sets a new state of the art for learning coordination and communication strategies in heterogeneous multi-agent teams, demonstrating that properly modeling heterogeneity yields significant gains in both performance and communication efficiency.

Abstract: High-performing human-human teams learn intelligent and efficient communication and coordination strategies to maximize their joint utility. These teams implicitly understand the different roles of heterogeneous team members and adapt their communication protocols accordingly. Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) has attempted to develop computational methods for synthesizing such joint coordination-communication strategies, but emulating heterogeneous communication patterns across agents with different state, action, and observation spaces has remained a challenge. Without properly modeling agent heterogeneity, as in prior MARL work that leverages homogeneous graph networks, communication becomes less helpful and can even deteriorate the team's performance. In the past, we proposed Heterogeneous Policy Networks (HetNet) to learn efficient and diverse communication models for coordinating cooperative heterogeneous teams. In this extended work, we extend Heterogeneous Policy Networks (HetNet) to support scaling heterogeneous robot teams. Building on heterogeneous graph-attention networks, we show that HetNet not only facilitates learning heterogeneous collaborative policies but also enables end-to-end training for learning highly efficient binarized messaging. Our empirical evaluation shows that HetNet sets a new state of the art in learning coordination and communication strategies for heterogeneous multi-agent teams by achieving an 5.84% to 707.65% performance improvement over the next-best baseline across multiple domains while simultaneously achieving a 200x reduction in the required communication bandwidth.

</details>


### [305] [Duet: Dual-Robot Understanding via Efficient Teaching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20990)
*Yiqi Zhao,Ruohai Ge,Celina Shiyu Wang,Junjie Ye,Muchen Xu,Minhao Li,Sergey Zakharov,Basile Van Hoorick,Vitor Campagnolo Guizilini,Leonidas Guibas,Gaurav S. Sukhatme,Jyotirmoy V. Deshmukh,Yue Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DUET is a dual-robot learning framework for mobile manipulation that uses VR-based teleoperation and human-human coordination tracking to efficiently collect data and train collaborative policies with an Action Chunking Transformer, achieving superior performance with 5.4x faster data collection than robot-only baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data acquisition is the primary bottleneck for training dual-robot collaborative systems, as collecting sufficient real-robot teleoperation data is extremely time-consuming and labor-intensive.

Method: (1) Unified dual-embodiment synchronized VR-based teleoperation system for in-domain robot data collection. (2) Complementary tracking pipeline to record human-human coordination priors. (3) Action Chunking Transformer architecture that pretrains on efficient human-human demonstrations then finetunes on minimal real-robot trajectories.

Result: DUET achieves 5.4x acceleration in data collection via teleoperation while performing equally or better than robot-only data trained policies across all four collaborative tasks using a Unitree G1 humanoid and Dexmate Vega1 mobile manipulator.

Conclusion: Harnessing human coordination priors reduces total human effort for data collection and yields superior task performance compared to baselines trained only on robot data.

Abstract: Dual-robot collaboration enables tasks that exceed the reach and payload of a single robot, such as collaboratively transporting objects across environments and executing coordinated handovers. Data acquisition is the primary bottleneck for training these systems. To this end, we introduce DUET, a dual-robot learning framework for mobile manipulation. For efficient data collection, we create a unified dual-embodiment synchronized VR-based teleoperation system for in-domain heterogeneous robot data collection. We further develop a complementary tracking pipeline that records human-human coordination and collaborative mobile manipulation priors. To allow efficient learning, we introduce an Action Chunking Transformer based architecture that first pretrains collaborative policies on efficient human-human demonstrations, before finetuning them on a minimal set of real-robot teleoperation trajectories. We develop a benchmark of four collaborative tasks to evaluate our framework using a Unitree G1 humanoid and a Dexmate Vega1 mobile manipulator. The results demonstrate that harnessing human priors not only yields superior task performance compared to baselines trained only on robot data, but also reduces the total human effort required for data collection. Our human data collection pipeline achieves 5.4x acceleration on average from teleoperation, but we perform equally or better than robot-only data trained policies across all tasks. Our project page is available at https://zhaoy37.github.io/Duet/.

</details>


### [306] [Inductive Generalization for Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20999)
*Annabella Macaluso,Haochen Zhang,Ishaan Masilamony,Yingshan Chang,Yonatan Bisk*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes "inductive generalization" as a new evaluation paradigm for visuomotor policies, testing them on progressively harder out-of-distribution task variants rather than interpolation on known distributions. The authors show that current SOTA models (including Vision-Language-Action models) that appear to generalize under typical domain shifts fail these inductive generalization tests, revealing a critical orthogonal challenge for general-purpose robotics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation methods for visuomotor policies test generalization via interpolation on known distributions with unstructured domain shifts (lighting, clutter, diverse objects). The authors argue this fails to measure true generalization, and instead propose testing inductive capacity on progressively harder out-of-distribution task variants—drawing inspiration from axis-based evaluation in language models.

Method: The authors introduce a reusable, formal evaluation protocol for measuring inductive generalization in any manipulation policy. They benchmark existing paradigms, including SoTA Vision-Language-Action models, against this new protocol.

Result: Existing paradigms that appear to generalize under prior domain shifts (distractors, etc.) fail inductive generalization tests. SoTA Vision-Language-Action models also fail these tests.

Conclusion: Inductive generalization reveals a class of learning challenges orthogonal to data and model scaling in robot learning, which must be solved to achieve general-purpose robots.

Abstract: Understanding the generalization capabilities of visuomotor policies is essential in the development of capable robotic agents. Generalizable models learn structures that transfer across domains. However, in practice, visuomotor policies test performance by interpolation on known distributions using unstructured domain shifts (e.g. lighting, clutter, diverse objects). We argue that to measure generalization capabilities we must instead test the inductive capacity of policies on progressively harder, out-of-distribution task variants. We call this inductive generalization, drawing directly on how axis-based evaluation has revealed inherent generalization limitations in language models (e.g. sequence length, counting) arXiv:2502.00197 . We provide a reusable and formal evaluation protocol for measuring inductive generalization in any manipulation policy, and establish baselines showing that existing paradigms fail this test; e.g. SoTA Vision-Language-Action models and find that policies that appear to generalize to prior domain shifts (distractors, etc) fail inductive generalization tests. These results expose a class of learning challenges orthogonal to those addressed by data and model scaling in robot learning, yet are imperative to solve in order to realize general purpose robots.

</details>


### [307] [R2HandoverSim: A Simulation Framework and Benchmark for Robot-to-Human Object Handovers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21011)
*Hanxin Zhang,Abdulqader Dhafer,Hongbiao Dong,Zhou Daniel Hao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: R2HandoverSim is a simulation benchmark for robot-to-human object handovers that enables reproducible evaluation through standardized protocols, a user study with 30 participants, and five complementary metrics (planning feasibility, reachability, grasp stability, grasp affordance, safety) that better reflect user-perceived quality than success rate alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robot-to-human handover methods have advanced rapidly, but there is a lack of standardized evaluation protocols, which impedes objective comparison between different approaches.

Method: The authors built a simulation benchmark (R2HandoverSim) that systematically compares four baselines on predicted shared grasp poses, complemented by a 30-participant user study to correlate simulation results with real-world evaluation.

Result: Simulation results correlate with real-world evaluation outcomes. Five complementary metrics (planning feasibility, reachability, grasp stability, grasp affordance, safety) better reflect user-perceived handover quality than overall success rate alone.

Conclusion: The benchmark enables reproducible and standardized evaluation of R2H handovers, and the proposed multi-metric approach provides a more holistic assessment of handover quality from the user's perspective.

Abstract: We present R2HandoverSim, a simulation benchmark for robot-to-human (R2H) object handovers. Although R2H handover methods have advanced rapidly, the lack of standardized evaluation protocols impedes objective comparison. Our benchmark enables reproducible evaluation by systematically comparing four baselines on their predicted shared grasp poses. We conduct a user study with 30 participants, analyze baseline performance, and show that simulation results correlate with real-world evaluation outcomes. Crucially, five complementary metrics (planning feasibility, reachability, grasp stability, grasp affordance, and safety) better reflect user-perceived handover quality than overall success rate alone. Website and code: https://robot-future.github.io/r2handoversim/.

</details>


### [308] [BayesFP: Posterior Estimation for Flow-Based Policies via Feynman-Kac Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21014)
*Sreevardhan Sirigiri,Weiming Zhi,Fabio Ramos*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A unified, retraining-free method for constrained trajectory generation from pretrained diffusion/flow policies using Bayesian posterior sampling and Feynman-Kac corrector framework, validated on manipulation tasks including obstacle avoidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots need to generate trajectories that faithfully follow learned expert behavior while also satisfying safety constraints and task-specific objectives that are only known at inference time, without retraining the base policy.

Method: Formulates constrained trajectory generation as Bayesian posterior sampling (expert prior + cost-derived likelihood). Extends the Feynman-Kac corrector framework from diffusion models to deterministic flow-matching policies, creating a unified inference-time sampler.

Result: Validated on pretrained Diffusion Policy, GR00T-N1.6, and π0.5 checkpoints in simulated and real-world manipulation tasks with non-convex obstacles, showing improvements over base π0.5 on zero-shot tasks.

Conclusion: The proposed unified sampler enables inference-time constrained generation for both diffusion and flow policies without retraining, effectively handling safety constraints and task objectives introduced at test time.

Abstract: Robots must generate trajectories that remain faithful to learned expert behavior while satisfying safety constraints and task-specific objectives specified only at inference time. We formulate constrained trajectory generation for pretrained diffusion and flow-matching policies as Bayesian posterior sampling, with the learned demonstration distribution as a prior and an inference-time, cost-derived likelihood tilting it toward feasible, optimal trajectories. To sample from this posterior without any retraining of the base policy, we leverage the Feynman--Kac corrector framework, originally formulated for diffusion models, and extend it to deterministic flow-matching policies. The result is a unified, inference-time, retraining-free sampler for diffusion and flow policies. We validate the approach on pretrained Diffusion Policy, GR00T-N1.6, and $π_{0.5}$ checkpoints across simulated and real-world manipulation tasks, including planning around non-convex obstacles introduced at inference time, and show improvements over the base $π_{0.5}$ on zero-shot tasks.

</details>


### [309] [Membrane-based Acoustic Microrobots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21047)
*Fatih Kocabas,Cemal Polat Avdar,Prithvi Venkatesh,Yunus Alapan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a membrane-based acoustic microrobot that overcomes the gas dissolution problem of conventional air-bubble microrobots by using a thin PDMS membrane over microcavities, achieving over 24 hours of stable operation, magnetic directional control, and scalability down to ~100 μm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional acoustic microrobots rely on air microbubbles trapped in microcavities, which suffer from rapid gas dissolution and resonance frequency shifts, severely limiting their operational longevity.

Method: A thin flexible PDMS membrane is bonded over confined microcavities to physically prevent gas diffusion, combined with embedded magnetic microparticles for directional control via low-intensity magnetic fields.

Result: Demonstrated consistent streaming and propulsion for over 24 hours of continuous operation, magnetic directional control at 2 mT fields, and design scalability down to ~100 μm.

Conclusion: This membrane-based approach provides a reliable, high-endurance framework for long-term acoustic microrobotic applications such as targeted drug delivery.

Abstract: Acoustic microrobots have emerged as a promising frontier for targeted drug delivery and minimally invasive medicine due to their high-power density and biocompatibility. Despite wide-ranging designs, conventional acoustic microrobots mostly rely on air microbubbles trapped within confined microcavities within the robot body, which suffer from limited operational longevity due to rapid gas dissolution and resultant shifts in resonance frequency. In this paper, we propose a robust, membrane-based acoustic microrobot that overcomes these limitations by employing a thin flexible Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) membrane bonded over confined microcavities for microstreaming. The introduced design physically prevents gas diffusion, ensuring stable performance over extended periods at high actuation voltages. We systematically characterized the membrane-based acoustic actuator longevity, demonstrating consistent streaming and propulsion for over 24 hours of continuous operation. In addition, by embedding magnetic microparticles into the structural body, these actuators were successfully employed as microswimmers with directional control using low-intensity (2 mT) external magnetic fields. Finally, we demonstrate the scalability of the proposed design architecture down to ~100 um. This membrane-based approach establishes a reliable framework for the development of high-endurance acoustic microactuators and microrobots capable of performing long-term tasks.

</details>


### [310] [ReFPO: Reflow Regularization for Flow Matching Policy Gradients](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21086)
*Ge Wang,Yibo Peng,Fan Feng,Shenhao Yan,Chengsi Yao,Jiahao Yang,Honghao Cai,Yiming Zhao,Xi Li,Jinke Ren,Shuguang Cui,Yatong Han,Zhen Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ReFPO enhances flow-based policy gradients with explicit Reflow regularization via a single line of code change, enabling stable training and high-fidelity one-step inference that matches or exceeds multi-step performance across various control tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Flow Matching Policy Gradients (FPO) suffer from instability (e.g., CFM proxy-ratio spikes) and often require multi-step sampling for good performance. The authors aim to stabilize training and enable efficient one-step inference without additional computational overhead.

Method: ReFPO adds an explicit geometric Reflow regularizer to FPO, building on the insight that FPO's gradient updates implicitly perform advantage-weighted Reflow. This regularizer is implemented with a single line of code change, requiring no extra computation or auxiliary distillation stages.

Result: ReFPO reduces CFM proxy-ratio spikes, stabilizes PPO-style training, achieves high-fidelity one-step inference matching/exceeding multi-step performance, and improves average performance and discretization robustness across GridWorld, MuJoCo Playground, and Humanoid Control tasks.

Conclusion: ReFPO provides a scalable, stable approach for generative policies in complex physical simulations, effectively synergizing advantage-guided updates with path rectification for flow-based control.

Abstract: We present Reflow-regularized Flow Matching Policy Gradients (ReFPO), a simple online RL method that adds explicit Reflow regularization to FPO for efficient flow-based control. We uncover a key structural property: the gradient updates in Flow Matching Policy Gradients (FPO) can be interpreted as an implicit advantage-weighted Reflow process, providing a new geometric perspective on flow-based policy gradients. Building on this insight, ReFPO introduces an explicit geometric regularizer that can be implemented with a single line of code change without incurring additional computational overhead or auxiliary distillation stages. By synergizing advantage-guided updates with path rectification, our method reduces CFM proxy-ratio spikes, stabilizes PPO-style training, and enables high-fidelity one-step inference that often matches or exceeds multi-step performance. We experimentally demonstrate that ReFPO improves average performance and discretization robustness across GridWorld, MuJoCo Playground, and high-dimensional Humanoid Control tasks, providing a scalable and stable approach for generative policies in complex physical simulations.

</details>


### [311] [MV-WAM: Manifold-Aware World Action Model with Value Augmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21088)
*Jintao Chen,Peidong Jia,Qingpo Wuwu,Jiaming Liu,Mengfei Du,Chun-Kai Fan,Xiaowei Chi,Hao Chen,Chengyu Bai,Zezhong Qian,Hao Wang,Jiajun Cao,Weishi Mi,Xiaozhu Ju,Jian Tang,Shanghang Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MV-WAM is a robotic manipulation framework that uses cross-modality causal masks, manifold-aware optimization, and progress-value regulation to achieve robust generalization, outperforming baselines by 29.3% in simulation and achieving 77.5% success rate on real-world dual-arm tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing world action models achieve strong in-domain performance but fail to generalize to out-of-distribution scenarios. The authors attribute this to a structural mismatch between visual and action modalities, where heterogeneous manifolds cause joint optimization to degrade action robustness under distribution shift.

Method: MV-WAM jointly models visual prediction, action generation, and value estimation with (1) a cross-modality causal mask that hierarchically grounds actions in predicted video frames and value tokens; (2) a manifold-aware optimization scheme accounting for structural heterogeneity across modalities; (3) a progress-value regulation mechanism that detects misalignment and enables value-guided rollback.

Result: On RoboTwin simulation: 55.7% mean success rate on random scenarios without randomized action supervision, outperforming strongest baseline by 29.3%. On real-world dual-arm robot: 77.5% mean success rate across four tasks of varying difficulty.

Conclusion: Manifold-aware cross-modal alignment is essential for robust policy generalization, offering a path toward deployable robotic manipulation.

Abstract: Achieving robust and generalizable manipulation across diverse environments remains a fundamental challenge in embodied robotics. Recent world action models achieve strong in-domain performance, yet their gains do not extend proportionally to out-of-distribution scenarios. We attribute this to a structural mismatch between visual and action modalities, whose intrinsically heterogeneous manifolds cause joint optimization to disproportionately degrade action robustness under distribution shift. To address this, we propose MV-WAM, a novel end-to-end framework that jointly models visual prediction, action generation, and value estimation designed to effectively leverage video priors during both training and inference for enhanced action generalization. Key to this unification is a cross-modality causal mask that hierarchically grounds actions in predicted video frames and value function tokens in both modalities. To further narrow the generalization gap, MV-WAM adopts a manifold-aware optimization scheme that explicitly accounts for the structural heterogeneity across modalities. Finally, MV-WAM introduces a progress-value regulation mechanism that estimates task completion and detects misalignment between predicted frames and generated actions, enabling the policy to autonomously identify execution deviations and recover through value-guided rollback. On the RoboTwin simulation, MV-WAM achieves a 55.7% mean success rate on random scenarios without any randomized action supervision, outperforming the strongest baseline by 29.3%. MV-WAM achieves a 77.5% mean success rate across four real-world tasks of varying difficulty on a dual-arm robot. Our results demonstrate that manifold-aware cross-modal alignment is essential for robust policy generalization, offering a path toward deployable robotic manipulation.

</details>


### [312] [How Should a Robot Configure Its Laser Scanner for Inspection?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21093)
*Zhiling Chen,David Gorsich,Matthew P. Castanier,Yang Zhang,Jiong Tang,Farhad Imani*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SenseHD is a robotic sensing system that treats scanner configuration as instruction-conditioned sensing decisions, using hyperdimensional associative memory to select stable sensing regimes, improving inspection reliability while remaining lightweight and efficient.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work has focused on robot motion and viewpoint planning, but how to configure sensing parameters remains largely underexplored, despite their decisive impact on measurement quality.

Method: SenseHD formulates scanner configuration as an instruction-conditioned sensing decision, treating sensing parameters as discrete sensing actions and selecting stable sensing regimes through hyperdimensional associative memory.

Result: Experiments on a real robotic inspection platform demonstrate that SenseHD robustly selects appropriate sensing configurations and significantly improves inspection reliability, while remaining lightweight and efficient compared to baseline methods.

Conclusion: SenseHD effectively addresses the underexplored problem of sensing parameter configuration, offering a robust, lightweight, and efficient solution for robotic inspection.

Abstract: Robotic inspection relies on accurate sensing to acquire high-fidelity geometric measurements for defect detection and metrology. While prior work has focused on robot motion and viewpoint planning, how to configure sensing parameters remains largely underexplored, despite their decisive impact on measurement quality. We propose SenseHD, a robotic sensing system that formulates scanner configuration as an instruction-conditioned sensing decision. Instead of predicting precise parameter values, SenseHD treats sensing parameters as discrete sensing actions and selects stable sensing regimes through hyperdimensional associative memory. Experiments on a real robotic inspection platform demonstrate that SenseHD robustly selects appropriate sensing configurations and significantly improves inspection reliability, while remaining lightweight and efficient compared to baseline methods.

</details>


### [313] [Factor-Aware Mixture-of-Experts with Pretrained Encoder for Combinatorial Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21100)
*Feihong Zhang,Guojian Zhan,Zeyu He,Yinuo Wang,Likun Wang,Tianze Zhu,Yao Lyu,Tao Zhang,Tinghao Yi,Wei You,Shengbo Eben Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FAME integrates factor-aware mixture-of-experts with pretrained encoders to improve visual robotic manipulation generalization across varying environmental conditions, achieving 34-35% improvement over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pretrained encoders with diffusion policies struggle to generalize across complex environments with varying factors like lighting and surface textures.

Method: Three-stage training: (1) policy warmup with frozen encoder, (2) factor-specific adapter training on customized datasets, (3) joint fine-tuning with a central router that softly weights adapters as a dense MoE.

Result: Outperforms diffusion policy baselines by 34% on Meta-World benchmark and achieves 35% improvement in real-world pick-and-place task.

Conclusion: Factor-aware MoE framework effectively enables combinatorial generalization across multiple environmental variations for robotic manipulation.

Abstract: The integration of pretrained encoders with diffusion policies has become a dominant paradigm for visual robotic manipulation. However, it still struggles to generalize across complex environments with varying factors such as lighting and surface textures. To address this, we propose FAME, a framework that integrates a factor-aware mixture-of-experts (MoE) with a pretrained encoder to enhance generalization to environmental variations. FAME follows a three-stage training process: (1) policy warmup, where a diffusion policy is trained on standard-environment data with a frozen encoder; (2) factor-specific adapter training, where lightweight adapters inserted between the frozen encoder and the temporarily frozen policy are trained on customized datasets, each targeting a distinct environmental variation; and (3) joint fine-tuning, where a central router and the warmed policy are trained on mixed data to handle multiple factors jointly. FAME is ``factor-aware'' because the central router softly weights frozen factor-specific adapters as a dense MoE, enabling combinatorial generalization across multiple factors. Evaluations on the Meta-World benchmark show that FAME outperforms diffusion policy baselines by 34%. We further validate FAME in a real-world pick-and-place task using a compact model trained on newly collected data, where FAME achieves a 35% improvement in generalization under real-world variations.

</details>


### [314] [PoLAR: Factorizing Extent and Mode in Latent Actions for Robot Policy Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21139)
*Youngjoon Jeong,Jihwan Yu,Minsoo Jo,Junha Chun,Taesup Kim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PoLAR introduces radial-direction structure to latent action spaces, using hyperbolic geometry to separate transition extent (radius) from transition mode (direction), improving robot policy learning across simulation and real-world tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing latent action pretraining methods encode each transition as a single unstructured representation that entangles transition extent (how much change) and transition mode (what kind of change), limiting downstream policy learning.

Method: PoLAR imposes radial-direction structure on latent actions where radius encodes transition extent and direction encodes transition mode. It uses temporal offset as a weak proxy for extent and instantiates this in hyperbolic space, whose expanding volume naturally accommodates more diverse modes at larger extents.

Result: PoLAR improves downstream policy performance in both simulation and real-world robot experiments, outperforming latent action baselines and strong pretrained VLAs across in-task and large-scale pretraining settings.

Conclusion: The geometry of the latent action space is an important design choice for transferring visual pretraining to downstream robot policy learning.

Abstract: Latent action pretraining learns representations of visual change from pairs of observations, but existing methods typically encode each transition as a single unstructured representation that entangles transition extent and transition mode. We introduce Polar Latent Actions with Radial structure (PoLAR), which imposes a radial-direction structure on latent actions, encouraging radius to encode transition extent and direction to retain transition mode. PoLAR uses temporal offset between two observations as a weak proxy for transition extent, encouraging latent action from observation pairs separated by larger temporal gaps to occupy larger radii. We instantiate this structure in hyperbolic space, whose expanding volume with radius offers a natural fit for more diverse transition modes at larger extents. Across in-task and large-scale pretraining settings, PoLAR improves downstream policy performance in simulation and real-world robot experiments, outperforming latent action baselines and strong pretrained VLAs. These results suggest that the geometry of the latent action space is an important design choice for transferring visual pretraining to downstream robot policy learning.

</details>


### [315] [Pose-Agnostic Robotic Functional Grasping via Observation-Action Canonicalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21148)
*Le Qiu,Cole Harrison,Jiankai Sun,Yao Liu,Suning Huang,Qianzhong Chen,Yang You,Marco Pavone*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AnyMug is a canonicalized visuomotor RL framework for functional robotic grasping that trains a single closed-loop policy in simulation and deploys zero-shot to real robots for mug-handle grasping, achieving >93% simulation and 80% real-world success rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Functional robotic grasping (e.g., mug-handle grasping) is challenging due to diverse object geometries, poses (upright/inverted), thin handle structures, and the need for task-specific contact precision. Existing methods (open-loop grasp detection or learned visuomotor policies) struggle with estimation errors, coupled visual-action variation, and limited generalization across configurations.

Method: AnyMug introduces observation-action canonicalization that transforms depth observations and predicted end-effector actions into a shared object-centric frame, enabling the policy to see a consistent mug-centered view and emit canonical-direction actions. It also uses a handle-aware reward for precise approach/gripper alignment, a pose curriculum, and domain randomization for sim-to-real transfer.

Result: Achieved >93% success rate in simulation on unseen upright and inverted mugs; zero-shot transferred to real Franka Panda robot with 80% success rate on 5 held-out physical mugs across both pose categories.

Conclusion: Canonicalized observation-action space enables a single closed-loop policy trained purely in simulation to generalize zero-shot to real-world functional grasping across diverse object poses and geometries, addressing key limitations of prior grasp detection and visuomotor policy approaches.

Abstract: Functional robotic grasping requires a policy that generalizes across diverse object geometries and poses while maintaining task-specific contact precision. We study this challenge through mug-handle grasping, where thin handles, instance variation, and upright or inverted placements make both perception and control sensitive to object configuration. Grasp pose detection methods operate open-loop and are sensitive to estimation errors on thin handle structures. Learned visuomotor policies must implicitly learn to handle the coupled variation in visual appearance and action direction induced by different object placements, limiting generalization. We propose AnyMug, a canonicalized visuomotor reinforcement learning framework for functional grasping that trains a single closed-loop policy entirely in simulation and deploys it zero-shot on a real robot. AnyMug introduces observation-action canonicalization, which transforms both the depth observation and the predicted end-effector action into a shared object-centric frame. The policy therefore sees a consistent mug-centered view and emits actions in a canonical direction regardless of mug placement, allowing the same grasping behavior to be reused across configurations. A handle-aware reward further encourages precise approach, gripper alignment, and opposing-finger placement, while a pose curriculum and domain randomization improve training stability and sim-to-real transfer. In simulation, AnyMug achieves over 93% success rate on both unseen upright and inverted mugs and transfers zero-shot to a real Franka Panda, reaching 80% success rate on 5 held-out physical mugs across both pose categories.

</details>


### [316] [OmniV2X: A Generative Foundation Planner for Efficient End-to-End Cooperative Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21165)
*Juntong Peng,Juanwu Lu,Yupeng Zhou,Can Cui,Yaobin Chen,Ziran Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OmniV2X is a generative foundation model for V2X cooperative driving that processes multi-modal, multi-agent context sequences, reduces computational costs, handles data scarcity, and achieves state-of-the-art performance with minimal fine-tuning data and bandwidth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for V2X cooperative driving suffer from high computational cost of dense 3D perception, vulnerability to data scarcity in cooperative scenarios, and poor compliance with standardized messaging when fusing multi-modal inputs into shared representations.

Method: OmniV2X uses an end-to-end supervised pipeline with a downstream trajectory generation loss. A high-capacity generative sequence planner implicitly learns to steer the model and leverage multi-modal inputs via cross-attention injection. It integrates lightweight, standard-compliant V2X tokens and can be pre-trained on single-agent data then adapted to cooperative environments.

Result: On DAIR-V2X-Seq, OmniV2X outperforms existing end-to-end cooperative driving baselines, achieving SOTA with less than 10% of fine-tune V2X dataset and less than 1% of the communication bandwidth.

Conclusion: OmniV2X demonstrates computational efficiency and robustness under real-world constraints, positioning itself as an effective foundation model for cooperative driving.

Abstract: We present OmniV2X, a generative foundation model for vehicle-to-everything (V2X) cooperative driving. The model directly interprets independent context sequences comprising multi-modal and multi-agent observations. The new design mitigates the computational cost of dense 3D perception, the vulnerability to data scarcity in cooperative scenarios, and the poor compliance with standardized messaging in existing methods that fuse multi-modal inputs into a shared representation. For training, we present an end-to-end supervised pipeline using a downstream trajectory generation loss, in which a high-capacity generative sequence planner implicitly learns to steer the model and leverage multi-modal inputs via cross-attention injection. As a foundation model, we demonstrate that OmniV2X pre-trained on large-scale single-agent planning datasets can efficiently adapt to cooperative environments by integrating the conditioning context with lightweight, standard-compliant V2X tokens. Evaluated on the DAIR-V2X-Seq dataset, OmniV2X outperforms existing end-to-end cooperative driving baselines, achieving state-of-the-art performance with less than 10% of the fine-tune V2X dataset and less than 1% of the communication bandwidth. We conduct comprehensive evaluations to demonstrate its computational efficiency and robustness under real-world constraints.

</details>


### [317] [Remember what you did?: Learning Behavioral Memories for Partially Observable Object Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21188)
*Kuancheng Wang,Seungho Yeom,Jinglin Cao,Yuheng Zhi,Nikhil Shinde,Michael Yip*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CAMP introduces a compressed action memory module that enables robots to handle long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation tasks by leveraging their own action history as a self-supervised signal, outperforming state-of-the-art methods across multiple real and simulated benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Contact-rich manipulation tasks are inherently partially observable — a single visual observation lacks the full context of prior attempts and progress, causing standard visuomotor policies and VLAs to fail due to insufficient memory.

Method: CAMP trains a memory module to maintain a compressed representation of past actions, encoding a latent behavioral memory that contextualizes future actions without additional supervision or external oversight.

Result: Evaluated across four real-robot setups and two novel simulation benchmarks (Memory-T-Bench and Memory-Manip-Bench), CAMP shows substantial gains over state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: CAMP is the first policy to demonstrate substantial success on contact-rich partially observable manipulation tasks purely through learned memory.

Abstract: Long horizon, contact-rich manipulation is inherently partially observable. This is as a single visual observation rarely captures a robot's full action context, including prior attempts, interactions, or progress. Consequently, standard visuomotor policies or vision-language-action models are prone to struggle in such tasks due to a lack of memory. To address this, we introduce Compressed Action Memory Policy (CAMP) based on the insight that a robot's own action history serves as a highly informative, self-supervised signal, enabling the policy to learn a robust, compact history representation. In our approach, we train a memory module to maintain a compressed representation of past actions, forcing it to encode a latent behavioral memory of all the robot's past interactions that can then be used to better contextualize future actions. This allows our approach to implicitly track generalized task progress and learn from failed attempts without any additional supervision, or external oversight. We evaluate CAMP across four real-robot setups and two novel simulation benchmarks: Memory-T-Bench and Memory-Manip-Bench. By demonstrating substantial gains over state-of-the-art baselines, CAMP is, to our knowledge, the first policy to demonstrate substantial success on contact-rich partially observable manipulation tasks purely through learned memory.

</details>


### [318] [A scalar per patch from pre-trained ViTs enables fast moving navigation in the real world](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21216)
*Steeven Janny,Leonid Antsfeld,Christian Wolf*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This large-scale study (966 navigation episodes, 24km) evaluates which computer vision components truly matter for real-world robotics navigation, finding that multi-teacher distillation, bottlenecked encoders with interpretable features, and finetuning from privileged information all improve performance over standard RGB-trained policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically determine which computer vision components (visual encoders, pre-training tasks, distillation, bottlenecking) are actually critical for real-world robot navigation performance, as prior work lacks large-scale real-world validation.

Method: Ran 966 static point-goal navigation episodes (24km) in a real building; compared SOTA visual encoders; evaluated heterogeneous multi-teacher distillation; applied principled bottlenecking to encoders; finetuned policies pre-trained on privileged information.

Result: Multi-teacher distillation produces encoders with complementary skills; bottlenecking leads to interpretable affordance-linked features; RGB-only training is suboptimal compared to finetuning from privileged information.

Conclusion: A more complete understanding of which computer vision aspects are relevant for real-world navigation is provided, highlighting the importance of pre-training objectives, encoder architecture choices, and privileged information beyond RGB data alone.

Abstract: Trained policies for real-world robotics rely on computer vision components, typically in the form of pre-trained visual encoders. These encoders are an essential component and it has been shown that their power does not emerge from training on robotics downstream losses alone. Pre-training with auxiliary losses in the form of computer-vision pre-text tasks is a defining factor and heavily conditions agent performance in robotics tasks. In this unprecedented large-scale study, we ran 966 navigation episodes of static point goal navigation in a real-world building for 24km and asked which components really matter for the computer vision aspects of robotics: we evaluate state-of-the art visual encoders in realistic conditions. We explore the usefulness of heterogeneous multi-teacher distillation leading to encoders with multiple different and complementary skills. We investigate how much information from these encoders is necessary for robotics by bottlenecking them in a principled and spatially useful way and we show that this leads to the emergence of interpretable features linked to affordances. We also argue that training policies on RGB data alone does not lead to an optimal usage of visual features and show this by finetuning policies pre-trained on privileged information. All in all, we paint a more complete picture of what aspects of computer vision are relevant for real-world navigation.

</details>


### [319] [A UAV-Mounted Sensor Network for Close-Range Inspection of Wind Turbine Rotor Blades](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21220)
*Jost Wittmann,Ahmed Abdullahi Hassan,Nils Kömpe,Andreas Nüchter*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a UAV-mounted multimodal sensor system (RGB camera, thermal infrared camera, and 3D scanner) for inspecting offshore wind turbine rotor blades, with co-calibrated sensors enabling spatial data fusion for improved defect detection and classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offshore wind turbine blade inspection is critical for predictive maintenance but remains challenging due to remote locations, large structure sizes, and limitations of current UAV sensor systems. Existing approaches can detect surface anomalies but defect classification is still manual and error-prone.

Method: Design of a UAV-mounted multimodal sensor network combining an industrial RGB camera, a passive thermal infrared camera, and an in-house developed 3D scanner, with all sensors co-calibrated into a common coordinate frame for spatial superimposition of geometric, color, and thermal data.

Result: Preliminary laboratory results demonstrate synchronized multi-sensor acquisition and initial point cloud reconstructions, forming the basis for future airborne inspection trials.

Conclusion: The system addresses three fundamental sensing challenges (platform motion, large field of view, and millimeter-level accuracy) and shows promise for automated defect classification in offshore wind turbine blade inspection.

Abstract: Inspection of offshore wind turbine rotor blades is critical for predictive maintenance to maximise efficiency and extend operational lifetime. However, it remains a challenging task due to remote locations, large structural dimensions, and the limitations of current UAV-compatible sensor systems. While existing approaches can detect certain types of surface anomalies, reliable classification of defect types often remains a manual and error-prone process.
  This paper presents the design of a UAV-mounted multimodal sensor network combining an industrial RGB camera, a passive thermal infrared camera, and an in-house developed 3D scanner. All sensors are co-calibrated into a common coordinate frame, enabling spatial superimposition of geometric, colour, and thermal data. The system is designed to operate at close range, addressing three fundamental sensing challenges: platform motion, large field of view, and millimetre-level measurement accuracy. Preliminary laboratory results demonstrate synchronised multi-sensor acquisition and initial point cloud reconstructions, forming the basis for future airborne inspection trials.

</details>


### [320] [FleetAgent: Teleoperation Assistant for Autonomous Fleets via Vectorized V2N Messages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21222)
*Juntong Peng,Qi Chen,Deyuan Qu,Takayuki Shimizu,Yaobin Chen,Ziran Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FleetAgent is a cloud-hosted MLLM assistant that uses compact vectorized V2N messages (instead of raw sensor data) to monitor autonomous fleets, reducing uplink payload by 625x and KV-cache memory by 16.54x while improving intervention accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Streaming raw sensor data from many autonomous vehicles is costly, and remote operators can only monitor a limited number of vehicles at a time, creating scalability challenges for large-scale fleet teleoperation.

Method: Proposes FleetAgent (MLLM assistant) + VecFormer (vector-to-embedding interface with differentiable top-K context selection) + VecEval (nuScenes-derived dataset with imperfect plans and human-verified labels).

Result: Reduces uplink payload by 625x vs raw images, reduces KV-cache memory by 16.54x vs text descriptions; improves Lingo-Judge score by 16.8% and reduces intervention failure rate by 19.9% over Qwen2.5-VL-7B baseline.

Conclusion: Compact structured V2N messaging with MLLM-based analysis enables efficient, explainable, and scalable teleoperation monitoring for autonomous vehicle fleets.

Abstract: Large-scale autonomous fleets rely on teleoperation to resolve rare failures, yet streaming raw sensor data from many vehicles is costly, and remote operators can only monitor a limited number of vehicles at a time. We introduce FleetAgent, a cloud-hosted multimodal large language model (MLLM) assistant that consumes compact vectorized vehicle-to-network (V2N) messages, such as map elements, detected objects, and the ego planned path. It provides a structured natural-language response (including narration, explanation, and evaluation of the plan and scene), along with an intervention urgency score for operator prioritization.
