Result: At 1-1.5 Hz, softer body (0 kPa) maximizes velocity and minimizes CoT; at 2.5-3 Hz, stiffer body (-40 kPa) delivers superior velocity and reduced transport cost. Active stiffness tuning is essential for efficient and high-speed swimming across frequencies.

Conclusion: Stiffness modulation is a key strategy for adaptive and efficient propulsion in bio-inspired robotic swimmers.

Abstract: Fish achieve efficient swimming across varied speeds through active modulation of their body flexibility. To explore the effects of tunable stiffness on swimming performance, we present a bio-inspired freely swimming fish robot with a rapidly tunable particle-jamming body. This design enables rapid stiffness adjustments with negligible changes in shape or volume, achieving a 54% variation in flexural rigidity across vacuum pressures of 0 to -40 kPa. We visualize the midline of the oscillating body under both low- and high-stiffness conditions, and the comparison confirms that the body curvature varies with stiffness. We further experimentally evaluate the tunable stiffness body's effects on swimming performance using velocity and cost of transport (CoT) measurements obtained via a motion tracking system. Results show that active stiffness tuning is essential for sustaining efficient and high-speed swimming across beating frequencies of 1-3 Hz. At low frequencies (1-1.5 Hz), a softer body (0 kPa) maximizes velocity and minimizes CoT, whereas at high frequencies (2.5-3 Hz), a stiffer body (-40 kPa) delivers superior velocity and reduced transport cost. These findings highlight stiffness modulation as a key strategy for adaptive and efficient propulsion in bio-inspired robotic swimmers.

</details>


### [348] [Rotation-Aware Point-Cloud Embeddings for Vision-Based In-Hand Reorientation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21788)
*Yashom Dighe,Karthik Dantu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a rotation-aware point-cloud embedding that enables direct goal conditioning for dexterous in-hand reorientation, eliminating the need for explicit pose estimation, dense flow, or privileged supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Raw point-cloud goal conditioning is poorly conditioned for policy learning due to unordered, independently sampled, and visibility-dependent clouds, making current-goal comparison entangle rotation with permutation, resampling, and unstable correspondence.

Method: Learn a rotation-aware point-cloud embedding where Euclidean latent distance is calibrated to SO(3) geodesic error, enabling model-free RL to act from embeddings, proprioception, and centroid metadata without explicit pose or flow.

Result: Matches privileged-state and distillation-based baselines in in-hand reorientation, while avoiding brittle test-time pose or flow computation.

Conclusion: Point-cloud goals become practical when the representation itself encodes task-relevant rotation geometry; generic visual pretraining is insufficient as it discards task-relevant state.

Abstract: Point-cloud goals provide a direct way to specify dexterous in-hand reorientation: instead of defining an object-specific pose frame or estimating 6D pose at test time, the policy is given the desired 3D geometry of the object. Yet raw point-cloud goal conditioning is poorly conditioned for policy learning. Current and goal clouds are unordered, independently sampled, and often visibility-dependent, so their discrepancy entangles object rotation with permutation, resampling, and unstable correspondence structure. For this reason, prior point-cloud manipulation methods typically add structure outside the representation itself, such as explicit pose or relative-pose inputs, dense flow features, or distillation from privileged teachers. We close this gap by learning a rotation-aware point-cloud embedding whose Euclidean latent distance is calibrated to the SO(3) geodesic error between object orientations. The resulting representation turns current-goal comparison into a smooth control signal, allowing a model-free RL policy to act from current and goal point-cloud embeddings, proprioception, and centroid metadata, without object pose, relative pose, dense flow, or teacher-action supervision. In in-hand reorientation experiments, this interface matches privileged-state and distillation-based baselines while avoiding brittle test-time computation of structured pose or flow inputs. These results suggest that point-cloud goals become practical for this task when the representation, rather than an external module, encodes the task-relevant geometry of rotation. We also show evidence that generic visual point-cloud pretraining is insufficient for such a current-goal comparison because it discards the task-relevant state and preserves only shape features.

</details>


### [349] [THREAD: Trajectory Planning for Hybrid Rigid-Soft Manipulators with Environment-Aware Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21792)
*Shivani Kamtikar,Pranav Asthana,Naveen Kumar Uppalapati,Girish Krishnan,Girish Chowdhary*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: THREAD is a diffusion-based trajectory planner for hybrid rigid-soft manipulators that learns a generative prior over physically realizable backbone trajectories, achieving 92.4% task success with 5x fewer collisions than baselines and enabling real-world threading through apertures as small as 1.3x the soft segment diameter.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manipulation in confined environments (e.g., threading through narrow apertures) remains challenging for conventional rigid robots. Hybrid rigid-soft manipulators show promise but face planning challenges: feasible free-space shapes become infeasible under contact, and planning rigid and soft segments independently ignores kinematic coupling.

Method: The authors propose THREAD, a diffusion-based trajectory planner that learns a generative prior over physically realizable backbone trajectories conditioned on local environment geometry. It uses physics-inspired losses encoding curvature, smoothness, and collision constraints jointly across both rigid and soft segments.

Result: THREAD achieves 92.4% task success with 5x fewer collisions than the strongest baseline. It demonstrates cross-embodiment real-world transfer with minimal online updates, successfully threading through apertures as small as 1.3x the soft segment diameter.

Conclusion: The proposed diffusion-based approach effectively addresses the compounding planning challenges for hybrid rigid-soft manipulators in confined environments, showing strong simulation performance and real-world transferability.

Abstract: Manipulation in confined environments, such as threading a manipulator through narrow apertures, remains a fundamental challenge, especially for conventional rigid robots. Hybrid rigid-soft manipulators offer promise but face two compounding planning challenges: backbone shapes feasible in free space become infeasible under environmental contact, and planning rigid and soft segments independently ignores their kinematic coupling. We present THREAD, the first diffusion-based trajectory planner for hybrid manipulation, learning a generative prior over physically realizable backbone trajectories conditioned on local environment geometry, with physics-inspired losses encoding curvature, smoothness, and collision constraints jointly across both segments. Trained in simulation, THREAD achieves 92.4% task success with 5x fewer collisions than the strongest baseline. We show cross-embodiment real-world transfer with minimal online updates, successfully threading through apertures as small as 1.3x the soft segment diameter.

</details>


### [350] [SurGE: Surrogate Gradient-guided Evolution for Co-design of Legged Robots with Parallel Elasticity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21866)
*Yulun Zhuang,Yue Qin,Justin Lu,Zelin Shen,Yichen Wang,Sicheng He,Yanran Ding*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SurGE is a framework that uses surrogate gradients from a differentiable kinodynamic model to guide CMA-ES optimization for co-designing legged robots with elastic elements, achieving faster convergence, lower variance, and successful real-world hardware transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Co-design of legged robots with elastic elements is challenging because contact dynamics and mechanism engagement are non-differentiable, making gradient-based optimization infeasible.

Method: SurGE computes surrogate gradients through a differentiable pipeline (Kino-SRB model + design-aware control policy) and injects them into CMA-ES via mean shift with cosine-annealed step decay to guide the population toward promising regions.

Result: On a 4-DOF hopping robot design space, SurGE achieves 6x lower cross-seed standard deviation and 18% tighter population concentration vs. vanilla CMA-ES. Hardware experiments show 37.65% reduction in design objective starting from a hand-tuned initial design.

Conclusion: SurGE provides a promising approach to accelerate non-differentiable co-design problems in legged robots by leveraging surrogate gradients, with consistent simulation-to-hardware transfer.

Abstract: Co-design of legged robots with elastic elements is challenging due to the non-differentiability of contact dynamics and mechanism engagement. This paper presents SurGE, a framework that computes surrogate gradients of the design objective through a differentiable pipeline consisting of a kinodynamic single-rigid-body (Kino-SRB) model and a design-aware control policy, and injects them into CMA-ES via mean shift with cosine-annealed step decay. On a 4-DOF design space of a hopping robot with unidirectional parallel spring, SurGE achieves 6 times lower cross-seed standard deviation and 18% tighter population concentration compared to vanilla CMA-ES, while matching or improving the best objective. Hardware experiments on a 2D design subspace show that, starting from a hand-tuned initial design, SurGE reduces the design objective by 37.65% on hardware, with the improvement trend identified in simulation transferring consistently to the physical system. SurGE provides the potential to accelerate non-differentiable co-design problems in legged robots via surrogate model gradients.

</details>


### [351] [Predictive Gaze Is Preserved but Reorganized toward Monitoring during Robot-Mediated Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21920)
*Manuela Uliano,Silvia Fattorini,Marco Controzzi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This study investigates whether predictive, goal-directed gaze behavior persists when humans control a robot remotely (teleoperation). It finds that gaze remains task-aligned and predictive, but visual attention shifts more toward the robotic end-effector and objects, indicating adaptive reorganization of visuomotor control under altered embodiment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether anticipatory, goal-directed eye movement behavior—well-established in natural action—is preserved when actions are executed through a robot rather than one's own body, addressing a gap in understanding visuomotor adaptation under altered embodiment.

Method: The study investigates gaze behavior during goal-directed telemanipulation tasks, comparing predictive and monitoring gaze patterns between natural and robot-mediated action execution.

Result: Gaze remains strongly predictive and aligned with task goals during teleoperation. However, visual attention is systematically redistributed toward the robotic end-effector and manipulated objects, increasing online monitoring.

Conclusion: Predictive gaze is not lost under altered embodiment but reorganized in response to changes in sensory feedback and control demands, revealing the flexibility of the human visuomotor system and highlighting gaze as a signal for inferring action intentions in human-robot interaction.

Abstract: Goal-directed eye movements are a fundamental component of visuomotor control, enabling humans to anticipate and guide their actions. Whether this anticipatory and task-driven behavior is preserved when actions are executed through a robot rather than through one's own body remains unclear. Here we address this question by investigating gaze behavior during goal-directed telemanipulation to determine how visuomotor control adapts to altered embodiment. Our findings show that gaze remains strongly aligned with task goals, preserving its predictive role even during robot-mediated manipulation. At the same time, teleoperation systematically redistributes visual attention toward the robotic end-effector and manipulated objects, increasing online monitoring. These findings show that predictive gaze is not lost under altered embodiment, but reorganized in response to changes in sensory feedback and control demands. More broadly, they reveal the flexibility of the human visuomotor system when the natural sensorimotor coupling is disrupted and identify gaze as an informative signal for inferring action intentions in human-robot interaction.

</details>


### [352] [CoRDE: Concept-Prior Routed Diffusion Experts for Structural Generalization in Robot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21935)
*Haidong Huang,Xixin Zhao,Yaohua Zhou,Jiayu Song,Jiayi Zhang,Jun Ma,Haiyue Zhu,Xiaocong Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CoRDE is a structure-guided variational distillation framework for robot imitation learning that uses concept-prior routing and LoRA-based parameter-efficient experts to prevent routing collapse, reduce parameter explosion, and achieve superior multi-task and long-horizon action generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion models in robot imitation learning suffer from gradient conflicts between distinct semantic sub-stages in multi-task/long-horizon scenarios, and pure MoE methods cause routing collapse and parameter explosion.

Method: CoRDE extracts semantic distributions from a frozen concept encoder, uses a learnable soft mapping matrix to guide variational posterior responsibility with entropy-controlled routing, and employs LoRA-based parameter-efficient experts on a shared frozen backbone to avoid parameter inflation.

Result: CoRDE systematically reduces routing collapse, forms semantically aligned expert allocations, achieves superior action quality, and demonstrates incremental learning efficiency compared to existing baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed structure-guided variational distillation framework with entropy-controlled routing and low-rank expert adaptation effectively addresses structural generalization limitations and parameter explosion in multi-task robot imitation learning.

Abstract: Diffusion models excel at capturing multi-modal action distributions in robot imitation learning. However, in multi-task and long-horizon scenarios, monolithic architectures lack structural generalization capabilities, suffering from gradient conflicts between distinct semantic sub-stages. While pure data-driven Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) methods introduce labor division, they frequently trigger routing collapse, and instantiating full-scale experts causes parameter explosion and high expansion costs. To address these issues, we propose Concept-prior Routed Diffusion Experts (CoRDE), a structure-guided variational distillation framework. CoRDE extracts semantic distributions from a frozen concept encoder to guide the variational posterior responsibility via a learnable soft mapping matrix. This mechanism introduces an entropy-controlled responsibility inference process that encourages confident routing under reliable semantic predictions while preserving the stochastic diffusion term for behavioral diversity. To overcome parameter inflation, CoRDE employs a parameter-efficient expert pool using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) on a shared frozen backbone. Theoretical analysis shows that the mixture score discrepancy is bounded by responsibility-weighted local expert errors, supporting high-fidelity generation under low-rank expert adaptation. Empirical evaluations confirm that, compared to existing baselines, CoRDE systematically reduces routing collapse, forming robust, semantically aligned expert allocations while achieving superior action quality and incremental learning efficiency.

</details>


### [353] [RARM: Confidence-Gated Progress Reward Modeling for RL in Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22027)
*Pengzhi Yang,Xinyu Wang,Pengyu Jing,Kehan Wen,Yiduo Qu,Zhenhao Huang,Minghao Fu,Xin Liu,Yaheng Shen,Fan Shi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RARM is a lightweight visual comparator that converts a single successful demonstration into dense, progress-aware rewards for robot manipulation RL, achieving strong results across simulation and real-world tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforcement learning for robot manipulation is bottlenecked by reward design: sparse rewards provide weak supervision, and hand-crafted dense rewards are tedious and generalize poorly. Progress-based reward models are promising but often require task-specific demonstrations or labels.

Method: RARM is a visual comparator trained once on general-purpose videos with a contrastive temporal objective. It matches rollout clips to a single reference demonstration clip and rewards only confident forward progress, suppressing uncertain matches to avoid false-positive rewards.

Result: Across 9 simulated tasks (LIBERO, MetaWorld) and 4 real-world tasks, RARM achieves the best overall success rates in subsequent RL training, with large gains on long-horizon tasks like cloth folding.

Conclusion: RARM effectively replaces hand-crafted reward engineering by leveraging a single demonstration as a reference anchor, enabling dense progress-aware rewards without task-specific data or reward labels.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning for robot manipulation is often bottlenecked by reward design, especially in long-horizon tasks: sparse success rewards provide weak supervision, while hand-crafted dense rewards are tedious to design and generalize poorly across tasks. Progress-based reward models offer a promising alternative by estimating how far an observation has advanced toward task completion, but existing approaches often require task-specific demonstrations or progress labels, and can assign high rewards to visually plausible but physically incorrect states. We introduce the Reference-Anchored Reward Model (RARM), a lightweight visual comparator that converts a single successful demonstration into a dense, progress-aware reward. RARM is trained once on general-purpose videos with a contrastive temporal objective, requiring no robot-specific data, task-specific reward labels, or per-task reward engineering. At deployment, RARM matches rollout clips to reference clips and rewards only confident forward progress, suppressing uncertain matches that may otherwise produce false-positive rewards. Across 9 simulated manipulation tasks from LIBERO and MetaWorld and 4 real-world tasks, RARM achieves the best overall success rates in subsequent RL training, with particularly large gains on long-horizon tasks such as cloth folding, where unreliable progress estimates are especially harmful.

</details>


### [354] [Deep RL- Tuned Mo del-Free Adaptive Control for Lower-Limb Exoskeletons During Sit-to-Stand Transitions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22040)
*Ranjeet Kumbhar,Appaso M. Gadade,Rajmeet Singh,Ashish Singla,Ravinder Kumar*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes an intelligent model-free adaptive backstepping control strategy for bilateral lower-limb exoskeletons during sit-to-stand (STS) motion, combining an ultra-local model, RBF neural network, and TD3 reinforcement learning for adaptive gain scheduling. The controller achieves superior tracking accuracy (average RMSE 0.078°) with 60.2% improvement over PID and significant reductions across all joints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: STS transitions impose high joint-loading demands on elderly individuals, making them a key target for exoskeleton assistance. However, accurate trajectory tracking is challenging due to complex time-varying human-exoskeleton interaction dynamics and inter-subject variability, which render model-based control approaches difficult to apply in practice.

Method: The controller uses an ultra-local second-order model to avoid explicit system identification, a Gaussian RBF neural network to estimate unknown lumped dynamics online, and a TD3 reinforcement learning agent as a supervisory gain scheduler to adaptively adjust controller gains across distinct STS phases.

Result: The proposed controller achieves the lowest average RMSE of 0.078° across all joints, with improvements of 60.2%, 54.4%, 48.7%, and 42.6% over PID, MFAC, LQR, and SMC, respectively. TD3 integration further reduces tracking error by 35%, 33%, and 79% at hip, knee, and ankle joints compared to the RBF-MFAC baseline.

Conclusion: The results demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed controller design for assistive exoskeleton control during STS transitions, highlighting its potential for practical applications in elderly assistance.

Abstract: Sit-to-stand (STS) transitions impose significant joint-loading demands on elderly individuals, making them a primary target for lower-limb exoskeleton assistance. However, accurate trajectory tracking during STS is challenging due to complex, time-varying human exoskeleton interaction dynamics and inter-subject variability that render model-based control approaches difficult to apply in practice. This paper presents an intelligent model free adaptive backstepping control strategy for a bilateral lower-limb exoskeleton during STS motion. The proposed controller design uses an ultra-local second-order model to avoid explicit system identification, while a Gaussian radial basis function (RBF) neural network estimates the unknown lumped dynamics online. To further improve phase-aware tracking performance, a Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (TD3) reinforcement learning agent is integrated as a supervisory gain scheduler that adaptively adjusts controller gains across the distinct phases of STS motion. The proposed controller is evaluated through co-simulation in MATLAB/Simulink and Simscape Multibody using OpenSim-derived reference trajectories and benchmarked against state-of-the-art controllers. Results demonstrate that the proposed controller achieves the lowest average RMSE of 0.078 degree across all joints, representing improvements of 60.2%, 54.4%, 48.7%, and 42.6% over proportional integral derivative (PID), model-free adaptive control (MFAC), linear quadratic regulator (LQR), and sliding-mode control (SMC), respectively. TD3 integration further reduces tracking error by 35%, 33%, and 79% at the hip, knee, and ankle joints compared to the standalone RBF-MFAC baseline. These results demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed controller design for assistive exoskeleton control during STS transitions.

</details>


### [355] [A Multimodal Tiltwing Framework for Bioinspired Aerial Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22046)
*Krispin C. V. Broers,Sophie F. Armanini*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes an adaptable tiltwing framework for tailless flapping-wing micro-aerial vehicles (FWMAVs) that enables switching between hovering, high-speed directional flight, and energy-efficient gliding. The design uses dual independent propulsion units with a hybrid Scotch-yoke flapping mechanism, passive wing-rotation, and thrust vectoring for enhanced control. Testing shows 21.1 gf lift per wing at 188° flapping amplitude with a 3.1g motor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing tailless FWMAV designs have limited flight envelopes, poor endurance, and are purpose-built for specific flight modes. There is a need for a more versatile aerial robot capable of multiple flight regimes (hovering, high-speed flight, gliding).

Method: The authors propose a tiltwing framework with two fully independent propulsion units (each flapping a single wing) for thrust vectoring. Key components include: (1) a hybrid Scotch-yoke flapping mechanism for symmetric motion and wide flapping angles, (2) a passive wing-rotation mechanism for wing-design freedom, (3) a contactless leading-edge tracking sensor for wing orientation feedback and dihedral-angle control, and (4) wide-angle tilting capability for thrust-vectoring control.

Result: Testing with a six-axis force/torque sensor showed: (1) at full throttle with 188° flapping amplitude, average lift force of 21.1 gf per wing using a small 3.1g 1S BLDC motor; (2) effective thrust-vectoring control with linear and symmetric moment response across the full wide-angle tilting range.

Conclusion: The proposed adaptable tiltwing framework successfully enables multiple flight modes (hovering, high-speed directional flight, gliding) in tailless FWMAVs. The dual-wing thrust-vectoring approach combined with passive wing rotation offers unprecedented wing-design freedom, and the system demonstrates effective performance with linear control characteristics.

Abstract: Tailless flapping-wing micro-aerial vehicles (FWMAVs) mimic the impressive flight performance of hummingbirds, utilising unsteady aerodynamic effects. However, existing designs are still limited and purpose-built with a restricted flight envelope and poor endurance. We therefore propose an adaptable tiltwing framework enabling bioinspired aerial robots to switch between hovering flight, high-speed directional flight, and energy-efficient gliding flight. The proposed framework utilises thrust vectoring with a wide actuation range via two fully independent propulsion units, each flapping a single wing, for effective control and enhanced manoeuvrability. For this, we developed a hybrid Scotch-yoke-based flapping mechanism that ensures a symmetric motion profile with a modular design guaranteeing an arbitrarily wide flapping angle to exploit the lift-enhancing clap-and-fling effect. Additionally, we implemented a passive wing-rotation mechanism, which, in combination with our dual-wing thrust-vectoring approach, allows unprecedented wing-design freedom, unlocking potential for precise optimisation. A contactless leading-edge tracking sensor provides accurate feedback on the wing's orientation and, in the gliding mode, enables dihedral-angle control, augmenting the active wing-pitch control. Extensive testing of a propulsion unit was conducted with a six-axis force/torque sensor, demonstrating the flapping mechanism's performance while optimising transmission efficiency and the passive wing-pitch mechanism. At full throttle, the average lift force generated by a single wing, flapping with a 188° amplitude, was 21.1 gf for a small 3.1 g 1S BLDC motor. Additional tests covering the full range of the wide-angle tilting capability showed an effective thrust-vectoring control architecture with a linear and symmetric response curve of the moments generated.

</details>


### [356] [GeoFlow-SLAM++: A Robust Multi-Camera Visual-Inertial SLAM System with Relocalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22051)
*Wei Fen,Tingyang Xiao,Liu Liu,Xiaolin Zhou,Zhizhong Su*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GeoFlow-SLAM++ extends GeoFlow-SLAM from single RGB-D to a multi-camera visual-inertial SLAM system that unifies tracking, mapping, and relocalization. It supports ORB and neural network feature front-ends, combines multi-camera constraints, IMU, place recognition, and optional pseudo-depth, achieving robust and accurate localization across multiple datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Monocular/RGB-D visual-inertial SLAM suffers from limited field of view, sensor-specific failures, and unreliable cross-session relocalization.

Method: Extends GeoFlow-SLAM to a calibrated multi-camera rig with interchangeable ORB and NN-Feature (SuperPoint+LightGlue) front-ends; unifies tracking, mapping, and relocalization on a shared body state; integrates multi-camera reprojection, IMU pre-integration, cross-view place recognition, dual-stream optical flow/NN-Feature tracking, and optional pseudo-depth.

Result: Evaluated on EuRoC, OpenLORIS, TUM, Hilti, and a self-collected multi-camera dataset, showing improved robustness with NN-Features, competitive accuracy on Hilti, and LiDAR-comparable performance on the handheld dataset.

Conclusion: The multi-camera formulation with unified relocalization and flexible front-ends significantly enhances robustness and accuracy, achieving LiDAR-comparable results without depth sensors.

Abstract: Monocular and RGB-D visual-inertial SLAM systems remain susceptible to limited field of view, sensor-specific failure modes, and unreliable cross-session relocalization. To address these issues, we present GeoFlow-SLAM++, a tightly coupled multi-camera visual-inertial SLAM system that extends GeoFlow-SLAM from a single RGB-D sensor to a calibrated multi-camera rig with a unified body-centric formulation. Within this multi-camera framework, GeoFlow-SLAM++ supports two interchangeable visual front-ends: a conventional ORB front-end and a neural network feature (NN-Feature) front-end built on SuperPoint and LightGlue. The system unifies tracking, mapping, and relocalization on a shared body state, and combines multi-camera reprojection constraints, IMU pre-integration, cross-view place recognition, and dual-stream optical flow/NN-Feature tracking for robust localization. As an optional extension, the system can further incorporate cross-view-consistent pseudo-depth predictions from RGB images as auxiliary geometric constraints. We evaluate GeoFlow-SLAM++ on EuRoC, OpenLORIS, TUM, Hilti, and a self-collected handheld multi-camera dataset. Results show that the NN-Feature front-end improves robustness in appearance-challenging scenarios, the multi-camera formulation achieves competitive localization accuracy on Hilti, and the unified cross-view relocalization design reaches LiDAR-comparable performance on the handheld dataset.

</details>


### [357] [How Should a Simulation-to-Reality Transfer Budget Be Spent?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22062)
*Syed Hamzah Rizvi,Yash Vardhan Tomar*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper studies the tradeoff between system identification (measuring real robot parameters) and domain randomization (training over varied simulations) in sim-to-real transfer, finding that a small number of identification rollouts closes most of the transfer gap, and policies perform best when trained at estimated parameters rather than using broad randomization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The tradeoff between measuring a system more accurately and training over a broader range of simulated dynamics in sim-to-real transfer is still poorly understood.

Method: Controlled sim-to-sim pendulum experiments where a hidden-parameter model stands for the physical robot, sweeping identification rollouts against the width of the randomization distribution across various reality gaps and noise levels.

Result: Measurement budget did most of the work; a small number of identification rollouts closed most of the transfer gap; policies performed best at estimated parameters rather than widened randomization; broad randomization did not substitute for measurement.

Conclusion: Sim-to-real pipelines should first measure the parameters they can and reserve randomization for the uncertainty that remains, though results hold only in benign, identifiable regimes without structural model mismatch.

Abstract: Simulation-to-reality transfer, often called sim-to-real transfer, is a central challenge in robot learning. Yet, the tradeoff between measuring a system more accurately and training over a broader range of simulated dynamics is still poorly understood. In this work, we focused on the allocation of real-robot measurement time between system identification and domain randomization. We studied this tradeoff in a controlled sim-to-sim pendulum setting, where a hidden-parameter model stands in for the physical robot, and the experiment sweeps identification rollouts against the width of the randomization distribution. Across the reality gaps and noise levels we tested, the measurement budget did most of the work. A small number of identification rollouts closed most of the transfer gap, and once any real data was available, policies performed best when trained at the estimated parameters rather than over a widened randomization band. Broad randomization that contained the true system still did not substitute for measurement. These results hold in a benign regime where the dynamics are identifiable and only two parameters are unknown, so structural model mismatch remains the setting where randomization breadth may become more valuable. Overall, our results suggest that sim-to-real pipelines should first measure the parameters they can and reserve randomization for the uncertainty that remains.

</details>


### [358] [Dynamics, stability, and energy efficiency of an energy-recycling rimless wheel with spring-clutch legs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22073)
*Tongchen Lin,Yanqiu Zheng,Chuhan Zhang,Ruigang Chen,Yizhar Or,Mingyi Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents an energy-recycling rimless wheel with spring-clutch legs that stores impact energy and reinjects it into the next gait cycle, achieving up to 16.13% CoT reduction vs. viscoelastic models and demonstrating passive walking on a 1° slope in prototype experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve walking efficiency by recycling impact-induced elastic energy that is otherwise dissipated, reducing the Cost of Transport (CoT) in legged locomotion.

Method: A hybrid dynamic model of the rimless wheel with lockable spring-clutch legs was developed, analyzed via numerical simulations for stability and CoT, and validated through prototype experiments on an inclined plane.

Result: The spring-clutch mechanism reduced CoT by up to 16.13% vs. viscoelastic-legged rimless wheels. Both viscoelastic and energy-recycling models reduced CoT by >50% vs. rigid rimless wheels. The prototype achieved passive walking on a 1° slope with CoT ≈ 0.02.

Conclusion: The proposed spring-clutch mechanism improves simulated walking efficiency, and prototype experiments confirm the feasibility of passive walking with this energy-recycling design.

Abstract: This paper proposes an energy-recycling rimless wheel with spring-clutch legs. The proposed mechanism uses a lockable clutch to store part of the impact-induced elastic energy after foot contact and reinject it in the next gait cycle. First, we develop a hybrid dynamic model of the energy-recycling rimless wheel. Second, numerical simulations are used to examine the dynamics, local stability of periodic gaits, and the Cost of Transport (CoT) of the proposed mechanism. The simulation results show that the proposed mechanism reduces the CoT by up to 16.13% compared with a benchmark viscoelastic-legged rimless wheel with telescopic spring-damper legs. Compared with the rigid rimless wheel, the viscoelastic-legged and energy-recycling models reduce the CoT by more than 50%. The energy-recycling model also maintains locally stable periodic gaits over the tested slope and stiffness ranges. Finally, prototype experiments on an inclined plane are conducted to examine the feasibility of the proposed mechanism. The experimental results show that the proposed rimless wheel achieves passive walking on a shallow 1° slope, corresponding to a CoT of approximately 0.02. These results suggest that the proposed spring-clutch mechanism can improve the simulated walking efficiency of the energy-recycling rimless wheel, while the prototype experiments support the feasibility of passive walking with the mechanism.

</details>


### [359] [ACEsplat: Accelerated 3D Gaussian Scene Regression via RGB and Poses Only](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22091)
*Mingkai Liu,Haohua Que,Dikai Fan,Haojia Gao,Tianle Zhu,Handong Yao,Qian Zhang,Ruopeng Zhang,Xianliang Huang,Fei Qiao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ACEsplat is a fast per-scene optimization framework that reconstructs 3D Gaussian representations from only RGB images and camera poses, without requiring external 3D priors like SfM or depth maps, achieving competitive rendering quality in 15-25 minutes on a single GPU.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 3DGS methods rely on external geometric initialization (e.g., SfM point clouds or depth estimates), which is slow and brittle for on-site robotic and AR deployments. The goal is to enable rapid, RGB+pose-only scene reconstruction without external 3D priors.

Method: Two-stage pipeline: (1) Self-supervised scene coordinate regression (SCR) module builds an internal geometry prior in 4-5 minutes; (2) SCR features and coordinate priors are fused by a lightweight Gaussian initialization head, followed by per-scene 3DGS optimization.

Result: 29.11 dB PSNR on Wayspots with real-time SLAM poses; 33.20 dB on Cambridge Landmarks with SfM-refined poses; competitive performance on RealEstate10K sparse-view novel view synthesis under 2-view setting; completes mapping and reconstruction in 15-25 minutes on a single GPU.

Conclusion: ACEsplat is a practical RGB+pose-only solution for rapid scene setup in robotics and mixed-reality applications, eliminating the dependency on slow external geometric initialization.

Abstract: Per-scene 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) enables high-fidelity rendering, but practical robotic and AR scene capture pipelines often depend on external geometric initialization (e.g., SfM point clouds or depth estimates), which can be slow and brittle in on-site deployment. We present ACEsplat, a fast per-scene optimization framework that reconstructs 3D Gaussian representations from RGB images and camera poses only, without requiring external 3D priors (e.g., precomputed SfM models or supervised depth maps). ACEsplat uses a two-stage pipeline: (1) a self-supervised scene coordinate regression (SCR) module builds an internal geometry prior within 4--5 minutes; (2) SCR features and coordinate priors are fused by a lightweight Gaussian initialization head, followed by per-scene 3DGS optimization. On static-view rendering, ACEsplat achieves 29.11 dB PSNR on Wayspots with real-time SLAM poses and 33.20 dB on Cambridge Landmarks with SfM-refined poses. On RealEstate10K sparse-view novel view synthesis, it achieves competitive image fidelity under a challenging 2-view setting. ACEsplat completes scene-specific SCR mapping and 3DGS reconstruction within 15--25 minutes on a single GPU, making it a practical RGB+pose-only solution for rapid scene setup in robotics and mixed-reality applications.

</details>


### [360] [KITE: Decoupling Kinematics and Interaction for Zero-Shot Cross-Embodiment Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22113)
*Qianxu Wang,Kuan Fang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: KITE is a framework for zero-shot cross-embodiment robot manipulation that decouples task reasoning from embodiment-specific control using learned latent intents based on contact patterns, enabling policy transfer across structurally different robots (e.g., grippers, hands) without retraining on new demonstrations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalizing manipulation policies across different robot embodiments is difficult because standard policies entangle task reasoning with embodiment-specific motor control, requiring retraining for each new robot.

Method: KITE decouples manipulation into: (1) embodiment-agnostic task reasoning via a shared policy predicting latent intents based on contact patterns, and (2) embodiment-specific motor control via an intent-conditioned action decoder trained from each embodiment's kinematic model. Adaptation to new embodiments only requires training a new action decoder without recollecting demonstrations.

Result: KITE achieves zero-shot transfer across parallel grippers, dexterous hands, and composite embodiments on three manipulation tasks, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines in transfer success and task-embodiment scope.

Conclusion: Decoupling task reasoning from embodiment-specific control via learned latent intents enables effective zero-shot cross-embodiment policy transfer, significantly reducing the need for new demonstrations when deploying on structurally different robots.

Abstract: Generalizing manipulation policies across robot embodiments remains difficult because standard policies entangle task reasoning with embodiment-specific motor control. We study zero-shot cross-embodiment manipulation, where a policy trained on source embodiments must be deployed on a structurally different target embodiment without additional task demonstrations. We introduce Kinematic Interaction Transfer across Embodiments (KITE), which decouples manipulation into embodiment-agnostic task reasoning and embodiment-specific motor control, connected through a learned latent representation of interaction intent based on contact patterns. Task reasoning is performed by a shared policy that predicts latent intents from source demonstrations, while motor control is performed by an intent-conditioned action decoder learned from each embodiment's kinematic model. With KITE, adaptation to a new embodiment requires only training a new action decoder using its kinematic model, without recollecting demonstration data. We evaluate KITE on three manipulation tasks spanning transfer between parallel grippers, dexterous hands, and composite embodiments. KITE consistently achieves zero-shot transfer to structurally different target embodiments, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines in transfer success and task-embodiment scope.

</details>


### [361] [DeformX: A Versatile Co-Simulation Framework for Deformable Linear Objects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22116)
*Yi Yang,Xiang Fei,Lehong Wang,Chenhao Li,Zilin Dai,Henry Kou,Lu Li,Howie Choset*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DeformX is a co-simulation framework that integrates a Cosserat rod physics engine with NVIDIA Isaac Sim, enabling physically faithful and visually realistic simulation of deformable linear objects (DLOs) like wires and ropes. It supports robot learning pipelines, achieving strong sim-to-real transfer results including a 10.2% mAP@75 improvement in real wire segmentation and 6.6 cm mean error in real-world rope-swinging policy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DLO simulation methods either lack physically grounded deformation behavior (visual methods using procedural primitives) or fail to capture bending/twisting/shear mechanics of slender structures (physics-based methods using rigid-link chains or generic soft bodies). There is no unified framework that combines realistic visualization, principled physics, and robot learning compatibility.

Method: DeformX integrates a dedicated Cosserat rod physics engine with NVIDIA Isaac Sim. The Cosserat rod engine simulates dynamics, self-collisions of DLOs, and contact interactions with arbitrary free-form meshes. Mesh skinning maps discrete rod deformations onto imported CAD models for high-fidelity visualization.

Result: Fine-tuning SAM3 on DeformX-generated data yields 10.2% mAP@75 improvement in real-image wire segmentation. A rope-swinging policy trained entirely in DeformX achieves 6.6 cm mean target-hitting error on a real UR5e manipulator.

Conclusion: DeformX is one of the first frameworks unifying realistic visualization, principled physics, and robot learning pipeline compatibility for DLO simulation. Strong sim-to-real transfer results demonstrate its effectiveness for synthetic data generation and policy learning.

Abstract: Deformable linear objects (DLOs) such as wires, cables, and ropes are common in robotic manipulation tasks, yet simulating them with both visual realism and physical accuracy remains challenging. Existing visual simulation methods typically rely on procedural geometric primitives that lack physically grounded deformation behavior, while physics-based approaches with robot learning support often approximate DLOs as rigid-link chains or generic soft bodies, failing to accurately capture the bending, twisting, and shear mechanics of slender elastic structures. In this work, we introduce DeformX, a co-simulation framework that integrates a dedicated Cosserat rod physics engine with NVIDIA Isaac Sim, enabling DLO simulations that are both physically faithful and visually realistic. Our Cosserat rod engine simulates the dynamics and self-collisions of DLOs, and contact interactions with arbitrary free-form meshes. To achieve high-fidelity visualization, we employ mesh skinning to map discrete rod deformations onto imported CAD models. To the best of our knowledge, DeformX is the one of the first frameworks for DLO simulation that unifies realistic visualization, principled physics, and compatibility with robot learning pipelines. We demonstrate its versatility across synthetic data generation and policy learning for DLO manipulation, and validate visual and physical fidelity through comparisons against real-world experiments. Notably, fine-tuning Segment Anything Model 3 (SAM3) on DeformX-generated data yields a 10.2% mAP@75 improvement in real-image wire segmentation, and a rope-swinging policy trained entirely in DeformX achieves a mean target-hitting error of 6.6 cm on a UR5e manipulator in real-world trials, highlighting its strong sim-to-real transfer capability.

</details>


### [362] [Durability-Aware Multi-Objective Optimization of the Jansen Linkage: Trading Gait Quality Against Joint Wear](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22129)
*Jichao Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces durability optimization into Jansen linkage design by coupling kinematic, dynamic, and wear models with NSGA-II, achieving designs that reduce joint wear by ~56% while improving gait quality, with robustness validated through sensitivity, Sobol, and Monte-Carlo tolerance analyses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The original Jansen linkage was optimized solely for foot-path gait quality, ignoring joint wear and durability. This paper aims to incorporate a durability (wear) objective into the design process.

Method: A parametric forward-kinematic model (two-circle-intersection), inverse-dynamic model (constraint-Jacobian/Lagrange-multiplier for a seven-body, ten-joint system, cross-verified by reduced-DOF energy method), and Archard wear model are coupled. A bi-objective optimization (gait error vs. total joint wear) is solved using NSGA-II, with constraints on step length, ground clearance, duty factor, and assembly. Sensitivity and Sobol analyses identify dominant wear parameters, and Monte-Carlo simulation tests manufacturing tolerance effects.

Result: The classical Jansen design is Pareto-dominated. A representative design with link-length adjustments within ±29% reduces stance error by -28%, smooths stance velocity by -58%, and reduces total joint wear by ~56%. Wear advantage is robust (48%-56%) across crank-speed × payload envelope. Two link lengths dominate wear variance. Wear advantage degrades gracefully under realistic fabrication error.

Conclusion: The framework provides a practical route to longer-lived walking linkages and establishes a baseline for future wear-clearance-impact coupled studies.

Abstract: The Jansen linkage is a single-degree-of-freedom planar leg mechanism whose eleven "holy numbers" were evolved by Theo Jansen to optimize the foot-path gait alone, with no regard for the wear of its revolute joints. This paper introduces a durability objective into the design of the Jansen leg. A parametric forward-kinematic model (two-circle-intersection solver), an inverse-dynamic model (constraint-Jacobian / Lagrange-multiplier formulation of a seven-body, ten-joint system, independently cross-verified by a reduced-DOF energy method), and an Archard wear model are coupled to evaluate, for any set of link lengths, both gait quality and the per-cycle sliding wear at every pin. Because the wear is computed on ideal, clearance-free revolute joints, the resulting wear figures are a relative comparative ranking rather than an absolute life prediction. A bi-objective problem -- composite gait error versus total joint wear, subject to step-length, ground-clearance, duty-factor and assembly constraints -- is solved with NSGA-II. Under the adopted gait metric the classical Jansen design is Pareto-dominated: for a representative design, link-length adjustments within +/-29% simultaneously flatten the stance (-28%), smooth the stance velocity (-58%) and reduce total joint wear by ~56%. A sensitivity study shows the wear advantage is robust across a crank-speed x payload envelope (48%-56%) and identifies the link lengths that most strongly govern wear. A variance-based global (Sobol) analysis confirms that two link lengths dominate the wear variance, and a Monte-Carlo manufacturing-tolerance study shows the wear advantage degrades gracefully under realistic fabrication error. The framework provides a practical route to longer-lived walking linkages and a baseline for future wear-clearance-impact coupled studies.

</details>


### [363] [Wh0: Generative World Models as Scalable Sources of Egocentric Human Hand Manipulation Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22136)
*Yangtao Chen,Zixuan Chen,Peiyang Wang,Yong-Lu Li,Jing Huo,Jieqi Shi,Yang Gao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Wh0 uses generative video world models to produce a large-scale dataset (WM-H) of egocentric human-hand manipulation videos, which are converted into robot-trainable supervision via hand motion reconstruction and visual editing, boosting zero-shot dexterous manipulation success from 8.3% to 38.9% on real-world tasks when co-trained with limited robot data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing data sources for dexterous manipulation face a trade-off between scale and alignment: teleoperation is expensive, simulation suffers from sim-to-real gap, and real egocentric videos lack robot embodiment alignment.

Method: Wh0 uses a generative video world model conditioned on language, objects, and scenes to produce WM-H (50k episodes of egocentric human-object interaction videos), then converts them into robot-trainable supervision through hand motion reconstruction and visual editing, co-trained with limited real robot data.

Result: Across 18 real-world dexterous manipulation tasks, Wh0 improves zero-shot success from 8.3% to 38.9% compared to model post-trained only on robot data.

Conclusion: Scalable generation and scene/embodiment alignment are key drivers of performance gains for dexterous manipulation in VLA models.

Abstract: Scaling dexterous manipulation requires generalization across objects, scenes, and tasks, yet existing data sources face a trade-off between scale and scene/embodiment alignment: teleoperation data is well aligned with robot deployment but expensive to collect; simulation is scalable but limited by the sim-to-real gap; and real egocentric videos scale effectively but remain misaligned with robot deployment. We propose Wh0, a framework that uses generative video world models as scalable and controllable sources of egocentric human-hand manipulation data to unlock the manipulation capabilities of pretrained dexterous VLA models. Conditioned on language, objects, and scenes, Wh0 uses a generative world model to produce WM-H, a 50k-episode dataset of egocentric human-object interaction videos. Wh0 then converts the generated videos into robot-trainable supervision through hand motion reconstruction and visual editing. Co-trained with a limited amount of real robot data, WM-H adapts pretrained VLA models to dexterous manipulation deployment. Across 18 real-world dexterous manipulation tasks, compared with a model post-trained only on robot data, Wh0 improves zero-shot success on unseen tasks from 8.3% to 38.9%. Ablation studies further show that scalable generation and scene/embodiment alignment are key drivers of performance gains. Videos and open-source code can be found on our project website: https://chenyt31.github.io/wh0.github.io/.

</details>


### [364] [RoboLineage: Agent-Native Data Lifecycle Governance Across Robot Policy Iterations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22142)
*Qian Luo,Wentao Guo,Zhennan Qin,Nanchun Guo,Yunhan Zhao,Yi Ma,Yanchao Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RoboLineage is an agent-native data lifecycle governance system that makes robot policy iteration explicit and auditable by representing rollouts, reviews, datasets, training, evaluations, and deployment as typed lineage artifacts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evidence connecting data collection, review, retraining, evaluation, and release in robot policy iteration is scattered across local tools, scripts, and expert memory, making the process slow and un-auditable.

Method: RoboLineage represents all lifecycle components (rollouts, reviews, dataset decisions, training runs, policy metadata, evaluations, deployment recommendations, collection plans) as typed lineage artifacts. Agents interpret embodied evidence, adapt accepted data to training stacks, maintain data health, and summarize cross-iteration state under artifact boundaries.

Result: In real-robot manipulation workflows, RoboLineage makes routine policy iteration faster and more auditable while maintaining downstream policy performance.

Conclusion: RoboLineage is open-sourced as a lightweight lifecycle layer applicable to different robot embodiments and training families.

Abstract: We present RoboLineage, an agent-native data lifecycle governance system for robot policy iteration. Modern robot policies improve through repeated data collection, review, retraining, evaluation, and release decisions, but the evidence connecting these steps is often scattered across local tools, scripts, and expert memory. RoboLineage makes this lifecycle explicit by representing rollouts, reviews, dataset decisions, training runs, policy metadata, evaluations, deployment recommendations, and next-collection plans as typed lineage artifacts. Agents interpret embodied rollout evidence, adapt accepted data to existing training stacks, maintain data health, and summarize cross-iteration state under explicit artifact boundaries. In real-robot manipulation workflows, RoboLineage makes routine policy iteration faster and more auditable while maintaining downstream policy performance. We open source RoboLineage as a lightweight lifecycle layer for different robot embodiments and training families. Project page: https://robolineage.github.io/

</details>


### [365] [Physics-Informed Eikonal Caging for Whole-Arm Manipulation Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22143)
*Yan Zhang,Yiming Li,Yifei Dong,Florian T. Pokorny,Sylvain Calinon*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A method reformulates caging as a minimum-time escape problem solved via physics-informed neural networks to produce a differentiable escape-time field, enabling robust whole-arm manipulation planning without precise contact models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Planning contact-rich whole-arm manipulation is challenging because complex contact dynamics are difficult to model accurately, creating a need for planning principles that don't rely heavily on precise contact models.

Method: Reformulates caging as a minimum-time escape problem where the object tries to leave enclosing robot geometry; shows the escape-time field satisfies an eikonal equation; approximates this field using a physics-informed neural network (PINN) to produce a smooth differentiable representation for integration into optimization-based planning.

Result: Improved robustness to disturbances and contact-model mismatch relative to baselines in both simulation and real-world experiments. Enables planning with simplified contact models and object geometry.

Conclusion: Geometric enclosure (caging) can serve as a practical robustness primitive for whole-arm manipulation, allowing simplified contact models while maintaining robust performance.

Abstract: Planning contact-rich whole-arm manipulation is challenging because interactions that involve extended robot geometry give rise to complex contact dynamics that are difficult to model accurately. This creates a need for planning principles that do not rely heavily on precise contact models. Caging offers one such geometric notion of robustness to modeling inaccuracy by restricting object escape through geometrically enclosing the object. However, existing caging formulations are difficult to incorporate into continuous optimization-based manipulation planning. We reformulate caging as a minimum-time escape problem in which the object seeks to leave an enclosing robot geometry in the shortest time. This yields a continuous escape-time field that measures the robot's enclosure quality and we show it satisfies an eikonal equation. We therefore can approximate this field using a physics-informed neural network, producing a smooth differentiable representation that can be embedded directly into manipulation planning. The resulting objective supports whole-arm manipulation planning to favor robot configurations resisting object escape. This improves the manipulation robustness to contact model mismatch, thus enabling planning with simplified contact models, including quasi-dynamic approximations and simplified object geometry. Across simulation and real-world experiments, we show improved robustness to disturbances and contact-model mismatch relative to baselines. These results suggest that geometric enclosure can serve as a practical robustness primitive for whole-arm manipulation. A supplementary video, which includes an intuitive overview of our method and experiment video results, is available on our project webpage.

</details>


### [366] [Zero-shot Transfer of Reinforcement Learning Control Policies for the Swing-Up and Stabilization of a Cart-Pole System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22145)
*Nikki Xu,Hien Tran*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper demonstrates zero-shot transfer of RL-based control policies from simulation to hardware for cart-pole swing-up and stabilization, using two independently trained policies with a switching logic handoff, and achieving successful real-world performance through first-order action smoothing, domain randomization, and curriculum learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To modernize controller design using RL and enable zero-shot transfer of learned policies from simulation to real hardware without fine-tuning on the physical system.

Method: Two independently trained RL policies (swing-up and stabilization) with Simulink-based switching logic for handoff; first-order action smoothing filter to prevent hardware damage; sensitivity-guided domain randomization (DR); linear curriculum learning (CL) schedule.

Result: Swing-up policy consistently injects sufficient energy for handoff; stabilization policy rejects disturbances within tested range; swing-up policy re-engages after larger perturbations and restores pendulum to inverted position.

Conclusion: Combining bandwidth-aware filtering with domain randomization and curriculum learning enables successful zero-shot sim-to-real transfer of RL policies for cart-pole control.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) is a powerful and convenient tool to modernize controller design. In this work, we study the zero-shot transfer of RL-based control policies from simulation to hardware for cart-pole swing-up and stabilization. The two policies are trained independently, and the handoff is implemented in Simulink via switching logic. We apply a first-order action smoothing filter to prevent hardware damage from high-frequency oscillatory actuation. Pairing this bandwidth-aware filtering with sensitivity-guided domain randomization (DR) and a simple linear curriculum learning (CL) schedule, we obtain a swing-up policy that in all of our experiments injects sufficient energy for handoff into the stabilizer's region of attraction. The stabilization policy rejects disturbances within the tested range, and the swing-up policy can re-engage after larger perturbations and restores the pendulum to the inverted position.

</details>


### [367] [Full Nonlinear Nonholonomic Dynamics and Motion Analysis of a 3-DoF Underactuated Spherical Rolling Robot](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22169)
*Lakshmesha Krishnapa,Ahnaf Sharaar Mazahar,Seyed Amir Tafrishi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a full nonlinear constrained dynamic model for MonoRollBot, a spherical rolling robot with a single motor and internal spring-coupled mass, using Lagrange-d'Alembert formulation to handle nonholonomic rolling constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide one of the first full nonlinear nonholonomic models for a mono-actuated, super-underactuated spherical rolling robot, enabling understanding of its locomotion and serving as a basis for future control design.

Method: Lagrange-d'Alembert formulation with holonomic (lead-screw) and nonholonomic (rolling without slipping) constraints; retains full generalized coordinates including shell translation, attitude, screw travel, nut rotation, and radial mass motion.

Result: Simulations show qualitative agreement with prototype behavior, revealing how gravity, compliance, and inertia jointly shape locomotion in this strongly underactuated robot.

Conclusion: The model provides a mechanically consistent foundation for future state estimation and hybrid controller design for this nonholonomic mono-actuated rolling robot.

Abstract: This paper presents a full nonlinear constrained dynamic model of MonoRollBot, a novel 3-DoF spherical rolling robot driven by a single motor, a lead-screw transmission, and a spring-coupled internal moving mass, together with motion analysis of its behavior. To the best of our knowledge, this is one of the first full nonlinear nonholonomic models reported for a mono-actuated, super-underactuated spherical rolling robot of this kind. Because rolling without slipping is nonholonomic, the dynamics are derived using the Lagrange--d'Alembert formulation, with the lead-screw relation imposed as a holonomic constraint and the rolling condition imposed in Pfaffian form. The formulation retains the complete generalized coordinates of shell translation, shell attitude, screw travel, nut rotation, and radial mass motion. Simulations and representative motion studies show qualitative agreement with prototype behavior and reveal how gravity, compliance, and inertia jointly shape the locomotion and motion capabilities of this strongly underactuated robot. The resulting model also provides a mechanically consistent basis for future state estimation and hybrid controller design for this nonholonomic mono-actuated rolling robot.

</details>


### [368] [OpenHLM: An Empirical Recipe for Whole-Body Humanoid Loco-Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22174)
*Yingdong Hu,Haodong Zhu,Boyuan Zheng,Yihang Hu,Tong Zhang,Zunhao Chen,Junming Zhao,Ruiqian Nai,Yang Gao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents OpenHLM, an open-source recipe for whole-body humanoid loco-manipulation using a vision-language-action (VLA) model that maps language and pixels to all humanoid degrees of freedom. Through systematic experiments across teleoperation, model design, and co-training phases, they find whole-body joint teleoperation works best, VLA pretrained on wheeled robots transfers well to humanoids, and co-training with HuMI extends policy generalization. OpenHLM outperforms GR00T N1.6 and Ψ₀ on long-horizon tasks with less demonstration time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing humanoid loco-manipulation systems decouple upper and lower body control, limiting coordination and producing behaviors similar to wheeled dual-arm platforms. The authors aim to build a whole-body native VLA model that directly maps language and pixels to all humanoid degrees of freedom.

Method: Systematic empirical study following a one-variable-at-a-time roadmap across three phases: (1) whole-body teleoperation design (joint-based vs. partial DOF interfaces), (2) VLA model design (pretraining on static/wheeled dual-arm platforms then transferring to humanoid action space), and (3) heterogeneous co-training with HuMI (humanoid analog of UMI) to extend policy to new objects and instructions without additional whole-body teleoperation.

Result: Joint-based whole-body teleoperation outperforms alternatives with partial DOF exposure. VLA pretrained on static/wheeled dual-arm platforms transfers surprisingly well to humanoid full action space. Co-training with HuMI extends policies without additional whole-body teleoperation. OpenHLM outperforms GR00T N1.6 and Ψ₀ on challenging long-horizon tasks spanning wide vertical ranges, using less than half the total demonstration time.

Conclusion: The paper provides an open-source recipe (OpenHLM) for whole-body humanoid loco-manipulation that successfully coordinates the entire kinematic chain. It demonstrates that leveraging pretrained VLA models from simpler platforms and co-training strategies can achieve superior performance with less demonstration effort.

Abstract: Whole-body humanoid loco-manipulation requires coordinating the robot's entire kinematic chain. However, most existing systems typically decouple the upper and lower bodies into separate controllers, limiting such coordination and yielding behaviors similar to those of a wheeled dual-arm platform. In this paper, we ask what it takes to build a whole-body native vision-language-action (VLA) model that maps language and pixels directly to all of the humanoid's degrees of freedom. We conduct a systematic empirical study organized as a roadmap of one-variable-at-a-time experiments across three phases: whole-body teleoperation, VLA model design, and heterogeneous co-training. Our study yields several intriguing findings: a joint-based whole-body teleoperation interface outperforms alternatives that only partially expose the humanoid's degrees of freedom; a VLA pretrained on static and wheeled dual-arm platforms transfers surprisingly well to a humanoid's full action space; and co-training with HuMI, the humanoid analog of UMI, extends the policy to new objects and instructions without additional whole-body teleoperation on those targets. Following this roadmap yields OpenHLM, an open-source recipe for whole-body humanoid loco-manipulation. In a challenging long-horizon task that spans a wide vertical range of the humanoid, OpenHLM outperforms two state-of-the-art humanoid VLA baselines (GR00T N1.6 and $Ψ_0$) using less than half the total demonstration time. Our code, training data, and model checkpoints are available at [https://openhlm-project.github.io/].

</details>


### [369] [Geometric Reconstruction of Extrinsic Contact Trajectories using Tactile Sensing and Proprioception for Tool Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22251)
*Seojung Min,Yoonjin Kim,Jeong-Jung Kim,Jung Kim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper reconstructs tool-tip contact trajectories from tactile sensing and robot proprioception during tool-mediated manipulation, achieving ~8.6mm RMSE at 14Hz online.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tactile sensors at the grasp cannot directly sense tool-tip contact in tool-mediated manipulation, making it difficult to perceive critical task-relevant interactions that occur away from the sensor.

Method: Formulates tool-tip trajectory reconstruction as a geometric inference problem under a single-point contact assumption. First estimates global contact location via a fixed-point calibration segment, then reconstructs the full trajectory by composing relative tool motion from tactile marker observations under continuous contact.

Result: Achieves trajectory RMSE of 8.59±2.41mm (world frame) and shape RMSE of 5.96±1.16mm across 51 trials with varied tools, wrist poses, and grasps, operating online at 14.00±4.11Hz.

Conclusion: Extrinsic tool-tip trajectory geometry can be consistently recovered from grasp-level tactile sensing, with shape remaining stable across variations in tools, wrist poses, and grasp configurations.

Abstract: Tactile sensing enables robots to perceive rich contact information at the grasp, supporting tasks such as object recognition, in-hand pose estimation, and slip detection. However, in many tool-mediated manipulation tasks, the interaction that determines task success occurs at the tool tip, away from the tactile sensor, making direct sensing of tool-environment contact difficult, particularly when the contact moves during interaction. In this work, we reconstruct the trajectory of extrinsic tool-tip contact using tactile sensing and robot proprioception. We formulate tool-tip trajectory reconstruction as a geometric inference problem under a single-point contact assumption. Our method first estimates the global tool-tip contact location from a calibration segment designed to approximate fixed-point behavior, and then reconstructs the full trajectory by composing relative tool motion estimated from tactile marker observations under continuous contact. Across n=51 trials with multiple trajectories, tools, wrist poses, and grasp configurations, the proposed pipeline achieves a trajectory RMSE of 8.59 +/- 2.41 mm in the world frame and a shape RMSE of 5.96 +/- 1.16 mm, while operating online at 14.00 +/- 4.11 Hz. Overall, the results show that extrinsic tool-tip trajectory geometry can be recovered consistently from grasp-level tactile sensing, with trajectory shape remaining stable across variations in tools, wrist poses, and grasp configurations.

</details>


### [370] [Any-Body Guard: Universal Safeguarding for Manipulation Policies via Action Masking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22278)
*Alex Beaudin,Hanna Krasowski,Kartik Nagpal,Sanjit A. Seshia,Murat Arcak,Negar Mehr*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: X-Safe is a universal safeguarding approach for robotic manipulation that operates in configuration space to provide formal probabilistic guarantees for collision avoidance across diverse embodiments without requiring additional data or engineering effort.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing safety approaches for robotic manipulation are too conservative, computationally expensive, heuristic, or engineering-heavy to transfer across different embodiments and tasks.

Method: X-Safe operates in configuration space using an object-based, quasi-static scene representation and a forward kinematics model to provide formal probabilistic collision avoidance guarantees.

Result: X-Safe shows less task performance degradation than state-of-the-art methods, zero collisions in hardware experiments, and empirical validation of formal guarantees.

Conclusion: X-Safe provides a transferable, formal safety guarantee framework that eliminates the need for data collection or re-engineering across different robot embodiments and scenes.

Abstract: Ensuring safety of learning-enabled robotic manipulation across diverse embodiments and tasks still requires significant manual engineering. Existing approaches typically rely on heuristically designed fallback controllers or complex forward invariance assessments. These methods are often too conservative for task success, too computationally expensive for real-time execution, too heuristic to provide useful safety guarantees, or too engineering-heavy to transfer between setups. In this paper, we propose a universal safeguarding approach, X-Safe, which reasons directly in the robot's configuration space to provide formal probabilistic guarantees for collision avoidance. By operating in the configuration space, our method transfers across embodiments while relying solely on an object-based, quasi-static scene representation and a forward kinematics model of the robotic manipulator. Thus, X-Safe provides useful formal safety guarantees without requiring additional data, or engineering effort for different embodiments or scenes. We demonstrate X-Safe for diverse embodiments and policies, both in simulation and on hardware. We observe less degradation in task performance compared to state-of-the-art safeguarding, no collisions on hardware experiments, and empirically corroborate our formal guarantees.

</details>


### [371] [FlowDPG: Deterministic Policy Gradient on Flow Matching Policies for Real-World Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22303)
*Kexin Shi,Junyao Shi,Poorvi Hebbar,Zhuolun Zhao,Tarun Amarnath,Yifan Su,Shikhar Bahl,Deepak Pathak*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FlowDPG is a DDPG-style RL method for flow matching policies that bypasses backpropagation-through-time (BPTT) by distilling critic gradients into the velocity field, achieving 92% success on a real-world dual-arm assembly task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Applying policy gradient methods to flow matching policies requires backpropagating through time (BPTT) along the multi-step ODE that maps noise to actions, which is computationally prohibitive and numerically fragile.

Method: FlowDPG distills the critic gradient into the velocity field at training time, combining demonstration-driven velocity (keeping actions feasible) with critic-driven correction (steering toward higher value), entirely bypassing BPTT.

Result: 92% end-to-end success rate on a long-horizon, multi-stage, dual-arm AirPods assembly task, substantially outperforming value-conditioning, auxiliary-module adaptation, and adjoint-based critic-gradient approaches.

Conclusion: FlowDPG provides a BPTT-free distillation framework for stable DDPG-style policy improvement on flow matching policies, with formal connections to vanilla DPG demonstrated via three explicit approximations.

Abstract: Real-world reinforcement learning for robotic manipulation remains challenging, and this difficulty is amplified for flow matching policies: applying policy gradient methods to these policies is fundamentally limited by the need to backpropagate through time(BPTT) along the multi-step ODE that maps noise to actions, which is computationally prohibitive and numerically fragile. We propose FlowDPG, a DDPG-style method specifically designed for flow matching policies that distills the critic gradient into the velocity field at training time, bypassing BPTT entirely. Intuitively, FlowDPG combines two complementary vectors: the demonstration-driven velocity that keeps the action feasible, and the critic-driven correction that steers it toward higher value. Our contributions are threefold: (1) a BPTT-free distillation framework that enables stable DDPG-style policy improvement on flow matching policies, (2) a formal connection between the FlowDPG update direction and vanilla Deterministic Policy Gradient via three explicit approximations, and (3) real-world validation on a long-horizon, multi-stage, dual-arm AirPods assembly task, where FlowDPG attains a 92% end-to-end success rate, substantially outperforming recent RL methods spanning value-conditioning, auxiliary-module adaptation, and adjoint-based critic-gradient approaches. Videos and more results are provided on the project page https://flowdpg.github.io.

</details>


### [372] [EmbodiedUS-FS: Fast Slow Intelligence for Ultrasound Robotics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22319)
*Fangzhuo Zhang,Xinyu Wang,Xiao Yang,Jinchang Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hierarchical robotic ultrasound system with a "Slow Brain" for high-level clinical reasoning and a "Fast Brain" for low-level closed-loop execution, integrated with a Safety Shield for safe and interpretable assistance in real clinical environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Physicians' natural-language instructions contain implicit anatomical targets, procedural logic, and safety constraints, while execution faces challenges like patient motion, contact variations, and target drift. Existing systems lack integration of high-level reasoning with low-level closed-loop control.

Method: The system consists of: (1) Slow Brain - intent parsing, stage-wise task planning with API/handbook knowledge augmentation, task-graph construction, and structured plan verification; (2) Fast Brain - multimodal feedback fusion (ultrasound images, robot pose/force, patient motion) for local action refinement and image-quality-guided recovery; (3) Safety Shield - hierarchical escalation policy for risk constraint, replanning, and human confirmation.

Result: The hierarchical design improves task success rates while reducing safety violations, validated through planning evaluation, closed-loop execution under dynamic perturbations, and safety-mechanism tests.

Conclusion: A fast-and-slow hierarchical embodied approach effectively bridges high-level clinical reasoning and low-level execution for safe, interpretable robotic ultrasound assistance in real clinical settings.

Abstract: Robotic ultrasound scanning in real clinical environments requires both high-level clinical workflow reasoning and low-level closed-loop execution. Physicians natural-language instructions often contain implicit anatomical targets, procedural logic, image-quality requirements, and safety constraints, while execution is affected by patient motion, contact variations, and target drift. We propose a fast and slow hierarchical embodied ultrasound system for safe and interpretable robotic ultrasound assistance. The Slow Brain performs intent parsing and stage-wise task planning with knowledge augmentation from an API and handbook corpus, and generates executable plans through task-graph construction and structured plan verification. The Fast Brain fuses multimodal feedback, including ultrasound images, robot pose and force states, and patient-motion information, to refine local actions and perform image-quality-guided recovery behaviors. The system further integrates a Safety Shield and a hierarchical escalation policy to constrain risky actions and trigger replanning or human confirmation under persistent failures or safety-bound violations. Experiments on planning evaluation, closed-loop execution under dynamic perturbations, and safety-mechanism validation demonstrate that the proposed hierarchical design improves task success rates while reducing safety violations.

</details>


### [373] [Tactile Genesis: Exploring Tactile Sensors at Scale for Learning Dexterous Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22332)
*Trinity Chung,Kashu Yamazaki,Dhruv Patel,Alexis Duburcq,Yiling Qiao,Katerina Fragkiadaki,Aran Nayebi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Tactile Genesis is a GPU-parallel tactile sensor simulation platform that supports multiple sensor types (binary contact, force/torque, temperature field, etc.) at scale. Using this platform, the authors find that sensor coverage (whole-hand) matters far more than resolution or sensor type, with force/torque per taxel being the most useful, providing concrete guidance for tactile hardware design and dexterous manipulation policy observation choices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tactile sensing is critical for dexterous manipulation, but it is unclear which tactile abstractions policies need and when richer tactile fields justify their hardware cost. Empirical study is difficult because each sensor defines a new robot, and no lab can replicate experiments across all sensors.
