Method: The authors develop Tactile Genesis, a GPU-parallel tactile sensor simulation platform supporting binary contact, contact depth, per-taxel kinematic force/torque, elastomer marker displacement, geometry-aware proximity, contact audio, and voxelized temperature fields under a common interface with configurable placement, resolution, and realistic noise models. They train teacher-student policies on three dexterous tasks, ablating sensor type, placement, resolution, and noise, and verify transfer to a real XHand1 robot.

Result: The platform scales to 20,000+ parallel environments and 1,000 taxels on a single GPU, with 3-20x throughput improvement. Key findings: (1) Proprioception alone is insufficient on all tasks. (2) Sensor placement dominates sensor type — fingertip-only coverage trails whole-hand coverage. (3) Resolution matters far less than coverage; 200 taxels across the whole hand suffices. (4) Force/torque per taxel is consistently the most useful sensor type.

Conclusion: These results provide concrete guidance for both future tactile hardware design (prioritize whole-hand coverage, force/torque sensing, not high resolution) and policy-side observation choices in dexterous manipulation. The platform is open-source.

Abstract: Tactile sensing is critical for contact-rich dexterous manipulation, yet it remains unclear which tactile abstractions a policy needs and when richer tactile fields justify their hardware cost. This is hard to study empirically: each sensor effectively defines a new robot, and no lab can replicate the same learning experiment across all of them. We present Tactile Genesis, a GPU-parallel tactile sensor simulation platform that exposes binary contact, contact depth, per-taxel kinematic force/torque, elastomer marker displacement, geometry-aware proximity, contact audio, and a voxelized temperature field (the first of its kind in robot learning physics simulation platforms) under a common interface, with configurable placement, resolution, and a realistic noise model (drift, hysteresis, dead taxels, crosstalk). It scales past 20,000 parallel environments and 1,000 taxels on a single GPU, improving throughput by 3 to 20 times over previous tactile simulators. We train teacher-student policies on three dexterous tasks, ablating sensor type, placement, resolution, and noise, and verify transfer to the real XHand1. Proprioception alone is insufficient on every task. Sensor placement dominates sensor type: fingertip-only coverage trails whole-hand coverage by a wide margin, while adding the palm and proximal phalanges closes most of the gap to the privileged teacher. Resolution matters far less than coverage: placing 200 taxels across the whole hand suffices across tasks. We find that force/torque per taxel is consistently the most useful sensor type. These results give concrete guidance for both future tactile hardware design for improving robot hands and policy-side observation choice in dexterous manipulation. https://neuroagents-lab.github.io/2026-tactile-genesis/

</details>


### [374] [Multi-AUV Marine Life Tracking with Single Hydrophone Payloads via a Hidden Markov Model Equipped Particle Filter](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22335)
*Christopher Herrera,Kehlani Fay,Christopher Clark,Alberto Soto,Christopher Lowe,Mario Espinoza*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a multi-AUV system with single compact hydrophones each, using a particle filter with HMM-based behavioral motion model to track acoustically tagged marine animals, achieving ~10m RMSE in real-world tests and ~15m RMSE in simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional acoustic tagging suffers data loss when animals move beyond stationary hydrophone range. Existing AUV-based solutions use bulky multi-hydrophone arrays that increase drag and limit speed, making it difficult to track highly mobile animals.

Method: Multiple AUVs each equipped with a single compact hydrophone payload. A particle filter algorithm with a hidden Markov model (HMM) behavioral motion model (fit to historical animal movement data) fuses measurements from multiple AUVs to estimate transmitter position.

Result: Real-world data shows ~10m RMSE for short-term deployments; simulated larger dataset shows ~15m RMSE for longer deployments over larger areas. HMM motion model outperforms generic velocity motion model, and both outperform baseline random walk model.

Conclusion: The proposed multi-AUV single-hydrophone approach reduces drag while maintaining accurate localization, enabling faster tracking of highly mobile aquatic animals over longer durations.

Abstract: Researchers tag and track marine animals to study migration patterns, human impacts on behavior, and behavioral shifts due to climate change. Accurate data collection often requires tagging individual animals to collect spatio-temporal state estimates of the animal's geo-position and depth. Acoustic transmitters are prominent due to their continuous communication without requiring retrieval or surfacing to collect data. These transmitters emit underwater acoustic pulses that can be detected by hydrophones. However, the frequent movement of aquatic animals results in high data loss when the animal moves out of the detection range of a stationary hydrophone. Autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV) systems offer a solution for localizing transmitters with higher resolution over longer periods of time. Such systems previously deployed have often required multiple hydrophones mounted on a large frame carried by the AUV. This increases drag, limiting the speed at which the AUV can track highly mobile animals. This work provides an alternative by equipping multiple AUVs with a single compact hydrophone payload. A particle filter algorithm equipped with a hidden Markov model (HMM) behavioral motion model fuses measurements from multiple AUVs to estimate the transmitter's position. Real-world data shows a root mean square error (RMSE) of approximately 10 meters for short-term deployments, and a larger simulated dataset shows an RMSE of approximately 15 meters for longer deployments over a larger area. The HMM fit to historical animal movement data outperforms a generic velocity motion model, and both outperform a baseline random walk motion model.

</details>


### [375] [Benchmarking Robot Memory Under Interference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22338)
*Soumil Rathi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces RoboMME-Interference, a benchmark testing robot memory systems on long-context, cross-session scenarios. Current systems (e.g., π₀.₅ variants) degrade sharply as unrelated sessions accumulate, highlighting the need for robust long-term memory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world robots operate across many sessions and tasks, requiring long-context memory, but existing benchmarks only evaluate single-episode or short-context performance.

Method: Builds RoboMME-Interference on top of RoboMME by constructing session histories with a relevant prior demonstration plus controlled numbers of unrelated distractor sessions, then measuring VLA accuracy on query episodes.

Result: Perceptual memory variants of π₀.₅ succeed without distractors but show strong, steady performance decay as unrelated sessions accumulate.

Conclusion: Current memory-augmented robot systems largely fail at long-context memory and robustness to interference; the field needs better solutions for cross-session memory.

Abstract: Robots deployed in realistic settings will accumulate experience across many sessions and tasks over their deployment. The robot's tasks may often require it to remember information from multiple sessions ago, making long-context robot memory important for real-world deployments. However, most robot-memory benchmarks today are based on single episodes or a short context. To measure how current robot memory systems perform on longer sessions with more distractions, we introduce RoboMME-Interference, a cross-session benchmark built on RoboMME. For each query episode, we construct a session history using the query's relevant prior demonstration followed by a controlled number of unrelated sessions, which we provide to the VLA as memory and measure accuracy. Running RoboMME's released memory-augmented $π_{0.5}$ variants unmodified through this benchmark, we find that while perceptual memory variants improve success when given the history without any distractors, they decay strongly and steadily as unrelated sessions accumulate. With this release, we emphasize the importance of long-context memory and robustness to interference and show that current systems largely fail on such capabilities. The project page, videos, code, and data are at https://robotmemorybench.com.

</details>


### [376] [A Taxonomy of Conceptual Alignment in Human-Robot Dialogue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22360)
*Shengchen Zhang,Xiaohua Sun,Weiwei Guo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a design-centric framework for conceptual alignment in human-robot interaction, introducing a taxonomy and dialogue act schema to analyze and design bidirectional, co-constructive alignment processes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Successful human-robot interaction requires shared understanding of concepts, but existing work is hindered by ambiguous definitions of "conceptual alignment" and fragmented, unidirectional investigations of the design space.

Method: The authors develop a taxonomy characterizing conceptual alignment dialogues by (1) what triggers initiation and (2) what levels of conceptual understanding are involved, plus a dialogue act schema as an operational tool to capture interactional moves for achieving alignment.

Result: A structured foundation for analyzing, comparing, and designing conceptual alignment in HRI, including a taxonomy and a dialogue act schema.

Conclusion: Conceptual alignment should be understood as a bidirectional, co-constructive process, and the proposed framework provides practical tools for advancing HRI design and research.

Abstract: Successful conversations require speakers to align on the meaning of concepts, a challenging but crucial task for human-robot interaction. Understanding the process of establishing such alignment is hindered by competing interpretations of the term and isolated, unidirectional investigations of its design space. This paper argues for a design-centric understanding of conceptual alignment as a bidirectional and co-constructive process. We introduce a taxonomy that characterizes conceptual alignment dialogues along what triggers its initiation and what level(s) of conceptual understanding it concerns. We further present a dialogue act schema as an operational tool that captures the interactional moves through which alignment is achieved. Together, these contributions provide a structured foundation for analyzing, comparing, and designing conceptual alignment in human-robot interaction.

</details>


### [377] [Do Rigid-Body Simulators Dream of Soft Robots? Learning Contact-Rich Manipulation for Tendon-Driven Continuum Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22397)
*Chengnan Shentu,Nicholas Baldassini,Tongjia Zheng,Priyanka Rao,Jessica Burgner-Kahrs*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper presents a new simulation framework that bridges soft continuum robots with MuJoCo, enabling the first successful sim-to-real transfer of contact-rich manipulation policies for tendon-driven continuum robots (TDCRs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing soft robot simulators lack contact handling, actuation support, and learning integration for contact-rich manipulation, while rigid-body approximations sacrifice physical grounding.

Method: The authors derive a continuum-mechanics-informed discretization to place TDCRs natively inside MuJoCo, unifying tendon forces, body contact, and dynamics in a single physics pipeline.

Result: The simulator is validated against Cosserat rod reference and real TDCR hardware. Imitation learning policies trained in simulation transfer zero-shot to a physical 3-segment TDCR on a Franka arm for two contact-rich tasks.

Conclusion: This is the first demonstration of sim-to-real transfer for contact-rich manipulation with continuum robots.

Abstract: Learning contact-rich, whole-body manipulation for soft continuum robots is held back by the lack of simulation infrastructure that has accelerated rigid-robot manipulation. Existing soft robot simulators are physically grounded but lack the contact handling, actuation support, or learning integration needed for contact-rich manipulation; rigid-body approximations offer these capabilities but sacrifice physical grounding. We bridge this gap for tendon-driven continuum robots (TDCRs) by deriving a continuum-mechanics-informed discretization that places the soft robot natively inside MuJoCo, unifying tendon forces, body contact, and dynamics in a single physics pipeline. We validate the simulator against a Cosserat rod reference (static and dynamic) and real TDCR hardware. We then train state-based imitation learning policies via teleoperation in simulation and deploy them zero-shot to a physical 3-segment TDCR on a 7-DoF Franka arm across two contact-rich manipulation tasks. To our knowledge, this is the first demonstration of sim-to-real transfer for contact-rich manipulation with continuum robots.

</details>


### [378] [SPiralRoll: A Novel Adjustable-Stiffness Underactuated 3-DoF Joint with Torsion Springs for Rolling Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22443)
*Louis Keith,George Ripper,and Seyed Amir Tafrishi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SPiralRoll is a novel torsion-spring-based underactuated compliant mechanism for rolling robots, using two motors to produce three motions (rotation, radial expansion/contraction, axial spin). Two designs (full-arc and single-arc) are evaluated, with the mechanism integrated into a spherical rolling robot for proof-of-concept locomotion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Compliant mechanisms improve robot adaptability, safety, and energy efficiency while reducing hardware complexity. The authors aim to develop a low-cost, compact, underactuated compliant mechanism for rolling robots and adaptive robotic actuation.

Method: The mechanism uses arc-distributed elastic members with two motor inputs to generate three output motions via nonlinear compliant deformation. Two configurations (full-arc and single-arc) were designed, 3D-printed, and experimentally validated, including integration into a spherical rolling robot for forward rolling and turning tests.

Result: Full-arc design offers better structural support and smoother deformation; single-arc design yields larger deformation and stronger inertial excitation, better for pendulum-driven rolling. Proof-of-concept experiments demonstrated forward rolling and turning.

Conclusion: SPiralRoll is a low-cost, compact, fully 3D-printable solution for underactuated compliant rolling robots and adaptive robotic joints.

Abstract: Compliant mechanisms are important in robotics because they can improve adaptability, safety, and energy efficiency while reducing hardware complexity. This paper presents SPiralRoll, a novel torsion-spring-based underactuated compliant mechanism for rolling robots and compliant robotic actuation. The mechanism uses arc-distributed elastic members and two motor inputs to realize three physically observable output motions: rotational motion, radial expansion/contraction, and axial spin induced by nonlinear compliant deformation. Two configurations, namely full-arc and single-arc designs, are developed and experimentally evaluated. Beyond benchtop validation, the mechanism is integrated into a spherical rolling robot, where proof-of-concept experiments demonstrate forward rolling and turning. The results show that the full-arc design provides better structural support and smoother deformation, whereas the single-arc design yields larger deformation and stronger inertial excitation, making it more suitable for pendulum-driven rolling locomotion. Overall, SPiralRoll provides a low-cost, compact, and fully 3D-printable solution for underactuated compliant rolling robots and adaptive robotic joints.

</details>


### [379] [BLENDS: Bayesian Learning-Enhanced Deep Smoothing for GNSS-Denied Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22456)
*Nadav Cohen,Itzik Klein*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: BLENDS is a transformer-based framework that enhances Bayesian smoothing with learned covariance adaptation and state correction, achieving up to 25.6% better position accuracy during GNSS outages on quadrotor datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Maintaining accurate navigation during GNSS outages is challenging for autonomous systems with low-cost inertial sensors, and classical smoothing methods are limited by the accuracy of conventional GNSS measurements.

Method: A transformer-based framework (BLENDS) that augments Bayesian smoothing with learned covariance adaptation and state correction, preserving Bayesian estimation foundations while adding data-driven learning.

Result: Up to 25.6% improvement in position RMSE over model-based smoothers on a quadrotor dataset with GNSS outages, with reduced estimation uncertainty and ability to compensate for systematic bias between conventional GNSS and RTK ground truth.

Conclusion: Learning-enhanced Bayesian smoothing shows strong potential for resilient, high-accuracy navigation in GNSS-challenged environments, surpassing the accuracy achievable with conventional GNSS measurements alone.

Abstract: Maintaining accurate navigation during GNSS outages remains a significant challenge for autonomous systems relying on low-cost inertial sensors. While classical smoothing methods, such as the two-filter smoother and Rauch-Tung-Striebel smoother, exploit measurements collected before and after an outage, their performance remains limited by the accuracy of conventional GNSS measurements. This paper presents Bayesian learning-enhanced navigation with deep smoothing (BLENDS), a transformer-based framework that augments Bayesian smoothing with learned covariance adaptation and state correction. The proposed method preserves the statistical foundations of Bayesian estimation while leveraging data-driven learning to improve navigation accuracy. Evaluations on the quadrotor dataset with GNSS outages demonstrate that BLENDS consistently outperforms both model-based smoothers, achieving up to 25.6% improvement in the position root mean square error while also reducing estimation uncertainty. Furthermore, BLENDS learns to compensate for the systematic bias between conventional GNSS positioning and RTK ground truth, enabling accuracy beyond that achievable with conventional GNSS measurements alone. The results demonstrate the potential of learning-enhanced Bayesian smoothing for resilient and high-accuracy navigation in GNSS-challenged environments.

</details>


### [380] [Scalable Multi-Task Data Generation via Reinforcement Learning for Language-Conditioned Bimanual Dexterous Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22471)
*Zechu Li,Yufeng Jin,Puze Liu,Jan Peters,Georgia Chalvatzaki*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a systematic RL-based data generation pipeline for bimanual dexterous manipulation that uses generalizable reward design, domain randomization, and language-conditioned annotations to synthesize high-quality training data, overcoming the limitations of existing human teleoperation and handcrafted reward approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The key bottleneck in training generalist policies for bimanual dexterous manipulation is the lack of large-scale, high-quality datasets. Existing methods (human teleoperation, object-centric trajectory matching, handcrafted RL rewards) suffer from limited task diversity, morphology mismatch, and scalability issues.

Method: A systematic RL-based data generation pipeline that integrates: (1) generalizable reward design, (2) effective domain randomization, and (3) language-conditioned task annotations to synthesize diverse, high-quality datasets.

Result: The generated data significantly improves generalization across three representative manipulation tasks, enabling training of language-conditioned multi-task policies.

Conclusion: Synthetic RL-based data generation with generalizable rewards and domain randomization can effectively scale training data for bimanual dexterous manipulation, outperforming existing human teleoperation and handcrafted reward approaches.

Abstract: A key bottleneck in training generalist policies for bimanual dexterous manipulation is the lack of large-scale, high-quality datasets. Synthetic data generation in simulation provides a scalable alternative to human video demonstrations by overcoming challenges such as morphology mismatch, missing physical interactions, and the generation of robot actions. However, existing approaches based on human teleoperation offer limited task diversity, as object-centric trajectory matching often neglects the feasibility of robot execution. Reinforcement learning (RL) enables broader scalability but is often constrained by handcrafted, task-specific rewards. In this work, we propose a systematic RL-based data generation pipeline that integrates generalizable reward design, effective domain randomization, and language-conditioned task annotations. This pipeline synthesizes diverse, high-quality datasets for dexterous bimanual manipulation and enables training of language-conditioned multi-task policies. Our experiments show that the generated data significantly improves generalization across three representative manipulation tasks.

</details>


### [381] [ARP: Enhancing Quantized Skill Abstractions via Visual Alignment and Iterative Refinement for Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22480)
*Yuntian Wang,Zesheng Jia,Yuhui Duan,Qibing Wang,Yang Liu,Song Wang,Siao Liu,Jin Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ARP proposes a discrete-skill framework with visual-action alignment and iterative residual refinement for improved long-horizon visuomotor policy learning, achieving SOTA on LIBERO, Meta-World, and real-world humanoid tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing skill-based imitation learning methods using discrete quantization have weak visual-semantic grounding and suffer from precision errors in continuous action generation, limiting long-horizon manipulation performance.

Method: ARP introduces (1) a visual-action alignment objective that contrastively aligns visual embeddings with pre-quantized action representations in shared latent space while preserving a state-independent skill decoder, and (2) a lightweight Iterative Residual Head (IRH) that performs two-step refinement for fine-grained control.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on LIBERO and Meta-World benchmarks; consistent performance gains on Kuavo 4 Pro humanoid platform for two challenging manipulation tasks.

Conclusion: Semantic grounding and execution-level refinement are crucial for discrete-skill frameworks; ARP effectively addresses both visual-semantic grounding and precision error issues in long-horizon manipulation.

Abstract: Learning visuomotor policies for long-horizon manipulation remains a fundamental challenge. Recent skill-based imitation learning methods based on discrete quantization have shown promising results by representing complex behaviors as temporally extended skills. However, most existing approaches primarily encode action trajectories into latent skills, yielding weak visual-semantic grounding and limiting the ability to leverage visual observations for skill selection. Moreover, discrete tokenization inevitably incurs precision errors during continuous action generation. To alleviate these issues, we propose Aligned Refinement Policy (ARP), a discrete-skill framework that couples semantic grounding with execution-level refinement. Specifically, ARP introduces (i) a visual--action alignment objective that contrastively aligns visual embeddings with pre-quantized action representations in a shared latent space while preserving a state-independent skill decoder, and (ii) a lightweight Iterative Residual Head (IRH) that performs a two-step refinement to recover fine-grained control for precise execution. Extensive experiments show that ARP achieves state-of-the-art performance on the LIBERO and Meta-World benchmarks. Moreover, real-robot experiments on the Kuavo 4 Pro humanoid platform further validate its effectiveness, yielding consistent performance gains over several baselines on two challenging manipulation tasks.

</details>


### [382] [PenduMorph: Development and Motion Analysis of Pendulum-Actuated Rolling Reconfigurable Spherical Robot with Magnetic-Coupling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22491)
*Aung Myat,Peter Noyce,May Forgan,Qing Yu,Seyed Amir Tafrishi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PenduMorph is a wireless, reconfigurable rolling spherical robot with a 2-DoF internal pendulum and magnetic docking for enclosed locomotion and inter-module interaction in challenging environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a modular rolling robot platform that combines independent mobility with protected hardware and reliable reconfigurability for operation in contact-rich, demanding environments.

Method: Extends a previous pendulum-actuated rolling disk concept into a fully enclosed spherical robot with a 2-DoF internal pendulum, onboard control, battery power, and magnetic docking. Includes analytical study of magnetic coupling and experimental investigation of single/dual-module behaviors.

Result: The platform enables stable wireless operation and distinctive reconfigurable rolling behaviors, including independent rolling, magnetic coupling, and coordinated coupled motion.

Conclusion: PenduMorph provides a useful foundation for future modular spherical robots operating in challenging, contact-rich environments.

Abstract: This paper presents "PenduMorph", a wireless reconfigurable rolling spherical robot designed as a modular platform for enclosed locomotion and inter-module interaction in challenging environments. The proposed robot extends our previous pendulum-actuated rolling disk concept to a fully enclosed spherical architecture integrating a 2-DoF internal pendulum, onboard control, battery-powered operation, and magnetic docking. The design aims to combine independent rolling mobility with protected hardware and reliable reconfigurability. We first present the robot design and an analytical study of the magnetic coupling mechanism to evaluate retention and interaction between coupled modules. We then experimentally investigate key motion behaviors at both the single-module and dual-module levels, including independent rolling, magnetic coupling, and coordinated coupled motion. The results show that the proposed platform enables stable wireless operation and a set of distinctive reconfigurable rolling behaviors, providing a useful foundation for future modular spherical robots operating in contact-rich and demanding environments.

</details>


### [383] [Semantic-Aware Autonomous Exploration for UAVs in Unknown Indoor Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22670)
*Duc-Thien Nguyen,Ngoc Minh Do,Xiem HoangVan,Thanh Nguyen Canh*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a semantic-aware exploration framework for UAVs that augments a probabilistic roadmap with semantic information and a semantic reward function, achieving 90-94% coverage with reduced time and travel distance compared to geometry-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing UAV exploration methods rely on geometric information and lack semantic awareness, leading to suboptimal exploration efficiency and limited environmental understanding.

Method: The framework builds on the Dynamic Exploration Planner (DEP) by augmenting its Probabilistic Roadmap (PRM) with a semantic layer, introducing a semantic reward function to prioritize regions with meaningful objects/structures, and continuously updating the roadmap for frontier selection and path planning.

Result: Achieves 90-94% exploration coverage rates while reducing exploration time and travel distance compared to conventional geometry-based methods in multiple simulated environments.

Conclusion: Adding semantic awareness to roadmap-based exploration significantly improves UAV autonomous exploration efficiency and environmental understanding over purely geometric approaches.

Abstract: Autonomous exploration in unknown environments requires unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) to efficiently generate informative trajectories while simultaneously constructing accurate maps. Although many existing exploration methods rely on geometric information, they often lack semantic awareness, resulting in suboptimal exploration efficiency and limited environmental understanding. To address this limitation, this paper proposes a semantic-aware exploration framework that adds semantic information to a roadmap-based exploration strategy. The proposed method builds on the Dynamic Exploration Planner (DEP), which incrementally constructs a Probabilistic Roadmap (PRM), and augments this roadmap with a semantic layer. A semantic reward function is introduced to prioritize regions containing meaningful objects and structures, enabling the UAV to focus on areas with higher information value. Furthermore, the roadmap is continuously updated to support efficient frontier selection and path planning during exploration. The proposed framework is implemented in ROS Noetic and Gazebo using an RGB-D sensor for simultaneous acquisition of geometric and semantic information. Experimental results in multiple simulated environments demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves exploration coverage rates between 90% and 94% while reducing exploration time and travel distance compared with conventional geometry-based exploration methods.

</details>


### [384] [Efficient Continuous Semantic Mapping based on Spatio-Temporal Awareness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22672)
*My Le Pham,Dinh Trieu Duong,Xiem HoangVan,Thanh Nguyen Canh*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a spatiotemporal semantic mapping method for robots that adjusts inference range based on semantic uncertainty and fuses labels over time, achieving 12% higher accuracy and 54.92% mIoU on SemanticKITTI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing semantic mapping methods treat voxels independently, process the entire space, and lack temporal label consistency, causing high computational cost and poor robustness in dynamic scenes.

Method: The method introduces spatial and temporal relationships into semantic inference, adaptively adjusts the inference range based on local semantic uncertainty, and fuses semantic labels over time to improve stability and efficiency.

Result: Improves mapping accuracy by ~12% and achieves 54.92% mIoU (13.18 points higher than spatial-only mapping) on SemanticKITTI dataset.

Conclusion: Spatiotemporal reasoning is effective for continuous semantic mapping in autonomous robotic systems.

Abstract: Continuous semantic mapping allows autonomous robots to understand both the spatial structure and the semantic content of complex environments. However, most existing methods process the entire space, treat voxels as independent units, and do not keep the semantic labels consistent over time. This leads to high computational cost and reduced robustness in dynamic scenes. This paper proposes a semantic mapping method that brings spatial and temporal relationships into the semantic inference process. The method adjusts the inference range according to the local semantic uncertainty and fuses labels over time to improve map stability and computational efficiency. Experiments on the SemanticKITTI dataset show that the proposed method improves mapping accuracy by about 12% and reaches an mIoU of 54.92%, which is 13.18 percentage points higher than spatial-only mapping. These results show that spatiotemporal reasoning is effective for continuous semantic mapping in autonomous robotic systems.

</details>


### [385] [Integrated cloud-based architecture for robot-robot and human-robot collaboration using ROS 2--MQTT in Mediterranean Greenhouses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22682)
*F. Cañadas-Aránega,M. Muñoz,J. C. Moreno,J. L. Blanco-Claraco*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a cloud-based hybrid architecture (ROS 2 + iVeg DSS via MQTT/FIWARE) for multi-robot communication in challenging Mediterranean greenhouse environments, validated in simulation and real-world tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sustainable agriculture requires multi-robot systems in greenhouses, but narrow corridors, dense biomass, and metallic interference cause ROS 2 communication failures (node discovery issues, latency spikes), creating a bottleneck for real-time coordination.

Method: A cloud-based hybrid architecture bridging ROS 2 (edge computing) and iVeg (Decision Support System) using MQTT and the European FIWARE platform, enabling two-way communication for multi-robot fleets under constrained conditions.

Result: Validated in high-fidelity simulation and real-world greenhouse scenarios; demonstrated persistent connectivity and data integrity under adverse network conditions; MQTT integration eliminated information silos.

Conclusion: The architecture provides a scalable, decentralized solution for complex robotic missions executed locally via Edge Computing, establishing Greenhouse Models as a Service (GMaaS) and bridging low-level robotic control with high-level cloud-based IoT decision-making.

Abstract: The imperative to develop more sustainable agriculture demands a transition from isolated automation toward the deployment of multi-robot systems (MRS) in agrifood environments. However, Mediterranean greenhouse settings-characterized by narrow corridors, dense biomass, and structural metallic interference-pose significant challenges for robust and scalable communication between agents. Traditional robotic frameworks, such as ROS 2, frequently encounter node discovery issues and latency spikes due to dynamic obstacles, dense foliage, and other characteristic greenhouse elements, creating a critical bottleneck for real-time coordination. This paper proposes an innovative cloud-based hybrid architecture that establishes a two-way communication bridge between ROS 2, acting as an edge computing platform, and iVeg as a Decision Support System (DSS), using MQTT and the European FIWARE platform. The proposed framework enables seamless interoperability between fleets of multiple robots in environments with communication constraints, facilitating the synchronised exchange of high-level telemetry, point cloud data and farmer identification for collaboration, amongst other critical data. The architecture was validated in a high-fidelity simulation environment and subsequently tested in a real-world greenhouse scenario, demonstrating its ability to maintain persistent connectivity and data integrity under adverse network conditions. The results indicate that the integration of MQTT effectively eliminates information silos, providing a scalable and decentralised solution for managing complex robotic missions, which are executed locally via Edge Computing. This work sets a new methodological precedent for the concept of Greenhouse Models as a Service (GMaaS), bridging the gap between low-level robotic control and high-level, cloud-based IoT decision-making.

</details>


### [386] [Temporal Logic Guidance for Action-Only Diffusion Policies with World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22729)
*Moritz Zoellner,Anastasios Manganaris,Rohan Paleja*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a guidance method for action-only diffusion policies using a learned world model and STL robustness gradients to steer robot behavior toward constraint satisfaction without retraining, reducing constraint violations from 80% to 4% on a Robomimic task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion policies enable multimodal behavior but offer limited control over behavior mode selection at inference time, which is problematic in human-robot interaction settings where specific constraint satisfaction is needed.

Method: A separate learned world model enables differentiable evaluation of STL robustness; its gradient is injected into the diffusion process to steer action-only diffusion policies toward constraint satisfaction without retraining.

Result: On the Can Transport task from Robomimic, the method maintains 100% task success while reducing constraint violations from over 80% for baselines to 4%.

Conclusion: The approach improves constraint adherence while preserving task performance, with extensions discussed for improved robustness and more complex constraints.

Abstract: Diffusion policies enable multimodal robot behavior but offer limited ability to choose among behavior modes at inference time, even though such control is desirable in human-robot settings. Prior solutions to this lack of control have utilized Signal Temporal Logic (STL) to express human intentions and provide corresponding guidance for diffusion policy inference. However, these approaches can only guide diffusion policies that jointly generate future actions and states, increasing both complexity and runtime. We propose a novel guidance method for action-only diffusion policies that uses a separate learned world model to enable differentiable evaluation of STL robustness, with its gradient then injected into the diffusion process. This steers behavior toward constraint satisfaction without retraining, improving constraint adherence while preserving task performance. On the Can Transport task from Robomimic, our method maintains 100% task success while reducing constraint violations from over 80% for baseline methods to 4%. We also discuss extensions toward improved robustness and more complex constraints.

</details>


### [387] [HERCULES: An Open-Source Simulation Framework for Heterogeneous Multi-Robot SLAM, Collaborative Perception, and Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22756)
*Sandilya Sai Garimella,Daniel Chase Butterfield,Sean Wilson,Lu Gan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HERCULES is an open-source UE5-based simulator for heterogeneous multi-robot (UAV-UGV) autonomy, featuring a shared navigation stack, LWIR cameras, night-vision modes, dynamic environments (fire, flooding, crop disease), ROS 2 support, and both passive data-collection and active closed-loop execution modes. It includes a benchmark dataset for multi-robot SLAM with two UAVs and two UGVs across desert, forest, and city environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome architectural limitations of prior simulators (AirSim, Cosys-AirSim) and enable concurrent UAV-UGV operation in large-scale, photorealistic, dynamic environments for heterogeneous multi-robot autonomy research.

Method: Built on UE5 via AirSim/Cosys-AirSim; introduces a waypoint-tracking UGV controller matching UAV interfaces; provides a shared navigation stack (mapping, traversability, planning, control); adds physics-based LWIR cameras and configurable night-vision; integrates intelligent agents (pedestrians, traffic, wildlife) and dynamic phenomena (fire, flooding, crop disease); supports ROS 2, lightweight APIs, and rigorous time synchronization; operates in passive (offline trajectory replay) and active (online closed-loop planning) modes.

Result: Demonstrated utility via experiments in heterogeneous multi-robot SLAM, collaborative perception, and exploration using both generated datasets and closed-loop execution. Publicly released source code, documentation, and datasets including a multi-robot SLAM benchmark (2 UAVs + 2 UGVs across km-scale desert, forest, city environments).

Conclusion: HERCULES advances heterogeneous multi-robot autonomy research by providing a high-fidelity, extensible simulation platform with dynamic environments, multi-modal sensors, and both data-collection and online execution capabilities.

Abstract: We present HERCULES, an open-source simulator and data-collection pipeline for heterogeneous multi-robot autonomy. Built upon the Unreal Engine 5 (UE5)-based simulators AirSim and Cosys-AirSim, HERCULES resolves key architectural limitations of prior frameworks to enable concurrent unmanned aerial and ground vehicle (UAV-UGV) operation in large-scale, photorealistic, dynamic environments. It introduces a new waypoint-tracking UGV controller that mirrors existing UAV control interfaces, and provides a shared navigation stack for mapping, traversability analysis, planning, and control across heterogeneous platforms. Expanding inherited sensor suites, it adds physics-based long-wave infrared (LWIR) cameras and configurable night-vision modes for degraded visual environments. HERCULES provides lightweight APIs, ROS 2 wrappers, and rigorous time synchronization across sensors and platforms, and brings state-of-the-art game-engine capabilities into robotics simulation, integrating intelligent agents such as pedestrians, traffic, and wildlife with high-fidelity dynamic phenomena, including fire, flooding, and crop disease spread. HERCULES runs in two modes: passively, replaying offline-designed trajectories to generate reproducible multi-modal datasets, and actively, running an online planner in closed loop from live observations. Our experiments in heterogeneous multi-robot SLAM, collaborative perception, and exploration, using both HERCULES-generated data and active closed-loop execution, demonstrate its utility for advancing heterogeneous multi-robot autonomy. We publicly release our source code, experiment code, documentation, and datasets, including a heterogeneous multi-robot SLAM benchmark collected with two UAVs and two UGVs across kilometer-scale desert, forest, and city environments, at https://lunarlab-gatech.github.io/HERCULES-website.

</details>


### [388] [Cooperative-ORCA*: Real-Time Proactive Deadlock Avoidance for Continuous-Space Multi-Agent Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22757)
*Junfeng Wu,Jiaqi Chen,Hongkun Lyu,Kevin Zheng,Andy Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: C-ORCA* and C-ORCA*-MAPF are continuous-space MAPF algorithms that proactively prevent deadlocks by modeling agents' full spatial trajectories, outperforming prior ORCA*-MAPF methods in solve rate, runtime, and flowtime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ORCA* is myopic to future deadlocks, and ORCA*-MAPF's post-hoc fallback mechanisms introduce significant flowtime overhead. The authors aim to proactively prevent deadlocks to reduce this overhead.

Method: C-ORCA* and C-ORCA*-MAPF incorporate agents' entire spatial trajectories and spatial dependencies to proactively prevent deadlocks, rather than reacting after detection.

Result: The C-ORCA* family significantly outperforms previous state-of-the-art in solve rate, runtime, and flowtime.

Conclusion: Proactive deadlock avoidance via continuous-space trajectory modeling yields better performance than reactive post-hoc correction methods like ORCA*-MAPF.

Abstract: Multi-Agent Path Finding (MAPF) is a problem that requires computing collision-free paths for a set of agents from their start locations to designated goal locations. The problem has broad applications in domains where teams of robots must operate in a coordinated manner. ORCA* is a real time MAPF solver that assigns for each timestep a velocity for each agent. Due to its real time nature, it is myopic to future deadlocks that result from current decisions. ORCA*-MAPF attempts to remedy this limitation by introducing fallback mechanisms when deadlocks are detected. However, post hoc interventions often introduce significant flowtime overhead. In this paper, we introduce C-ORCA* and C-ORCA*-MAPF, continuous space MAPF algorithms that incorporate agents' entire spatial trajectory and their spatial dependencies to proactively prevent deadlocks from occurring, thus avoiding the high flowtime overhead associated with post hoc corrections in ORCA*-MAPF. The C-ORCA* family of algorithms significantly outperform previous state-of-the-art in terms of solve rate, runtime, and flowtime.

</details>


### [389] [UniFS: Unified Fast-to-Slow Hierarchical Architecture for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22794)
*Lin Sun,Zhiwei Guan,Conglin Wang,Zihong Chen,Jianhai Yu,Zongsheng Li,Boyong He,Tao Sun,Jiale Cao,Lige Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: UniFS is a unified fast-to-slow vision-language-action model that stratifies VLM layers into multi-frequency groups, uses latent vector inversion for cross-frequency interaction, and applies multi-level supervision, achieving 98.3% success rate (2.5% gain) with 2.1× speedup on LIBERO.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Fast-Slow dual-system VLA models suffer from a frequency dilemma: large update gaps cause semantic drift, small gaps waste computation. They also discard rich intermediate VLM features, limiting information coupling and manipulation precision.

Method: (1) Stratifying VLM layers into groups with decreasing update frequencies (shallow=fast, deep=slow); (2) latent vector inversion mechanism to re-route multi-scale VLM features to appropriate action decoding granularity; (3) multi-level coarse-to-fine supervision across temporal scales.

Result: 98.3% average success rate on LIBERO (2.5% gain over VLA-Adapter), inference latency reduced from 36.5ms to 17.8ms (2.1× speedup), validated on real-robot Franka platform.

Conclusion: UniFS resolves the frequency dilemma by enabling richer cross-frequency information transfer within a single backbone, with low-frequency pathways preserving temporal context across steps, achieving both superior performance and efficiency.

Abstract: Mainstream Fast-Slow dual system vision-language-action models decouple a high-frequency action expert from a low-frequency vision-language model for efficiency, yet they face a fundamental frequency dilemma: large update gaps cause semantic drift from stale context, while small gaps erode the intended computational savings. Moreover, because the action expert receives only the VLM's final-layer representation at a single fixed frequency, rich intermediate features are discarded, limiting both information coupling and manipulation precision. Inspired by multi-timescale neural processing in the human brain, we introduce UniFS, a unified fast-to-slow architecture that resolves these challenges through three key designs. First, we stratify the VLM layers into groups with progressively decreasing update frequencies, enabling shallow layers to capture fast-changing dynamics while deeper layers cache stable semantic context. Second, a latent vector inversion mechanism re-routes the interaction order between multi-scale VLM features and the action expert, aligning fast-varying representations with fine-grained action decoding and slow-varying ones with coarse planning. Third, a multi-level supervision strategy enforces a coarse-to-fine learning hierarchy across temporal scales. Together, these designs enable richer cross-frequency information transfer within a single backbone, while the low-frequency pathways additionally preserve temporal context across steps. Experiments on LIBERO show that UniFS achieves state-of-the-art performance (98.3\% average success rate, a 2.5\% gain over VLA-Adapter baseline) while reducing average inference latency from 36.5~ms to 17.8~ms (2.1$\times$ speedup). Real-robot experiments on a Franka platform further validate its practical applicability. Code is opensourced at https://github.com/linsun449/UniFS.

</details>


### [390] [Cloak: Zero-Shot Cross-Embodiment Manipulation by Masking the End-Effector from the VLA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22836)
*Michael Piseno,Guy Tevet,C. Karen Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Cloak is a training recipe that masks the end-effector from wrist camera views, enabling a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model to achieve zero-shot cross-embodiment transfer without requiring data from new embodiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable VLA models to transfer across different robot embodiments (grippers, arms, hands) without collecting new training data, by removing embodiment-specific visual cues from wrist camera views.

Method: Cloak renders a real-time mask in simulation using known robot geometry to hide the end-effector from wrist camera images, augmented during training to generalize to unseen embodiments. Trained on single parallel-jaw gripper data only.

Result: Cloak-VLA transfers zero-shot to various unseen embodiments (different gripper, arm, five-fingered hand) while preserving source embodiment performance, with no new data collection.

Conclusion: Decoupling wrist view from embodiment enables data to outlive the hardware it was collected on, offering a practical path for cross-embodiment robot learning.

Abstract: We present Cloak, a training recipe that endows a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model with zero-shot cross-embodiment transfer by cloaking the end-effector from its own wrist camera. The end-effector occupies a large and consistent region of the wrist view and masking it allows for embodiment-agnostic visual reasoning. Cloak renders a mask in simulation from the robot's known geometry, accurately and in real time, with no segmentation or generative models. During training, we augment the mask so the model generalizes to embodiments unseen at training time. We demonstrate the recipe with Cloak-VLA, a VLA trained with Cloak on a single parallel-jaw gripper dataset. No data of new embodiments is ever collected. Cloak-VLA transfers zero-shot to various unseen embodiments, including another gripper, another arm, and a five-fingered hand, while preserving the source embodiment's performance. By decoupling the wrist view from its own embodiment, Cloak allows data to outlive the hardware it was collected on.

</details>


### [391] [FPAS: Frontier-Based Path Planning with Adaptive Sampling for Large-Scale Unknown Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22838)
*Jinwoo Choi,Yeonkyu Lee,Jung-Taak Kim,Jisung Bae,Seung-Woo Seo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FPAS is a novel framework for efficient goal-reaching in large-scale unknown environments that uses frontier-based path planning with adaptive sampling to improve computational efficiency while maintaining competitive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing planners struggle with computational bottlenecks and inefficient paths during long-range navigation in large-scale, unknown environments.

Method: FPAS reinterprets the frontier concept for goal-directed tasks, using frontiers to guide forward progression and select subgoals for backtracking. It also introduces an adaptive sampling mechanism based on a frontier-derived openness metric, using sparse nodes in open areas and denser sampling in narrow passages.

Result: Substantially improves computational efficiency over baseline methods while maintaining highly competitive goal-reaching performance.

Conclusion: FPAS effectively overcomes computational bottlenecks and inefficient path challenges in long-range navigation through adaptive frontier-based planning.

Abstract: In this work, we propose Frontier-based Path Planning with Adaptive Sampling (FPAS), a novel framework designed for efficient goal-reaching in large-scale, unknown environments. While existing planners often struggle with computational bottlenecks or inefficient paths during long-range navigation, FPAS overcomes these challenges by reinterpreting the frontier concept for goal-directed tasks. Specifically, our method leverages frontiers to effectively guide forward progression into unobserved regions and to select promising subgoals for backtracking from dead-ends or inefficient paths. Furthermore, FPAS introduces an adaptive sampling mechanism based on a frontier-derived openness metric. This mechanism dynamically adjusts the global graph's density by employing sparse nodes in open areas to alleviate computational burdens, while preserving denser sampling in narrow passages to ensure connectivity. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that FPAS substantially improves computational efficiency over baseline methods while maintaining highly competitive goal-reaching performance.

</details>


### [392] [HiL-ResRL: A Model-Agnostic Finetuning Adapter via Human-in-the-loop Residual Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22860)
*Jingyi Liu,Zhaohong Mai,ShunSen He,Hang Ren,Chao Wang,Shunbo Zhou,XiaoDong Wu,Heng Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces a plug-and-play fine-tuning pipeline that trains a residual policy on top of existing VLA models with human-in-the-loop guidance, achieving over 95% success rate on real robots after only 1.5 hours of online RL training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing generative imitation learning models rely on Behavior Cloning (BC), which suffers from compounding errors and distributional shift, limiting their practical industrial deployment.

Method: A model-agnostic, plug-and-play fine-tuning pipeline that conceptualizes VLA-generated actions as a unified interface, trains a residual policy to rectify suboptimal actions, and incorporates human-in-the-loop guidance for safe exploration.

Result: Within only 1.5 hours of real-world online RL training, the average success rate exceeds 95% on real robots.

Conclusion: The proposed pipeline offers a practical solution for deploying behavior cloning models in industrial robotic scenarios, overcoming distributional shift issues efficiently.

Abstract: Recent advancements in generative imitation learning have significantly propelled the field of robotic manipulation. However, the majority of existing models rely heavily on Behavior Cloning (BC), a paradigm that suffers from compounding errors and distributional shift. Consequently, the efficacy of these models in practical industrial deployments remains limited. To address these challenges, we introduce a novel, plug-and-play fine-tuning pipeline designed to facilitate the robust deployment of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models in real-world environments. In contrast to contemporary reinforcement learning (RL) fine-tuning strategies, which are often constrained by specific model architectures, our proposed framework is model-agnostic and adaptable to a diverse range of VLA models. We conceptualize VLA-generated actions as a unified interface, upon which we train a residual policy. This policy is designed to rectify suboptimal actions and address the distributional shift inherent in imitation learning. Additionally, we incorporate human-in-the-loop guidance to ensure safe exploration and maximize training efficiency. We conduct experiments directly in real-world robotic settings. The results demonstrate that within only 1.5 hour of real-world online RL training, the average success rate exceeds 95% on real robots. Our work presents a practical solution for deploying behavior cloning models in industrial scenarios.

</details>


### [393] [Improving Robotic Imitation Learning via Trajectory Standardization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22907)
*Licheng Yang,Lingfeng Qian,Fei Zheng,Yonghao He,Wei Sui,Shuangshuang Li,Hu Su*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ISR is an offline preprocessing method that resamples robot manipulation trajectories by enforcing equal information distance between points, improving imitation learning success rates by ~25% over time-uniform downsampling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human demonstration trajectories for robotic manipulation are noisy and temporally irregular due to variable operator speed, pauses, and inconsistent action density. Time-uniform downsampling cannot effectively remove speed-induced non-uniformity or redundant pauses, degrading data quality and hindering policy learning.

Method: ISR maps trajectories onto an information-modulated Riemannian manifold and performs geodesic-equidistant parameterization. It constructs an information-intensity field from velocity norms (removing small-motion redundancy) and acceleration norms (preserving high-curvature and fine-manipulation phases).

Result: Evaluated on three real-world manipulation tasks with mainstream imitation learning policies, ISR improves task success rates by ~25% compared to time-uniform 3x downsampling, remains robust across different operators, and reduces dataset size and training cost.

Conclusion: ISR effectively addresses temporal irregularity in demonstration data, providing a simple offline preprocessing solution that significantly boosts imitation learning performance while reducing computational costs.

Abstract: Imitation learning for robotic manipulation relies on large sets of human demonstration trajectories, which are often noisy and temporally irregular due to variable operator speed, intermittent pauses, and inconsistent action density. A common preprocessing strategy is time-uniform downsampling to shorten sequences, but it cannot effectively remove speed-induced non-uniformity or redundant pauses. This mismatch degrades data quality and hinders policy learning. To address this issue, we propose Information-Standardized Trajectory Resampling (ISR), an offline preprocessing method for effective imitation learning. ISR resamples each trajectory by enforcing approximately equal information distance between adjacent points. Specifically, we map trajectories onto an information-modulated Riemannian manifold and perform geodesic-equidistant parameterization. We construct an information-intensity field from velocity and acceleration norms: the velocity term removes small-motion redundancy, while the acceleration term preserves high-curvature and fine-manipulation phases. We evaluate ISR on three real-world manipulation tasks with mainstream imitation learning policies. Compared with the baseline time-uniform 3x downsampling, ISR improves task success rates by about 25%, remains robust across datasets collected from different operators, and reduces both dataset size and training cost. The code and videos are publicly available at https://d-robotics-ai-lab.github.io/isr.page.

</details>


### [394] [PanoVine: Whole-Body Visuomotor Control for Soft Growing Vine Robot](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22923)
*Yimeng Qin,Xiaomeng Xu,William Heap,Aditi Oak,Shuran Song,Allison Okamura*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper presents a vision-based, data-driven control framework for autonomous vine robots, using 19 distributed cameras and end-to-end visuomotor policy learning from demonstrations to enable robust navigation and manipulation in complex, confined environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vine robots are promising for navigating complex/confined environments, but hysteresis, tether interactions, and deformations make them hard to model, limiting conventional planning and control approaches.

Method: A system integrating 19 cameras along the robot's body for whole-body vision feedback, with an end-to-end visuomotor policy trained from demonstrations for closed-loop autonomous control. The policy aggregates distributed sensing information robustly.

Result: The learned policy enables robust navigation and manipulation in challenging scenarios: steering through branched structures, climbing slopes, traversing unsupported terrain, reaching objects precisely, and maneuvering through confined spaces and obstacles.

Conclusion: This work presents the first autonomous vine robot system with a data-driven, vision-based control framework that overcomes modeling difficulties through distributed sensing and imitation learning.

Abstract: Vine robots, a class of soft, growing robots, are suitable for navigating complex and confined environments due to their compliant bodies and self-supporting growth mechanism. However, hysteresis, tether interactions, and deformations make them difficult to predict and model, which in turn limits the effectiveness of conventional planning and control approaches. In this work, we present a data-driven, vision-based control framework for the first autonomous vine robot system. Our system integrates 19 cameras distributed along the robot's body to provide comprehensive feedback of both the robot state and the surrounding environment. Using this rich whole-body vision feedback, we train an end-to-end visuomotor policy from demonstrations for closed-loop autonomous control in complex environments. The policy efficiently aggregates information from distributed sensing while maintaining robustness to inaccurate robot states and actuation. Experimental results demonstrate that the learned policy enables robust navigation and manipulation in challenging scenarios, including steering through branched structures, climbing up slopes, traversing unsupported terrain, reaching objects precisely, and maneuvering through confined spaces and obstacles. Project website https://panovine-bot.github.io

</details>


### [395] [Humanoid-OmniOcc: Stereo-Based Full-View Occupancy Dataset for Embodied AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22971)
*Xianda Guo,Bohao Zhang,Chenwei Huang,Shiyuan Chen,Ruilin Wang,Yiqun Duan,Cong Yang,Qin Zou,Wei Sui*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces Humanoid-OmniOcc, a large-scale panoramic stereo-based occupancy dataset and model for humanoid robots, using a Real2Sim2Real closed-loop paradigm to bridge simulation and real-world performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing occupancy datasets are designed for autonomous driving (forward-facing cameras, far-field geometry, static road priors) and are not suitable for embodied humanoid perception, which requires panoramic stereo sensing in diverse indoor and real-world environments.

Method: The authors propose a Real2Sim2Real closed-loop paradigm: real sensor specs drive simulation, simulation produces large-scale annotated data, and models trained in simulation are evaluated on real captures. They also propose Humanoid Surround Stereo-guided Occupancy model (Humanoid-OmniOcc) that leverages robust depth priors for accurate 2D-to-3D lifting.

Result: The dataset contains 15 simulated indoor scenes and 5 real-world environments with over 155K samples. The proposed model consistently outperforms monocular baselines and generalizes well to both unseen simulated test scenes and real-world environments.

Conclusion: Humanoid-OmniOcc validates the effectiveness of the Real2Sim2Real design for humanoid robot occupancy prediction, offering a scalable solution that bridges simulation and reality for embodied perception tasks.

Abstract: Occupancy prediction at voxel-level granularity is essential for safe robotic navigation and interaction in complex environments. Existing occupancy datasets, however, are predominantly designed for autonomous driving with vehicle-centric biases -- forward-facing cameras, far-field geometry, and static road priors -- limiting their applicability to embodied humanoid perception. We present Humanoid-OmniOcc, a large-scale panoramic stereo-based occupancy dataset tailored for humanoid robots. The dataset encompasses 15 diverse simulated indoor scenes and 5 real-world environments, yielding over 155K samples with broad scene and style diversity. Importantly, the dataset is designed around a Real2Sim2Real closed-loop paradigm: real sensor specifications drive physically accurate simulation, simulation produces large-scale annotated training data, and models trained in simulation are directly evaluated on real-world captures -- enabling iterative refinement of the sim-to-real pipeline. We further propose \textbf{H}umanoid \textbf{S}urround \textbf{S}tereo-guided \textbf{Occ}upancy model (Humanoid-OmniOcc) that exploits robust depth priors for accurate 2D-to-3D lifting. Extensive experiments show that Humanoid-OmniOcc consistently outperforms monocular baselines and generalizes well to both unseen simulated test scenes and real-world environments, validating the effectiveness of the Real2Sim2Real design. Code and data will be available upon acceptance at https://d-robotics-ai-lab.github.io/humanoid-omniocc.

</details>


### [396] [Distilling Collaborative Dynamics into Latent Space for Implicit Coordination in Decentralized Multi-Agent Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22982)
*Chanyoung Park,Minsung Yoon,Andrew Jeong,Sung-eui Yoon*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CLS-DP is a decentralized multi-agent manipulation framework that achieves implicit coordination by distilling privileged multi-agent dynamics into a latent space, enabling each agent to infer collaborative information from local RGB observations and task instructions. It outperforms centralized baselines by 38% vs 20% on RoboFactory benchmarks while maintaining team-size-independent per-agent cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Centralized multi-arm manipulation approaches scale poorly as team size increases. The authors aim to address this by proposing a decentralized framework that enables implicit coordination under partial observability without requiring global views, explicit state information, or inter-agent communication.

Method: Under CTDE paradigm, CLS-DP distills privileged multi-agent dynamics into a latent space. At deployment, each agent infers a collaborative latent from its local RGB observation and a shared task instruction, then conditions diffusion denoising on this latent for implicit coordination with per-agent cost independent of team size.

Result: Across six RoboFactory tasks (2-4 agents), CLS-DP achieves 38% mean success rate, outperforming the best centralized baseline (20%) and a decentralized ablation without collaborative latent (9%). It maintains superior parameter efficiency and attribution maps show agents conditioned on the latent attend to both their own and teammates' joints/grippers.

Conclusion: The learned collaborative latent efficiently encodes collaborative dynamics from local observation, facilitating implicit coordination under partial observability without communication or global views.

Abstract: Multi-arm manipulation demands precise spatiotemporal coordination, yet many centralized approaches scale poorly as team size increases. To address this, we propose CLS-DP, a decentralized multi-agent framework that enables implicit coordination under partial observability without shared global views, explicit state information, or inter-agent communication. Under the centralized training and decentralized execution (CTDE) paradigm, CLS-DP distills privileged multi-agent dynamics into a latent space. At deployment, each agent infers a collaborative latent from its local RGB observation and a shared task instruction; it then conditions the diffusion denoising process on this latent. This design enables implicit coordination with a per-agent cost independent of team size. Across six RoboFactory benchmark tasks spanning two to four agents, CLS-DP achieves a 38% mean success rate, outperforming the best centralized baseline (20%) and a decentralized ablation without the collaborative latent (9%). It also maintains superior parameter efficiency across all agent configurations. Attribution maps show that an agent conditioned on the collaborative latent places high attribution on the joints and grippers of both itself and its teammates throughout execution. This suggests that the learned latent efficiently encodes collaborative dynamics from local observation, which facilitates implicit coordination in realistic settings characterized by partial observability.

</details>


### [397] [TEXEDO : Test Time Scaling for Controller-aware Language-conditioned Humanoid Motion Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22998)
*Jianuo Cao,Yuxin Chen,Yuzhen Song,Masayoshi Tomizuka,Chenran Li,Thomas Tian*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TEXEDO is a test-time scaling framework that improves text-conditioned humanoid motion generation by sampling multiple candidates and selecting the best one based on dynamic feasibility (hard constraint) and semantic alignment (selection objective), validated through simulation and real-world deployment on a Unitree G1 robot.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current motion generators trained on retargeted human motion data fail to capture whole-body tracking controller nuances (balance, contact, actuation limits), leading to semantically plausible but physically unexecutable motions for humanoid robots.

Method: TEXEDO samples multiple candidate motions from a pretrained text-conditioned generator, then selects via a reward model combining: (1) a dynamic feasibility verifier distilled from whole-body tracking rollouts, and (2) a semantic alignment verifier using a learned co-embedding space. Feasibility is a hard constraint; alignment is the selection objective within the feasible set.

Result: Through large-scale simulation studies and real-world deployment on a Unitree G1 humanoid robot, TEXEDO consistently improves both tracking fidelity and text alignment.

Conclusion: Grounded verification is an effective path toward deployable language-guided humanoid motion generation.

Abstract: Text-conditioned motion generation is a promising interface for programming humanoid robots, yet current generators are often trained on human motion datasets retargeted to robot morphologies. Although such data provides rich semantic and kinematic priors, it fails to capture the nuances of whole-body tracking controllers, including balance, contact dynamics, actuation limits, and controller-specific failure modes. As a result, generated motions can be semantically plausible but difficult or impossible for the robot to execute. We introduce TEXEDO, a test-time scaling framework for humanoid motion generation that improves motion quality without requiring a stronger underlying generator. Given a text prompt, TEXEDO samples multiple candidate motions from a pretrained text-conditioned generator and selects the best motion that is both executable and task-aligned. The reward model combines a dynamic feasibility verifier, distilled from whole-body tracking rollouts to predict physical executability, with a semantic alignment verifier that measures text-motion alignment in a learned co-embedding space. Our pipeline treats dynamic feasibility as a hard constraint and semantic alignment as the selection objective within the feasible set. Through large-scale simulation studies and real-world deployment on a Unitree G1 humanoid robot, we show that TEXEDO consistently improves both tracking fidelity and text alignment. These results demonstrate that grounded verification is an effective path toward deployable language-guided humanoid motion generation. Project website: https://jianuocao.github.io/TEXEDO/

</details>


### [398] [ISOPoT: Imaging Sonar Odometry by Point Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23006)
*Jaša Samec,Vid Rijavec,Marko Peljhan,Aleksander Grm,Andrej Androjna,Danijel Skočaj,Matej Dobrevski*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ISOPoT introduces a point-tracking-based odometry method for forward-looking sonars, outperforming previous state-of-the-art in underwater navigation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable underwater navigation is challenging; sonar images are noisy and lack semantic structure, causing standard vision methods to fail.

Method: Uses multi-frame point tracks as the primary correspondence representation with lightweight optimizations in a sonar odometry pipeline.

Result: Outperforms prior state-of-the-art consistently on the Aracati 2017 dataset and internal real-world sonar data, in both sonar-only and multi-sensor settings.

Conclusion: Point tracking is an effective correspondence representation for sonar odometry, enabling robust performance in challenging underwater conditions.

Abstract: Reliable navigation in underwater environments remains a key challenge in marine robotics. In such scenarios, forward-looking sonars are a natural choice for long-range perception, offering wide coverage even in turbid, low-visibility conditions. However, sonar images are inherently noisy, contain artifacts, and lack rich semantic structure, causing standard computer vision methods for keypoint detection and matching to perform poorly. In this paper, we introduce ISOPoT, an imaging sonar odometry method based on modern point tracking techniques. We propose a sonar odometry pipeline that uses multi-frame point tracks as its primary correspondence representation, augmented with lightweight optimizations to improve robustness. We evaluated the proposed method on the Aracati 2017 dataset, as well as on an internal sonar dataset collected in real-world underwater environments. Our results show that ISOPoT outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods consistently in both sonar-only scenarios and in multi-sensor settings.

</details>


### [399] [AdaReP:Adaptive Re-Planning under Model Mismatch for Neural World-Model Predictive Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23079)
*Yutian Cheng,Xiaojian Ma,Xianhao Wang,Min Yang,Rongpeng Su,Hangxin Liu,Xi Chen,Shuai Li,Qing Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper analyzes the trade-off between computational cost and accumulated error in neural world model-based MPC, deriving a perturbation-based dynamic-regret framework to model stale-plan penalties. It proposes AdaReP, a training-free wrapper that adaptively adjusts replanning tolerance online based on rollout deviation and local sensitivity, achieving significant computation reduction (e.g., 80% fewer queries on real robots) without sacrificing task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural world models with MPC replan at every step to limit prediction errors, but this creates high computational overhead. The authors aim to reduce this overhead by reusing cached plans while understanding how prediction mismatch degrades performance.

Method: The authors develop a perturbation-based dynamic-regret framework to analyze the trade-off between plan reuse and error accumulation. They propose AdaReP, a training-free wrapper that adaptively adjusts replanning tolerance online using current rollout deviation and local sensitivity estimates, without modifying the world model or planner.

Result: AdaReP substantially reduces planner-side computation while maintaining comparable task performance across image-space planning, latent-space control, and real-world robotic manipulation, including over 80% fewer queries in a 50-trial physical robot study.

Conclusion: Adaptive replanning tolerance based on local dynamics sensitivity and deviation from cached rollout is an effective, training-free strategy to reduce MPC computational cost without performance degradation in neural world models.

Abstract: Neural world models coupled with model predictive control (MPC) replan at every environment step to bound accumulated prediction error, but this incurs substantial computational overhead. Reusing a cached plan reduces this overhead, yet its effectiveness depends on how prediction mismatch propagates through the local dynamics. We analyze this trade-off with a perturbation-based dynamic-regret framework and show that stale-plan penalties scale with the reuse tolerance, the accumulated mismatch since the last replanning step, and the local dynamics sensitivity. Based on this structure, we propose AdaReP, a training-free wrapper that adapts the replanning tolerance online using the current deviation from the cached rollout and a local sensitivity estimate, without modifying the learned world model or planner. Across image-space planning, latent-space control, and real-world robotic manipulation, AdaReP substantially reduces planner-side computation while maintaining comparable task performance, including over 80% fewer queries on a 50-trial physical robot study.

</details>


### [400] [Foresight: Failure Detection for Long-Horizon Robotic Manipulation with Action-Conditioned World Model Latents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23085)
*Haoran Zhang,Yifu Lu,Boyang Wang,Xuhui Kang,Yen-Ling Kuo,Zezhou Cheng,Mengdi Wang,Odest Chadwicke Jenkins*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Foresight is a failure detection framework for long-horizon robotic tasks that uses action-conditioned world-model embeddings trained only on final success/failure labels, with adaptive threshold calibration via conformal prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon robotic tasks are common but failure detection remains underexplored because failure onset is ambiguous and dense temporal annotations are typically unavailable.

Method: Foresight monitors manipulation trajectories using latent representations from an action-conditioned world model, trained only with final task-level labels, and uses functional conformal prediction (FCP) to adaptively calibrate detection thresholds.

Result: Evaluated on LIBERO-Long, ManiSkill-Long, BEHAVIOR-1K, and real robots (ReactorX-200 and Franka arms), outperforming state-of-the-art failure detection methods.

Conclusion: Action-conditioned world-model embeddings provide a scalable representation for reliable failure monitoring in long-horizon manipulation.

Abstract: Long-horizon tasks are common in real-world robotic deployments, yet failure detection for such tasks remains underexplored. Detecting failures in long-horizon robotic tasks is particularly challenging because failure onset is often ambiguous and dense temporal annotations are typically unavailable. We present Foresight, a failure detection framework that monitors manipulation trajectories using latent representations from an action-conditioned world model. Foresight is trained using only final task-level success or failure labels. By leveraging predictive world-model embeddings, our method provides a unified framework for failure detection across different policies. We further use functional conformal prediction (FCP) to calibrate detection thresholds adaptively. We evaluate Foresight with state-of-the-art vision-language-action policies in simulation on LIBERO-Long, ManiSkill-Long, and BEHAVIOR-1K, compare it against state-of-the-artfailure detection methods, and validate it on real robots with three long-horizon tasks on a ReactorX-200 arm and one task on a Franka arm. Our results suggest that action-conditioned world-model embeddings provide a scalable representation for reliable failure monitoring in long-horizon manipulation.

</details>


### [401] [Flow as Flow: Modeling Robot Velocity Fields as Probability Velocity Fields for Flow-Based Object Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23090)
*Koki Seno,Daichi Yashima,Yusuke Takagi,Kento Tokura,Komei Sugiura*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Flow as Flow proposes a flow matching framework that models robot motions as dense probability velocity fields rather than sparse keypoint displacements, achieving 33× faster generation and higher success rates across benchmarks and real-world tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
