  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-training frontier models normally requires weeks of human labor (proposing data/recipe changes, launching runs, reading evals). The goal is to automate this entire loop to reduce human effort and potentially discover better strategies than humans would.

Method: An autonomous closed-loop system that iteratively proposes data and recipe changes, launches runs, reads evals, and decides what to keep — operating over multiple weeks with no human intervention across four rounds on a 30B Nemotron model. The system also demonstrated the ability to detect when its dev metric stopped tracking external performance and autonomously revised its search policy.

Result: The autonomously produced 30B model scored 0.86 vs top human submission's 0.87 on the NVIDIA Nemotron-Reasoning Challenge leaderboard (8th of ~4000). The system also successfully post-trained 120B and 550B Nemotron variants, though no human baseline exists for comparison at those scales.

Conclusion: A scaled autonomous loop can produce discovery, not just optimization — it detected a misleading measurement frame and changed what counted as evidence. This provides a datapoint for the claim that autonomous systems can perform end-to-end post-training of frontier-class models, though the authors do not claim a "first autonomous match" of human researchers, only the first publicly reported autonomous post-training at this scale.

Abstract: Post-training a frontier model is normally weeks of human work: proposing data and recipe changes, launching runs, reading evals, deciding what to keep. We report an autonomous system that runs this loop with no human in the loop, post-training a 30B Nemotron across four rounds over multiple weeks. The autonomously produced model reaches a held-out score of 0.86 against the top human submission's 0.87 on the public NVIDIA Nemotron-Reasoning Challenge leaderboard, placing 8th of ~4000 at the time of writing. More striking than the number: the loop detected that its own dev metric had stopped tracking external performance on the weakest domain -- candidates drove dev to record highs without moving the external target -- and revised its own search policy, no longer maximizing dev but seeking interventions that lowered the now-misleading proxy while improving the external target. We treat this as direct, auditable evidence that a scaled autonomous loop can produce discovery, not only optimization: it detected that its measurement frame had become misleading and changed what counted as evidence. We take the operational view that any system worth the "recursive self-improvement" label must eventually perform end-to-end post-training of a frontier-class model; this is one datapoint of that bar being cleared. We do not claim a "first autonomous match" of human researchers. The claim we make is narrower and auditable: to our knowledge, this is the first publicly reported autonomous post-training run at this scale, where prior public autonomous-ML-research demonstrations sit at GPT-2-class (~124M) budgets. The same system also post-trains the 120B and 550B Nemotron; with no public human baseline there, this shows only that the loop closes at that scale, not that its output is competitive -- infrastructure evidence, with the effectiveness claim deferred until a comparable human anchor exists.

</details>


### [456] [Expected Free Energy-based Planning as Variational Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20658)
*Wouter W. L. Nuijten,Thijs van de Laar,Bert de Vries*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper reformulates Expected Free Energy (EFE) based planning as Variational Free Energy minimization on a generative model with epistemic priors, showing that this yields EFE plus complexity. Experiments on T-maze, Reactivity Maze, and MiniGrid demonstrate information-seeking behavior, robustness to stochasticity, and scalability beyond tabular methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing EFE-based planning methods use specialized optimization procedures that are difficult to extend or analyze. The authors aim to unify planning with perception and learning under a single Free Energy Principle framework.

Method: The authors formulate planning as Variational Free Energy minimization on a generative model augmented with epistemic priors. They show this minimization decomposes into expected plan costs (EFE) plus a complexity term.

Result: Epistemic priors induce information-seeking behavior; the variational (policy-based) formulation outperforms plan-based methods under stochastic transitions; temporal factorization enables scalability beyond tabular active inference methods.

Conclusion: The variational formulation unifies planning with perception/learning under the Free Energy Principle, and scales to partially observable environments where tabular methods fail.

Abstract: Planning under uncertainty requires agents to balance goal achievement with information gathering. Active inference addresses this through the Expected Free Energy (EFE), a cost function that unifies instrumental and epistemic objectives. However, existing EFE-based methods typically employ specialized optimization procedures that are difficult to extend or analyze. In this paper, we show that EFE-based planning can be formulated as Variational Free Energy minimization on a generative model augmented with epistemic priors. Our main result demonstrates that minimizing a Variational Free Energy functional with appropriately chosen priors yields a decomposition into expected plan costs (the EFE) plus a complexity term. This formulation reinforces theoretical consistency with the Free Energy Principle by casting planning as the same inferential process that governs perception and learning. We validate our approach on three environments of increasing complexity: a deterministic T-maze, a stochastic Reactivity Maze, and a partially observable MiniGrid DoorKey-8x8 environment. The experiments demonstrate that the epistemic priors induce information-seeking behavior, that the variational formulation yields policy-based inference outperforming plan-based methods under stochastic transitions, and that temporal factorization enables scalability to environments where existing tabular active inference methods cannot operate.

</details>


### [457] [Skill Coverage: A Test Adequacy Metric for Agent Skills](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20659)
*Boyin Tan,Xiaowei Huang,Youcheng Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces "skill coverage," a test adequacy metric that measures how much of a skill's documented behavior constraints are actually exercised during agent execution. Applying it to SkillsBench shows only 39.90-43.98% coverage, meaning task success alone doesn't guarantee thorough skill testing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations assess skills only through task-level outcomes (success/failure), but this fails to reveal which parts of a skill were actually exercised or remain untested.

Method: The authors define skill coverage by extracting observable behavior constraints from skill documents, then determining whether an agent trajectory provides sufficient evidence to exercise and evaluate each constraint, yielding a binary cover/not-cover judgment.

Result: Applying skill coverage to SkillsBench shows existing benchmarks cover only 39.90% to 43.98% of skill behavior constraints, revealing that successful task completion does not imply adequate testing.

Conclusion: Task success alone is insufficient for evaluating agent skills; skill coverage provides a complementary measure of how thoroughly agent skills are tested.

Abstract: Agent skills encode reusable procedural knowledge that guides large language model agents across tasks and execution contexts. Existing evaluations primarily assess skills through task level outcomes, yet task success alone does not reveal which parts of a skill have been exercised or which remain untested. We introduce skill coverage, a test adequacy metric that treats the skill artifact as the object under test. Our approach extracts observable skill behavior constraints from skill documents and measures whether an agent trajectory provides sufficient evidence to exercise and evaluate each constraint. Skill coverage uses a binary cover or not cover judgment, which reports whether a documented behavior has been exercised with sufficient observable evidence, without assigning an additional outcome label to the behavior. Applying skill coverage to SkillsBench reveals that existing benchmark executions cover only 39.90 to 43.98% of skill behavior constraints, suggesting that current benchmark tasks leave large portions of documented skill guidance untested. These findings show that successful task completion does not imply adequate testing of the skill artifact, highlighting skill coverage as a measure of how thoroughly agent skills are tested.

</details>


### [458] [From Knowing to Acting: Benchmarking Self-Awareness Capability of LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20661)
*Yifan Li,Shengbin Yue,Boyu Feng,Jinhu Qi,Bo Ke,Zixing Song,Hongru Wang,Zhongyu Wei,Irwin King*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: KAPRO is a framework that evaluates whether LLM agents know when they need external tools versus when they should rely on internal knowledge, finding that self-awareness strongly correlates with success but degrades on simple tasks, and that open-source models overuse tools more than proprietary ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks focus on execution success but neglect self-awareness — the agent's ability to judge whether a problem requires external tools or can be solved via internal parametric knowledge.

Method: KAPRO (Knowing-Acting Quadrant PRObe) decouples metacognitive judgment (Knowing) from spontaneous execution (Acting). The authors construct KAware, a dataset partitioning tasks into external, internal, and hybrid subspaces to systematically probe epistemic boundaries.

Result: Self-awareness strongly correlates with task success but degrades sharply in internal-capability settings. Open-source and instruction-following models show tool overuse due to shallow pattern matching; proprietary and reasoning-oriented models exhibit more reliable cognitive gating.

Conclusion: Self-awareness is a critical dimension for evaluating LLM agents beyond execution success, and current models — especially open-source ones — struggle with knowing when not to use tools.

Abstract: The integration of external tools has transitioned LLM agents from passive responders to autonomous systems. However, current benchmarks prioritize execution success, neglecting self-awareness capability, the ability to discern whether a problem requires necessary external resources or can be solved via internal parametric knowledge. To address this, we introduce KAPRO (Knowing-Acting Quadrant PRObe), a framework that evaluates cognitive-behavioral alignment by decoupling an agent's metacognitive judgment (Knowing) from its spontaneous execution (Acting). We further construct KAware, a dataset rigorously partitioning tasks into external, internal, and hybrid subspaces to systematically probe these epistemic boundaries. Extensive experiments across diverse agent architectures show that self-awareness capability is strongly correlated with task success but degrades sharply in internal-capability settings. Moreover, open-source and instruction-following models exhibit stronger tool overuse due to shallow pattern matching, while proprietary and reasoning-oriented models demonstrate more reliable cognitive gating. Benchmark and codes are available at https://github.com/AI-Santiago/KAware.

</details>


### [459] [Confidence Laundering in Agent Systems: Why Uncertainty Needs a Latent Carrier](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20662)
*Kaiwen Shi,Zheyuan Zhang,Han Bao,Colby Nelson,Yanfang Ye*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper identifies an "interface bottleneck" in agent systems where uncertainty from upstream decisions is lost when handed off to downstream components, leading to error amplification. The authors propose "latent uncertainty" as a carrier to preserve and propagate uncertainty across component interfaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agent systems fail to propagate uncertainty across component boundaries, causing local ambiguity to become system-level error amplification. The "confidence laundering" phenomenon—where fragile upstream states are repackaged as clean artifacts—makes downstream agents over-trust flawed intermediate decisions.

Method: The paper introduces the concept of "latent uncertainty"—an uncertainty-bearing carrier attached to decision handoffs that preserves pre-commitment fragility in a form downstream components can use, shifting focus from step-wise estimation to interface design for uncertainty preservation.

Result: The paper identifies two key failure concepts: "uncertain decision handoff" (transfer of decisions made under uncertainty) and "confidence laundering" (repackaging fragile states as valid artifacts). It proposes latent uncertainty as a solution to address the interface bottleneck.

Conclusion: To build more recoverable agent systems, the field should shift from step-wise uncertainty estimation toward uncertainty-preserving interface design, where latent uncertainty carriers maintain fragility information across component boundaries.

Abstract: Modern agent systems can turn uncertainty into overconfidence. Fragile upstream decisions are often exposed to downstream components as clean intermediate artifacts, while the uncertainty behind those decisions is lost at the interface. As a result, local ambiguity can become system-level error amplification. We argue that this reveals an interface bottleneck in agent uncertainty propagation: uncertainty does not propagate simply because a trajectory contains uncertain steps; it propagates only when it survives the handoff between components. We define uncertain decision handoff as the transfer of an intermediate decision made under uncertainty, and identify confidence laundering as a failure mode in which fragile upstream states are repackaged as procedurally valid artifacts that downstream agents over-trust. To address this bottleneck, we propose latent uncertainty as an uncertainty-bearing carrier attached to decision handoffs. Rather than replacing text with hidden states, latent uncertainty aims to preserve pre-commitment fragility in a form that downstream components can use. This position shifts agent uncertainty propagation from step-wise uncertainty estimation toward uncertainty-preserving interface design for more recoverable agent systems.

</details>


### [460] [DrugBench: Evaluating AI Control Protocols for Medication Harm Mitigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20663)
*Guido Freire,Agustín Martínez-Suñé,Viviana Cotik*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces DrugBench, an evaluation benchmark for AI control protocols in medical question-answering, combining medical conversations with FDA drug labels to mitigate medication-related harm. The authors propose severity-based monitoring to address limitations in existing control protocols, moving beyond probability-based safety to account for the severity of unsafe outputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large Language Models in medical question-answering are safety-critical since misaligned outputs can cause severe patient harm. AI control protocols have been effective in domains like code generation but have not been systematically studied in medical settings. Existing safety approaches focus on probability of unsafe outputs rather than their severity, which is inadequate for medical applications.

Method: The authors (1) develop DrugBench, combining 3,671 multi-turn medical conversations from HealthBench with official FDA drug labels covering four harm categories (drug interactions, contraindications, dosing constraints, patient action restrictions); (2) construct a pipeline for evaluating AI control protocols; and (3) propose severity-based monitoring that accounts for the severity of unsafe outputs, not just their probability.

Result: Existing control protocols can be subverted under the revised safety definition that accounts for severity. The proposed severity-based monitoring addresses this limitation, though specific quantitative results are not detailed in the abstract.

Conclusion: AI control protocols need to be adapted for medical settings with severity-aware safety metrics. DrugBench provides a systematic benchmark for this purpose, and severity-based monitoring offers a promising direction for safer medical LLM deployment.

Abstract: Large Language Models have the potential to expand and improve the access to clinical information by enabling new ways of interacting with medical knowledge in natural language. However, their deployment in medical question-answering settings is safety-critical, since misaligned outputs can lead to severe patient harm. AI control is an emerging approach that introduces external safeguards to mitigate unsafe behaviours in misaligned systems and has been shown to be effective in domains such as code generation. However, its applicability and effectiveness in medical settings have not been systematically studied. In this work, we present a pipeline for evaluating AI control protocols to mitigate medication-related harm. To this end, we introduce DrugBench, an AI control evaluation benchmark which combines 3,671 multi-turn medical conversations from HealthBench with drug information from official FDA labels, covering four categories of medication-related harm: drug interactions, contraindications, dosing constraints, and patient action restrictions. Furthermore, inspired by the medical domain, we argue that safety should account for the severity of unsafe outputs, not just their probability. Under this revised definition, we show that existing control protocols can be subverted and propose severity-based monitoring to address this limitation.

</details>


### [461] [A Quantum-Assisted Agentic Distributed Artificial Intelligence Framework for Deadline-Bounded Orchestration of Hybrid Renewable Microgrids](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20667)
*Iacovos I. Ioannou,Saher Javaid,Minella Bezha,Yasuo Tan,Naoto Nagaoka,Vasos Vassiliou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a quantum-assisted agentic distributed AI framework for real-time microgrid dispatch, using BDIx agents to formulate dispatch as QUBO problems solved by a portfolio of quantum, quantum-inspired, and classical solvers. A belief-shaped storage valuation mechanism is introduced. Evaluation on a 24-hour simulation achieves exact optimal daily cost (146.24 EUR) with zero deadline misses, 97.83% renewable utilization, and zero unserved energy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time orchestration of microgrids with fluctuating renewables, dispatchable units, storage, and curtailable consumers requires solving combinatorial dispatch and coalition formation problems under hard control deadlines—a challenging optimization problem needing efficient, deadline-aware solutions.

Method: A quantum-assisted agentic DAI framework where BDIx agents formulate each slot's dispatch as a QUBO problem. A coordinator agent dynamically selects from a portfolio of solvers (QAOA, tabu search, simulated annealing, etc.) based on learned latency beliefs. A belief-shaped storage valuation mechanism prices storage at discounted future-peak value to inject intertemporal information.

Result: Zero deliberation deadlines missed; exact optimum achieved every slot; daily cost of 146.24 EUR equals exact lower bound; 97.83% renewable utilization; zero unserved energy. Deactivating the storage valuation mechanism increases cost by 4.5% to 152.75 EUR.

Conclusion: The proposed quantum-assisted agentic framework reliably meets real-time microgrid control deadlines while achieving optimal dispatch, demonstrating that combining quantum, quantum-inspired, and classical solvers with agentic deliberation is viable for microgrid orchestration.

Abstract: The real-time orchestration of microgrids that combine fluctuating renewable sources, dispatchable units, storage and curtailable consumers requires the repeated solution of combinatorial dispatch and coalition formation problems under hard control deadlines. In this paper, a quantum-assisted agentic distributed artificial intelligence (DAI) framework is proposed in which the dispatch problem of each control slot is formulated as a quadratic unconstrained binary optimization (QUBO) problem by Belief-Desire-Intention extended (BDIx) agents and is solved by a portfolio of quantum, quantum-inspired and classical solvers. Solver selection is elevated to a first-class agentic deliberation action of the coordinator agent. Learned beliefs about solver latencies are maintained and the solver intention that is expected to satisfy the prevailing deliberation deadline is committed in each slot. In addition, a belief-shaped storage valuation mechanism is introduced through which the storage agent prices its energy at a discounted future-peak value, injecting intertemporal information into the otherwise myopic per-slot optimization. The framework is evaluated on a 24-hour simulation of a grid-connected microgrid with photovoltaic, wind, battery, genset and demand-response assets, with the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) executed by statevector simulation and benchmarked per slot against tabu search, simulated annealing, binary particle swarm optimization, greedy descent and exhaustive enumeration. Zero deliberation deadlines are missed, the committed dispatch attains the exact optimum on every slot and the realized daily cost of 146.24 EUR equals the exact lower bound, with 97.83 percent renewable utilization and zero unserved energy. When the storage valuation mechanism is deactivated, the daily cost is increased to 152.75 EUR, a 4.5 percent increase.

</details>


### [462] [Agent Behavior Mining: Generative AI Agent Governance in Business Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20669)
*Hoang Vu,Maximilian Körner,Adrian Rebmann,Gabriel Kevorkian,Michael Perscheid,Gregor Berg,Timotheus Kampik*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces Agent Behavior Mining, a governance capability that applies process mining techniques to make generative AI agent decision-making observable and traceable, addressing the "invisible autonomy risk" in business processes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Organizations deploying generative AI agents face a governance dilemma: these agents increase operational flexibility but their non-deterministic nature challenges the control and standardization that Business Process Management seeks to enforce, creating an "invisible autonomy risk."

Method: (1) An event data model translating granular agent activities (reasoning traces, tool usage, token costs) into standardized process logs; (2) instantiation in a multi-agent order-to-cash implementation; (3) an exploratory study with 18 industry practitioners evaluating perceived practical utility.

Result: Practitioners view behavioral transparency as a prerequisite for trust and consider the ability to examine agent reasoning as an important governance requirement for next-generation AI-driven business processes.

Conclusion: Agent Behavior Mining enables process managers to detect policy deviations and quantify operational variability, making generative AI agent decision-making observable and traceable for governance purposes.

Abstract: As organizations increasingly deploy generative AI agents to automate business processes, they face a governance dilemma: although these agents can increase operational flexibility, their non-deterministic nature challenges the control and standardization that Business Process Management seeks to enforce. This paper addresses this \emph{invisible autonomy risk} by introducing \emph{Agent Behavior Mining}, a governance capability that enables the application of process mining techniques to render generative AI agent decision-making observable and traceable. We (1) improve the understanding of generative AI agent behavior through an event data model that translates granular agent activities -- including reasoning traces, tool usage, and token costs -- into standardized process logs; (2) instantiate the data model in a multi-agent order-to-cash implementation, demonstrating how process managers can leverage agent logs to detect policy deviations and quantify operational variability; and (3) evaluate the perceived practical utility of the approach in an exploratory study with 18 industry practitioners. The results indicate that practitioners view behavioral transparency as a prerequisite for trust and consider the ability to examine agent reasoning as an important governance requirement for the next generation of AI-driven business processes.

</details>


### [463] [Democratizing and accelerating AI-driven pathology research through agentic intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20677)
*Jiabo Ma,Cheng Jin,Yihui Wang,Hao Jiang,Ling Liang,Yingxue Xu,Junlin Hou,Zhengrui Guo,Zhengyu Zhang,Yifei Xia,Hongyi Wang,Fengtao Zhou,Zhe Xu,Huajun Zhou,Jiarui Ouyang,Qian Zeng,On Ki Tang,Eunhyang Park,Carolyn Glass,Ronald Cheong Kin Chan,Li Liang,Hao Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PathLab is an autonomous agentic framework that translates natural-language research goals into executable computational pathology workflows, achieving non-inferior performance to expert implementations across 12 datasets while enabling non-programmers to design and execute studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite advances in computational pathology foundation models, widespread adoption is limited by technical complexity and programming requirements. The authors aim to lower these barriers so domain experts without coding skills can independently conduct computational pathology studies.

Method: PathLab organizes workflow generation around reusable methodological modules (data preprocessing, model development, evaluation, interpretation) and uses an agentic framework to translate natural-language prompts into structured, executable pipelines with built-in semantic validation.

Result: Evaluated on 12 public datasets across ROI classification, WSI classification, segmentation, and survival prediction. Achieved non-inferior performance to expert implementations, enforced semantic validity of prompts, and rejected incompatible workflows proactively. In user studies, it drastically reduced pipeline generation time and enabled non-programmers to independently design and execute studies.

Conclusion: PathLab serves as a reliable bridge between biomedical intent and computational execution, democratizing computational pathology by allowing studies to be designed at the level of scientific questions rather than programming expertise.

Abstract: Computational pathology has advanced rapidly with the emergence of foundation models, yet widespread adoption remains limited by substantial technical complexity and programming requirements. Here we present PathLab, an autonomous agentic framework that translates natural-language research objectives into executable and validated computational pathology workflows through the structured composition of domain-specific skills and tools. By organizing workflow generation around reusable methodological modules, including data preprocessing, model development, evaluation and interpretation, PathLab enables studies to be specified at the level of scientific intent rather than implementation details. We evaluated PathLab across 12 public datasets spanning four representative task families: region-of-interest classification, whole-slide image classification, segmentation and survival prediction. Across all task categories, PathLab achieved non-inferior performance relative to expert implementations, while consistently enforcing semantic validity of user prompts and proactively rejecting incompatible workflow specifications prior to execution. In controlled user studies, PathLab substantially reduced the time required to generate executable analytical pipelines and enabled domain experts without programming experience to independently design, execute and evaluate computational pathology studies. Together, these results establish PathLab as a reliable interface between biomedical intent and computational execution, enabling computational pathology studies to be designed at the level of scientific questions rather than programming expertise. By lowering technical barriers to advanced AI methodologies, PathLab provides a foundation for the broader democratization of computational pathology.

</details>


### [464] [From Question Answering to Task Completion: A Survey on Agent System and Harness Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20683)
*Jianyuan Guo,Zhiwei Hao,Chengcheng Wang,Cheng Fan,Tingzhang Luo,Hongguang Li,Ying Gao,Hefei Mei,Jiankun Peng,Rongjian Xu,Minjing Dong,Han Wu,Mengyu Zheng,Kai Han,Shiqi Wang,Chang Xu,Yunhe Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This survey examines LLM-based agents through a model-harness lens, arguing that agent quality emerges from the interaction between model capability, runtime infrastructure, task structure, and evaluation design, rather than from models alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM-based agents evolve from simple prompt engineering to complex workflows and agent-native training, it becomes critical to identify where the performance bottleneck lies—whether in the foundation model, the execution harness, or the coupling between them.

Method: The authors propose a model-harness analytical framework, define agents functionally, decompose the execution harness into six runtime responsibilities (observation, context, control, action, state, verification), trace four paradigms of agent engineering, and synthesize evidence on how runtime design affects task outcomes.

Result: The decomposition reveals that agent performance bottlenecks are not solely model-centric; runtime harness design significantly impacts long-horizon task completion, efficiency, and reliability. The survey maps task properties to harness configurations and reviews benchmark practices.

Conclusion: Agent quality emerges from the interaction between model capability, runtime infrastructure, task structure, and evaluation design. Open challenges include value-aware evaluation, safety, harness generalization, and model-harness co-evolution.

Abstract: LLM-based agents mark a shift from passive question answering to active task completion: they perceive environments, invoke tools, maintain state, and act over extended horizons. As agent systems have evolved from prompt engineering to workflows and context engineering, harness engineering, and agent-native training with co-evolution, a central question has become increasingly important: where does the bottleneck in agent performance reside, in the foundation model, in the execution harness, or in the coupling between them? This survey examines LLM-based agents through a model-harness lens. We first clarify the functional definition of agents and the implementation view of an LLM-based agent as a foundation model coupled with an execution harness. We then analyze the limits of model-centric scaling, trace four paradigms of agent engineering, and decompose the execution harness into six coupled runtime responsibilities: observation, context, control, action, state, and verification. Using this decomposition, we map task properties and domain pressures to harness configurations, review benchmark and evaluation practices, and synthesize model-harness evidence on how runtime design affects long-horizon task completion, efficiency, and reliability. Finally, we identify open challenges in value-aware evaluation, safety, harness generalization, and model-harness co-evolution. Rather than treating agents as models with auxiliary tools, this survey argues that agent quality -- including success, efficiency, safety, and generalization -- emerges from the interaction between model capability, runtime infrastructure, task structure, and evaluation design. A collection of papers discussed in this survey is provided in https://github.com/ggjy/Awesome-Agent-Engineering.

</details>


### [465] [Artificial Intelligence as Monism: Ontological, Organisational, and Methodological Implications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20704)
*Bertrand K. Hassani*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes understanding AI as monism—a unified, indivisible essence—rather than a collection of separable components like data and algorithms, arguing this perspective has major implications for epistemology, ethics, organization, project management, and data governance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors argue that reductionist approaches to AI (treating it as decomposable parts) lead to structural inefficiencies, siloed thinking, and fragmented governance, and a paradigm shift is needed to achieve genuine organizational agility.

Method: The paper draws from philosophical traditions (monism, dualism, holism) to conceptually re-frame AI as monism, then explores implications across epistemological, ethical, organizational, project management, and data management dimensions.

Result: The paper presents a philosophical argument rather than empirical results, proposing that a monistic view of AI positions it as a central interpretive force, challenges siloed structures, advocates for transversal teams, and calls for unified evaluation and data architectures.

Conclusion: The paper calls for a paradigm shift in how AI is conceptualized, governed, and integrated, suggesting that embracing AI as monism is necessary to avoid reductionist inefficiencies and achieve genuine organizational agility.

Abstract: This paper argues that Artificial Intelligence should be understood as a form of monism:
  a unified substance that cannot be decomposed into separate elements such as data,
  algorithms, or technical architectures. Drawing from philosophical traditions of monism,
  dualism, and holism, the paper contends that AI is not merely a collection of components
  but a single, indivisible essence reflecting the phenomena it replicates. Treating AI as
  monism has deep implications across multiple dimensions. Epistemologically, it positions
  AI as the central interpretive force across technological, organisational, and societal
  domains, while raising ethical and existential concerns regarding singularity, the
  homogenisation of innovation, and the concentration of decision-making power. At the
  organisational level, a monistic approach challenges traditional siloed structures,
  advocating instead for transversal, problem-centric teams whose mandate derives from
  the integrity of the problem rather than from departmental hierarchy. In project
  management, it implies a unified vision and an integrated evaluation of complexity in
  which no single stakeholder perspective dominates the assessment of outcomes. In data
  and information management, it calls for architectures that reflect the irreducible unity
  of the phenomena being modelled. Ultimately, this paper calls for a paradigm shift in
  how AI is conceptualised, governed, and integrated, suggesting that only by embracing AI
  as monism can organisations achieve genuine agility and avoid the structural
  inefficiencies inherent to reductionist approaches.

</details>


### [466] [Simulated Customers Never Walk Away: Decision Fidelity of LLM User Simulators Measured Against Real Purchase Outcomes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20708)
*Liang Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM-as-user simulators accurately mimic eventual buyers but fail to reproduce the disengagement behavior of real non-buyers, leading to systematically overoptimistic evaluation of conversational AI agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing frameworks only measure "communicative fidelity" (whether simulators talk like humans with assigned goals), but fail to test "decision fidelity" (whether simulators reproduce real users' endogenous, latent, and decaying motivation when facing real choices).

Method: Using 2,790 production conversations between an LLM sales agent and real customers (793 with verified payment), authors apply a teacher-forced probe protocol to compare simulator behavior against real users, measuring decision-state dynamics and outcome-correlated biases.

Result: Simulators reproduce eventual buyers accurately (depth bias +0.09) but inflate non-buyers' engagement (depth bias +0.40, d=0.38, p<0.001), halving expressed resistance (25.1% to 13.5%) and nearly doubling deliberation (21.9% to 40.1%). This "disengagement deficit" replicates across model families and resists mitigation (instructing simulators to disengage barely reduces the outcome-conditioned contrast: d=0.34, p=0.008).

Conclusion: Evaluating or training sales/persuasion agents against current simulators overstates funnel progress precisely where it matters most — for customers who would walk away. Decision fidelity is a critical missing dimension in user simulation evaluation.

Abstract: LLM-as-user-simulation has become core infrastructure for conversational AI: agent benchmarks (tau-bench), training pipelines, and a growing body of fidelity studies all rely on LLMs role-playing the human side of dialogue. Existing frameworks measure communicative fidelity -- whether simulators talk like humans -- against ground truth from paid participants role-playing assigned goals. We argue this has a structural blind spot: when the goal is assigned, the user's willingness is exogenous, so no framework can test whether simulators make decisions like real users whose motivation is endogenous, latent, and decaying. We introduce decision fidelity -- whether a simulated population reproduces the decision-state dynamics of real users facing real, consequential choices -- and measure it on a unique testbed: 2,790 production conversations between an LLM sales agent and real customers, including 793 with verified payment outcomes. Using a teacher-forced probe protocol that holds context and instrument fixed, we find a systematic, outcome-correlated failure we call the disengagement deficit: simulators reproduce eventual buyers almost exactly (depth bias +0.09) but inflate eventual non-buyers toward the purchase frame (depth bias +0.40; d=0.38, p<0.001), halving expressed resistance (25.1% to 13.5%) and nearly doubling deliberation (21.9% to 40.1%) while fabricating no purchases. The deficit replicates across model families (DeepSeek: d=0.41, p=0.002) and resists the obvious fix: instructing the simulator that it may disengage cuts marginal bias five-fold but barely moves the outcome-conditioned contrast (d=0.34, p=0.008). Real non-buyers say "not now" and stop; simulated non-buyers ask about price. Evaluating or training sales and persuasion agents against such simulators overstates funnel progress exactly where it matters most -- the customers who walk away.

</details>


### [467] [FairTutor: Equity-Aware Pedagogical LLM Routing for Budget-Constrained AI Tutoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20713)
*Qingyang Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FairTutor is an equity-aware AI tutoring framework that achieves 97.1% of premium tutoring quality while cutting costs by 71.6% through intelligent model routing and multi-agent orchestration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the emerging education inequity where students with access to premium AI services receive better explanations and guidance than those limited to free/low-cost AI tutoring.

Method: A multi-agent framework combining query analysis, pedagogical planning, low-cost model generation, evaluator-guided critique and revision, and selective escalation to premium models.

Result: FairTutor achieves 97.1% of premium pedagogical quality (floor-adjusted Likert scale) while reducing serving cost by 71.6%, with a tunable cost-quality Pareto frontier.

Conclusion: FairTutor enables equitable, cost-effective AI tutoring that can be tailored to diverse student populations, significantly narrowing the access-tier AIED Advantage Gap.

Abstract: Generative AI tutors provide real-time, personalized learning support, but also create a new education inequity: students with access to premium AI services may receive clearer explanations, more personalized guidance, and better scaffolding than students limited to free or low-cost services. To address this challenge, we propose FairTutor, an equity-aware model-routing framework that achieves cost-effective AI tutoring via pedagogically motivated multi-agent orchestration. FairTutor combines query analysis, pedagogical planning, low-cost model generation, evaluator-guided critique and revision, and selective escalation to premium AI models. We introduce access-tier AI Education (AIED) Advantage Gap to measure the quality difference between premium-access and budget-constrained tutoring, and TutorAccessEval, a benchmark spanning math, reading, writing, science, and language learning. Empirical evaluations show that FairTutor achieves 97.1% of premium pedagogical quality (in floor-adjusted Likert scale) while reducing serving cost by 71.6%. Sensitivity analysis reveals a tunable cost--quality Pareto frontier, enabling FairTutor to be tailored to the needs of diverse student populations.

</details>


### [468] [Escape from Delusional Echo Trap: Symmetry Breaking, Stochastic Dynamics and Mathematical Mitigation Strategies for Algorithmic Sycophancy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20718)
*Sayantari Ghosh,Saumik Bhattacharya,Partha Pratim Chakrabarti*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper develops a mathematical framework using dynamical systems and stochastic differential equations to model how AI sycophancy can trap users in self-reinforcing delusional belief states, and shows that sufficiently strong genuine evidence can reverse this effect.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and formally model the dangerous phenomenon where AI chatbots with sycophantic tendencies can systematically distort user beliefs into unshakeable delusions, potentially causing real-world harm.

Method: The authors model belief evolution as a continuous log-odds state variable coupled into a stochastic differential equation, navigating a multi-valley potential energy landscape. They analyze phase transitions in this landscape under sycophantic feedback.

Result: Sycophantic feedback beyond a critical threshold induces a structural phase transition in the belief potential landscape, creating deep, resilient attractor basins that trap users in rigid delusional states. However, sufficiently strong authentic external information can overcome the internal feedback barrier and restore objective beliefs.

Conclusion: The framework provides rigorous mathematical grounding for understanding AI-induced belief distortion and offers hope that genuine evidence can still correct sycophancy-driven delusions if presented with sufficient strength.

Abstract: We propose a rigorous and systematic mathematical framework for tracking the cognitive trajectories of a user, in the context of algorithmic sycophancy and AI-driven delusional spiraling. Using tools from dynamical systems theory and stochastic differential equations, we explore how individuals perceive, interpret, and update their beliefs as they interact with AI chatbots that possess hidden traits of sycophancy. We treat the evolving conviction as a continuous log-odds state variable, coupled into a stochastic differential equation, navigating a multi-valley potential energy landscape. Our analysis reveals several critical observations governing the stability and rigidity of belief dynamics. We demonstrate that the baseline prior perception of the individual is systematically enhanced by sycophantic feedback beyond a critical threshold. Here, the perceptual potential landscape undergoes a structural phase transition that severely deepens any incremental initial tilt present in the baseline state, transforming the landscape and giving rise to deep, highly resilient attractor basins that trap the individual in unshakeable, self-reinforcing, delusional convictions. Finally, we demonstrate that genuine external information can successfully challenge these rigid states. If this incoming evidence is strong and authentic enough to overcome the internal feedback barrier, it can correct the structural asymmetry caused by sycophancy, inducing a perception reversal that successfully restores the objective belief state.

</details>


### [469] [When Web Agents Finish but Still Fail: Reproducible Triggers and Trace Diagnostics for Parallel Web Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20724)
*Aagam Sogani,Botao Rui,Swetha Vaidyanathan,Rishi Agarwal,Minghao Yan,Shivaram Venkataraman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces Parallel WebBench (1,679 records), trains GRPO-based web agents under different data mixtures, and finds that while synthetic-data GRPO boosts completion from 50.7% to 96.0% and F1 from 0.2489 to 0.4529, binary accuracy remains low due to three persistent failure modes: context-bound search loops, premature termination, and synthesis collapse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon web agents often fail in ways hidden by final-answer evaluation — e.g., missing fields, over-inclusion, or stale evidence — motivating the need for finer-grained, trace-level analysis beyond simple completion metrics.

Method: The authors construct Parallel WebBench (1,679 records: 350 manual + 1,329 reconstructed with verified URL trajectories), train WebExplorer-style agents using GRPO under human-only, balanced human-synthetic, and synthetic-heavy data mixtures, and evaluate with element-wise F1 judged by GPT-4.1-mini alongside trace-level failure analysis.

Result: Best GRPO model achieves 96.0% completion (vs. 50.7% baseline) and 0.4529 element-wise F1 (vs. 0.2489), but binary accuracy remains far below completion, revealing three persistent failure modes.

Conclusion: Synthetic-data GRPO reduces abstention and improves partial correctness but leaves a completion-correctness gap that requires evidence-grounded coverage and synthesis diagnostics.

Abstract: Long-horizon web agents often fail in ways hidden by final-answer evaluation: they may visit useful pages, produce a well-formed answer, and terminate confidently while still missing fields, over-including unsupported items, or relying on stale evidence. We study these failures with Parallel WebBench, a parallel web-exploration benchmark containing 1,679 verified records: 350 manually curated parallel tasks and 1,329 reconstructed records with verified URL-based trajectories. We train WebExplorer-style agents with GRPO under human-only, balanced human-synthetic, and synthetic-heavy data mixtures. At 16k context and 16 interaction rounds, the best GRPO model improves completion over WebExplorer-8B from 50.7% to 96.0% and GPT-4.1-mini-judged element-wise F1 from 0.2489 to 0.4529, but binary accuracy remains far below completion. Trace-level analysis identifies three persistent failure modes: context-bound search loops, premature termination on partial answers, and synthesis collapse after relevant evidence has already been retrieved. These results show that synthetic-data GRPO reduces abstention and improves partial correctness, but leaves a completion-correctness gap that requires evidence-grounded coverage and synthesis diagnostics.

</details>


### [470] [Repeated Shared Access Enables Grokking, but Edit Propagation Depends on a Fine-Grained Addressable Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20737)
*Yanan Niu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper studies factual edit propagation in synthetic knowledge-graph QA, comparing four architectures (Dense, Loop, Dense+Mem, LMC). It finds that repeated shared access enables learning OOD compositions, but edit propagation success depends on whether the architecture provides fine-grained addressable memory—loop recurrence alone is insufficient.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To dissociate learning competence from editing affordance by investigating why some architectures can learn compositional facts but fail to propagate edits, revealing that repeated shared access enables grokking while edit propagation requires addressable memory.

Method: Four architectures (Dense, Loop, Dense+Mem, LMC) are compared on synthetic knowledge-graph QA. Factual edit propagation is measured via single-row value edits, using Mann-Whitney tests and HOLDANS interchange diagnostics to assess edit localization and propagation across substrates.

Result: Memory-bearing cells (LMC, Dense+Mem) show strong edit propagation (0.71-0.96) while non-memory cells show weak propagation (0.00-0.30); separation is statistically significant (p=0.008). Atomic facts localize to dominant memory sites. Learning OOD compositions requires repeated shared access, not a specific architecture.

Conclusion: Learning competence and editing affordance are dissociated: repeated shared access suffices for grokking, but edit propagation requires fine-grained addressable memory that the forward computation can write to and later reread—a capability loop recurrence provides only partially.

Abstract: We study factual edit propagation in a controlled synthetic knowledge-graph QA setting, comparing four architectures that cross loop recurrence with shared memory access: dense (Dense), looped (Loop), dense with shared memory (Dense+Mem), and looped with shared memory (LMC). Dense fits in-distribution 2-hop compositions but fails OOD; both looped recomputation and memory rereading cross this OOD grokking barrier, indicating that repeated shared access -- not a specific architecture -- is the common ingredient for learning. Editing, however, separates the substrates along a different axis. On a shared pre-edit-correct ID set, a single-row factual edit propagates strongly in the two memory-bearing cells (LMC 0.78-0.92, Dense+Mem 0.71-0.96) and only weakly in the others (Loop 0.04-0.30, Dense 0.00-0.03); the separation is statistically clean (Mann-Whitney p=0.008 between memory and non-memory cells, p=0.55 between the two memory cells, though n=5 vs n=5 is underpowered to rule out a moderate gap). In LMC, atomic facts localize to dominant memory sites that composition rereads, and a one-row value edit on LMC's own pre-edit-correct probes achieves 100% direct success with mean 0.989 intended propagation while moving unrelated facts ~0.1% of the time (matched specificity across substrates pending). A coarse hold-answer-subspace (HOLDANS) interchange diagnostic is consistent with this ordering, suggesting that what separates the substrates is when an edited fact is injected and how much computation remains afterwards to reuse it. These results dissociate learning competence from editing affordance: repeated shared access suffices to grok, but edit propagation depends on whether the substrate exposes a fine-grained addressable memory the forward computation can write to and later reread -- an affordance that loop recurrence provides only partially.

</details>


### [471] [Fara-1.5: Scalable Learning Environments for Computer Use Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20785)
*Ahmed Awadallah,Sahil Gupta,Yash Lara,Yadong Lu,Hussein Mozannar,Akshay Nambi,Zach Nussbaum,Yash Pandya,Aravind Rajeswaran,Corby Rosset,Alexey Taymanov,Luiz do Valle,Vibhav Vineet,Spencer Whitehead,Andrew Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FaraGen1.5 is a scalable data pipeline for generating computer use agent training data using environments, solvers, and verifiers. Using this data, the authors train Fara1.5 models (4B-27B parameters) that achieve state-of-the-art results on browser-use benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Collecting human demonstration data for computer use agents is expensive and slow, motivating the need for scalable synthetic data generation strategies.

Method: FaraGen1.5 pipeline has three components: (1) environments (live websites + synthetic environments for gated/irreversible actions), (2) solvers (harness supporting multiple models including GPT-5.4 + user simulator for multi-turn rollouts), and (3) verifiers (three complementary verifiers for correctness, efficiency, and critical-point adherence). Models (Fara1.5 at 4B/9B/27B on Qwen3.5) are trained via SFT with balanced data coverage, high-value tasks, and iterative deficiency targeting.

Result: Fara1.5-9B achieves 63.4% on Online-Mind2Web and 86.6% on WebVoyager. Fara1.5-27B achieves 72.3% on Online-Mind2Web, competitive with much larger proprietary systems.

Conclusion: Each Fara1.5 model sets a new state of the art for its size class on browser-use benchmarks, demonstrating that scalable synthetic data pipelines can effectively train competitive computer use agents.

Abstract: Collecting computer use data from human demonstrations is expensive and slow, motivating the need for scalable generation strategies. This requires two key ingredients: environments in which agents can act and verifiers that can judge whether their demonstrations succeeded. We introduce FaraGen1.5, a scalable data pipeline for computer use agents composed of three modular components: environments, solvers, and verifiers. FaraGen1.5 uses both live websites and synthetic environments that faithfully simulate domains gated by authentication or that require irreversible actions. It employs a solver harness that can be powered by multiple models, including strong frontier models such as GPT-5.4, and also incorporates a user simulator to enable multi-turn rollouts. Finally, FaraGen1.5 scores the resulting trajectories with three complementary verifiers covering task correctness, efficiency, and critical-point adherence. Using data produced by this pipeline, we train Fara1.5, a family of native computer use agents (CUAs) at three scales built on Qwen3.5 (4B, 9B, and 27B). To train these models, we employ a supervised finetuning (SFT) recipe that carefully balances data from FaraGen1.5 for broad coverage, specific high-value tasks, and target model deficiencies in an iterative approach. Each model sets a new state of the art for its size class on browser-use benchmarks: Fara1.5-9B reaches 63.4% on Online-Mind2Web and 86.6% on WebVoyager, while Fara1.5-27B achieves 72.3% on Online-Mind2Web, which is competitive with much larger proprietary systems.

</details>


### [472] [What Shapes Emergent Misalignment? Insights from Training Dynamics, Model Priors, and Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20814)
*Yuchen Zhang,Anietta Weckauff,Diego Garcia-Olano,Maksym Andriushchenko*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper investigates emergent misalignment (EM) in fine-tuned models, finding that in-domain training loss doesn't tightly constrain out-of-domain alignment, that pre-fine-tuning activations can predict post-fine-tuning alignment scores, and that activation shifts from training and evaluation prompts share moderate-to-high subspace overlap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how narrow fine-tuning leads to broad misalignment (EM) and what factors (training dynamics, model priors, data) drive this variability.

Method: (1) Analyzed in-domain loss vs. out-domain alignment; tested alternative learning schedules for minima inducing better alignment. (2) Compared misaligned vs. pre-trained model statistics; used prompt-only activations from pre-trained/instruct models to predict fine-grained alignment. (3) Computed activation deltas before/after fine-tuning; measured subspace overlap and similarity between training and evaluation prompt shifts.

Result: (1) No strong runs found with better broad alignment at similar/lower loss. (2) Misaligned models' scores are statistically different from pre-trained, with some positive correlation signals; pre-fine-tuning activations can predict alignment after fine-tuning. (3) Moderate-to-high subspace overlap between training/evaluation activation shifts; overlap correlates with shift similarity for last prompt-token activations.

Conclusion: Emergent misalignment is not simply explained by training loss alone; model priors (pre-fine-tuning activations) carry signals predictive of downstream alignment, and the geometry of activation shifts shows meaningful structure linking training and evaluation data.

Abstract: Emergent misalignment (EM) is a phenomenon in which models generalize with narrow fine-tuning, leading to broad (yet uneven) misalignment across evaluation questions. We study EM and its variability directly through the components of fine-tuning: training dynamics, model priors, and data. (1) We first explored how in-domain training loss relates to out-of-domain alignment scores across datasets and model families. Then, we tried to induce potential alternative local minima through different learning schedules for one narrow fine-tuning, but did not find strong runs with better broad alignment scores conditioned on similar or lower training loss. (2) We found that although the mean and standard deviations of the misaligned model scores are usually statistically different from those of the pre-trained model, there are some potential signals on overall positive correlation. The evaluation prompt-only activations from both the pre-trained and the original instruct models (prior to narrow fine-tuning) could predict fine-grained alignment scores after narrow fine-tuning. (3) Finally, we compared activation deltas before and after narrow fine-tuning and found moderate-to-high subspace overlap and similarity between the resulting activation shifts for training and evaluation prompts. Subspace overlaps between training and evaluation prompt activations correlate with their shifts' similarities when measuring with the last prompt-token activations. The train-evaluation data prompt overlap is controlled against overlap computed from random vectors and evaluation prompts activations.

</details>


### [473] [Study on Quantitative Dynamic Epistemic Logic for Belief Revision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20837)
*Felipe Nunes de Souza Camargo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces a Dynamic Epistemic Logic (P*) that extends AGM belief revision theory by incorporating modal logic with quantitative "degrees of conviction." The author critiques existing revision functions from van Ditmarsch (2005) and proposes a new function (*⁰) inspired by van Benthem (2007), which better captures the philosophical intuition behind AGM belief revision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between AGM belief revision postulates and Dynamic Epistemic Logic by providing a formalization that not only satisfies the postulates but also aligns with the philosophical intuition behind them.

Method: The author builds on Gärdenfors and Hansson's AGM postulates, extends van Ditmarsch's modal logic P (which captures quantitative degrees of conviction), introduces revision operators to form P*, proves theorems relating AGM postulates to P* revisions, and analyzes revisions against philosophical criteria. A new revision function (*⁰) is defined and implemented.

Result: The revision operators from van Ditmarsch (2005) fail to properly capture the philosophical intuition behind AGM belief revision. The newly defined function (*⁰), inspired by van Benthem (2007), successfully captures this intuition.

Conclusion: The AGM philosophical intuition is better formalized by the proposed function *⁰ rather than the existing functions in the literature, and an implementation of *⁰ is provided.

Abstract: Belief revision is a process in which an agent begins to believe in something she previously did not. I begin the paper by presenting, based on (Gärdenfors, 1998; Hansson, 1999), postulates for belief revision that constitute the basis of the AGM theory. I will then briefly show the semantics of a modal logic introduced in (van Ditmarsch, 2005), which I call `$P$'. This logic formalizes static epistemic states and has greater expressive power than AGM in doing so because it captures the quantitative notion of "degrees of conviction". The third step is to introduce revision operators on $P$ and, mostly following (van Ditmarsch, 2005), obtain the Dynamic Epistemic Logic (DEL) I call `$P*$'. It models processes of belief revision in several ways. Original results are presented in the following two sections. The first one of these sections revolves around a formalization of AGM postulates within $P*$ by proving some theorems related to the satisfaction of those postulates by revisions defined in $P*$. The last section features an analysis of $P*$'s revisions that go beyond the mere satisfaction of postulates. I compare their formal behavior with respect to some philosophical criteria. At last, I conclude that the functions presented in (van Ditmarsch, 2005) are not good formalizations of the philosophical intuition behind AGM. Instead, it is captured by the function $*^0$ originally defined in this paper (but highly inspired by (van Benthem, 2007)). An implementation of this function is also provided.

</details>


### [474] [Process-Reward Tactic Evolution for Long-Horizon Bioinformatics Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20839)
*Lingzhi Yang,Yubo Fan,Song Wu,Gilchan Park*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes Process-Reward Tactic Evolution, a Galaxy-based training framework that uses process verifiers and tactic libraries to improve LLM agents' ability to execute long-horizon bioinformatics workflows, evaluated on converted BioWorkflow Bench and BioAgent Bench tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents struggle with reliable bioinformatics work that requires long-horizon interaction with workflow software, typed data objects, provenance tracking, and biological validation checks.

Method: Process-Reward Tactic Evolution framework with: (1) Agent Gym for curriculum-organized Galaxy task training, (2) process verifiers scoring workflow construction, software interaction, execution, and biological correctness, (3) tactic library built from successful/failed traces, (4) inference using distilled tactics for held-out tasks.

Result: Evaluated on BioWorkflow Bench and BioAgent Bench tasks, comparing against no-memory and reflection-style baselines on workflow completion, biological correctness, and execution efficiency metrics.

Conclusion: Process-supervised tactic accumulation improves long-horizon bioinformatics workflow completion, biological correctness, and execution efficiency compared to baseline approaches.

Abstract: LLM agents can write code and call tools, but reliable bioinformatics work requires long-horizon interaction with workflow software, typed data objects, provenance, and biological checks. We study this setting through Galaxy workflow execution. The agent must explore task data, construct or adapt an executable workflow DAG, bind inputs and dataset collections, monitor execution, debug failures, and validate biological outputs. We propose Process-Reward Tactic Evolution, a Galaxy-based training framework that turns verified workflow rollouts into reusable \tactics. During training, agents practice on curriculum-organized Galaxy tasks in Agent Gym; process verifiers score workflow construction, software interaction, execution, and biological correctness; successful and failed traces are distilled into a tactic library. At inference, the trained executor, Process-Reward Tactic Evolution, uses this library to execute held-out peer reviewed Galaxy workflow converted BioWorkflow Bench and BioAgent Bench tasks in isolated environments. The paper evaluates whether process-supervised tactic accumulation improves long-horizon bioinformatics workflow completion, biological correctness, and execution efficiency over no-memory and reflection-style baselines.

</details>


### [475] [SignVLA: Real-Time Sign Language-Guided Robotic Manipulation via Attention LSTM and Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20857)
*Ningwei Bai,Xinyu Tan,Harry Gardner,Zhengyang Zhong,Liuhaichen Yang,Luoyu Zhang,Zhekai Duan,Monkgogi Galeitsiwe,Zezhi Tang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SignVLA is a real-time sign-language-guided framework that converts visual sign gestures into text commands for robot manipulation, using hand landmark features and an attention-enhanced LSTM network with temporal stabilization, enabling deaf, hard-of-hearing, and speech-impaired users to control robots via sign language.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA interfaces rely on speech or text input, limiting accessibility for deaf, hard-of-hearing, and speech-impaired users. The authors aim to bridge this gap by introducing sign-language-based interaction.

Method: The system extracts hand landmark features from video streams, uses an attention-enhanced LSTM network for temporal gesture recognition at alphabet and command levels, applies a temporal stabilization module for real-time consistency, and passes generated text instructions to a downstream VLA policy for robotic manipulation.

Result: Experimental results demonstrate stable real-time sign recognition and successful execution of manipulation tasks driven by sign-language inputs.

Conclusion: Lightweight temporal sign recognition can serve as an effective and practical accessibility layer for multimodal embodied intelligence.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models enable robots to execute manipulation tasks from natural-language instructions grounded in visual observations. However, existing VLA interfaces primarily rely on speech or text input, limiting accessibility for deaf, hard-of-hearing, and speech-impaired users. We present SignVLA, a real-time sign-language-guided VLA framework for accessible human-robot interaction. The system introduces a modular sign-to-text interface that converts visual sign gestures into semantic instructions compatible with downstream VLA policies. Given video streams, SignVLA extracts hand landmark features and employs an attention-enhanced Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network to capture temporal gesture dynamics for alphabet- and command-level sign recognition. A temporal stabilization module further improves prediction consistency in real-time interaction settings.The generated instruction sequence is then passed to a downstream VLA policy for sign-conditioned robotic manipulation. Experimental results demonstrate stable real-time sign recognition and successful execution of manipulation tasks driven by sign-language inputs. Our findings suggest that lightweight temporal sign recognition can serve as an effective and practical accessibility layer for multimodal embodied intelligence.

</details>


### [476] [When Do Intrinsic Rewards Work for Code Reasoning? A Comprehensive Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20881)
*Xiaolong Jin,Xuandong Zhao,Wenbo Guo,Xiangyu Zhang,Dawn Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper systematically studies intrinsic reward methods (RLIF) for code generation, finding they cause early gains but eventual collapse—models shorten outputs and lose reasoning—and offer no benefit as pre-training for RLVR.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RLVR relies on costly ground-truth supervision; intrinsic rewards (e.g., majority voting, confidence scores) work for math reasoning, but it's unknown if they transfer to code generation, which has structural complexity and execution-dependent verification.

Method: Systematic empirical study on LiveCodeBench, evaluating certainty-based RLIF approaches under various training scenarios and hyperparameters (sample size, temperature).

Result: Certainty-based RLIF yields early improvements but models collapse (shorter outputs, lost reasoning); collapse speed depends on sample size and temperature. RLIF pre-training does not improve RLVR training from scratch.

Conclusion: Intrinsic reward methods show promise for code but have clear limitations (collapse), and the paper provides actionable recommendations for future work on code reasoning models.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has driven substantial progress in large language model reasoning, but relies on ground-truth supervision that is costly or infeasible, especially in coding tasks. Recent work addresses this by deriving rewards from a model's own signals, such as majority voting or confidence-based scores, achieving notable success on mathematical reasoning benchmarks. However, code generation poses distinct challenges: programs are structurally complex, semantically equivalent solutions may differ syntactically, and verification typically requires execution. Whether these intrinsic reward methods transfer effectively to code remains unexplored. In this work, we present a systematic empirical study of intrinsic reward methods for code generation. We conduct extensive experiments on LiveCodeBench, systematically evaluating representative certainty-based Reinforcement Learning from Internal Feedback (RLIF) approaches under different training scenarios and hyperparameter settings. Our experiments reveal that certainty-based methods yield early gains but inevitably collapse: models progressively shorten outputs and lose reasoning capability, with collapse speed sensitive to sample size and temperature. When used to initialize RLVR training, RLIF pre-training offers no significant improvement over training from scratch. We also provide actionable recommendations for using intrinsic rewards for training code reasoning models. Our study shows both the promise and limitations of intrinsic reward methods for code, informing future work on code models and agents.

</details>


### [477] [Neurosymbolic Clinical Trial Matching via LLM-Driven Abduction and Logical Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20895)
*Baiyang Qu,Leonardo Ranaldi,Xi Wang,Marco Valentino*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A hybrid neurosymbolic framework (αNeSy-CTM) combining LLMs with logical verification improves Clinical Trial Matching, achieving up to 30% relative improvement over zero-shot baselines by leveraging abductive reasoning for handling noisy and incomplete clinical data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs struggle with deterministic verification for complex eligibility criteria in Clinical Trial Matching, while purely symbolic methods fail with incomplete patient records and noisy clinical evidence. A hybrid approach is needed to bridge this gap.

Method: Proposes αNeSy-CTM, an abductive neurosymbolic framework that combines LLMs (for linguistic/world knowledge) with logical verification to reason over noisy and underspecified clinical text for automated Clinical Trial Matching.

Result: αNeSy-CTM achieves up to 30% relative improvement over zero-shot LLM baselines; outperforms non-abductive neurosymbolic settings in accuracy, specificity, and robustness; and proves highly complementary with Chain-of-Thought reasoning.

Conclusion: Neurosymbolic methods show significant promise for automating CTM, paving the way for auditable, LLM-driven clinical applications with hybrid routing policies combining αNeSy-CTM and CoT reasoning.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a promising path to automate Clinical Trial Matching (CTM), but still struggle with the deterministic verification required for complex eligibility criteria. Conversely, purely symbolic methods provide formal rigour but break down when faced with incomplete patient records and noisy clinical evidence. To bridge this gap, we investigate a hybrid framework for CTM combining LLMs with logical verification. In particular, we introduce an abductive neurosymbolic CTM framework (αNeSy-CTM), which leverages the linguistic and world knowledge in LLMs to support reasoning over noisy and underspecified clinical text. Extensive evaluation demonstrates that αNeSy-CTM substantially outperforms standalone LLM baselines, achieving up to 30% relative improvement over zero-shot baselines. In addition, our analyses confirm the impact of abductive reasoning on CTM, with αNeSy-CTM exhibiting improved accuracy, specificity, and robustness over a non-abductive neurosymbolic setting. Furthermore, αNeSy-CTM and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning prove highly complementary, highlighting the potential for a hybrid routing policy. Ultimately, this paper demonstrates the impact of neurosymbolic methods for automating CTM, providing a path toward the next generation of auditable, LLM-driven clinical applications.

</details>


### [478] [Root Cause Analysis with Latent Confounders using Partial Ancestral Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20912)
*Henrique O. Caetano,Rafael Arone,Carlos Dias Maciel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PAG-RCA is a novel root cause analysis framework that models failures as parametric interventions over Partial Ancestral Graphs, handling latent variables and using partial identification when causal effects are unidentifiable, outperforming baselines on synthetic and real-world benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing data-driven RCA frameworks assume causal sufficiency and fail to account for unobserved latent variables prevalent in real-world environments, limiting their applicability.

Method: PAG-RCA models system failures as parametric interventions over Partial Ancestral Graphs (PAGs) and uses causal identification algorithms to quantify effects. When effects are identifiable, candidates are ranked by exact intervention effects; when structurally unidentifiable, partial identification is used to compute analytical causal bounds for scoring.

Result: Evaluations on synthetic data, microservice anomaly benchmarks, and power-grid cascading failures show PAG-RCA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art data-driven baselines.

Conclusion: By integrating latent variables and partial identification, PAG-RCA ensures robust RCA even under data scarcity and latent-variable scenarios, advancing reliable automated diagnostics in partially observable complex networks.

Abstract: Finding the source of failures, known as Root Cause Analysis (RCA), is essential for identifying the root causes of anomalies and maintaining the reliability of complex systems. While causal theory has advanced data-driven RCA, existing frameworks assume causal sufficiency, failing to account for the unobserved latent variables prevalent in real-world environments. To address this gap, we propose PAG-RCA. This framework models system failures as parametric interventions over Partial Ancestral Graphs (PAGs) to perform RCA in the presence of latent variables. We use standard causal identification algorithms to find the source of failures by quantifying causal effects over the PAG. When an effect is identifiable, candidate root causes are ranked based on their exact intervention effects. When effects are structurally unidentifiable, our framework (for the first time in the RCA literature) integrates partial identification to evaluate and score candidates using analytical causal bounds. By integrating latent variables and partial identification at once our framework ensures robust RCA even under data scarcity and latent-variable scenarios where traditional methods degrade. Evaluations on synthetic data, microservice anomaly benchmarks and power-grid cascading failures demonstrate that PAG-RCA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art data-driven baselines. By improving data-driven RCA performance under data scarcity, this methodology advances reliable automated diagnostics in partially observable complex networks.

</details>


### [479] [Power Systems Agent Benchmark: Executable Evaluation of AI Agents in Electric Power Engineering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20950)
*Sergei Trashchenkov*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces the Power Systems Agent Benchmark, an executable benchmark for evaluating tool-using AI agents in power engineering. It contains 41 task families across eight power engineering areas, uses held-out cases synthesized from private seeds to resist contamination, and includes a deterministic evaluator that checks operational constraints. Reference evaluations with three command-line agents show performance differences and also serve as quality control, catching a latent evaluator bug.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Electric power engineering lacks an executable benchmark for tool-using AI agents. Existing language-model use in this domain is dominated by retrieval and text QA, while agent-based approaches remain academic prototypes. The authors aim to fill this gap with a structured, deterministic evaluation framework.

Method: The benchmark provides structured tasks to agents, which return structured solutions. A deterministic evaluator recomputes engineering quantities, checks operational constraints, and returns a feasibility flag, normalized score, and explicit violations. It includes 41 task families across eight power engineering areas, each grounded in citable sources. Held-out cases are generated on demand from private seeds to prevent contamination.

Result: In reference evaluations with three command-line agents, the strongest model neared the compact tier's ceiling, while a smaller open model lagged. Public and held-out performance were broadly consistent. The evaluation also served as quality control: unanimous failures flagged defects, and it exposed a latent evaluator bug missed by self-consistency checks.

Conclusion: The Power Systems Agent Benchmark provides a reliable, inspectable, and contamination-resistant evaluation framework for power-engineering agents. Its deterministic evaluators can be upgraded to simulator-backed checks without changing the task interface, ensuring future extensibility.

Abstract: Executable evaluation -- checking the consequences of an agent's actions with a program rather than grading its prose -- has become a prominent way to assess tool-using AI agents in software settings. Electric power engineering has not yet had an analogous benchmark: language-model use is still dominated by retrieval and text question answering, while agents acting on power-system artifacts remain mostly academic prototypes. We introduce the Power Systems Agent Benchmark, an executable benchmark for power-engineering agents. An agent receives a structured task and returns a structured solution; a deterministic evaluator recomputes the engineering quantities, checks operational constraints, and returns a feasibility flag, a normalized score, and explicit violations.
  The benchmark contains 41 task families across eight areas of power engineering, from power flow and protection to stability, microgrids, reliability, power quality, and forecasting. Each task is grounded in a citable source, standard, or documented engineering formulation. To resist contamination, held-out cases are synthesized on demand by per-family generators from private seeds: the construction is inspectable, but the instances remain private.
