We implemented these rules in a software prototype and evaluated them using two openly released datasets: 48 workflows with known design flaws and 168 variants that preserve workflow logic but alter graph structure. Results show that our verifier reliably detects violations even when flawed designs are obscured through structural transformations such as splitting tasks between agents. Future works could combine our verification with community repositories of building blocks to compose safe agentic workflows.

</details>


### [508] [Counsel: A Meta-Evaluation Dataset for Agentic Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21627)
*Sashank Pisupati,Henry Broomfield,Eujeong Choi,Antonia Calvi,Charlie Wang,Roman Engeler,Max Bartolo,Patrick Lewis*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Counsel is the first public dataset of meta-evaluations for agentic tasks, providing process-level critiques from LLM judges on agent benchmarks (tau-bench and DA-Code) with human annotations achieving high inter-annotator agreement, enabling calibration and improvement of LLM-based evaluators for agentic systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human annotation of agentic trajectories is extremely time-consuming (taking hours per trajectory), creating a bottleneck for scaling evaluations and training data curation. While LLM-as-a-judge (LLMJ) approaches are widely used as alternatives, their soundness often goes unmeasured.

Method: The authors introduce Counsel, a dataset of process-level critiques from open-weight LLMJs on two agent benchmarks (tau-bench for customer support and DA-Code for coding agents), with human meta-evaluations labeling critiques as "spot on", "correct location but poor reasoning", or "should not have flagged".

Result: Achieved reliable inter-annotator agreement (Krippendorff's alpha 0.78). Strongest judge reached ~88% agreement on error location and ~65% on reasoning. More capable judge models and more reasoning effort improved human agreement.

Conclusion: Counsel enables rigorous study and improved alignment of LLM-based evaluators for agentic systems, is permissively licensed, and generated using open-weight models for broad community use.

Abstract: As agentic systems tackle increasingly complex multi-step tasks, evaluating their trajectories presents a major bottleneck - human annotation of a single trajectory on popular agentic benchmarks can take hours, making it difficult to scale evaluations for measuring performance or curating training data. This has driven widespread reliance on automated approaches such as LLM-as-a-judge (LLMJ) to critique agents at the process and outcome-levels at scale, however, the soundness of LLMJ critiques often goes unmeasured. Here, we introduce Counsel, the first public dataset of meta-evaluations for agentic tasks. Counsel consists of process-level critiques from open-weight LLMJs on two agent benchmarks: tau-bench (customer support agents) and DA-Code (coding agents), and human meta-evaluations of these critiques. Human annotators label critiques on each flagged error as "spot on", "correct location but poor reasoning", or "should not have flagged", achieving reliable inter-annotator agreement (Krippendorff's alpha of 0.78). The resulting dataset stratifies LLMJ critiques by human alignment across both error location within a trajectory and reasoning quality, serving as valuable data to calibrate, improve, or train LLMJs for agents. Comparing open-weight judges, we find that more capable judge models and more reasoning effort both enabled improved human agreement, with the strongest judge reaching ~88% agreement on location and ~65% on reasoning. Counsel is generated using open-weight models and is permissively licensed for broad community use, which we hope will enable rigorous study and improved alignment of LLM-based evaluators for agentic systems.

</details>


### [509] [ChainWorld: Composing Long-Horizon Desktop Workloads from Atomic OSWorld Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21654)
*Vincent Siu,Manasi Sharma,Dawn Song,Daniel Yue Zhang,Chenguang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces ChainWorld, a benchmark composing OSWorld tasks into long-horizon desktop workloads (347 chains of 2-4 tasks). Evaluating four computer use agents, max chain completion is only 31%. Multi-turn evaluation (tasks revealed sequentially) helps three models but both single-turn and multi-turn protocols remain challenging with different failure modes: single-turn struggles with artifact precision, multi-turn with session management problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current computer use agents are evaluated on atomic desktop tasks, but real desktop work requires sustaining state across multiple objectives. The authors identify and study this gap.

Method: Created ChainWorld by composing atomic OSWorld tasks into long-horizon workloads via directional compatibility search. Constructed 347 chains of length 2-4 tasks. Compared two evaluation protocols: single-turn (all tasks in one prompt) vs multi-turn (tasks revealed one at a time).

Result: Maximum chain completion across four agents is 31%. Multi-turn evaluation improves completion for three models but both protocols remain challenging. Different failure profiles emerge: single-turn failures focus on artifact precision, multi-turn failures reflect session management issues like fragmented progress and later turn disengagement.

Conclusion: Current computer use agents struggle with long-horizon desktop workloads regardless of evaluation protocol. The choice of protocol matters as it exposes different failure modes, suggesting that both atomic and sequential evaluation are needed for comprehensive assessment.

Abstract: Computer use agents are evaluated almost exclusively on atomic desktop tasks, but realistic desktop work requires sustaining state across multiple objectives. We study this gap with ChainWorld, which composes atomic OSWorld tasks into long horizon desktop workloads through directional compatibility search while preserving the source evaluators. The resulting workload contains 347 chains of length two to four and compares two renderings of the same task sequence. In single turn evaluation, all tasks are presented together in one prompt. In multi turn evaluation, tasks are revealed one at a time. Across four current computer use agents, maximum chain completion is 31%. Multi turn evaluation improves completion for three models, but both protocols remain challenging. The two protocols also expose different failure profiles. Single turn failures concentrate on artifact precision, while multi turn failures more often reflect session management problems such as fragmented progress and later turn disengagement.

</details>


### [510] [Hallucination as Context Drift: Synchronization Protocols for Multi-Agent LLM Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21666)
*Carson Rodrigues*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper identifies **context drift** (knowledge-state divergence between concurrent agents) as a source of hallucination in multi-agent LLM systems, proposes the **Context Divergence Score (CDS)** and **Shared State Verification Protocol (SSVP)** to mitigate it, and shows that naive full-broadcast synchronization can actually worsen hallucinations (34% increase) while SSVP reduces them modestly with 58% fewer API calls.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches attribute multi-agent hallucinations solely to model deficiencies, but the authors argue a significant class of failures stems from **context drift** — mismatched or stale shared world representations between agents during collaborative reasoning.

Method: The authors define the **Context Divergence Score (CDS)**, a lightweight scalar metric quantifying knowledge-state discrepancies across spatial, temporal, and task dimensions. They propose the **Shared State Verification Protocol (SSVP)**, which allows agents to periodically exchange compressed state summaries and flag high-divergence conditions before joint reasoning.

Result: In multi-agent travel (n=30/condition) and software project planning (n=10), naive full-broadcast synchronization increased hallucination rate by 34% (HR: 0.658 vs 0.492, p=0.0022, d=1.18), a **contamination effect**. SSVP achieved modest reduction (HR: 0.463, d=0.30), significantly lower than full-broadcast (p=0.0005, d=1.47), using 58% fewer API calls. The contamination effect was domain-specific, not replicating in software.

Conclusion: Hallucination mitigation in multi-agent LLM systems should be reframed as a **distributed systems problem**, and context synchronization is established as a first-class design primitive.

Abstract: Multi-agent LLM systems routinely produce hallucinated outputs that cannot be explained by model deficiencies alone. A significant class of these failures arises not from model incapacity but from context drift: the divergence of internal knowledge states between concurrent agents. When agents enter a collaborative task with mismatched or stale representations of shared world state, their joint reasoning produces contradictions that manifest as hallucination. We define the Context Divergence Score (CDS), a lightweight scalar metric quantifying knowledge-state discrepancy between agent pairs across spatial, temporal, and task dimensions, and propose the Shared State Verification Protocol (SSVP), which lets agents periodically exchange compressed state summaries and flag high-divergence conditions before joint reasoning. We evaluate SSVP across two domains (multi-agent travel and software project planning) using Claude Haiku. In controlled experiments (n=30 per condition, travel; n=10, software) across 8 scenarios, naive full-broadcast synchronization increases hallucination rate by 34% above the no-sync baseline (HR: 0.658 vs. 0.492, p=0.0022, d=1.18), a contamination effect from propagating erroneous agent states. SSVP avoids this failure mode while showing modest, consistent reduction (HR: 0.463, d=0.30) and achieves significantly lower hallucination than full-broadcast (p=0.0005, d=1.47) using 58% fewer API calls. The contamination effect does not replicate in the software domain, where all conditions converge to low HR (<0.2), confirming it is specific to tasks where one erroneous shared belief cascades across evaluation dimensions. Our results reframe hallucination mitigation as a distributed systems problem and establish context synchronization as a first-class primitive in multi-agent LLM design.

</details>


### [511] [Entropy Objectives in Markov Decision Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21726)
*S. Akshay,Raghav Goyal,Aditya Neeraje,Piyush Srivastava*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents a method for synthesizing control policies in Markov Decision Processes to maintain entropy-based concentration objectives, addressing the non-linear challenge through convex duality and invariant synthesis, with complexity hardness results and empirical evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To synthesize control policies that enforce concentration properties on state distributions of stochastic systems, formalized as entropy-based objectives in MDPs, which is a non-linear and computationally hard problem.

Method: Combines convex duality and invariant synthesis to verify and synthesize strategies for entropy objectives in MDPs; investigates the role of memory and randomization.

Result: Shows the problem is complexity-theoretically hard even in relaxed versions; presents a sound and conditionally relatively complete verification/synthesis method.

Conclusion: The proposed approach successfully addresses non-linear entropy objectives through convex duality and invariant synthesis, with empirical validation on benchmarks.

Abstract: We consider the problem of synthesizing control policies that enforce a concentration property on the state distributions of a stochastic system. We present a formalization of this problem in terms of synthesizing strategies for maintaining an entropy-based objective in Markov Decision Processes (MDPs). We first show that even relaxed versions of this problem are complexity-theoretically hard. We then present a sound and (conditionally) relatively complete method to verify and synthesize strategies for such entropy objectives. The main challenge is the non-linear nature of such objectives, and our approach addresses this by exploiting and combining ideas from convex duality and invariant synthesis. We also investigate the role of memory and randomization in ensuring entropy objectives. Finally, we implement our ideas to evaluate our approach empirically on a few illustrative benchmarks.

</details>


### [512] [Training the Orchestrator: A Supervised Approach to End-to-End PDDL Planning with LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21740)
*Rajesh Mangannavar,Zachary Coalson,Pranay Dugar,Prasad Tadepalli*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HALO trains a small, efficient orchestrator from verifier-certified refinement trajectories, using a QLoRA-tuned policy with hardcoded rules, to replace expensive frontier LLM calls in plan refinement, achieving comparable success rates at drastically reduced cost (45× cheaper than GPT-5-mini, 15× cheaper than Gemini-3-Flash) and 40-50% fewer LLM calls per episode.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Translating natural-language planning intent into verified plans requires bridging language goals with formal PDDL specifications. Existing agentic frameworks rely on prompted frontier LLMs as orchestrators inside a verifier-checked refinement loop, incurring high API costs at every step. The goal is to reduce orchestration cost while maintaining plan success rates.

Method: HALO pairs a small QLoRA-tuned policy with three hardcoded rules for trivially decidable selections, operating over an expanded 21-agent action space. Training uses refinement trajectories that an external verifier has certified as ending in valid plans across 11 PDDL domains, leveraging the verifier's strong supervision signal directly from (state, agent) decisions.

Result: HALO matches or exceeds GPT-5-mini on success rate; within 3 percentage points of Gemini-3-Flash; reduces orchestration cost by ~45× vs GPT-5-mini ($0.18 to $0.004 per task) and ~15× vs Gemini-3-Flash; cuts total LLM calls per episode by 40-50% across PlanBench, Natural Plan, and classical planning benchmarks.

Conclusion: Verifier-certified trajectories provide direct supervision for training efficient orchestrators, enabling cost-effective natural-language-to-PDDL planning without sacrificing success rates.

Abstract: Translating natural-language planning intent into verified plans is a longstanding challenge: people communicate goals in language, while classical planners require formal PDDL specifications. Recent agentic frameworks bridge this gap by orchestrating a pool of specialized repair agents inside a verifier-checked refinement loop, but the orchestrator at the centre is itself a prompted frontier LLM, paying a frontier-LLM API call at every refinement step. We present HALO (Hybrid Agent-Learned Orchestrator), which trains the orchestrator from refinement trajectories that an external verifier has certified as ending in valid plans, across 11 PDDL domains. HALO pairs a small QLoRA-tuned policy with three hardcoded rules for trivially decidable selections, and operates over an expanded 21-agent action space. Unlike approaches that prompt a frontier LLM at every step or learn an orchestrator from sparse end-of-episode rewards, our key observation is that the verifier already provides strong guidance: every accepted trajectory is a sequence of demonstrably correct (state, agent) decisions, directly usable as supervision. Across PlanBench, Natural Plan, and classical planning benchmarks, HALO matches or exceeds the GPT-5-mini prompted baseline on success rate, sits within three percentage points of the stronger Gemini-3-Flash prompted baseline, reduces orchestration cost by more than an order of magnitude (\$0.18 to \$0.004 per task against GPT-5-mini, roughly 45$\times$ cheaper; roughly 15$\times$ cheaper than Gemini-3-Flash), and cuts total LLM calls per episode by 40 to 50 percent.

</details>


### [513] [AgentCAT: Simulating Computerized Adaptive Testing via Multi-Agent Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21832)
*Weiyuan Zhou,Haiping Ma,Xiaoshan Yu,Changqian Wang,Shangshang Yang,Xingyi Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentCAT is an LLM-based multi-agent simulation system that overcomes the limitations of static offline data and isolated component optimization in Computerized Adaptive Testing by creating a high-fidelity dynamic testing environment with three specialized agents (examinee, selection, supervisor) for personalized education assessment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current CAT research is constrained by static offline data with partial labels, forcing researchers to degrade dynamic assessment into static sequence prediction. Additionally, existing work focuses on isolated components (e.g., selection or diagnosis) rather than the holistic CAT interaction process.

Method: AgentCAT consists of three modules: (1) Examinee agent - uses memory retrieval and Chain-of-Thought reasoning to simulate responses based on cognitive profiles; (2) Selection agent - employs coarse-to-fine bucketing and knowledge graph exploration for balancing local difficulty and global coverage; (3) Supervisor - applies dual-auditing and robust update mechanisms to ensure convergence and validity.

Result: Evaluated on two real-world datasets across three dimensions (macro-level ability convergence, micro-level interaction logic, data sparsity resilience). AgentCAT achieves effective ability estimation with a selection strategy that balances difficulty adaptation and instructional coherence, aligning with human pedagogical intuition.

Conclusion: AgentCAT successfully addresses the limitations of prior CAT approaches by providing a holistic, dynamic simulation framework that produces more realistic and pedagogically sound adaptive testing outcomes.

Abstract: Computerized Adaptive Testing (CAT), as a key technology for personalized education, aims to accurately assess examinee proficiency by retrieving exercises dynamically matching current ability estimates. However, existing CAT research is constrained by limitations of static offline data and isolated component optimization. Restricted by partial labels in offline logs, researchers degrade the dynamic assessment process into static sequence prediction. Current research focuses on isolated perspectives, e.g., selection or diagnosis, neglecting the overall CAT interaction process. To address this, we propose AgentCAT, a Large Language Model-based multi-agent simulation system, to construct a high-fidelity benchmarking environment for dynamic testing. This framework comprises three modules: (1) The examinee agent with memory retrieval and Chain-of-Thought reasoning simulates responses based on cognitive profiles; (2) The selection agent uses coarse-to-fine bucketing and knowledge graph exploration to balance local difficulty and global coverage; (3) The supervisor uses dual-auditing and robust update to ensure convergence and validity. To validate the framework, we evaluated on two real-world datasets across three dimensions: macro-level ability convergence, micro-level interaction logic, and data sparsity resilience. Results show AgentCAT achieves effective ability estimation, and its selection strategy balances difficulty adaptation and instructional coherence, aligning with human pedagogical intuition.

</details>


### [514] [Measuring What Persists: Conditioning Mechanisms and a Geometric Framework for AI Agent Identity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21843)
*Andrew Tanner*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A geometric framework using √JSD metric spaces and magnitude homology detects AI agent identity drift, revealing two conditioning mechanisms (identity-vacuum and safety-basin clusters). Drift experiments were confounded by padding artifacts; the framework's full diagnostic potential remains empirically unconfirmed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI agents in long-context applications gradually drift from their specified identity, and existing methods only detect this after qualitative degradation is already visible. A more principled, quantitative detection framework is needed.

Method: A geometric framework based on √JSD metric spaces and magnitude homology from enriched category theory, treating identity as non-geodesic structure and drift as relaxation toward the geodesic. Validated on a persistent AI agent with probe experiments and perturbation theory analysis.

Result: Two conditioning mechanisms identified: identity-vacuum cluster (specification fills a behavioral void) and safety-basin cluster (displaces post-training attractors). Identity specification creates 55 unique response patterns vs. 1 for the base model. Drift findings initially attributed to context length were actually repetitive-padding artifacts.

Conclusion: The magnitude homology framework's full diagnostic potential (anisotropic contraction, structural collapse detection) is theoretically grounded but empirically unconfirmed. Careful control of experimental artifacts (e.g., padding) is essential for valid drift measurement.

Abstract: AI agents in long-context applications drift from their specified identity. Current methods detect this only after qualitative degradation is visible. We present a geometric framework for measuring identity structure using $\sqrt{\mathrm{JSD}}$ metric spaces and magnitude homology from enriched category theory, where identity is non-geodesic structure and drift is its relaxation toward the geodesic.
  Validated on a persistent AI agent, the framework's strongest empirical finding is a two-mechanism conditioning structure: cross-condition distances reveal an identity-vacuum cluster where the identity specification fills a behavioral void, and a safety-basin cluster where it displaces from post-training attractors. An equilateral probe baseline confirms that the identity specification creates measurable behavioral richness (55 unique response patterns vs. 1 for the base model) at maximum probe separation. A first-order perturbation theory for equilateral configurations predicts magnitude changes from perimeter changes alone, with shape perturbations first-order cancelled by the $S_n$ symmetry; the formula is self-consistent at the observed perturbation amplitudes.
  A drift experiment measuring magnitude decrease under context pressure was subsequently found to reflect repetitive-padding artifacts rather than genuine context-length drift; diverse padding produces no measurable deformation through 150K tokens. The magnitude homology framework's full diagnostic promise -- detecting anisotropic contraction and structural collapse via homological simplification -- is architecturally grounded in the perturbation theory and selection rules but remains empirically unconfirmed.

</details>


### [515] [ForEx: A Formal Verification Framework for Explainable Reasoning in Logical Fallacy Detection and Annotation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21867)
*Pei-Cing Huang,Chienyu Liu,Chan Hsu,Ci-Siang Chen,Pei-Ju Lee,Yihuang Kang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ForEx is a framework that translates LLM-generated explanations for logical fallacy detection into Lean4 code to verify formal derivability, revealing a large gap between formal reasoning validity and label agreement with human annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluations of LLMs on logical fallacy detection focus only on predicted labels and do not check whether the models' reasoning actually supports those labels.

Method: ForEx translates LLM-generated explanations into Lean4 formal proofs, checks derivability under encoded premises, and introduces the LLM Argument Verification Matrix to separate label consistency from formal verification status.

Result: Over 90% of LLM outputs can be translated into formally verifiable reasoning chains, but agreement with human annotations is only around 20%.

Conclusion: There is a systematic gap between formal derivability and label agreement that prediction-based metrics cannot capture; ForEx enables machine-checkable analysis beyond label correctness.

Abstract: Current evaluations of Large Language Models (LLMs) on logical fallacy detection focus on predicted labels, but do not establish whether those labels are supported by the reasoning the models provide. We propose ForEx (Formal Verification for Explainable Reasoning), a framework that translates LLM-generated explanations into Lean4 and verifies whether the translated rationale is derivable under encoded premises, not the logical validity of the original natural language argument. To distinguish prediction outcomes from the formal status of the supporting reasoning, we introduce the LLM Argument Verification Matrix, which separates label consistency from formal verification status. Experiments on LOGIC-Climate show that over 90% of LLM outputs can be translated into formal reasoning chains that pass verification, while agreement with human annotations remains around 20%. These results expose a systematic gap between formal derivability and label agreement, a distinction invisible to prediction-based metrics. ForEx moves LLM evaluation beyond label correctness toward machine-checkable analysis of formalized reasoning chains.

</details>


### [516] [AgentRiskBOM: A Risk-Scoping Security Bill of Materials for Agentic AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21877)
*Srimonti Dutta,Akshata Kishore Moharir*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentRiskBOM is a new security Bill of Materials (BOM) artifact designed to capture runtime authority and risk information for agentic AI systems—covering autonomy, permissions, memory, credentials, and inter-agent communication—which existing BOMs (SBOM, AIBOM, MLBOM) fail to address, achieving 100% risk-category visibility vs. ≤20.9% for existing approaches, validated on 13 open-source agents and 52 risk scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing BOMs (SBOM, AIBOM, MLBOM) focus on dependencies, model metadata, and training provenance but leave an "agentic transparency gap"—capability opacity, meaning there is no structured account of what a deployed agent can access, remember, change, delegate, or prove afterward. This lack of visibility into runtime authority creates security risks before incidents occur.

Method: The paper introduces AgentRiskBOM as a JSON-schema artifact that adds fields for runtime authority (autonomy, tool permissions, memory, credential scope, approval gates, audit signals, inter-agent communication, external action capability) as an additive layer over existing BOM standards. It includes a reproducible corpus, risk scenarios from 14 categories, a scorer, diff detector (for detecting authority drift), control mapper, and reports. Evaluated on 13 open-source agents (coding, RAG, multi-agent) with 33 structured deployment mutations.

Result: AgentRiskBOM achieves: (1) native-equivalent score of 14 across 16 capability dimensions (vs. 1 for SBOM, 1.5 for AIBOM, 2 for MLBOM); (2) 100% risk-category visibility (vs. 10.5% SBOM-like, 20.9% AIBOM-like); (3) correct identification of change type for all 33 injected mutations via diff detector; (4) Spearman correlation of 0.73 between primary and penalty-based secondary scorers, indicating rank consistency but need for human calibration of thresholds.

Conclusion: Agentic AI security requires a machine-readable authority-and-risk artifact before incidents occur. AgentRiskBOM fills the critical transparency gap left by existing BOMs, enabling structured risk scoping, authority drift detection, and improved security posture for deployed AI agents.

Abstract: Agentic AI systems retrieve private context, invoke tools, write files, call external services, coordinate with other agents, and may act without human approval. Existing bill of materials artifacts improve transparency for dependencies, model metadata, and training provenance, but leave an agentic transparency gap: capability opacity, the absence of a structured account of what a deployed agent can access, remember, change, delegate, and prove afterward. This paper introduces AgentRiskBOM, a security BOM for risk-scoping tool-using AI agents. It is an additive layer over SBOM, AIBOM, and MLBOM artifacts, referencing them where authoritative while adding fields for runtime authority: autonomy, tool permissions, memory, credential scope, approval gates, audit signals, inter-agent communication, and external action capability. We implement AgentRiskBOM as a JSON-schema artifact with a reproducible corpus, risk scenarios, scorer, diff detector, control mapper, and reports. We evaluate AgentRiskBOM on 13 open-source agents spanning coding, RAG, and multi-agent archetypes, plus 52 risk scenarios across 14 categories. The schema validates all 13 corpus artifacts. Coverage analysis gives AgentRiskBOM a native-equivalent score of 14 across 16 capability dimensions, vs. 1 for SBOM, 1.5 for AIBOM and 2 for MLBOM. Across modeled risk categories, AgentRiskBOM exposes 100% risk-category visibility vs. 10.5% for SBOM-like and 20.9% for AIBOM-like views. To test agentic authority drift, we inject 33 structured deployment mutations; the diff detector identifies the correct change type for all mutations. A secondary penalty-based scorer yields a Spearman correlation of 0.73 with the primary scorer, supporting rank-level consistency while showing that thresholds require human calibration. The results show that agentic AI security needs a machine-readable authority-and-risk artifact before incidents occur.

</details>


### [517] [Learning the ARTS of Search for Automated Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21891)
*Gurusha Juneja,Arnav Kumar Jain,Deepak Nathani,William Yang Wang,Xin Eric Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ARTS uses a reasoning language model with test-time training to improve scientific discovery search by separating hypothesis quality from execution quality, outperforming prior methods while using smaller models at lower cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior methods conflate hypothesis merit with execution quality, and prune search logs as context grows, leading to suboptimal search decisions.

Method: ARTS uses a reasoning LM to inspect execution logs, diagnose failures (faulty implementation vs. bad hypothesis), and select next hypotheses. Test-time training instills search tree knowledge into model weights to address context length limitations.

Result: Outperforms leading algorithms by 15.3% relative improvement across 22 tasks. Qwen3-4B with test-time training matches Gemini-3 Pro and GPT o3-reasoning with 5x lower cost. On RL tasks, it rediscovered human-best solutions that heuristic methods prune away.

Conclusion: ARTS demonstrates that language model-based reasoning agents with test-time training can efficiently navigate scientific discovery spaces, outperforming heuristic methods and matching frontier models at lower cost.

Abstract: Scientific discovery can be formulated as an iterative search process over the space of hypotheses and experiments. Contemporary methods navigate this space using heuristics such as MCTS. These algorithms conflate the merit of a hypothesis with the quality of its experimental execution. A promising hypothesis with preliminary execution is therefore ranked below a modest hypothesis whose execution is refined. Moreover, prior methods prune the search logs as the search progresses because the accumulated history outgrows the context window. We propose Agentic Reasoning for Tree Search (ARTS), where we deploy a reasoning language model to navigate this space. The model inspects prior execution logs, diagnoses whether earlier failures arose from faulty implementations or bad hypotheses, and selects the hypothesis to build on next. To mitigate challenges with context length, ARTS uses test-time training to instill the knowledge of search tree in the model weights. Across 22 tasks from MLGym and MLEBench, we show that ARTS outperforms leading algorithms, with over 15.3% relative improvement in the normalized score. With test-time training we show that a Qwen3-4B agent can match performance with closed-source frontier models like Gemini-3 Pro and GPT o3-reasoning with upto 5x lower inference cost. We further observe that on partially observable RL tasks, the test-time trained Qwen3-4B scientist surpasses ARTS with the o3 scientist by rediscovering the human-best recurrent-memory solution that heuristic methods prune away.

</details>


### [518] [Holmes: Multimodal Agentic Diagnosis for Mixed-Language Mobile Crashes at Industrial Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21963)
*Jia Li,Wenyuan Ma,Ting Peng,Haibin Zheng,Yuetang Deng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Holmes is a multi-agent LLM system that automates root cause analysis of mobile crashes in ultra-large-scale industrial apps (like WeChat's 70M-line codebase) by synthesizing multimodal runtime signals (stack traces, logs, thread states, registers, assembly) without needing failure reproduction, achieving 87.6% accuracy in fault localization and reducing investigation time by 98% to ~77 seconds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diagnosing mobile crashes in ultra-large-scale industrial applications is extremely difficult due to massive code volume, mixed-language complexity, and inability to reproduce failures locally. Traditional static analysis lacks scalability, and existing LLM agents require reproducible environments unavailable in post-mortem scenarios.

Method: Holmes introduces a multi-agent system with a hierarchical Retrieve-Explore-Reason architecture. It synthesizes multimodal runtime signals (stack traces, logs, thread states) to reconstruct failure contexts. It leverages low-level artifacts (registers, assembly) to bridge the semantic gap between open-source business logic and closed-source system frameworks, dynamically compressing the search space using runtime clues to navigate 70-million-line codebases.

Result: Evaluated on real-world crashes from WeChat, Holmes achieves 87.6% accuracy in function-level fault localization and reduces average investigation time by over 98% (to approximately 77 seconds).

Conclusion: Holmes demonstrates effectiveness in transforming labor-intensive debugging into an efficient verification workflow for ultra-large-scale industrial mobile applications.

Abstract: Diagnosing mobile crashes in ultra-large-scale industrial applications is a formidable challenge due to the sheer volume of code, the complexity of mixed-language environments, and the inability to reproduce failures locally. Traditional static analysis struggles with scalability, while existing LLM-based agents often rely on reproducible environments unavailable in post-mortem scenarios. We present Holmes, a multi-agent system that automates root cause analysis by synthesizing multimodal runtime signals--stack traces, logs, and thread states--to reconstruct failure contexts without reproduction. Holmes introduces a hierarchical Retrieve-Explore-Reason architecture that leverages low-level artifacts (e.g., registers, assembly) to bridge the semantic gap between open-source business logic and closed-source system frameworks. By dynamically compressing the search space using runtime clues, Holmes precisely navigates 70-million-line codebases to identify non-local defects. Evaluated on real-world crashes from WeChat, Holmes achieves 87.6% accuracy in function-level fault localization and reduces average investigation time by over 98% (to ~77 seconds), demonstrating its effectiveness in transforming labor-intensive debugging into an efficient verification workflow.

</details>


### [519] [REBA: A Revealed Belief Automaton Framework for Online Planning in Continuous POMDPs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21971)
*Xiangwei Chen,Lingling Fang,Andreas Holzinger,Liming Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: REBA introduces an event-driven framework called Revealed Belief Automaton that enables online certification of revelation events to construct finite symbolic memory from continuous beliefs, supporting ω-regular policy synthesis in continuous POMDPs without predefined discrete abstractions, achieving +17% to +47.4% improvement over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for online planning in continuous POMDPs with ω-regular specifications suffer from lack of symbolic memory for long-horizon logical progress and difficulty certifying noisy online beliefs. Obtaining reliable symbolic states online from continuous observations remains a challenge.

Method: REBA uses information-theoretic gates for online revelation to dynamically analyze and establish belief abstraction from continuous belief space by discovering reliable anchors. It develops incremental topology adaptation over certified anchors to realize an online finite Belief Automaton, combined with ω-regular specification for formal parity policy synthesis guiding Monte Carlo Tree Search.

Result: Empirical evaluations in patrolling and navigation scenarios show REBA matches or exceeds all baselines, with primary metric gains of +17.0% to +47.4% over state-of-the-art approaches.

Conclusion: REBA provides a new way of thinking that advances research from global belief-space discretization to online certification of revelation events, enabling reliable discrete guidance for continuous POMDP planning without predefined abstractions, with error decomposition analysis to assess effectiveness.

Abstract: Online planning in continuous partially observable Markov decision processes (POMDPs) using $ω$-regular specifications requires handling continuous belief dynamics within the finite symbolic memory in order to track temporal progress. Existing methods based on either direct search in belief space or predefined discrete abstractions suffer from drawbacks, e.g., lack of symbolic memory for long-horizon logical progress or difficult to certify from noisy online beliefs. As such, obtaining reliable symbolic states online from continuous observations remains a challenge. To address this issue, we introduce the Revealed Belief Automaton (REBA), an event-driven framework that advances the research from global belief-space discretization to a fundamental new way of thinking, namely online certification of revelation events. Specifically, we propose an online revelation method that, through information-theoretic gates, can dynamically analyse and establish belief abstraction from the continuous belief space by discovering reliable anchors among noisy beliefs. We then develop an incremental topology adaptation mechanism over the certified anchors to realise the online finite Belief Automaton. By combining with the $ω$-regular specification, REBA is able to support formal parity policy synthesis without a predefined discrete abstraction, which in turn can guide the Monte Carlo Tree Search process to perform online search beyond its local horizon. In addition, we design an error decomposition analysis which can assess the effectiveness and reliability of this discrete guidance for the underlying continuous POMDP. Empirical evaluations in patrolling and navigation scenarios show that REBA matches or exceeds all evaluated baselines, with primary metric gains of +17.0\% to +47.4\% over state-of-the-art approaches.

</details>


### [520] [Human vs Machine Mathematical Difficulty on Project Euler: An Experimental Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21972)
*David Holmes,Johannes Schmitt*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper studies how AI system effort and success scale with human difficulty using Project Euler problems. It tests power-law and exponential decay models, finding that machine scaling is better than expected (b<1), and reports SOTA task-length horizons doubling roughly every 75 days.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test a conjecture by Timothy Gowers about whether AI systems scale worse than humans with problem difficulty, and to empirically characterize the scaling behavior of frontier AI models on mathematical reasoning tasks.

Method: Analyzed 3840 attempts across 50 Project Euler problems and 26 model configurations. Tested power-law relation between machine token cost and human solve time (t_machine = a * t_human^b), and exponential decay model for success probability (p_success = e^{c*t_human}). Used binning for data aggregation and logistic success curves to extract 50% task-length horizons (h_50).

Result: Found b<1 for 20 of 25 models with usable fits (contrary to Gowers' prediction). Exponential decay model had moderate support (median R²=0.92). Strongest configurations reached 2.5-4.3 hour horizons on fastest-five human baseline, with SOTA h_50 doubling every ~75 days.

Conclusion: The operationalization does not support the earlier prediction that machines scale worse than humans with difficulty; instead, scaling is favorable. Frontier AI capabilities on math problems are improving rapidly, with task-length horizons doubling approximately every 75 days.

Abstract: We study how the effort and success probability of frontier AI systems scale with human difficulty on problems from Project Euler, an online platform of computational mathematics problems. Our dataset, from the MathArena benchmark, consists of 3840 attempts across 50 problems and 26 model configurations, with problem difficulty measured by the site's public human solve times. Motivated by a proposal of Timothy Gowers, we test a power-law relation $t_{\text{machine}} = a \cdot t_{\text{human}}^b$ between generated-token cost per successful answer and human time, and find $b < 1$ for 20 of the 25 models with usable fits, including the strongest base models; this operationalization therefore does not support an earlier prediction that machines scale worse than humans with difficulty. We also investigate whether success probability on the tested problems can be modeled by a simple exponential decay $p_{\text{success}} = e^{c t_{\text{human}}}$, predicting a linear relation between $\log p_{\text{success}}$ and $t_{\text{human}}$. Using a binning approach for data aggregation we find moderate empirical support (median bin-level $R^2 = 0.92$ across the 22 best-covered configurations) for this model. Following METR, we also fit logistic success curves and extract 50\% task-length horizons $h_{50}$; the strongest configurations in our 20 April 2026 snapshot reach roughly $2.5$--$4.3$ hours on our fastest-five human baseline, with a log-linear fit through the state-of-the-art frontier giving a descriptive doubling time of about $75$~days for the SOTA $h_{50}$.

</details>


### [521] [IRumAI: Reinforcement Learning for Indian Rummy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21975)
*Vignesh Mohan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: IRumAI is the first RL agent for Indian Rummy, using PPO with meld-aware encoding and dual-branch CNN, achieving 53.9% win rate against search-based opponents while being 7,000x faster at inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Indian Rummy has a massive player base and complex hidden-information dynamics but has received no RL attention; existing search-based agents are tactically strong but slow at inference.

Method: Uses PPO with meld-aware observation encoding, deadwood-driven reward shaping, and a dual-branch convolutional architecture; trained with one-time behavior cloning warm-start on strong demonstration data, then RL-trained solely against weak heuristics.

Result: Defeats the entire baseline hierarchy including 53.9% win rate against the strongest search-based opponent unseen during RL training; requires only 0.33 ms per action (7,000x faster than state-of-the-art heuristic); ablations validate architectural choices; linear probing shows network implicitly models opponent's hidden hand.

Conclusion: RL can effectively beat search-based approaches in Indian Rummy while being dramatically faster, and the network learns to implicitly model opponents' hidden information from public interactions.

Abstract: Despite its massive player base and complex hidden-information dynamics, Indian Rummy has received no reinforcement learning attention. Existing agents rely on combinatorial search, which is tactically strong but slow at inference. We present IRumAI, the first RL agent for the domain. IRumAI integrates Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), meld-aware observation encoding, deadwood-driven reward shaping, and a dual-branch convolutional architecture. IRumAI is RL-trained solely against weak heuristics, after a one-time behaviour-cloning warm-start on stronger demonstration data. It generalises to defeat the entire baseline hierarchy, including a 53.9% win rate against the strongest search-based opponent unseen during RL training. Bypassing explicit search, IRumAI requires just 0.33 ms per action, which is over 7,000x faster than the state-of-the-art heuristic. Ablations validate our architectural choices, and linear probing reveals that the network implicitly models the opponent's hidden hand from public interactions.

</details>


### [522] [CFAgentBench: A Reproducible Environment and Benchmark for Autonomous Construction-Finance Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22000)
*Rishi Srivastava*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CFAgentBench is a benchmark for autonomous construction-finance agents operating across real software stacks, featuring 1,014 machine-gradeable tasks, 35 mock applications, and a money-movement guard principle. Results show that even the strongest agent (pass¹=0.67) collapses to pass⁵=0.38 under repeated temperature-0 decoding, indicating single-attempt accuracy overstates real competence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a reproducible, self-hostable benchmark for evaluating autonomous construction-finance agents that reflects real-world software environments and includes critical safety constraints like money-movement guards.

Method: Built on an executable environment with 35 mock applications across 9 archetypes, containing 1,014 tasks (711 public, 303 private). Tasks are graded by functional correctness with state diff, side-effect checks, and regex outputs. Money-movement guard prevents agents from executing financial transactions even if technically correct.

Result: Strongest open-weight agent achieved pass¹=0.67 but only pass⁵=0.38 under temperature-0 decoding, a 43% success rate drop. Significant per-domain heterogeneity observed.

Conclusion: Single-attempt accuracy overstates deployable construction-finance competence; repeated evaluation is necessary for realistic assessment. The benchmark reveals substantial gaps between current agent capabilities and production-ready performance.

Abstract: We introduce CFAgentBench, a reproducible, self-hostable environment and benchmark for autonomous construction-finance agents: a CFO/controller-class agent operating across the real software stack a US construction finance team runs - ERP, project management, email, documents, pay applications, payroll, certified payroll, lien waivers, and bank/treasury portals. It contains 1,014 machine-gradeable task specifications across 8 domains and 77 families, every family grounded in a real source; a self-validated subset of 40 tasks (54 with a project-management extension) is compiled into oracle-validated executable evaluators, the runnable suite reported here. Following WebArena, the benchmark runs on an executable environment rather than static traces: 35 mock applications (31 reconciled to one company book, plus 4 PM platforms) over 9 archetypes, each implementing a uniform self-hostable app contract, so every task is graded by functional correctness - a state diff plus forbidden-side-effect checks plus required-output regexes - with an LLM judge used only for reply quality, never as reward. A distinguishing principle is a money-movement guard: 278 instances embed a payment, payroll, e-signature, or e-filing step where the correct behavior is to stop and stage for human approval, and executing even the correct transaction fails the task. The public split (n=711) is sized for a 95% Wilson half-width of +/-4.1%; a private, contamination-protected split (n=303) is reserved for remote scoring. In a first three-model open-weight sweep (k=5), the strongest agent reaches pass^1 = 0.67 but only pass^5 = 0.38 - losing 43% of its successes when required to repeat them under temperature-0 decoding. The within-model pass^1 to pass^5 collapse and sharp per-domain heterogeneity are clear evidence that single-attempt accuracy overstates deployable construction-finance competence.

</details>


### [523] [Nous: A Predictive World Model for Long-Term Agent Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22030)
*Pranav Singh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Nous is a novel agent memory architecture that treats memory as predictive probability distributions rather than stored facts, achieving strong results on conversational memory benchmarks while requiring no external database.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current memory architectures store facts as fixed records (databases, vectors, or graphs), which fails to capture the inherently predictive and uncertain nature of memory. The authors propose that memory should be about predicting observations, not storing them.

Method: Nous maintains a predictive world model of categorical probability distributions ("dimensions") per entity-attribute pair. New observations are scored by surprise (negative log probability), distributions update via closed-form Bayesian posteriors, and the stored artifact is the belief shift (delta) rather than the fact itself. Forgetting occurs through natural entropy decay, and identity resolution uses mutual information between entity dimension sets.

Result: Evaluated on LoCoMo benchmark (10 conversations, 1,540 questions) with GPT-4o-mini backbone, Nous achieves F1 scores: 63.50 (single-hop), 55.32 (multi-hop), 58.57 (temporal), 62.50 (open-domain). It outperforms A-MEM in 3/4 categories and exceeds BeliefMem in all 4 categories.

Conclusion: Knowledge-as-prediction is a viable alternative to storage-based memory. Nous demonstrates that Bayesian belief updating with surprise-based scoring provides natural forgetting and strong performance without external vector databases. However, reproducibility issues in baselines and uncontrolled evaluation differences with concurrent work limit direct comparison.

Abstract: We present Nous, a novel agent memory architecture grounded in the principle that knowledge is prediction, not storage. Rather than persisting facts as database records, vector embeddings, or knowledge-graph triples, Nous maintains a predictive world model: a collection of categorical probability distributions, called dimensions, one per entity-attribute pair observed in conversation. Each incoming observation is scored by its information-theoretic surprise S = -log2 P(obs | D), and the distribution is updated via a closed-form Bayesian posterior. The primary stored artifact is the delta, a record of the shift from prior to posterior belief, rather than the fact itself. Forgetting emerges naturally as entropy decay toward the uniform distribution, and identity resolution is handled through mutual information between entity dimension sets. Evaluated on the LoCoMo long-term conversational memory benchmark across ten conversations (1,540 questions) using GPT-4o-mini as backbone, Nous achieves F1 of 63.50 (single-hop), 55.32 (multi-hop), 58.57 (temporal), and 62.50 (open-domain). Against A-MEM's self-reported GPT-4o-mini numbers, Nous shows substantial gains in three of four categories, though we note that independent citations of A-MEM's results disagree with each other on category assignment, a reproducibility issue we discuss openly rather than resolve unilaterally. We additionally compare against BeliefMem, a concurrently developed system built on the same core premise of belief-based rather than deterministic memory; on the same benchmark and backbone, Nous's self-reported numbers exceed BeliefMem's self-reported numbers on all four categories, though we flag several uncontrolled differences between the two evaluation pipelines that prevent this from being a fully controlled comparison. Nous requires no external vector database or graph engine.

</details>


### [524] [Attractor Domain Theory: A Mathematical Framework for Cardiovascular Attractor Analysis with Wearable Photoplethysmography (PPG) Validation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22039)
*Timothy Oladunni,Farouk Ganiyu Adewumi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Attractor Domain Theory (ADT), which proves that reconstructed cardiac attractor information partitions into three necessary and sufficient domains (Geometry, Ergodic, Variational). Applied to 176,742 PPG segments, the Geometry Domain achieves strong artifact rejection performance (AUC=0.757, NPV=0.966) after correcting evaluation artifacts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite three decades of nonlinear cardiac dynamics research using attractor reconstruction (Lyapunov exponents, recurrence statistics, sample entropy), there is no principled theoretical framework explaining which attractor properties capture which cardiovascular quantities or why, leaving feature selection as a blind search problem and making negative results uninterpretable.

Method: The paper proves Attractor Domain Theory (ADT), which partitions reconstructed attractor information into three mutually non-redundant domains: Geometry Domain G (delay embedding; artifact rejection), Ergodic Domain S (asymptotic statistical invariants; stability estimation), and Variational Domain V (finite-time Lyapunov exponent field; hemodynamic inference). A Domain Sufficiency Theorem establishes these three domains as necessary and sufficient. Validation uses SCSI framework on 176,742 PPG segments across four datasets.

Result: Geometry Domain validation achieved AUC = 0.757 [0.686-0.828] and NPV = 0.966 after correcting three systematic evaluation artifacts (+0.179 net inflation). Ablation studies identified C_NL as the dominant Geometry Domain component (Delta AUC = -0.413) and revealed intra-domain redundancy across five components.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that ADT provides a principled theoretical foundation for understanding which attractor properties correspond to which cardiovascular functions, eliminating the need for blind feature search in nonlinear cardiac dynamics analysis.

Abstract: The cardiovascular system evolves along a bounded trajectory in physiological state space that converges to a compact geometric object: the cardiac attractor. A wearable photoplethysmograph (PPG) or electrocardiograph (ECG) observes a one-dimensional projection of this attractor; by Takens' embedding theorem, delay coordinates reconstruct its full geometry. Three decades of nonlinear cardiac dynamics have extracted Lyapunov exponents, recurrence statistics, and sample entropy from reconstructed attractors, yet no principled account exists of which attractor properties capture which cardiovascular quantities, or why, leaving feature selection as a search problem and negative results uninterpretable. We introduce Attractor Domain Theory (ADT), which proves that the reconstructed attractor's information partitions into three mutually non-redundant domains: the Geometry Domain G (delay embedding; native capability: artifact rejection), the Ergodic Domain S (asymptotic statistical invariants; native capability: stability estimation), and the Variational Domain V (finite-time Lyapunov exponent field; native capability: hemodynamic inference). We prove a Domain Sufficiency Theorem (the Parseval analog for attractor information) and establish that three domains are necessary and sufficient. Geometry Domain validation via the SCSI framework across 176,742 PPG segments from four datasets yields AUC = 0.757 [0.686-0.828] and NPV = 0.966 after correcting three systematic evaluation artifacts (+0.179 net inflation). Ablation confirms C_NL as the dominant Geometry Domain component (Delta AUC = -0.413) and intra-domain redundancy across five components.

</details>


### [525] [When Does a Video-Language Model Stop Watching? Reward Strength Controls the Formation and Reversal of Visual Shortcuts in Multimodal RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22043)
*Zekun Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper studies how visual shortcuts (models ignoring video and exploiting linguistic priors) form during RLVR training of LVLMs, revealing they emerge abruptly, follow a hysteresis-like asymmetry between formation and reversal, and have a critical intervention window where early regularization is far more effective than late application.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the formation, reversibility, and optimal intervention timing of visual shortcut collapse in RLVR-trained LVLMs, since previous work only documented existence but not dynamics.

Method: Treats grounding penalty strength (lambda) as a control knob and systematically characterizes shortcut formation-reversal dynamics along the training time axis using an out-of-distribution diagnostic set.

Result: (i) Sharp onset: shortcuts emerge abruptly; (ii) Monotone dose-response: increasing lambda suppresses shortcuts with hysteresis-like asymmetry; (iii) Critical intervention window: pre-onset penalty arrests formation, post-consolidation penalty is less effective.

Conclusion: Visual-shortcut collapse is a controllable, time-dependent, and asymmetric process, not a binary defect, with direct implications for when and how strongly to regularize multimodal RLVR.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) is increasingly applied to large vision-language models (LVLMs), yet outcome-only optimization can drive a model to stop attending to the video and instead exploit linguistic priors -- a failure we call a visual shortcut. While the existence of such perception bypass is by now documented, how it forms, whether it can be undone, and when intervention still helps remain open. We treat the strength of a grounding penalty, lambda, as a control knob and characterize the formation-reversal dynamics of visual shortcuts along the training time axis. On a held-out, out-of-distribution diagnostic set, we find: (i) a sharp onset -- shortcut reliance emerges abruptly over a narrow window of optimization steps and is robust across random seeds; (ii) a monotone dose-response -- increasing lambda progressively suppresses the shortcut, and at an intermediate dose the trajectory first forms and then reverses the shortcut, exposing a hysteresis-like asymmetry between acquiring and removing it; and (iii) a critical intervention window -- applying the penalty before onset arrests shortcut formation, whereas the same penalty applied after consolidation is markedly less effective. Together these results recast visual-shortcut collapse not as a binary defect but as a controllable, time-dependent, and asymmetric process, with direct implications for when and how strongly to regularize multimodal RLVR.

</details>


### [526] [Can Reasoning Models Detect Changes to their Chains of Thought?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22085)
*Sathvik Napa,Utkarsh Singh,Chengyuan Xue,Miriam Wanner,William Walden*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether reasoning models can detect tampering with their chain-of-thought (CoT) outputs, finding that models show only modest detection accuracy, struggle to identify how their CoT was modified, and perform similarly regardless of whether the CoT is their own or another model's.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Editing a model's chain-of-thought (CoT) can be useful for prefilling with stronger reasoning or removing unsafe steps, but success depends on whether the model can detect such tampering and alter its behavior accordingly.

Method: The authors systematically test reasoning models' ability to detect CoT interventions under various conditions: during vs. after reasoning, and when prefilled with their own CoTs vs. those of other models.

Result: (i) Models show only very modest detection accuracy; (ii) Models struggle to identify how their CoT was modified; (iii) Models are about as good at detecting changes to their own CoTs as to those of other models.

Conclusion: Current reasoning models have limited ability to detect CoT interventions, suggesting that such editing techniques may be viable without models altering their behavior due to suspicion of tampering.

Abstract: There are many reasons one may want to edit a model's chain of thought (CoT) -- e.g., to prefill it with reasoning from a stronger model or to remove steps that may yield unsafe outputs. The success of these interventions plausibly depends on a model's inability to notice them, as the model may alter its behavior if it suspects tampering. In this work, we study whether recent reasoning models are able to detect such interventions on their CoTs under a variety of conditions: both during reasoning and after it, and when prefilled both with their own CoTs and with those of other models. Broadly, we find that (i) models exhibit only very modest detection accuracy; (ii) models struggle to identify *how* their CoT was modified; and (iii) models are about as good at detecting changes to their own CoTs as to those of other models.

</details>


### [527] [Geometry-Aware Online Scheduling for LLM Serving: From Theoretical Bound to System Practice](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22327)
*Li Kong,Qi Qi,Yinyu Ye,Zijie Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces geometry-aware online scheduling for LLM inference, proposing the Smallest Volume First (SVF) algorithm and its 1-bit variant. It significantly tightens the competitive ratio from CR ≤ 48 to CR ≤ 5, provides a comprehensive theoretical taxonomy, and demonstrates strong latency/throughput gains when integrated into vLLM with Llama-3.1 models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing time-centric scheduling heuristics (e.g., Shortest Job First) fail to capture the 2D spatio-temporal geometric growth specific to LLM inference's KV-cache memory dynamics, leading to suboptimal performance in memory-constrained serving.

Method: Proposes Smallest Volume First (SVF) and 1-bit SVF algorithms based on geometry-aware scheduling. Uses a novel proof methodology to tighten worst-case competitive ratio. Implements as a plug-and-play layer in vLLM.

Result: Tightens competitive ratio from CR ≤ 48 to CR ≤ 5 for SVF with known output lengths. SVF achieves strong reductions in average and tail latency; 1-bit SVF achieves competitive throughput and latency with only a single bit of information.

Conclusion: Establishes a theoretically sound and empirically proven approach for memory-constrained scheduling in LLM deployments, with open-source code available.

Abstract: The explosive demand for interactive Large Language Model serving has highlighted the management of the Key-Value cache's dynamic memory footprint as a critical area for performance optimization in inference engines. Modern inference systems overwhelmingly rely on time-centric scheduling heuristics, such as Shortest Job First. However, their theoretical optimality is rooted in traditional schedule modeling, failing to capture the highly dynamic, 2D spatio-temporal geometric growth specific to LLM inference mechanisms. To resolve this, we propose the geometry-aware online scheduling by introducing the Smallest Volume First (SVF) algorithm and its highly efficient variant, 1-bit SVF. Theoretically, we provide a rigorous mathematical foundation for our approach. Utilizing a novel proof methodology, we tighten the worst-case competitive ratio ($\text{CR} \le 48 \rightarrow \text{CR} \le 5$) for SVF with known output lengths. Building upon this core breakthrough, we complete a comprehensive theoretical taxonomy analyzing our algorithms across different traffic scenarios and information availability. Practically, we seamlessly integrate our approach as a plug-and-play layer in vLLM. Extensive evaluations on Llama-3.1 models demonstrate comprehensive performance gains: SVF delivers strong reductions in both average and tail latency, while 1-bit SVF, with merely a single bit information, achieves competitive throughput and latency. This work establishes a theoretically sound and empirically proven approach for resolving memory-constrained scheduling in modern LLM deployments. To facilitate future research, our code is available at https://github.com/Aurora-Kl/Geometry-Aware-Online-Scheduling.git.

</details>


### [528] [Hypothesis-Driven Skill Optimization for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22330)
*Fangxin Shang,Yehui Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HDSO is a train-free framework using frozen LLM inference endpoints to curate, validate, and approve external skills for action-oriented agents, achieving gains of +6.9% on ALFWorld for Qwen3-8B and remaining robust under noisy feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: External skills can improve action-oriented LLM agents without weight changes, but persistent updates from sparse/noisy trajectories risk encoding spurious shortcuts or unexecutable rules.

Method: A frozen curator proposes falsifiable hypotheses with validation plans, instantiates candidate skill packages, runs paired control/treatment executions, reviews behavior differences, and consolidates only supported skills. The executor uses progressive disclosure, preserving the executor-only baseline path when no skill is selected.

Result: On ALFWorld, HDSO improves executor-only baselines by +6.9 Avg. SR for Qwen3-8B and +4.0 for Qwen3.6-27B. Under 20% flipped feedback, it preserves a +7.1-point gain. Validated repositories transfer across runs but cross-model curation succeeds only when curator diagnosis, executor capability, and validation evidence align.

Conclusion: HDSO provides an auditable, safe skill lifecycle for frozen action agents, replacing unconstrained memory accumulation with falsifiable hypothesis testing and progressive disclosure.

Abstract: External skills can improve action-oriented LLM agents without changing model weights, but persistent skill updates are risky when they are distilled from sparse or noisy trajectories. A plausible reflection may encode a useful procedure, a spurious shortcut, or a rule that the target executor cannot reliably follow. We propose Hypothesis-Driven Skill Optimization (HDSO), a train-free framework in which both the skill curator and the agent executor are frozen inference endpoints. The curator observes executor traces, proposes a falsifiable hypothesis with an explicit validation plan, instantiates the hypothesis as a candidate skill package, validates the package through paired control/treatment executions, reviews behavior differences, and consolidates only supported candidates into an approved repository. The executor consumes approved skills through progressive disclosure, preserving the executor-only path when no skill is selected. On ALFWorld, HDSO improves executor-only baselines by +6.9 Avg. SR points for Qwen3-8B and +4.0 points for Qwen3.6-27B. Under 20% randomly flipped success/failure feedback during skill discovery and validation, HDSO preserves a +7.1-point gain for Qwen3-8B. Transfer and heterogeneous-pair diagnostics further show that validated repositories can be useful beyond the run that produced them, but cross-model curation succeeds only when curator diagnosis, executor capability, and validation evidence align. HDSO provides an auditable skill lifecycle for frozen action agents rather than an unconstrained memory accumulation procedure.

</details>


### [529] [Reference-Free Assessment of Physical Consistency in World Model-based Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22363)
*Yun Oh,Sukmin Yun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces reference-free evaluation metrics for video generation physical consistency, using DROID-SLAM and SEA-RAFT to quantify inconsistencies, achieving >8% improvement in task success rates when filtering videos and enabling spatio-temporal artifact localization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Physical fidelity gaps in video generation (e.g., WorldGym, WorldEval) prevent accurate simulation of real-world task success rates for VLA models. Existing methods like Elo are costly and FVD requires unavailable ground-truth references.

Method: Combines relative and absolute reference-free approaches using DROID-SLAM and SEA-RAFT to quantify physical inconsistencies, motivated by WorldScore.

Result: Videos filtered with relative consistency assessment improve task success rates by over 8%, narrowing the simulation-to-reality gap.

Conclusion: The proposed method enables both relative filtering for improved task success and absolute assessment for spatio-temporal localization of physical artifacts.

Abstract: We introduce reference-free measures for evaluating the physical consistency of generated videos, combining relative and absolute approaches to assess fidelity. Although tools like WorldGym or WorldEval enable robotic simulation via video generation, physical fidelity gaps often prevent these environments from accurately reproducing real-world task success rates of VLA models. Unlike existing evaluation methods, which require costly human voting (Elo) or unavailable ground-truth references (FVD), our approach utilizes DROID-SLAM and SEA-RAFT to quantify physical inconsistencies, motivated by WorldScore. Videos filtered using our relative consistency assessment show an improvement in task success rates of over 8%, effectively narrowing the simulation-to-reality gap. Furthermore, our absolute assessment enables spatio-temporal localization, providing visualization of when and where physical artifacts occur.

</details>


### [530] [ARIA: A Causal-Aware Framework for Rescuing LLM Reasoning in Trustworthy Materials Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22375)
*Yi Cao,Liaoyaqi Wang,Jieneng Chen,Benjamin Van Durme,Alan Yuille,Paulette Clancy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ARIA is a causal-aware AI framework for materials discovery that mitigates "contextual tunneling" (over-anchoring on narrow evidence) by routing queries through a three-tier cascade: direct causal reasoning, physics-informed analogical transfer, and parametric fallback, outperforming baselines on 2D materials tasks while producing auditable causal traces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative models for materials discovery often fail to satisfy underlying physical causality. LLMs augmented with knowledge graphs exhibit "contextual tunneling," where they over-anchor on narrow retrieved evidence and suppress global physical reasoning.

Method: ARIA uses a three-tier cascade: (i) direct causal reasoning when complete Process-Structure-Property (PSP) chains exist, (ii) physics-informed analogical transfer for sparse/novel systems, and (iii) explicit parametric fallback when evidence is incomplete. A KG with 2,839 PSP relations was constructed.

Result: ARIA mitigates contextual tunneling, outperforms unaugmented and naive KG-augmented baselines on forward prediction and inverse design for 2D materials, with further gains from online literature enrichment.

Conclusion: ARIA produces auditable causal traces, enabling physically grounded and trustworthy AI-assisted materials discovery.

Abstract: Generative models have revolutionized the process of materials discovery, yet they often fail to satisfy underlying physical causality. Through an analysis of Large Language Models (LLMs) augmented with knowledge graphs derived from current literature, we uncover a phenomenon termed contextual tunneling, where models "over-anchor" on narrow, retrieved evidence while suppressing global physical reasoning. To address this problem, we introduce ARIA, a causal-aware framework that conditions knowledge use on mechanistic completeness. ARIA routes each query through a three-tier cascade: (i) direct causal reasoning when complete evidence chains of Process-Structure-Property (PSP) are available, (ii) physics-informed analogical transfer for sparse or novel material systems, and (iii) explicit parametric fallback when external evidence is incomplete. As a proof of concept, we construct a Knowledge Graph (KG) containing 2,839 extracted PSP relations from peer-reviewed articles in the materials literature and evaluate ARIA on forward prediction and inverse design tasks for two-dimensional (2D) materials. ARIA mitigates contextual tunneling, improves over unaugmented and naive KG-augmented baselines, and provides further gains when an online literature search is used for evidence enrichment. Crucially, ARIA produces auditable causal traces, enabling physically grounded and trustworthy AI-assisted materials discovery.

</details>


### [531] [MetaPS: Adaptive Programmatic Strategy Selection for Market Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22385)
*Jiaxiang Chen,Aotian Luo,Zhouyi Zheng,Weiyi Huang,Chi Zhang,Zenglin Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MetaPS uses simulations to train LLMs to select from pre-coded programmatic trading strategies, outperforming fixed strategies and direct decision-making agents across model scales from 0.8B to 9B parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: No single market strategy consistently wins across all conditions, so the authors aim to create an adaptive system that can dynamically select the best strategy based on current market state, rather than relying on LLMs to directly generate market actions.

Method: MetaPS rolls out candidate strategies in simulated/backtested markets, identifies states where certain strategies lead to better outcomes, converts these state-strategy pairs into supervised fine-tuning data, and trains LLMs to select the appropriate strategy program during inference without further simulation.

Result: MetaPS consistently improves across model scales (0.8B-9B), outperforms fixed-strategy baselines, direct decision-making agents, and prompted API-based LLM agents. Compact fine-tuned models sometimes surpass stronger API models.

Conclusion: Market simulations can provide scalable and targeted supervision for learning adaptive, interpretable, and executable strategy selection.

Abstract: No single market strategy always wins: momentum, mean reversion, risk control,and event-driven rules can each succeed or fail as market conditions change.Rather than asking large language models to directly generate market actions,we study an executable decision paradigm where an agent selects from a library of programmatic strategies, each implemented as a code module mapping market observations to actions.We propose \textbf{MetaPS}, a simulation-guided framework for adaptive programmatic strategy selection. MetaPS rolls out candidate strategies in simulated or backtested markets, identifies states where particular strategies lead to better future outcomes, and converts these state--strategy pairs into supervised fine-tuning data. During inference, the simulator is no longer queried: MetaPS observes only the current market state and candidate strategy context, selects a suitable strategy program, and the selected program produces the final action. Experiments on multi-stock trading and a controlled goods-exchange sandbox show that MetaPS consistently improves across model scales from 0.8B to 9B parameters. It outperforms fixed-strategy baselines, direct decision-making agents, and prompted API-based LLM agents; in several settings, compact fine-tuned models even surpass stronger API models. These results demonstrate that market simulations can provide scalable and targeted supervision for learning adaptive, interpretable, and executable strategy selection.

</details>


### [532] [PlanBench-XL: Evaluating Long-Horizon Planning of LLM Tool-Use Agents in Large-Scale Tool Ecosystems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22388)
*Jiayu Liu,Qihan Lin,Cheng Qian,Rui Wang,Emre Can Acikgoz,Xiaocheng Yang,Jiateng Liu,Zhenhailong Wang,Xiusi Chen,Heng Ji,Dilek Hakkani-Tür*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PlanBench-XL is a new benchmark with 327 retail tasks over 1,665 tools that evaluates LLM agents' ability to iteratively retrieve and use tools under varying blocking conditions. GPT-5.4 achieves 51.90% accuracy without blocks but collapses to 11.36% under severe blocking, revealing that adaptive planning in large tool environments remains a major challenge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks rarely evaluate planning under retrieval-limited tool visibility, despite LLM agents increasingly operating in large, dynamic tool ecosystems where discovering relevant tools and adapting to failures is essential.

Method: PlanBench-XL includes 327 retail tasks over 1,665 tools, requiring agents to iteratively retrieve tools, invoke them for intermediate evidence, and adapt. It features an optional blocking mechanism simulating real-world unpredictability (missing, failing, or distracting functions).

Result: GPT-5.4 achieves 51.90% accuracy without blocks but drops to 11.36% under most severe blocking. Agents struggle especially when failures lack explicit error signals or recovery requires longer alternative tool-use paths.

Conclusion: PlanBench-XL serves as a testbed for diagnosing agentic planning failures, highlighting the need for robust adaptive planning in long-horizon tasks with large, imperfect tool environments.

Abstract: LLM agents increasingly operate in large tool ecosystems, where real-world tasks require discovering relevant tools, inferring implicit sub-goals, and adapting to dynamic environments over long horizons. However, existing benchmarks rarely evaluate planning under retrieval-limited tool visibility. To address this gap, we introduce PlanBench-XL, an interactive benchmark of 327 retail tasks over 1,665 tools that tests whether agents can iteratively retrieve usable tools, invoke them to uncover intermediate evidence for subsequent calls toward the final goal. PlanBench-XL further features an optional blocking mechanism that simulates real-world unpredictability through missing, failing, or distracting tool functions, forcing agents to detect disrupted paths and adapt at runtime. Experiments on ten leading LLMs show that massive-tool planning remains challenging: while GPT-5.4 achieves 51.90% accuracy in block-free settings, it collapses to 11.36% under the most severe blocking condition. Further analysis shows that agents are especially vulnerable when failures lack explicit error signals or when recovery requires longer alternative tool-use paths. These results establish PlanBench-XL as a testbed for diagnosing agentic planning failures and highlight the need for robust adaptive planning in long-horizon tasks with large, imperfect tool environments.

</details>


### [533] [Code Isn't Memory: A Structural Codebase Index Inside a Coding Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22417)
*Ishaan Bhola,Adithyan Krishnan,Sravanth Kurmala,Mukunda NS*

Main category: cs.AI
