TL;DR: A controlled ablation study on SWE-PolyBench Verified and SWE-bench Pro shows that adding a structural codebase index to a coding agent harness yields statistically significant gains in localization and resolve, with no cost penalty and lower cost per solve compared to agentic grep.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether adding a structural codebase index to a coding-agent harness actually changes cost or resolve, given that retrieval implementations vary widely across deployed systems.

Method: Three-arm comparison (harness with index, without index, and agentic-grep comparator) on SWE-PolyBench Verified and SWE-bench Pro, using Claude Opus 4.7 held fixed across three seeds, inside leak-audited per-task sandboxes.

Result: Within-harness ablation shows large localization gain and statistically separated resolve gain with no cost penalty per cell and lower cost per solve. Cross-harness check shows no regression against agentic-grep baseline on resolve or localization, at no cost penalty.

Conclusion: The deployment question for a structural codebase index shifts from cost concerns to workload characteristics—specifically whether multi-file changes where structural ranking pays off are present.

Abstract: Coding agents now interleave LLMs with retrieval over the working repository, and retrieval implementations vary widely across deployed harnesses. Inside a fixed coding-agent harness on a fixed model, does adding a structural codebase index actually change cost or resolve? We ran three arms (the harness with the index, the same harness without it, and an agentic-grep comparator) on SWE-PolyBench Verified and SWE-bench Pro with Claude Opus 4.7 held fixed throughout, across three seeds, inside a leak-audited per-task sandbox. The within-harness ablation produces a large localization gain and a statistically separated resolve gain, with no cost penalty per cell and lower cost per solve. The cross-harness check shows that the index does not regress against an agentic-grep baseline on resolve or localization, again at no cost penalty. We release the per-cell exclusion ledger, the leak-audit script, the localization extractor, and the results database. The deployment question for a structural codebase index is thus not whether it is too expensive to run (across seeds, the index lands at a lower $/solved than agentic grep) but whether the workload includes multi-file changes where structural ranking pays off.

</details>


### [534] [SVGym (SciVerseGym): An Environment for Reinforcement Learning and Bayesian Optimization in Crystal Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22425)
*Bin Cao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SciVerseGym is a Gymnasium-compatible environment for sequential crystal discovery that frames crystal design as a Markov decision process, supporting various actions, observations, and evaluators for reinforcement learning, Bayesian optimization, evolutionary search, and language-agent workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Closed-loop crystal search methods remain fragmented across bespoke pipelines for editing, relaxation, scoring, constraints, and bookkeeping, lacking a unified, open, and reproducible testbed.

Method: SciVerseGym frames crystal design as a Markov decision process with a Gymnasium-compatible interface, supporting local/global actions (substitution, lattice perturbation, displacement, vacancy, insertion), configurable chemical spaces, structure pools, atomistic/graph observations, custom rewards, relaxation, and stability/phonon diagnostics, evaluated using machine-learned interatomic potentials or ASE-compatible calculators.

Result: Provides an open, reproducible, and extensible testbed enabling reinforcement learning, Bayesian optimization, evolutionary search, and language-agent workflows for closed-loop crystal discovery.

Conclusion: SciVerseGym decouples agent logic from materials infrastructure, offering a standardized environment to accelerate interactive materials discovery.

Abstract: Machine-learned interatomic potentials now enable efficient atomistic evaluation for interactive materials discovery, yet closed-loop crystal search methods remain fragmented across bespoke pipelines for editing, relaxation, scoring, constraints, and bookkeeping. We introduce SciVerseGym, a Gymnasium-compatible environment for sequential crystal discovery that frames crystal design as a Markov decision process. Agents observe an atomistic structure, apply chemically meaningful edits, and receive feedback from a configurable evaluator. SciVerseGym supports local and global actions, including elemental substitution, lattice perturbation, atomic displacement, vacancy creation, and atom insertion, along with configurable chemical spaces, structure pools, atomistic and graph-based observations, custom rewards, optional relaxation, and stability or phonon-related diagnostics. Each step applies an edit, evaluates the candidate using a machine-learned interatomic potential or any ASE-compatible calculator, and returns the standard (obs, reward, terminated, truncated, info) tuple. By decoupling agent logic from materials infrastructure, SciVerseGym provides an open, reproducible, and extensible testbed for reinforcement learning, Bayesian optimization, evolutionary search, and language-agent workflows in closed-loop crystal discovery. Code is available at: https://github.com/Bin-Cao/SciVerseGym.

</details>


### [535] [Efficient Multimodal Clinical Question Answering for Pulmonary Embolism Risk Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22442)
*Xiangyuan Xue,Yang Yu,Yan Gao,Junyan Wang,Bin Chen,Lingyan Ruan,Ting Dang,Hong Jia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper evaluates compact multimodal large language models (MLLMs) on pulmonary embolism (PE) diagnosis and prognosis using the INSPECT dataset (23,248 CTPA studies from 19,402 patients), finding that Gemma4 models perform best with combined CTPA+EHR input, and that diagnostic tasks outperform prognostic ones, especially readmission prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PE management requires timely diagnosis and reliable risk assessment. The combination of CTPA imaging, radiology reports, and EHR data provides a clinically meaningful setting for evaluating compact multimodal language models.

Method: The authors built a benchmark using MLLMs on the INSPECT dataset, formulating 8 diagnostic/prognostic tasks as structured QA problems. They evaluated efficient MLLMs under CTPA-Only, EHR-Only, and CTPA+EHR settings with zero-shot and few-shot prompting.

Result: Gemma4 E4B and Gemma4 E2B performed most strongly with EHR evidence, especially in the CTPA+EHR setting. PE diagnosis outperformed prognostic tasks, with readmission prediction being the most challenging.

Conclusion: Compact multimodal models show great potential for early-stage PE risk detection and explanation, particularly when combining imaging and EHR data.

Abstract: Pulmonary embolism (PE) is a high risk cardiopulmonary condition whose management requires both timely diagnosis and reliable assessment of future clinical risk. Because PE care routinely combines computed tomography pulmonary angiography (CTPA), radiology interpretation, and longitudinal electronic health record (EHR) evidence, it provides a clinically meaningful setting for evaluating compact multimodal language models. In this work, we build a benchmark using efficient multimodal large language models (MLLMs) on INSPECT, a multimodal PE dataset containing 23,248 CTPA studies from 19,402 patients. We formulate eight diagnostic and prognostic tasks as structured clinical question answering problems and evaluate on typical efficient MLLMs under CTPA-Only, EHR-Only, and CTPA+EHR settings with zero-shot and few-shot prompting. Results show that Gemma4 E4B and Gemma4 E2B perform more strongly when EHR evidence is available, especially under CTPA+EHR input. Task level analysis further shows that PE diagnosis achieves higher performance than prognostic tasks, particularly readmission prediction. These observations suggest that compact multimodal models have the great potential in early stage PE risk detection and explanation.

</details>


### [536] [A Differentiable Atari VCS:A Complex, Fully Known Ground Truth for Explainable AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22447)
*Andreas Maier,Siming Bayer,Patrick Krauss*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The authors build two fully differentiable, bit-accurate emulators of the Atari 2600 (in Julia and JAX), creating a complex yet ground-truth-inspectable system for explainable AI research, addressing the fundamental dilemma that XAI methods cannot be verified when ground truth is unknown.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current XAI faces a dilemma: simple models are explainable but trivial, while complex models (deep networks) need XAI but lack ground-truth inner workings, making explanations unverifiable. The authors aim to remove this dichotomy by creating a system that is both genuinely complex and fully inspectable.

Method: The authors reimplement the Atari 2600 VCS as two end-to-end differentiable emulators (jutari in Julia, jaxtari in JAX), validated bit-for-bit against xitari. They treat ROM as weight tensor, RAM as soft tape, and control flow as gates, proving differentiable execution equals original hard execution in the forward pass at any finite temperature.

Result: Both emulators reproduce xitari on all 64 ALE games: 64/64 byte-identical RAM and 64/64 pixel-identical screens. The JAX port enables batched differentiable rollouts reaching millions of environment-steps/s on one GPU. The system was built in ~137 active hours over 29 days, largely by coding agents.

Conclusion: The paper builds and validates the foundation, showing theoretically and via a qualitative gradient study that gradient-based XAI on this complex yet fully specified system is feasible. Full code is open-source under MIT license.

Abstract: Explanation requires ground truth: to verify an account of a system we must know its inner functioning-just what is missing where explainable AI (XAI) is most needed. Systems we can study fall into two camps. Simple, procedural one-decision trees, rule lists, sparse linear models-have a known but trivial mechanism, so explaining them tests nothing; genuinely complex ones-deep networks, real-world tasks-need XAI but have no ground-truth inner functioning, so an explanation can be plausible, confident, and wrong with no way to tell. We remove this dichotomy with a study object both genuinely complex and fully specified-inspectable by construction-and, so gradient methods apply, fully differentiable. We reimplement the Atari 2600 Video Computer System (VCS)-a real computer architecture, and the cradle of deep reinforcement learning-as two independent end-to-end differentiable emulators in Julia (jutari) and JAX (jaxtari), each validated bit-for-bit against xitari. Both reproduce xitari on all 64 supported Arcade Learning Environment (ALE) games: 64/64 byte-identical RAM and 64/64 pixel-identical screens. Treating the cartridge ROM as a weight tensor, RAM as a soft tape, and control flow as gates, we prove the differentiable (soft) execution equals the original (hard) one bit-for-bit in the forward pass at any finite temperature, while exposing surrogate gradients where the bit logic has none. The JAX port also opens a GPU path: batched differentiable rollouts reach millions of environment-steps/s on one commodity GPU. The system was built in roughly 137 active hours over 29 calendar days, much of it written autonomously by coding agents. This paper builds and validates the foundation, showing-theoretically and in a qualitative gradient study-that gradient-based XAI on it is feasible. Both ports' full code is available under the MIT license at https://github.com/akmaier/UnderstandingVCS.

</details>


### [537] [Self-Evolving Cognitive Framework via Causal World Modeling for Embodied Scientific Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22449)
*Yi Yu,Tetsunari Inamura*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a self-evolving cognitive framework that shifts embodied intelligence from predictive world modeling to continual causal reasoning and refinement through environmental interaction, integrating causal discovery, intervention-driven reasoning, and counterfactual analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current embodied world models are limited to predictive objectives and fail to generalize under distribution shifts or reason about unseen situations and hypothetical interventions.

Method: A self-evolving cognitive framework with three components: (1) causal world modeling, (2) intervention-driven causal reasoning, and (3) continual cognitive refinement. Embodied interaction is reinterpreted as an epistemic process for causal hypothesis generation and experimentation.

Result: Conceptual and theoretical foundation for transitioning from predictive intelligence to epistemic intelligence, where intelligence emerges through continual construction and refinement of causal world models.

Conclusion: Embodied scientific intelligence should move beyond predictive modeling toward self-evolving cognitive systems with causal-epistemic benchmarking paradigms.

Abstract: Current embodied world models are primarily optimized for predictive objectives, limiting their ability to generalize under distribution shifts and reason systematically about unseen situations and hypothetical interventions. We argue that embodied intelligence should move beyond predictive world modeling toward self-evolving cognitive systems that continually construct and refine internal causal representations through interaction with the environment. To this end, we propose a self-evolving cognitive framework via causal world modeling for embodied scientific intelligence, which integrates three complementary components: causal world modeling, intervention-driven causal reasoning, and continual cognitive refinement. The proposed framework continuously revises and expands its internal causal world model through causal discovery, intervention-driven feedback, and counterfactual reasoning, supporting continual cognitive refinement and enabling cognition itself to evolve over time. Furthermore, we reinterpret embodied interaction not merely as a means of trajectory optimization, but as an epistemic process for causal hypothesis generation, intervention-driven experimentation, and continual knowledge acquisition. This work provides a conceptual and theoretical foundation for a transition from predictive intelligence toward epistemic intelligence, in which intelligence emerges through the continual construction, revision, and refinement of causal world models via interaction with the environment. Accordingly, an intervention-driven causal-epistemic benchmarking paradigm is suggested for evaluating self-evolving embodied scientific intelligence.

</details>


### [538] [PRIME: Evaluating Prompt Resolution Under Incompatible Instructions in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22470)
*Tehreem Javed,Shumaim Fatimah,Masooma Bakhtiari,Gibrail Islam,Mehwish Fatima*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PRIME is a framework that evaluates how LLMs handle conflicting meta-instructions (length, format, reasoning), finding that conflict type matters more than model size, with distinct failure modes across conflict categories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current instruction-following benchmarks test meta-instructions in isolation, failing to reveal how LLMs behave when given incompatible/conflicting prompts. This gap limits understanding of LLM robustness and instruction processing.

Method: The authors introduce PRIME, a framework generating calibrated conflicts across response length, output format, and reasoning dimensions. They classify model responses using a deterministic behavioral taxonomy and evaluate five instruction-tuned open-weight LLMs under balanced and naturally distributed settings.

Result: Conflict type has a stronger influence on model behavior than model scale. Different categories of conflict produce distinct failure modes in LLMs.

Conclusion: Developing conflict awareness in LLMs is valuable. Instruction-following ability cannot be properly assessed through isolated constraints alone; conflicting instruction scenarios must be explicitly tested.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) often encounter conflicting prompts, although current instruction following benchmarks assess those meta-instructions in isolation, limiting the insights about how models process conflicting instructions. We introduce a framework \textit{PRIME}(\textit{Prompt Resolution under Incompatible Meta-Instructions Evaluation}) to analyze behavior of LLMs when provided with conflicting instructions. \textit{PRIME} purposefully produces calibrated conflicts across response length, output format, and reasoning; classifying model responses with a deterministic behavioral taxonomy. We are evaluating five instruction tuned open weight LLMs in two distinct settings, balanced and naturally distributed. The conclusion we reach upon analysis is that conflict type is more significant in affecting behavior than model scale, and various failure modes across different categories of conflict. Our findings emphasize the value of developing conflict awareness and suggest ability of LLM to follow instructions cannot be assessed through isolated constraints alone.

</details>


### [539] [VADAOrchestra: Neurosymbolic Orchestration of Adaptive Reasoning Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22485)
*Teodoro Baldazzi,Luigi Bellomarini,Andrea Coletta,Michela Iezzi,Carsten Maple,Alessandro Pesare,Emanuel Sallinger*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VADAOrchestra is a neurosymbolic framework combining LLM-based orchestration with Datalog+/- symbolic inference to enable adaptive, auditable, and scalable workflow execution for complex decision-making over large datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional BPM systems are rigid and cannot adapt at runtime, while LLM-based agentic systems are flexible but opaque, unreliable, and scale poorly. The authors aim to combine the strengths of both paradigms.

Method: A hybrid neurosymbolic approach: an LLM-based orchestrator incrementally plans workflows, encoded as logic programs in Datalog+/- where predicates map to tool invocations and rules represent domain dependencies. All logical inference is executed by a Datalog+/- symbolic engine.

Result: Evaluated on real-world financial use cases, demonstrating faithfulness, scalability, and explainability compared to standard agentic architectures.

Conclusion: VADAOrchestra successfully bridges the gap between rigid BPM systems and flexible but opaque LLM agents, providing verifiable reasoning traces and scalable complex reasoning over large datasets.

Abstract: Decision-making in real-world settings rarely follows a fixed script. Instead, it unfolds as a dynamic reasoning process in which the appropriate course of action evolves as new context and data become available. Traditional Business Process Management systems provide rigor, determinism, and auditability, yet they generally struggle to adapt their execution at runtime. Conversely, agentic systems based on Large Language Models (LLMs) bring flexibility to decision-making, but they are inherently opaque, often unreliable, and suffer from significant scalability constraints when operating over large datasets. To combine these complementary paradigms, we introduce VADAOrchestra, a neurosymbolic framework that models complex workflows as evolving reasoning processes. The framework adopts a hybrid approach: given a user query and a collection of data sources, an LLM-based orchestrator incrementally plans and adapts the workflow. This is encoded as a logic program in a fragment of Datalog+/- where predicates correspond to tool invocations and rules represent both predefined domain dependencies and logic constructs synthesized on demand to manipulate intermediate results. All logical inference tasks are then executed by a state-of-the-art Datalog+/- symbolic engine. This approach provides a verifiable reasoning trace, supporting the auditability and reproducibility of the entire process. Furthermore, by decoupling high-level orchestration from symbolic inference, it addresses scalability concerns, enabling complex reasoning over large datasets through targeted data querying. We evaluate VADAOrchestra on real-world financial use cases, demonstrating faithfulness, scalability, and explainability compared to standard agentic architectures.

</details>


### [540] [SCOPE: Evolving Symbolic World for Planning in Open-Ended Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22488)
*Yundaichuan Zhan,Minghe Gao,Zhongqi Yue,Wendong Bu,Wenqiao Zhang,Guoming Wang,Jisheng Dang,Juncheng Li,Siliang Tang,Yueting Zhuang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SCOPE is a self-adaptive symbolic planning framework that uses symbolic execution simulation and self-adaptive memory to refine action plans and evolve symbolic world representations in open-ended environments, improving planning completeness and success rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLM+planner approaches suffer from incomplete symbolic representations in open-ended environments, leading to suboptimal long-horizon planning performance.

Method: SCOPE consists of two modules: (1) Symbolic Execution Simulator (SESim) for symbolic validation and real execution with feedback-driven refinement, and (2) Self-Adaptive Symbolic Memory (SASMem) for distilling feedback into evolving symbolic knowledge.

Result: SCOPE significantly improves symbolic world completeness, plan success rates under perturbations, and cross-task grounding/adaptability across diverse embodied scenarios.

Conclusion: Self-adaptive symbolic evolution is an effective approach to handle incomplete symbolic representations in open-ended embodied planning tasks.

Abstract: Recent works have explored integrating Vision-Language Models (VLMs) with classical planners that rely on symbolic representations of planning problems to generate long-horizon plans for complex embodied tasks. However, in open-ended environments, these symbolic representations obtained from perception are often incomplete, leading to suboptimal performance. To address this, we introduce SCOPE, a self-adaptive symbolic planning framework that supports refining action plans and evolving the symbolic world, i.e., the symbolic representations of open-ended environments. SCOPE comprises two synergistic modules: a Symbolic Execution Simulator (SESim) that conducts symbolic validation and real execution of action plans, leveraging the feedback to refine the plans and evolve the symbolic world; and a Self-Adaptive Symbolic Memory (SASMem) that further distills feedback into evolving symbolic knowledge to enhance long-horizon planning and modeling of the symbolic world. Experiments in open-ended environments show that SCOPE significantly improves the completeness of the symbolic world, the success rate of plans under environment perturbations, and cross-task grounding and adaptability across diverse embodied scenarios.

</details>


### [541] [Deep Learning-Based Sign Language Recognition from Videos and Cross-Lingual Translation to Indian Vernaculars](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22494)
*Chandranath Adak,Ramesh Nandipalli*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A two-stage pipeline using VideoMAE video transformer for sign language classification (13-class subset) and NLLB-200 for multilingual translation (Hindi, Telugu, Bengali), achieving 78% validation accuracy on a small Indian Sign Language dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated sign language recognition tools remain limited, especially for low-resource Indian languages, leaving the deaf and hard-of-hearing community underserved.

Method: Two-stage deep learning pipeline: (1) fine-tuned VideoMAE video transformer classifies 16-frame video clips from the AI4Bharat Indian Sign Language corpus into English labels; (2) NLLB-200 translates predicted labels into Hindi, Telugu, and Bengali.

Result: 99% training accuracy and 78% validation accuracy on a 13-class, 197-clip dataset. Dominant failure modes include confusable adjective pairs (ugly, deaf, blind, hat, dress).

Conclusion: Promising initial results but limited by small label set, isolated-word recognition, single-signer sensitivity, and ambiguity of single-word translation. Future work includes sentence-level generation and larger vocabulary expansion.

Abstract: Sign language is a primary mode of communication for the global deaf and hard-of-hearing community, yet automated tools that recognize sign gestures from video and translate them into natural language text remain limited, particularly for low-resource Indian languages. We present a two-stage deep learning pipeline that (i) classifies short sign language video clips into English word labels using a fine-tuned VideoMAE video transformer, and (ii) translates the predicted English label into Hindi, Telugu, and Bengali using Meta AI's No Language Left Behind (NLLB-200) multilingual translation model. The classification model is fine-tuned on a 13-class subset of the AI4Bharat Indian Sign Language video corpus from IIT Madras, processing 16-frame clips sampled uniformly from each video at 224 x 224 resolution. Under a small-scale academic setting (13 classes, 197 clips, 80-20 split), the fine-tuned model reaches 99% training accuracy and 78% validation accuracy after 15 epochs. We provide a per-class breakdown via a confusion matrix and classification report, identify the dominant failure modes (confusable adjective pairs such as ugly, deaf, blind, hat, and dress), and describe a Streamlit-based inference demo that takes a user-uploaded video and returns the predicted English label alongside its Hindi, Telugu, and Bengali translations. We discuss the scope, limitations (small label set, isolated-word rather than continuous signing, single-signer style sensitivity, ambiguity of single-word machine translation), and directions for future work, including expanding to sentence-level generation and a larger vocabulary. Code is released to support reproducibility.

</details>


### [542] [Grounded Scaling: Why Agentic AI Needs Deterministic Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22495)
*Liang Ding,Xintong Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper argues that environment determinism (per-step success probability δ) is a critical bottleneck for long-chain AI agent execution, where k-step success degrades as δ^k. It proposes formal bounds on chain-task success, identifies a Verifier-Goodharting problem, and introduces practical tools (SCI index, DMM maturity model, and falsifiable open questions) to measure and manage this limitation across competing AI development paradigms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The AGI-to-ASI scaling debate has focused on compute growth vs. frictions (data wall, abstraction, embodiment, trust), but overlooks environment determinism as a binding cross-cutting constraint. For verifiable agentic AI tasks, per-step unreliability causes exponential failure in long chains.

Method: Three formal results: (1) Determinism-Efficiency Bound on chain-task success, (2) Verifier-Goodharting Floor on reward flywheels, (3) convergence condition for environment-side skill evolution. Operationalized via Supply Certainty Index (5 properties), Determinism Maturity Model (5 levels), and 5 falsifiable open questions.

Result: For any per-step determinism δ < 1, k-step chain success degrades as δ^k (exponential failure). The framework is platform-agnostic and engages three competing positions: sim-to-real sufficiency, alignment sufficiency, and AI-as-normal-technology.

Conclusion: Environment determinism is a fundamental bottleneck in agentic AI that cuts across existing friction categories. The proposed SCI and DMM offer practical measurement, while the falsifiable open-question program (OQ1-OQ5) provides explicit conditions for refutation.

Abstract: Long-chain agent execution fails exponentially in environments designed for human tolerance: with per-step determinism $δ< 1$, $k$-step chain success degrades as $δ^k$. The AGI-to-ASI scaling debate (Genewein et al., 2026) has so far framed progress as a race between compute growth and a list of frictions (data wall, abstraction barrier, embodied bottleneck, multi-agent trust); we argue that environment determinism is a complementary binding axis cutting across all four, for the broad class of agentic AI tasks whose outcomes are verifiable economically, physically, or through multi-party settlement. Three formal results pin down the regime: a Determinism-Efficiency Bound on chain-task success, a Verifier-Goodharting Floor on flywheel ceilings under imperfect rewards, and a convergence condition for environment-side skill evolution. We operationalise the framework as a Supply Certainty Index (SCI) over five measurable properties, a five-level Determinism Maturity Model (DMM) as adoption ladder, and a falsifiable open-question programme (OQ1-OQ5) with explicit null results that would force retraction. The position is platform-agnostic. We engage three competing positions: sim-to-real sufficiency, alignment sufficiency, and AI-as-normal-technology.

</details>


### [543] [Imagine to Ensure Safety in Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22509)
*Gregory Gorbov,Artem Latyshev,Aleksandr I. Panov*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A hierarchical safe RL method using a world model with high-level (subgoal generation) and low-level (safe rollouts) policies that significantly outperforms baselines on long-horizon tasks by improving exploration safety and constraint satisfaction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safe exploration in RL is challenging, especially in long-horizon tasks where compounding estimation errors and restricted exploration capabilities fundamentally limit existing safe methods.

Method: Combines a learnable world model with two complementary policies: a high-level policy that generates intermediate safe subgoals to bias exploration, and a low-level policy that uses imagined rollouts in the learned world model to reduce unsafe behaviors when reaching subgoals.

Result: Significantly outperforms existing Safe RL baselines in success rate and empirical constraint satisfaction on long-horizon navigation and manipulation tasks; consistently meets prescribed safety budgets across seeds.

Conclusion: The hierarchical approach with world model-based planning effectively addresses safe exploration in long-horizon tasks where prior methods fail to solve complex scenarios.

Abstract: This work investigates the safe exploration problem in reinforcement learning, where an agent must maximize cumulative performance while simultaneously satisfying safety constraints. This challenge becomes even more pronounced in long-horizon tasks, where existing safe methods face fundamental limitations due to compounding estimation errors and restricted exploration capabilities. To address this problem, we propose a method that combines a learnable world model with two complementary policies a high-level policy and a low-level policy to promote safety at both hierarchical levels. The high-level policy generates intermediate subgoals that bias exploration toward safe regions, while the low-level policy uses imagined rollouts in the learned world model to reduce unsafe behaviors when reaching these subgoals. The proposed method was evaluated on challenging long-horizon navigation and manipulation tasks with high-dimensional action spaces, where it significantly outperforms existing Safe RL baselines in both success rate and strong empirical constraint satisfaction, consistently meeting the prescribed safety budget across seeds, while prior approaches fail to effectively solve these complex long-horizon scenarios.

</details>


### [544] [Governance Decay: How Context Compaction Silently Erases Safety Constraints in Long-Horizon LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22528)
*Shiyang Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper identifies "Governance Decay" — a failure mode where context compaction (summarization/eviction) in LLM agents silently removes in-context governance constraints, causing agents to violate safety rules they previously obeyed. The authors introduce ConstraintRot benchmark, measure violations across 7 model families (0%→30%+ after compaction), demonstrate adversarial attacks that exploit this, and propose Constraint Pinning as a mitigation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To highlight that the context-management layer (compaction, summarization, eviction) is a safety-critical but overlooked failure surface in LLM agents, where governance constraints can be silently dropped.

Method: The authors built ConstraintRot (a benchmark with long-horizon agent scenarios and deterministic tool-call grading), ran 1,323 episodes across 7 model families, measured violation rates before/after compaction, studied adversarial compaction-eviction attacks, and proposed Constraint Pinning (training-free mitigation that quarantines constraints from compaction).

Result: Violation rose from 0% (full context) to 30% after compaction (59% for some models). When constraints survived summary: 0% violation; when dropped: 38% violation. Optimized adversarial injections defeated all evaluated models. Constraint Pinning restored violation to 0%.

Conclusion: Context management must be treated as a first-class governance surface for deployed LLM agents, and Constraint Pinning offers a simple, effective mitigation.

Abstract: Modern LLM agents increasingly rely on context compaction, summarization, or eviction to keep long-running sessions within a token budget. We show that this context-management layer is a safety-critical failure surface: in-context governance constraints that agents reliably obey while visible can be silently removed by compaction, causing the same agent to perform prohibited tool actions later in the session. We call this failure mode Governance Decay. We introduce ConstraintRot, a benchmark of long-horizon agent scenarios with deterministic tool-call grading, and measure compaction-induced violations across seven model families. Across 1,323 episodes, violation rises from 0% with the policy in full context to 30% after compaction, reaching 59% for some models; when the constraint survives the summary, violation remains 0%, but when it is dropped, violation reaches 38%. We further study a Compaction-Eviction Attack, in which adversarial in-context content biases the summarizer to omit a legitimate policy, and show that optimized injections defeat every evaluated model. Finally, we propose Constraint Pinning, a simple training-free mitigation that quarantines governance constraints from lossy compaction and restores violation to 0% in our benchmark. These results identify context management as a first-class governance surface for deployed LLM agents.

</details>


### [545] [MacAgentBench: Benchmarking AI Agents on Real-World macOS Desktop](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22557)
*Yikun Fu,Bowen Fu,Zhenyu Wu,Shuang Cheng,Xiaowei Sun,Bowen Yang,Zehao Li,Yibo Zhao,Zichen Ding,Zhoumianze Liu,Shijie Wang,Biqing Qi,Bowen Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MacAgentBench is a new macOS agent benchmark with 676 tasks across 25 apps using fine-grained multi-checkpoint scoring. The best system (Claude Opus 4.6 on OpenClaw) achieves 73.7% Pass@1, but success is driven more by the skill library than framework design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for macOS agents lack framework augmentation evaluation and rely on binary scoring, failing to capture partial progress on long-horizon, multi-application tasks.

Method: MacAgentBench comprises 676 tasks across 25 applications (~60% involving both GUI and CLI), uses deterministic rule-based evaluation, and introduces fine-grained multi-checkpoint scoring with capability annotations for multi-application tasks.

Result: Best configuration (Claude Opus 4.6 on OpenClaw) achieves 73.7% Pass@1; advantage driven by skill library, not framework design; models with similar Pass@1 differ substantially in sub-goal completion.

Conclusion: Framework design matters less than skill library quality for CUAs; fine-grained metrics reveal hidden performance differences not captured by binary evaluation.

Abstract: Computer use agents (CUAs) have advanced rapidly in desktop automation, and a growing number of users deploy CUAs such as OpenClaw on Mac Mini for always-on automation. However, existing benchmarks, including those for macOS, evaluate agents without framework augmentation and rely on binary evaluation. As a result, they fail to capture both the framework capabilities leveraged by modern CUAs and the partial progress on long-horizon, multi-application tasks. We present MacAgentBench, a comprehensive macOS agent benchmark comprising 676 tasks across 25 applications, with nearly 60% involving both GUI and CLI interaction. The benchmark adopts deterministic rule-based evaluation and introduces fine-grained multi-checkpoint scoring with capability annotations for multi-application tasks. Experiments across three frameworks and 16 models show that the best configuration, Claude Opus 4.6 on OpenClaw, attains 73.7% Pass@1, while this advantage is primarily driven by the skill library rather than by framework design. Fine-grained metrics further reveal that models with similar Pass@1 can differ substantially in sub-goal completion. Our code and data are publicly available at https://github.com/JetAstra/MacAgentBench.

</details>


### [546] [Text2DSL: LLM-Based Code Generation for Domain-Specific Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22586)
*Alexander V. Kozachok,Alexander M. Nazimov,Shamil G. Magomedov*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper formalizes Text2DSL as a new problem class (distinct from Text-to-SQL and code generation), introduces the PolkitBench dataset of 4,204 NL-to-Polkit-rule pairs, and shows via controlled experiments on two MoE models that providing structured context (BNF grammar, API spec, identifier vocabulary) dramatically improves DSL code quality—achieving 98.6-99.4% syntactic validity, +9.7 to +35.5 pp structural validity, and +60-95% CodeBLEU without fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DSLs for OS security policies are powerful but manually authoring rules is expertise-heavy and error-prone; existing work focuses on Text-to-SQL or general code generation, leaving DSL-specific generation unformalized and underexplored.

Method: Formalized Text2DSL as a distinct problem class; created PolkitBench (4,204 NL-Polkit rule pairs validated via 3-level AST pipeline); conducted controlled prompt experiments on two MoE models (GigaChat-10B-A1.8B and Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B) comparing generation with vs. without structured context (BNF grammar, API spec, permitted identifier vocabulary).

Result: With structured context: syntactic validity 98.6-99.4%, structural validity improved by +9.7 to +35.5 pp, CodeBLEU improved by +60% to +95% across both models.

Conclusion: Injecting formal target-language specification into the prompt context is a robust enabling factor for high-quality Text2DSL generation without model fine-tuning, with consistent effects across models of different scale and provenance.

Abstract: Domain-specific languages (DSLs) are widely used for managing operating system security policies, yet manually authoring rules in such languages demands high expertise and is error-prone. This paper formalises the task of automatic DSL code generation from natural language descriptions - Text2DSL - as a distinct problem class, separate from Text-to-SQL and general-purpose code generation. We introduce the PolkitBench dataset comprising 4,204 verified natural-language-to-Polkit-rule pairs, each validated through a three-level AST-based pipeline. Controlled prompt experiments on two MoE models of different scale and provenance - GigaChat-10B-A1.8B (1.8B active parameters) and Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B (3B active) - demonstrate the critical role of structured context (BNF grammar, API specification, permitted identifier vocabulary) for LLM-based DSL code generation. Across both models, supplying context raises syntactic validity to 98.6-99.4%, structural validity by +9.7 to +35.5 pp, and the CodeBLEU score by +60% to +95%. The consistency of the effect across models of different scale and provenance indicates that, for the Text2DSL class of problems, injecting a formal target-language specification into the prompt context is a robust enabling factor for high-quality generation without model fine-tuning.

</details>


### [547] [PaperClaw: Harnessing Agents for Autonomous Research and Human-in-the-Loop Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22610)
*Weiwei Ye,Hangchen Liu,Dongyuan Li,Renhe Jiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PAPERCLAW is a multi-agent system that autonomously conducts research from literature curation to paper writing, with human-in-the-loop capability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models have advanced to the point where automating the entire research process is realistic, but no existing system fully handles the complete pipeline from literature review to finished paper in a grounded, checkable manner.

Method: PAPERCLAW uses a harnessed multi-agent system with four stages: (1) domain curation from live literature/datasets/code, (2) brainstorming with pre-registered main-result contract, (3) iterative propose-test-reflect loop with stoppable hypothesis map, and (4) venue-compliant paper writing. It features full-lifecycle memory, a in-cycle research assistant, and supports human-in-the-loop refinement.

Result: LLM judge evaluation shows PAPERCLAW produces strong papers both fully autonomously and with human-in-the-loop refinement.

Conclusion: PAPERCLAW successfully demonstrates end-to-end autonomous research capability while maintaining grounded outputs with validated citations and genuinely executed results, with the flexibility of human collaboration at any stage.

Abstract: Large language models have become capable reasoners and tool users that write and run code and search the literature, which makes automating the research process itself a realistic goal. We present PAPERCLAW, a harnessed multi-agent system that carries a project autonomously, from a field of study to a finished paper. PAPERCLAW curates a domain from a field's live literature, datasets, and code; brainstorms it into an idea with a pre-registered main-result contract; and drives a stoppable hypothesis map through an iterative propose, test, reflect loop that grows only from measured verdicts and halts once the evidence supports the idea, at which point it writes a venue-compliant paper. A full-lifecycle memory keeps each stage in a single living record, so a long run can be paused, inspected, and resumed without losing context. At the centre is an in-cycle research assistant with research tools and skills: it can drive the whole pipeline on its own, while the same interface lets a person step in at any stage, turning a first autonomous draft into a stronger paper through human-in-the-loop refinement. Throughout, PAPERCLAW keeps its output grounded and checkable, citing only references validated against open scholarly indexes and reporting results that genuinely ran. An evaluation with an LLM judge finds that PAPERCLAW produces strong papers both fully autonomously and with human-in-the-loop refinement.

</details>


### [548] [SkillAudit: From Fixed-Suite Benchmarking to Skill-Centered Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22613)
*Dexu Yu,Youhua Li,Zhaoyang Guan,Xianhao Lin,Jining Luan,Zihao Rao,Xuanqi Lan,Yang Ran,Bo Lan,Nai-Xin Zhai,Hanwen Du,Junchen Fu,Wenhao Deng,Yongxin Ni,Chunxiao Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillAudit is an end-to-end framework that automatically evaluates arbitrary agent skills across utility, efficiency/cost, and safety dimensions by generating capability-aligned tasks, running them in sandboxed environments, and using LLM-based judging plus static/dynamic safety analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluation methods rely on fixed task suites that conflate a skill's marginal contribution with backbone strength and miss value outside defined scopes, which becomes inadequate as skill marketplaces grow.

Method: SkillAudit takes an arbitrary agent skill as input, constructs capability-aligned tasks directly from the skill package, executes them in isolated sandbox environments, uses LLM-based judging for automated checks, applies baseline comparison for utility/cost, and employs a two-stage safety detection (static semantic analysis + dynamic runtime verification).

Result: After scanning top-ranked real-world skills across 23 occupational categories, over 7% of skills were found to be at risky status.

Conclusion: SkillAudit provides a reliable, scalable, and multi-dimensional evaluation approach for agent skills, revealing that a non-trivial portion (7%+) of deployed skills pose safety risks.

Abstract: Agent skills have become a practical way to extend large language model agents, but the growing skill ecosystem still lacks a reliable way to judge whether a skill is worth deploying. Existing evaluation methods remain largely anchored to fixed task suites, assessing skills through performance on predefined tasks and environments. As skill marketplaces expand, this paradigm becomes inadequate: fixed suites can conflate a skill's marginal contribution with backbone strength and miss its value when tasks fall outside the skill's intended scope. We introduce SkillAudit, an end-to-end framework for skill-centered assessment that takes an arbitrary agent skill as input and automatically generates a comprehensive, multi-dimensional evaluation report spanning utility, efficiency/cost, and safety. SkillAudit focuses on the skill artifact itself and constructs capability-aligned evaluation tasks directly from the skill package. The generated tasks are conducted in isolated sandbox environments to collect execution evidence, followed by automated checks with LLM-based judging to produce auditable results. To dissect the agent skills, we propose the baseline comparison principle to measure utility and efficiency/cost, and introduce a two-stage detection paradigm combining static semantic analysis with dynamic runtime verification to assess safety risks. After scanning top-ranked real-world skill packages spanning 23 occupational categories, we found that over 7% of skills are at risky status.

</details>


### [549] [Confident but Conflicted: Internal Uncertainty and Cognitive Dissonance Resolution in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22633)
*Weihong Qi,Kristina Lerman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how LLMs resolve cognitive dissonance when encountering conflicting information, introducing Trust Elasticity (TE) to measure persuasion, and linking behavioral variation to internal uncertainty indicators across models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the connection between LLMs' behavioral responses to conflicting information and their internal model uncertainty, which has not been well characterized.

Method: Varied persuasion attempts along source authority and evidence quality across 12 health-science claims; introduced Trust Elasticity (TE) as an econometrics-inspired measure; analyzed four LLMs including two open-weight models to examine internal uncertainty indicators.

Result: TE varies substantially across models; clearly false claims elicit near-zero TE across all models; in open-weight models, variation associates with Confidence Miscalibration (Qwen) and Internal Uncertainty Change (Llama).

Conclusion: Cross-model behavioral variation links to measurable internal properties, suggesting future interventions targeting internal uncertainty.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) frequently encounter inputs that disagree with their prior outputs, through user pushback, retrieved documents, or web search results. While the way they resolve such conflicts -- a process we frame as cognitive dissonance resolution -- has been characterized behaviorally, its connection to internal model uncertainty is not well understood. To study this systematically, we vary persuasion attempts along two dimensions, source authority and evidence quality, across 12 health-science claims of stratified epistemic status. Dissonance can be resolved through persuasion, backfire, or immunity. We introduce Trust Elasticity (TE), an econometrics-inspired measure of how readily a model is persuaded toward conflicting evidence. Across four LLMs, TE varies substantially, while clearly false claims elicit near-zero TE across all models. On two open-weight models, we further find that this variation is associated with two complementary internal uncertainty indicators, Confidence Miscalibration in Qwen and Internal Uncertainty Change in Llama. These results link cross-model behavioral variation to a measurable internal property and point to interventions targeting internal uncertainty as future work.

</details>


### [550] [AgentLens: Interpretable Safety Steering via Mechanistic Subspaces for Multi-Turn Coding Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22673)
*Weidi Luo,Qiming Zhang,Yihao Quan,Mingyu Jin,Jie Cai,Chaowei Xiao,Jingcheng Niu,Zhen Xiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentLens is a white-box defense framework that detects and mitigates unsafe behavior in multi-turn coding agents by analyzing step-level hidden representations and intervening in a 10-dimensional subspace within a single layer, supported by a new MAS benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing safety mechanisms rely on external guardrails with limited fine-grained control, and mechanistic interpretability methods are confined to single-turn or jailbreak settings, failing to capture evolving risk dynamics in multi-turn agent execution.

Method: AgentLens performs runtime safety detection from step-level hidden representations and mitigates unsafe behavior by intervening in a 10-dimensional subspace within a single layer of LLMs used as coding agents.

Result: AgentLens achieves strong safety detection performance, provides evidence for lookahead risk anticipation, and substantially reduces harmful actions of coding agents across LLaMA-3.1-8B, Qwen-2.5-7B, and Gemma-2-9B models.

Conclusion: The work establishes a foundation for applying mechanistic interpretability to dynamic LLM agent safety, offering a new direction for fine-grained, internal safety control in multi-turn settings.

Abstract: Coding agents based on large language models (LLMs) demonstrate remarkable autonomous capabilities, but they also introduce significant safety and misuse risks during multi-turn interactions with external environments. Existing safety mechanisms mainly rely on external guardrails, which have a limited ability to perform fine-grained behavioral control during execution. Meanwhile, recent mechanistic interpretability methods for LLM safety are mostly confined to single-turn or jailbreak-style QA settings, limiting their ability to capture the evolving risk dynamics of multi-turn agent execution. In this paper, we investigate the safety of multi-turn coding agents from an internal perspective. We propose AgentLens (Mechanistic Subspace Intervention and Steering), a white-box defense framework that performs runtime safety detection and representation-level mitigation for coding agents. Unlike conventional agent guardrails, AgentLens detect harmful execution states from step-level hidden representations and mitigate unsafe behavior by intervening in a 10-dimensional subspace within a single layer. To support this research, we introduce the Mechanistic Agent Safety (MAS) benchmark, comprising comprehensively annotated multi-turn execution trajectories across 194 tasks using LLaMA-3.1-8B, Qwen-2.5-7B, and Gemma-2-9B. Extensive experiments show that AgentLens achieves strong safety detection performance, provides preliminary evidence for lookahead risk anticipation, and substantially reduces harmful actions of the coding agent, establishing a foundation for applying mechanistic interpretability to dynamic LLM agent safety. The code is available at: https://github.com/EddyLuo1232/AgentLens

</details>


### [551] [Skin-Deep: A Geometric Diagnostic for Alignment Fragility in Large Language Model Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22676)
*Dongyub Jude Lee,Jungseob Lee,Seungyoon Lee,Seongtae Hong,Suhyune Son,Sugyeong Eo,Jaehyung Seo,Heuiseok Lim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Skin-Deep introduces a geometric diagnostic called Geometric Fragility Score (GFS) that detects alignment fragility in open-weight LLMs by analyzing hidden-state activations, predicting which models will lose refusal safety under fine-tuning before any attack is run.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-weight models can pass safety refusal tests at release but lose this safety behavior after benign fine-tuning, creating a deployment risk. Prior work only observes this fragility after attacks, lacking a method to detect it pre-deployment.

Method: Proposes Skin-Deep, a geometric diagnostic that extracts a low-rank safety subspace from aligned model's hidden-state activations and compresses it into a single scalar (Geometric Fragility Score, GFS). Validated across 21 instruction-tuned models (3B-32B parameters, 6 alignment recipes).

Result: GFS successfully identifies, before any fine-tuning, which initially safe models will retain the most refusal after small-scale LoRA fine-tuning. Direction ablations confirm the recovered subspace causally underlies refusal behavior.

Conclusion: GFS serves as a practical pre-deployment diagnostic for flagging fragile refusal behavior without needing to run an attack, addressing a key safety risk in open-weight model deployment.

Abstract: Alignment tuning is meant to make harmful-request refusal robust, yet this safety behavior can be erased by a small set of benign fine-tuning examples. This is a deployment risk for open-weight models because a checkpoint can pass refusal tests at release time and later lose refusal under low-cost downstream fine-tuning. Prior work has established these refusal failures, but existing studies do not show how to detect this fragility in the aligned model itself before an attack or fine-tuning intervention is run. We introduce Skin-Deep, a geometric diagnostic that detects alignment fragility directly from the aligned model's hidden-state activations before such an intervention is run and compresses the layer-wise safety geometry into a single scalar, the Geometric Fragility Score (GFS). Applied to twenty-one instruction-tuned models spanning six alignment recipes and 3B--32B parameters, Skin-Deep reveals a recurring low-rank safety subspace across model families. Direction ablations show that removing directions in this subspace weakens harmful-request refusal, providing causal evidence that the recovered geometry underlies refusal behavior. Crucially, GFS identifies, before any fine-tuning, the initially safe model that retains the most refusal after small-scale LoRA fine-tuning. These results establish GFS as a practical pre-deployment diagnostic for flagging fragile refusal behavior without running an attack.

</details>


### [552] [VISTA Architect: A graph database-oriented health AI system demonstrated in multidisciplinary tumor boards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22692)
*Tuomo Kiiskinen,Jason Fries,Philip Adamson,David Wu,Timothy John Ellis-Caleo,Aaron Fanous,Balasubramanian Narasimhan,Joel Neal,Sylvia Plevritis,Manuel A. Rivas*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VISTA Architect is a database-oriented AI architecture that integrates LLMs with EHRs by precomputing clinical data into a persistent knowledge graph and timeline, achieving 96.4% accuracy across 17,700 evaluations on thoracic oncology tumor board tasks while reducing preparation time to ~2.2 minutes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM approaches (long-context prompting and RAG) for clinical EHR analysis miss temporal relationships, incur high costs, and suffer from latency due to repeated raw-text processing at query time.

Method: The architecture has two layers: (1) a source-faithful MEDS Graph preserving granular EHR structure with provenance, and (2) a clinically abstracted Timeline Object Architecture (TOA) using graph-guided LLM extraction to create deduplicated, temporally coherent clinical event timelines.

Result: On 1,180 patients at Stanford Medicine, the system achieved 96.4% accuracy (mean 9.75/10) on 15 tumor board variables across 17,700 evaluations (95% CI 96.1-96.7%), surpassing BM25 RAG baselines, and reduced preparation for a 30-patient cohort to ~2.2 minutes.

Conclusion: VISTA Architect effectively addresses key limitations of existing LLM-based clinical extraction methods by precomputing clinical synthesis once, and its modular design can adapt to other specialties beyond thoracic oncology, though further validation is needed.

Abstract: We introduce VISTA Architect, a database-oriented AI architecture for integrating large language models (LLMs) with longitudinal electronic health records (EHRs). At ingestion, it transforms complex clinical documentation into a persistent, provenance-linked knowledge graph, eliminating repeated reprocessing of raw records at query time. The architecture has two layers: a source-faithful MEDS Graph preserving granular EHR structure with full provenance, and a clinically abstracted Timeline Object Architecture (TOA) that uses graph-guided LLM extraction to synthesize a concise timeline of deduplicated, temporally coherent clinical events. This addresses key limitations of direct long-context prompting and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), which often miss temporal relationships and incur high cost and latency from repeated raw-text processing. By precomputing clinical synthesis once, downstream queries access an organized patient state and traverse to source documentation only when detailed verification is needed.
  We demonstrate the system in multidisciplinary thoracic oncology tumor boards at Stanford Medicine, where precise reconstruction of patient histories is critical. Across 1,180 patients, VISTA Architect achieved 96.4% accuracy (mean 9.75/10) on 15 tumor board-salient variables (17,700 evaluations; 95% CI 96.1-96.7%), surpassing a matched BM25 RAG baseline and recent benchmarks for LLM-based clinical extraction. An agentic interface reduced preparation for a 30-patient held-out cohort to about 2.2 minutes without sacrificing accuracy. While configured here for thoracic oncology, the modular design adapts to other specialties through customizable event definitions, episode structures, and agentic tools; validation beyond thoracic oncology remains future work.

</details>


### [553] [Safety-Aware Evaluation of LLM-Generated Driver Intervention Messages through Multi-Task Risk Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22706)
*Keito Inoshita*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces DSAIS, a domain-specific metric and LLM-based framework for evaluating driver intervention systems, achieving high reliability (ICC 0.798-0.840) and demonstrating that multi-task integration improves contextual relevance by 9.1% over rule-based baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing driver intervention systems rely on auditory alerts and fixed templates, failing to leverage multi-task recognition outputs. General-purpose metrics like BLEU and BERTScore cannot capture intervention-specific quality dimensions such as risk-urgency alignment, cognitive load, and driver acceptability.

Method: Proposes DSAIS (Driver Safety-Aware Intervention Score) evaluating five dimensions through hybrid rule-based + LLM Judge architecture, plus an end-to-end framework integrating four-task recognition outputs via risk fusion, state history management, and dynamic prompt construction.

Result: DSAIS achieves ICC 0.798-0.840 across three architecturally distinct judges, Cohen's d > 1.5 across all control conditions. Multi-task integration improves contextual relevance by 9.1% over rule-based baselines. Compact local LLMs (7B-9B) outperform API-based models.

Conclusion: Driver emotion recognition is the most critical upstream factor. Compact local LLMs are superior for in-vehicle deployment. Each framework component contributes meaningfully to contextual relevance.

Abstract: Existing driver intervention systems rely on auditory alerts and fixed templates, failing to leverage multi-task recognition outputs. General-purpose metrics such as BLEU and BERTScore cannot capture intervention-specific quality dimensions including risk-urgency alignment, cognitive load, and driver acceptability. In this paper, we propose the Driver Safety-Aware Intervention Score (DSAIS), a domain-specific metric evaluating five dimensions through a hybrid architecture combining lightweight rule-based computation with LLM Judge evaluation, together with an end-to-end framework integrating four-task recognition outputs into an LLM through risk fusion, state history management, and dynamic prompt construction. Experiments on the AIDE dataset with five models and seven conditions demonstrate that DSAIS achieves ICC 0.798-0.840 across three architecturally distinct judges and Cohen's d > 1.5 across all control conditions. Multi-dimensional sub-score analysis quantifies the contextual adaptability gap between rule-based and LLM-based systems, revealing that multi-task integration improves contextual relevance by 9.1% over rule-based baselines. Ablation experiments demonstrate that each framework component contributes to contextual relevance, with sub-score decomposition revealing gains that aggregate scoring masks. Driver emotion recognition is identified as the most critical upstream factor, and compact local LLMs (7B--9B parameters) achieve quality superior to API-based models, providing practical design guidelines for in-vehicle deployment.

</details>


### [554] [Beyond Penalizing Mistakes: Stabilizing Efficiency Training in Large Reasoning Models via Adaptive Correct-Only Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22716)
*Jungseob Lee,Seungyoon Lee,Seongtae Hong,Minhyuk Kim,Chanjun Park,Heuiseok Lim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper identifies two reward collapse failure modes in length-penalized GRPO for LLM reasoning, and proposes ACOER (Adaptive Correct-Only Efficiency Reward) that applies brevity bonuses only to correct completions with dynamic budget normalization, achieving over 60% token reduction while maintaining accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training large language models to reason efficiently is critical, but integrating length-penalizing rewards into GRPO causes reward collapse and degrades reasoning capabilities.

Method: The paper first systematically evaluates reward configurations to identify collapse mechanisms. It then proposes ACOER, which isolates brevity bonuses to correct completions and prevents stochastic compression via dynamic budget normalization and control-loop penalty adjustments.

Result: ACOER improves overall accuracy compared to the base model while reducing token generation by over 60% across diverse mathematical reasoning benchmarks.

Conclusion: ACOER establishes a fundamentally stable approach for efficiency-aware optimization that robustly prevents both structural and stochastic reward collapse.

Abstract: Training large language models to reason efficiently is a critical challenge. While integrating length-penalizing rewards into Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) aims to reduce verbosity, it frequently triggers reward collapse, severely degrading reasoning capabilities. Through a systematic evaluation of various reward configurations, we identify the root mechanism: GRPO's group normalization creates divergent advantages when incorrect answers receive continuous length penalties. Consequently, methods penalizing the length of incorrect answers are structurally prone to collapse under sustained optimization. Furthermore, restricting penalties exclusively to correct answers avoids this primary failure, but leaves the model susceptible to a stochastic collapse driven by response over-compression. To robustly prevent both failure modes, we propose ACOER (Adaptive Correct-Only Efficiency Reward). ACOER eliminates the structural penalty loop by isolating brevity bonuses to correct completions and prevents stochastic compression via dynamic budget normalization and control-loop penalty adjustments. Evaluated across diverse mathematical reasoning benchmarks, ACOER improves overall accuracy compared to the base model while reducing token generation by over 60%, establishing a fundamentally stable approach for efficiency-aware optimization.

</details>


### [555] [Text Dictates, Music Decorates: Energy-based Attention for Editable Dance Motion Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22726)
*Seong Jong Yoo,Siyuan Peng,Felix Gu,Stratis Aloimonos,Cornelia Fermüller*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: STREAM is a modality-decoupled diffusion transformer that separates text and music conditioning pathways for choreographic motion generation, preventing modality collapse and achieving state-of-the-art semantic control while preserving musical alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing music-to-motion models are black boxes, and jointly conditioning on text+music causes modality collapse where acoustic rhythms overwhelm semantic text prompts, destroying controllability.

Method: Proposes STREAM (Structural-Temporal Rhythmic Energy-based Attention for Motion), a modality-decoupled diffusion transformer with: (1) AdaLN for global text semantics controlling kinematic structure, (2) BEAM (Bimodal Energy-Based Attention Module) for routing features to musical beats without overwriting semantics, (3) Motorica++ dataset with dance vocabulary and frame-level annotations, and (4) Exchange Evaluation Protocol with Editable Dance Score (EDS).

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art alignment between motion and music while perfectly preserving choreographic semantics, as quantified by the proposed EDS metric.

Conclusion: Positions AI as a controllable, collaborative partner for artistic direction rather than a reactive synthesizer.

Abstract: Choreographic motion generation poses unique challenges for AI, demanding precise semantic control over complex, temporally structured, and expressive full-body dynamics. While existing models can synthesize motion from music, they remain largely black boxes. Conversely, attempting to condition generation on both text and music frequently leads to modality collapse, where dense acoustic rhythms overwhelm sparse semantic text prompts, destroying user controllability. To resolve this spatial-temporal conflict, we propose STREAM (Structural-Temporal Rhythmic Energy-based Attention for Motion), a modality-decoupled diffusion transformer. STREAM strictly separates conditioning pathways: global text semantics dictate the kinematic structure via Adaptive Layer Normalization (AdaLN), while a novel Bimodal Energy-Based Attention Module (BEAM) routes these features to the musical beat without overwriting the semantics. We further introduce Motorica++, a newly curated dataset enriched with domain-specific dance vocabulary and frame-level semantic annotations from existing Motorica dataset. Additionally, to rigorously quantify zero-shot editability, we propose the Exchange Evaluation Protocol and Editable Dance Score (EDS). Through extensive experiments, STREAM achieves state-of-the-art alignment between motion and music while perfectly preserving choreographic semantics, positioning AI not merely as a reactive synthesizer, but as a controllable, collaborative partner for artistic direction. The source code and datasets are available at https://github.com/SeongJong-Yoo/STREAM.

</details>


### [556] [Closed-loop Auto Research for Molecular Property Prediction: Discovering and Certifying Generalizable Improvements](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22731)
*Jingjie Ning,Xiaochuan Li,Ji Zeng,Chenyan Xiong,Guolin Ke*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether Auto Research (language-model agents that edit features, models, and gather external evidence) can improve molecular property prediction beyond validation signal, using file-level ablation across 36 endpoints. Key findings: gains vary by benchmark suite; model-search gains drop significantly from validation to test, indicating non-transfer; curated external data helps specific tasks but requires contamination filtering; the Auto Research pipeline outperforms automated ML controls and competes with large pretrained 3D models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine if closed-loop Auto Research, which extends automated ML to changing representations, model code, and external evidence, can produce improvements that generalize beyond the validation signal used to select them, specifically in the domain of molecular property prediction.

Method: Isolated three Auto Research axes (features, models, external evidence) under file-level ablation lock, attributing each gain to one axis over a strong baseline. Tested across 36 endpoints in three benchmark suites (TDC, Polaris, MoleculeNet), scoring each selected configuration on held-out test labels. Used a routed pipeline taking each endpoint's best validation axis, and included a matched-trial automated ML control and contamination filter for external data.

Result: Held-out gains: 0.013 (TDC), 0.011 (Polaris), 0.042 (MoleculeNet). Transferable axis differs by suite. Model-search gain dropped from 0.041 (validation) to 0.003 (test); curated data showed 0.022 validation but -0.019 test. External data raised CYP2C9-substrate by 0.17 and half-life by 0.08 through contamination filtering. Automated ML control achieved 0.006 vs 0.042. Pipeline competitive with 84M-parameter pretrained 3D model.

Conclusion: While Auto Research can yield gains, many improvements do not transfer to held-out test sets—a critical domain-agnostic lesson for any closed-loop system optimizing a proxy for a held-out quantity. Success requires careful validation design and contamination filtering.

Abstract: Closed-loop Auto Research extends automated machine learning from fixed-dataset fitting to changing the research workflow, with language-model agents editing representations and model code and acquiring external evidence. Molecular property prediction spans many small endpoints. We ask whether this action space yields improvements generalizing beyond the validation signal selecting them.
  We isolate three Auto Research axes, features, models, and external evidence, under a file-level ablation lock attributing each gain to one axis over a strong baseline. Across 36 endpoints in three benchmark suites we score each selected configuration once on a held-out test whose labels the search never read. A routed pipeline taking each endpoint's best validation axis reaches positive held-out gains of 0.013, 0.011, and 0.042, the transferable axis differing by suite, data on TDC, model on Polaris, feature and model on MoleculeNet. The largest model-search gain falls from 0.041 on validation to 0.003 on test, while curated data reaches 0.022 but negative 0.019 on test, two non-transfer signatures. Curated external data raises held-out CYP2C9-substrate performance by 0.17 and half-life by 0.08, admitted through a contamination filter rejecting same-source files overlapping 64 to 89 percent of test structures, necessary but not sufficient for transfer. A matched-trial automated machine learning control did not reproduce the agent's code-level model intervention, reaching 0.006 against 0.042, and the pipeline stays competitive with an 84M-parameter pretrained 3D model on the shared training split.
  The experiments stay within molecular property prediction, but separating discovery from held-out certification is a domain-agnostic lesson for any closed-loop system optimising a proxy for a held-out quantity.

</details>


### [557] [GroundEval: A Deterministic Replacement for LLM-as-Judge in Stateful Agent Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22737)
*Jeffrey Flynt*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GroundEval is a judge-free evaluation framework that detects when an agent's plausible answers are based on invalid evidence, by scoring both the final answer and the recorded interaction trace against grounded, time-bounded, and access-controlled evidence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-as-judge evaluation methods fail to detect when agents use wrong evidence paths to produce plausible answers. GroundEval addresses this blind spot by making the evidence path inspectable and scoreable.

Method: GroundEval uses a domain configuration to generate questions, lets the agent answer freely, then scores both the final answer and the entire trajectory (tool activity + turn-level narration). It targets three failure modes: Silence (did the agent check before claiming absence?), Perspective (did the agent reason only from temporally available evidence?), and Counterfactual (did the agent use the correct causal mechanism?).

Result: In a case study, two frontier LLM judges scored a plausible agent response above 0.85, but GroundEval revealed the agent never retrieved the needed evidence, yielding a score of 0.000. The gap between judge-based scoring and evidence trace scoring is shown to be systematic, not rare.

Conclusion: Final-answer and judge-based scoring systems have a systematic blind spot that GroundEval exposes: plausible answers can rest on invalid evidence paths. GroundEval makes each score inspectable through structured per-question diagnostics.

Abstract: Before letting an agent operate over real context, can you prove it used the right evidence? GroundEval turns that question into a deterministic test of what the agent searched, fetched, cited, and was permitted to access. In one case study, two frontier LLM judges scored a plausible agent response above 0.85. But the trace told a different story: the agent had never retrieved the artifact its answer depended on, yielding a GroundEval score of 0.000.
  We introduce GroundEval, a judge-free framework for evaluating agents against grounded, time-bounded, and access-controlled evidence. GroundEval uses a domain configuration to generate questions, lets the agent choose how to answer, and then scores both the final answer and the recorded trajectory that produced it. The benchmark targets three failures that LLM-as-judge evaluation struggles to detect: whether an agent checked before claiming absence, reasoned only from evidence available to the actor at the relevant time, and used the correct causal mechanism rather than a plausible one. These correspond to three tracks: Silence, Perspective, and Counterfactual. GroundEval exposes when plausible answers rest on invalid evidence paths, and produces structured per-question diagnostics that pair tool activity with the agent's turn-level narration, making each score inspectable rather than merely reported. What our case studies turned up is that this gap isn't some rare corner case. It's exactly the blind spot that final-answer and judge-based scoring were never built to catch.

</details>


### [558] [Learning Filters with Certainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22786)
*Yuval Banoun,Daniel Sadoc Menasche,Ori Rottenstreich*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes using the certainty information from Counting Bloom Filter counters (beyond binary membership) to improve hybrid architectures that combine Bloom Filters with machine learning models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hash-based data structures (e.g., Bloom filters) provide only binary membership indications with uncertainty from collisions, but the certainty level itself is useful information that is currently discarded.

Method: The work focuses on Counting Bloom Filters (CBFs), which maintain counters instead of bits. These counters provide additional information to estimate the certainty of positive membership indications.

Result: Shows how the certainty signal from CBF counters can be exploited in architectures that combine Bloom Filters with machine learning models.

Conclusion: Certainty information from CBF counters is valuable for improving ML-Bloom filter hybrid architectures, leveraging uncertainty estimates beyond simple binary outputs.

Abstract: Hash-based data structures such as Bloom filters are widely used in network systems for tasks including caching, anomaly detection, and machine learning pipelines. They typically provide binary indications of whether an element belongs to a set of interest, e.g., the contents of a cache. When uncertainty arises due to hash collisions, a positive indication is returned to avoid false negatives. We argue that the certainty associated with such indications can itself be useful information. This work focuses on Counting Bloom Filters (CBFs), a Bloom-filter variant that maintains counters rather than bits. Besides supporting insertions and deletions, these counters provide additional information that can be used to estimate the certainty of positive membership indications. We show how this certainty signal can be exploited in architectures that combine Bloom Filters with machine learning (ML) models.

</details>


### [559] [The Origins of Stochasticity: Comprehensive Investigations on Uncertainty Quantification for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22792)
*Xiang-Jun Ou,Shuang Liang,Xin-Yu Hu,Rong-Hao Huang,Jing Wang,Shao-Qun Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a granular uncertainty taxonomy for LLMs (input-level, parameter-level, token-level, decoding-process) with corresponding UQ methods (Bayesian, ensemble, consensus-based, single-pass). Evaluates 21 UQ methods across Qwen3, Llama 3.2, DeepSeek-V3 on TriviaQA, GSM8K, HumanEval, finding consensus-based methods (Deg, EigV) perform best, and larger models yield lower uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing uncertainty taxonomies (aleatoric vs. epistemic) fail to capture the multi-component, multi-stage nature of LLM generation, making it difficult to evaluate the effectiveness of various UQ methods for LLMs.

Method: Proposes a granular uncertainty taxonomy (input-level, parameter-level, token-level, decoding-process) and categorizes UQ methods into Bayesian, ensemble, consensus-based, and single-pass approaches. Empirically evaluates 21 UQ methods across three LLM families on diverse benchmarks.

Result: Consensus-based methods (Deg and EigV) consistently outperform other UQ approaches; UQ effectiveness is sensitive to task types and generation settings; larger model scales correlate with lower uncertainty estimates (empirical scaling law).

Conclusion: Bridges the gap between theoretical uncertainty origins and practical deployment, offering a systematic diagnostic tool for quantifying uncertainty in LLM applications.
