Applied to two autoregressive base models, UniSE and GenSE, our approach achieves state-of-the-art results on the DNS2020 benchmark. A human evaluation ablation further shows that the composite multi-metric reward is preferred over any single-metric variant, confirming that multi-reward optimization avoids the reward hacking observed with single-metric training.

</details>


### [690] [Deep Learning for Soil Moisture Estimation: Fusing Satellite Data with Optimally-Lagged Meteorological Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21475)
*Adrian Canovas-Rodriguez,Aurora González Vidal,Antonio F. Skarmeta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study introduces a Cross-Correlation Function methodology to determine optimal temporal lags between meteorological variables and soil moisture, then evaluates three deep learning architectures (CNN, LSTM, CNN-LSTM) across five feature configurations. Results show that modelling atmospheric and subsurface delays substantially improves soil moisture estimation, with the CNN-LSTM hybrid achieving the best overall performance (R²=0.930).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate soil moisture estimation in semi-arid agricultural regions is challenging due to the delayed response of soil moisture to atmospheric forcing. Existing methods often ignore these temporal lags, leading to suboptimal predictions.

Method: A Cross-Correlation Function (CCF) methodology determines optimal temporal lags (0-30 days) between meteorological variables and soil moisture, plus inter-depth lags (0-15 days) for vertical moisture propagation. Three architectures (CNN per-pixel, LSTM frame-level, CNN-LSTM hybrid) were evaluated under five feature configurations across seven agricultural plots.

Result: Meteorological variables improved over satellite-only baseline; subsurface depth was decisive. Per-pixel CNN: R²=0.877, RMSE=2.28 (single-patch); CNN-LSTM hybrid: R²=0.930, CVRMSE=8.0% (best overall).

Conclusion: Explicitly modelling atmospheric and vertical subsurface delays substantially improves soil moisture estimation for precision agriculture, with fusion of satellite, meteorological, and depth data being critical.

Abstract: Accurate soil moisture estimation in semi-arid agricultural regions requires integrating remote sensing and meteorological information while accounting for the delayed response of soil moisture to atmospheric forcing. This study introduces a Cross-Correlation Function (CCF) methodology to determine optimal temporal lags (0-30 days) between meteorological variables and soil moisture, as well as inter-depth lags (0-15 days) describing vertical moisture propagation from the surface (10 cm) to deeper layers (20-50 cm). The approach was validated across seven agricultural plots in southeastern Spain. Three deep learning architectures, each targeting a distinct prediction granularity, were evaluated under five feature configurations ranging from satellite-only to full satellite-meteorology-depth fusion: a CNN for per-pixel estimation within each plot, an LSTM for frame-level (daily plot-mean) prediction, and a CNN-LSTM hybrid operating on sliding windows with pooled multi-patch training. Models were assessed on held-out data to measure genuine generalisation. Meteorological variables improved performance over the satellite-only baseline, while subsurface depth information proved decisive across all architectures. The per-pixel CNN achieved the strongest single-patch result (R^2 = 0.877, RMSE = 2.28), with a seven-patch average R^2 of 0.535, representing an improvement of +1.00 over the satellite-only baseline. The pooled CNN-LSTM hybrid obtained the highest overall performance (R^2 = 0.930, CVRMSE = 8.0%). These results demonstrate that explicitly modelling atmospheric and vertical subsurface delays substantially improves soil moisture estimation for precision agriculture.

</details>


### [691] [Robustness Cannot be Reduced to Regularization: Studying Adversarial Training Beyond the Linear Case](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21488)
*David A. R. Robin,Rafael Pinot,Yann Chevaleyre*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper formally proves that the adversarial risk for two-layer neural networks cannot be reduced to a simpler regularized risk (unlike linear models), and provides empirical evidence suggesting the same impossibility extends to deeper architectures like Wide-ResNets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adversarial training is effective but computationally expensive. For linear models, a formal equivalence exists between adversarial risk and simpler regularized risk, enabling efficient training. The key question is whether this equivalence extends beyond linear models to reduce computational costs.

Method: The authors formally prove via reduction that no such equivalence holds for two-layer networks, showing that adversarial risk fundamentally differs from any simple regularized risk with weak data dependence. They also provide empirical evidence on Wide-ResNets to demonstrate the same impossibility in deeper architectures.

Result: The equivalence between adversarial risk and regularized risk that exists for linear models does NOT hold for two-layer networks. Empirical results on Wide-ResNets suggest this impossibility persists in deeper and more expressive architectures.

Conclusion: The computational efficiency gain from reducing adversarial risk to regularized risk in linear models cannot be replicated for neural networks (two-layer or deeper), as demonstrated both theoretically and empirically.

Abstract: The vulnerability of ML models to adversarial examples has recently emerged as a major concern. While adversarial training is one of the most effective countermeasures to this issue, its high computational cost remains an obstacle to practical deployment. Recent progress in reducing this cost has relied, in the case of linear models, on a formal equivalence between the adversarial risk and a simpler form of regularized risk. This enabled significantly more efficient training procedures, which naturally raises the question of whether such an equivalence can be extended beyond linear models. In this work, we formally show that no such equivalence is possible for two-layer networks. Our proofs proceed via a reduction to key properties that fundamentally separate the adversarial risk from any simple regularized risk which would only exhibit a weak form of data dependence. Beyond this setting, we provide empirical evidence on Wide-ResNets indicating that the same type of impossibility persists in deeper and more expressive architectures.

</details>


### [692] [Predicting High-Risk Colorectal Polyps in African Americans Using Pre-Colonoscopy Clinical Features: Machine Learning Model Development and Temporal Validation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21492)
*Basheer Qolomany,Mrinalini Deverapall,Adeyinka Laiyemo,Zaki Sherif,Mori Yuichi,Omer Ahmed,Hassan Brim,Hassan Ashktorab*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Researchers developed and externally validated machine learning models using only non-invasive, pre-colonoscopy features (demographic, clinical, behavioral) to predict high-risk colorectal polyps in a predominantly African American urban cohort, comparing 8 ML models on 4,681 internal and 1,562 external validation patients.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether non-invasive, pre-colonoscopy features can identify patients at higher risk for advanced colorectal polyps, potentially improving clinical decision-making, prioritizing surveillance, reducing unnecessary procedures, and promoting equitable access to risk stratification, especially in resource-limited settings.

Method: Retrospective cohort study using 4,681 patients (2015-2022, internal) and 1,562 patients (2023-2024, external) from Howard University Hospital. Eight ML models (neural networks, random forest, SVM, Naive Bayes, logistic regression, decision trees, KNN, XGBoost) were developed using only demographic, lifestyle, and comorbidity data. HRP defined as villous/tubulovillous adenomas, high-grade dysplasia, polyps >=10mm, or >=3 polyps.

Result: Results not yet detailed in the abstract, but the models were developed and externally validated on a diverse, predominantly African American urban cohort with a split of 4,681 internal and 1,562 external validation patients.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates feasibility of using only non-invasive pre-colonoscopy features for ML-based prediction of high-risk colorectal polyps, with external validation in a diverse real-world cohort.

Abstract: Risk stratification for advanced colorectal polyps typically relies on colonoscopy and/or pathology findings. However, there is growing interest in whether non-invasive features available prior to colonoscopy can help identify patients at higher risk. Such approaches may enhance clinical decision-making by prioritizing surveillance for individuals most likely to harbor high-risk polyps, when colonoscopy resources are limited while potentially reducing unnecessary procedures in lower-risk patients. Importantly, the use of non-invasive, pre-procedural information may also help promote more equitable access to risk stratification, particularly in settings where colonoscopy resources are limited or unevenly distributed. We aimed to develop and externally validate machine learning models to predict high-risk colorectal polyps using only non-invasive, pre-colonoscopy demographic, clinical, and behavioral features in a diverse, predominantly African American, urban cohort.
  We conducted a retrospective cohort study using demographic, lifestyle, and comorbidity data from patients who underwent colonoscopy at Howard University Hospital to develop and validate several machine learning models, including neural networks, random forest, support vector machines (SVM), Naive Bayes, logistic regression, decision trees, k-nearest neighbors (KNN), and XGBoost, for predicting high-risk colorectal polyps. High-risk polyps (HRP) were defined as villous or tubullovillous adenomas, high-grade dysplasia, polyps >= 10 mm in size, and/or the presence of >= 3 polyps per procedure; all other cases were classified as low-risk polyps (LRP). The dataset included 4,681 patients from 2015-2022 used for internal validation and 1,562 patients from 2023-2024 used for external validation.

</details>


### [693] [Breaking chains with trees: Deep learning with $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ parallel time complexity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21497)
*Neeraj Mohan Sushma,Aditya Nagarsekar,Cabrel Teguemne Fokam,Robin Schiewer,Amit Kumar Pal,Anand Subramoney,David Kappel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hierarchical Block-Local Learning (HBLL) is a framework that enables training deep neural networks with O(log N) parallel time complexity by decomposing networks into hierarchically linked blocks trained with local objectives, eliminating backpropagation's locking and weight transport constraints while achieving competitive performance on vision and language tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Backpropagation requires sequential error propagation through all layers, causing two key limitations: (1) locking, where layer updates are interdependent and cannot be parallelized, and (2) the weight transport problem, needing symmetric forward/backward pathways. These constraints limit parallelism, increase overhead, and hinder scalable learning.

Method: HBLL decomposes deep neural networks into hierarchically linked blocks, each trained with local learning objectives derived from variational principles. This eliminates full end-to-end backpropagation while maintaining effective information propagation. The framework achieves O(log N) parallel time complexity by enabling independent block-level training.

Result: HBLL achieves O(log N) parallel time complexity (first algorithm to do so for deep networks), implicitly defines a family of subnetworks enabling flexible inference, and demonstrates competitive performance on challenging vision and language modeling tasks, with extension to recurrent architectures avoiding backpropagation through time.

Conclusion: HBLL offers a scalable alternative to backpropagation by overcoming locking and weight transport constraints through hierarchical local learning, with theoretical guarantees on parallelism and empirical success across diverse tasks, suggesting a promising direction for more efficient deep network training.

Abstract: Modern deep neural network architectures are trained via backpropagation, which requires errors to be sequentially propagated through all layers before parameters can be updated. This introduces two limitations: locking, where layer-wise updates are strictly interdependent and cannot proceed in parallel, and the weight transport problem, which requires symmetric forward and backward pathways for exact gradient computation. These constraints restrict parallelism, increase memory and communication overhead, and pose challenges for scalable learning. In this work, we propose Hierarchical Block-Local Learning (HBLL), a framework that decomposes deep neural networks into hierarchically linked blocks trained using local learning objectives derived from variational principles, eliminating the need for full end-to-end backpropagation while maintaining effective information propagation across the network. HBLL is the first algorithm that is able to train deep neural networks in $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ parallel time complexity, where $N$ is the number of network layers. We show that HBLL implicitly defines a family of subnetworks corresponding to different hierarchical paths, enabling flexible inference with different effective numbers of layers. We evaluate HBLL on a set of challenging vision and language modeling tasks, achieving competitive performance. We also extend HBLL to recurrent sequence architectures, applying to settings that otherwise rely on backpropagation through time.

</details>


### [694] [Privacy-Preserving Federated Temporal Graph Learning with Digital Twin--Guided Adaptive Deception for Cyber-Resilient IoMT](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21513)
*Syed Zeeshan Haider,Anwar Shah,Muneeb Arif,Hamza Iftikhar,Waqas Ali*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents Federated TGCN-A2C, a privacy-preserving defense framework for IoT/IoMT security that integrates Temporal GCN, LSTM-based Digital Twins, Federated A2C reinforcement learning, and honeypot redirection. It achieves 99.48%-99.61% accuracy on benchmark datasets, converges within 10-25 rounds, and provides SHAP/LIME explainability for regulatory compliance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IoT/IoMT devices are resource-constrained, operate under strict latency and data-privacy regulations, and face critical cybersecurity vulnerabilities. Existing solutions lack privacy preservation, real-time adaptability, or comprehensive threat coverage.

Method: Four integrated mechanisms: (1) PyG-based Temporal GCN with GCNConv, global mean pooling, and anomaly gate for flow classification; (2) LSTM-based Digital Twins generating per-device anomaly scores; (3) Federated A2C agent with 7D state (confidence, entropy, anomaly magnitude, traffic composition) selecting ALLOW/ISOLATE/HONEYPOT-REDIRECT; (4) Enhanced honeypot with adaptive thresholds. Uses EMA-smoothed inverse-weighted FedAvg and cosine-annealed learning rates.

Result: 99.48% (CICDDoS 2019) and 99.61% (TON-IoT) test accuracy; weighted-F1 0.9948 and 0.9961; converges within 25 and 10 rounds; outperforms Fed-Inforce-Fusion by 0.21pp; all 16 CICDDoS classes ≥0.9237 F1; all 10 TON-IoT classes ≥0.9488 F1 including imbalanced MITM.

Conclusion: Federated TGCN-A2C is an effective privacy-preserving defense for IoT/IoMT security with strong accuracy, rapid convergence, comprehensive attack coverage, and explainability supporting clinical regulatory accountability.

Abstract: The rapid proliferation of IoT and IoMT devices introduces critical cybersecurity vulnerabilities in healthcare and industrial environments where resource-constrained devices operate under strict latency and data-privacy regulations. This paper presents the Federated Temporal Graph Convolutional Network with Advantage Actor-Critic (Federated TGCN-A2C), a privacy-preserving defense architecture integrating four mechanisms: a PyG-based Temporal GCN using GCNConv layers with global mean pooling and a learned anomaly gate for flow-level threat classification; LSTM-based Digital Twins generating per-device anomaly scores gating the classifier via learned sigmoid coupling; a Federated A2C agent selecting among ALLOW, ISOLATE, and HONEYPOT-REDIRECT actions based on a seven-dimensional state capturing confidence, entropy, anomaly magnitude, and traffic composition; and an enhanced honeypot layer converting suspicious traffic into threat intelligence with adaptive thresholds. Federated aggregation employs EMA-smoothed per-client validation losses as inverse-weighted FedAvg coefficients to stabilize global model updates under non-IID distributions, with cosine-annealed learning rates per round. Evaluated on CICDDoS 2019 and TON-IoT benchmarks, the framework achieves 99.48% and 99.61% test accuracy with weighted-F1 scores of 0.9948 and 0.9961, converging within 25 and 10 federated rounds, outperforming Fed-Inforce-Fusion by 0.21 percentage points while covering three additional attack categories. All sixteen CICDDoS 2019 classes achieve F1 of at least 0.9237 and all ten TON-IoT classes achieve F1 of at least 0.9488, including the severely imbalanced MITM category. Post-hoc explainability via SHAP, LIME, Grad-CAM, and counterfactual analysis confirms decisions are grounded in semantically meaningful flow features, supporting regulatory accountability in clinical deployments.

</details>


### [695] [Towards Understanding the Power and Limits of the Muon Optimizer: A River-Valley Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21514)
*Tianqi Shen,Jinji Yang,Runze Shi,Jianhao Ma,Jiaye Teng,Ziye Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a trajectory-level theory for Muon optimizer, showing it converges faster initially but suffers from oscillation near solutions due to orthogonalized updates. The authors propose a two-stage approach switching to GD-like optimizers in the final phase.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between the theoretical narrative highlighting Muon's advantages (spectral normalization, improved conditioning) and its mixed empirical performance in LLM training, where gains over Adam/AdamW are often schedule-sensitive and not uniformly superior.

Method: The authors develop a mixed-spiked matrix sensing model and a river-valley perspective (river direction = desired solution, hill directions = noise) to characterize Muon's dynamics. They analyze both momentum-free and momentum settings, and propose a two-stage switching approach.

Result: Muon moves faster along information-bearing directions early in optimization but converges much more slowly near the solution. With momentum, orthogonalized updates remove residual scale information, causing overshooting and oscillation near the target. The two-stage approach shows promising preliminary results in LLM training.

Conclusion: The characterizations extend beyond spiked matrix sensing, motivating switching to GD-like refinement optimizers in the final phase rather than relying only on fixed learning-rate schedules for Muon.

Abstract: Recently, Muon has gained substantial attention as an appealing alternative to Adam-like optimizers, with many works highlighting its advantages through spectral normalization and improved conditioning. Yet this positive theoretical narrative contrasts with its empirical performance in large language model (LLM) training, where Muon's gains over Adam/AdamW are often mixed, schedule-sensitive, and not uniformly superior. To address this gap, we develop a trajectory-level theory characterizing both the strengths and limitations of Muon. We introduce a mixed-spiked matrix sensing model whose sensing operator decomposes into signal, spike, and bulk components, capturing a mixture of anisotropic structure and long-tail information reminiscent of LLM training. On top of it, we adopted a river-valley perspective in which we view the landscape as composed of a river direction flowing to the desired solution and hill directions encoding nuisance or task-irrelevant information. In the momentum-free setting, we show that Muon moves faster along the information-bearing river direction during early optimization, but can converge much more slowly near the river bottom than gradient descent. We then extend the river-valley perspective to general nonconvex objectives with momentum by studying points on the spectral river. There, while Muon converges faster early on, its orthogonalized update removes residual scale information, making it prone to overshooting and oscillation near the target solution. Together, these results suggest that our characterizations extend beyond spiked matrix sensing and motivate switching to GD-like refinement optimizers in the final phase, rather than relying only on a fixed learning-rate schedule for Muon. We also provide preliminary evidence supporting this two-stage approach in language model training experiments.

</details>


### [696] [Backpropagating Through Simulation: Analytic Policy Gradients for Sample and Learning Efficient Differentiable Continuous Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21525)
*Yueci Deng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Analytic Policy Gradients (APG), which uses differentiable environment dynamics for exact policy gradient computation via backpropagation through simulation, and compares it against PPO on four continuous control tasks of increasing complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Model-free RL algorithms like PPO treat the environment as a black box, requiring millions of interactions and suffering from high-variance advantage estimates. The authors aim to improve sample efficiency by leveraging differentiable environment dynamics for exact gradient computation.

Method: APG computes exact policy gradients via backpropagation through differentiable simulation. A segmented backpropagation scheme with MC and critic-based bootstrap modes is introduced to mitigate gradient degradation on long-horizon tasks. APG is evaluated against PPO on four tasks with identical architectures and settings.

Result: APG is evaluated on four continuous control tasks: 1D point-mass reaching, 2D navigation with obstacle avoidance, 2D T-block pushing, and 7-DOF Franka FR3 reaching. A multi-axis evaluation protocol records performance against environment steps and gradient steps. Ablations over segment length and bootstrap strategy are reported.

Conclusion: APG leverages differentiable dynamics for exact gradients, potentially offering improved sample efficiency over model-free PPO, with segmented backpropagation addressing long-horizon gradient degradation.

Abstract: Model-free reinforcement learning algorithms such as Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) treat the environment as a black box, estimating policy gradients from sampled rewards; this process demands millions of interactions and relies on high-variance advantage estimates. When environment dynamics are differentiable, the return is an end-to-end differentiable function of the policy parameters, enabling exact gradient computation via backpropagation through simulation. We term this approach Analytic Policy Gradients (APG) and evaluate it against PPO on four continuous control tasks of increasing dynamical complexity: a one-dimensional point-mass target-reaching task, a 2D point-mass navigation task with obstacle avoidance, a 2D rigid-body T-block pushing task, and a 7-DOF Franka FR3 end-effector reaching task. Both algorithms share identical model architectures, observation normalization, and optimizer settings. To decouple sample efficiency from compute efficiency, we design a multi-axis evaluation protocol that records performance against environment steps and gradient steps. We report a segmented backpropagation scheme with MC and critic-based bootstrap modes that mitigates gradient degradation on long-horizon tasks, and present ablations over segment length and bootstrap strategy.

</details>


### [697] [LIG: Layer-wise Integrated Gradients for Within-Layer Flow Analysis in Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21564)
*Eight Suzuki,Hideitsu Hino,Noboru Murata*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LIG (Layer-wise Integrated Gradients) extends Integrated Gradients to set-to-set mappings within Transformer layers, enabling token-to-token attribution at module boundaries (ATT and MLP) without retraining or per-operation interpreters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformers are opaque; existing XAI methods (like standard IG) only handle scalar objectives and cannot track token-to-token information flow within layers at module granularity.

Method: LIG applies set-to-set Integrated Gradients via L2 scalarization at nonlinear module boundaries (ATT and MLP), composing within-layer contributions akin to Layer-wise Relevance Propagation, using token embedding as ATT baseline and ATT output at a=0 or Zero as MLP baseline.

Result: On BERT-base and PTB, configurations using target token embedding as ATT baseline and either ATT output at a=0 or Zero as MLP baseline best preserved within-layer consistency. LIG reveals module-wise vs. layer-whole attribution agreement and separated ATT/MLP information flow.

Conclusion: LIG serves as a diagnostic XAI tool at module-boundary granularity for Transformers, requiring no model-specific retraining or per-operation interpreter design.

Abstract: Transformers achieve strong performance, but their internal computations remain opaque. We view each Transformer layer as a dynamic graph whose nodes are token representations and per-head attention outputs, with Multi-Head Attention (ATT) and MLP as module boundaries. On this graph we use LIG (Layer-wise Integrated Gradients), which applies set-to-set Integrated Gradients (IG) at nonlinear module boundaries. Set-to-set IG applies IG to a map from a set of input token representations to a set of output representations, evaluating token-to-token contributions, which is not standard in prior IG applications. This extends IG from the usual scalar-objective setting to set-to-set maps via an L2 scalarization, and composes within-layer contributions in the spirit of Layer-wise Relevance Propagation (LRP), with IG completeness playing the role of LRP-style conservation at each boundary. We use LIG to analyze (i) the agreement between module-wise composition and layer-whole attribution under an L2 criterion, and (ii) within-layer information flow by tracing separated ATT and MLP contributions. On BERT-base and PTB, configurations that best preserved within-layer consistency used the target token's embedding as the ATT baseline and either the ATT output at a=0 or Zero as the MLP baseline. We therefore present LIG as a diagnostic XAI tool at module-boundary granularity, without model-specific retraining or per-operation interpreter design. Code is available at https://github.com/eightsuzuki/layer-wise-integrated-gradients.

</details>


### [698] [FAST: A Framework for Aligned Sampling and Training in Parallel Reinforcement Learning for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21587)
*Bonan Wang,Letian Tao,Bin Shuai,Jiaxin Gao,Wenxin Zhao,Wei Xiong,Kehua Sheng,Bo Zhang,Yang Guan,Shengbo Eben Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FAST is a synchronous parallel framework for closed-loop autonomous driving that uses Dynamic Parallel Sampling Alignment (DPSA) to extend terminated episodes via virtual continuation and Scaled Mask-Padding Optimization (SMPO) to maintain statistical consistency, achieving 1.78x speedup over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard parallel sampling in deep reinforcement learning for autonomous driving suffers from the straggler effect—premature termination of a single environment forces synchronized batch re-initialization, causing poor sample utilization and high latency.

Method: FAST introduces (1) Dynamic Parallel Sampling Alignment (DPSA) that extends terminated episodes via virtual continuation to maintain vectorization synchronization, and (2) Scaled Mask-Padding Optimization (SMPO) with validity masking and adaptive loss normalization to eliminate bias from auxiliary padding data.

Result: FAST achieves at least a 1.78 times wall-clock speedup over the single-clip baseline while preserving statistical unbiasedness.

Conclusion: The proposed synchronous parallel framework effectively eliminates the straggler effect bottleneck without sacrificing data diversity or theoretical consistency, offering a practical speedup for closed-loop autonomous driving reinforcement learning.

Abstract: Deep reinforcement learning is pivotal for closed-loop autonomous driving yet remains constrained by severe bottlenecks in sampling efficiency. Standard parallel sampling mitigates this but suffers from the straggler effect, where the premature termination of a single environment necessitates a synchronized batch re-initialization, leading to suboptimal sample utilization and prohibitive re-initialization latency. To address this, we propose FAST, a synchronous parallel framework tailored for closed-loop simulation. Specifically, FAST employs Dynamic Parallel Sampling Alignment (DPSA) to maintain vectorization synchronization by extending terminated episodes via virtual continuation, thereby decoupling the sampling loop from individual terminations. By dynamically triggering global truncation based on the termination rate of parallel clips, FAST effectively eliminates the bottleneck of premature resets without sacrificing data diversity. Furthermore, to strictly preserve theoretical consistency, we incorporate a Scaled Mask-Padding Optimization (SMPO) that leverages validity masking and adaptive loss normalization to nullify the bias from auxiliary padding data. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that FAST achieves at least a 1.78 times wall-clock speedup over the single-clip baseline while preserving statistical unbiasedness.

</details>


### [699] [Learning to Place Guards by Reinforcement: A Geo-Free Neural Policy for the Vertex-Guard Art Gallery Problem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21604)
*Domagoj Ševerdija,Jurica Maltar,Nathan Chappel,Domagoj Matijević*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that in neural combinatorial optimization for the Art Gallery Problem, a reinforcement-trained encoder already encodes the geometry needed for feasibility, but failures stem from decoder mis-calibration—not missing knowledge—as evidenced by a simple classifier probing the frozen encoder closing most of the feasibility gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand what a neural combinatorial optimization policy actually learns internally (beyond what its decoder expresses), specifically probing whether failures arise from missing geometric knowledge or from decoder calibration issues.

Method: Train a pointer-network policy with coverage-aware reinforcement learning under geo-free inference (no visibility computation). Then freeze the trained encoder and probe its embeddings with a small single-shot classifier that is also geo-free, comparing performance on feasibility.

Result: The classifier closes most of the feasibility gap, cutting under-covered polygons by ~10x in and out of distribution and at up to ~5x the training range, at a reported cost in guard count.

Conclusion: The reinforcement-trained representation already encodes the required geometry; residual failures reflect decoder calibration, not missing knowledge. Probing a frozen encoder is a practical way to assess what a neural combinatorial solver has internalized.

Abstract: Neural combinatorial optimization (NCO) has shown that policies trained by reinforcement can construct strong solutions to NP-hard problems directly from raw instances. What such a policy actually learns, as opposed to what its decoder expresses, remains much less clear. We study this distinction on the vertex-guard Art Gallery Problem, the NP-hard task of choosing polygon vertices from which to observe an entire region. A pointer-network policy is trained from a coverage-aware reward over its own rollouts under the constraint we call geo-free inference: at test time it sees only vertex coordinates, with no visibility computation and no geometric oracle. The policy places guards economically but leaves a tail of under-covered polygons that widens far beyond the training range. To locate the cause, we freeze the trained encoder and read its embeddings with a small single-shot classifier, still geo-free at inference. The classifier closes most of the feasibility gap, in and out of distribution and at up to roughly five times the training range, cutting under-covered polygons by about an order of magnitude at an explicitly reported cost in guard count. We read this as evidence that the reinforcement-trained representation already encodes the geometry required for feasibility, and that residual failures reflect decoder calibration rather than missing knowledge. Probing a frozen encoder thus offers a practical way to ask what a neural combinatorial solver has internalized.

</details>


### [700] [The Two-Hump Problem: Bridging the Difficulty Gap in Mathematical Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21611)
*Lucas Fagan,Michele Tarquini,Ali Shehper,Maksymilian Manko,Angus Gruen,Coco Huang,Giorgi Butbaia,Davide Passaro,Sergei Gukov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper addresses the challenge of sparse rewards and vast search spaces in RL for mathematical search problems, specifically the Andrews-Curtis conjecture, by identifying a "Two-Hump" difficulty distribution, and proposes novel data generation techniques and algorithmic enhancements (supermoves, Transformer architectures) to bridge the gap, showing significant performance gains and releasing two large-scale benchmark datasets (AC-19 and AC-1M).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mathematical search problems like the Andrews-Curtis conjecture are hard for RL due to vast search spaces and sparse rewards; a key bottleneck is the "Two-Hump" distribution where instances are either trivial or impossible, with few hard-but-solvable cases needed for effective learning.

Method: Novel data generation techniques to populate the difficulty gap; algorithmic enhancements including supermoves and Transformer-based architectures.

Result: Substantial performance improvements over previous baselines; release of AC-19 (125,192 instances) and AC-1M (1,136,154 instances) datasets.

Conclusion: Addressing the data scarcity in the intermediate difficulty regime combined with better algorithms (supermoves, Transformers) significantly improves RL performance on mathematical search problems, and the new datasets enable further research.

Abstract: Mathematical search problems present a unique challenge for Reinforcement Learning (RL) due to vast search spaces and sparse rewards. In previous works, the Andrews-Curtis (AC) conjecture was established as an illustrative example of such problems. In this work, we identify a critical structural barrier in the AC landscape: a "Two-Hump" distribution, where problem instances are either trivially solvable or effectively impossible, with a scarcity of intermediate "hard-but-solvable" instances required for effective learning. We tackle this challenge through two primary avenues: novel data generation techniques to populate the difficulty gap, and significant algorithmic enhancements including the introduction of supermoves and Transformer-based architectures. We demonstrate substantial performance improvements over previous baselines, and release new comprehensive benchmark datasets including AC-19 (125,192 AC-trivial presentations of varying difficulty with length at most 19) and AC-1M (1,136,154 hard AC-trivial presentations of length at most 30), the first large-scale, publicly available datasets of this kind.

</details>


### [701] [HERALD: High-Throughput Block Diffusion LLM Serving via CPU-GPU Cooperative KV Cache Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21633)
*Omin Kwon,Doyeon Kim,Jongseok Park,Seung Yul Lee,Ion Stoica,Jae W. Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HERALD is a KV cache offloading system for block diffusion LLMs that reduces GPU memory pressure by selectively offloading KV entries to host DRAM, achieving up to 1.59x lower latency and 2.47x higher throughput with near-lossless accuracy at 5-10% KV budget.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion LLMs suffer from linearly growing KV cache with context length, limiting throughput at long contexts. While KV cache offloading to host DRAM can alleviate memory pressure, the limited PCIe bandwidth requires recalling only sparse KV entries. Existing approaches are expensive under offloading because they require exact attention over the full KV cache.

Method: HERALD exploits the property of block dLLMs where relevant KV entries remain consistent across denoising steps within a block. It amortizes selection overhead across the entire block by identifying top-k entries once and reusing them. The system reduces selection compute by a factor of the block size and overlaps selection with denoising steps.

Result: Across three block dLLMs and five long context tasks, HERALD achieves near-lossless accuracy at 5-10% KV budget, up to 1.59x lower per-block latency, and 2.47x higher throughput over GPU-only inference, with speedups growing with context length.

Conclusion: HERALD demonstrates that leveraging the unique properties of block diffusion LLMs (consistent KV relevance across denoising steps) enables efficient KV cache offloading that overcomes PCIe bandwidth limitations, making long-context inference more practical and scalable.

Abstract: Diffusion LLMs (dLLMs) improve GPU utilization over autoregressive decoding by generating multiple tokens per forward pass, but their KV cache still grows linearly with context, limiting throughput at long contexts. KV cache offloading to host DRAM alleviates this memory pressure, but the limited PCIe bandwidth necessitates recalling only a sparse subset of KV entries. In block dLLMs, the relevant KV entries remain consistent across denoising steps within a block, enabling high-accuracy selection by identifying the top-k entries once and reusing them throughout all denoising steps. This property appears attractive for offloading as it amortizes the selection overhead across the entire block, but it requires exact attention over the full KV cache, which is too expensive under offloading. We present HERALD, a KV offloading system for block dLLMs that resolves this through two opportunities that reduce the required selection compute by a factor of the block size and enable selection to be overlapped with denoising. Across three block dLLMs and five long context tasks, HERALD achieves near-lossless accuracy at 5-10% KV budget and up to 1.59x lower per block latency and 2.47x higher throughput over GPU-only inference, with speedups growing with context length.

</details>


### [702] [A new classification method based on Minimum Spanning Trees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21639)
*Julio González-Díaz,Beatriz Pateiro-López,Iria Rodríguez-Acevedo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a classification algorithm based on Minimum Spanning Trees, adapted from unsupervised learning to supervised learning, with a robust and computationally efficient version. It is validated through simulations and applied to aircraft trajectory classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To extend the use of Minimum Spanning Trees from unsupervised (clustering) to supervised learning (classification), addressing a gap in existing research.

Method: A classification algorithm based on Minimum Spanning Trees is proposed, along with a robust and computationally efficient version.

Result: The method is evaluated through extensive simulations and a real-world aircraft trajectory case study, showing effectiveness.

Conclusion: Minimum Spanning Trees can be effectively adapted for supervised classification tasks, with the robust variant offering improved performance and efficiency.

Abstract: Minimum Spanning Trees have been used in unsupervised learning, particularly in clustering tasks, due to their ability to recognize clusters by removing edges that are considered inconsistent in defining those clusters. This paper aims to study the use of Minimum Spanning Trees in supervised learning. Specifically, we propose a classification algorithm based on Minimum Spanning Trees. To improve its performance, we introduce a robust version of the method that is also computationally more efficient. We evaluate the effectiveness of our proposed method through an extensive simulation study. We also apply the proposed methodology to a real-world case study involving aircraft trajectories.

</details>


### [703] [When Is an LLM Worth It for Hyperparameter Optimization? A Budget-Matched Study on Tabular Data Finds the Warm-Start Is a Default Configuration, Not the Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21641)
*Carson Rodrigues,Oysturn Vas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper tests LLMs as HPO advisors and finds they provide no measurable benefit over classical methods when controlling for the default seed configuration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To critically evaluate the claim that LLMs can "warm-start" hyperparameter optimization with strong configurations from prior knowledge.

Method: Budget-matched, multi-seed protocol on 8 PMLB tabular benchmarks; compared LLM advisor (LLM-OptFlow) vs 4 classical baselines (random search, Optuna-TPE, GP-BO, successive halving) over a shared search space, with paired tests and bootstrap 95% CIs across 40 (task, seed) units.

Result: The strong first point is from a fixed default seed (88.7% mean best-CV), not LLM output. LLM proposals add only +0.40 pp CV accuracy and nothing on test (-0.01 pp, p=0.92). Classical search with the same seed ties or beats the advisor within 5-12 evaluations (+0.6 to +0.8 pp by 40 evaluations, p<=1e-4).

Conclusion: On tabular HPO, seeding classical search with a sensible default is sufficient; LLM advisors add no measurable generalization benefit and are quickly overtaken.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have been proposed as hyperparameter-optimization (HPO) advisors that "warm-start" search from prior knowledge, proposing strong configurations in very few evaluations. We test that claim under a budget-matched, multi-seed protocol on eight PMLB tabular benchmarks, comparing an LLM advisor (LLM-OptFlow) against four classical baselines (random search, Optuna-TPE, Gaussian-process Bayesian optimization, and successive halving) over one shared search space, with paired tests and bootstrap 95% CIs across 8 x 5 = 40 (task, seed) units. The finding is cautionary. The advisor's strong first point is not an LLM output at all: like prior LLM-HPO systems the loop is seeded with a fixed default configuration, evaluated before any model call, which alone reaches 88.7% mean best-CV, identical to within 0.01 pp across all seven advisor models tested. The LLM's own proposals add only +0.40 pp of cross-validation accuracy over that seed and nothing on held-out test (LLM-Default = -0.01 pp, p = 0.92). When the same seed is granted to classical search, the apparent lead collapses: against seeded random search it leads by +0.20 pp at 2 evaluations, is tied by 5, and is behind by 12 (-0.37 pp). Without the seed, classical search ties the advisor by 12 evaluations and beats it by 40 (+0.6 to +0.8 pp, p <= 1e-4). Two LLM-specific behaviors survive: a single-task exploration failure (vehicle), and a rule-based confidence filter that removes ~33% of wasted compute without changing accuracy. The recommendation is deflationary: on tabular HPO, seed classical search with a sensible default; an LLM advisor adds no measurable generalization benefit and is overtaken within a handful of evaluations. We release the harness and a script that reproduces every statistic.

</details>


### [704] [A Framework for Directed Acyclic Hypergraph Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21668)
*Zhiyuan Dong,Carlos Mundo-Levano,Wei Qian,Daniel Lau,Gonzalo R. Arce*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a continuous optimization framework for learning Directed Acyclic Hypergraphs (DAHGs) from observational data, capturing joint parental influences that pairwise DAG models cannot represent, using a tensor-based acyclicity constraint and generalized linear SEM with interaction terms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DAG learning methods are limited to pairwise causal relationships and cannot capture joint parental influences (e.g., interactions where multiple parents collectively cause an effect).

Method: (i) A generalized linear SEM with multiplicative interaction terms where non-zero weights map to directed hyperedges; (ii) a weighted adjacency tensor with acyclicity characterized via nilpotency under tensor t-product; (iii) a differentiable acyclicity constraint via Fourier decomposition of t-product, reducing tensor nilpotency to slice-wise matrix nilpotency, enabling augmented Lagrangian optimization.

Result: The method enables differentiable, continuous optimization for learning DAHGs, extending beyond pairwise causal discovery to capture higher-order joint parental effects.

Conclusion: The framework successfully extends continuous DAG learning to hypergraph structures, allowing modeling of complex multi-parent causal relationships through tensor algebra and differentiable constraints.

Abstract: Continuous optimization methods for learning Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) operate on weighted adjacency matrices and are therefore limited to pairwise causal relationships. We propose a framework for learning Directed Acyclic Hypergraphs (DAHGs) from observational data, capturing joint parental influences that pairwise models cannot represent. Our approach rests on three components: (i) a generalized linear structural equation model (SEM) with multiplicative interaction terms whose non-zero weights correspond one-to-one with directed hyperedges; (ii) a weighted adjacency tensor representation whose acyclicity is characterized via nilpotency under the tensor t-product; and (iii) a differentiable acyclicity constraint derived through the Fourier decomposition of the t-product, which reduces tensor nilpotency to slice-wise matrix nilpotency and enables least-squares learning via the augmented Lagrangian method.

</details>


### [705] [Decodable but Not Faithful: Coupling Natural-Language Rationales to Programmatic Verifiers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21678)
*Vatsal Ananthula,Adarsh Kumarappan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Consistency training makes verifier information decodable from model representations, but decodability does not guarantee faithful explanation generation—models can produce fluent yet unfaithful rationales even with high coupling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether training language models to produce consistent rationales (via verifier-coupled reasoning) actually ensures that those rationales faithfully represent the model's internal reasoning process.

Method: Proposes verifier-coupled reasoning: inserts inline claims into reasoning traces and trains an auxiliary consistency head to predict verifier outputs from rationale-span hidden states. Evaluated across LeanCheck (theorem proving), KataGo (Go engine commentary), and a code generation setting.

Result: Consistency training achieves high decodability (81% win-rate bucket accuracy in KataGo, 98.6% coupling in code), but generated explanations remain unfaithful (fluent prose with unrelated algorithms). Synthetic activation patching confirms causal influence (73-89% vs. 31% baseline), FEVER shows evidence-only pooling trades accuracy for sensitivity, and per-claim analysis shows fine-grained claims benefit more from consistency loss.

Conclusion: Consistency losses are effective as diagnostics and representation-shaping tools, but are not sufficient conditions for faithful reasoning.

Abstract: Language models can generate plausible rationales for their predictions, but these explanations may not faithfully represent the model's internal reasoning. We propose verifier-coupled reasoning, a framework that inserts inline claims into reasoning traces and trains an auxiliary consistency head to predict programmatic verifier outputs from rationale-span hidden states. The central finding is a gap between decodability and faithfulness: consistency training reliably makes verifier information decodable from rationale representations, but decodability does not guarantee faithful generation. In LeanCheck (formal theorem proving), rationale-only and proof-only pooling achieve perfect directional separation under counterfactual conflict. In KataGo (Go engine), commentary spans encode 10-way win-rate buckets at 81% accuracy. Yet in a code setting, the model achieves 98.6% coupling while its generated explanations remain unfaithful: fluent prose with correct structured claims, but describing unrelated algorithms; a controlled pretrained-vs-from-scratch comparison shows the gap is not capacity-driven. Synthetic activation patching confirms causal influence (73-89% vs. 31% baseline), FEVER reveals that evidence-only pooling isolates genuine evidence sensitivity at the cost of raw accuracy, and per-claim analysis shows that consistency loss disproportionately benefits fine-grained claims over binary ones. These results establish that consistency losses are effective diagnostics and representation-shaping tools, but not sufficient conditions for faithful reasoning.

</details>


### [706] [Expressivity Saturation: Reduced Affine Region Usage Under Increasing Task Complexity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21687)
*Xuan Qi,Yi Wei,Fanqi Yu,Manuel Lecha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies the gap between theoretical region capacity and realized affine regions in piecewise-affine neural networks, proving upper bounds on region counts along line-segment probes and introducing "expressivity saturation" — where increased task complexity leads to fewer actually realized regions despite unchanged architectural capacity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why theoretical expressive capacity (number of affine regions) often does not match the actual regions realized after training, especially under increasing task complexity.

Method: (1) A theoretical theorem deriving architecture-dependent upper bounds on affine pieces along affine line-segment probes; (2) Exact enumeration of realized affine regions in bounded 2D/higher-dimensional domains under controlled task complexity; (3) Visualization of training dynamics of region partitions and decision boundaries.

Result: The number of realized affine regions is markedly fewer under higher input-label complexity (expressivity saturation), despite unchanged worst-case capacity. Region-usage collapse often coincides with degraded decision boundaries in challenging regimes.

Conclusion: There is a systematic gap between theoretical and realized expressive capacity; increased task complexity saturates expressivity, and this collapse can degrade decision boundary quality.

Abstract: Piecewise-affine neural networks (e.g., with ReLU or LeakyReLU activations) implement continuous piecewise-affine maps, and the number of affine regions provides a natural proxy for expressive capacity. However, the gap between theoretical region capacity and the affine regions realized after training remains insufficiently understood. We study this gap from two complementary perspectives. First, we give a rigorous, architecture-dependent theorem for affine line-segment probes: for multilayer perceptrons with piecewise-affine activations, the number of affine pieces realized along an affine line-segment probe is upper bounded by an explicit product of layer-wise width terms (and activation breakpoint factors). This yields a neuron-threshold lower bound for representing target functions with prescribed one-dimensional piece complexity, formalizing the minimal region budget required for complex signals. Second, we exactly enumerate affine regions realized within bounded 2D and higher-dimensional domains under controlled task complexity. Under fixed architectures and training protocols, increasing input--label complexity yields trained solutions with markedly fewer realized regions in the evaluation domain, even though worst-case architectural capacity is unchanged; we call this reduced region usage expressivity saturation. Moreover, in the most challenging regimes, 2D visualizations show that region-usage collapse often coincides with degraded decision boundaries. Finally, we visualize the training dynamics of affine-region partitions and decision boundaries, revealing a consistent refinement process during optimization.

</details>


### [707] [Embedding Linear Equality Constraints in Probabilistic Neural Networks for Dynamic Modelling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21728)
*Matthew Marsh,Benoit Chachuat,Antonio del Rio Chanona*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A probabilistic neural network framework that enforces linear equality constraints (e.g., mass balances) with uncertainty quantification, showing better accuracy, calibration, and faster training than state-of-the-art methods on batch reactor case studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ML models for chemical processes lack uncertainty quantification and physical constraint enforcement, which are critical for reliability in safety-sensitive applications.

Method: A probabilistic neural network framework that guarantees linear equality constraints (within a tolerance) while capturing aleatoric uncertainty.

Result: Improved predictive accuracy, uncertainty calibration, and constraint adherence on reduced data; competitive performance with significantly faster training on large data regimes.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively combines uncertainty quantification with physical constraint satisfaction, offering a practical and efficient solution for chemical process modeling.

Abstract: Machine learning models are increasingly used to model chemical process systems, yet they often lack principled uncertainty quantification and mechanisms to enforce physical constraints. We propose a probabilistic neural network framework that guarantees satisfaction of linear equality constraints within a given tolerance, while capturing aleatoric uncertainty. Compared to state-of-the-art methods, our formulation demonstrates improved predictive accuracy, uncertainty calibration, and adherence to constraints on reduced data. It also demonstrates competitive performance, but with significantly faster training times when evaluated on large data regimes. We evaluated this on two batch reactor case studies, enforcing mass balances.

</details>


### [708] [AdaPrivate-TS: Private Thompson Sampling for Contextual Bandits with Privacy Amplification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21757)
*Mohammadreza Riyazat,Eranga Ukwatta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AdaPrivate-TS is a differentially private contextual bandit algorithm using Thompson Sampling with batched zCDP composition, achieving 93-99% of non-private performance at moderate privacy budgets by treating DP noise as structured uncertainty rather than corruption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable private online decision-making in contextual bandits where existing methods suffer from excessive noise corruption; the authors aim to show that DP noise can be reinterpreted as structured uncertainty, which Thompson Sampling can naturally handle.

Method: Combines Thompson Sampling with batched zCDP composition; adds Gaussian noise to sufficient statistics, which inflates posterior covariance in a structured way ($v^2 A^{-1} + \sigma^2 A^{-2}$); also explores privacy amplification via Poisson subsampling.

Result: Achieves 93-99% of non-private performance at $\varepsilon \in [0.5, 5]$; outperforms UCB by 0.5-3.7% and up to 18% at extreme $\varepsilon$; privacy amplification adds 2-5% gains at low $\varepsilon$; TS advantage over UCB grows to +11% under DP-SVD private features.

Conclusion: Treating DP noise as structured uncertainty is effective for private bandits; AdaPrivate-TS dominates baselines at $\varepsilon \geq 2$ on real-world datasets; the noise-as-uncertainty insight extends beyond reward privacy to feature privacy.

Abstract: We present AdaPrivate-TS, a differentially private contextual bandit algorithm that combines Thompson Sampling with batched zCDP composition. Our key insight is that differential privacy noise inflates the posterior covariance in a structured way: adding Gaussian noise $N(0,σ^2 I)$ to $b$ yields sampling covariance $v^2 A^{-1} + σ^2 A^{-2}$, which Thompson Sampling interprets as increased uncertainty rather than pure corruption. Under event-level privacy (protecting individual interactions) with stochastic contexts, we prove that the privacy cost is only $O(\sqrt{d}\,\log T/\sqrtρ)$, logarithmic in $T$, because parallel composition amortizes noise across batches. Additionally, we explore privacy amplification via Poisson subsampling, which can reduce effective noise at stringent privacy budgets. Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate: (1) AdaPrivate-TS achieves 93-99% of non-private performance at $\varepsilon \in [0.5, 5]$, outperforming UCB by 0.5-3.7% and up to 18% with tuned adaptive exploration at extreme $\varepsilon$; (2) privacy amplification provides additional 2-5% gains at low $\varepsilon$; (3) on MovieLens and Jester, AdaPrivate-TS achieves the best overall performance among event-level baselines, dominating at $\varepsilon \geq 2$; (4) under DP-SVD private features, TS's advantage over UCB grows to +11%, confirming noise-as-uncertainty is not limited to reward privacy. We provide rigorous proofs for privacy guarantees under interactive zCDP composition and comprehensive evaluation including convergence curves, 12-seed CIs, and DP-SVD feature ablation.

</details>


### [709] [Decision-Focused Learning: When and Why Traditional Prediction Models Fail](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21773)
*Mo Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A tutorial reviewing decision-focused learning (DFL) for the predict-then-optimize paradigm, emphasizing why standard statistical learning tools must be rethought for decision quality rather than predictive accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improved predictive accuracy does not generally translate into better decision quality, creating a disconnect that motivates the need for decision-focused learning approaches.

Method: Reviews DFL developments focusing on stochastic linear programming as the downstream problem, and critically examines why standard tools like uncertainty-driven data collection and Wasserstein distance are unsuitable.

Result: Identifies key distinctions between DFL and conventional predictive modeling, providing insights for developing new decision-focused tools.

Conclusion: Standard statistical learning tools must be fundamentally rethought for DFL; the paper offers guidance on building decision-focused alternatives.

Abstract: Plugging predictions of unknown parameters into downstream optimization problems, often referred to as the ``predict-then-optimize'' paradigm, has long been a standard approach in decision-making under uncertainty. However, improved predictive accuracy does not, in general, translate into improved decision quality. This disconnect has motivated growing interest in decision-focused learning (DFL) within the operations research community. This tutorial reviews recent developments in DFL and highlights key methodological insights, with a particular focus on stochastic linear programming as the downstream decision-making problem. We discuss why several widely used tools in traditional statistical learning are not directly suited to decision-focused settings and must be rethought, including (i) data collection strategies driven purely by predictive uncertainty and (ii) distributional distance measures such as the Wasserstein distance. We summarize properties of DFL that distinguish it from conventional predictive modeling and provide insights into the development of new decision-focused tools.

</details>


### [710] [Beyond the Next Step: Variable-Length Latent World Models for Long-Horizon Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21775)
*Tianqi Du,Qi Zhang,Yifei Wang,Yisen Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VLWMs learn to predict future latent states over variable-length action sequences, reducing compounding errors in long-horizon planning and achieving 13% improvement over SOTA LeWM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing latent world models rely on one-step prediction and recursive rollout for long-horizon planning, causing compounding errors and a mismatch between training objectives and downstream planning tasks.

Method: (1) A framework that predicts future latent states conditioned on action sequences of variable lengths. (2) A curriculum training strategy that progressively expands action horizon. (3) Tailored planning methods at test time to exploit variable-length predictive capabilities.

Result: 13% average improvement over LeWM across datasets, with especially large gains on tasks requiring extended planning.

Conclusion: VLWM provides a simple yet effective paradigm for improving long-horizon prediction and planning in latent world models.

Abstract: Recently, world models have emerged as a promising paradigm for building intelligent agents by learning predictive models that estimate future environment states conditioned on observations and actions. In particular, JEPA-style latent world models provide an efficient alternative to pixel space prediction by learning action-conditioned dynamics in compact representation spaces. However, existing latent world models typically rely on one-step prediction and must be recursively rolled out for long-horizon planning, which leads to compounding errors and a mismatch between training objectives and downstream planning tasks. To address this limitation, we propose Variable-length Latent World Models (VLWMs), a framework that learns to predict future latent states conditioned on action sequences of variable lengths. Instead of training only on one-step transitions, VLWMs directly model temporally extended dynamics, allowing the same predictor to evaluate action plans over different horizons. We further introduce a curriculum training strategy that progressively expands the action horizon, stabilizing optimization from short-range dynamics to long-range prediction. At test time, we design planning methods tailored to VLWMs to better exploit their variable-length predictive capabilities. Experiments on long-horizon control tasks show that VLWMs significantly improve latent space world models, achieving 13\% average improvement over the state-of-the-art LeWM across different datasets, with especially large gains on tasks requiring extended planning. These results suggest that VLWM provides a simple yet effective paradigm for improving long-horizon prediction and planning in latent world models.

</details>


### [711] [A Causal DAG Prior for Synthetic Time-Series Classification Datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21776)
*Franco Martino O'Rourke,Ana Trisovic,Dimitris Bertsimas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a causal prior for generating synthetic multivariate time-series classification (TSC) datasets with cross-modal temporal structure. By finetuning TabPFN v2.5 on this prior, they achieve significant improvements over the unmodified model and tabular-only baselines, demonstrating the value of cross-modal temporal dependencies in TSC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing synthetic priors fail to generate multivariate, multi-class TSC datasets with cross-modal causal structure across channels, timesteps, and labels. The authors aim to fill this gap to enable prior-data fitted networks (like TabPFN) to effectively handle time-series classification.

Method: They propose a causal prior that generates complete labeled TSC datasets by sampling from a random DAG over typed nodes (tabular attributes + time series), producing multivariate TSC with cross-modal structure. They finetune TabPFN v2.5 on this synthetic data with minimal adaptations.

Result: Finetuning on the proposed generator significantly outperforms both the unmodified upstream model and a tabular-only ablation of the same prior, with a Wilcoxon signed-rank p=3.0×10⁻⁸ on ROC-AUC across 75 UCR/UEA datasets.

Conclusion: The cross-modal temporal structure in the synthetic prior is crucial for improved TSC performance, as isolating this component (via the tabular-only ablation) yields substantially worse results. The approach works within TabPFN's operating regime.

Abstract: A Prior-data fitted Network learns the posterior predictive induced by its training prior; bringing this paradigm to multivariate time-series classification therefore calls for a synthetic generator that produces complete labelled datasets with temporal structure. We introduce a causal prior that synthesizes each dataset from a randomly sampled DAG over typed nodes across two modalities (tabular attributes and time series), natively producing multivariate, multi-class TSC datasets with cross-modal causal structure across channels, timesteps and labels, a regime not addressed by existing synthetic priors. To validate the prior, we finetune TabPFN v2.5 with minimal adaptations and evaluate on 75 UCR/UEA datasets within TabPFN's operating regime. Finetuning on our generator significantly outperforms both the unmodified upstream model and a tabular-only ablation of the same prior (Wilcoxon signed-rank $p=3.0\times 10^{-8}$ on ROC-AUC), isolating the contribution of the cross-modal temporal structure.

</details>


### [712] [RocketPFN: Accurate Time Series Classification via In-Context Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21786)
*Franco Martino O'Rourke,Ana Trisovic,Dimitris Bertsimas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RocketPFN combines Rocket feature extraction with TabPFN classifier to achieve state-of-the-art time series classification without training, matching HC2 accuracy (0.900) on 92 UCR datasets while being much simpler and faster.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a training-free time series classification pipeline that can serve as a strong reference point for evaluating zero-shot foundation models, while achieving competitive performance without learned parameters.

Method: Two-stage pipeline: (1) random convolutional feature extraction via Rocket, (2) in-context classification using pretrained TabPFN v2.5 tabular foundation model. No training on target data required.

Result: Matches HC2 accuracy (0.900) on 92 UCR datasets; significantly outperforms all individual HC2 ensemble classifiers, MOMENT, Mantis, and MantisV2 (p<0.001); median inference time 30s per fold.

Conclusion: RocketPFN is proposed as a zero-shot reference point for evaluating TSC foundation models, achieving competitive results without training or learned parameters.

Abstract: We introduce RocketPFN, a training-free pipeline for time series classification that combines random convolutional feature extraction (Rocket) with in-context classification via a pretrained tabular foundation model (TabPFN v2.5). On 92 UCR datasets (30-resample protocol), RocketPFN matches HC2, the strongest published method on the archive, in mean accuracy (both 0.900, Wilcoxon p=0.50), with no training on the target data and a median inference time of 30 seconds per fold. It also significantly outperforms every individual classifier in the HC2 ensemble. On UEA (20 datasets) the difference is likewise not statistically significant. A separate comparison concerns TSC foundation models: when paired with the same downstream classifier, MOMENT, Mantis, and MantisV2 are all significantly outperformed by RocketPFN using fewer extracted features and no learned parameters (p<0.001 in each case). This holds even when the encoders were pretrained on corpora that include the UCR training samples. We propose this two-stage pipeline as a reference point for evaluating zero-shot TSC foundation models.

</details>


### [713] [What Do Lorentz-Equivariant Jet Taggers Learn?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21790)
*Jay Agarwal,Siddharth Khare,Dhruv Kumar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper analyzes what Lorentz-equivariant jet taggers learn internally, finding they suppress frame-dependent pseudorapidity, encode jet mass and N-subjettiness strongly, and primarily use vector-like channels while bivector channels are negligible for top-quark tagging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand what internal representations and physical features Lorentz-equivariant jet taggers learn, and which algebraic grade structures carry discriminative information.

Method: Used equivariance tests, linear probes, and grade ablations across five models including L-GATr, L-GATr-slim, and LLoCa-T.

Result: Equivariant models suppress frame-dependent pseudorapidity to zero; encode jet mass and N-subjettiness strongly; bivector channels are negligible while vector-like channels dominate for top-quark tagging.

Conclusion: The network exploits multiple representational pathways, with vector-like channels being dominant but seed variable; findings may inform future development of equivariant taggers.

Abstract: We study what Lorentz-equivariant jet taggers learn internally, using equivariance tests, linear probes and grade ablations across five models including L-GATr, L-GATr-slim and LLoCa-T. Linear probes show that equivariant models suppress frame-dependent pseudorapidity to zero while encoding jet mass and N-subjettiness strongly. Grade ablations on L-GATr reveal that bivector channels are negligible for top-quark tagging while vector-like channels are dominant but seed variable, consistent with the network exploiting multiple representational pathways. These results characterize which physical features and algebraic grade structures carry discriminative information in equivariant taggers and may inform future development of such models.

</details>


### [714] [Discretizing Reward Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21795)
*Vijay Viswanathan,Shiqi Wang,Devamanyu Hazarika,Chirag Nagpal,Tongshuang Wu,Graham Neubig,Yuning Mao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reward models in RL are oversensitive—assigning different scores to equally good responses—which harms policy learning. The paper proposes evaluating reward models via discriminative ability and specificity, and introduces a training-free Monte Carlo dropout method to discretize rewards, reducing oversensitivity and improving policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reward models are widely used but poorly understood; continuous scoring is assumed beneficial but can be a weakness due to oversensitivity, leading to bad policies.

Method: A training-free algorithm using Monte Carlo dropout on neural reward models to produce discrete reward clusters, balancing discriminative ability and specificity.

Result: Theoretically, perfect reward models can be oversensitive; discretization reduces oversensitivity with minimal loss of discriminative ability. Empirically, discretized rewards reduce reward hacking and yield better policies in both controlled and natural RL settings.

Conclusion: Oversensitivity of reward models is a key weakness; evaluating specificity alongside discriminative ability is important, and discretizing rewards via Monte Carlo dropout offers a simple, effective fix.

Abstract: Despite their widespread use, the role of reward models in shaping reinforcement learning is poorly understood. Reward models offer a tempting promise: they automatically estimate response quality in the absence of verifiers or human judges. Unlike "verifiable rewards" which typically produce binary scores, reward models typically produce continuous scores, allowing them to be sensitive to fine-grained differences in responses. However, we show this apparent strength is a serious weakness: many popular reward models are oversensitive, assigning different scores to equally good responses. Theoretically, we show that seemingly perfect reward models can be highly oversensitive; empirically, this oversensitivity can lead to bad policies. In place of existing notions of "reward model accuracy," we propose evaluating reward models using distinct measures of "discriminative ability" and "specificity" (the complement of oversensitivity). As a solution, we describe a training-free algorithm that uses Monte Carlo dropout on any neural reward model to produce discrete reward clusters. Theoretically, we prove there exist discretizations that reduce oversensitivity at minimal expense of discriminative ability; empirically we show, in both controlled and natural RL settings, that discretizing rewards leads to less reward hacking and better policies than training on the original rewards.

</details>


### [715] [Causal Variational Deep Embedding: A Family of Interventional Generators for Confounded Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21806)
*Jingyuan Chen,Kangrui Ruan,Junzhe Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CauVaDE is a causal generative model that handles unobserved confounders by learning a feasible region (not a single point) of interventional distributions using a canonical augmented SCM with a discrete latent confounder and entropy regularization, enabling diverse and realistic controlled image generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep generative models inherit spurious associations from training data due to unobserved confounders that affect both controllable and response attributes. Existing causal approaches impose unrealistic structural assumptions to identify a single interventional distribution, but in image domains, multiple causal mechanisms are consistent with the data.

Method: CauVaDE uses a canonical augmented SCM where the unobserved confounder collapses into a discrete latent cluster with bounded support, while continuous variation is absorbed into independent noises. This is instantiated as a mixture VAE with an entropy regularizer (weight γ) on the cluster posterior to trace a family of candidate causal effects spanning the feasible region.

Result: Demonstrates that the canonical class is dense (in both observational and interventional Wasserstein distance) in the class of augmented SCMs. On image benchmarks, CauVaDE produces diverse interventional samples and improves FID against an unconfounded reference.

Conclusion: By relaxing the requirement for a unique causal mechanism and instead modeling the feasible region, CauVaDE offers a more practical and theoretically grounded approach for causal image generation under unobserved confounding, yielding both diversity and fidelity improvements.

Abstract: Deep generative models reproduce the observational distribution of their training data, inheriting any spurious associations it contains. A common source is an unobserved confounder that shapes both an attribute the user wants to control at sampling time and an attribute expected to vary in response. Existing causal generative approaches resolve the resulting ambiguity by imposing structural assumptions strong enough to single out one interventional distribution; in image domains, such assumptions are rarely warranted, and the data is generally consistent with a set of distinct causal mechanisms -- a feasible region of interventional distributions. We propose CauVaDE (Causal Variational Deep Embedding), built on a canonical augmented SCM in which the unobserved confounder collapses, without loss of generality, into a discrete latent cluster of bounded support while continuous variation is absorbed into independent noises. We prove that this canonical class is dense, in both observational and interventional Wasserstein distance, in the class of augmented SCMs compatible with a given causal diagram, and instantiate it as a mixture variational autoencoder whose cluster variable plays the role of the canonical confounder. An entropy regularizer with weight $γ$ on the cluster posterior then traces a family of candidate causal effects that fit the observational data to comparable likelihood while spanning the feasible region. Experiments on image data benchmarks show that CauVaDE produces diverse interventional samples and improves FID against an unconfounded reference.

</details>


### [716] [Causal Gaussian Processes for Robust Treatment Effect Evaluation with Unobserved Confounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21809)
*Junzhe Zhang,Jingyuan Chen,Elias Bareinboim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a method to evaluate causal effects in continuous domains with confounded observational data, requiring only temporal ordering between treatment and outcome. The authors develop a universal discretization of exogenous domains and propose Causal Gaussian process (CGP) models that can approximate any causal model's observational and interventional distributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Confounding bias hinders policy evaluation because causal effects are not identifiable from observational data. Existing robust strategies require detailed prior knowledge or apply only to discrete treatments/outcomes, creating a gap for continuous domain causal effect evaluation with limited assumptions.

Method: The authors introduce a universal discretization of exogenous domains that approximates any causal model's distributions with finite latent states. They then develop Causal Gaussian process (CGP) models that leverage this approximation property to handle confounded observations in continuous domains.

Result: The universal discretization achieves arbitrary accuracy in approximating observational and interventional distributions. The CGP models effectively approximate these distributions for confounded causal models in continuous domains.

Conclusion: The paper provides a novel approach for causal effect evaluation in continuous domains under confounding, requiring only temporal ordering as prior knowledge, thus relaxing the strong assumptions needed by prior methods.

Abstract: The presence of confounding bias poses a key challenge in policy evaluation, as the target causal effects of actions are not identifiable (i.e., underdetermined) from observational data. On the other hand, existing confounding-robust evaluation strategies require detailed prior knowledge about the environment or apply only to discrete treatments and outcomes. This paper investigates causal effect evaluation over the continuous domain from confounded observations, while requiring only basic temporal ordering between the treatment and the outcome.
