In this paper, we propose the Shift-Invariant Variance Estimator (SIVE), a variance-based local LLC probe that structurally eliminates the unknown additive baseline through the variance operator. Combining this shift-invariant observable with an explicit correction derived from the Law of Total Variance, SIVE separates geometric loss fluctuations from mini-batch evaluation noise. Controlled experiments on analytically tractable toy models show that SIVE recovers the expected finite-temperature geometric signal in regimes where anchored mean estimators fail. Applied to deep neural networks, SIVE provides a robust, localized online diagnostic for tracking structural phase transitions throughout training.

</details>


### [771] [QeHDC: Hyperdimensional Computing based on Quantum-enhanced binding and SuperClass Construction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22421)
*Yangjie Xu,Hui Huang,Li Ning,Radu State*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Quantum Hyperdimensional Computing (QHDC) framework with one-pass training, quantum encoding, reference-state-based quantum binding, and density-matrix superclass generation, showing superior performance and noise robustness on benchmark datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To extend classical Hyperdimensional Computing (HDC) by leveraging quantum mechanical properties to enhance computational efficiency and robustness for classification tasks.

Method: 1) One-pass training method using sinusoidal and quantum encoding to project classical data into quantum amplitude states. 2) Reference-state-based quantum binding operation realized via quantum circuits. 3) Density-matrix-based superclass generation using eigenvalue decomposition for feature extraction.

Result: Superior performance, robustness to noise, and computational feasibility compared to classical and existing quantum-enhanced approaches on standard benchmark datasets.

Conclusion: The proposed Quantum HDC framework shows practical benefits for quantum-enhanced classification tasks and paves the way for future quantum-inspired computational paradigms.

Abstract: Hyperdimensional Computing (HDC) is a robust computational framework inspired by human cognition characterized by simple and efficient operations within high-dimensional vector spaces. Quantum-enhanced Hyperdimensional Computing (QeHDC) extends classical HDC by leveraging quantum mechanical properties to enhance computational efficiency. In this paper, we propose a novel Quantum HDC framework featuring a one-pass training method, leveraging sinusoidal and quantum encoding to project classical data into quantum amplitude states efficiently. Our framework introduces an innovative reference-state-based quantum binding operation realized via quantum circuits. Furthermore, we propose a density-matrix-based superclass generation strategy employing eigenvalue decomposition to extract critical quantum state features effectively, enabling a more accurate and robust class representation. Experimental evaluations conducted on standard benchmark datasets demonstrate our approach's superior performance, robustness to noise, and computational feasibility compared to traditional classical and existing quantum-enhanced approaches. The results highlight the practical benefits and potential of Quantum HDC for quantum-enhanced classification tasks and pave the way for future advancements in quantum-inspired computational paradigms.

</details>


### [772] [Enhancing LLMs for Graph Tasks via Graph-aware LoRA Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22429)
*Junshu Sun,Wanxing Chang,Qingming Huang,Shuhui Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GaRA is a novel weight-level information injection paradigm that adapts language models to graph tasks by generating task-specific low-rank weight updates conditioned on whole-graph information, outperforming baselines on zero-shot graph learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GNNs lack transferability across datasets due to tightly coupled input-output parameters, while existing LM adaptation methods fail to encode whole-graph information, causing information loss and suboptimal graph understanding.

Method: Proposes a weight-level information injection paradigm that generates task-specific weight updates interacting with hidden representations, instantiated via LoRA as GaRA (Graph-aware LoRA generation) which conditions updates on original graph structures and constrains update norms.

Result: GaRA consistently outperforms baselines on zero-shot graph learning tasks.

Conclusion: The proposed weight-level injection paradigm effectively injects whole-graph information into LMs, improving graph task performance while avoiding optimization bias.

Abstract: Graph neural networks (GNNs) tightly couple their input-output parameters to dataset-specific feature spaces and target sets, exhibiting limited transferability across different datasets. In contrast, language models (LMs) generalize flexibly via a unified input-output interface, motivating recent attempts to adapt LMs to graph tasks. However, existing methods struggle to encode whole-graph information, leading to potential information loss and suboptimal graph understanding. In this work, we propose a novel weight-level information injection paradigm for adapting LMs to graph tasks. This paradigm injects whole-graph information by generating task-specific weight updates that interact directly with hidden representations. Instantiating this paradigm following low-rank adaptation (LoRA), we introduce GaRA, a Graph-aware LoRA generation model. GaRA constructs low-rank weight updates conditioned on the original graph structures and constrains the norm of the generated updates, thus injecting whole-graph information and avoiding the optimization bias in the weight generation. Empirical studies demonstrate that GaRA consistently outperforms baselines on zero-shot graph learning tasks.

</details>


### [773] [Escaping the Variance Trap: Jacobian-Free Dynamics for Root-Finding Bilevel Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22433)
*Zhiyu Li,Xi Xuan,Davide Carbone*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies the "Variance Trap" in converting stochastic root-finding problems (like entropy tuning in RL or GAN equilibration) into loss minimization, and proposes Root-Finding Bilevel Optimization (RF-BO) solved via Two-Time-Scale Stochastic Approximation (TTSA) to avoid variance amplification, achieving significant gains across multiple domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many core ML tasks (e.g., entropy tuning in RL, GAN training) are inherently stochastic root-finding problems but are incorrectly forced into a minimization framework via squared residuals, introducing a "Variance Trap" where implicit Jacobian estimation amplifies noise and destabilizes convergence.

Method: The authors formalize Root-Finding Bilevel Optimization (RF-BO) as a distinct problem class and propose a Jacobian-free Two-Time-Scale Stochastic Approximation (TTSA) algorithm that updates directly along the root error, structurally avoiding variance amplification. They provide non-asymptotic convergence guarantees under Markovian noise.

Result: RF-BO achieves 2.6% top-1 accuracy gain in SimCLR, 17× faster convergence in non-linear ODE control where baselines fail, improved entropy stability in RL, and 11.1% quality improvement in generative modeling.

Conclusion: The root-finding paradigm (RF-BO + TTSA) decisively outperforms squared-residual and implicit-gradient baselines across diverse tasks, offering a principled and practical alternative to misapplied minimization frameworks for stochastic root-finding problems.

Abstract: Many central machine learning tasks, from entropy tuning in reinforcement learning to equilibrating generative adversarial networks, are fundamentally stochastic root-finding problems rather than loss minimization. Yet, they are frequently forced into a minimization framework via squared residuals, introducing a critical flaw we identify as the Variance Trap. Standard bilevel minimization algorithms require estimating hypergradients involving implicit Jacobians; in stochastic settings, these terms act as noise amplifiers, destabilizing convergence. We formalize Root-Finding Bilevel Optimization (RF-BO) as a distinct problem class that bypasses this pathology. We propose a Jacobian-free solution using Two-Time-Scale Stochastic Approximation (TTSA) that updates directly along the root error, structurally avoiding variance amplification. We provide the first non-asymptotic convergence guarantees for TTSA in this setting under Markovian noise. Extensive experiments demonstrate the decisive advantage of this paradigm: compared to squared-residual and implicit-gradient baselines, our framework achieves a 2.6\% top-1 accuracy gain in SimCLR, 17$\times$ faster convergence in non-linear ODE control where baselines fail, significantly improved entropy stability in reinforcement learning, and an 11.1\% quality improvement in generative modeling.

</details>


### [774] [Distribution-Aware Robust Bilevel Optimization: Quantile-Guided Huber Updates in Two-Timescale Stochastic Approximation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22436)
*Zhiyu Li,Xi Xuan,Davide Carbone*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RQ-TTSA proposes a quantile-guided Huber-style clipping method for bilevel optimization under heavy-tailed noise, achieving stable convergence with theoretical guarantees and outperforming baselines across diverse tasks with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bilevel optimization suffers from instability under heavy-tailed stochastic noise; existing variance-reduction techniques rely on myopic magnitude checks that fail to distinguish informative geometric signals from impulsive outliers.

Method: RQ-TTSA (Robust Quantile-guided TTSA) uses historical gradient buffers to estimate rolling quantiles for adaptive Huber-style clipping, preserving local optimization geometry while bounding effective variance.

Result: Convergence rate of 𝒪(T^{-(p-1)/(3p-2)}) under infinite-variance noise (p∈(1,2]); outperforms SOTA baselines across six tasks (vision benchmarks, dynamic games, offline RL) with no divergence spikes and ~2.7% computational overhead.

Conclusion: Distribution-aware gradient control via quantile-guided clipping is a practical and necessary component for reliable bilevel learning, showing robustness to hyperparameter variations.

Abstract: Bilevel optimization (BLO) is fundamental to hierarchical decision-making but suffers from critical instability under heavy-tailed stochastic noise. Existing variance-reduction techniques typically rely on myopic magnitude checks, which fail to distinguish informative geometric signals from impulsive outliers. To resolve this, we propose \textbf{RQ-TTSA} (Robust Quantile-guided TTSA), a distribution-aware framework that leverages historical gradient buffers to estimate rolling quantiles for adaptive Huber-style clipping, effectively preserving local optimization geometry while strictly bounding effective variance. Theoretically, we provide a convergence analysis for quantile-guided TTSA under nonconvex-strongly convex assumptions with infinite-variance noise ($p \in (1,2]$), deriving a rate of $\mathcal{O}(T^{-\frac{p-1}{3p-2}})$ that recovers optimal dependence on the heavy-tailed parameter. Empirically, across six diverse tasks, spanning heterogeneous vision benchmarks, dynamic games under momentum poisoning, and offline reinforcement learning, RQ-TTSA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by eliminating divergence spikes and ensuring stable convergence. Our method demonstrates significant robustness to hyperparameter variations and incurs negligible computational overhead ($\approx 2.7\%$ increase), validating distribution-aware gradient control as a practical and necessary component for reliable bilevel learning.

</details>


### [775] [Adaptive Recurrent Message Passing for Test Time Computing on Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22462)
*Junshu Sun,Wanxing Chang,Qingming Huang,Shuhui Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes AdaR, an adaptive recurrent graph model that overcomes the mismatch between fixed architectures and graph data by enabling flexible test-time computation without changing parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pre-trained foundation models struggle to generalize to graph learning due to the intrinsic mismatch between graph data and fixed architectural designs.

Method: AdaR is an adaptive recurrent graph model that encodes normalized step information and representation-target relations, uses gradient-based supervision for convergence, and is built on a theoretical analysis establishing step dependence as necessary and sufficient for adaptive convergence.

Result: AdaR consistently outperforms strong baselines in both inductive and transductive settings.

Conclusion: Recurrent graph models with adaptive inference can overcome the architectural limitations of fixed backbones, enabling flexible test-time computation for graph learning.

Abstract: Pre-trained foundation models have demonstrated remarkable success in many domains, enabling a unified backbone to generalize across diverse downstream tasks. However, extending this paradigm to graph learning remains challenging due to the intrinsic mismatch between graph data and fixed architectural designs. In this work, we show that this limitation can be overcome via recurrent graph models. To achieve this, we conduct a systematic theoretical analysis, rigorously deriving step dependence as a necessary and sufficient condition for an adaptively convergent recurrent process. Building on this foundation, we propose AdaR, an Adaptive Recurrent graph model, empowering flexible test-time computing on various downstream tasks without changing model parameters. To enable adaptive inference, AdaR explicitly encodes normalized step information and representation-target relations into the recurrent updates. To ensure convergence of the recurrent process, AdaR employs gradient-based supervision signals that guide representation updates throughout the recurrence. Empirical results demonstrate that AdaR consistently outperforms strong baselines in both inductive and transductive settings.

</details>


### [776] [Federated learning with heavy-tailed gradient noise and communication noise: a variance-reduction based algorithm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22466)
*Shengchao Zhao,Yongchao Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VRA-FedSGD is a variance-reduction federated learning algorithm that handles both heavy-tailed gradient noise and communication noise, achieving theoretical convergence guarantees for nonconvex (mean sense) and strongly convex (almost sure sense) objectives, with experimental validation on logistic regression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing FL algorithms suffer from heavy-tailed gradient noise and communication noise commonly found in wireless networks and IoT deployments, which degrade convergence and stability. There is a need for robust algorithms that can handle such noise distributions.

Method: VRA-FedSGD combines momentum variance reduction with nonlinear mapping to mitigate heavy-tailed gradient noise, and uses a variance-reduced aggregation mechanism to suppress heavy-tailed communication noise.

Result: Achieves $\mathcal{O}\left(K^{-(p-1)/(2p-1)}\right)$ convergence rate in mean sense for nonconvex objectives, and $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}\left(K^{-(1-1/(p-\epsilon))}\right)$ almost sure convergence rate for strongly convex objectives. Experiments on logistic regression confirm effectiveness.

Conclusion: VRA-FedSGD is an effective FL algorithm robust to both heavy-tailed gradient and communication noises, with solid theoretical convergence guarantees validated by experiments.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) is an emerging distributed machine learning paradigm that enables local devices to jointly train a global model while keeping data decentralized and private. We propose a variance-reduction based algorithm, VRA-FedSGD, for FL in the presence of heavy-tailed gradient noise and communication noise, where these noises are prevalent in large-scale machine learning over wireless networks and Internet of Things deployments. VRA-FedSGD employs a momentum variance reduction technique together with a nonlinear mapping to mitigate heavy-tailed gradient noise, and uses a variance-reduced aggregation mechanism to suppress heavy-tailed communication noise. In the mean sense, VRA-FedSGD achieves a convergence rate of {\small$\mathcal{O}\left(K^{-(p-1)/(2p-1)}\right)$} for nonconvex objective functions, where $p$ is the tail index of heavy-tailed noise. In the almost sure sense, VRA-FedSGD achieves a convergence rate of $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}\left(K^{-(1-1/(p-ε))}\right)$ for strongly convex objective functions, where $ε$ is an arbitrarily small constant. Simulated experiments on a logistic regression problem with real-world data verify the effectiveness of VRA-FedSGD.

</details>


### [777] [Learning Process Rewards via Success Visitation Matching for Efficient RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23640)
*Raymond Tsao,Andrew Wagenmaker,Sergey Levine*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a method to convert sparse outcome rewards into dense process rewards for RL by training a discriminator to distinguish successful vs. unsuccessful episodes, providing dense feedback that speeds up policy finetuning without changing the optimal policy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse rewards in RL (only +1 at task completion, 0 otherwise) create a challenging credit assignment problem, leading to slow or ineffective learning.

Method: Train a discriminator to differentiate between successful and unsuccessful episodes, then use it to reward the policy for matching state-action visitations of successful trajectories while avoiding those of unsuccessful ones.

Result: Significantly faster RL finetuning performance on both simulated and real-world robotic manipulation tasks compared to maximizing sparse outcome rewards directly.

Conclusion: The proposed dense reward approach provably preserves the optimal policy while providing dense feedback, enabling more efficient learning.

Abstract: In many modern applications of reinforcement learning (RL), the natural reward for a task of interest is inherently sparse: a reward of 0 is given everywhere except when the task is completed, when a reward of +1 is given. Training a policy to maximize such a sparse reward requires solving a challenging credit assignment problem, leading to slow or ineffective RL improvement. We propose a simple approach to transform a sparse outcome reward into a dense process reward. Our approach relies on training a discriminator to distinguish between previous successful and unsuccessful episodes, and using this discriminator to incentivize the RL-learned policy to match the state-action visitations of successful episodes, while avoiding those of unsuccessful episodes. By incentivizing the policy to match the visitations over all states, not just those that correspond to task success, this reward provides dense feedback on whether progress is being made towards task completion, and, we show, provably achieves this without changing the optimal policy. Focusing on finetuning of robotic control policies, we demonstrate that our approach leads to significantly faster RL finetuning performance on both simulated and real-world manipulation tasks, as compared to simply maximizing the sparse outcome reward.

</details>


### [778] [Fed-CausalDiff: Decoupled Synchronization for Federated Do-Simulation and Policy Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22510)
*Pengfei Li,Mohammad Khalil*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fed-CausalDiff is a federated causal diffusion framework for do-simulation that decomposes latent state evolution into global causal and local confounding score functions, enabling decoupled synchronization where clients share causal mechanisms while keeping site-specific confounders local.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard federated learning methods are purely observational and insufficient for interventional inference and policy evaluation because sequential actions dynamically alter future states.

Method: The framework decomposes latent state evolution into a global causal score function and a local confounding score function, using decoupled synchronization (DSS) where clients aggregate shared causal mechanisms while retaining site-specific confounders locally.

Result: Fed-CausalDiff achieves better ATE and policy-value estimation accuracy on four datasets, with a favorable trade-off between communication cost and inference fidelity.

Conclusion: The proposed approach effectively handles data heterogeneity in federated settings for causal inference tasks.

Abstract: While federated learning enables collaborative modelling on decentralised data, standard methods merely fit historical observations. This purely observational approach is fundamentally insufficient for interventional inference and policy evaluation, as sequential actions dynamically alter future states. We propose \textbf{Fed-CausalDiff}, a federated causal diffusion framework for do-simulation. The architecture decomposes the evolution of the latent state into a global causal score function and a local confounding score function. This design enables \emph{decoupled synchronisation} (DSS), where clients aggregate only the shared causal mechanism while retaining site-specific confounders locally to handle heterogeneity. Experiments on four datasets demonstrate that Fed-CausalDiff achieves better ATE and policy-value estimation accuracy, offering a favorable trade-off between communication cost and inference fidelity.

</details>


### [779] [The Scissors Effect: When Resize-Based Input Diversity Helps or Hurts Transfer Attacks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22516)
*Yuhang Jiang,Xiaojing Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Input Diversity (DI) helps transfer-based adversarial attacks from standard surrogates but harms them from robustly trained surrogates, a pattern called the Scissors Effect; traced to gradient geometry (resize bias), and a training-free rule (CG-DI) avoids the loss while retaining the benefit.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Input Diversity (DI) is widely assumed to improve transferability in transfer-based adversarial attacks, but the paper questions whether this assumption holds universally, especially for robustly trained surrogates.

Method: Systematically varies the DI probability and surrogate robustness (standard vs. robust) on ImageNet and CIFAR-10; measures attack success rates across diverse targets (CNN, ViT, Swin, ConvNeXt) and attacks (2018-2024); uses gradient geometry analysis (resize/translation decomposition, source-target gradient alignment) and introduces Local Gradient Consistency (LGC) as a diagnostic probe; proves a bias-variance crossover theorem and proposes a training-free rule CG-DI.

Result: On ImageNet, robust surrogates lose 10.3% attack success with blind DI; the harm is graded and appears already at low robustness; ~67% of the harm is attributed to resize; LGC separates the two surrogate types; CG-DI avoids the loss on robust surrogates while keeping DI's benefit on standard ones.

Conclusion: The Scissors Effect is a DI-specific manifestation of the broader robustness-transferability trade-off; the proposed CG-DI rule offers a practical remedy without training.

Abstract: Input Diversity (DI), which applies random resizing and padding at each attack iteration, is a near-default ingredient of transfer-based adversarial attacks, widely assumed to improve transferability. We show this assumption is regime-dependent and, for robustly trained surrogates, often reversed. Varying only the surrogate, increasing the DI probability raises transfer success for standard surrogates but lowers it for robust ones: the two response curves separate like a pair of scissors, a pattern we call the Scissors Effect. The effect is strong and consistent on ImageNet, where blind DI costs the robust source 10.3% attack success on average across CNN, ViT, Swin, and ConvNeXt targets and across ten attacks spanning 2018-2024; it is smaller on CIFAR-10 unless DI is made aggressive. A controlled robustness-strength sweep that varies only the training budget shows the harm is graded rather than binary, crossing from beneficial to harmful already in the little-robustness regime. We trace it to gradient geometry: a resize/translation decomposition attributes roughly 67% of the harm to resize, and a direct source-target gradient-alignment measurement confirms the same resize operation improves alignment for standard surrogates but degrades it for robust ones. We summarize the regime with Local Gradient Consistency (LGC), a single input-space probe that separates the two surrogate types, and prove a bias-variance crossover theorem isolating where DI helps from where its resize bias dominates. A training-free rule (CG-DI) that disables diversity when LGC is high avoids the loss on robust surrogates while keeping DI's benefit on standard ones, positioning the Scissors Effect as a DI-specific manifestation of the broader robustness-transferability trade-off.

</details>


### [780] [Generative Robust Optimisation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22536)
*Yuhui Yin,Vassilis M. Charitopoulos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Generative Robust Optimisation (GRO), a framework using deep generative models (specifically a Wasserstein Adversarial Autoencoder) to define uncertainty sets for robust optimisation, capturing complex real-world data dependencies. It proposes a five-point evaluation framework and validates the approach through extensive experiments on production planning and facility location problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical robust optimisation uses fixed geometric uncertainty sets (e.g., ellipsoids, boxes) that cannot capture complex, real-world data dependencies like nonlinear correlations, asymmetry, and multimodality.

Method: GRO defines uncertainty sets as the image of a neural network decoder over a calibrated latent set. It uses a Wasserstein Adversarial Autoencoder with GMM-guided training for latent regularity and constraint-consistency regularisation. Decoder uses ReLU activations for exact worst-case verification via mixed-integer programming.

Result: Extensive experiments on production planning (six uncertainty distributions, six generative architectures) and multi-period facility location show that systematic attention to all five criteria yields expressive, well-calibrated, and optimisation-tractable uncertainty sets.

Conclusion: The GRO framework with the proposed five-point evaluation criteria enables neural network-based uncertainty sets that are simultaneously expressive, well-calibrated, and computationally tractable for robust optimisation.

Abstract: Classical uncertainty sets for robust optimisation impose fixed geometric shapes that cannot represent the complex dependencies present in real-world data. We propose Generative Robust Optimisation (GRO), a framework in which a deep generative model defines the uncertainty set as the image of a neural network decoder over a calibrated latent set, naturally accommodating nonlinear correlations, asymmetry, and multimodality. A five-point evaluation framework (reconstruction fidelity, distribution matching, latent regularity, robust relevance, and computational tractability) provides systematic, model-agnostic criteria for assessing any neural network-based uncertainty set. We instantiate this framework with a Wasserstein Adversarial Autoencoder employing Gaussian mixture model-guided training for latent regularity and constraint-consistency regularisation for robust relevance. Restricting the decoder to ReLU activations enables exact worst-case verification through mixed-integer programming embedding. Extensive experiments on a production planning problem across six uncertainty distributions and six generative architectures, together with a multi-period facility location study, validate the framework and demonstrate that systematic attention to all five criteria yields uncertainty sets that are simultaneously expressive, well-calibrated, and optimisation-tractable.

</details>


### [781] [Mitigating Measurement-Induced Training Instability in Hybrid Quantum Neural Networks for Protein Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22551)
*Milton Mondal,Sushovan Chanda,Mohamad Mahdi Alawieh,Brijesh Sukhadiya,Donatus Krah,Clinton Gonsalves,Antonios Ntolkeras,Silvio O. Rizzoli,Ali H. Shaib*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies "measurement-induced logit contraction" as a trainability issue in hybrid Quantum Neural Networks (QNNs) where bounded quantum measurement outputs ([-1,1]) cause weak loss sensitivity and suppressed gradients. To fix this, they propose a learnable scaling parameter called Quantum Measurement Temperature (QMT) that rescales outputs before loss computation, improving gradient magnitude and classification accuracy without modifying the quantum architecture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hybrid QNN classifiers produce bounded logits from quantum measurements, which cause the cross-entropy loss to operate in a regime of weak sensitivity to logit differences, leading to suppressed gradients and unstable optimization in variational quantum classifiers.

Method: Introduces a learnable scaling parameter (Quantum Measurement Temperature, QMT) that rescales quantum measurement outputs before applying the loss function, compensating for the physical bounds on quantum measurements.

Result: Experiments on fluorescence microscopy images and six-class Fashion MNIST show that QMT improves logit separation, strengthens gradients, stabilizes training across random initializations, and enhances classification accuracy compared to unscaled measurements.

Conclusion: QMT enables stable and reliable training of hybrid QNNs for practical applications by mitigating measurement-induced logit contraction, and is architecture-agnostic without modifying the quantum ansatz or circuit depth.

Abstract: Hybrid Quantum Neural Network (QNN) classifiers produce logits as expectation values of quantum measurement operators. For standard Pauli measurements, these outputs are intrinsically bounded to the interval [-1,1]. When such bounded logits are used directly with the cross-entropy loss applied to softmax-normalized logits for multi-class classification, the loss function operates in a regime of weak sensitivity to logit differences. As a consequence, parameter gradients are suppressed, leading to unstable optimization in variational quantum classifiers (VQCs). In this work, we identify this effect as measurement-induced logit contraction, a previously uncharacterized source of trainability degradation in hybrid QNNs. To address this limitation, we introduce a learnable scaling parameter, termed Quantum Measurement Temperature (QMT), which rescales quantum measurement outputs prior to the loss. Unlike post-hoc calibration, QMT acts during training and compensates for the physically imposed bounds on quantum measurement outputs. This rescaling increases gradient magnitude and variance, thereby improving loss sensitivity. The proposed mechanism is architecture-agnostic and does not modify the quantum ansatz, circuit depth, or measurement operators. Experiments on fluorescence microscopy images and a six-class variant of Fashion MNIST demonstrate that QMT consistently enhances logit separation, strengthens gradients, stabilizes training across random initializations, and improves classification accuracy, relative to unscaled measurement readouts. These results demonstrate that QMT enables stable and reliable training of hybrid QNNs for practical applications.

</details>


### [782] [Concept-Constrained Prompt Learning for Few-Shot CLIP Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22567)
*Na Sang,Ding Ma,Rui Sang,Yuxuan Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CCPL is a lightweight regularization method that anchors learnable class prompts to frozen concept prototypes to improve CLIP's base-to-new class generalization, showing gains on DTD and EuroSAT but neutral on OxfordPets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Class-only prompt optimization in few-shot prompt learning overfits to base-class supervision and weakens transfer to unseen classes.

Method: CCPL learns shared context tokens, constructs frozen concept prototypes from a class-level concept bank, uses text-space cosine consistency loss and concept dropout for regularization, and optionally fuses class-prompt logits with concept-prototype logits at inference.

Result: CCPL improves base-to-new harmonic mean on DTD (+0.6) and EuroSAT (+2.9) over CoOp, while near-neutral on OxfordPets (-0.1). Text-space concept regularization is consistently beneficial; optimal inference fusion strength is dataset-dependent.

Conclusion: Concept constraints are most effective when concept prototypes align well with dataset semantics; fine-grained categories remain a boundary condition.

Abstract: Few-shot prompt learning is an effective strategy for adapting CLIP to downstream tasks, but class-only prompt optimization can overfit base-class supervision and weaken transfer to unseen classes. We propose Concept-Constrained Prompt Learning (CCPL), a lightweight regularization framework that anchors learnable class prompts to frozen concept-level text prototypes without updating CLIP encoders. CCPL learns a set of shared context tokens, instantiates class prompts by appending class names, and constructs frozen concept prototypes from a class-level concept bank. During training, a text-space cosine consistency objective aligns learnable class-prompt embeddings with frozen concept prototypes; concept dropout provides additional regularization against over-reliance on fixed concept lists. At inference, CCPL optionally fuses class-prompt logits with concept-prototype logits using a controllable ensemble weight alpha. Our default configuration uses text-space concept regularization lambda = 0.5, concept dropout p = 0.3 and weak concept-guided fusion (alpha = 0.1), with no KL-based prediction consistency term. Experiments under identical automatically-generated fallback splits show that CCPL improves the base-to-new harmonic mean on DTD (+0.6) and EuroSAT (+2.9) compared with CoOp, while remaining near-neutral on OxfordPets (-0.1). Ablations indicate that text-space concept regularization is consistently beneficial, while the best concept-guided inference strength is dataset- and protocol-sensitive. These results suggest concept constraints are most effective when concept prototypes align naturally with dataset semantics, and identify fine-grained categories as a current boundary condition. The code is released at: https://github.com/richael-sang/concept-constrained-prompt-learning.

</details>


### [783] [Stationary Robust Mean-Field Games under Model Mismatches](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22579)
*Yue Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a robust mean-field game framework for multi-agent reinforcement learning that handles model mismatches between training simulators and real environments by incorporating distributional uncertainty into population-coupled dynamics, proving equilibrium existence, convergence guarantees, and finite-population approximation bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world deployment of MARL suffers from model mismatches between training simulators and true environments, which are amplified through strategic interactions, causing severe performance degradation. Standard robust MARL becomes intractable as agent count grows.

Method: The authors develop an infinite-horizon, stationary mean-field game framework that incorporates distributional model uncertainty into population-coupled dynamics. They establish a robust dynamic programming principle with a contractive Bellman operator and prove stationary robust mean-field equilibrium existence via fixed-point argument. They develop the first convergent algorithm and connect the mean-field solution to finite-population robust games.

Result: The paper proves existence of stationary robust mean-field equilibrium, provides the first convergent algorithm, shows mean-field equilibrium induces approximate equilibrium in finite populations with explicit non-asymptotic error bounds under contractive robust-dynamics regime, and validates findings with numerical experiments.

Conclusion: The robust mean-field game framework effectively addresses scalability issues in robust MARL while providing theoretical guarantees on equilibrium existence, algorithm convergence, and finite-population approximation, making it viable for real-world deployment.

Abstract: Deploying multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) in the real world is often limited by model mismatches between the training simulators and the true environment, which could be further amplified through strategic interactions and result in severe performance degradation upon deployment. Distributional robustness offers a principled response by optimizing policies against worst-case transition models drawn from an uncertainty set, but standard robust MARL frameworks become increasingly intractable as the number of agents grows. This paper develops an infinite-horizon, stationary mean-field game framework that incorporates distributional model uncertainty directly into the population-coupled dynamics. We establish a robust dynamic programming principle with a contractive Bellman operator and prove the existence of a stationary robust mean-field equilibrium via a fixed-point argument. We further develop the first concrete algorithm with convergence guarantees. We then connect the mean-field solution to a finite-population robust game whose ambiguity sets depend on the empirical distribution, showing that the mean-field equilibrium policy induces approximate equilibrium behavior as the population size increases. Under a contractive robust-dynamics regime, we further obtain explicit non-asymptotic error bounds. Numerical experiments further illustrate the qualitative and quantitative impact of robustness under multiple uncertainty models, validating our theoretical findings.

</details>


### [784] [Training-free Task Classification for Multi-Task Model Merging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22589)
*Jungyong Son,Jinwook Jung,Sungyong Baik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SiM proposes a training-free, task-ID-free dynamic model merging method that routes test inputs to task-relevant parameters by computing projection residuals onto SVD-based low-rank task manifolds, using only a tiny support set (e.g., 32 examples per task) computed offline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dynamic model merging methods require either additional router training with abundant labeled data or assume access to task IDs at inference time, both of which are restrictive. The goal is to close the performance gap to individual task experts without these requirements.

Method: SiM formulates routing as training-free task classification. It uses SVD-based low-rank manifold approximations per task, computes projection residuals of test input features onto each task manifold, routes the input to the task with the smallest residual, and integrates with subspace-/mask-based merging for efficiency.

Result: Experiments across CV and NLP benchmarks under task-unknown inference show SiM substantially improves merged-model performance and consistently narrows the gap to individual task experts.

Conclusion: SiM effectively bridges the gap to expert performance for multi-task model merging without requiring router training or task-ID access, offering a practical and efficient solution for dynamic model merging.

Abstract: Ever since the advent of foundation models and the pre-training-finetuning paradigm, there have been numerous efforts to merge multiple task-specific experts into a single multi-task model. Prior work largely focuses on finding a single merged model, but it often underperforms individual experts due to parameter interference. To resolve this, dynamic model merging employs routing to activate task-relevant parameters per input. However, existing routers typically require either additional training with abundant labeled datasets or assume the access to task IDs of each input at inference time. In this work, we aim to close the gap to expert performance without additional training or task-ID-access assumption. To this end, we formulate routing as training-free task classification for each test input. Using singular value decomposition (SVD)-based low-rank manifold approximations for each task, SiM scores tasks by the projection residual of the test input feature onto each task manifold and routes accordingly. The task manifolds are pre-computable offline from a pretrained backbone using a small per-task support set (e.g., 32 examples per task) prior to merging process, requiring no router training and no data during the merging process. Moreover, SiM integrates seamlessly with subspace-/mask-based merging that represents task-expert via lightweight compressed task vectors, avoiding the need to store full expert parameters. Experiments across computer vision and natural language processing benchmarks under task-unknown inference demonstrate that SiM substantially improves merged-model performance and consistently narrows the gap to individual task experts.

</details>


### [785] [On the Position Bias of On-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22600)
*Yan Xie,Sijie Zhu,Tiansheng Wen,Bo Chen,Yifei Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: On-Policy Distillation (OPD) treats all tokens equally, but later tokens have lower supervision quality due to distribution shift. The authors propose Importance-Weighted On-Policy Distillation (IW-OPD) that reweights tokens based on accumulated discrepancy, leading to faster convergence and up to 6.9 points improvement on AIME-2025.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard OPD uniformly averages token-level losses, but not all tokens contribute equally—later tokens in student rollouts deviate further from the teacher's distribution, degrading supervision quality. Using only first 30% tokens matches full performance, while last 30% barely helps.

Method: The authors frame the issue through constrained optimization and derive IW-OPD, where each token's weight depends on accumulated distribution discrepancy between student and teacher, naturally upweighting earlier tokens and downweighting later ones.

Result: IW-OPD converges significantly faster than OPD, achieves better learning efficiency, and improves final performance up to 6.9 points on AIME-2025 in both same-size and cross-scale settings.

Conclusion: Token-level weighting based on distribution discrepancy is an effective and principled improvement over uniform weighting in on-policy distillation, addressing the supervision quality degradation at later positions.

Abstract: On-Policy Distillation (OPD) improves the learning efficiency of standard reinforcement learning through dense, token-level supervision from teachers. In the standard KL objective of OPD, token-level losses are uniformly averaged, implying equal weights for all tokens. However, we discover that not all tokens are created equal: as student rollouts grow longer, they deviate further from the teacher's distribution, leading to degraded supervision quality at later positions. As a result, OPD using only the first 30% of tokens can perform comparably to using all tokens, whereas OPD using only the last 30% of tokens barely learns anything. In this work, we provide a principled understanding of this issue through the lens of constrained optimization. Based on these insights, we derive Importance-Weighted On-Policy Distillation (IW-OPD), in which the weight assigned to each token depends on the accumulated discrepancy between the student's and teacher's distributions, naturally upweighting earlier tokens and downweighting later ones with larger deviations. We show that IW-OPD converges significantly faster than OPD, with better learning efficiency, and achieves better final performance than standard OPD in both same-size and cross-scale settings, improving performance up to 6.9 points on AIME-2025.

</details>


### [786] [Federated Learning for Global Carbon Emission Forecasting: A Hybrid Time-Series Approach with Statistical and Neural Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22618)
*Attia Qammar,Qazi Haseeb Yousaf,Ali Azam,Ammar Ahmed,Abdenacer Naouri,Tianrui Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a federated hybrid forecasting framework combining ARIMA, GARCH, LSTM-Attention, and XGBoost for privacy-preserving collaborative carbon emission forecasting across distributed clients.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing carbon emission forecasting relies on centralized data collection, which is restricted by privacy regulations and the distributed nature of emission data across countries and sectors.

Method: A federated hybrid framework integrating ARIMA (trend), GARCH (volatility), LSTM-Attention (temporal representation), and XGBoost (prediction) within a privacy-preserving federated learning environment.

Result: Evaluated on 14 clients: R² 0.50-0.97 (avg 0.73), RMSE 0.06-2.35 (avg 1.21), MAPE 1.5%-11.3% (avg 6.5%).

Conclusion: The framework provides an accurate, scalable, and regulation-compliant solution for collaborative carbon-emission forecasting.

Abstract: Climate change, primarily driven by carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions, requires accurate forecasting tools to support effective mitigation policies and sustainable development strategies. Existing forecasting approaches typically rely on centralized data collection, which is often restricted by privacy regulations and the distributed nature of emission data across countries and industrial sectors. This paper proposes a novel federated hybrid forecasting framework that integrates ARIMA-based trend modeling, GARCH-based volatility modeling, LSTM-Attention temporal representation learning, and XGBoost prediction within a privacy-preserving federated learning environment. The proposed framework enables collaborative learning among distributed clients without requiring the exchange of raw data. Experimental evaluation across 14 clients demonstrates strong forecasting performance, achieving client R2 values between 0.50 and 0.97 with an average of 0.73, RMSE values ranging from 0.06 to 2.35 with an average of 1.21, and MAPE values between 1.5% and 11.3% with an average of 6.5%. The results indicate that the proposed framework provides an accurate, scalable, and regulation-compliant solution for collaborative carbon-emission forecasting.

</details>


### [787] [Scalable Maximum Entropy Reinforcement Learning for Diffusion Policies via Adjoint Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22630)
*Serge Thilges,Onur Celik,Denis Blessing,Emiliyan Gospodinov,Gerhard Neumann*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces an efficient algorithm for optimizing diffusion policies in online RL using adjoint matching, enabling simulation-free training with reduced computational overhead while achieving competitive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion policies are powerful for complex action distributions in RL but lack scalable training methods for online RL without ground-truth data, as standard score matching is not directly applicable.

Method: The authors leverage stochastic optimal control and propose adjoint matching, which enables simulation-free training and avoids explicit likelihood estimation or costly backpropagation through the diffusion process.

Result: The approach achieves competitive performance while significantly reducing computational overhead compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: Adjoint matching makes diffusion policies more viable for online RL scenarios by improving efficiency and robustness.

Abstract: Diffusion policies have recently emerged as a powerful paradigm for representing complex action distributions in reinforcement learning (RL). However, their application to online RL remains limited by the challenge of scalable training in the absence of ground-truth data, where standard optimization techniques such as score matching are not directly applicable. In this work, we introduce a highly efficient algorithm for optimizing diffusion policies by leveraging recent advances in stochastic optimal control. Our approach is based on adjoint matching, which enables simulation-free training and circumvents the need for explicit likelihood estimation or costly backpropagation through the diffusion process. Furthermore, we propose several extensions that improve the robustness and stability of the method in practical settings. Empirical results demonstrate that our approach achieves competitive performance while significantly reducing computational overhead, making diffusion policies more viable for online RL scenarios.

</details>


### [788] [A Markov Chain Approach to Preference Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22652)
*Takuya Koriyama,Tengyuan Liang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MCHF proposes using pairwise human preferences directly to define a Markov chain for aligning generative models, showing geometric convergence and unified connections to RLHF and NLHF approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a simpler alignment approach that uses pairwise preferences directly without reducing to scalar rewards (RLHF) or complex minimax optimization (NLHF), while maintaining theoretical guarantees.

Method: Define a Markov kernel P(x,dy) ∝ exp(U(x,y))μ_ref(dy) from pairwise utility U, forming a Markov chain from reference distribution μ_ref as an iterative alignment procedure.

Result: MCHF converges geometrically fast with rate governed by ∥U∥_⊕ (non-transitive structure); mirror-descent NLHF has analogous guarantees; when ∥U∥_⊕ is small, MCHF and NLHF agree up to first order around RLHF solution; first step of both recovers RLHF with column-sum reward.

Conclusion: MCHF provides a unified view connecting reward-based (RLHF), game-theoretic (NLHF), and Markovian alignment approaches, with the non-transitive component of preferences being captured from the second iteration onward.

Abstract: We propose Markov Chain from Human Feedback (MCHF), an elementary approach for aligning generative models from pairwise human preferences. Unlike Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), which reduces comparisons to a scalar reward, and Nash Learning from Human Feedback (NLHF), which preserves pairwise utilities through a KL-regularized minimax optimization, MCHF uses pairwise preferences directly to define a transition mechanism over model outputs. Given a pairwise utility $U(x,y)$, which quantifies human preference for $y$ over $x$, and a reference probability distribution $μ_{\mathsf{ref}}$, we define a Markov kernel $\mathsf{P}(x, dy)\propto \exp(U(x,y))μ_{\mathsf{ref}}(dy)$, and take the Markov chain starting from $μ_{\mathsf{ref}}$ as an iterative alignment procedure. We show that MCHF converges geometrically fast to the stationary distribution, with a convergence rate governed by the seminorm $\|U\|_\oplus=\inf_{g,f\in L^\infty(μ_{\mathsf{ref}})}\|U-g\oplus f\|_\infty$, which quantifies the non-transitive structure of the pairwise utility. We further show that a mirror-descent algorithm for NLHF satisfies an analogous structure-adaptive convergence guarantee. Finally, through a perturbation analysis, we prove that when $\|U\|_\oplus$ is small, MCHF and NLHF agree up to first order around an RLHF solution, which yields a unified view of reward-based, game-theoretic, and Markovian approaches to alignment. In particular, for two natural algorithms that converge to the MCHF/NLHF equilibria, we show that the first step of MCHF and NLHF recovers the RLHF solution based on the column-sum reward $\hat{f}(y)=\int μ_{\mathsf{ref}}(dx) U(x, y)$, and starting from the second iteration, both algorithms incorporate the same linear functional of the residual $U-(-\hat f)\oplus \hat f$, which captures the non-transitive structure of the pairwise utility $U$.

</details>


### [789] [Foundation Models for Epileptogenic Zone Identification in Drug-Resistant Epilepsy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22657)
*Thi Kieu Khanh Ho,Thomas Lai,Petr Klimes,Jan Cimbalnik,Martin Pail,Milan Brazdil,Birgit Frauscher,Narges Armanfard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EpiiSLM is a dual foundation model system (signal + language) that identifies the epileptogenic zone from sEEG recordings, achieving superior accuracy (0.978 PPV, 100% region-level) using just one night of interictal sleep data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve seizure freedom rates (currently below 50%) after resective surgery for drug-resistant epilepsy by developing a more accurate method for epileptogenic zone identification using sEEG.

Method: A dual foundation model system: (1) a signal foundation model trained on 104,990 minutes of sEEG recordings to extract EZ biomarkers, and (2) a language foundation model that integrates sEEG outputs with multimodal clinical data for interpretable predictions.

Result: 0.978 contact-level PPV (15.1% improvement over SOZ-as-EZ baseline, p<0.05), 100% region-level accuracy on internal data; 0.857 contact-level PPV on external dataset; requires only one night of interictal sleep data.

Conclusion: EpiiSLM has potential to reduce invasive sEEG monitoring duration and improve surgical outcomes in drug-resistant epilepsy.

Abstract: Accurate identification of the epileptogenic zone (EZ) is essential for seizure freedom after resective surgery in drug-resistant epilepsy, yet seizure freedom rates remain below 50%. We developed EpiiSLM, a dual foundation model system for EZ identification with stereo-electroencephalography (sEEG), by training a signal foundation model on 104,990 minutes of sEEG recordings from the Montreal Neurological Institute & Hospital, while leveraging all recordings regardless of surgical outcome and anchoring EZ biomarker extraction on non-epileptic signals. A language foundation model then integrates sEEG-derived outputs with multimodal clinical information to produce interpretable predictions. Under leave-one-patient-out evaluation, EpiiSLM achieved 0.978 contact-level positive predictive value (PPV), outperforming the seizure onset zone(SOZ)-as-EZ baseline by 15.1% (p < 0.05), and 100% region-level accuracy; on an external dataset, EpiiSLM achieved 0.857 contact-level PPV. EpiiSLM requires only one night of interictal sleep data, suggesting potential to reduce invasive sEEG monitoring duration and improve surgical outcomes.

</details>


### [790] [Paraphrasing Attack Resilience of Various AI-Generated Text Detection Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14240)
*Andrii Shportko,Inessa Verbitsky*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates the resilience of machine-generated text detection methods against paraphrasing attacks, finding that Binoculars-inclusive ensembles perform best but suffer the most significant performance losses under attack, revealing a performance-versus-resilience dichotomy in AI text detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The rise of LLMs has created challenges like plagiarism and misinformation, compounded by AI detector bypassing tools, creating an urgent need for reliable machine-generated text detection.

Method: The authors evaluate three detection approaches (fine-tuned RoBERTa, Binoculars, and text feature analysis) along with their ensembles using Random Forest classifiers, testing their resilience against paraphrasing attacks.

Result: Binoculars-inclusive ensembles achieve the strongest detection performance but suffer the most significant performance losses when subjected to paraphrasing attacks.

Conclusion: There exists a fundamental dichotomy between performance and resilience in AI text detection, complicating the current perception of reliability among state-of-the-art techniques.

Abstract: The recent large-scale emergence of LLMs has left an open space for dealing with their consequences, such as plagiarism or the spread of false information on the Internet. Coupling this with the rise of AI detector bypassing tools, reliable machine-generated text detection is in increasingly high demand. We investigate the paraphrasing attack resilience of various machine-generated text detection methods, evaluating three approaches: fine-tuned RoBERTa, Binoculars, and text feature analysis, along with their ensembles using Random Forest classifiers. We discovered that Binoculars-inclusive ensembles yield the strongest results, but they also suffer the most significant losses during attacks. In this paper, we present the dichotomy of performance versus resilience in the world of AI text detection, which complicates the current perception of reliability among state-of-the-art techniques.

</details>


### [791] [LSTM Variants for Chaotic Dynamical Systems: An Empirical Study on the Lorenz Attractor](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22662)
*Ruslan Gokhman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper evaluates seven recurrent and convolutional architectures for forecasting the Lorenz chaotic system. The BiLSTM with Huber loss achieves the best score (58.81/100). Key findings: additive attention hurts performance (by >10 points) and CNN front-ends do not help. Bidirectional context and robust loss are beneficial for chaotic forecasting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Forecasting chaotic dynamical systems (e.g., Lorenz attractor) is extremely difficult because small numerical errors amplify exponentially over long autoregressive rollouts. The authors aim to identify which architectural design choices are effective for this challenging task.

Method: Seven models are compared: vanilla LSTM, LSTM with additive attention, BiLSTM, BiLSTM with Huber loss, TCN, CNN+LSTM, and CNN+BiLSTM. All models share identical pre-processing, sequence length, and rollout procedure to isolate the effect of each design choice. Evaluation uses the AI-DEEDS 2026 challenge scoring (0-100).

Result: Leaderboard scores range from 45.72 to 58.81. The BiLSTM with Huber loss is the best. Additive attention degrades performance by over 10 points. CNN front-ends slightly hurt both LSTM and BiLSTM. Per-pair RMSE shows BiLSTM family generalizes better on hard pairs (6-7), while LSTM+Attention collapses (RMSE up to 8.94 on pair 6).

Conclusion: Bidirectional context and robust loss functions help in chaotic regimes, while additive attention and CNN front-ends fail in this setting.

Abstract: Forecasting chaotic dynamical systems such as the Lorenz attractor is notoriously difficult: small numerical errors are amplified exponentially over long autoregressive rollouts. We study seven recurrent and convolutional architectures for the AI-DEEDS 2026 Chaotic Systems Challenge: a vanilla LSTM, an LSTM with additive attention, a Bidirectional LSTM (BiLSTM), a BiLSTM trained with the Huber loss, a Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN), a CNN front-end followed by an LSTM, and a CNN front-end followed by a BiLSTM. All models share the same pre-processing, sequence length, and rollout procedure, isolating the contribution of each design choice. The challenge scores predictions on a 0-100 scale where higher is better. We obtain leaderboard scores between 45.72 and 58.81, with the BiLSTM trained with Huber loss being the strongest configuration. Two findings stand out: (i) adding additive attention to the unidirectional baseline degraded performance by over ten points, and (ii) prepending a CNN front-end to either an LSTM or a BiLSTM did not help and slightly hurt the score. Per-pair RMSE measurements confirm that the BiLSTM family generalizes better in the harder pairs (6-7), while the LSTM + Attention model collapses there (RMSE up to 8.94 on pair 6). We discuss why bidirectional context and a robust loss help in chaotic regimes while attention and CNN front-ends fail in this setting.

</details>


### [792] [Clipping the Price of Adaptivity at the Tail](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22669)
*Itai Kreisler,Yair Carmon,Oliver Hinder*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper overcomes the "price of adaptivity" barrier in stochastic convex optimization by assuming the objective separates into a model and loss function, enabling a clipping method that adapts to large uncertainty in both distance to optimality and Lipschitz constant, matching optimal known-parameter bounds up to logarithmic factors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adaptive SCO methods cannot efficiently handle large simultaneous uncertainty in both the initial distance to optimality and the Lipschitz constant due to the "price of adaptivity" barrier.

Method: The method assumes the objective decomposes into a model and loss function, then clips the model's output when it deviates too far from a fixed reference model in tail events.

Result: The method matches optimal bounds for known-parameter SCO up to logarithmic factors, efficiently adapting to large uncertainty in both distance and Lipschitz parameters.

Conclusion: By requiring a small amount of additional structure common to many learning problems, the price of adaptivity barrier can be circumvented.

Abstract: Adaptive stochastic convex optimization (SCO) methods face a fundamental ``price of adaptivity'' barrier: under the standard set of assumptions, they cannot efficiently adapt to large uncertainty in both the initial distance to optimality and the Lipschitz constant. We circumvent this barrier by requiring a small amount of additional structure common to many learning problems. Specifically, we assume that the objective decomposes into a model and a loss function, enabling us to intervene by modifying the model's output before it passes to the loss function. Under this assumption, we design a method that clips the learned model output in tail events where it deviates too much from the output of a fixed reference model. Our method matches the optimal bounds for known-parameter SCO up to logarithmic factors in the uncertainty in the distance and Lipschitz parameters, thus efficiently adapting to large uncertainty in both.

</details>


### [793] [SCRUB-FL: Sanitizing and Cleansing Representations via Unlearning of Backdoors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22700)
*Osama Wehbi,Sarhad Arisdakessian,Omar Abdel Wahab,Azzam Mourad,Hadi Otrok*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SCRUB-FL is a two-phase post-training defense for federated learning backdoor attacks that uses spectral analysis, WGAN-GP, and machine unlearning to reduce attack success rate to 3.88% while maintaining 91% accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing FL defenses fail to fully eliminate backdoor behaviors in the converged global model, and post-training sanitization is limited by the server's lack of knowledge of trigger patterns or poisoned clients, resulting in residual backdoors or accuracy degradation.

Method: Two-phase approach: (1) during training, clients use spectral analysis and activation clustering to detect suspicious samples, train lightweight WGAN-GP models to capture trigger distributions, and aggregate generator parameters server-side; (2) after convergence, server synthesizes trigger-approximating samples and applies machine unlearning to erase trigger-target associations by redistributing predictions toward uniform distribution.

Result: On CIFAR-10 and GTSRB across three attack types and up to 40% malicious participation, SCRUB-FL reduces backdoor attack success rate to as low as 3.88% while maintaining over 91% normal task accuracy, outperforming state-of-the-art defenses.

Conclusion: SCRUB-FL effectively removes backdoors post-training without requiring prior trigger knowledge or a large clean proxy dataset, addressing key limitations of existing FL defenses.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) enables collaborative model training without sharing raw data, making it a promising paradigm for privacy-sensitive applications. However, its decentralized nature makes it inherently vulnerable to backdoor attacks, where malicious clients embed hidden triggers into local training data to manipulate model predictions. Existing defenses mainly operate during before and during aggregation cannot fully eliminate backdoor behaviors that persist in the converged global model. Moreover, the effectiveness of post-training sanitization is often limited by the server's lack of knowledge of trigger patterns or poisoned clients after convergence, resulting in residual backdoor behaviors or accuracy degradation due to neuron entanglement. To address this limitation, we propose SCRUB-FL (Sanitizing and Cleansing Representations via Unlearning of Backdoors), a two-phase solution for post-training backdoor removal in FL. During training, clients identify suspicious samples using spectral analysis and activation clustering, then train lightweight Wasserstein Generative Adversarial Network with Gradient Penalty (WGAN-GP) models to capture trigger-related distributions. The generator parameters are aggregated server-side to construct a global representation of suspicious patterns without exposing raw data. After convergence, the server synthesizes trigger-approximating samples and applies machine unlearning to erase the trigger-target association by redistributing predictions toward a uniform distribution. Experimental evaluations on CIFAR-10 and GTSRB across three attack types and up to 40% malicious participation demonstrate that SCRUB-FL reduces the backdoor attack success rate to as low as 3.88% while maintaining over 91% normal task accuracy, outperforming state-of-the-art defenses without requiring prior trigger knowledge or a large clean proxy dataset at the server.

</details>


### [794] [Subspace-Constrained Federated Learning with Low-Rank Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22724)
*Neranjan Senarath,Rohit Muralitharan,Sadia Asif*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a subspace-regularized federated LoRA method (Subspace-Reg) that encourages local client updates to stay close to a shared global subspace, addressing geometric misalignment in heterogeneous federated learning. Experiments on RoBERTa-large and SmolLM-360M show that Subspace-Reg achieves near-perfect basis overlap (0.9999) and strong accuracy on RoBERTa-large, though results vary by model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Heterogeneous client data in federated learning can cause geometric misalignment between local low-rank (LoRA) updates, potentially leading to destructive aggregation and slower convergence.

Method: A subspace-regularized federated LoRA objective that regularizes local client updates to remain close to a shared global reference subspace.

Result: On RoBERTa-large, Subspace-Reg achieved best mean best accuracy (0.454 ± 0.023), mean final accuracy (0.429 ± 0.011), and lowest final loss (1.363), outperforming baselines. On SmolLM-360M, FedAvg led on accuracy. Subspace-Reg achieved ~0.9999 basis overlap across all settings vs. 0.958–0.991 for baselines.

Conclusion: Subspace regularization effectively improves geometric alignment in federated LoRA, but accuracy gains are model-dependent. The near-perfect basis overlap strongly supports the geometric alignment hypothesis.

Abstract: Federated low-rank adaptation methods are attractive for fine-tuning large models under communication and privacy constraints, but heterogeneous client data can induce geometric misalignment between local low-rank updates. We study whether this subspace misalignment leads to destructive aggregation and slower convergence in LoRA-based federated learning. We propose a subspace-regularized federated LoRA objective that encourages local client updates to remain close to a shared global reference subspace. We present a complete empirical evaluation on two pretrained models, RoBERTa-large and SmolLM-360M, over HellaSwag in a non-IID 10-client federated setting, across 3 random seeds (42, 43, 44), yielding 24 total experimental runs (4 methods x 3 seeds x 2 models). On RoBERTa-large, Subspace-Reg achieves the strongest mean best accuracy (0.454 +/- 0.023), mean final accuracy (0.429 +/- 0.011), and lowest final loss (1.363) across all three seeds, outperforming FedAvg, SVD redistribution, and FedSVD baselines by a large margin. On SmolLM-360M, FedAvg leads on accuracy, revealing that accuracy gains are model-dependent. Crucially, Subspace-Reg achieves near-perfect basis overlap, approximately 0.9999, on both models and across all seeds, versus 0.958 to 0.991 for all baselines, providing robust support for the geometric alignment hypothesis. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/sadia-sigma-lab/Subspace-Constrained-Federated-learning-with-Lora.

</details>


### [795] [GRADE: Graph Representation of LLM Agent Dependency and Execution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22741)
*Yue Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GRADE models any LLM agent run as a graph with two edge layers (execution and dependency), where dependency edges are graded by certainty (observed/declared/inferred), enabling failure prediction and fault localization across six corpora better than run size alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current trace logs record what each step did but not what it relied on, the state it read, or results it reused. This missing dependency information limits failure diagnosis and optimization of LLM agent runs.

Method: GRADE represents any LLM agent run as a graph over step nodes with two edge layers: (1) execution edges (order of operations) read from trace logs, and (2) dependency edges (what each step relied on) graded by certainty levels: observed, declared, or inferred. The paper tests across six corpora covering tool use, coding, and web agents, comparing against run size baselines and generic GNNs.

Result: The dependency layer predicts failure where run size alone is weak. Under leave-one-corpus-out transfer, dependency-layer predictions stay above chance on every held-out class while run size fails. The execution layer localizes faulting steps in failed multi-agent runs. Generic GNNs may misread the dependency layer, unlike the feature-based alternative.

Conclusion: GRADE's dual-layer graph representation enables unified modeling of LLM agent runs, with demonstrated utility for failure prediction and fault localization, while opening further uses for efficiency and robustness optimization at scale.

Abstract: Can one graph represent every kind of LLM agent's run? A trace records what each step did, never what it relied on, the state it read, and the results it reused. GRADE recovers that missing layer: it models any run as one graph over its step nodes with two edge layers, execution edges (what ran in what order) read from the trace for free, and dependency edges (what each step relied on) rarely logged, so each is graded by how it is known, observed, declared, or inferred. One representation, and each layer earns its place. Across six corpora of LLM agents spanning tool use, coding, and the web, the dependency layer can predict failure where run size is weak and, under leave-one-corpus-out transfer, stays above chance on every held-out class while run size fails. Meanwhile, the execution layer localizes the faulting step in a failed multi-agent run. This work also provides a more in-depth analysis of why generic graph neural networks may misread the dependency layer, unlike our feature-based alternative. The same graph representation opens further uses, carrying from failure diagnosis in a single run to efficiency and robustness optimization at scale.

</details>


### [796] [Error Highways: Scaling Predictive Coding to Very Deep Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22744)
*Amirhossein Mohammadi,Alexander G. Ororbia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Highway Error Propagation (HEP) to solve the depth-related learning signal decay problem in Predictive Coding Networks, enabling training of deep MLPs (up to 128 layers) by adding direct feedback connections from the output error to hidden layers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling PCNs to deep architectures is hindered by the exponential decay of learning signals as they propagate away from clamped boundaries, leaving interior layers largely unchanged.

Method: HEP augments the PC free energy function by adding feedback matrices V_{L→i} that directly couple selected hidden states to the clamped output error, providing a depth-independent correction signal at every inference step while preserving local PC synaptic updates.

Result: HEP enables training of MLPs with up to 128 layers on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST, achieving accuracy robust to depth, unlike vanilla PC which fails for deep architectures.

Conclusion: HEP effectively addresses the depth limitations of PCNs by providing direct error highways that bypass the Jacobian chain, maintaining local learning rules while scaling to deep networks.

Abstract: Predictive coding networks (PCNs) offer a biologically-plausible, local-learning alternative to back-propagation of errors (backprop). Nevertheless, they have remained largely confined to shallow architectures and evaluated on simple machine intelligence benchmarks. A central obstacle to scaling PCNs is that the learning signal decays rapidly as it propagates away from the clamped boundaries, leaving interior layers effectively unchanged. To directly counter this problem, we propose highway error propagation (HEP), a scheme that augments the free energy function underlying predictive coding (PC) by altering its neural structure with feedback matrices $V_{L\to i}$ that couple selected hidden states directly to the clamped output error. Since this coupling is linear in the hidden state, the highway pathway delivers a correction at every inference step whose magnitude is independent of depth, in contrast to vanilla PC where the output error reaches the $i$-th hidden layer with attenuation that decays exponentially in depth. This bypasses the Jacobian chain while preserving the local PC synaptic update rule. On MNIST and Fashion-MNIST, we show that HEP effectively trains MLPs of up to 128 layers with accuracy that is robust with respect to depth.

</details>


### [797] [One-Step Flow Matching for Generative Modeling of Path-Dependent Physical Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22752)
*Yijing Zhou,Jasmin Jelovica*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a flow matching model based on a transformer backbone for high-resolution path-dependent stress field generation in physical simulations, achieving significant speedup over finite element analysis (6-7x on CPUs, ~100x on GPUs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Physical simulations for complex geometries with path-dependent constitutive models (e.g., plastic stress fields) are computationally expensive. Existing diffusion models require many sampling steps and have limited application to path-dependent stress fields.

Method: The authors propose a flow matching (FM) model with a transformer backbone operating in VAE latent space, formulating plastic field simulation as video synthesis. They introduce a non-Gaussian source distribution to reduce conditional transport path crossings, enabling one-step generation, plus token-level loading embeddings and auxiliary networks.

Result: The model accurately generates high-resolution path-dependent stress fields with limited training data. It achieves 6-7x speedup over FEM on CPUs and ~100x speedup on consumer-grade GPUs.

Conclusion: Flow matching with transformer backbone is a promising generative AI approach for efficient path-dependent physical simulations, substantially outperforming traditional FEM in computational efficiency.

Abstract: Physical simulations for intricate geometries with path-dependent constitutive models face difficulties due to the enormous computational cost they require. Recently, the emergence of generative AI models, which succeed in image and video synthesis tasks, has provided a promise to further improve simulations. Although U-Net-based denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) have been adopted for elastic stress field generation, they typically require hundreds of sampling steps, and applications of generative models to path-dependent, e.g. plastic, stress fields remain very limited. In this work, we propose a novel flow matching (FM) model based on a transformer backbone for high-resolution path-dependent stress field generation with stochastic loading-unloading paths and geometry. The proposed model operates within the latent space of a variational autoencoder (VAE) and formulates the simulation of plastic fields as a video synthesis task, directly generating the stress fields across all time steps. Meanwhile, we design a non-Gaussian source distribution for flow matching, such that crossings among conditional transport paths are reduced during training. This enables our model to generate satisfactory samples in one step without relying on distillation. In addition, we introduce token-level loading embeddings and two auxiliary networks to further enhance the model performance in path-dependent simulation. The results demonstrate that, even with a limited training dataset, our model can accurately generate high-resolution path-dependent fields. It is much more computationally efficient than finite element analysis, providing a speedup of 6 to 7 times over FEM on CPUs and approximately two orders of magnitude speedup on consumer-grade GPUs.

</details>


### [798] [Factored Gossip DiLoCo: Reducing Blocking Communication in DiLoCo](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22768)
*Chamin Hewa Koneputugodage,Thalaiyasingam Ajanthan,Sameera Ramasinghe,Hadi Mohaghegh Dolatabadi,Shamane Siriwardhana,Gil Avraham,Violetta Shevchenko,Karol Pajak,James Snewin,Alexander Long*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework that relaxes DiLoCo's exact synchronization to approximate mixing/gossip, enabling non-blocking communication that overlaps with computation, improving compute utilization and robustness in low-bandwidth distributed training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To make large-scale distributed training practical outside high-bandwidth datacenters by reducing blocking, high-volume synchronization that is bandwidth-heavy and brittle to stragglers and transient failures.

Method: Factorizes DiLoCo synchronization into a non-blocking mixing step (overlaps computation with no staleness) and a blocking mixing step (tightens worker agreement), using approximate synchronization via mixing/gossip instead of exact synchronization.

Result: Substantially improves compute utilization compared to DiLoCo on up to billion-parameter language models in low-bandwidth settings, with training progress ranging from comparable to closely matching it, and is more robust to failures.
