It is central to any good scientific theory, which we expect to be able to make predictions about regimes not covered by currently available data. Recent hierarchical and hyper-network guided approaches for DS reconstruction (DSR) enable training on many DS simultaneously, and revealed that extracted latent features are often related to crucial control parameters of the underlying DS that varied across the training corpus. However, true out-of-domain forecasting abilities of these models, e.g., across tipping points, remain limited, and fine-tuning, or even full model retraining, on time series from the new dynamical regime is usually required. Here, we mathematically analyze the root of these limitations in previous model formulations and identify three core shortcomings rooted in a mismatch between structural assumptions of the reconstruction model and typical properties of physical systems. We propose a combination of remedies for these shortcomings, most importantly feature splitting, and furthermore derive a closed-form bound on the reliable extrapolation range. We demonstrate empirically that our techniques allow for accurate zero-shot prediction into new dynamical regimes, outside the observed training regime, as, e.g., encountered across tipping points.

</details>


### [826] [TaLK: Text-attributed Graph Dataset Distillation via Coupling Language Model with Graph-Aware Kernel](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22975)
*Yeongho Kim,Yeonje Choi,Kijung Shin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TaLK introduces an efficient dataset distillation method for text-attributed graphs that couples a language model with a graph-aware neural tangent kernel, achieving up to 97% of full-dataset performance using only 1% synthetic data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dataset distillation methods are not suited for TAGs because they are designed for single modalities or still require repeated expensive LM-GNN training on the full dataset during distillation.

Method: TaLK couples a language model (LM) with a graph-aware neural tangent kernel to enable efficient dataset distillation, avoiding repeated joint training on the full dataset while preserving both textual and structural information.

Result: TaLK consistently outperforms existing baselines on multiple TAG benchmarks and achieves up to 97% of full-dataset performance with only 1% synthetic data.

Conclusion: TaLK is an effective and scalable dataset distillation method for TAGs, significantly reducing training costs while maintaining high performance.

Abstract: Text-attributed graphs (TAGs) are widely used in many real-world domains, and learning on TAGs requires jointly modeling text semantics and graph structure. A standard approach for modeling TAGs is to combine a language model (LM) and a graph neural network (GNN), but joint training is computationally expensive and difficult to scale. Dataset distillation is a promising way to reduce training costs, but existing methods are not well suited to TAGs because they are typically designed for a single modality or still require repeatedly training expensive LM-GNN models on the full dataset during distillation. To address this, we propose TaLK, an effective dataset distillation method for TAGs that couples an LM with a graph-aware neural tangent kernel.This design enables efficient dataset distillation, avoiding repeated joint training on the full dataset while reflecting both textual and structural information for effective TAG learning.Experiments on multiple TAG benchmarks show that TaLK consistently outperforms existing baselines and achieves up to 97% of full-dataset performance with only 1% synthetic data.

</details>


### [827] [Understanding Parallel Samplers in Masked Diffusion via Random Walks on Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22976)
*Vansh Bansal,Cho Cholyeon,Syamantak Kumar,Sujay Sanghavi,Purnamrita Sarkar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper uses random walks on graphs as a verifiable sandbox to study parallel sampling strategies in masked diffusion models, proving that no single parallel sampler is universally optimal and proposing a new bisection sampler that is provably exact under perfect training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a controlled, verifiable benchmark (like a "Sudoku check") for evaluating parallel sampling strategies in masked diffusion models, since real-world sequence data lacks ground-truth latent structure for quantitative comparison.

Method: Train masked diffusion models on random walk samples from fixed graphs (hidden from the model); theoretically analyze parallel unmasking strategies (lowest entropy vs. random); propose a bisection sampler that takes logarithmic steps in sequence length; validate on graph walk tasks and a pretrained OpenWebText MDM.

Result: Theoretically proved that parallel unmasking via lowest entropy is not uniformly better than random sampling—performance depends on graph structure. The bisection sampler is provably exact under perfect training. Experiments confirm different parallel samplers are better for different graphs, and bisection-style samplers improve speed-quality tradeoffs for language generation.

Conclusion: Graph random walks provide a mechanistic benchmark for diagnosing and designing parallel samplers for masked diffusion models, and the bisection sampler offers a promising direction for improving efficiency.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose using random walks on graphs as a verifiable sandbox to study different parallel sampling strategies in masked diffusion models (MDMs). We train an MDM on random walk samples from a fixed graph. The graph or the transition kernel is never shown to the model explicitly and plays the role of latent structure in the sequences, albeit one that is controllable and can be used for quantitative evaluation. Thus, this framework enjoys a Sudoku-like validity check: verifying that an output is a valid walk and estimating the Markov kernel from the walks to measure distribution fidelity.
  Using simple graphs, we theoretically prove that parallel unmasking via widely used scores like lowest entropy is not uniformly better than a random parallel sampler; the performance critically depends on the structure of the underlying graph. We develop a new bisection sampler for random walks, which takes logarithmic steps in the sequence length and is provably exact under perfect training. Experiments on various graph walk tasks show that different parallel samplers are better for different graphs even in practice. Our initial experiments on a pretrained OpenWebText MDM show that the bisection-style samplers improve speed-quality tradeoffs even for language generation. Together, these results position graph random walks as a mechanistic benchmark for diagnosing and designing parallel samplers for masked diffusion models.

</details>


### [828] [Neural Architecture Search of Sample Reweighting Networks for Complex Distribution Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22991)
*Keisuke Sugawara,Kento Uchida,Shinichi Shirakawa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper improves Meta-Weight-Net (a sample reweighting network) by incorporating neural architecture search to handle combined label noise and class imbalance, using tree-structured Parzen estimators to optimize network architecture and input layer selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MW-Net degrades when facing both label noise and class imbalance simultaneously, as it's difficult to determine appropriate weights solely from classification loss using a simple network.

Method: Introduced neural architecture search (using tree-structured Parzen estimator) to explore optimal hidden layers, nodes, and select the best intermediate layer in the classification model as input for MW-Net.

Result: Experiments on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 with both label noise and class imbalance show the effectiveness of the proposed NAS approach for MW-Net.

Conclusion: Neural architecture search effectively mitigates MW-Net's performance degradation under combined distribution shifts (label noise + class imbalance).

Abstract: Sample reweighting is a major approach to addressing distribution shifts, such as label noise and class imbalance. Meta-Weight-Net (MW-Net) is a promising sample reweighting network that computes weights based on classification loss. Although MW-Net improves prediction performance under a single type of distribution shift using a simple neural network, its performance degrades when facing both label noise and class imbalance, where it is hard to determine appropriate weights solely from classification loss and using a simple network. In this study, we introduce neural architecture search to MW-Net to mitigate such performance degradation. Using the tree-structured Parzen estimator, we explore the optimal number of hidden layers and nodes and select the most suitable intermediate layer in the classification model to serve as the input for MW-Net. Experimental results on the CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 datasets that were modified to include both label noise and class imbalance demonstrate the effectiveness of neural architecture search for MW-Net.

</details>


### [829] [Do Sparse Autoencoders Learn Meaningful Concept Hierarchies?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22994)
*Nils Grandien,David Steinmann,Felix Friedrich,Kristian Kersting*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies the lack of rigorous evaluation for hierarchical sparse autoencoders, proposes a formal set of requirements and evaluation protocol for feature hierarchies, and demonstrates that both hard and soft feature absorption systematically degrade hierarchy quality in current visual SAE approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hierarchical SAEs lack agreed-upon evaluation standards, with existing assessments relying on qualitative examples and fragmented protocols, making rigorous comparison between approaches impossible.

Method: The authors derive key requirements for generalization/specialization hierarchies from semantic net and taxonomy research combined with recent SAE work, then construct a concrete evaluation protocol. They apply this protocol to current SAE approaches on visual data.

Result: Feature spaces generally support sensible hierarchies, but establishing good hierarchical structure remains difficult. Both hard and continuous soft feature absorption systematically compromise hierarchy quality.

Conclusion: A fundamental tension exists between current SAE training approaches and meaningful hierarchical organization, which future work must address.

Abstract: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have become an important tool for unsupervised concept discovery in large models. To make the resulting feature spaces more interpretable and manageable, recent approaches have begun imposing hierarchical structure, either explicitly or as an implicit effect of training constraints, yet rigorous comparison remains difficult. There are no agreed-upon requirements for what a meaningful feature hierarchy should satisfy, and evaluation has largely relied on qualitative illustrations with fragmented quantitative protocols. To address this, we derive a set of key requirements for generalization/specialization hierarchies in unsupervised concept discovery, drawing on semantic net and taxonomy research alongside recent SAE work, and use them to derive a concrete evaluation protocol. Applying this protocol to current SAE approaches trained on visual data, we find that while feature spaces generally provide a basis for sensible hierarchies, establishing good hierarchical structure remains challenging. In particular, feature absorption, both in its well-known hard form and in a continuous, soft form, systematically compromises hierarchy quality, pointing to a fundamental tension that future approaches will need to navigate.

</details>


### [830] [Group-Graph Policy Optimization for Long-Horizon Agentic Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22995)
*Yunan Wang,Minghui Song,Zihan Zhang,Shaohan Huang,Haizhen Huang,Furu Wei,Weiwei Deng,Feng Sun,Qi Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: G2PO transforms linear agent trajectories into a global state-transition graph, enabling group-aggregated value estimation and edge-centric advantage computation to overcome reward sparsity and coarse credit assignment in long-horizon agentic RL, achieving up to 22.2% improvement over GRPO on benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing step-level RL frameworks for LLMs suffer from reward sparsity/delay in long-horizon tasks and treat agent exploration as isolated linear trajectories, ignoring the inherent graph structure of state transitions, which leads to high-variance value estimation and myopic credit assignment.

Method: G2PO transforms linear trajectories into a global state-transition graph, aggregates identical observations across trajectories for group-aggregation state-value estimation, redefines actions as transitions between state nodes, and computes edge-centric advantage via globally standardized TD errors to prioritize critical transitions.

Result: G2PO substantially outperforms state-of-the-art prompt-based and RL baselines on WebShop, ALFWorld, and AppWorld, achieving up to 22.2% improvement in success rate over GRPO.

Conclusion: Modeling agent interactions as a graph rather than linear trajectories enables finer-grained, globally-aware credit assignment and more robust value estimation, significantly improving long-horizon agentic RL performance.

Abstract: Group-based Reinforcement Learning (RL) has significantly enhanced Large Language Models (LLMs) in agentic scenarios. To achieve finer-grained policy updates, recent agentic RL frameworks have shifted from trajectory-level to step-level training. However, long-horizon agentic RL suffers from severe reward sparsity and delay, as feedback is often deferred for dozens of interaction steps. While existing step-level frameworks refine training granularity, their credit assignment remains coarse-grained and still treats agent exploration as isolated, linear trajectories. This oversimplified perspective ignores the inherent graph structure of state transitions, leading to high-variance state-value estimation and myopic, localized credit assignment. To overcome these critical bottlenecks, we propose Group-Graph Policy Optimization (G2PO), a novel group-based RL algorithm tailored for multi-turn agentic tasks. G2PO explicitly transforms linear interaction trajectories into a global state-transition graph. By aggregating identical observations across different trajectories, we introduce group-aggregation state-value estimation that reduces sampling variance and trajectory-dependent bias. Furthermore, we redefine agent actions as transitions between state nodes and propose an edge-centric advantage estimation strategy. By globally standardizing Temporal Difference (TD) errors across the entire graph, G2PO explicitly identifies and prioritizes critical transitions that drive absolute task progress. Extensive experiments on representative long-horizon benchmarks-WebShop, ALFWorld, and AppWorld-demonstrate that G2PO substantially outperforms state-of-the-art prompt-based and RL baselines, achieving remarkable success rate improvements of up to 22.2% over GRPO.

</details>


### [831] [A Novel Approach to Temporal QoS Estimation via Extended Kalman Filter-Incorporated Latent Feature Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23010)
*Ye Yuan,Song Wang,Hongxun Zhou,Ling Wang,Xin Luo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes an Extended Kalman Filter-Enhanced Latent Feature Analysis (EKL) model for temporal QoS prediction, combining model-driven and data-driven learning to improve accuracy and efficiency over existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing data-driven methods fail to capture non-stationary temporal patterns in QoS data, leading to accuracy degradation when fluctuations occur.

Method: Three components: (1) model-driven feature producer using Extended Kalman Filter to capture temporal patterns; (2) data-driven feature producer via alternating least squares for time-invariant features; (3) density-oriented parallel strategy for workload balancing.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art models in both computational efficiency and prediction accuracy on real-world temporal QoS datasets.

Conclusion: The bidirectional model-data-driven EKL effectively addresses the limitations of purely data-driven approaches, achieving more accurate and efficient temporal QoS prediction.

Abstract: Predicting temporal Quality of Service (QoS) data is critical for optimizing network services and rationalizing resource allocation in cloud computing and service-oriented systems. Existing mainstream methods have achieved promising predictive performance. However, their purely data-driven manner limits their ability to capture non-stationary temporal patterns, thereby leading to accuracy degradation when temporal QoS data exhibits fluctuations. To tackle this limitation, we propose a novel Extended Kalman Filter-Enhanced Latent Feature Analysis (EKL) model to perform efficient and accurate temporal QoS prediction from the perspective of bidirectional model-data-driven learning. Its main idea is three-fold: a) designing a model-driven feature producer to obtain the temporal latent features to capture the intricate temporal pattern following the principle of an Extended Kalman Filter; b) building a data-driven feature producer based on the alternating least squares algorithm to identify time-invariant latent features describing intrinsic user-service characteristics; c) exploiting a density-oriented parallel strategy that achieves workload balancing by sorting users in accordance with their service invocation density, which effectively elevates computational efficiency. In addition, we provide a rigorous theoretical analysis to formally prove the convergence of the proposed EKL. Experimental evaluations conducted on real-world temporal QoS datasets reveal that our proposed EKL surpasses existing state-of-the-art models with respect to both computational efficiency and prediction accuracy for missing temporal QoS data.

</details>


### [832] [Counterfactual learning of new adaptive instructional policies using logged data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23015)
*Samuel Girard,Sein Minn,Amel Bouzeghoub,Jill-Jênn Vie*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces an offline contextual bandit framework that learns adaptive instructional policies for Intelligent Tutoring Systems from logged interaction data using a Rasch model and flow-optimizing reward function, demonstrating consistent improvements across four real-world datasets without requiring costly online experimentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Optimizing instructional policies in ITS typically requires costly online experimentation or unreliable student simulators. The authors aim to learn effective adaptive policies directly from existing logged interaction data without additional data collection.

Method: The framework uses a Rasch model to map student-item interactions onto a continuous latent proficiency-difficulty scale, casting tutoring as a continuous stochastic bandit problem. It introduces a novel reward function optimizing "flow" (balancing challenge with success) and includes round-specific behavior policy estimation for off-policy evaluation and diagnostic analysis.

Result: Tested on four large-scale real-world datasets. Achieves consistent policy improvements over the logged behavior policy. Policies can be learned and visualized within seconds of computation.

Conclusion: Effective instructional policies can be learned offline from logged data without further data collection, providing a scalable and efficient path for improving adaptive learning systems.

Abstract: Optimizing instructional policies in Intelligent Tutoring Systems (ITS) typically requires costly online experimentation or student simulators that may fail to capture real-world dynamics. This paper introduces an offline contextual bandit framework that learns new adaptive policies directly from logged interaction data. By mapping student-item interactions onto a continuous latent proficiency-difficulty scale using a Rasch model, we cast the tutoring process as a continuous stochastic bandit problem. We propose a novel reward function designed to optimize ''flow'' by balancing task challenge with student success. Our approach includes a round-specific behavior policy estimation that serves as both a propensity model for off-policy evaluation and a diagnostic tool for ITS adaptivity. We demonstrate the efficacy of this framework across four large-scale real-world datasets, achieving consistent policy improvements over the logged behavior policy. The results show that effective instructional policies can be learned and visualized within seconds of computation, providing a scalable path for improving adaptive learning systems without further data collection.

</details>


### [833] [EvoRubrics: Dynamic Rubrics as Rewards via Adversarial Co-Evolution for LLM Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23038)
*Hongxin Ding,Baixiang Huang,Yue Fang,Weibin Liao,Zheng Li,Jinyang Zhang,Zhijing Wu,Junfeng Zhao,Yasha Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EvoRubrics proposes a co-evolutionary RL framework where a Policy LLM and a Rubric Generator mutually improve through adversarial interaction, enabling dynamic, adaptive reward criteria that outperform static/dynamic rubric baselines and generalize as transferable reward models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static rubric-based rewards lose discriminative power as the policy improves, causing reward saturation and potential hacking. Existing dynamic methods rely on external frontier models or ground-truth answers and update rubrics only at coarse granularity.

Method: A co-evolutionary RL framework where a Policy LLM and a Rubric Generator adversarially interact within each training step. The rubric generator adapts its criteria in real-time to remain discriminative as the policy improves, creating an automatic curriculum.

Result: Consistently outperforms static and dynamic rubric baselines across benchmarks. The learned Rubric Generator generalizes as a transferable reward model. Even a fully self-supervised variant achieves meaningful gains.

Conclusion: Co-evolution between generation and evaluation alone can provide sufficiently rich learning signals without external supervision, suggesting a promising paradigm for open-ended RL tasks.

Abstract: Rubric-based rewards offer interpretable and fine-grained optimization signals for reinforcement learning in open-ended tasks where verifiable answers are unavailable. However, pre-constructed rubrics remain static throughout training, creating a fundamental mismatch with the evolving policy: fixed criteria gradually lose discriminative power as the model improves, leading to reward saturation and potential hacking. Recent dynamic rubric methods partially address this but rely on external frontier models or ground-truth answers, and update rubrics only at coarse granularity. We propose EvoRubrics, a co-evolutionary RL framework where a Policy LLM and a Rubric Generator jointly improve through adversarial interaction within each training step. As the policy improves under the rubric generator's guidance, the rubric generator adapts its criteria to remain discriminative and informative, enabling evaluation to track the policy in real time and naturally inducing an automatic curriculum. Experiments show that EvoRubrics consistently outperforms static and dynamic rubric baselines across benchmarks. The learned Rubric Generator further generalizes as a transferable reward model. Notably, even a fully self-supervised variant without any external supervision achieves meaningful gains, suggesting that co-evolution between generation and evaluation alone can provide sufficiently rich learning signals. Our code is publicly available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/EvoRubrics-2155/.

</details>


### [834] [Prime Fourier Embeddings: A Principled Basis for Modular Arithmetic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23044)
*Hyunsang Hwang,Suhyun Bae,Donghun Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Prime Fourier Embeddings (PFE) encode integers using prime-indexed (cos, sin) pairs, enabling modular arithmetic by selecting relevant prime channels rather than learning algebraic structure. The method achieves perfect test accuracy on square-free composite moduli with 500x channel specialization ratios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard neural embeddings fail to expose the algebraic structure of numbers, particularly for modular arithmetic tasks that require discovering algebraic relations from scratch.

Method: PFE encodes integers as prime-indexed (cos, sin) pairs derived from harmonic analysis of Q. The representation is structured so modular arithmetic reduces to selecting the relevant prime channel. The paper proves via Schur's lemma that linear maps equivariant to the product group action are block-diagonal per prime, and uses the Chinese Remainder Theorem to predict task-relevant channels for square-free composite moduli.

Result: Empirical ablation studies show specialization ratios exceeding 500x between task-relevant and task-irrelevant channels, with perfect in-distribution test accuracy across all square-free composite moduli tested.

Conclusion: Pre-structuring embeddings with algebraic priors (prime-indexed Fourier features) can dramatically improve neural network performance on arithmetic tasks by eliminating the need to discover algebraic structure during learning.

Abstract: Numbers have algebraic structure that standard neural embeddings often fail to expose. We introduce Prime Fourier Embeddings (PFE), which encode integers as prime-indexed (cos, sin) pairs derived from the harmonic analysis of Q, providing a pre-structured representation in which modular arithmetic reduces to selecting the relevant prime channel rather than discovering algebraic structure from scratch. We prove that any linear map equivariant with respect to the product group action on PFE must be block-diagonal with one independent block per prime -- a consequence of Schur's lemma applied to the resulting character decomposition. For square-free composite moduli, the Chinese Remainder Theorem predicts which prime channels are task-relevant. Both predictions are confirmed empirically: ablation studies show specialization ratios exceeding 500x between task-relevant and task-irrelevant channels, with perfect in-distribution test accuracy across all square-free composite moduli tested.

</details>


### [835] [PeLAP-A: Adaptive Latent Pruning for Lightweight Latent Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23086)
*Kissa Zahra,Zaib Un Nisa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PeLAP-A introduces a lightweight channel-wise importance predictor for latent diffusion models, revealing that under aggressive sparsity regularization, all latent channels can be suppressed to near-zero while achieving lower diffusion loss and reconstruction MSE than the unpruned baseline—a phenomenon termed "sparsity collapse."


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether all latent channels in VAE-compressed representations contribute equally to the diffusion process, and to explore potential redundancy that could be exploited for efficiency.

Method: A two-layer MLP predicts channel-wise importance from globally pooled latent features, producing a soft mask to suppress unimportant channels before the denoising UNet. The system is trained jointly on CIFAR-10 with combined diffusion, reconstruction, and sparsity losses.

Result: Under aggressive sparsity regularization (λ=0.01), all latent channels are driven to near-zero importance, yet the model achieves lower diffusion loss (0.0236 vs 0.0240) and lower VAE reconstruction MSE (22.59 vs 24.67) compared to the unpruned baseline.

Conclusion: Denoising UNets are remarkably robust to latent channel suppression, and the "sparsity collapse" phenomenon reveals fundamental insights about the information requirements and redundancy in latent diffusion models.

Abstract: Latent diffusion models achieve strong generative performance by operating in a compressed latent space produced by a variational autoencoder (VAE). However, it remains unclear whether all latent channels contribute equally to the diffusion process, or whether significant redundancy exists. We introduce PeLAP-A (Adaptive Latent Pruning for Diffusion), a lightweight framework that augments a standard latent diffusion pipeline with a learnable channel-wise importance predictor. A two-layer MLP operating on globally pooled latent features produces a soft mask that suppresses unimportant latent channels before they enter the denoising UNet. The entire system is trained jointly on CIFAR-10 under a combined diffusion, reconstruction, and sparsity loss. Experiments reveal a striking result: under aggressive sparsity regularization (lambda = 0.01), the importance predictor drives all latent channels to near-zero yet the denoising UNet achieves lower diffusion loss (0.0236 vs. 0.0240) and lower VAE reconstruction MSE (22.59 vs. 24.67) compared to the unpruned baseline. We term this the sparsity collapse phenomenon and provide an analysis of why it occurs and what it reveals about the information requirements of latent diffusion models. These findings constitute an exploratory study of sparsity dynamics in latent diffusion training, and demonstrate that denoising UNets can remain remarkably robust to latent channel suppression even under aggressive regularization. Code is available at: https://github.com/kissasium/PeLAP-A.git.

</details>


### [836] [FlowTrain: Flow-Based Decoupled Training for Industrial-Grade Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23087)
*Zhida Jiang,Zhaolong Xing,Yang Pei,Xiaolong Chen,Yuanhang Xiao,Chengzhi Huang,Xiyu Liu,Haopeng Liu,Qingyuan Sang,Lingfeng Zhou,Jiaxing Wang,Zicheng Zhang,Wenzhe Wang,Xinyu Liu,Yan Li,Zhen Chen,Ke Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FlowTrain is a decoupled training framework for vision-language models that uses a producer-consumer dataflow with a unified memory pool, heterogeneous parallel allocator, and dynamic packing scheduler, achieving over 50% MFU and up to 1.7x throughput improvement over existing solutions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial-grade distributed training of vision-language models (VLMs) remains far less efficient than unimodal LLMs. Existing monolithic or disaggregated solutions are still insufficient for efficient VLM training.

Method: FlowTrain reformulates VLM training as a producer-consumer dataflow coordinated through a unified memory pool. It introduces a heterogeneous parallel allocator that assigns module-specific parallelism strategies via throughput matching, and a dynamic packing scheduler that constructs balanced microbatches based on actual LLM-side computation cost.

Result: Achieves over 50% MFU (Model FLOPS Utilization) and up to 1.7x throughput improvement on real-world workloads, narrowing the efficiency gap to LLM-only training.

Conclusion: FlowTrain demonstrates that further decoupling of VLM training is possible and effective, significantly improving training efficiency to approach that of unimodal LLM training.

Abstract: Industrial-grade distributed training of vision-language models (VLMs) remains far less efficient than that of unimodal LLMs. Existing solutions either follow a monolithic design that assigns uniform parallelism to heterogeneous modules or adopt a disaggregated deployment that separates modules while executing them as a batch-synchronized pipeline. In this paper, we highlight that the above solutions are still not sufficient, and VLM training can be further decoupled. To this end, we present FlowTrain, a flow-based decoupled training framework that reformulates VLM training as a producer-consumer dataflow coordinated through a unified memory pool. The encoder and backbone can progress independently over a global virtual address space. Since this execution decoupling fundamentally changes the optimization objective of allocation and scheduling, FlowTrain further introduces a heterogeneous parallel allocator that assigns module-specific parallelism strategies by solving a throughput matching problem. The dynamic packing scheduler is used to construct balanced microbatches at runtime according to the actual LLM-side computation cost. Extensive experiments on real-world workloads show that FlowTrain achieves over 50% MFU and up to 1.7x throughput improvement, narrowing the efficiency gap to LLM-only training.

</details>


### [837] [FLFL: Federated Latent Factor Learning for Private Recovery of Spatio-Temporal Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23091)
*Chengjun Yu,Di Wu,Yi He,Jia Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Federated Latent Factor Learning (FLFL) model for privacy-preserving spatio-temporal signal recovery in WSNs, where sensors upload gradient information instead of raw data, achieving high recovery accuracy while protecting data privacy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LFL models for WSN missing data recovery require centralizing raw sensing data, raising privacy concerns for data owners.

Method: FLFL combines a sensor-level federated learning framework (sharing gradients only) with spatio-temporal correlation regularization constraints.

Result: Experiments on four real-world WSN datasets show FLFL outperforms eight state-of-the-art models in recovery accuracy with privacy preservation.

Conclusion: FLFL effectively recovers missing WSN data while preserving data privacy, offering a promising approach for privacy-sensitive sensing applications.

Abstract: Wireless sensor network (WSNs) stands out as a burgeoning and promising domain in intelligent sensing. Owing to various factors such as sudden sensor malfunctions or deliberate shutdown of partial nodes to save energy, the collected sensing signals from WSNs commonly have massive missing data, leading to adverse effects on subsequent analysis or decision-making. Latent factor learning (LFL) has proven to be highly effective in recovering the missing data for WSNs. However, the existing LFL models require the collected sensing signals to be maintained in one central place like a central server, which is becoming unacceptable for data owners who are getting increasingly privacy-sensitive. To address this issue, this paper innovatively proposes a federated latent factor learning (FLFL) model for privacy-preserving spatio-temporal signal recovery. Its main idea is two-fold: 1) it designs a sensor-level federated learning framework based on LFL, where each sensor only needs to upload gradient information rather than raw data for training a privacy-preserving recovery model, and 2) it incorporates the spatio-temporal correlation into the designed federated learning framework as the regularization constraint to improve its recovery accuracy. With such designs, FLFL can not only accurately recover the missing data of WSNs but also ensure data owners' privacy-preserving of raw data. To evaluate the proposed FLFL model, extensive experiments have been conducted on four real-world WSN datasets. The results demonstrate that FLFL significantly outperforms eight state-of-the-art federated and non-federated signal recovery models in terms of recovery accuracy with privacy-preserving.

</details>


### [838] [ReNIO: Reweighting Negative Trajectory Importance for LLM On-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23104)
*Chen Lin,Kedi Chen,Wei Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ReNIO improves on-policy distillation for LLMs by selectively upweighting incorrect student-generated reasoning trajectories using token-level probability ratios, without needing final answer labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard on-policy distillation treats all student-generated outputs equally, but the authors discover a consistent asymmetry: training only on incorrect outputs outperforms training only on correct ones, as correct outputs lead to shorter reasoning traces and weaker reflection, while incorrect outputs preserve exploratory reasoning near the model's capability boundary.

Method: ReNIO (Reweights Negative trajectory Importance for LLM On-policy distillation) uses the student-to-teacher probability ratio to identify pivotal tokens in wrong reasoning traces, aggregates them into a normalized sample weight, and inherently assigns larger weights to likely negative trajectories without needing final answer correctness. It preserves prefix training advantage over full-rollout RL.

Result: Across mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks, ReNIO improves both OPD and OPSD, with representative relative gains of up to 8.90% for Qwen3-1.7B and 10.00% for R1-Distill-Qwen-7B on mathematical reasoning benchmarks.

Conclusion: ReNIO effectively leverages the asymmetric signal in student-generated outputs to enhance on-policy distillation without requiring answer labels, achieving significant improvements across models and tasks.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) improves LLM reasoning by training a student model on its own generated outputs, but standard OPD treats all student-generated outputs (SGOs) equally regardless of their informativeness. We observe a consistent asymmetry in controlled filtering experiments: in both OPD and on-policy self distillation (OPSD), training only on incorrect SGOs outperforms training only on correct ones. Our further analysis suggests that models trained on correct-only SGOs tend to generate shorter reasoning traces and show weaker reflection behavior, while incorrect SGOs better preserve exploratory reasoning near the model's capability boundary. To exploit this signal without requiring full answer-containing rollouts, we introduce ReNIO, which Reweights Negative trajectory Importance for LLM On-policy distillation. By using the student-to-teacher probability ratio, ReNIO identifies pivotal tokens leading to wrong reasoning traces and aggregates their information into a normalized sample weight, inherently assigning larger weights to likely negative trajectories without observing the correctness of final-answer. Since Re-NIO only uses prefix-conditioned token probabilities, it preserves OPD's prefix training advantage over full-rollout reinforcement learning. Across both mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks, ReNIO improves both OPD and OPSD, with representative relative gains of up to 8.90% for Qwen3-1.7B and 10.00% for R1-Distill-Qwen-7B on mathematical reasoning benchmarks. Code repo: https://github.com/BDML-lab/ReNIO.

</details>


### [839] [Self-Evolution for Multi-Turn Tool-Calling Agents via Divergence-Point Preference Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23112)
*Jiaqiang Tang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ToolGraph combines a graph-based tool orchestration layer with DPO preference tuning to improve multi-turn tool-using agents, achieving +16.8% reward improvement over baseline across 375 tau2-bench tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-turn tool-using agents separate inference-time orchestration from parameter-level learning, leading to weakly structured tool selection and vulnerability to train-deployment prompt mismatch, motivating a unified approach.

Method: ToolGraph uses schema-derived topology, transition weights from successful rollouts, and history-aware controls. 161 preference pairs are constructed via state-based matching and prefix alignment, filtered by action-correctness annotations, then DPO is trained under the same ToolGraph context used at inference.

Result: Across 375 tau2-bench tasks, ToolGraph raises weighted average reward from 0.304 to 0.338 (+11.2%). ToolGraph+DPO reaches 0.355 (+16.8% over baseline), with DPO gains concentrated in airline and retail domains.

Conclusion: Graph-structured orchestration and in-benchmark DPO fine-tuning can effectively improve tool-using agent performance, with chosen reward positivity being the most useful checkpoint signal across DPO configurations. However, half of telecom trajectories exhaust step budgets before action execution, indicating a bottleneck.

Abstract: Multi-turn tool-using agents must coordinate long-horizon tool sequences while tracking dialogue state and policy constraints. Existing approaches often separate inference-time orchestration from parameter-level learning, leaving tool selection weakly structured and preference updates vulnerable to train--deployment prompt mismatch. For within-benchmark self-improvement, ToolGraph combines schema-derived topology, transition weights estimated from successful rollouts, and history-aware controls for write prerequisites and repeated-search loops. We then construct 161 preference pairs by locating divergence points via state-based matching and prefix-based alignment, filtered through action-correctness annotations, and train DPO under the same ToolGraph context used at inference. Across 375 tau2-bench tasks, ToolGraph raises the weighted average reward from 0.304 to 0.338 (+11.2% relative), while ToolGraph+DPO reaches 0.355 (+16.8% over the baseline), with the DPO gain concentrated in airline and retail. Fine-grained diagnostics further show that roughly half of telecom trajectories exhaust the step budget before action execution and that chosen reward positivity is the most useful checkpoint signal across our 16 evaluated DPO configurations.

</details>


### [840] [Temporal-Spectral Alignment with Frequency Adaptation for Source-Free Time-Series Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23120)
*Shichang Meng,Linquan Wu,Xuan Ai,Linqi Song*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAFA is a source-free domain adaptation method for time-series data that aligns target and source domains by jointly modeling temporal and spectral characteristics, using a trainable frequency adaptation module to modulate phase and amplitude in the frequency domain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SFDA methods for time-series data overlook spectral shifts, focusing only on temporal dynamics. This paper aims to address both feature shift and temporal drift by incorporating spectral alignment.

Method: 1) Multi-scale source modeling that captures both temporal dependencies and spectral characteristics. 2) A trainable frequency adaptation module that modulates phase and amplitude of target signals in the frequency domain to align with source distribution.

Result: Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate the efficacy and robustness of SAFA.

Conclusion: SAFA effectively addresses spectral shifts in time-series SFDA by combining temporal-spectral alignment with frequency adaptation, outperforming existing approaches.

Abstract: The goal of source-free domain adaptation (SFDA) for time-series data is to transfer knowledge from a pre-trained source model to an unlabeled target domain without requiring access to source data, while addressing feature shift and temporal drift inherent in the signals. Although existing approaches have explored temporal dynamics in unsupervised source-free adaptation, they largely overlook spectral shifts in time-series data. Towards this end, we propose a novel approach termed temporal-Spectral Alignment with Frequency Adaptation (SAFA) for source-free time-series domain adaptation. Specifically, we first model the source domain at multiple scales by jointly capturing temporal dependencies and spectral characteristics. To adapt time-series data in the target domain, we introduce a trainable frequency adaptation module that modulates the phase and amplitude of target signals in the frequency domain to align them with the source distribution. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate the efficacy and robustness of SAFA.

</details>


### [841] [The Fractal Neural Operator: Overcoming Spectral Bias in Chaotic Attractors via Prime-Harmonic Weierstrass Encodings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23123)
*Kanishk Awadhiya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fractal Neural Operator (FNO) uses a prime-number-based Harmonic Weierstrass Encoder to inject infinite spectral resolution into latent space, extending Lorenz-63 prediction to 347 Lyapunov times—2.3x better than Reservoir Computing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard deep learning models (Transformers, Neural Operators) exhibit spectral bias, acting as low-pass filters that fail on chaotic dynamical systems where strange attractors have fractal geometry and infinite spectral density.

Method: FNO replaces standard geometric encodings (2^k) with a non-resonant prime number basis via a Harmonic Weierstrass Encoder, providing infinite spectral resolution without spectral gaps or resonance artifacts.

Result: FNO achieves 347 Lyapunov times on Lorenz-63, outperforming SOTA Reservoir Computing by 2.3x.

Conclusion: Chaos is not inherently unpredictable to NNs; it requires non-differentiable, fractal embedding manifolds to overcome spectral bias.

Abstract: Deep learning models, particularly Transformers and Neural Operators, exhibit a well-documented "spectral bias," effectively acting as low-pass filters that smooth out high-frequency information. While benign in fluid dynamics, this bias is catastrophic for Chaotic Dynamical Systems, where the underlying strange attractor is characterized by fractal geometry and infinite spectral density. We introduce the Fractal Neural Operator (FNO), a novel architecture that utilizes a non-resonant prime number basis to approximate continuous dynamical systems. Unlike geometric encodings ($2^k$), which suffer from spectral gaps and resonance, our Harmonic Weierstrass Encoder injects infinite spectral resolution into the latent space. We demonstrate that FNO extends the valid prediction horizon of the Lorenz-63 system to 347 Lyapunov times, exceeding state-of-the-art Reservoir Computing baselines by a factor of 2.3x. These results suggest that "chaos" is not inherently unpredictable to neural networks, but rather requires non-differentiable, fractal embedding manifolds.

</details>


### [842] [Weighted Score-Oriented Losses for Temporally Localized Event Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23145)
*Edoardo Legnaro,Sabrina Guastavino,Francesco Marchetti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a temporally localized weighted score-oriented loss (wSOL) for event prediction that aligns training objectives with event-based evaluation metrics, showing improved performance on time-series benchmark datasets compared to cross-entropy and unweighted alternatives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Operational event-detection systems are assessed using event-based metrics (e.g., detection windows, false-alarm costs) rather than pointwise accuracy, creating a mismatch between training loss (e.g., cross-entropy) and deployment evaluation. This score-loss mismatch motivates a training objective that better reflects temporal evaluation criteria.

Method: The paper extends the weighted score-oriented loss (wSOL) framework by introducing temporal weights that discount near-event false positives and reduce false-negative penalties when an event is preceded by an admissible alarm. The resulting objective is differentiable and optimizable via back-propagation, supporting metrics like balanced accuracy, F1, and critical success index.

Result: Evaluated on three time-series benchmark datasets for event prediction/detection, wSOL outperformed cross-entropy and unweighted score-oriented loss when evaluation utility is temporally localized and not already encoded by pointwise labels.

Conclusion: Temporally weighted score-oriented losses can effectively bridge the gap between training objectives and deployment metrics in event detection, improving performance under realistic evaluation conditions.

Abstract: Operational event-detection systems are rarely assessed by pointwise accuracy alone. In anomaly detection, changepoint detection, and warning systems, the utility of an alarm depends on its temporal position relative to an event. This produces a score-loss mismatch. Neural networks are commonly trained with classical loss functions, such as cross-entropy, whereas deployment decisions are obtained by thresholding network predictions, merging alarms through post-processing rules, and evaluating them with event-based metrics defined by detection windows and false-alarm costs. This paper studies a temporally localized specialization of weighted score-oriented loss (wSOL) for event prediction. Starting from score-oriented losses based on expected confusion matrices and from the weighted SOL framework of Marchetti et al., we consider temporal weights that discount near-event false positives and reduce false-negative penalties when an event is preceded by an admissible alarm. The resulting objective is differentiable with respect to the network predictions, and therefore can be optimized by back-propagation. It can be instantiated with balanced accuracy, true skill statistic, F1, critical success index, and related confusion-matrix scores. We evaluate the proposed approach by comparing cross-entropy, unweighted score-oriented loss, and wSOL on three benchmark datasets for time-series event prediction and detection. The results show that wSOL can improve performance when the evaluation utility is localized in time and is not already encoded by the pointwise labels.

</details>


### [843] [Substitution-Based Analysis of Structural Novelty for Generative Models of Materials](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23166)
*Masahiro Negishi,Aron Walsh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper evaluates AI-generated inorganic crystal designs and finds that 81-92% of chemically valid and metastable crystals are either duplicates of training structures or obtainable via elemental substitution, revealing current generative AI models' bias toward known high-symmetry structural prototypes while offering limited exploration in low-symmetry structural space.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess whether AI generative models for inorganic crystal design genuinely expand the accessible materials search space beyond conventional strategies like elemental substitution within known structure types.

Method: A workflow is developed to classify AI-generated crystals as training duplicates, substitution-derived structures, or unmatched by either criterion. Representative generative models are tested against this workflow, and structural fingerprint analysis is performed to interpret the origins of duplicates vs. novel structures.

Result: 81-92% of chemically valid and metastable AI-generated crystals are either training duplicates or substitution-derived. High-symmetry duplicates are linked to memorization in data-sparse regions, while low-symmetry novel structures arise from interpolation in data-rich regions.

Conclusion: Current generative AI models have a notable limitation: they exhibit strong bias toward known high-symmetry structural prototypes, limiting novelty in high-symmetry space, but they do enable wider exploration of low-symmetry structural space.

Abstract: There has been rapid progress in generative artificial intelligence (AI) models for inorganic crystal design, which can efficiently generate large numbers of candidate compounds after being trained on databases of known crystals. However, it remains unclear whether they genuinely expand the accessible materials search space beyond conventional strategies such as elemental substitution within known structure types. We address this question by developing a workflow to assess whether AI-generated crystals are duplicates of training structures, reproducible by elemental substitution, or unmatched by either criterion. Applying this workflow to representative generative models reveals that 81-92% of chemically valid and metastable generated crystals are either training duplicates or substitution-derived structures. This tendency is particularly strong in high-symmetry crystal systems, even though many possible structural prototypes remain unexplored. Further analysis of the underlying structural fingerprints shows that low-symmetry structures beyond duplication or substitution can be interpreted as interpolation in training-data-rich regions, while high-symmetry duplicates appear to result from memorisation in training-sparse regions. Our findings highlight a limitation in the current generation of models that exhibit a bias towards known structural prototypes in the high symmetry regions, but enable wider exploration of the low-symmetry structural space.

</details>


### [844] [Position: Correct Answer, Wrong Mechanism -- When AI Scientists Defend General Claims Their Own Data Contradicts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23175)
*Steven Young Eulig*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper argues that AI scientist systems should not be evaluated solely on final outcomes, but also on mechanism fidelity and epistemic honesty. Through 28 episodes of a coding agent in a physics simulation task, they find frequent "Correct Answer, Wrong Mechanism" (CAWM) cases where agents produce right answers through flawed reasoning. They propose lightweight tests to detect such failures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation of AI scientist systems focuses only on final answers, ignoring whether the reasoning process is sound. This is problematic because incorrect mechanisms that happen to produce correct answers can lead to unreliable scientific claims.

Method: Analyzed 28 episodes of a coding agent attempting to rediscover a known particle identification observable in Geant4 simulation, including 8 cross-model probe episodes. Evaluated task outcome, mechanism fidelity, and epistemic honesty as separate dimensions.

Result: Found 4/20 primary-model and 3/8 cross-model episodes exhibited CAWM (Correct Answer, Wrong Mechanism). Honesty and mechanism fidelity dissociate within single agent trajectories. Five agents rejected false prior components but one defended its observable with self-inconsistent physics.

Conclusion: Coding agents are reliable tools but unreliable co-authors for open-ended claim-making. The proposed lightweight tests (one-step regime-shift check + companion recomputation) successfully flagged all CAWM cases in the study.

Abstract: AI scientist systems are described as tools, coauthors, or founders, but we evaluate them as if only the final answer matters. This position paper argues that outcome-only evaluation is insufficient, and that task outcome, mechanism fidelity, and epistemic honesty must be measured separately. Our evidence comes from 28 episodes of a coding agent attempting to rediscover a known particle identification observable in a Geant4 simulation, including an 8-episode probe across two additional frontier models. In 4/20 primary-model and 3/8 cross-model episodes, agents reach right-looking results through incorrect reasoning that breaks when conditions change, which we call Correct Answer, Wrong Mechanism (CAWM). Honesty and mechanism fidelity dissociate within a single agent trajectory. When given a partially misleading prior, all five agents reject the false component on evidence, yet one defends its chosen observable with physics inconsistent with its own data. In the simulation-based discovery setting studied here, coding agents prove reliable tools but unreliable scientific co-authors for open-ended claim-making, where co-author trust requires mechanism-fidelity verification they do not reliably self-apply. The failure is detectable, and we propose a lightweight test. A one-step regime-shift check needs only the agent's claim and flags the over-generalized cases. A companion recomputation flags the remaining cases when the correct observable is known. Together, these checks flag every CAWM case in this study.

</details>


### [845] [EML Trees Are Universal Approximators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23179)
*Joe Germany,Elie Abdo,Joseph Bakarji*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces EML (Exp-Minus-Log) trees as continuous analogs of NAND gates, proves they can universally approximate functions in Sobolev spaces $W^{k,\infty}$, and demonstrates a practical learning algorithm for fitting parameters in optimization problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish a theoretically grounded compositional framework for function approximation using EML functions as building blocks, bridging the gap between discrete logic gates and continuous function approximation.

Method: The authors use classical neural network approximation arguments combined with explicit construction of EML trees that mimic polynomial representations to prove universal approximation. They also propose a learning algorithm for EML trees with fitting parameters.

Result: EML trees achieve universal approximation for functions in $W^{k,\infty}$ ($k \in \mathbb N$), and the proposed learning algorithm shows feasibility in practical optimization problems.

Conclusion: EML trees provide a theoretically sound framework for function approximation, with both strong approximation guarantees and practical learnability.

Abstract: The recently introduced EML (Exp-Minus-Log) function acts as continuous analogue of NAND gates, providing a compositional building block capable of representing elementary functions. In this work, we study the expressive power of tree-structured compositions of EML functions. We show that such trees enjoy a universal approximation property for functions in $W^{k, \infty}$ for $k \in \mathbb N$, drawing on classical neural network approximation arguments while exploiting the ability to explicitly construct EML trees that mimic polynomial representations. We further propose a learning algorithm for EML-type trees equipped with fitting parameters, and demonstrate its feasibility in practical optimization problems. Our results establish EML trees as a theoretically grounded framework for function approximation.

</details>


### [846] [Stage-dependent integer-binary encoding in factorization-machine black-box optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23188)
*Ryo Ogawa,Mayumi Nakano,Yuya Seki,Shu Tanaka*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a stage-dependent encoding framework for FMQA in black-box optimization, where one-hot encoding is used for surrogate learning and domain-wall encoding for Ising-machine search. One-hot learning is the primary performance driver, and switching to domain-wall search benefits finer discretizations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional FMQA uses a single integer-binary encoding throughout the optimization process, but the optimal encoding for surrogate learning may differ from that for Ising-machine solution search. This mismatch motivates a stage-dependent encoding approach.

Method: The authors propose a stage-dependent FMQA framework with conversion formulas between one-hot and domain-wall QUBO matrices that preserve the surrogate objective over feasible integer states up to an additive constant. They introduce the OhDw variant (one-hot for learning, domain-wall for search) and evaluate it on the Rastrigin function.

Result: Across all conditions, one-hot encoding in the learning stage yields lower residual errors than domain-wall or binary encoding. OhDw outperforms one-hot-only FMQA for N=5, q=301, but one-hot-only performs better for N=5, q=61.

Conclusion: One-hot encoding in the learning stage is the primary driver of optimization performance, and stage-dependent encoding can provide further improvement under finer discretization.

Abstract: Black-box optimization (BBO) deals with problems where objective functions lack explicit analytical forms and are expensive to evaluate. Factorization machine with quadratic-optimization annealing (FMQA) constructs a surrogate model using a factorization machine (FM) and optimizes it with an Ising machine. Conventional FMQA applies a single integer-binary encoding throughout the optimization process, although the encoding best suited to surrogate learning may differ from the one best suited to Ising-machine solution search. We propose a stage-dependent FMQA framework and derive conversion formulas between one-hot and domain-wall QUBO matrices that preserve the surrogate objective over feasible integer states up to an additive constant. We evaluate the OhDw variant, which employs one-hot encoding for learning and domain-wall encoding for search, on the Rastrigin function with input dimensions N = 2 and 5 and discretization levels q = 61 and 301. Across all conditions, the dominant factor governing optimization performance is the encoding used in the learning stage, with one-hot encoding consistently yielding lower residual errors than domain-wall or binary encoding. The additional benefit of switching to domain-wall encoding for solution search is condition-dependent. For N = 5 and q = 301, OhDw achieves a lower residual error and solutions closer to the global optimum than one-hot-only FMQA, whereas for N = 5 and q = 61 the latter achieves a lower residual error. These results indicate that one-hot encoding in the learning stage is the primary performance driver and that stage-dependent encoding can provide further improvement under finer discretization.

</details>


### [847] [Memory Contagion: Cross-Temporal Propagation of Evaluator Bias via Agent Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23195)
*Zewen Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies and formalizes "Memory Contagion" — the cross-temporal propagation of evaluator bias through LLM agent memory systems. It shows that biased experiences stored in memory can influence future agents even with perfect consolidation, and that bias propagation occurs at contamination rates as low as p=0.2, with effects varying by bias type.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing research on agent memory degradation assumes memories are derived from unbiased experiences, overlooking the possibility that biased evaluators can introduce systematic distortions into agent memories that then propagate across time.

Method: The authors formalize Memory Contagion as cross-temporal bias propagation, testing two bias types (length preference, authority bias) across four experimental phases, including a perfect consolidation (oracle) condition to isolate the effect of biased input alone.

Result: (1) Memory Contagion occurs even with perfect (oracle) consolidation; (2) Consolidation attenuates length bias but amplifies authority bias, showing bias-type-dependent interaction; (3) No safe threshold — bias propagates at contamination rates as low as p=0.2.

Conclusion: Current agent memory designs have a critical vulnerability: biased evaluator input alone is sufficient to cause cross-temporal bias propagation, and there is no safe contamination threshold. The paper provides formal tools for measuring this phenomenon.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents increasingly rely on memory systems to maintain long-term coherence. Recent work shows that agent memories degrade during continuous consolidation. However, existing research assumes memories are derived from unbiased experiences. In this work, we identify and formalize a novel phenomenon: Memory Contagion -- the cross-temporal propagation of evaluator bias through agent memory. We show that when agents are trained or guided by biased evaluators, their experiences become biased; when these trajectories are stored and consolidated into memory, the bias propagates to future agents retrieving from the same memory store, even when consolidation is perfect (oracle). Across two bias types (length preference, authority bias) and four experimental phases, we demonstrate: (1) Memory Contagion occurs even with perfect consolidation (oracle condition), proving that biased input is a sufficient cause of contagion; (2) Consolidation has opposite effects depending on bias type -- robustly attenuating length bias while preliminarily amplifying authority bias (single-run estimate), suggesting a bias-type-dependent interaction; (3) No observed safe threshold: bias propagation is detected at contamination rates as low as p=0.2. Our findings expose a critical vulnerability in current agent memory designs and provide formal tools for measuring cross-temporal bias propagation.

</details>


### [848] [Bridge the Gaps: Heterogeneous Attributed Graph Clustering via Quaternion Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23199)
*Xinxi Chen,Junyang Chen,Yiqun Zhang,Chuangming Qiu,Xiang Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AGREE is an end-to-end attributed graph clustering framework that handles heterogeneous attributes (numerical & categorical), tackles over-smoothing and over-dominating effects via quaternion-based graph convolution and shallow architectures, and achieves strong performance without requiring predefined cluster numbers during training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods struggle with three key challenges: (1) heterogeneous attribute types (numerical + categorical) in real-world graphs, (2) over-smoothing (OS) where repeated propagation makes node embeddings too similar, and (3) over-dominating (OD) where topological influence overshadows discriminative attribute information during representation learning.

Method: AGREE unifies any-type attributed data via multi-level alignment and similarity-based graph construction. It uses quaternion-based graph convolution to strengthen attribute interaction (mitigating OD) and shallow graph architectures (relieving OS). The framework jointly optimizes for graph reconstruction and clustering in an end-to-end manner without requiring predefined cluster numbers during training.

Result: Experiments on diverse benchmarks demonstrate that AGREE achieves strong overall performance in accuracy, robustness, and adaptability compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: AGREE effectively addresses attribute heterogeneity, over-smoothing, and over-dominating in attributed graph clustering, offering a unified and adaptable solution that performs well across varied datasets.

Abstract: Attributed graph clustering partitions nodes by jointly exploiting node attributes and graph topology. It remains challenging due to attribute heterogeneity and representation degradation during graph learning. Real-world datasets often contain heterogeneous attributes, i.e., numerical and categorical attributes, complicating unified representation learning. This challenge becomes more complex in attributed graphs, where constructing a clustering-friendly graph structure from attributes and topology remains difficult. Under deep graph architectures, repeated graph propagation causes node embeddings to become overly similar, leading to the over-smoothing (OS) effect. Meanwhile, graph representation learning amplifies topological influence, making discriminative attribute information harder to exploit for clustering, an effect we refer to as over-dominating (OD). To bridge these gaps, an end-to-end framework, Any-type attributed Graph REpresentation lEarning (AGREE), is proposed. It unifies attributed graphs and any-type attributed data through multi-level alignment and similarity-based graph construction. Quaternion-based graph convolution strengthens attribute interaction to alleviate OD, while shallow graph architectures help relieve OS. The learned embeddings are jointly optimized for graph reconstruction and clustering, without requiring a predefined number of clusters during training. Experiments on diverse benchmarks show that AGREE achieves strong overall performance in accuracy, robustness, and adaptability.

</details>


### [849] [Leveraging AutoML for Sustainable Deep Learning: A Multi-Objective HPO Approach on Deep Shift Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23208)
*Leona Hennig,Marius Lindauer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper uses AutoML-based multi-fidelity multi-objective optimization to design Deep Shift Neural Networks (DSNNs) that achieve up to 20% performance improvement and over 60% emission reduction, revealing surprising quantization trade-offs where low-precision on smaller network portions can be optimal for energy efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep Learning models have high computational demands causing environmental and resource challenges. DSNNs offer a solution via shift operations but are less understood and optimized than common DNNs.

Method: The authors combine multi-fidelity hyperparameter optimization (HPO) with multi-objective optimization (accuracy vs. energy consumption) using AutoML techniques to find Pareto optimal DSNN configurations for image classification.

Result: Achieved significantly better DSNN configurations: ~20% performance increase and >60% emission reduction in some cases. Found that quantizing smaller portions with low precision can be optimal for energy efficiency while maintaining/improving performance—contrary to common expectations.

Conclusion: The results demonstrate the potential of automated DSNN design for low-resource environments and reveal nuanced quantization trade-offs. The automated approach effectively balances energy efficiency and model performance across multiple backbone architectures.

Abstract: Deep Learning (DL) has advanced various fields by extracting complex patterns from large datasets. However, the computational demands of DL models pose environmental and resource challenges. Deep Shift Neural Networks (DSNNs) present a solution by leveraging shift operations to reduce computational complexity at inference. Compared to common DNNs, DSNNs are still less well understood and less well optimized. By leveraging AutoML techniques, we provide valuable insights into the potential of DSNNs and how to design them in a better way. We focus on image classification, a core task in computer vision, especially in low-resource environments. Since we consider complementary objectives such as accuracy and energy consumption, we combine state-of-the-art multi-fidelity (MF) hyperparameter optimization (HPO) with multi-objective optimization to find a set of Pareto optimal trade-offs on how to design DSNNs. Our approach led to significantly better configurations of DSNNs regarding loss and emissions compared to default DSNNs. This includes simultaneously increasing performance by about 20% and reducing emissions, in some cases by more than 60%. Investigating the behavior of quantized networks in terms of both emissions and accuracy, our experiments reveal surprising model-specific trade-offs, yielding the greatest energy savings. For example, in contrast to common expectations, quantizing smaller portions of the network with low precision can be optimal with respect to energy consumption while retaining or improving performance. We corroborated these findings across multiple backbone architectures, highlighting important nuances in quantization strategies and offering an automated approach to balancing energy efficiency and model performance.

</details>


### [850] [Deep learning-based detection of cessation of breathing in pre-term infants](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23213)
*Dineo Serame,Lionel Tarassenko,Mauricio Villarroel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Deep learning models (CNNs, ResNets, ConvNeXt) using routinely monitored NICU signals (IP, ECG, PPG) can detect apnoea-related breathing cessation events in preterm infants. Impedance pneumography (IP) alone achieved ~87-88% balanced accuracy, outperforming ECG/PPG-based surrogates. Multimodal fusion (IP+PPG) with ConvNeXt achieved best results at 88.7% balanced accuracy and 0.75 F1 score.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Apnoea of prematurity remains difficult to detect reliably in the NICU. Existing bedside monitors relying on respiratory rate and oxygen saturation thresholds generate high false-positive alarm rates and miss short/irregular events, motivating the need for improved automated detection using routinely acquired signals without additional hardware.

Method: Evaluated deep learning-based detection (shallow CNN, ResNets, ConvNeXt) of COBE events using impedance pneumography (IP), ECG, and PPG signals from ~430 hours of NICU recordings (24 preterm infants). Three independent reviewers annotated 346 COBE and 608 non-COBE events. Models were compared on an independent held-out test set.

Result: Signal modality influenced performance more than architectural complexity. Unimodal IP models achieved 86.8-88.0% balanced accuracy, outperforming ECG (62.6-69.7%) and PPG (65.1-66.4%). Multimodal fusion gave modest improvements. Best model (ConvNeXt with IP+PPG) achieved 88.7% balanced accuracy and 0.75 F1 score.

Conclusion: Deep learning applied to routinely monitored NICU signals can reliably detect COBE events. Signal modality matters more than model complexity in data-constrained neonatal monitoring settings.

Abstract: Apnoea of prematurity is characterised by recurrent episodes of cessation of breathing and remains difficult to detect reliably using routinely monitored physiological signals in the Neonatal Intensive Care Unit (NICU). Existing bedside monitors rely primarily on respiratory rate and oxygen saturation thresholds, often generating high false-positive alarm rates and missing short or irregular events. Improving automated detection using routinely acquired clinical signals could enhance identification of clinically meaningful events without additional sensing hardware.
  We evaluated deep learning-based detection of apnoea-related Cessation Of BrEathing (COBE) events using impedance pneumography (IP), electrocardiography (ECG), and photoplethysmography (PPG) signals from approximately 430 hours of NICU recordings collected from 24 pre-term infants. Three independent reviewers annotated COBE events, producing a dataset of 346 COBE and 608 non-COBE events. We compared a shallow convolutional neural network (CNN), residual networks (ResNets), and a ConvNeXt architecture using an independent held-out test set.
  Across all architectures, detection performance was influenced more strongly by signal modality than by architectural complexity. Unimodal IP-based models achieved balanced accuracies of 86.8-88.0%, outperforming ECG-derived (62.6-69.7%) and PPG-derived (65.1-66.4%) respiratory surrogates. Multimodal fusion yielded modest improvements over IP alone. The best-performing model, a ConvNeXt architecture combining IP and PPG inputs, achieved 88.7% balanced accuracy and an F1 score of 0.75 on the independent test set.
  These findings demonstrate that deep learning models applied to routinely monitored NICU signals can reliably detect COBE events and highlight the importance of signal modality in data-constrained neonatal monitoring settings.

</details>


### [851] [Unlocking In-Context Learning in Audio-Language Models from Decentralized Medical Audio](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23243)
*Ran Piao,Tsai-Ning Wang,Martijn den Dekker,Linda Moonen,Hareld Kemps,Yuan Lu,Aaqib Saeed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FSC is a multimodal framework for in-context clinical audio diagnosis in low-resource, federated settings. It uses unsupervised clustering to create pseudo-label episodes, aligns audio with language models, and achieves 71.6% accuracy in 2-way 2-shot tasks, outperforming baselines by 9%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable clinical audio diagnosis in low-resource settings where labeled data is scarce across federated hospital clients, avoiding the need for large annotated corpora.

Method: FSC uses three stages: (1) caption-based pretraining to align audio embeddings with the language model, (2) unsupervised clustering to construct pseudo-label episodes, and (3) federated optimization for episodic in-context inference. At test time, it uses a small labeled support set for multimodal reasoning on unseen queries.

Result: 71.6% accuracy in 2-way 2-shot evaluation on respiratory and cardiac conditions, outperforming audio-language baselines by over 9%.

Conclusion: The FSC framework effectively addresses data scarcity and privacy constraints in federated clinical settings, demonstrating strong few-shot diagnostic performance.

Abstract: Clinical audio diagnosis in low-resource settings requires models that identify conditions from minimal examples without large annotated corpora. We propose Federated Self-Contextualization (FSC), a multimodal language model framework for in-context clinical audio diagnosis across federated hospital clients. FSC constructs pseudo-label episodes via unsupervised clustering of audio representations, bypassing scarce real diagnostic labels, and enables contextual reasoning from support-query pairs. Our progressive three-stage pipeline first aligns audio embeddings with the language model via caption-based pretraining, then adapts it for episodic in-context inference through federated optimization. At test time, given a small labeled support set, the model diagnoses an unseen query through multimodal reasoning. On held-out respiratory and cardiac conditions, FSC achieves 71.6% accuracy in 2-way 2-shot evaluation, outperforming audio-language baselines by over 9%.

</details>


### [852] [Dynamic multi-agent deep reinforcement learning-based pricing and incentivization approach in multimodal transportation networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23257)
*Khadidja Kadem,Mostafa Ameli,Carlos Lima Azevedo,Mahdi Zargayouna,Latifa Oukhellou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a multi-agent deep reinforcement learning framework where a public authority and an SMS provider dynamically adjust public transport incentives and SMS fares to balance sustainability, equity, and revenue objectives in multimodal transportation systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SMS demand is concentrated in high-density areas, causing uneven integration and equity issues. Multiple stakeholders (authorities, providers, travelers) have conflicting objectives, and existing approaches lack coordination mechanisms to balance these goals simultaneously.

Method: A multi-agent deep RL framework with two agents: (1) a public authority allocating spatio-temporal public transport incentives, and (2) an SMS provider dynamically adjusting fares. Agents interact with the transportation system and adapt to evolving demand, congestion, and network conditions.

Result: Dynamic incentivization reduces congestion peaks, lowers commuters' costs by ~20% and emissions by ~10%, nearly doubles public transport profit, and supports more equitable benefit distribution. Combined with dynamic SMS pricing, the two RL agents balance conflicting objectives.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a decision-support tool for sustainable and equitable multimodal mobility planning by balancing private provider and public authority objectives.

Abstract: In multimodal transportation systems, shared mobility services (SMSs) are promoted for their potential to enhance flexibility and reduce congestion.
