Centrally, a ridge model built only from in-distribution score statistics predicts the gap for a detector it has never seen (R^2 = 0.47) and for an impairment class it has never seen (R^2 = 0.46); both are significant against a 2000-fold permutation null (p < 0.001) and survive removing the feature that is, by construction, part of the target. The headline findings are synthetic. We then run the pre-registered protocol on three open field corpora: on Jammertest 2024 the cross-detector prediction holds (R^2 = 0.11, p = 0.009), and on SatGrid, whose spoofer power sweep gives a calibrated severity axis, in-distribution AUC overstates higher-severity AUC by up to 0.22 and to the point of sign inversion, with in-distribution AUC and realised gap perfectly rank-correlated (Spearman rho = 1.0). The mechanism survives contact with real data, at smaller magnitude than in simulation. We release the testbed, a software-receiver front end, the ingest adapters and the protocol.

</details>


### [908] [A Novel Grant Prediction Method for 5G NR Terminals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22125)
*Chenhao Wu,Xiaojiang Xu,Yuxuan Li,Yuanhao Xu,Wenhui Xiong,Xiaoyu Fu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A paper proposing IOHMM-BO, a high-order input-output hidden Markov model with Bayesian optimization, to predict data grants in 5G NR for energy-efficient power management, achieving 45.3% accuracy, 5.0% false negative rate, and 43% energy savings on real traces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 5G NR user equipment suffers from high power consumption due to continuous PDCCH monitoring. Predictive dynamic power management (DPM) can save energy by forecasting data grants, but accurate prediction is challenging due to unobservable scheduling states and bursty grant patterns.

Method: The paper proposes IOHMM-BO, a high-order input-output hidden Markov model with Bayesian optimization. It captures long-range dependencies via a compound state and jointly optimizes model order and listening window using Bayesian optimization.

Result: Experiments on real 5G NR traces show IOHMM-BO achieves 45.3% accuracy, 5.0% false negative rate, and 43% energy saving with low computational overhead.

Conclusion: The method provides a balanced trade-off between reliability and energy efficiency for 5G NR power management.

Abstract: 5G NR user equipment suffers from high power consumption due to continuous PDCCH monitoring. Predictive dynamic power management (DPM) can save energy by forecasting data grants, but accurate prediction is challenging due to unobservable scheduling states and bursty grant patterns. This paper proposes IOHMM-BO, a high-order input-output hidden Markov model with Bayesian optimization. Based on real 5G NR traces, we capture long-range dependencies via a compound state and jointly optimize model order and listening window using Bayesian optimization. Experiments on real traces show that IOHMM-BO achieves 45.3% accuracy, 5.0% false negative rate, and 43% energy saving with low computational overhead. The method provides a balanced trade-off between reliability and energy efficiency.

</details>


### [909] [RemoteRF: An Open-Source Platform to Democratize Access to Software-Defined Radios in Wireless Research and Education](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22161)
*Ethan Y. Ge,Ian P. Roberts*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: RemoteRF is an open-source platform that enables remote access to distributed software-defined radios (SDRs) for wireless research and education, democratizing SDR access globally.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SDRs are costly and complex, making them inaccessible to many universities and researchers worldwide; RemoteRF aims to democratize access by enabling remote sharing of SDRs.

Method: RemoteRF creates large-scale testbeds of distributed SDRs centrally managed by a single server, allowing users to access them over the internet for wireless experiments from anywhere.

Result: Over a year of deployment at UCLA, RemoteRF logged nearly 4,000 hours of use by more than 200 students and researchers.

Conclusion: The platform is open-source, extensively documented, and can be deployed quickly, making SDRs accessible for both research (e.g., developing communication techniques, collecting real-world ML data) and education (e.g., active learning lab exercises).

Abstract: Software-defined radios (SDRs) are powerful tools for research and education in wireless communications, but their cost and complexity put them out of reach for many universities and researchers worldwide. To address this, we introduce RemoteRF, a platform for creating large-scale testbeds of distributed SDRs that are centrally managed by a single server. These SDRs can be remotely accessed by users over the internet, allowing them to conduct wireless experiments at any time from virtually anywhere, as long as they have a network connection. When used in research, RemoteRF can be used to develop and experimentally evaluate new communication techniques or to collect real-world data to train and test machine learning models. When used in education, RemoteRF can allow students in virtually any sized class to share a handful of SDRs to complete active learning lab exercises that parallel course lectures. In an effort to democratize access to SDRs across the globe, the software powering RemoteRF has been made open-source and is extensively documented, allowing anyone to deploy their own instance today in a matter of minutes. Over the past year or so, RemoteRF has been used in both teaching and research at UCLA, where it has logged nearly 4,000 hours of use by more than 200 students and researchers to date.

</details>


### [910] [Outage Analysis and Fairness Design for Spatially Correlated FAS-Enabled RSMA Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22287)
*Jinyuan Liu,Yong Liang Guan,Tuo Wu,Hong Niu,Kai-Kit Wong,Bruno Clerckx*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper combines rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) with fluid antenna systems (FAS) in multiuser MISO networks, deriving closed-form outage probability expressions and a fairness optimization algorithm that reduces outage probability by up to 92% compared to fixed-position antennas.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 6G systems demand higher reliability, denser connectivity, and tighter interference control. While RSMA and FAS individually offer benefits for interference management and spatial selectivity, their combined analysis—particularly closed-form outage probability and user fairness optimization—remains unexplored.

Method: The paper develops a spatial correlation model for FAS using block correlation, incorporates linear precoding (ZF and MRT), derives closed-form OP via one-factor construction and Gauss-Laguerre quadrature, and proposes a low-complexity fairness algorithm with linear-program feasibility checks.

Result: FAS-RSMA reduces outage probability by up to 92% relative to fixed-position antenna baselines. The fairness-oriented design equalizes user reliability and delivers a 1 dB SNR gain for the worst user at a fixed outage level.

Conclusion: Combining RSMA with FAS is a promising approach for 6G, enabling both efficient interference control and adaptive antenna utilization; the proposed closed-form expressions and fairness algorithm provide tractable tools for system design.

Abstract: Sixth-generation (6G) systems target higher reliability, denser connectivity, and tighter interference control. {Within this context, rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) is envisioned as a promising candidate to enhance interference management in future wireless networks by flexibly splitting messages into a common and a private part, while fluid antenna systems (FAS) offer the potential to improve spatial selectivity through dynamic port reconfiguration.} Combining RSMA and FAS therefore enables efficient interference control and adaptive antenna utilization in multiuser multi-input single-output (MISO) networks. However, deriving closed-form outage probability (OP) expressions and tractable user fairness optimization in this scenario remains scarce in the literature. This paper studies a multiuser MISO downlink that jointly leverages RSMA and FAS. We develop a spatial correlation model for FAS using block correlation and incorporate linear precoding with zero-forcing and maximum-ratio transmission. Within this model, we derive closed-form OP expressions using a one-factor construction and generalized Gauss-Laguerre quadrature. Building on these expressions, we formulate a fairness objective that minimizes the worst-user OP and propose a low-complexity algorithm with a linear-program feasibility check to obtain the closed-form solution per iteration. Numerical results across different port counts, channel conditions, and target rates validate the analytical analysis, show that FAS-RSMA reduces OP by up to 92% relative to the fixed-position antenna (FPA) baseline, and demonstrate that fairness-oriented design equalizes user reliability while delivering a 1 dB SNR gain for the worst user at a fixed outage level.

</details>


### [911] [Towards Whole Hand and Wrist Kinematic Tracking with a Wearable A-Mode Ultrasound Probe](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22333)
*Giusy Spacone,Luca Benini,Andrea Cossettini*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper presents a wearable A-mode ultrasound system (WULPUS) for tracking 23 degrees of freedom of hand/wrist kinematics, using a compact CNN with incremental training deployed entirely on a microcontroller, achieving low energy (0.73 mJ/inference), low latency (29.1 ms), and 36-hour continuous operation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior A-mode ultrasound works focus on static gestures or few DoFs, rely on non-wearable systems/external computing, and lack robustness to sensor repositioning across sessions.

Method: A compact multi-output CNN (11,285 params) with incremental training strategy, deployed on WULPUS nRF52832 MCU for on-device inference.

Result: 17% MAE reduction via incremental training; 0.73 mJ/inference, 29.1 ms latency, 33 mW total power, 36-hour battery life, 88% bandwidth reduction.

Conclusion: First demonstration of end-to-end hand/wrist kinematic tracking entirely on a wearable ultrasound device, proving feasibility for long-term continuous use.

Abstract: A-mode ultrasound (US) has emerged as a promising modality for hand and wrist motion tracking. Prior works have mainly addressed static gesture classification or regression of a few degrees of freedom (DoFs), typically relying on non-wearable systems and external computing devices, and highlight the need for strategies to ensure robustness to sensor repositioning. In this work, we propose a framework for robust whole-hand and wrist kinematic tracking via wearable A-mode US using the WULPUS platform, tackling the regression of 23 DoFs directly on the probe. First, we introduce a compact (11285 parameters) multi-output convolutional neural network combined with an incremental training strategy, which improves inter-session generalization and reduces mean absolute error by more than 17% compared to a non-incremental approach. Second, we demonstrate, for the first time, the feasibility of end-to-end hand and wrist kinematic tracking entirely on-device. We deploy the model on the WULPUS nRF52832 microcontroller, achieving 0.73 mJ per inference, 29.1 ms latency, and showing the feasibility of full operation (data acquisition, online inference, and BLE streaming of results) within 33 mW, enabling up to 36 hours of continuous use and an 88% reduction in wireless bandwidth compared to raw data transmission.

</details>


### [912] [PAPR Reduction for AFDM by Affine-Domain Circular Shift without Side Information](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22464)
*The Khai Nguyen,Ebrahim Bedeer*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A novel PAPR reduction method for AFDM signals using circular shifting in the affine domain, with an MLB receiver that detects the shift without side information, achieving 2.5-4 dB PAPR reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce the high peak-to-average-power-ratio (PAPR) of affine frequency division multiplexing (AFDM) signals without requiring side information (SI), which degrades power amplifier efficiency and increases error rates.

Method: Circularly shifting the original transmit signal in the affine domain and selecting the candidate with the lowest PAPR; a maximum-likelihood-based (MLB) receiver exploits the AFDM pilot and guard band positions to detect the shift without side information.

Result: Achieves 2.5-4 dB PAPR reduction compared to original AFDM and existing schemes, translating to significantly lower error rates depending on power amplifier quality.

Conclusion: The proposed SI-free circular shifting method effectively reduces PAPR in AFDM systems, improving error rate performance without requiring additional overhead for side information transmission.

Abstract: This paper proposes a novel method to reduce the peak-to-average-power-ratio (PAPR) of affine frequency division multiplexing (AFDM) signals without side information (SI). The method is based on circularly shifting the original transmit signal in the affine domain, and selecting the shifted candidate with the lowest PAPR. Next, a maximum-likelihood-based (MLB) receiver is derived, which exploits the position of the AFDM pilot and guard band to detect the shift applied at the transmitter without SI. Simulation results show that the proposed method can achieve 2.5 to 4 dB in PAPR reduction as compared to original AFDM and existing schemes, which can be translated into significantly lower error rate, depending on the quality of power amplifiers.

</details>


### [913] [Generative Site-Specific Beamforming for UPAs via Decoupled Channel Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22522)
*Yao Tang,Zhaolin Wang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A cross-fused generative site-specific beamforming (GenSSBF) framework for UPA systems reduces beam alignment overhead from multiplicative to linear complexity by decoupling azimuth/elevation sensing, uses bidirectional cross-attention to fuse the lost coupling information, and generates high-fidelity beam candidates via conditional normalizing flow, achieving up to 83.6% gain improvement over full 1024-beam DFT search with 93.8% sweeping overhead reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce the high overhead of exhaustive 2D codebook sweeping for beam alignment in UPA systems by decoupling azimuth and elevation sensing, while recovering the lost coupling information between the two domains.

Method: (1) Decoupled channel sensing: probe azimuth and elevation domains independently, reducing overhead from multiplicative to linear complexity; (2) Bidirectional cross-attention encoder: fuses latent dependencies between azimuth and elevation sensing branches; (3) Conditional normalizing flow generator: generates compact high-fidelity beam candidates from fused features; (4) Lightweight pilot verification for final selection; (5) Task-oriented training objective: encourages at least one high-gain beam in the candidate set.

Result: Outperforms deterministic beam prediction and conventional DFT codebook search; achieves 83.6%, 74.6%, and 38.1% normalized beamforming gain improvements in I2_28, O1B_28, and Boston5G_28 scenarios respectively, with 93.8% sweeping overhead reduction compared to full 1024-beam 2D DFT search.

Conclusion: The GenSSBF framework effectively balances beam alignment accuracy and overhead reduction by decoupling sensing and using generative fusion, demonstrating consistent superiority across diverse DeepMIMO scenarios.

Abstract: A cross-fused generative site-specific beamforming (GenSSBF) framework is proposed for low-overhead beam alignment in uniform planar array (UPA) systems. A decoupled channel sensing strategy is developed, where the azimuth and elevation domains of the UPA are probed independently, and the online sweeping overhead is reduced from multiplicative to linear complexity compared to exhaustive two-dimensional codebook sweeping. However, the resulting reference signal received power (RSRP) observations only contain marginal angular power information. The explicit azimuth-elevation coupling of the UPA channel is therefore lost. Beam generation from these separate observations becomes highly ambiguous. To address this issue, a bidirectional cross-attention encoder is designed to extract and fuse the latent dependency between the azimuth and elevation sensing branches. Conditioned on the fused feature, a conditional normalizing flow generator is proposed to generate a compact set of high-fidelity beam candidates. These candidates are further verified through lightweight pilot measurements for final beam selection. A task-oriented training objective is also introduced to encourage the generated candidate set to contain at least one high-gain beam, rather than fitting the full conditional beam distribution. Simulation results based on DeepMIMO scenarios show that the proposed framework consistently outperforms deterministic beam prediction and conventional discrete Fourier transform (DFT) codebook search. Compared with the full 1024-beam two-dimensional DFT search, normalized beamforming gain improvements of 83.6%, 74.6%, and 38.1% are achieved in the I2_28, O1B_28, and Boston5G_28 scenarios, respectively, while the sweeping overhead is reduced by 93.8%.

</details>


### [914] [Low-Complexity Direct Geolocation of Terrestrial GNSS Jammers from Low Earth Orbit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22751)
*Giacomo Pojani,Javier Tegedor,Joaquim Fortuny-Guasch*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper introduces Quasi-Direct Geolocation (QDG), a low-complexity technique for passively geolocating RF emitters directly in the position domain, demonstrated using a LEO satellite (OPS-SAT PRETTY) to locate a terrestrial jammer at Jammertest 2025. QDG reduces complexity of traditional DG/DPD methods, enabling use on resource-constrained satellites for GNSS interference monitoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable low-SWaP satellites to contribute to multi-constellation RF interference monitoring in GNSS bands by drastically reducing the computational complexity of direct geolocation methods.

Method: Quasi-Direct Geolocation (QDG) — a low-complexity technique that performs passive RF geolocation directly in the position domain, compressing I/Q samples into minimal signal information for either downlink transmission or on-board edge computing.

Result: Experimental demonstration at Jammertest 2025 successfully geolocated a terrestrial jammer from the OPS-SAT PRETTY LEO satellite, validating the QDG approach.

Conclusion: QDG enables resource-constrained satellites to serve as opportunistic spectrum sensors for GNSS RFI monitoring, with acceptable trade-offs in sensitivity and accuracy compared to full DG/DPD methods.

Abstract: This paper introduces a low-complexity technique named quasi-direct geolocation (QDG) to perform passive radio-frequency (RF) geolocation of emitters directly in the position domain, akin to direct geolocation (DG) and direct position determination (DPD). The proposed technique drastically reduces the complexity of DG/DPD and is experimentally demonstrated in geolocating a terrestrial jammer at Jammertest 2025 from a repurposed satellite in low Earth orbit (LEO): OPS-SAT PRETTY. The goal of QDG is to enable satellites to contribute to a multi-constellation system for RF interference (RFI) monitoring as opportunistic spectrum sensors in global navigation satellite systems (GNSS) bands, even if these are constrained by low size, weight, and power (SWaP). They can serve as data collectors and/or edge computers. In the former case, QDG can be used to compress large volumes of I/Q samples into minimal signal information, which can be relayed to ground for post-processing via low-capacity downlinks. In the latter case, QDG can be used to compute the geolocation of RFI sources in orbit on low-power on-board computers (OBC). The drawback of these capabilities is lower sensitivity and accuracy than DG/DPD plus limitations on the types of signal sources that can be geolocated, which, nonetheless, include the most common GNSS jammers.

</details>


### [915] [Radio Resource Allocation for Beam Hopping Scheduling in LEO Satellite Communications: A Spatio-Temporal Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22900)
*Hao Yuan,Lanyining Li,Jianghua Long,Xing Zhang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Proposes a Tabu Search-based spatio-temporal beam-hopping resource allocation strategy for LEO satellite networks that improves system throughput by 17.2% and user satisfaction by 11.7% over greedy baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LEO satellite networks face critical challenges in radio resource allocation due to dynamic traffic demands and stringent interference constraints.

Method: Formulates BH scheduling to maximize user demand satisfaction under interference constraints; uses Tabu Search with adaptive tabu tenure, greedy-based initialization with interference-aware beam selection, and Simulated Annealing acceptance criteria.

Result: Achieves 17.2% improvement in system throughput and 11.7% improvement in user satisfaction compared to greedy-based BH strategies.

Conclusion: Provides a scalable and robust solution for dynamic resource allocation in interference-limited LEO satellite networks.

Abstract: Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite networks face critical challenges in radio resource allocation due to dynamic traffic demands and stringent interference constraints. Beam-hopping (BH) technology offers a promising solution by enabling dynamic beam resource allocation across spatial and temporal domains. In this paper, we propose a Tabu Search-based spatio-temporal BH resource allocation strategy for LEO satellite communication systems. Specifically, the BH scheduling problem is formulated to maximize user demand satisfaction under interference constraints. To solve this problem efficiently, the proposed Tabu Search framework integrates adaptive tabu tenure control, greedy-based initialization with interference-aware beam selection, and Simulated Annealing acceptance criteria. Extensive simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method consistently improves system throughput by 17.2\% and user satisfaction by 11.7\% compared with greedy-based BH strategies. These results indicate that the proposed approach provides a scalable and robust solution for dynamic resource allocation in interference-limited LEO satellite networks.

</details>


### [916] [Integrating Sensing into Covert Communications: Opportunities and Challenges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23073)
*Jun Wu,Xiaoqi Zhang,Haoyuan Pan,Gaosheng Zhao,Dong In Kim,Tse-Tin Chan*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper explores how sensing (environmental and adversary awareness) can enhance covert wireless communications by transitioning from passive concealment to state-aware decision-making, using intelligent sensing paradigms and sensing-assisted beamforming to improve reliability and resource utilization in dynamic environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional covert communication designs rely on blind interference or passive uncertainty, which become ineffective in dynamic propagation environments. The authors aim to address this limitation by leveraging sensing information.

Method: The paper proposes sensing-empowered covert communications, where adversary and environmental information guide transmission and jamming control. It discusses intelligent sensing paradigms that extract task-relevant information with limited active probing, and presents a case study using sensing-assisted beamforming in low-altitude wireless networks.

Result: Sensing-assisted beamforming improves spatial resource utilization and the reliability of covert data delivery in time-varying channels. However, sensing also introduces new challenges related to exposure and resource consumption.

Conclusion: Sensing changes covert system design from passive concealment to state-aware decision-making, and several open issues remain to support more adaptive covert wireless systems.

Abstract: Covert communications aim to hide the existence of wireless transmissions from unauthorized adversaries. However, conventional designs based on blind interference or passive uncertainty can be ineffective in dynamic propagation environments. This article investigates sensing-empowered covert communications, where adversary and environmental information are used to guide transmission and jamming control. We show how sensing changes covert system design from passive concealment to state-aware decision-making, while also introducing new challenges related to exposure and resource consumption. We further discuss several intelligent sensing paradigms that extract task-relevant information with limited active probing. A case study in low-altitude wireless networks illustrates that sensing-assisted beamforming can improve spatial resource utilization and the reliability of covert data delivery in time-varying channels. Finally, several open issues are discussed to support more adaptive covert wireless systems.

</details>


### [917] [LOLLA: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Closed-Loop Link Adaptation Towards a GPU-Accelerated AI-RAN](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23110)
*Rui Wang,Linchao Zhang,Qiang Liu,Kun Yang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: LOLLA replaces conventional OLLA with a deep RL framework that learns a continuous SINR offset for link adaptation in 5G NR, achieving 15-92% throughput gains under high mobility while maintaining 3GPP compliance and sub-500μs control latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional OLLA relies on first-order, single-bit feedback and suffers significant performance degradation under high-mobility and fast-varying channels.

Method: A deep RL (PPO) framework that learns a continuous SINR offset conditioned on rich PHY/MAC telemetry, with Lagrangian BLER constraint for tunable reliability targets, realized as a closed-loop AI-native control dApp on a GPU-accelerated 5G NR stack.

Result: 15-92% throughput gains over OLLA across Doppler frequencies up to 400 Hz, Pareto-dominant performance across reliability targets (1-15%), generalization to unseen channel models, scalability to 8 concurrent UEs under shared scheduling.

Conclusion: LOLLA provably subsumes conventional OLLA and demonstrates that learned, continuous offset policies can significantly outperform discrete staircase approaches in dynamic channel conditions while preserving 3GPP compliance.

Abstract: Outer-loop link adaptation (OLLA) is widely deployed in 5G NR to track channel variations, yet its reliance on first-order, single-bit feedback degrades performance significantly under high-mobility and fast-varying channels. This paper presents LOLLA (Learned Outer-Loop Link Adaptation), a deep reinforcement learning framework that replaces the conventional OLLA staircase with a learned, continuous SINR offset conditioned on rich PHY/MAC telemetry inaccessible to OLLA. The offset modulates the SINR-to-MCS lookup table, preserving 3GPP-compliant MCS selection and provably subsuming the conventional OLLA update rule. A Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) policy trained under a Lagrangian block error rate (BLER) constraint automatically enforces tunable reliability targets from 1% to 15% without manual penalty calibration. The framework is realized as the first closed-loop AI-native control dApp on a GPU-accelerated 5G NR stack, achieving end-to-end control latencies under 500 microseconds. Evaluations under 3GPP TDL channel models demonstrate 15% to 92% throughput gains over OLLA across Doppler frequencies up to 400 Hz, while attaining a Pareto frontier that strictly dominates OLLA across all evaluated reliability targets. The learned policy generalizes to unseen channel models and scales to eight concurrent UEs under shared-resource scheduling. In the uplink formulation, the gNB directly observes decoding outcomes, enabling simulation-to-deployment parity.

</details>


### [918] [Fault Inception Detection in Real-World Disturbance Data for Power System Protection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23111)
*Julian Oelhaf,Mehran Pashaei,Paula Andrea Perez-Toro,Georg Kordowich,Christian Bergler,Andreas Maier,Johann Jaeger,Siming Bayer*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a training-free fault inception detection framework that combines protection-domain indicators, robust normalization, and fusion logic to detect fault onsets in real-world transmission disturbance recordings, achieving 96.6% recall and 79.2% precision on 300 reviewed events from the RTE database.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large collections of real-world disturbance recordings are increasingly available but lack precise event-onset annotations, limiting their value for protection and automated analysis. Non-ideal effects in field recordings (switching, transformer energization, resonance, saturation) obscure genuine fault signatures, making reliable detection difficult.

Method: A training-free framework combining: (1) protection-domain indicators, (2) robust median/MAD-based normalization, (3) a low-latency transient path, and (4) persistence-aware fusion and veto logic to distinguish fault-consistent disturbances from non-fault transients.

Result: Applied to 12,053 recordings from the RTE database; on a manually reviewed 300-event subset, achieved 96.6% recall, 79.2% precision, and a median timing error of 4.2ms for matched detections.

Conclusion: The approach can support protection-oriented disturbance screening, relay and post-event analysis, and creation of timestamp annotations for downstream data-driven monitoring tasks.

Abstract: Large collections of real-world disturbance recordings are increasingly available in transmission networks, but their value for power system protection and automated disturbance analysis is limited by the absence of precise event-onset annotations. In practice, field-recorded voltage and current waveforms contain switching operations, transformer energization, resonance, saturation, and other non-ideal effects that can obscure or mimic genuine fault signatures, making reliable fault inception detection difficult. This paper presents an training-free framework for fault inception detection in real-world transmission disturbance data. The method combines protection-domain indicators, robust median/MAD-based normalization, a low-latency transient path, and persistence-aware fusion and veto logic to distinguish fault-consistent disturbances from non-fault transients. We apply the framework to 12053 transmission-level recordings from the publicly available RTE database and further assess detector performance on a manually reviewed subset of 300 events. On the reviewed subset, the detector achieves 96.6% recall, 79.2% precision, and a median timing error of 4.2ms for matched detections. These results indicate that the proposed approach can support protection-oriented disturbance screening, relay and post-event analysis, and the creation of timestamp annotations for downstream data-driven monitoring tasks.

</details>


### [919] [AI-Empowered UAV-Assisted Backscatter Localization and ISAC for Zero-Energy IoT: A Comprehensive Survey](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23125)
*Ruhul Amin Khalil*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This article surveys AI-empowered UAV-assisted backscatter localization and integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) for zero-energy IoT, covering enabling technologies, taxonomies, comparative analysis, and future research directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Zero-energy IoT aims to enable passive devices operating on harvested energy, but backscatter communication (BackCom) suffers from limitations like weak reflections, double-path loss, limited coverage, and interference. UAVs and ISAC can mitigate these issues, motivating a comprehensive survey of AI-empowered UAV-assisted backscatter localization and ISAC systems.

Method: The paper follows a PRISMA-informed structured methodology, developing a unified taxonomy covering network architectures, UAV roles, backscatter modes, RF sources, localization and sensing functions, AI techniques, and performance metrics. It includes comparative tables, quantitative trend analysis, coverage evaluation, and tutorial-style numerical illustrations.

Result: The survey presents a comprehensive review of UAV-assisted BackCom, passive localization, ISAC-enabled UAV-backscatter systems, and AI-driven optimization, supported by comparative analysis and quantitative evaluations.

Conclusion: The paper identifies open challenges and future directions in realistic channel modeling, energy-neutral operation, benchmarking, reproducibility, scalable and trustworthy AI, security, privacy, hardware validation, and integration with RIS, MEC, digital twins, and 6G technologies.

Abstract: Zero-energy Internet of Things (IoT) enables passive or near-passive devices to operate on harvested energy rather than batteries. Backscatter communication (BackCom) supports this vision by enabling tags to transmit data via reflection and modulation of incident RF signals, but it suffers from weak reflections, double-path loss, limited coverage, direct-link interference, and dependence on external RF sources. Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) can mitigate these limitations by acting as mobile carrier emitters, data collectors, relays, aerial receivers, mobile anchors, sensing platforms, and edge-intelligence nodes. Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) further enables the sharing of wireless resources for data transmission, localization, target sensing, and environmental awareness. This article surveys RF-based AI-empowered UAV-assisted backscatter localization and ISAC for zero-energy IoT. It reviews enabling technologies, presents a structured PRISMA-informed methodology, and develops a unified taxonomy covering network architectures, UAV roles, backscatter modes, RF sources, localization and sensing functions, AI techniques, and performance metrics. It also discusses UAV-assisted BackCom, passive localization, ISAC-enabled UAV-backscatter systems, and AI-driven optimization through comparative tables, quantitative trend analysis, coverage evaluation, and tutorial-style numerical illustrations. Finally, it identifies open challenges and future directions in realistic channel modeling, energy-neutral operation, benchmarking, reproducibility, scalable and trustworthy AI, security, privacy, hardware validation, and integration with RIS, MEC, digital twins, and 6G technologies.

</details>


### [920] [When Distortion Helps: Secure GNN Precoding with Nonlinear Power Amplifiers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23141)
*Reza Ghasemi Alavicheh,Thomas Feys,Md Arifur Rahman,François Rottenberg*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Proposes a GNN-based precoding framework that exploits PA nonlinear distortion to enhance physical layer security in MISO wiretap channels, achieving significant secrecy rate gains over traditional methods without needing eavesdropper CSI or dedicated AN power allocation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PLS techniques assume ideal linear transmitters, but real-world PAs introduce nonlinear distortion which is typically viewed as harmful. This paper explores turning this nonlinearity into a security asset.

Method: A GNN-based precoding framework for multi-user MISO wiretap channels that learns precoding strategies from legitimate users' channel data, using Bussgang decomposition and a high-order polynomial PA model to derive an analytical secrecy rate as the training objective.

Result: At 22 dB SNR under severe PA saturation (IBO = -1 dB), the GNN achieves 39.89% and 35.26% higher sum secrecy rate over MRT and ZF, 17.99% over AN-aided MRT and 8.67% over AN-aided ZF, with 58.13-75.31% lower standard deviation across all baselines.

Conclusion: PA nonlinear distortion can be exploited as a security mechanism, and GNN-based learning provides an effective solution for the resulting non-convex optimization problem without requiring eavesdropper CSI or artificial noise allocation.

Abstract: Physical layer security (PLS) provides information-theoretic protection against eavesdropping. While existing techniques assume ideal linear transmitters, power amplifiers (PAs) in practice introduce nonlinear distortion, typically considered detrimental to signal quality. This paper demonstrates that such distortion can instead be exploited as a security asset by redirecting it toward eavesdroppers, particularly in the power-efficient PA saturation regime. To this end, we propose a graph neural network (GNN)-based precoding framework for multi-user multiple-input single-output (MISO) wiretap channels that maximizes the sum secrecy rate by exploiting PA nonlinearity. Since the resulting optimization is highly non-convex, classical methods are intractable. The GNN instead learns precoding strategies directly from legitimate users' channel data, requiring neither eavesdropper channel state information (CSI) nor dedicated artificial noise (AN) power allocation. For this, the Bussgang decomposition and a high-order polynomial PA model provide an analytical secrecy rate as the training objective. At 22 dB signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) under severe PA saturation with input back-off (IBO) $= -1$ dB, the proposed GNN achieves 39.89% and 35.26% higher sum secrecy rate over maximum ratio transmission (MRT) and zero-forcing (ZF), respectively, 17.99% over AN-aided MRT and 8.67% over AN-aided ZF, with 58.13-75.31% lower standard deviation across all baselines.

</details>


### [921] [Phase Uniformity Detector for GRSMReceivers in mmWave and Sub-THz Bands](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23182)
*Oshin Daoud,Haifa Fares,Yahia Medjahdi,Laurent Clavier,Amor Nafkha*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Phase Uniformity Detector (PUD) for GRSM systems that uses directional statistics and phase-domain detection to robustly detect spatial symbols under phase noise, outperforming conventional energy-based detectors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional Energy Detection (ED) in GRSM systems suffers from performance degradation due to energy fluctuations and noise uncertainty, especially under Independent Local Oscillator Phase Noise (ILO-PN). A more robust detection method is needed.

Method: The PUD uses direct RF sampling, models received signal phases via Directional Statistics (DS), derives a GLRT reduced to a Rayleigh uniformity test with a closed-form threshold, and employs a phase-coherence-aware combining scheme to mitigate ILO-PN without estimation.

Result: The proposed PUD offers robust spatial detection under ILO-PN and remains insensitive to energy fluctuations and noise uncertainty, unlike conventional ED.

Conclusion: The PUD is an effective alternative to ED for GRSM systems under challenging phase noise conditions, providing robust detection with a simple, threshold-independent implementation.

Abstract: This paper introduces a phase-domain statistical detector, the Phase Uniformity Detector (PUD), for binary hypothesis testing in Generalized Receive Spatial Modulation (GRSM) systems. The PUD uses direct RF sampling to obtain received signal samples, their phases are modeled via Directional Statistics (DS). A Generalized Likelihood Ratio Test (GLRT) is derived and reduced to a Rayleigh uniformity test with a closed-form, noise-variance-independent threshold. Unlike conventional Energy Detection (ED), the PUD offers robust spatial detection under Independent Local Oscillator Phase Noise (ILO-PN), remaining insensitive to energy fluctuations and noise uncertainty. Additionally, a phase-coherence-aware combining scheme mitigates ILO-PN without requiring estimation.

</details>


### [922] [Learning to Compute on Dirty Paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23252)
*Shreesal Shrestha,Kuranage Roche Rayan Ranasinghe,Giuseppe Thadeu Freitas de Abreu,Elza Erkip*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A fully learning-based ICC framework combining DPC with over-the-air computation using neural networks with sinusoidal activations for interference pre-cancellation and function estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop the first fully learning-based approach that jointly addresses DPC-based interference pre-cancellation and over-the-air computation in a unified framework.

Method: Each user uses a neural encoder with sinusoidal activations for DPC-like interference pre-cancellation; a joint neural decoder recovers messages; a separate neural AirComp estimator leverages multi-slot block structure for target function estimation after encoder-decoder convergence.

Result: The neural encoder recovers modulo-like periodic structures consistent with lattice-based DPC schemes, enabling effective interference pre-cancellation.

Conclusion: This is the first fully learning-based approach that unifies DPC and over-the-air computation, demonstrating the potential of neural networks for integrated communication and computing tasks.

Abstract: We propose a fully learning-based approach to integrated communication and computing (ICC) that combines dirty paper coding (DPC) with over-the-air computation. Each user employs a neural encoder with sinusoidal activations that learns to pre-cancel its own computing symbol as non-causally known interference, recovering modulo-like periodic structures consistent with lattice-based DPC schemes. A joint neural decoder recovers all users' messages from the received signal, while a separate neural AirComp estimator exploits a multi-slot block structure to estimate a target function of the computing symbols after the encoder-decoder network converges. To our knowledge, this is the first fully learning-based approach to jointly address DPC-based interference pre-cancellation and over-the-air computation in a unified framework.

</details>


### [923] [Symbol Rate-Code Rate Trade-offs for IM/DD 200G/400G per Lane LPO Transceivers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23390)
*José Núñez-Kasaneva,Yunus Can Gültekin,Stefanos Dris,Paraskevas Bakopoulos,Nikos Argyris,Gabriele Liga*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper investigates the trade-off between symbol rate and code rate in bandwidth-limited IM/DD systems for LPO transceivers, showing that using lower FEC code rates with higher-order PAM formats (PAM-6/8) enables significantly lower symbol rates compared to PAM-4 while meeting 200G/400G throughput requirements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the symbol rate versus code rate trade-off in bandwidth-limited IM/DD systems for LPO transceivers, with the goal of achieving 200G and 400G per-lane throughput.

Method: Analyzed PAM-4/6/8 modulation formats using capacity of the binary symmetric channel under hard-decision decoding as the achievable information rate metric.

Result: Reducing FEC code rate below the KP4 baseline allows higher-order PAM formats to operate at substantially lower symbol rates than PAM-4 while meeting throughput requirements.

Conclusion: Higher-order PAM formats with reduced FEC code rates offer a viable trade-off for achieving target throughput at lower symbol rates in bandwidth-limited systems.

Abstract: We analyze the symbol rate-code rate trade-off in bandwidth-limited IM/DD systems targeting LPO transceivers, using PAM-4/6/8 as candidate modulation formats. We use the capacity of the binary symmetric channel as an achievable information rate under hard-decision decoding, serving as a performance metric for 200G and 400G per-lane throughput targets. Our results show that reducing the FEC code rate below the KP4 baseline allows higher-order PAM formats to operate at substantially lower symbol rates than PAM-4 while meeting the throughput requirement.

</details>


### [924] [Sensor-Stack Limits on Contactless In-Bed Body Position: A 20-Subject Multimodal Radar + Thermal LOSO Characterization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23534)
*Dovy Paukstys*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper evaluates a bedside 60 GHz FMCW radar and low-resolution thermal array for contactless in-bed body-position inference, finding that sensor representation (CFAR point-cloud vs. raw range-FFT) is the key bottleneck, not classifier choice, with prone detection being non-deployable (0.50 recall, 0.41 precision) due to thermal resolution limits and weak breathing cues in CFAR data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is prone-position monitoring for sudden unexpected death in epilepsy (SUDEP), as retrospective studies link prone sleeping position to increased SUDEP risk. The work aims to characterize sensor-level limitations of a combined radar + thermal setup for contactless body-position inference.

Method: A bedside 60 GHz FMCW radar (with on-device CFAR point-cloud output) plus a 24x32 nominal (but effectively lower resolution) thermal array were used on 20 subjects. Two LOSO evaluations were conducted: 273 supervised in-bed posture holds (148.7 min) and an enter/exit bed-presence audit. Multiple classifiers were tested including logistic regression, stacked ensembles, and ablation studies.

Result: Fused radar+thermal logistic regression achieved 0.871 median LOSO balanced accuracy for in/out-of-bed classification. Four-class posture best aggregate balanced accuracy was 0.674 (full-feature stacked ensemble). Prone recall=0.50, precision=0.41. Thermal solved left-vs-right discrimination (radar-only lateral swaps 35-42%; thermal-only ~8%). Supine-vs-prone cue appeared as class-level aggregate CFAR shift (Cohen's d=0.61) with clean per-hold peaks in only 8.4% of holds. Thermal usable resolution was half nominal column count.

Conclusion: Sensor representation (CFAR point-cloud) is the limiting factor, not classifier choice. The authors conclude that raw range-FFT access, rather than further classifier tuning on CFAR detections, is the next necessary hardware experiment to improve prone detection performance.

Abstract: Contactless in-bed body-position inference can be limited by exposed sensor representation rather than classifier choice. We characterize a bedside 60 GHz frequency-modulated continuous-wave (FMCW) radar with on-device constant-false-alarm-rate (CFAR) point-cloud output plus a low-resolution (24 x 32 nominal, rows x columns) thermal array on two leave-one-subject-out (LOSO) evaluations derived from the same 20-subject cohort: 273 supervised in-bed posture holds (148.7 minutes) and an enter/exit bed-presence audit from the same cohort. The cohort is a 20-subject friends-and-family calibration sample (13 minors, ages 5-68; 8 residences), so these are characterization figures on a convenience cohort, not population-level performance. The motivating use case is prone-position monitoring, because prone position has been associated with sudden unexpected death in epilepsy (SUDEP) in retrospective studies. Fused radar + thermal logistic regression reaches a 0.871 median leave-one-subject-out balanced accuracy for in-bed vs out-of-bed classification. For four-class posture, the best tested pipeline (a full-feature stacked ensemble) reaches 0.674 aggregate balanced accuracy. Prone recall is 0.50 and prone precision is 0.41, so this is not deployable prone detection. Ablations show that thermal solves left-vs-right discrimination (radar-only lateral swaps 35-42%; thermal-only ~8%), but the expected supine-vs-prone breathing cue appears only as a class-level aggregate shift in CFAR output (Cohen's d=0.61), with clean per-hold peaks in 8.4% of holds. The thermal array's usable resolution in this cache was half its nominal column count, too coarse to separate face-from-back-of-head signatures. The results point to raw range-FFT access, rather than classifier tuning on CFAR detections, as the next hardware experiment.

</details>


### [925] [High Speed High Signal-to-Noise Ratio Antenna Measurements -- Demonstration for UAV-Based Near-Field Measurements of Modulated Terrestrial Navigation Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23569)
*Thomas F. Eibert,Denis Unruh,Thomas Mittereder,Alexander H. Paulus*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper presents a method to speed up antenna measurements by using short measurement times, Fourier transform, bandpass filtering, and inverse Fourier transform to achieve high SNR, demonstrated with simulated data and applied to UAV-based NF measurements of DVOR and ILS systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional high-SNR antenna measurements require long integration times, leading to excessively long total measurement durations, especially when many frequency and spatial samples are needed. The paper aims to accelerate this process.

Method: The approach collects measurement samples with short times, applies Fourier transform, bandpass filters the signals with small bandwidth, and then uses inverse Fourier transform to obtain high-SNR samples. It works with CW signals and modulated multi-frequency signals.

Result: The method was demonstrated on simulated test data and successfully applied to modulated NF data collected via UAV at DVOR and ILS localizer systems. Extracted CW NF data was transformed to FF, and diagnostic information was obtained from inverse source solutions.

Conclusion: The proposed approach enables faster antenna measurements without sacrificing SNR by employing signal processing techniques, and is validated in both simulated and real-world NF measurement scenarios.

Abstract: Antenna measurements with high signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) require long measurement or integration times of the receiver and can, thus, lead to a very long duration of the measurements, especially if many frequency and spatial samples need to be collected. In order to speed up such measurements, an approach is presented, which collects all measurement samples with short measurement times, performs a Fourier transform of the measurement samples, bandpass filters the desired measurement signals with a small bandwidth, and obtains high-SNR measurement samples according to the short measurement times by inverse Fourier transform. This approach can be utilized with single-frequency continuous wave (CW) transmit signals, but also with transmit signals containing several discrete frequency components, as, e.g., found for periodically modulated CW carriers. The approach is first worked out and demonstrated for simulated test data. Next, it is utilized for the processing of modulated near-field (NF) measurement data collected via an uninhabited aerial vehicle (UAV) at a Doppler high-frequency omnidirectional radio range (DVOR) and at the localizer of an instrument landing system (ILS). The extracted CW NF data is transformed into the far field (FF) and diagnostic information is obtained from the underlying inverse source solutions.

</details>


<div id='cs.IT'></div>

# cs.IT [[Back]](#toc)

### [926] [Holographic Beamforming for Semantic Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20887)
*Shuhao Zeng,Haobo Zhang,Su Wang,Boya Di,Hongliang Zhang,Zhu Han,H. Vincent Poor,Lingyang Song*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a semantic-importance-aware holographic beamforming scheme using metamaterial antennas to support semantic communication, addressing the mismatch between semantic importance and received SNR.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing holographic beamforming schemes are designed for conventional bit-communication where all bits are equally important, but semantic communication requires different transmission quality for information with varying semantic importance. Ignoring this causes performance degradation.

Method: The authors characterize the dependence of semantic communication performance on semantic importance and received SNR via data fitting, then design a semantic-aware holographic beamforming algorithm leveraging metamaterial antennas with tunable radiated amplitudes.

Result: Simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed semantic-importance-aware holographic beamforming method.

Conclusion: The proposed scheme effectively ensures reliable delivery of highly important semantic information by integrating semantic importance into holographic beamforming design.

Abstract: Holographic beamforming enabled by metamaterial antennas has been proposed to facilitate spatial multiplexing at low hardware cost and low power consumption. However, existing holographic beamforming schemes are mainly developed for conventional bit-communication systems, which have not considered semantic-level importance and thus cannot be directly applied to support semantic communication. Specifically, in conventional bit communication, all bits are treated as equally important. In contrast, in semantic communication, different semantic information contribute unequally to task completion and therefore has different degrees of importance, with more important information requiring higher transmission quality. Ignoring semantic importance in holographic beamforming causes mismatches between importance of semantic information and its received SNR, thus degrading performances. In this paper, we propose a semantic-importance-aware holographic beamforming scheme enabled by metamaterial antennas with tunable radiated amplitudes to support semantic communication. It is challenging to design semantic-aware holographic beamforming schemes due to non-trivial modeling of the impact of semantic importance and unique amplitude-controlled structures of holographic beamforming. To address this, we characterize the dependence of semantic communication performance on semantic importance and received SNR via data fitting, and design a semantic-aware holographic beamforming algorithm to ensure reliable delivery of highly important semantic information. Simulation results validate effectiveness of the proposed method.

</details>


### [927] [Learning-Based List Sequential Belief Propagation Decoding of Quantum LDPC Codes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20926)
*Mohsen Moradi,Taejoon Kim,Remi A. Chou*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper proposes a reinforcement learning-based list sequential (RL-LS) BP decoder for QLDPC codes that combines learned sequential scheduling with list-based search to improve decoding performance over standard BP decoders.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: QLDPC codes are promising for fault-tolerant quantum computation, but efficient decoding is challenging due to short cycles, degeneracy, and poor convergence of standard BP decoders.

Method: The RL-LS decoder extends the RL-S framework by adding list-based search. At each step, a learned policy selects the next variable node; the decoder retains the ordinary trajectory while also exploring competing branches by biasing LLRs toward the second-most likely Pauli symbol. Candidate trajectories are ranked and pruned via a cumulative path metric.

Result: Numerical results on representative QLDPC benchmark codes over the depolarizing channel show improved decoding performance compared to the underlying decoder and favorable comparison with existing BP-based decoding methods.

Conclusion: The RL-LS BP decoder effectively combines learned sequential scheduling with list exploration, offering a promising approach to improve QLDPC decoding.

Abstract: Quantum low-density parity-check (QLDPC) codes are strong candidates for fault-tolerant quantum computation, but efficient decoding remains a major challenge due to short cycles, degeneracy, and the poor convergence of standard belief-propagation (BP) decoders. We propose a reinforcement learning-based list sequential (RL-LS) BP decoder for QLDPC codes by extending the reinforcement-learning-based sequential variable-node scheduling (RL-S) framework with list-based search. At each step, the learned policy selects the next variable node to update; the decoder then retains the ordinary RL-S trajectory while also exploring a competing branch obtained by softly biasing the post-update LLR pair toward the second-most likely Pauli symbol, recomputing the incident local BP messages, and setting the visited variable node to that second-best symbol. Candidate trajectories are ranked and pruned using our proposed cumulative path metric. The resulting decoder extends the learned decoder by combining the improved convergence of learned sequential scheduling with list exploration. Numerical results on representative QLDPC benchmark codes over the depolarizing channel show that our proposed method improves the decoding performance of the underlying decoder and compares favorably with existing BP-based decoding methods.

</details>


### [928] [Reinforcement Learning-Based Secure Beamforming Against Satellite Eavesdroppers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21025)
*Juhwan Seo,Hyesang Cho,Dong-Hyun Jung*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a secure beamforming framework for LEO satellite uplink communications against multiple satellite eavesdroppers, using a primal-dual soft actor-critic algorithm with multi-head cost critic to jointly optimize beamforming under outage constraints, achieving near-optimal performance with low complexity for real-time operation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Secure beamforming in uplink LEO satellite communications is challenging due to time-varying orbital geometry and probabilistic fading-induced outage constraints, and existing methods like maximum ratio transmission and zero-forcing are suboptimal in such dynamic environments.

Method: Derives tractable closed-form expressions for connection and secrecy outage probabilities under Nakagami-m fading, reformulates the secrecy rate maximization as a constrained Markov decision process, and develops a primal-dual soft actor-critic algorithm with multi-head cost critic using Lagrangian relaxation.

Result: Improves ergodic secrecy rate over maximum ratio transmission across all eavesdropper configurations, outperforms zero-forcing in dense eavesdropping regimes, and achieves within 7% of an offline successive convex approximation benchmark with only a single forward pass.

Conclusion: The proposed approach is applicable to secure beamforming in dynamic LEO satellite environments, enabling low-complexity real-time operation.

Abstract: This paper investigates physical-layer security for uplink low Earth orbit (LEO) satellite communications in the presence of multiple non-colluding satellite eavesdroppers. Secure beamforming design in such systems is challenging due to time-varying orbital geometry and probabilistic fading-induced outage constraints. To address this, we first derive tractable closed-form expressions for both connection and secrecy outage probabilities under Nakagami-m fading, and develop differentiable upper-bound cost functions that are amenable to optimization. Next, to exploit the predictable orbital dynamics and temporal correlation of satellite mobility, we reformulate the non-convex secrecy rate maximization problem as a constrained Markov decision process. We then develop a primal-dual soft actor-critic algorithm with a multi-head cost critic that jointly optimizes beamforming while enforcing average outage constraints via Lagrangian relaxation. Numerical results show that the proposed framework improves the ergodic secrecy rate over maximum ratio transmission across all eavesdropper configurations, and outperforms zero-forcing in dense eavesdropping regimes. It achieves within 7 percent of an offline successive convex approximation benchmark while requiring only a single forward pass, enabling low-complexity real-time operation. These results indicate that the proposed approach is applicable to secure beamforming in dynamic LEO satellite environments.

</details>


### [929] [Error Exponent Bounds for Optimal Short-Read Clustering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21218)
*Yoav Chachamovitz,Nir Weinberger*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper studies unsupervised clustering of noisy short sequences for DNA storage decoders, deriving bounds on clustering error probability based on sequence length, read count, and channel statistics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop optimal clustering rules for DNA storage decoders that can handle noisy short sequences from unknown source sequences.

Method: Derives upper and lower bounds on the probability of incorrect clustering under the statistically optimal clustering rule, considering a memoryless channel model.

Result: Provides theoretical bounds on clustering error probability as functions of sequence length, number of reads, and channel statistics.

Conclusion: The derived bounds characterize the fundamental limits of unsupervised clustering for DNA storage applications.

Abstract: Motivated by the operation of decoders for DNA storage, we consider the problem of unsupervised clustering of noisy short sequences, each generated from one of multiple possible unknown source sequences after passing through a memoryless channel. Focusing on the statistically optimal clustering rule, we derive upper and lower bounds on the probability of incorrect clustering as a function of the sequence length, the number of reads, and the channel statistics.

</details>


### [930] [On the Weight Spectrum of the Reed-Muller Codes $RM(7,14)$](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21425)
*Milo Leuenberger,Manuel Albrizzio*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper attempts to find the weight spectrum of Reed-Muller codes RM(m-7,m) and nearly determines the weight spectrum of RM(7,14), missing only eight weights needed to answer an open question.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine the weight spectrum of Reed-Muller codes RM(m-7,m) and answer an open question regarding the weight spectrum of RM(m-c,m) for c=7.

Method: The authors attempt to fully determine the weight spectrum of RM(7,14) by computational or theoretical analysis, finding almost all weights except for eight missing ones.

Result: Almost all weights of RM(7,14) were found, but eight weights remain missing; these missing weights are needed to positively answer the open question.

Conclusion: The weight spectrum of RM(m-7,m) is nearly determined; the eight missing weights are crucial for resolving the open problem for c=7.

Abstract: In this paper, we attempt to find the weight spectrum of the Reed-Muller codes $RM(m-7,m)$. In the process, we tried to fully determine the weight spectrum of $RM(7,14)$. We found almost all of the weights, except for a select number of them. The remaining eight missing weights are necessary to give a positive answer to an open question on the weight spectrum of $RM(m-c,m)$ for $c=7$.

</details>


### [931] [Embedded Polygon Symbolic Transfer Entropy (EPSTE): A Geometric Token and Deep Learning Approach to Estimating Transfer Entropy in Neuroimaging Time Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21754)
*David Alexander Finnigan*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: EPSTE reframes Transfer Entropy estimation as a learnable problem using geometric symbolic tokens from neural time series, combined with RNN and attention-based MIL, achieving near-perfect recovery of directed dependencies in MEG data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transfer Entropy is theoretically principled for directed information flow but practically unstable in finite data regimes, especially at higher embedding dimensions. Existing methods struggle with noise and nonstationarity in EEG/MEG.

Method: Neural time series are decomposed into geometric primitives (magnitude, curvature, directional change) from local triplets, discretized into symbolic tokens. Symbolic TE is estimated from these tokens. An RNN with attention-based multiple-instance learning is trained to predict surrogate-validated TE from bags of symbolic windows.

Result: EPSTE achieves near-perfect recovery of ground-truth directed structure and significantly lower absolute error than the symbolic baseline. Local window-level predictions are noisy, but aggregation across trials and channel pairs yields stable directed dependencies.

Conclusion: Representational geometry (structured symbolic tokens) is critical for enabling practical learnability of information-theoretic dependencies from neural time series.

Abstract: Inferring directed interactions between neural systems from EEG and MEG remains challenging due to noise, nonstationarity, and the high sample complexity of information-theoretic estimators. Transfer Entropy (TE) provides a principled and model-free measure of directed information flow; however, its practical estimation is not stable in finite data regimes (particularly as embedding dimension increases). This work introduces Embedded Polygon Symbolic Transfer Entropy (EPSTE), a framework that reframes TE estimation as a learnable problem operating on structured symbolic representations of local temporal morphology rather than raw signal amplitudes. Neural time series are decomposed into sequences of geometric primitives derived from local triplets of samples encoding complementary aspects of waveform structure such as magnitude, curvature and directional change. These primitives are discretised into symbolic tokens, yielding a compact but expressive state space over which symbolic TE is estimated. A recurrent neural network with attention-based multiple-instance learning is trained to predict surrogate-validated TE values from bags of symbolic temporal windows. The method is evaluated on source-reconstructed MEG data parcellated using the AAL90 atlas and compared against a standard symbolic baseline using identical architectures and supervision. The results demonstrate that while local window-level predictions are noisy, aggregation across trials and channel pairs yields stable directed dependencies. At the pair level, EPSTE achieves near-perfect recovery of ground-truth directed structure and significantly lower absolute error than the baseline, indicating that representational geometry plays a critical role in enabling practical learnability of information-theoretic dependencies.

</details>


### [932] [One-Bit Clustering for Two Component Sub-Gaussian Mixture Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21873)
*Junren Chen,Yun Yang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes the first one-bit clustering method for two-component sub-Gaussian mixture models, using only one bit per entry via dithered quantization. A variant of Lloyd's algorithm achieves exponentially decaying misclassification rates and exact recovery with optimal separation conditions up to logarithmic factors, supported by minimax lower bounds and numerical results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clustering is fundamental in statistics and machine learning, but existing methods typically require full-precision data. The authors aim to develop a clustering method that works with extremely quantized data (one bit per entry), reducing storage and communication costs while maintaining statistical performance comparable to unquantized settings.

Method: The paper proposes a one-bit clustering method using dithered quantization (one bit per entry), combined with a variant of Lloyd's algorithm. A random rotation (Haar distributed matrix) is applied before quantization to enforce a non-spikiness condition when dimension is sufficiently large. Theoretical analysis includes misclassification rate bounds, exact recovery conditions, and minimax lower bounds.

Result: The method achieves misclassification rates that decay exponentially with a signal-to-noise ratio comparable to unquantized settings. Exact recovery is possible under an explicit separation condition exceeding the optimal unquantized threshold by only a logarithmic factor. For partial recovery, dimension p ≳ 1 suffices; for exact recovery, p ≳ log n log log n. Minimax lower bounds show sharp constants in general.

Conclusion: One-bit clustering is feasible and statistically efficient for two-component sub-Gaussian mixtures under mild conditions. The random rotation technique enables the method to work even in moderate dimensions. Numerical results validate the theoretical findings and demonstrate practical efficacy.

Abstract: Clustering is a fundamental problem in statistics and machine learning. We propose the first one-bit clustering method for two-component sub-Gaussian mixture models. The method uses only one bit per entry of each sample obtained via a dithered quantizer. Under a mild non-spikiness condition on the cluster centers, we show that a variant of Lloyd's algorithm achieves a misclassification rate that decays exponentially with a signal-to-noise ratio comparable to that in the unquantized setting. This result further implies exact recovery under an explicit separation condition, which exceeds the optimal threshold for unquantized data by only a logarithmic factor. When the dimension $p$ is sufficiently large, the non-spikiness condition can be enforced by applying a random rotation using a Haar distributed matrix prior to quantization. In particular, it holds with high probability when $p \gtrsim 1$ for partial recovery and $p \gtrsim \log n \log\log n$ for exact recovery, where $n$ is the sample size. We also establish a minimax lower bound, showing that the misclassification rate and separation condition exhibit sharp constants in general. Numerical results are provided to corroborate the theory and demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed method.

</details>


### [933] [Auditing Combinatorial Randomness from Finite Transcripts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22034)
*Faruk Alpay,Levent Sarioglu*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper studies how to audit public randomness (specifically, k-subset draws from m labels under uniform-without-replacement) using only observable transcripts. It shows that while full uniformity testing is information-theoretically expensive, structured audits (marginal chi-square, pair maxima, serial overlap, anchored-box discrepancy, and low-dimensional geometry) can detect specific deviations efficiently. GPU Monte Carlo experiments reveal that marginal-preserving faults evade 1D tests but are caught by joint geometric tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Public randomness is often deployed as a black box observable only through finite transcripts. The paper aims to understand what structured deviations can be detected from such limited information, given that full uniformity testing over the entire outcome space is sample-inefficient.

Method: The authors construct generator-agnostic audits on the hypersimplex using multiple test statistics: marginal chi-square, pair maxima, serial overlap, anchored-box discrepancy, and low-dimensional H0/MST geometry. They calibrate these under the exact combinatorial null and prove a finite-witness guarantee. GPU Monte Carlo experiments with up to 300k null and 60k alternative replications are used to evaluate power.

Result: No observed statistic remains significant after false-discovery correction (min BH q=0.279). Marginal-preserving deviations evade 1D tests but are detectable via joint geometry. For n=1,956, a block-cluster alternative (strength 0.04) yields power 0.638 for pair maxima vs 0.051 for marginal chi-square; band-repulsion (strength 0.08) yields power 0.741 for anchored boxes vs 0.051.

Conclusion: The results characterize which structured deviations finite public transcripts can detect and the sample sizes required, showing that joint geometric tests significantly outperform marginal tests for certain fault patterns.

Abstract: Public randomness is a security primitive whose deployed behavior is often observable only through a finite transcript. We study black-box auditing of $k$-subset draws from $m$ labels under the exact uniform-without-replacement null. The outcome space has size $\binom{m}{k}$, and unrestricted uniformity testing therefore requires $Θ(\sqrt{\binom{m}{k}}/\varepsilon^2)$ samples, establishing an information-theoretic limit on transcript-only certification. For structured faults, we construct generator-agnostic audits on the hypersimplex using marginal chi-square, pair maxima, serial overlap, anchored-box discrepancy, and low-dimensional $H_0$/MST geometry, all calibrated under the exact combinatorial null. We also prove a finite-witness guarantee whose sample complexity depends logarithmically on the number of audited witnesses rather than on the full support size. Across observed and reference-source audits, no statistic remains significant after false-discovery correction (minimum BH $q=0.279$). GPU Monte Carlo experiments, using up to 300,000 null and 60,000 alternative replications per condition, show that marginal-preserving deviations can evade one-dimensional tests while remaining detectable through joint geometry. At $n=1{,}956$, a block-cluster alternative of strength 0.04 yields power 0.638 for pair maxima versus 0.051 for marginal chi-square; a band-repulsion alternative of strength 0.08 yields power 0.741 for anchored boxes versus 0.051. These results characterize which structured deviations finite public transcripts can detect and the sample sizes required for doing so.

</details>


### [934] [Hulls and sums of separable constacyclic codes over $\mathbb{F}_q \times (\mathbb{F}_q+v\mathbb{F}_q)$ and new quantum codes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22069)
*Yu Qian,Yu Wang,Liqi Wang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper derives generator polynomials for duals, hulls, and sums of separable constacyclic codes over a specific algebraic structure, and uses them to construct new and better quantum error-correcting codes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop algebraic tools (generator polynomials for duals, hulls, sums) for separable constacyclic codes over a mixed ring, and leverage them to construct superior quantum error-correcting codes.

Method: Establishes generator polynomials for Euclidean/Hermitian duals, hulls, and sums of separable constacyclic codes over $\mathbb{F}_q \times (\mathbb{F}_q+v\mathbb{F}_q)$ with $v^2=v$; derives Gray image polynomials; proposes two QECC construction methods from hulls and sums.

Result: Successfully derived generator polynomials for duals, hulls, and sums; obtained new QECCs with parameters outperforming existing ones.

Conclusion: The algebraic framework enables systematic construction of better quantum codes from classical constacyclic codes over the mixed ring.

Abstract: We establish the generator polynomials of the Euclidean and Hermitian duals of separable constacyclic codes over $\mathcal{S} = \mathbb{F}_q \times (\mathbb{F}_q+v\mathbb{F}_q)$, with $q$ an odd prime power and $v^2=v$, and we derive the generator polynomials of their Gray images, respectively. The generator polynomials of the Euclidean hulls and Hermitian hulls of separable constacyclic codes over $\mathcal{S}$ and their Gray images are presented, respectively. Furthermore, we provide the generator polynomials of the Euclidean sums and Hermitian sums of separable constacyclic codes and their Gray images, respectively. Finally, we propose two methods to yield quantum error-correcting codes (QECCs) from the hulls and sums of separable constacyclic codes over $\mathcal{S}$, and generate new QECCs that outperform the existing ones in terms of parameters.

</details>


### [935] [Spatial Modulation for Tx-SIMO-FAS: Port Selection and Performance Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22280)
*Xusheng Zhu,Kai-Kit Won,Hanjiang Hong,Chenguang Rao,Kaitao Meng*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes three correlation-aware port-selection schemes (SF-EDAS, SOPS, CC-COAS) for spatial modulation with fluid antenna systems (Tx-SIMO-FAS) to mitigate strong spatial correlation in limited apertures, and provides a reliability analysis using energy-based and extreme-value degrees of freedom.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of strong spatial correlation in fluid antenna systems that degrades index detection reliability in spatial modulation, by proposing correlation-aware port-selection schemes.

Method: Three schemes: (1) SF-EDAS (enhancing received-constellation separation), (2) SOPS (improving channel-basis conditioning), (3) CC-COAS (jointly optimizing channel gain and inter-port decorrelation). Reliability analysis via energy-based DoF and extreme-value DoF decomposition.

Result: High SNR analysis reveals effective diversity order determined by selected ports, receive antennas, and energy-based spatial DoF. Aperture-limited array gain characterized via Digamma function approximation. Proposed schemes outperform conventional SM and grouping benchmarks; CC-COAS achieves best error-complexity tradeoff.

Conclusion: The proposed correlation-aware port-selection schemes effectively mitigate spatial correlation in FAS-SM systems, with CC-COAS offering the most favorable balance between performance and complexity.

Abstract: This paper considers a single-input multiple-output (SIMO) setup with a fluid antenna system (FAS) at the transmitter side and multiple fixed antennas at the receiver, which is referred to as a Tx-SIMO-FAS. We investigate the use of spatial modulation (SM) utilizing the FAS on a single radio-frequency (RF) chain while the receiver side performs maximum-likelihood detection. Unlike conventional antenna arrays, however, the large number of fluid antenna ports accommodated within a limited aperture introduces strong spatial correlation, which reduces the distinguishability of port indices and degrades the reliability of index detection. To address this challenge, three correlation-aware port-selection schemes are proposed: successive fluid Euclidean-distance-optimized selection (SF-EDAS), successive orthogonal port selection (SOPS), and correlation-constrained orthogonal array selection (CC-COAS). These schemes focus on enhancing received-constellation separation, improving channel-basis conditioning, and jointly optimizing channel gain and inter-port decorrelation, respectively. To understand the performance limits of FAS-SM, a reliability analysis is developed by decomposing the channel into an energy-based degree of freedom (DoF), and an extreme-value DoF. High signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) analysis reveals an effective diversity order determined by the number of selected ports, the number of receive antennas, and the energy-based spatial DoF. Furthermore, the aperture-limited array gain is characterized through a scalar equivalent independent-look approximation involving the Digamma function. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed schemes significantly outperform conventional SM and grouping-based benchmarks. Among them, CC-COAS achieves the most favorable tradeoff between error performance and computational complexity.

</details>


### [936] [Differentiable Conditional Mutual Information for Multi-Terminal Linear Gaussian Wireless Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22301)
*Tadashi Wadayama,Siqi Na*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper presents a unified framework for computing conditional mutual information and its gradients in Gaussian multi-terminal channels using a DAG model and automatic differentiation, enabling differentiable physical-layer design without problem-specific derivations.
