<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rate regions of multi-terminal Gaussian channels are defined by conditional mutual informations, but evaluating these quantities and their gradients for optimization requires problem-specific derivations, which is tedious and limits differentiable design.

Method: Models the network as a linear Gaussian DAG, computes conditional MI as a log-determinant difference of Schur complements via one K-recursion forward pass, and leverages automatic differentiation (AD) primitives so any differentiable function of conditional MIs is end-to-end differentiable.

Result: Demonstrated on three experiments: MIMO multiple-access channel rate-region maximization, MIMO wiretap channel secure precoding, and multi-hop multiple-access network optimization.

Conclusion: The framework enables gradient-based optimization of complex rate-region objectives without problem-specific gradient derivation, applicable to a broad class of multi-terminal communication problems.

Abstract: The rate regions of multi-terminal Gaussian channels (multiple-access, broadcast, interference, relay) are delimited by conditional mutual informations $I(V_A;V_B\,|\,V_C)$ among groups of input and output nodes; bringing such channels under differentiable physical-layer design therefore hinges on evaluating any such conditional MI, and its gradient, on a unified computation graph. Modeling the network as a linear Gaussian directed acyclic graph (Gaussian-DAG), we obtain $I(V_A;V_B\,|\,V_C)$ in closed form: from the node-pair covariances produced by one K-recursion forward pass, it is a log-determinant difference of two sub-block Schur complements of the support covariance. The construction is built entirely from automatic-differentiation (AD) primitives, so any differentiable function of finitely many conditional MIs is end-to-end differentiable in the design parameters; this broad class includes linear objectives (weighted sum-rate, secrecy), the rate functions of standard multi-terminal rate regions, and non-linear composites of these. A single reverse-mode AD sweep yields the Wirtinger gradient with respect to all controllable factors at once, so any such objective can be handled by projected gradient iterations without problem-specific gradient derivation. We demonstrate the framework on three experiments: rate-region maximization for a two-user MIMO multiple-access channel, secure precoding on a MIMO wiretap channel, and the same rate-region objective applied to a larger multi-hop multiple-access network.

</details>


### [937] [Modulo Quantization Coding for Primitive Relay and Diamond Channels with Correlated Noises](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22313)
*Yuanxin Guo,Stark C. Draper,Wei Yu*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes modulo quantization (MQ) coding for Gaussian relay/diamond channels with finite-capacity digital relay links and correlated noises, achieving capacity with perfect correlation and outperforming compress-forward and decode-forward under partial correlation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a low-complexity, structured coding scheme for Gaussian channels with primitive relay links and correlated noises, exploiting common noise components to improve performance over existing schemes like compress-forward and decode-forward.

Method: Modulo quantization (MQ) coding: maps real-valued symbols to uniform-quantization indices taken modulo a fixed integer, enabling exploitation of common noise across terminals.

Result: - Achieves capacity for Gaussian primitive relay channel with perfectly correlated noises<br>- Improves upon prior achievability bounds for Gaussian primitive diamond channel with perfect correlation, matching cut-set bound in certain SNR regimes<br>- Approaches CF performance with lower complexity and surpasses DF for relay channel with highly correlated noises<br>- Outperforms both CF and DF for diamond channel with non-perfectly correlated noises at moderate SNR

Conclusion: MQ coding is a simple, low-complexity scheme effective for Gaussian networks with correlated noises and primitive relay links, providing significant performance gains over conventional relaying strategies.

Abstract: This paper proposes modulo quantization (MQ) coding as a simple, structured, and low-complexity scheme for channels with primitive (i.e., noiseless digital) relay links and correlated Gaussian noises across terminals. The key component of MQ coding is the modulo quantization operation, which maps a real-valued symbol to its uniform-quantization index taken modulo a fixed integer. This operation allows effective exploitation of the common noise component shared across the terminals. For the Gaussian primitive relay channel with perfectly correlated noises, where a relay has a finite-capacity link to the receiver, MQ coding can be shown to achieve the capacity of this channel. For the Gaussian primitive diamond channel with perfectly correlated noises, where two relays can forward information through finite-capacity links to a receiver that has no direct observation of the transmitted signal, MQ coding yields novel achievability bounds that improve upon previously known bounds and coincide with the cut-set upper bound in certain signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) regimes. In scenarios with highly but non-perfectly correlated noises, MQ coding can approach the performance of compress-forward (CF) at significantly lower complexity, while surpassing decode-forward (DF) for the Gaussian primitive relay channel in certain SNR ranges. For the Gaussian primitive diamond channel with non-perfectly correlated noises, MQ can outperform both CF and DF at moderate SNR.

</details>


### [938] [Random Reed--Solomon Codes Correcting Permutations, Insertions, and Deletions over Polynomial-Size Alphabets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22344)
*Yijun Zhang,Yubo Sun,Xiande Zhang,Gennian Ge*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper studies Reed-Solomon codes under adversarial permutations and insertion-deletion errors. It proves that random Reed-Solomon codes over polynomial-size alphabets can achieve near-optimal insdel error correction, establishes a lower bound requiring polynomially superlinear alphabet sizes for positive-rate codes, and provides an efficient decoder for explicit 2D Reed-Solomon codes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the feasibility of Reed-Solomon codes in the challenging setting of adversarial coordinate permutations combined with insertion-deletion errors, and to reduce the required alphabet size from exponential to polynomial while maintaining near-optimal error correction capability.

Method: The paper uses probabilistic methods to analyze random Reed-Solomon codes over polynomial-size alphabets, derives alphabet-size lower bounds for positive-rate permutation-insdel codes, and designs an average O(n)-time decoder for explicit two-dimensional Reed-Solomon codes.

Result: Shows that for fixed R,ε with 2R+ε<1, a random Reed-Solomon code over alphabet n^{O(1)} is robust against permutations followed by (1-ε)n-2k+1 insdel errors with high probability; proves polynomially superlinear alphabet lower bound; gives O(n)-time decoder for explicit 2D codes against n-3 insdel errors.

Conclusion: Polynomial-size alphabets suffice for near-optimal Reed-Solomon codes in the permutation-insdel setting, and efficient decoding is achievable for explicit constructions.

Abstract: We study Reed--Solomon codes against adversarial coordinate permutations followed by insertion-deletion (insdel) errors. It was previously shown by Con (2025) that Reed--Solomon codes can attain the exact half-Singleton bound in this setting, but only over exponentially large alphabets. We prove that, by allowing an additive $εn$ gap from this bound, the alphabet size can be reduced to polynomial. More precisely, for fixed constants $R,ε\in(0,1)$ satisfying $2R+ε<1$ and $k=Rn$, a random Reed--Solomon code of length $n$ and dimension $k$ over an alphabet of size $n^{O_{R,ε}(1)}$ is, with high probability, robust against arbitrary coordinate permutations followed by up to $(1-ε)n-2k+1$ insdel errors.
  We also prove a complementary alphabet-size lower bound, showing that positive-rate codes, which are robust against linearly many insdel errors in the permutation-insdel setting, require a polynomially superlinear alphabet.
  Finally, for the explicit two-dimensional Reed--Solomon codes constructed by Con et al. (2024) over alphabet size $O(n^3)$, we give an average $O(n)$-time decoder against arbitrary coordinate permutations followed by $n-3$ insdel errors. Previously, an $O(n)$-time decoder for this code was known only for the deletion setting.

</details>


### [939] [Study of Code-Aided Channel Estimation for Metasurface-Based Holographic MIMO Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22465)
*Roberto C. G. Porto,Rodrigo C. de Lamare*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes an iterative code-aided detection, decoding, and channel estimation scheme for holographic MIMO systems using stacked intelligent metasurfaces (SIM-RIS and BD-SIM-RIS). By exploiting LDPC coding, both pilot and parity bits refine channel estimation, and closed-form metasurface parameter designs are integrated into an alternating-optimization procedure. Results show significant gains in NMSE and BER, especially for BD-SIM-RIS architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve channel estimation and detection performance in metasurface-based holographic MIMO systems by leveraging coding information (LDPC) beyond conventional pilot-only approaches, particularly for stacked and fully connected intelligent metasurface architectures.

Method: Iterative code-aided detection, decoding, and channel estimation scheme; LDPC coding exploitation where both pilot and parity bits contribute to iterative channel refinement; closed-form metasurface parameter design integrated into an alternating-optimization (AO) procedure.

Result: Substantial gains in normalized mean square error (NMSE) and bit error rate (BER), with particularly strong improvements for BD-SIM-RIS architectures.

Conclusion: The proposed iterative scheme effectively improves channel estimation and error performance in holographic MIMO systems, with BD-SIM-RIS benefiting most due to its fully connected architecture.

Abstract: This work proposes an iterative code-aided detection, decoding, and channel estimation scheme for metasurface-based holographic MIMO systems employing stacked intelligent metasurfaces (SIM-RIS) and their fully connected counterparts (BD-SIM-RIS). A novel channel estimation strategy is developed by exploiting low-density parity-check (LDPC) coding in the uplink, enabling both pilot and parity bits of the encoded packet to contribute to the iterative refinement of the channel. In addition, closed-form expressions for the metasurface parameter design are derived and incorporated into an alternating-optimization (AO) procedure. Numerical results demonstrate substantial gains in normalized mean square error (NMSE) and bit error rate (BER), with particularly strong improvements observed for BD-SIM-RIS architectures.

</details>


### [940] [Quantum Codes with Transversal $CCZ$ Gates and Sublinear $Z$-Stabilizers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22472)
*Ohad Elishco,Itzhak Tamo*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper constructs quantum CSS codes with transversal CCZ gates, achieving sublinear-weight Z-stabilizer generating sets and near-linear dimension, using a refined puncturing theorem applied to algebraic expander codes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To construct quantum CSS codes with transversal CCZ gates that have both high dimension and sublinear stabilizer weight, overcoming the dual-distance limitation of algebraic expander codes in the existing puncturing framework.

Method: The authors develop a refined puncturing theorem (replacing global dual-distance with a condition on the puncturing set), apply it to algebraic expander codes, and further reduce to fixed prime fields via projective-multiplicity versions of multiplication-friendly codes.

Result: Explicit CSS codes with parameters [[N, Θ(N), Ω(N^{1/m})]] for any m≥3, transversal CCZ gates, and Z-stabilizer weight O(N^{1/m}); near-linear dimension and sublinear locality also achieved over fixed prime fields with polylogarithmic losses.

Conclusion: The refined puncturing theorem successfully yields high-dimension CSS codes with transversal CCZ and low-weight stabilizers, advancing the algebraic approach to fault-tolerant quantum computation.

Abstract: We construct quantum CSS codes with transversal \(CCZ\) gates whose \(Z\)-stabilizers admit sublinear-weight generating sets. We build on the algebraic puncturing framework of Guruswami and Golowich \cite{GG24}, which turns classical codes with the required Schur-product and distance conditions into CSS codes with transversal \(CCZ\). However, applying the framework directly to the algebraic expander codes of \cite{KT26} runs into their small dual distance, and therefore produces only sublinear quantum dimension. Our main technical step is a refined puncturing theorem in which the global dual-distance assumption is replaced by a condition only on the selected puncturing set. Applying this theorem to algebraic expander codes gives explicit growing-alphabet CSS codes with parameters \([[N,Θ(N),Ω(N^{1/m})]]\), for every fixed \(m\geq 3\), and with transversal \(CCZ\) gates. Moreover, the \(Z\)-stabilizer space has an explicit generating set of weight \(O(N^{1/m})\). We also reduce the alphabet to a fixed prime field using a projective-multiplicity version of multiplication-friendly codes. The resulting fixed-prime-field CSS code triples, of length \(n\), still have transversal \(CCZ\) gates. Their dimension is near-linear, their distance is \(n^{1/m}\)
  up to polylogarithmic factors, and the \(Z\)-stabilizer locality remains sublinear, again up to polylogarithmic losses.

</details>


### [941] [Risk-Aware Information Theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22524)
*Hamidou Tembine*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper develops a risk-aware information theory using expectiles instead of expectations, introducing expectile entropy, divergence, and mutual information. It shows these measures can produce negative divergence in risk-seeking settings, differ fundamentally from classical Shannon mutual information, and naturally lead to a mean-field-type game theory for multiuser systems. The work argues that traditional Shannon information cannot capture extreme risks relevant to advanced machine intelligence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical Shannon information theory is risk-neutral and cannot quantify extreme risks that are critical for advanced machine intelligence, safe autonomous systems, and collective intelligence. The authors aim to bridge this gap by developing a risk-aware information framework.

Method: The method replaces traditional expectation operators with expectiles (a risk-sensitive statistic) to define new information-theoretic quantities: expectile entropy, divergence, and mutual information. This naturally induces a mean-field-type game-theoretic framework for multiuser systems where rate regions depend on heterogeneous risk preferences.

Result: The new framework exhibits unconventional behaviors under Shannon's theory, such as negative divergence under risk-seeking regimes. Achievable rate regions in multiuser systems become endogenous to heterogeneous risk-sensitivity indices, showing a fundamental departure from classical mutual information.

Conclusion: Shannon information alone is insufficient for quantifying extreme risks in advanced machine intelligence. This risk-aware framework establishes a foundation for risk-aware communication, learning, collective intelligence, and safe autonomous systems.

Abstract: We develop a risk-aware information theory by replacing expectation with expectiles, introducing expectile entropy, divergence, and mutual information. These quantities exhibit behaviors impossible under Shannon's risk-neutral framework, including negative divergence under risk-seeking regimes and a fundamental separation from classical mutual information. In multiuser systems, the framework naturally induces a mean-field-type game theory of information exchange, where achievable rate regions become endogenous to heterogeneous risk-sensitivity indices. Our results reveal that Shannon information alone cannot quantify the extreme risks driving advanced machine intelligence, establishing a foundation for risk-aware communication, learning, collective intelligence, and safe autonomous systems.

</details>


### [942] [Robust SCMA Codebook Design: A Hardware-Aware Autoencoder Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22603)
*Zihao Liu,Zilong Liu,Leila Musavian*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a hardware-aware autoencoder for SCMA codebook design that incorporates CFO and phase noise layers during training to suppress error floors without needing real-time phase tracking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Carrier frequency offset (CFO) and phase noise (PN) disrupt subcarrier orthogonality in OFDM-SCMA systems, causing bit error floors.

Method: A hardware-aware end-to-end autoencoder is proposed that embeds differentiable CFO and Wiener PN layers into the training loop to design robust SCMA codebooks.

Result: The proposed codebook effectively suppresses bit error floors caused by CFO and PN without requiring real-time phase tracking.

Conclusion: The hardware-aware autoencoder approach offers a new angle for SCMA codebook design to mitigate practical RF impairments.

Abstract: Sparse code multiple access (SCMA) is a promising code-domain non-orthogonal multiple access scheme which is transmitted over orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) to exploit multicarrier diversity. In practice, however, carrier frequency offset (CFO) and phase noise (PN) may disrupt the subcarrier orthogonality in OFDM-SCMA systems. Addressing this research problem from a new SCMA codebook design angle, we propose a hardware-aware end-to-end autoencoder that embeds differentiable CFO and Wiener PN layers into the training loop. Simulations show that the proposed codebook effectively suppresses the bit error floors caused by CFO and PN without requiring real-time phase tracking.

</details>


### [943] [Private Information Retrieval from Joint Systematic MDS-Coded with Non-Colluding Servers: Bounds and Constructions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22802)
*Jingke Xu,Lirong Shi,Peng Lan,Weijun Fang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper studies joint MDS-coded private information retrieval (PIR) in distributed storage systems. It derives capacity upper bounds for cases K=Mt and K=Mt+1, constructs three new PIR schemes with small file sizes that achieve higher retrieval rates than separate MDS-coded PIR, and establishes optimal capacity as 1-(1-1/M)K/N for the K=Mt, N≤K+t case.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The capacity of joint MDS-coded PIR introduced by Sun and Tian in 2019 remains largely unexplored, creating a gap in understanding optimal retrieval rates for private information retrieval in distributed storage systems using MDS codes.

Method: The authors derive theoretical upper bounds on capacity for K=Mt and K=Mt+1, then construct three joint MDS-coded PIR schemes for different parameter regimes (N≤K+t with K=Mt, N>K+t with K=Mt, and N≤K+t with K=Mt+1) that require small file sizes and achieve higher retrieval rates.

Result: The proposed schemes exceed the capacity of separate MDS-coded PIR schemes. For K=Mt and N≤K+t, optimal capacity is proven to be 1-(1-1/M)K/N. The maximum relative retrieval-rate improvement exceeds 15% for M≥4, 20% for M≥9, and asymptotically approaches 1-2/e≈26.42%.

Conclusion: Joint MDS-coded PIR schemes can break the capacity limits of separate MDS-coded PIR, with significant retrieval-rate improvements that grow with M, approaching approximately 26.42% asymptotically.

Abstract: Consider a distributed storage system consisting of $N$ non-colluding servers that collectively store a database of $M$ files encoded using an $[N,K]$ maximum distance separable(MDS) code. A user wishes to retrieve one file privately by accessing the servers without revealing the identity of the requested file. A scheme designed for this purpose is called a joint MDS-coded private information retrieval(PIR) scheme, which was first introduced by Sun and Tian in 2019 to break the capacity $\frac{1-K/N}{1-(K/N)^M}$ of the separate MDS-coded PIR schemes established by Banawan and Ulukus. However, the capacity of joint MDS-coded PIR remains largely unexplored.
  In this paper, we study the capacity of joint MDS-coded PIR with systematic MDS array storage codes under prescribed storage patterns. Specifically, we first derive upper bounds on the capacity of joint MDS-coded PIR for $K=Mt$ and $K=Mt+1$, respectively. We then construct three joint MDS-coded PIR schemes for the cases $N\le K+t, K=Mt$, $N>K+t, K=Mt$ and $N\le K+t, K=Mt+1$. The proposed schemes require small file sizes and achieve higher retrieval rates: the first and third schemes exceed the capacity of separate MDS-coded PIR schemes, while the second scheme does so when the storage rate $\frac{K}{N}>r_M$ for some $0<r_M<\frac{M}{M+1}$. In particular, for $K=Mt$ and $N\leq K+t$, the proposed scheme achieves the derived upper bound, thereby establishing that the optimal joint MDS-coded PIR capacity under the considered storage pattern is $1-(1-\frac{1}{M})\frac{K}{N}$. Compared with capacity-achieving separate MDS-coded PIR schemes at the same storage-code rate, the proposed schemes may achieve a substantial relative retrieval-rate improvement: the maximum improvement can exceed $15\%$ when $M\geq 4$, exceed $20\%$ when $M\geq 9$, and asymptotically approach $1-2/e\approx 26.42\%$ as M increases.

</details>


### [944] [Stable Image Reconstruction via Two-Parameter Power-Scale Variation Minimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23083)
*Ziwei Li,Wengu Chen,Huanmin Ge,Limei Huo,Dachun Yang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper introduces a power-scale variation (PSV) with two tunable parameters (a and p) for stable image reconstruction, develops an IRLS-based algorithm, and proves optimal RIP bounds and sensitivity properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a flexible and widely applicable image reconstruction model with tunable sparsity and scaling parameters, overcoming limitations of existing total variation methods.

Method: Proposes PSV_{a,p} minimization with two parameters; establishes RIP-based reconstruction guarantees; designs IRLSPSV algorithm; provides theoretical analysis on asymptotic optimality and parameter sensitivity.

Result: Numerical experiments show superior performance and broad applicability; the RIP upper bound is asymptotically optimal in a for gradient recovery; sensitivity analysis confirms distinct roles of a and p.

Conclusion: The PSV_{a,p} model offers great flexibility via two tunable parameters, achieves new RIP conditions for TV_p in the limit, and enables practical parameter tuning.

Abstract: In this article, we introduce a power-scale variation (PSV$_{a,p}$) with two tunable parameters: the sparsity-inducing exponent $p\in(0,1]$ and the scaling factor $a\in(0,\infty)$. By minimizing the PSV$_{a,p}$, we establish stable reconstructions in both the gradient and the image domains under the restricted isometry property (RIP) framework. Furthermore, we design an iteratively re-weighted least squares algorithm IRLSPSV to solve the unconstrained PSV$_{a,p}$ minimization. Numerical experiments demonstrate its superior performance and broad applicability. The main novelties are: (i) the PSV$_{a,p}$ minimization enjoys great flexibility and wide applicability due to its two tunable parameters $a$ and $p$, (ii) as $a\to\infty$, the PSV$_{a,p}$ minimization reduces to the $p$-th power total variation (TV$_p$) minimization and, even in this limiting case, the established RIP condition for image reconstruction is also new, (iii) the derived RIP upper bound $\overlineδ$ is proved to be asymptotically optimal in $a$ for gradient recovery, (iv) sensitivity analysis confirms the distinct roles of $a$ and $p$, thereby motivating a practical parameter tuning scheme for the proposed model.

</details>


### [945] [Enormous Fluid Antenna Systems (E-FAS) for Wireless Sensing: Channel Modeling and Conditional Estimation Limits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23119)
*Farshad Rostami Ghadi,Kai-Kit Wong,Jose D. Vega-Sanchez,Kin-Fai Tong,Hyundong Shin*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper develops a theoretical framework for Integrated Sensing and Communications (ISAC) using Enormous Fluid Antenna Systems (E-FAS), showing that E-FAS creates a new sensing regime with a fundamental trade-off between surface-wave routing gain and sensing diversity, and achieves better angular sensing than conventional architectures under the same power budget.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish a fundamental analytical framework for ISAC enabled by E-FAS, particularly focusing on angular sensing limits, and to understand how E-FAS differs from conventional array-based and reconfigurable-surface-aided ISAC architectures.

Method: The authors develop a bidirectional sensing channel model capturing the complete sensing process (SW routing, distributed reradiation, target scattering, echo propagation). They formulate a parametric observation model and derive the Fisher information matrix (FIM) and Cramér-Rao bound (CRB) for angular estimation.

Result: E-FAS introduces a fundamentally different sensing regime. Maximizing coherent routing gain does not maximize sensing performance, revealing a trade-off between SW routing gain and sensing diversity. E-FAS achieves substantial angular sensing gains over conventional architectures under the same transmit-power budget.

Conclusion: Joint optimization of propagation routing and sensing functionality is crucial, and E-FAS represents a new paradigm for ISAC systems.

Abstract: In this paper, we develop a fundamental analytical framework for integrated sensing and communications (ISAC) enabled by the Enormous Fluid Antenna System (E-FAS), which transforms a collection of coordinated intelligent surfaces into a gigantic reconfigurable electromagnetic aperture, with particular emphasis on the limits of angular sensing.We begin by developing a bidirectional sensing channel model that explicitly captures the complete sensing process, including surface-wave (SW) routing, distributed reradiation, target scattering, and echo propagation. Based on this channel model, we formulate a parametric observation model for target sensing and derive the associated Fisher information matrix (FIM) and Cramer-Rao bound (CRB) for angular estimation. The analysis demonstrates that E-FAS gives rise to a fundamentally different sensing regime compared with conventional array-based and reconfigurable-surface-aided ISAC architectures. Our analysis uncovers that maximizing coherent routing gain does not necessarily maximize sensing performance, exposing a fundamental trade-off between SW routing gain and sensing diversity in programmable propagation environments. Numerical results validate the developed framework and demonstrate that E-FAS-enabled ISAC systems can achieve substantial angular sensing gains over conventional architectures under the same transmit-power budget. The results further underscore the importance of jointly optimizing propagation routing and sensing functionality, positioning E-FAS as a new paradigm for ISAC.

</details>


### [946] [Movable Antennas for Robust Wireless Sensing via Joint Cramér-Rao Bound and Sidelobe Minimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23154)
*Wenyan Ma,Lipeng Zhu,Weitong Zhai,Rui Zhang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a movable antenna (MA) sensing system that jointly optimizes antenna positions to minimize both the Cramér-Rao bound (CRB) and maximum sidelobe level (MSL), balancing the trade-off between narrow mainlobe (concentrated edges) and wide mainlobe (dense center) configurations via SCA optimization, achieving lower AoA estimation MSE across all SNR regimes compared to fixed-position arrays.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing fixed-position antenna arrays suffer from inherent trade-offs between CRB (local estimation error within mainlobe) and sidelobe ambiguity errors in AoA estimation, with no flexibility to adapt antenna positions across different SNR regimes for robust sensing performance.

Method: 1) Decompose AoA estimation MSE into CRB (mainlobe) and sidelobe ambiguity error; 2) Formulate optimization minimizing CRB with MSL constraint; 3) Develop successive convex approximation (SCA) algorithm for antenna position vector optimization; 4) Design 1-D linear search for optimal MSL threshold per SNR.

Result: The proposed MA-enabled scheme achieves significantly lower AoA estimation MSE across the entire SNR range compared to conventional uniform and non-uniform fixed-position antenna arrays, effectively balancing the CRB-MSL trade-off.

Conclusion: Movable antennas can adaptively balance mainlobe width (for CRB) and sidelobe suppression by repositioning antennas—concentrating at edges for high SNR or center for low SNR—enabling robust sensing performance through joint CRB-MSL optimization.

Abstract: This paper presents a novel design approach for movable antenna (MA)-enabled wireless sensing systems by jointly minimizing the Cramér-Rao bound (CRB) and the maximum sidelobe level (MSL) of the ambiguity function via antenna position optimization. In particular, the mean squared error (MSE) of angle-of-arrival (AoA) estimation is decomposed into a local estimation error within the mainlobe of the ambiguity function (i.e., CRB) and an additional ambiguity error caused by its sidelobes. Since the MSE is dominated by the CRB in the high-signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) regime but by the sidelobes of the ambiguity function in the low-SNR regime, our analysis reveals a fundamental trade-off between CRB minimization and MSL minimization in the moderate-SNR regime. Specifically, minimizing the CRB prefers a narrower mainlobe, where antennas are concentrated near the two edges of the one-dimensional (1-D) movement region; whereas minimizing the MSL favors a wider mainlobe, where antennas are distributed more densely near the center of the movement region. Inspired by this and to ensure robust sensing performance across different SNR regimes, we formulate an optimization problem to minimize the CRB subject to a prescribed MSL constraint via antenna position optimization. An efficient successive convex approximation (SCA) algorithm is developed to optimize the antenna position vector (APV), and a 1-D linear search method is proposed to determine the optimal MSL threshold that minimizes the actual MSE for any given SNR. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed scheme effectively balances the trade-off between MSL and CRB minimization, thus achieving a significantly lower AoA estimation MSE across the entire SNR range compared to conventional uniform and non-uniform fixed-position antenna (FPA) arrays.

</details>


### [947] [Tighter Bounds for Algorithmic Complexity Estimation Using a Reusable Code-Based Block Decomposition Method](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23471)
*Eduardo Yuji Sakabe,Felipe S. Abrahão,Santiago Hernández-Orozco,Ricardo Gudwin,Hector Zenil*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper extends the Block Decomposition Method (BDM) for estimating algorithmic complexity by introducing algorithmic attention, which exploits dependencies between blocks to reduce description length through reuse of program code, formalized as a reuse optimization problem that is NP-hard but improves upon independent descriptions based on shared algorithmic information.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve BDM's estimation of algorithmic complexity by accounting for dependencies between blocks (shared structure) rather than treating them independently, thereby reducing description length through reusable program code.

Method: Introduces algorithmic attention as a mechanism for allocating descriptive resources; formalizes block reuse as an optimization problem; proves NP-hardness of exact optimization; derives conditions for improvement over independent descriptions; relates gains to algorithmic mutual information; proves relationship to previous BDM version.

Result: Exact optimization is NP-hard; conditions derived where reuse improves upon independent descriptions; gains are related to algorithmic mutual information; relationship with previous BDM version is proven.

Conclusion: The extended BDM with algorithmic attention provides a principled way to reduce description length by leveraging shared algorithmic information, with a roadmap for implementation using CTM-derived complexity and conditional complexity estimates.

Abstract: The Block Decomposition Method (BDM) was introduced as an alternative to popular lossless compression methods such as LZW for estimating algorithmic complexity from the principles of algorithmic probability and classical information theory. It extends the Coding Theorem Method (CTM) from small objects to larger ones by combining local estimates of algorithmic complexity with a global account of repetition based on Shannon entropy. Here, we introduce a version of BDM in which dependencies between blocks are utilized to reduce the length of the description based on reusable program code in the decomposition of an object, and on conditional descriptions capable of accounting for shared structure between observations. We formalize this allocation of descriptive resources as algorithmic attention. Repeated or related components need not be described independently, and the resulting reduction in description length is governed by the amount of shared algorithmic information. We formulate this extension as a reuse optimization problem, show that exact optimization is NP-hard, derive conditions under which it improves upon independent descriptions, relate the achievable gains to algorithmic mutual information, prove the relationship with the previous BDM version, and provide a roadmap for its implementation using CTM-derived complexity and conditional complexity estimates.

</details>


<div id='eess.IV'></div>

# eess.IV [[Back]](#toc)

### [948] [From Sparse X-rays to 3D CT: Training-Free Reconstruction with Diffusion Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20763)
*Zhenkai Zhang,Markus Hiller,Krista A. Ehinger,Tom Drummond*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: TF-PRDiT is a training-free framework that uses a frozen 3D Diffusion Transformer prior to solve various medical inverse problems (X-ray-to-CT, super-resolution, infilling, deblurring) by enforcing measurement consistency via task-specific forward operators instead of retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current 3D medical inverse problem solvers require dedicated supervised models for each task and measurement setting. The authors aim to break this dependency by enabling a single pretrained model to be reused across diverse conditional settings without retraining.

Method: TF-PRDiT combines a predictor-corrector sampler with likelihood-based guidance on denoised predictions, enforcing measurement consistency via task-specific forward operators (e.g., differentiable DRR projector for X-ray-to-CT) rather than updating model weights.

Result: On LIDC-IDRI, TF-PRDiT achieves strong reconstruction quality for X-ray-to-CT, scales to 1-12 input X-rays under a unified model with performance improving with more views, and extends to 3D super-resolution, volumetric infilling, and deblurring by simply swapping the forward operator.

Conclusion: A single 3D diffusion prior can serve as a universal solver for volumetric medical inverse problems, eliminating the need for task-specific retraining and enabling flexible multi-view reconstruction.

Abstract: Solving 3D medical inverse problems typically requires training dedicated supervised models for each specific task and measurement setting. To break this dependency, we present TF-PRDiT: a training-free conditional sampling framework that converts a frozen voxel-level 3D Diffusion Transformer prior into a versatile inverse medical problem solver. Building on the posterior-sampling view of diffusion inverse solvers, TF-PRDiT enforces measurement consistency during sampling via a task-specific forward operator rather than updating model weights, enabling a single pretrained prior to be reused across diverse conditional settings. Our method combines a predictor-corrector sampler with likelihood-based guidance on the denoised prediction, providing stable data-fidelity correction while preserving the underlying 3D anatomical prior. We highlight our framework's capability on the challenging task of X-ray-to-CT reconstruction by integrating a differentiable DRR projector to allow gradients to propagate directly from projection space back to voxels without any retraining. Experiments on LIDC-IDRI demonstrate that TF-PRDiT achieves strong reconstruction quality and uniquely scales to an arbitrary number of input X-rays (1-12) under a unified model, with performance improving consistently as additional views are provided. Beyond X-ray-to-CT, we show that simply swapping the forward operator extends the same frozen model to 3D super-resolution, volumetric infilling, and deblurring without any task-specific retraining, demonstrating that a single 3D diffusion prior can serve as a universal solver for volumetric medical inverse problems.

</details>


### [949] [Dataset-Aware Cold-Start Active Learning for Annotation-Efficient 3D Medical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20765)
*Rémi Hattat,Marine Beaumont,Charline Bertholdt,Gabriela Hossu,Olivier Morel,Bailiang Chen*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: CSCS is a cold-start active learning framework for 3D medical image segmentation that selects the first volumes for annotation using self-supervised signals (local typicality and reconstruction-based uncertainty) without needing any initial labeled data, showing strong performance across four benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Active learning reduces annotation burden for 3D medical image segmentation, but most methods assume an initial labeled set exists, leaving the cold-start problem unaddressed.

Method: Proposes CSCS combining two self-supervised signals: (1) local typicality for representativeness, (2) reconstruction-based uncertainty for difficulty. These are combined via a weighted geometric score with a closed-form pacing rule using annotation budget and Difficulty-Coverage Ratio.

Result: Evaluated on BraTS, FeTA, Spleen, and fetal MRI datasets with nnU-Net; outperforms baselines consistently, especially in low-to-mid annotation budgets.

Conclusion: Dataset-aware cold-start initialization improves active learning robustness for 3D medical image segmentation by adapting sample selection to the unlabeled pool structure.

Abstract: Deep learning for 3D medical image segmentation requires extensive manual annotations, a major bottleneck in volumetric medical imaging. Active learning aims to reduce this burden by selecting informative samples for annotation, but most methods assume that an initial labeled set is already available. This leaves the cold-start problem largely unresolved: how to select the first volumes from a fully unlabeled pool before any task-specific model is trained. We propose CSCS, a Curriculum-Stratified Cold-Start framework that adapts initial sample selection to the structure of the unlabeled dataset. CSCS combines two self-supervised, label-free signals: local typicality, measuring representativeness in the embedding space, and reconstruction-based uncertainty, used as a proxy for sample difficulty. These signals are combined through a weighted geometric score, where the weighting is determined by a closed-form pacing rule based on the effective annotation budget and the Difficulty-Coverage Ratio, a pool-level statistic measuring the alignment between difficulty and representativeness. We evaluate CSCS on four 3D medical image segmentation benchmarks: BraTS, FeTA, Spleen, and an in-house fetal MRI dataset. Using nnU-Net as downstream segmentation model, CSCS shows consistently competitive performance across datasets and annotation budgets, with the strongest gains in low-to-mid annotation regimes. These results suggest that dataset-aware cold-start initialization can improve the robustness of active learning for 3D medical image segmentation by adapting sample selection to the geometry of the unlabeled pool.

</details>


### [950] [LLM-Driven Heuristic Frame-Level Quantization Parameter Adaptation for VVenC](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20847)
*Liqiang He,Yingwen Zhang,Riyu Lu,Meng Wang,Shiqi Wang*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper proposes using LLMs within an evolutionary framework to automatically design RDO heuristics for frame-level QP adaptation in video encoding, achieving better rate-distortion performance than both fixed-QP and Lagrangian baseline methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed-QP schemes are content-agnostic, while classical Lagrangian RDO methods suffer from inaccurate multiplier settings. There's a need for more adaptive QP allocation methods that can automatically discover effective heuristics.

Method: A closed-loop evolutionary framework where an LLM iteratively proposes RDO heuristics as algorithmic ideas with executable code. Each heuristic is evaluated by encoding with VVenC, acting as a scoring function comparing QP choices based on encoding statistics of past frames and current candidates.

Result: The evolved heuristic achieves promising rate-distortion improvements over both fixed-QP and Lagrangian baselines across multiple test sets.

Conclusion: LLMs can autonomously discover adaptive heuristics that penalize QP fluctuations via entropy-based terms, offering new insights for RDO algorithm design.

Abstract: Optimal frame-level quantization parameter (QP) allocation remains a persistent challenge in modern video encoders. The fixed-QP scheme widely adopted in practical systems is inherently content-agnostic, while classical Lagrangian rate-distortion optimization (RDO) methods often suffer from inaccurate multiplier settings. In this paper, we explore the use of large language models (LLMs) to automatically design RDO heuristics for frame-level QP adaptation. We construct a closed-loop evolutionary framework in which the LLM iteratively proposes RDO heuristics as algorithmic ideas with executable code, and these candidates are evaluated directly through encoding with the Fraunhofer Versatile Video Encoder (VVenC), where each heuristic acts as a scoring function that compares different QP choices based on the encoding statistics of past frames and current candidates. Experimental results across multiple test sets show that the evolved heuristic achieves promising rate-distortion improvements over both the fixed-QP scheme and the Lagrangian baseline. Further analysis reveals that the LLM can autonomously discover an adaptive heuristic that penalizes QP fluctuations via entropy-based terms, providing new insights into the design of RDO algorithms

</details>


### [951] [FlowCodec: One-Step Flow Prior for Generative Image Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21030)
*Yinhuan Huang,Hao Cao,Pu chen,Wenqi Guo,Zhijin Qin*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: FlowCodec is a streamlined framework that plugs pretrained large-scale text-to-image diffusion priors into ultra-low-bitrate image compression, achieving strong perceptual quality at <0.05 bpp without requiring additional conditioning or auxiliary networks, and with minimal trainable parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion-based image compression methods rely on carefully engineered conditioning or auxiliary branches and substantial retraining, which become costly as models scale. The authors ask whether stronger generative priors can be integrated into compression through a simpler, more extensible design.

Method: FlowCodec decomposes the pipeline into two decoupled stages: (1) Latent Compression, mapping clean latents to bitrate-constrained noisy latents; and (2) Latent Transport, using a pretrained text-to-image prior (e.g., Qwen-image or FLUX.1-dev) to refine noisy latents back to clean ones in a single step. No additional conditioning or auxiliary networks are needed.

Result: FlowCodec preserves high visual quality at bitrates <0.05 bpp. The Qwen-image variant significantly outperforms existing methods in LPIPS and DISTS, while both variants deliver higher PSNR, faster encoding than one-step diffusion methods, and the FLUX variant maintains competitive decoding speed. Trainable parameters are below 0.54% of the generative backbone.

Conclusion: FlowCodec demonstrates that stronger generative priors can be integrated into ultra-low-bitrate compression via a simple, decoupled two-stage design without retraining heavy backbones, offering an extensible and parameter-efficient solution.

Abstract: Diffusion-based image compression methods, leveraging powerful generative priors, have demonstrated remarkable perceptual quality at ultra-low bitrates. However, adapting modern generative models to image compression often relies on carefully engineered conditioning or auxiliary branches, together with substantial retraining, and these costs grow as the models scale. This motivates an open question: Can stronger generative priors be integrated into compression through a simpler, more extensible design? To answer this, we propose FlowCodec, a streamlined framework that plugs pretrained large-scale text-to-image priors (e.g., Qwen-image-2512 and FLUX.1-dev) into ultra-low-bitrate codecs. FlowCodec decomposes the pipeline into two decoupled stages: (1) Latent Compression, which maps clean latents to bitrate-constrained noisy latents; and (2) Latent Transport, which leverages the pretrained prior to refine the noisy latents toward the clean ones in a single step. Notably, FlowCodec requires neither additional conditioning signals nor auxiliary networks. Furthermore, with lightweight adaptation, it can flexibly support multiple bitrates while keeping the number of trainable parameters below 0.54% of the generative backbone. Experiments show that FlowCodec preserves high visual quality at bitrates below 0.05 bits per pixel. The Qwen-image variant significantly outperforms existing methods in terms of LPIPS and DISTS, while both variants deliver higher PSNR and clearly faster encoding than existing one-step diffusion-based methods, with the FLUX variant also maintaining competitive decoding speed.

</details>


### [952] [MoECodec: Image Compression for joint human and machine perception via Mixture-of-Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21033)
*Jiancheng Zhao,Xiang Ji,Yifan Zhan,Zunian Wan,Yinqiang Zheng*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: MoECodec is a token-aware image compression framework using Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) with stable routing and Group Shuffle MLP to dynamically allocate computation per token for multiple downstream vision tasks in a single model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing image compression methods for machine tasks are either task-specific (high overhead) or use transfer-based adaptations (heuristic), and both apply static computation patterns that fail to account for varying semantic importance across image regions.

Method: Replaces FFN layers in transformer-based compression with token-wise MoE, uses expert-choice routing + spatial total variation regularization for stable routing, and proposes Group Shuffle MLP (GShMLP) for lightweight expert design.

Result: Consistent improvement over baselines on both conventional image reconstruction and multiple machine vision tasks.

Conclusion: Dynamic token-level computation via MoE is effective for unified image compression for machines, achieving better performance without task-specific models.

Abstract: Image compression for machines calls for a unified codec that serves multiple downstream vision tasks. Existing approaches either adopt task-specific end-to-end designs, raising parameter and deployment overhead, or rely on transfer-based adaptations that remain externally attached and heuristic task design. A key limitation shared by both lines of work is their largely static computation pattern, which applies similar transformations across tokens despite the fact that different image regions exhibit markedly different semantic importance and complexity for machine perception. We propose MoECodec, a token-aware image compression framework that supports multiple downstream tasks within a single model. MoECodec replaces the FFN layers in transformer-based compression model token-wise Mixture-of-Experts (MoE), enabling dynamic, token-level computation conditioned on the input content and task objective. To make MoE effective in compression model, we introduce a stable routing strategy that combines expert-choice routing with spatial total variation regularization to encourage spatially coherent assignments, and we propose a lightweight expert architecture, Group Shuffle MLP (GShMLP), to control parameter growth. Extensive experiments show consistent improvement against baselines on both conventional image reconstruction and machine tasks.

</details>


### [953] [Anatomically Consistent TMJ Disc Segmentation via Semantic Anchoring and Clinical Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21177)
*Dayun Ju,Chanyoung Kim,Sunyoung Jung,Hyo-Jung Jung,Chena Lee,Younjung Park,Seong Jae Hwang*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: TISC is a TMJ disc segmentation framework using prototypical semantic anchoring and clinical metadata-guided boundary refinement, achieving significant Dice improvements on a large MRI cohort.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TMJ disc segmentation from MRI is unreliable due to small size, low contrast, and morphological variability; existing methods produce fragmented or anatomically inconsistent masks.

Method: Proposes TISC with two modules: (1) Prototypical Semantic Anchoring (PSA) using adjacent-slice MedDINOv3 features for robust localization, and (2) Clinical-Metadata Point Refinement (C-MPR) using Mouth Open Limitation (MOL) for boundary refinement.

Result: On 2,488 PD MRI volumes from 1,300 patients, achieves up to 4.96 Dice improvement over strong baselines with more anatomically coherent segmentation.

Conclusion: Integrating semantic anchoring with clinical metadata guidance yields clinically reliable TMJ disc segmentation across diverse architectures.

Abstract: Segmenting the temporomandibular joint (TMJ) disc from MRI is essential for accurate diagnosis of internal derangement, yet it remains unreliable in practice due to its small size, low contrast, and morphological variability. Existing methods, primarily adapted from general segmentation architectures, often produce fragmented or anatomically inconsistent masks, leading to unstable measurements of disc position and shape for downstream diagnosis. To address these challenges, we propose TISC, a TMJ disc segmentation framework that integrates semantic anchoring with clinical metadata-guided boundary refinement. The framework first establishes robust disc localization in the foundation model feature space via a Prototypical Semantic Anchoring (PSA) module that aggregates adjacent-slice MedDINOv3 features and derives a prototype-driven similarity map. It then performs targeted boundary refinement through a Clinical-Metadata Point Refinement (C-MPR) module, with point-wise predictions modulated by Mouth Open Limitation (MOL), a clinical indicator associated with disc displacement without reduction. On a large-scale cohort of 2,488 PD MRI volumes from 1,300 patients, our method achieves up to a 4.96 Dice improvement over strong baselines across diverse architectures, delivering more anatomically coherent and clinically reliable TMJ disc segmentation.

</details>


### [954] [Geometry Calibration in Tomography with a Differentiable Ray-Based Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21405)
*Youssef Haouchat,Aleix Boquet-Pujadas,Sepand Kashani,Philippe Thévenaz,Michael Unser*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: A framework that jointly reconstructs tomographic volumes and calibrates acquisition geometry by using a differentiable x-ray transform operator with B-spline volume representation, validated across CT, micro-CT, nano-CT, and PET data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Geometric misalignments between nominal and true acquisition parameters in tomography degrade reconstruction quality, necessitating a method that can simultaneously reconstruct volumes and calibrate geometry.

Method: Uses an x-ray transform operator with efficient gradient computation via ray-tracing; represents volumes in a B-spline basis (continuously differentiable) instead of voxels; jointly optimizes volume and geometry parameters.

Result: Validated on CT, micro-CT, nano-CT, and PET data under various geometric misalignments, showing effective joint reconstruction and calibration.

Conclusion: The B-spline representation provides a better-behaved optimization landscape than voxel-based methods, and the framework generalizes across multiple tomographic modalities.

Abstract: Geometric misalignments between the nominal and true acquisition parameters in tomography degrade reconstructions. We propose a framework that jointly reconstructs the volume and calibrates the acquisition geometry for arbitrary source--detector configurations. The core of our framework is an x-ray transform operator whose gradients with respect to the acquisition geometry can be efficiently computed with a ray-tracing method of structure and computational complexity similar to those of the forward operator. We represent the volume in a B-spline basis to provide a continuously differentiable model. This results in a better-behaved optimization landscape compared to voxel-based representations. We validate our framework with CT, micro-CT, nano-CT, and positron emission tomography data under a variety of geometric misalignments.

</details>


### [955] [2D Versus 3D Diffusion for In Silico Training of Interventional X-ray AI Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21414)
*Sampath Rapuri,Jeremy Ko,Benjamin D. Killeen,Russell H. Taylor,Mathias Unberath*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper explores two methods for synthesizing X-ray training data—3D latent diffusion for CT generation and 2D view-conditioned diffusion for direct X-ray synthesis—and shows that 2D diffusion-based synthetic X-rays can train landmark detection models that perform nearly as well as models trained on real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Annotated X-ray data is scarce, and while mechanistic DRR synthesis can help, it requires high-resolution annotated CT models, which creates a bottleneck on data quantity and variability.

Method: Two methods: (1) a 3D conditional latent diffusion model to generate CT volumes for subsequent DRR generation, and (2) a view-conditioned 2D diffusion model to directly produce synthetic X-ray images.

Result: The 2D diffusion-based synthetic X-rays enabled training an anatomical landmark detection model that generalized to real X-rays with performance rivaling models trained on real data.

Conclusion: Synthetic 2D diffusion-based training data can substitute for real X-ray data, offering a promising path to generate large, diverse datasets for robust AI models in interventional X-ray imaging.

Abstract: The ability to synthesize realistic X-ray images has catalyzed the development of AI models for X-ray image-guided procedures, which otherwise suffer from a lack of available annotated data. Prior work has demonstrated the effectiveness of mechanistic simulation of digitally reconstructed radiographs (DRRs) as a training data source for a myriad of tasks, including segmentation and anatomical landmark detection, with comparable or superior performance to real data training. However, mechanistic DRR synthesis still relies on the availability of annotated high-resolution anatomical models. Deriving these from CT images of real patients or specimens imposes an undesirable bottleneck on data quantity and variability. In this work, we explore two methods for synthesizing training data: (1) a 3D conditional latent diffusion model that generates CT volumes to use as inputs for mechanistic DRR generation without real, 3D anatomical models, and (2) a view-conditioned 2D diffusion model that produces synthetic X-rays. In controlled experiments, we demonstrate that synthetic 2D diffusion-based X-rays can be used to train an anatomical landmark detection model that generalized to real X-ray images with performance rivaling that of a model trained on real X-ray images. Thus, we provide preliminary evidence that synthetic, 2D diffusion-based training data can substitute for real X-ray data, identifying a promising avenue towards generating large, diverse datasets for training robust AI models in interventional X-ray imaging.

</details>


### [956] [Optimising Inpainting Data with Delaunay Averages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21506)
*Vassillen Chizhov,Joachim Weickert*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a novel inpainting-based image compression method using Delaunay triangulation vertices and average triangle colors, combined with homogeneous diffusion inpainting, achieving superior reconstruction quality and favorable scaling where doubling resolution halves stored data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The reconstruction quality of inpainting-based image compression critically depends on the selection of stored data, motivating the need for better feature types and optimization strategies.

Method: Store Delaunay triangulation vertex locations with average color values inside triangles; use homogeneous diffusion inpainting with a positive definite linear system solved via conjugate gradient; employ a stippling-inspired data optimization strategy.

Result: Outperforms inpainting with optimized color values by a large margin; doubling image resolution allows halving stored data percentage while maintaining quality; data densities below 1% yield appealing reconstructions for high-resolution images.

Conclusion: The Delaunay-based approach with homogeneous diffusion inpainting is highly effective for compressing modern high-resolution images, with favorable scaling properties.

Abstract: Inpainting-based image compression usually stores an optimised subset of all pixel locations and their colour values. In the decoding phase, the missing data are approximated via inpainting. Since the reconstruction quality depends critically on the selection of the stored data, we introduce a novel feature type: We store the vertex locations of a Delaunay triangulation together with the average colour values inside all triangles. We show that combining this feature type with homogeneous diffusion inpainting creates an elegant mathematical formulation with a positive definite linear system of equations. Even a simple solver such as the conjugate gradient method allows the handling of large images. To make our Delaunay averages maximally adaptive to the image, we develop an efficient data optimisation strategy specifically tailored to them. It incorporates ideas successfully used in the stippling literature. Experiments show that our approach outperforms the popular inpainting with optimised colour values by a large margin. Last but not least, we discover a favourable scaling behaviour: Doubling the image resolution allows us to halve the percentage of stored data while maintaining the quality level. This is attractive for compressing modern high-resolution images, where even data densities below 1 % yield appealing reconstructions.

</details>


### [957] [A Skin-Tone-Aware Dual-Representation Remote Photoplethysmography Framework for Contactless Respiratory Rate Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21511)
*Trishna Saikia,Anup Kumar Gupta,Puneet Gupta,Pasi Liljeberg*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a skin-tone-aware dynamic RGB projection for remote respiratory rate estimation from facial videos, combining Eulerian and denoised Lagrangian representations with a phase-independent contrastive loss. It introduces the RR-rPPG dataset and achieves up to 42.1% MAE reduction over existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional contact-based respiratory rate monitoring is intrusive; remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) offers non-contact potential but existing methods use fixed RGB projections designed for heart rate, not respiratory dynamics. Lagrangian representation is sensitive to non-respiratory motion, and there is a lack of diverse demographic data (e.g., Indian representation).

Method: (1) Skin-tone-aware dynamic RGB signal projection to capture respiratory information. (2) A denoising network to mitigate motion sensitivity in Lagrangian rPPG signals. (3) A phase-independent contrastive loss for collaborative learning of Eulerian and Lagrangian representations. (4) Introduction of RR-rPPG dataset with Indian demographic representation.

Result: Outperforms comparison methods on both RR-rPPG and COHFACE datasets; achieves up to 42.1% reduction in mean absolute error across evaluated settings.

Conclusion: Jointly leveraging skin-tone-aware Eulerian and denoised Lagrangian representations is effective for contactless respiratory rate estimation from facial videos. RR-rPPG provides a diverse benchmark for future research.

Abstract: Respiratory rate is a vital indicator of pulmonary and cardiovascular health, yet conventional methods for estimating respiratory rate are often intrusive due to their contact-based nature. Remote photoplethysmography offers a promising non-contact alternative and has been widely used for heart rate estimation; however, its potential for respiratory rate estimation remains underexplored. Existing methods typically adapt green and chrominance-based projections originally designed for heart rate estimation, which only partially capture respiratory dynamics. Most prior work focuses on the Eulerian representation with fixed or empirically selected RGB projections.
  To address these gaps, we propose a skin-tone-aware dynamic RGB signal projection that captures respiratory information. To mitigate the sensitivity of the Lagrangian representation to non-respiratory motion, we introduce a denoising network for motion-based remote photoplethysmography signals. We further design a phase-independent contrastive loss that enables Eulerian and Lagrangian representations to collaboratively learn respiratory rate information. We also introduce RR-rPPG, a respiratory-rate facial video dataset with Indian demographic representation.
  We evaluate the method on RR-rPPG and the publicly available COHFACE dataset, where it consistently outperforms comparison methods and achieves up to a 42.1% reduction in mean absolute error across the evaluated settings.
  The proposed framework demonstrates the effectiveness of jointly leveraging skin-tone-aware Eulerian and denoised Lagrangian representations for contactless respiratory rate estimation from facial videos. In addition, RR-rPPG contributes a diverse benchmark resource for future research in remote respiratory monitoring. The code and dataset will be made publicly available upon paper acceptance.

</details>


### [958] [Unsupervised Susceptibility Distortion Correction of EPI without Calibration Scans via Image Translation-Based Registration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21588)
*Wooseung Kim,Sung-Hong Park*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: SACRED is a calibration-free framework using invertible neural networks and unsupervised registration to correct susceptibility-induced distortions in fMRI BOLD images, using only T1w images and unidirectional PE BOLD images, outperforming existing methods on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: EPI-based fMRI suffers from geometric distortions due to magnetic field inhomogeneities. Existing correction methods require additional calibration scans (field maps, reverse PE) which are often unavailable, motivating a calibration-free solution.

Method: Uses an invertible neural network for image translation between BOLD and T1w images, a modality-independent neighborhood descriptor for structural consistency, and unsupervised mono-contrast similarity registration, with optional test-time adaptation for out-of-distribution data.

Result: Evaluated on one in-distribution and two out-of-distribution datasets; SACRED significantly outperforms competing methods in distortion correction accuracy and shows robustness to scanner and population shifts, further improved by test-time adaptation.

Conclusion: SACRED provides an effective calibration-free alternative for fMRI distortion correction, reducing the need for extra scans while achieving strong performance and generalization across diverse datasets.

Abstract: Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) utilizes echo-planar imaging (EPI) to capture blood-oxygen-level-dependent (BOLD) signals with high temporal resolution. However, EPI is inherently sensitive to magnetic field inhomogeneities, resulting in susceptibility-induced geometric distortions along the phase-encoding (PE) direction. To correct these distortions, conventional approaches rely on additional calibration scans, such as field maps or reverse PE acquisitions, which are not always available in practice. To overcome this limitation, we propose SACRED, a calibration scan-free susceptibility distortion correction framework that corrects geometric distortions via image translation-based registration using only a routinely acquired anatomical T1-weighted (T1w) image and a unidirectional PE BOLD image. SACRED employs an invertible neural network as the image translation backbone to bridge the contrast gap between BOLD and T1w images while enforcing structural consistency through a modality independent neighborhood descriptor. This design enables the use of a mono-contrast similarity objective to train the registration network in an unsupervised manner without requiring distortion-corrected BOLD images. In addition, we incorporate test-time adaptation (TTA) to further enhance performance on out-of-distribution (OOD) data at inference time. SACRED was evaluated on one in-distribution (ID) dataset and two OOD datasets, and was compared with representative fMRI distortion correction methods. The results demonstrate that SACRED significantly outperforms competing methods on both ID and OOD datasets, exhibiting robustness to scanner and population shifts, partly enabled by TTA. The code will be made publicly available upon acceptance.

</details>


### [959] [Deep Unrolled Networks in Representation Space Applied to MRI Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21602)
*Efe Ilıcak,Baris Imre,Chloé Najac,Ruben van den Broek,Beatrice Lena,Andrew Webb,Marius Staring*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: DUNE introduces a framework for deep unrolled networks that operates in learned representation spaces while maintaining exact adherence to physical measurements via Vector-Jacobian Product gradients, achieving superior MRI reconstruction quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing deep unrolled networks either operate in the object domain or use heuristic methods for data consistency in representation spaces, sacrificing measurement fidelity. This work aims to combine the benefits of representation space learning with exact data consistency.

Method: DUNE derives the data consistency gradient via the chain rule and implements it through Vector-Jacobian Product (VJP), enabling exact backpropagation of measurement residuals into the representation space. It supports diverse architectures including pre-trained encoders.

Result: Evaluated on accelerated MRI reconstruction tasks, DUNE with exact VJP-based gradients achieves superior reconstruction quality and structural fidelity compared to state-of-the-art baselines, validated on both single-channel portable low-field and multi-channel clinical high-field MRI acquisitions.

Conclusion: Exact VJP-based data consistency in representation spaces significantly improves reconstruction performance, and the framework is flexible enough to support various backbone architectures.

Abstract: Deep unrolled networks (DUNs) integrate physical forward models with learned regularization in cascaded network architectures, achieving exceptional performance in inverse problems while maintaining interpretability. While most DUNs operate in the object domain (e.g., image space), recent variants explored representation spaces for improved information flow. However, these methods rely on heuristic methods for data consistency (DC), sacrificing fidelity with measurements.
  In this work, we introduce DUNE (Deep Unrolled Networks in rEpresentation space), a framework that maintains exact adherence to physical measurements while operating in learned representation spaces. By deriving the DC gradient via the chain rule and implementing it through the Vector-Jacobian Product (VJP), we enable exact backpropagation of measurement residuals into the representation space. This formulation supports diverse architectural backbones, including pre-trained encoders to guide the iterative process.
  We assess DUNE against state-of-the-art baselines on accelerated MRI reconstruction tasks, demonstrating that exact VJP-based gradients yield superior reconstruction quality and structural fidelity across both single-channel portable low-field and multi-channel clinical high-field MRI acquisitions. The code will be available upon publication at https://github.com/EfeIlicak/DUNE.

</details>


### [960] [PaaF: Raising the perceived quality of INR-Based Image Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21655)
*Lorenzo Catania,Dario Allegra*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: PaLF introduces a novel INR-based image compression method with improved architecture, adaptive quantization, and entropy coding, achieving better rate-distortion performance and faster encoding than prior INR methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: INR-based image compression suffers from long encoding times and poor PSNR performance compared to traditional and learned codecs. The authors aim to close this gap while preserving INR advantages like simplified decoding.

Method: Proposes PaLF (Picture as a Function) with three key components: improved architectural design for INRs, adaptive quantization, and an efficient entropy coding scheme.

Result: Consistent improvements over existing INR-based methods in both quantitative metrics (PSNR) and perceptual quality.

Conclusion: INR-based approaches have strong potential; PaLF helps narrow the performance gap between functional representations and established compression paradigms.

Abstract: Implicit Neural Representations (INRs) have recently emerged as a promising paradigm for image compression, offering a fundamentally different approach from traditional and learned codecs. Nevertheless, INR-based methods for image compression suffer from long encoding times and a consistent performance gap in classic quality metrics such as PSNR. In this work, we explore the potential of purely INR-based compression methods and we propose PaaF (Picture as a Function), a novel INR-based image codec that introduces improved architectural design, adaptive quantization, and an efficient entropy coding scheme. These components are designed to enhance rate-distortion performance while preserving the simplicity and parallelizability of INR-based decoding. Experimental results demonstrate consistent improvements over existing INR-based methods in both quantitative metrics and perceptual quality. These findings highlight the potential of INR-based approaches and contribute to narrowing the gap between functional representations and more established compression paradigms.

</details>


### [961] [Configurable Algorithms for Histopathologic Cancer Detection on Quantum Hardware](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21752)
*Nandika Goyal,Glen Uehara,Andreas Spanias*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper proposes two quantum algorithms (DG-CSWAP and DG-DST) for histopathologic cancer detection, validated on real quantum hardware via Amazon Braket, achieving 79.80% accuracy with a single ResNet-50 and demonstrating the first quantum hardware implementation with noise mitigation for histopathologic image classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Histopathologic cancer detection is challenging due to tissue variability, staining differences, and subtle visual distinctions between disease classes, motivating the need for novel computational approaches like quantum algorithms.

Method: Two quantum algorithms are proposed: (1) a configurable dual-gradient CSWAP circuit (DG-CSWAP) using per-pixel local Ry encoding for multi-directional edge responses, and (2) a hardware-efficient destructive swap circuit (DG-DST) matched to QPU gate sets. A three-stage NISQ mitigation pipeline (readout error correction, bias subtraction, slope regression) reduces hardware MSE by ~8x.

Result: Achieves inter-platform Pearson r ~ 0.93-0.94 across five quantum processors, 79.80% accuracy with a single ResNet-50 (vs. 85.55% baseline ensemble), and a Lite configuration provides 17x preprocessing speedup at 2.59% accuracy cost.

Conclusion: This is the first quantum hardware implementation study with noise mitigation for histopathologic image classification, demonstrating feasibility on real quantum processors.

Abstract: Histopathologic cancer detection is challenging due to tissue variability, staining differences, and subtle visual distinctions between disease classes. We propose two quantum algorithms for this task: a configurable dual-gradient CSWAP circuit (DG-CSWAP) that computes multi-directional edge responses in a single execution via per-pixel local Ry encoding, and a hardware-efficient destructive swap circuit (DG-DST) natively matched to quantum processing unit (QPU) gate sets at substantially lower circuit complexity. We prove algebraic equivalence between DG-CSWAP and DG-DST, enabling a two-circuit QPU validation strategy. A three-stage NISQ mitigation pipeline, including readout error correction, bias subtraction, and slope regression, reduces single-pixel hardware MSE by ~8x. Validated on five quantum processors via Amazon Braket, the method achieves inter-platform Pearson r ~ 0.93-0.94 across all local-simulator pairs. Compared to a prior Quantum Fourier Transform (QFT) based amplitude-encoding baseline requiring 12-qubit global state preparation and a three-model ensemble (85.55% on PatchCamelyon), the proposed method uses shot-based measurements, executes on real quantum hardware, and achieves 79.80% accuracy with a single ResNet-50. A Lite configuration delivers a 17x preprocessing speedup at a 2.59% accuracy cost. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first quantum hardware implementation study with noise mitigation for histopathologic image classification.

</details>


### [962] [Scaling up fine-grained intracranial vessel annotations in computed tomography angiography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21756)
*Chu-Hsuan Lin,Alberto Mario Ceballos-Arroyo,Jisoo Kim,Shrikanth M. Yadav,Huaizu Jiang,Lei Qin,Geoffrey S. Young*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: SemanticVessel is a new dataset for fine-grained brain vessel segmentation in CTA scans, featuring 20 arterial classes plus a generic artery class, generated from dynamic 4D-CTA with reusable labels across phases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing datasets discard minor arteries as background; this work aims to provide more complete vessel segmentation by including minor arteries via a generic class, enabling better fine-grained segmentation.

Method: Dynamic 4D-CTA scans are used to generate artery/vein traces via intensity-guided region growing, then refined by an expert radiologist into 20 arterial classes. Labels from one phase are reused across multiple phases to expand dataset size cost-effectively.

Result: Models trained with the additional generic artery class produce superior fine-grained segmentations compared to those trained without it.

Conclusion: SemanticVessel enhances brain vessel segmentation by including minor arteries, and multi-phase label reuse improves dataset scalability without extra annotation cost. Code, GUI, and weights will be publicly released.

Abstract: In this work, we present SemanticVessel, a dataset for fine-grained brain vessel segmentation in computed tomography angiography scans. Based on the detailed contrast provided by dynamic 4D-CTA scans, we generate segmentation traces for arteries and veins. We then use intensity-guided region growing to obtain segmentations of the majority of vascular territories in the human brain, which are refined and annotated with 20 unique arterial classes by an expert radiologist. Unlike existing datasets, where minor arteries are discarded as background content, we merge these minor arteries into a generic arterial class. Due to the multiple-phase acquisition of dynamic 4D-CTA, labels for a single phase can be re-used for other phases in the same series, greatly increasing the size of our dataset with no additional annotation cost. The results show that models trained with the additional generic artery class produce better fine-grained segmentations across the board. We will make our code, annotation GUI, and model weights available to the scientific community. Code, weights, and data will be made available on https://github.com/alceballosa/robust-vessel-segmentation

</details>


### [963] [Delta-Diffusion: Modeling Longitudinal Brain Amyloid-PET Trajectories via Conditional Poisson Diffusion Bridge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22216)
*Yongheng Sun,Minhui Yu,Mengqi Wu,Maureen Kohi,Mingxia Liu*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: Delta-Diffusion is a progression-aware framework for longitudinal PET synthesis, using a conditional Poisson Diffusion Bridge anchored to baseline PET, with a physically-grounded Poisson perturbation in a Diffusion Transformer, to accurately model Beta-amyloid accumulation over time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Longitudinal brain PET imaging is the gold standard for tracking Beta-amyloid accumulation but is limited by high costs and radiation risks. Existing deep generative models for longitudinal synthesis suffer from identity drift and bias toward replicating baseline signals instead of modeling temporal progression.
